Comment: not impressive at all. almost everything was poorly conserved. the third picture is the stelae of doctors, which is supposed to have inscriptions, but no efforts have been made to restore it at all so the most visible words that i could see are in the fourth image.  the exhibits were really repetitive as well. exact phrases and pieces of information were just reiterated on panels that were right next to one another again and again.  i spent about 40 minutes here. it’s not worth the entrance fee. i don’t get why the reviews of this place is so great.

Items in the souvenir shop are fake. We were told by the staff it was buffalo horn combs ect. Only to realise when we got home they are plastic. Just another scam in Vietnam at an official level. Terrible for the countries reputation. It's the consistent small things that build up over time.

I had a bad experience buying tickets with an unfriendly and bad-attitude ticket seller. Two Western customers who bought tickets before me got their change back, but if they hadn't counted it, she probably wouldn't have paid the extra 100k. She's so dishonest. I think this ticket seller needs to be replaced before she spreads a lot of bad impressions.

The older ticket seller (on the right) was extremely rude to young customers, and most of the staff here are like that =))) I put my ID card on the counter and bent down to stuff money into my pocket because the zipper was stuck. When I looked up, they said, "Where's your mouth? Can't you say anything? Why are you just handing it over like that?"  I was in a hurry and either dropped or forgot to take the ticket with the QR code. When I went back to ask, they said, "There are no tickets here." My family had to stand and talk for over 3-4 minutes before we got another ticket. When we tried to swipe it, they wouldn't accept the second printed ticket =))))))) I didn't want to argue with the "superior" staff, so I just left with the money and ticket.

TL;dr: Poor management. Terrible attitude. Racist ticketing and entry staff. No information given.When they say Exit only, they mean it, even if you need guidance on how to experience the park that wasn’t given at the counter.  When you enter, don’t expect explanations. The ticket seller didn’t explain anything nor did she smile, and the entrance staff didn’t speak any foreign language. I expressed I had questions and was told with a translator app that I needed to exit through the entrance and rejoin the line, but unsure if it was necessary, I went in without waiting for a second time. I found a great app audioguide (highly recommended, but bring your earplugs or you will hold your phone for 30+ min!).  Later, I went back to check if guides were available, as everyone else had them. My arm hurt, I was curious if this was the only option. Also, we saw people with guides (people who spoke both english and spanish language) who had a great experience. So we wanted to know the options and decide together. Information only at the entrance. So back to the front we went.  They wouldn’t let us both exit, even though there was no reason to block us. Walk all the way through the park, and then back to the front, instead of going out, getting the info and hopefully coming back in with an in person guided tour booked. “Exit only” signs were enforced, the staff blocked me with their bodies (!), so I couldn’t go ask. But the staff wasn’t helpful or friendly nor understood our concern, I hate to say it, straight up rude. They smiled at other Vietnamese but wiped the smile off for us. Also, I am not entitled to people speaking languages but even a few signs would have worked. The experience is not really that well thought out for foreigners/ tourists.  Meanwhile, My husband eventually just exited with the gardener, 10 m to the side. The garden is beautiful, and the kind coffee shop staff redeemed the experience, but that’s about it. All of this could have been avoided if people cared about their guests experience (in my personal opinion) and were at least a little flexible or explained the options.
Nothing of interest…Best part was the fan on the floor providing some much needed cool air

The national monument but the entrance fee is too high. Compared to the monuments in the south like Cu Chi, Independence Palace is less than 50k. The preservation is not good, too noisy, visitors are blocked by students taking pictures, …

Obviously bought a tram ticket and waited for almost 1 hour but no bus picked up. got carsick but took a car. complained but they didn't accept. during peak hours at 2am asked for a charging car
Didn’t get in because we were in SHORTS and one of us with sleeveless…🤷🏻‍♂️ Saved our pennies for ice cream and iced coffee instead. Lol. …

Just a heads-up—no card payments are accepted here, only cash. I wasn’t aware beforehand and had to walk 15 minutes to find an ATM, after already walking over two hours in the heat. It was really inconvenient, and in the end, I gave up and went back to my hotel. It would be great if they offered more payment options or at least had a nearby ATM.

My friend and I went to get calligraphy for the New Year, and we had a terrible experience with the paper seller (he was quite old). We were queuing, and two other people were already choosing paper. The seller became annoyed because the paper they were looking at wasn't of satisfactory quality – it was smudged with glue and ink. I didn't say anything, I just stood there watching, and he said, "Give me the money." I asked him the price. He replied, "200k, give me the money quickly. Higher price means better paper, why would you take inferior paper?" Then I chose some, but the paper was exactly the same as the other two. Two women sitting behind him were also arguing with him, saying things like, "If you want good paper, bring it here for 1 million VND," and "If there are creases after folding it, you'll have to pay for it." His tone was incredibly unpleasant. I'm not overly picky, but the paper had a lot of glue smudges, so I wanted to exchange it. When I exchanged it for another sheet, it had yellow ink dots everywhere (not the kind with the dragon design). The man said if I didn't like it, I should return the money and not make a fuss. The second time, after failing to choose the right paper, I received my money back. I can confirm that the issues I'm concerned about are true. I hope the monument management will consider and pay attention to the visitor experience to address these issues. February 4th, 2025, a little after 12 noon.

Very crowded with lots of locals in traditional costume doing photo shoots in preparation for LNY 3 weeks away… not much to see and peace is disturbed by crowds. The historical section was nice to see that the temple has been used for various intents over the centuries. But the actual place is in need of maintenance (paint, repair…)
The staff had a bad attitude and insisted that I buy a ticket even though I was under 16.

The ticket seller on the afternoon of January 17th, the female wearing glasses, was so arrogant with the customers. When visiting the relic site, she scolded the customers like a child. The relic site is beautiful, civilized, and solemn, but the staff should be reviewed.

The ticket price is too high, 70,000 VND is extremely expensive. This price has increased from 25,000 VND. In addition, there is trash everywhere inside. Poor management. The girl sells the tickets and manages the audio devices yell at the tourists and doesn't know what they are doing. Especially the one for audio devices. She was rude. Take a long time to do small things. How come the people are so rude and work in the temple of literature.

According to European law, people with severe disabilities, including profound deafness, must be granted free or adapted access to cultural and tourist sites. However, at the Temple of Literature in Hanoi, free access was denied to us on the pretext that most tours rely on audio headsets, which excludes us. Instead of granting free entry, the staff imposed a reduced fee. Worse still, the reception and security personnel behaved aggressively to force us out. Shocked by the situation, some hearing visitors tried to defend us, but it made no difference. This is shameful, as deaf people are recognized as having a severe disability under European law. 🤷🏼‍♂️😬
I find it not as nice and clean as advertised, the cost is also a bit high

The experience was so bad, guests came to the restroom but were not allowed to change clothes, while according to the rules of the Temple of Literature, changing clothes in the restroom is NOT prohibited, the attitude of the cleaning staff was disrespectful to guests, saying "if you come in to change, you will be charged 50k"; "go out to let guests in" is it discriminating between foreign guests and our guests, the ticket price is the same, but they did not receive respect, so disappointing!

It's still used as a functioning temple and is worth visiting for the garden and sense of peace in a crazy busy city.

This place is beautify and worth a visit.We took a trishaw ride here, we agreed on 50,000 VND. After we arrived here, the trishaw driver said one person is 50,000 VND and asked for 100,000 VND because there are two of us!The trishaw drivers in Hanoi tend to ask for prices that are two or three times that of a taxi ride. Beware of trishaw cheats!!! The taxi fare from one tourist spot to another one is usually less than 100,000 VND. Do not take small taxis, they are usually run by cheats with rigged taxi meters. A short trip from Ho Chi Ming Mausoleum to the Hanoi Opera House cost me 485,000 VND! It should only be about 100,000 VND according to my estimate. I was later advised by my hotel staff to avoid taking these taxis run by cheats and criminals.

We got out of the Tran Quoc Pagodaand this taxi guy zeroed in on usHe was a sweet talker, very smooth and offered his taxi serviceWe got in and he rode us around town pointed out interesting landmark and sweet talking the whole timeHe mentioned this was peak commute time so the rate was extra!!!We later on found out this was bogusSo the most expensive taxi from Tran Quoc Pagoda to Temple of Literature was 500000 dong ($20)We only realized the scam after the fact so we did not get his picture or anything to show the authorityThis guy was actively talking to tourists to use his serviceWatch out

It really is just a largish, not wholly kempt compound with a central courtyard, a temple, some steles that can only be understood if you know Vietnamese, and a smattering of souvenir stores.  There are far more architecturally interesting things to see in Hanoi, to justify the trek out to Van Mieu just for a stroll through a dusty compound.  It may have historical significant, but you can't quite experience it when visiting.  One way to have a more interesting visit is to come on a weekend or holiday, when the locals descent upon Van Mieu with great enthusiasm.  It's interesting to watch graduation parties and wedding couples, and that's about it.

According to our travel guide, this was supposed to be one of the most beautiful buildings in Hanoi, but we actually don't really understand why. It isn't very big, nor is it particularly special. It's fine for a quick walk around, but if you don't have much time, you might as well skip it.

There is actually not all that much to do as far as tourist attractions compared to most capital cities. Upon arrival he said that was per person. So x2.

My iPhone was pick-pocketed in front of the altar at this temple yesterday. Not sure if this money even went to the police -- or just into the pockets of the hotel staff. Very upset with the people of Hanoi right now. It seems like everything is a scam.

It is an oasis of calm in the middle of Hanoi. It could have been a very interesting site with a long story to tell about cultural influences and the training of state administrators. But it is not. It is not even clear which buildings are original and which are newly rebuilt. (Some of the buildings at the back of the site are marked as newly built, but looking at the rather good condition of other buildings, one suspects that many of them are relatively recent too.)The trouble with the Vietnamese is that they find it hard to admit that for 2,000 years, their chief source of cultural influence was China. As a result it is very difficult to tell a proper story of what was happening at this site. The politically-safe thing to do is to say next-to-nothing. Consequently, except for a handful of signs, the buildings here are mute. They tell nothing of why this site is dedicated to Confucius, a Chinese sage. There is brief mention on a plaque of the content that students had to master, but it would have been better if there had been a small museum to display and elaborate further on this mandatory body of knowledge. This would open a discussion into the strengths and weaknesses of such training and whether such a process for discovering talent/merit -- students were tested on their ability to write poetry -- benefitted the country.Instead, what little emphasis there is in the labelled exhibits extol the greatness of certain kings who built/improved this site. This is an easier approach when the primary political motive is Vietnamese nationalism rather than critical enquiry.In the absence of meaningful information about this site's history, one strolls around the park and wonders "so, what's the big deal here?"

We paid our entrance fee and double checked the change in full view of the ticket booth as we always do expecting it to be correct.  We checked again and again and came to the conclusion we were 100,000 short, a fellow tourist who was next to us in the queue was doing the same and she was 50,000 short. The historical sites are indeed beautiful. You can rent portable audio guides to hear explanations, or hire unofficial guides outside. However, it's a bit difficult to move strollers around, especially if you have small children, which is a bit of a shame.
Confucius's thoughts have also spread to Vietnam, which is really good👍 …
One of the group attractions, to be honest, Taiwan is better
It was just like that.. I went around mid-December and there were a lot of students taking graduation photos so it was fun to watch haha
A calm and quiet place, unlike the busy city. Good for soothing tired eyes and mind for a while. The audio guide gets boring after listening to it, but if there is no audio, you …
Worth seeing, but not an exceptional site.
It's a place worth visiting, not far from Hanoi. There are plenty of photo opportunities and unique sights to see. There are also many beautiful trees.
You can think of it as a normal temple. However, there are things to see all the way to the back, so you should take a good look around the entire temple.
A beautiful group of buildings, though little of the original remains, most having been rebuilt (apparently with concrete...). The visit is over quickly.
If you want to be a top scholar, come and pray here, kids.

My visit to the Temple of Literature in Hanoi was an enriching experience, although the abundance of tourists somewhat dampened my enjoyment. The architecture and history of the site are fascinating, but the crowds made it difficult to …

Without further ado. The historical part is interesting but in Vietnam you have much more beautiful temples and more comfortable to see since this one in particular, being so well located, is always packed.
If you're not interested in Confucius and Confucianism, then... well, I did listen to the audio guide, but it was so long that it was difficult to listen while standing outside.

Since an entrance fee is charged, this historical site should theoretically be well-maintained. However, in reality, it's not. The roof tiles are damaged, the wooden pillars are infested with insects, the pool water isn't changed regularly …
Come and experience the thoughts of Confucius and Mencius.
It's not really worth it, there are nicer free sites.
Just a foreign historical site. Nothing more, nothing less.

It is dedicated to Confucius. You can imagine that it is the Confucius Temple in Hanoi. Students also come here to pray or take graduation photos. The Imperial College building in the back is the first university in Hanoi, which has a unique historical significance for higher education in Vietnam.
It's quite nice to look at, but it didn't blow me away. In such cases, I always recommend forming your own opinion; tastes vary.
Not great, but it's still an activity in Hanoi.
On the tour. There was a vending machine.
Nice place but not much to see
The historical sites in any ordinary city in mainland China are better than this, but the roof tiles are somewhat distinctive.
Nice place for a short trip
You can do it but you don't have to
Remnants of the past, but in a very poor state of preservation. It's peaceful, and you can stroll around amidst the chaos of the city.
You can feel the history while taking your time to explore the place.
It's not worth going during the rainy season.  It's just hot. …
A soothing temple for its garden, with a room dedicated to Confucius
A complex with a temple and various structures, but I wasn't particularly impressed. Then again, all temples in Vietnam are similar.

This is the place depicted on Vietnamese banknotes. There's a tomb honoring Confucius and his disciples, but there's not much to see inside. The admission fee is a bit high compared to the amount of things to see, so I think it's only worth going if you want to take a picture holding a banknote.
You have to pay for it and you don't actually spend that much time on it…
There's a lack of information for tourists.
Historical sites worth visiting
If you come to Hanoi, you should definitely visit here.
Sadly, what was once an authentic temple has, as is so often the case in Vietnam, become a tourist attraction, or nearly so. Everything has been destroyed and rebuilt from scratch. …

The Confucius Temple has a long history, but there are no Chinese explanations. Looking at the architecture alone, Taiwanese temples seem more impressive… but it's still worth visiting to experience the historical significance.

The first university in Vietnam. Vietnam's First National University was built in 1076. Inside there is a souvenir shop. The building resembles an ancient palace. Old building. Not far from Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum or from the Old Quarter. You can take Grab. Not expensive. -+ 25000-35000 VND.

Although it's a popular tourist destination, it's just average for those without a particular interest in historical sites. Nothing particularly captivates. Many Vietnamese people visit here to pray for academic success. Avoiding the midday …
An interesting place in Hanoi. A little less crowded. Nothing special, but I think it was worth the visit. Tickets cost 30,000 vnd, or about 6 złoty.
A shrine dedicated to the gods of Confucius and his disciples. If you have nowhere else to go, go here. Elementary school students are admitted free.
Interesting for the history but not necessarily worth the detour. Lots of tourists...
It's a shame they didn't protect and care for their heritage more. It lacks maintenance, upkeep, and cleaning.
The entrance fee is 30,000 dong. They were trying to get me all the pamphlets at the ticket booth. Anyway, they're scammers. They're just lying around inside.

There were lots of locals wearing ao dai and taking lots of photos. There wasn't much to see, but the buildings and trees were beautiful. It would have been more enjoyable if there had been someone to explain things to …
It has a good space for photography, very good. The tickets are expensive compared to the space. It is always crowded. It has very suitable spaces for photography. They give you good information.

Many Vietnamese students take their graduation photos in the square inside this temple. Groups of them, dressed in traditional Vietnamese ao daisies, compete to outshine each other, creating a lively scene. It's a North Vietnamese Ly …
Don't expect too much and it's good for a quick walk
It's smaller than you'd think, so there's not much to see. It's a taste of Confucianism that Koreans are familiar with.

Wenmiao (Confucian Temple) is also known as the Temple of Confucius, primarily dedicated to Confucius. Ancient Vietnam, it turns out, inherited the imperial examination system from China, making it no wonder Confucius is worshipped there. Chinese visitors feel right at home, reminiscing about the golden age of cultural export.
Not a very pretty site. Not worth the time.
Many students go to the Confucius Temple to pray, but for tourists from Chinese culture, it is a place that can be visited or not.
30,000 or 40,000 dong (less than €2) for entry to see four gardens and ponds of little interest, but it's close to the art museum, so why not?
It's a shame it's in such poor condition. 50,000 per adult. You have to come and see it, but like most, it's not very well maintained.
The audio guide in Italian is terrible, you can't understand anything, it's not worth taking it!!!
Nice to walk through, but there were hordes of tourists there, brought in by bus.
Not recommended for short trips
Korean audio guide available.
If you're in Hanoi you have to see it, but it's nothing spectacular compared to other temples.
Not big, but peaceful and grand
I stopped by on a business trip holiday
There was a section under construction, and several groups of what looked like students were taking pictures everywhere. It was difficult to take pictures at my leisure. Graduation jeans?? So what?

The Temple of Literature, Vietnam's first university dedicated to Confucius... It seems like a popular tourist destination for students... It's also featured on Vietnamese currency... It's not as large as I expected...
It was closed when we went. We only saw the gardens, and they were lovely.
Crowded with tourists and school groups. Not really worth the 70k.
An average tourist attraction, well-organized. Benches, restrooms, and souvenirs are available. Entrance is charged. Audio guides are available for rent.
We were not that impressed, in our opinion it was not accessible enough for tourists interested in an explanation.
I came here only knowing it was Vietnam's first university, so I felt a bit disappointed. I think you should do some research before coming here.
It's a shame there's no audio guide. It's questionable whether this is a nationally important cultural property...
Similar to Taiwan Confucius Temple
A very nice temple but nothing too special.

Magnificent ancient architecture. I recommend taking a guide as the temple itself offers little explanation.

Good, but a bit expensive for what it is.
Nice, relaxing place. Be careful to recount your money, the cashier doesn't give full change.
Quick visit, poorly signposted. But interesting architecture.

It's always crowded and I couldn't find a reason to come here in Hanoi. Maybe it's because I'm not a Confucian?
I don't know much about Vietnamese history, so it wasn't an interesting place to visit.
Personally, it didn't impress me much. Still, it's worth a quick detour.
The temple enshrines Confucius, the most revered teacher, and serves as a center of faith for local students who pray for wisdom.
Worth a visit.
Don't go unless you want to add audio. But it's okay if you add a guided audio. The entrance fee seems expensive.
It's unbearably hot; it's best not to go if it's over 30 degrees Celsius. It's a historical building, but just so-so.
The room at the end of the visit with the figures is interesting, the rest not so much. The maintenance and restoration could be greatly improved.
It is a symbolic place, but you don't have to go out of your way to find it. The entrance and exit are different.
The vendors near the entrance are rip-offs so it's best to ignore them.
It's okay. But it's not very pretty. If you have time, visit it. But not to the point of killing yourself.
If you have the time, then take the detour with the audio guide... otherwise, skip it.
This shrine, dedicated to Confucian scholars, is a tranquil and peaceful place. It's worth a visit.
A rather uninteresting stroll
Nothing special. Many of the facilities are poorly maintained.

just average.

We visited it as it was listed as one of the top most attractions in Hanoi. But honestly, we did not find much things to do here. The architecture was nice but we had already seen much better architecture in Nihn Bihn. The place stands as the first university of Vietnam and so has a historical significance, hence would only recommend this place if someone is interested in its history.

This was recommended as a 'must do' by the hop-on,hop-off bus commentary, and was quite interesting, particularly about the early promotion of education, the doctorate students and the university . There was no information leaflet or audio despite being advertised as such. There was written information inside the display rooms; and calligraphers writing chinese characters representing themes such as hope etc.

The Temple of Literature is a historically significant site in Hanoi, often praised for its peaceful atmosphere and elegant traditional architecture. However, while the site itself is lovely, the experience can feel somewhat underwhelming if you’re expecting a lot of exhibits or interactive elements — it’s more of a quiet stroll than a deep historical immersion. Additionally, there is an entrance ticket required, and the price feels a bit on the costly side compared to what’s actually available to see inside.
This location is on Hop on Hop off bus route,about 3/4th of the way along the route.
Really disappointed with the place. You have to love history and take selfies in front of monuments. It's not our thing, we prefer people and we didn't like it.
The famous temple in Hanoi. Worth seeing, traditional and important for all students and their families. The layout itself is less spectacular.

This place is beautiful and has religious and spiritual significance- but as a tourist I didn’t see the need to go here specially cause of how crowded it was and cause it’s a paid entry. Unless you have a lot of time on your hands- this one is a skip.

The Temple of Literature and National University had a slight feeling of the Forbidden City in Beijing as you walked through one courtyard after another. It was interesting to read about the philosophy of the university where the first lesson for students to learn is how to behave respectfully, and acquiring knowledge comes later. Pleasant gardens, courtyards and religious areas, with some information on the history of the university and students’ examinations. On the day we went we had to navigate around hundreds of young girls dressed in their finest posing for photos, just like around Hoan Kiem lake. Perhaps it’s always the same?
We visited as part of our city tour and it was a nice peaceful place. Lots of history and interesting background. We did enjoy the visit just didn’t find it too special

Although it is not far to the left from Hanoi railway station, it is on the opposite side of the old town, so the area seemed to have a quiet atmosphere. Even so, when it was early afternoon, it seemed like it was time for the locals to go for a walk, and there were noticeable people walking around the perimeter. The whole of Wenmiao is a long, narrow rectangular site, and the open-top bus I used this time took me all the way around to get a full view.

I visited this place as part of a tour of Hanoi city. It was smaller than I expected, but the guide's enthusiastic explanations were very interesting. We took a commemorative photo with the banknotes in our hands, and there were also some unusual exhibits.

This is a historic site worth a visit but it was over run with  tourist groups which made it very busy and less enjoyable. We were there at 10 ish and it’s probably better to go early am or late afternoon.It is good to see the gardens and learn about education for mandarins and see the temple to Confucius .Information is well laid out, you don’t need a guide to enjoy it.
Watch out for the women selling entrance tickets. Proficient in short changing you. Check your change carefully. She was not happy to be caught out.
This temple has a beautiful garden and pond. The temple itself is not that impressive but it has historic value.
It’s nice, but very crowded. If you have plenty of time it’s an interesting place. If you are short on time, you can skip it.
The place is beautiful, cheap entry ticket as well and for sure you can learn a lot about the history of Vietnam. It is also easy to reach from the center of an Old Quarter.

Visited here after the Mausoleum of Ho Chi Minh and it was 40,000 entry to get in. The grounds are quite pretty and so are some of the buildings and the little ponds in the middle. There were lots of school kids there getting their photos taken and it is clearly an important part of Vietnamese tradition. For a tourist though it’s not too interesting and potentially a little bit boring, so we weren’t too overwhelmed by it. I’d say visit if you’re in the area but don’t go out your way.

Of all the places to visit in Hanoi, this is easily the best, although that doesn’t say much.The gardens are well maintained and quite pretty. The ticket prices were fair. And there are lots of interesting displays to learn more about a part of Vietnamese history that isn’t just about the war. I would visit even if you have no interest in history, I certainly don’t but found the experience enjoyable.You won’t need more than 1 hour.

Hanoi boasts a vast urban area and diverse landscapes; it features various temples, museums, parks, restaurants, and bustling pedestrians, especially motorcycles, many of which are ridden by multiple people! It's a city with much to see and explore!
Pretty complex but nothing too exciting here. I didn’t buy a guide, maybe that makes a difference as there are so many good reviews for this temple.

A calm and peaceful sanctuary in a city that is anything but calm and peaceful. The temple was a training institute for prospective mandarins and has little--no, nothing--to do with literature, to western ears anyway. Not that we expected it to. Anyhow, if you're in Confucian or other Chinese philosophy, architecture, etc., this is interesting. If not, it's a quick breeze-through of one of the "obligatory" stops in Hanoi.
Visited the Temple of Literature and the highlight, for me, was the Koi in the pond. I found the rest of the visit boring, except for the Bonsai.
A rather beautiful and quite large temple (compared to other Vietnamese temples I've visited) with lovely grounds.
This is worth a quick walk through to see rebuilt traditional architecture. Many locals taking photos in this complex.
This attraction is quite nice to visit, especially if you want to start Hanoi with a light touch!
It's interesting if you want to learn about the history of the city.

I did enjoy wandering around the grounds, admiring the garden and seeing the architecture of the different gates. As this was used as a centre for learning for centuries, there is so much available information to the history of it and you can get a translation guide to use. In the middle courtyard there is a temple to Confusius, which has a beautifully decorated statue of the philosopher. In the farthest courtyard there are large cast iron urns with great detail, some fine gift shops and a man who specializes in Vietnamese writing and can make personalized pictures.

This was the first stop on our tour of Hanoi. I think they wanted us to start with something less impressive. There is simply not much to see here. As a temple and university, its meaning is obviously important. But the sights inside are uninteresting.
If you have time you can easily include this in your downtown wanderings. If you have enjoyed Hue then this may be a little of a disappointment but it is still pleasant to see.

This was a breath of fresh air after the walk to reach it. It was well maintained. I enjoyed the gardens. The audio guide was interesting, just the headphones that were old and not comfortable to use. I would recommend the trip, as it had an interesting old history.
A visit to the Temple of Literature, the place of worship of Confucius, where the children of mandarins were educated as early as 1076, is exciting
It's worth visiting; it's very clean and well-maintained. We used the audio guide, which helps to understand the history of the temple.

Our guide recommended this place. There is an entrance fee. It seems to be a place like an old university where local students come to pray for success in their exams. They say that touching the crane riding on a turtle will bring good luck.
A bit disappointing. We visited so many temples in Hanoi and this was by far the worst one we visited!
This temple is divided into three parts. The third part requires barefoot access, and men should wear trousers; shorts are not permitted. Beautiful decorations and statues.
This is a nice area with a huge and quite park where you easily can take a break in the very busy city

Very quiet place with a lot of wisdom. I found it interested to hear the stories on the audio. However the quality of it was not that good as i found as the womans museum (which was excellent by the way) it still gave me some more detailed information about the students who where studying there. It was a privilege and they ad to go  through many systems especially values to become a student. Very strict!Also what they owned and had when they studied which was very simple is shown in the museum.
We arrived here on the day of the graduation ceremonies, and that perhaps made the whole experience a little more enjoyable. Otherwise, it is a fine way to spend your time, but nothing very special.

It takes under an hour to visit this site.  Temples in Vietnam are not religious places - rather they are places where one goes to pay tribute to leaders and to those who have made a difference.  This site is mostly a tribute to the teachers and philosophers who brought higher education to the area.  People like Confucius.Worth a quick visit

It's the oldest university in Hanoi of Temple of Literature. Place is well maintained and clean. The main highlights is the Pagoda image is printed behind the currency of 100k Vietnam Dong. Nothing much to see is mainly a place of tribute for students graduation.
Beautifull architecture, place of silence.The audio i found a bit so so.Little stories and sometimes i couldnt find the numbers. However the extra information given is better i quess than without it.

We visited the Temple of Literature as part of a hop-on hop-off tour. This is one of the oldest sites of scholarship and enjoys worldwide renown. The thought of it fills one with awe. Unfortunately, the crowds were so large that it diminished its dignity. Nevertheless, it is definitely worth a visit.

From the standpoint of cultural and philosophical history this is an excellent stop. Be sure to get a guide or rent an audio guide to be sure that you get the value out of the experience. It needs a bit of maintenance work; but, that was ongoing at the time of my visit so it should be improved later. As a bonus one of the original egg coffee cafes is just out side and an easy walk away.
It's worth a visit for the atmosphere this place creates, to immerse yourself in the past and appreciate how important it still is to the Vietnamese people today.
Visited the Temple of Literature on a Tuesday, have to say, I didn’t really get what all the fuss was about. A few courtyards and a pagoda, nice enough, but hardly a must see.
But we didn't find it very impressive. It's a bit neglected and touristy... Maybe it wasn't very interesting because we had high expectations.
We visited the temple of literature for a couple of one morning. The grounds are very beautiful and interesting. There isn’t loads to see but what there is makes for a good but short visit.

My trip to Temple of Literature was just ok. I would suggest to have a tour guide or at least use the Headset for 50,000 VND. We didnt book any tour guide and the place was packed with study tour from the school. We didnt enjoy that much. All in all, it was ok.

We didn't particularly like the temple. We'd seen everything in 10 minutes. We visited without an audio guide, so you learn nothing about it. If you've already seen everything else and just want to kill some time, you can visit this temple; otherwise, it's not worth it.
The visit to this temple was so educational.It was raining hard the day we visited the temple and wished we could have stayed longer.Very clean restroom.
There are interesting history on the scholars that our guide explained. The different turtles represent different level of scholarship which is interesting. There are a number of bonsai there.
It is the sanctuary of the sages of Vietnam; what is important is the history behind it to understand its civilization and way of thinking.
Well worth a visit.  Allow an hour or so.  Lovely architecture.Surprisingly good shops towards the back of the site, not your typical tourist tat.
Very nice grounds and buildings . Interesting offerings . Good Wifi at the coffee shop. Entrance at the far end of the grounds.
A nice place, but mainly for fans of museums and temples of some kind. The kids were bored and it was unnecessary for us.

Went to the temple on my own. The temple is in good condition and beautifully maintained; there are some information boards, but you actually miss quite a few. It's nice to spend an hour walking around, but I think a guide really adds value here.
Can get very hot in the day, there are refreshments to buy inside that are reasonable. There is no need for a tour as ticket can be bought directly outside.
It's alright. Decent way to spend a couple of hours, a bit overrated though. Nice little walk through the gardens whilst taking in some history. I recommend the audio book.

We went to the Temple of Literature just before a massive downpour, but I don't feel like we missed out on much because of the rain. The temple grounds are beautiful, but I didn't learn much about the true historical significance of the site. It seems like there are lots of opportunities for the temple to host educational activities and to create interactive exhibits of some kind. As it was, it was just a very beautifully cared for part of Hanoi's history.

The entrance fee is cheap: 30,000 dong. The place is very well maintained, both the gardens and the interior areas. Note: you enter and exit through the same place, even if you see signs pointing to different exits; they'll take you on a tour of the bar and souvenir area.
People can visit there to see the old structure. Normally calm place but weather is too hot specially in August.

This is the symbol of Hanoi. The first university in Vietnam. There are a number of Confucian temple type structures. They are interesting, beautiful and ancient. I've never seen these before. Perhaps our tour guide just overwhelmed us a bit with too much information

I'd seen photos of this place and read about it, but seeing it in person disappointed me. Sure, it's worth a visit, but honestly, it's nothing special—a couple of buildings with their respective gods inside, a few courtyards, and not much else.

Cost 30,000 dong. Yeah, it's just a temple with small buildings; you can see everything clearly. Didn't really think it was worth the money. It cost quite a bit more (50K) to include headphones, so we just used the signs (fine!).
You buy a ticket and enter.
An interesting temple. Compared to any European temple, it falls short.

A nice place if you like temples. You learn about the history of university scholarship in Hanoi. Try and plan your visit early in the morning or late in the afternoon because it will get very hot during the day.Audio tours are provided for a fee.

The Temple of Literature is divided into 5 parts depicting different aspects of Vietnamese history and education. It is better for educational trips - its very quiet and peaceful.Overall, it's worth a visit if you have a lot of time in Hanoi but its not a must-do. If you're not there to learn about the history, it's a decent place to walk around but thats pretty much it.

Being in education, I expected to enjoy this place far more than I did.  We used the audio guide, which I would recommend because there is little printed information elsewhere.  However, the information is not very interesting and the narrator is monotone.  Locations have up to five sections for each place that is not visually engaging.

This is a quiet and green oasis in Hanoi, but several in our family found this a bit boring. We were on a guided tour of Hanoi that day and during this visit we were explained a lot about the education system in Vietnam. Nice place to see, but not a must in our opinion.
A big compound with students taking pictures for good luck in their studies (as per our guide). It was nice to see the engraved names of the scholars on the turtles back spanning hundred of years.
Good place to discover and explore. Admission fee is 30,000 Dong, probably takes about 45 mins to finish the site seeing.

It's a beautiful place, mainly consisting of several rocks engraved in ancient Vietnamese without much explanation. You walk through several courtyards and can see models of old Hanoi and how it has changed over time.

After reading the reviews, I thought this would something that I would enjoy. There was nothing much to see other than 2 temples situated on the grounds which happened to be packed with people when we were there, as everyone was praying to Confucius to bless their kids in their studies. I thought the grounds of the temple would be nice to explore but it seems the pictures look nicer than the actual place.
A beautiful temple, but that day it was raining and there was an American-style graduation ceremony taking place on the temple grounds, which completely ruined the charm of the place.
The first great university at hanoi. One opportunity to better know the confucianism and the true moral values of the vietnamites

Not much to see except for couple of buildings Vietnamese style (very average at best!) and Steele. There are drums and pots decorating the space (some of which are as new as 2000's). Can spend an hour or so here if you're planning to visit.
Nice spot in the city center. 30k Dong entrance fee. Can be visited on the way to the Ho Chi Long Mausoleum, but not essential.
The setting is certainly nice and the pagodas are well decorated, but you should only go if you happen to be passing by. Not much interest for me.

Very interesting place to visit. Loved the architecture and the man doing calligraphy. Beautiful gardens and across the road is an awesome coffee shop. Easy walk from our accommodation in the Old Quarter.

It was nice to hear the stories and symbolism. Personally, I am more into 'doing' than history, so I found it a bit boring. However, it did provide a lot of insight into the history of Vietnam, and the city and garden were beautiful.
Unfortunately, we visited on a Sunday, when several events were underway, so it was extremely crowded. Moreover, we were prevented from entering by heavy rain.
If you're in Hanoi, you can't miss this place, however, they shouldn't charge an entrance fee for Vietnamese people over 65.

We went as a group with Tang as our guide. It was interesting from a cultural perspective because of the guide's explanations, but from a purely historical standpoint, I didn't find it very significant. We even rushed through it at the end because it started to rain—lightly at first, but then quite heavily.
This is not only a tourist spot but also the temple- place for praying, studying...so that it should be quiet. Stop using the very loudly speaker ...

This doesn’t take that long to walk through - 50 minutes or so and some of the water features were not looking that great when I was there, which may have been season related. The courtyards and pavilions are of course very reminiscent to what you see in China and are tastefully laid out. The main interest here is the link with Confucius and his teachings with lots of displays and things to read about him. The grand interiors and displays are truly beautiful and there is a lot of attention paid to them. One had a small musical group playing traditional instruments and was very relaxing. Plus the usual gift and snack stops.
Didn't get audio tour so possibly missed out on interesting information. Nonetheless a beautiful old area of architecture.
Seems boring if not for those graduates taking their grad photos. Just statues of Confucius and other gods. Graduating students liven up the compound on weekends.
Between my first visit in 2002 and this year, I was particularly disappointed. The calm and charm of this temple of literature are gone. Not everything is done to showcase it.
If you have seen other pagodas or temples then this won’t be exciting. It dates to 1050 AD but most is rebuilt.

This place provides a nice respite from the bustle of the city. It has a walkway leading up to the temple, flanked on both sides by greeneries. If you have seen other temples, this will not be very different but the place provides a good break from the hustle and bustle of the city.
Nothing spectacular. If you are nearby, it might be interesting, but not worth going out of your way for...
Bit disappointed , the courtyards are just that = courtyards.  The Confuscioun temple is dedicated to early followers and teachers after Confusciu.s

If you haven't been to many temples before you might like this place but it is nothing special. It was pretty crowded when we were there, mainly with students getting their group photos done before the exams.Given the choice between this or Train street I would choose Train street every time. If you are heading down to Hue you will see much better temples.

this was something different to see, but it wasn't anything special.  The highlight for me was seeing all the school children that go there for day trips.  Their traditional Vietnamese clothing was beautiful!
The temple of literature feels like a Temple for Confusions where you see the status of Confusions and some of its teaching. Not my favourite on this visit.
The influence of the Chinese is everywhere, great respect is due for the history.  It’s not a place for lingering.

Currently used by students to celebrate their graduation. When we were there, the place was decorated to celebrate Poetry festival. There are few buildings, it is mainly a nice garden with a pool. Not an essential visit if you are short of time.
This place is beautiful... there's clearly no place for impression seekers here.

It is a beautiful temple with lots of different items to see within it.  It might be a good idea to get the headset.  We had a guide, who wanted to describe everything in detail.  We were definitely burnt out by the time we left.

We were here and walked around and looked at a piece of Vietnam's history. Quite big but you quickly go through everything. The history goes back to around the 1000th century, there are many figures to photograph. Once you have seen this it is walking distance to the famous prison.
This was Vietnam's first university, constructed in 11th century. It will be of intrest if you are knowledgeable and interested in Confucius.

Students visit the temples to pray for good grades. The day we visited they were hosting a festival of poetry in the grounds with poets from all over the world showcasing their work. The grounds were beautifully decorated for the lunar new year. Unless you are particularly interested in Confucianism you can give this attraction a miss if you are short on time.
I had high expectations for this place, and perhaps because of that, it was a bit of a disappointment. It dates back to the 11th century and was Vietnam's first university.
Although there is a lot of history here we found it very boring, there are so many of these types of places in Asia.
A very interesting piece of Vietnamese history and well worth the small entrance fee. Probably don't need more than an hour to walk around.

This is an interesting place in Hanoi. I cannot say this is a must visit place. If you have time yes. The Confucious Temple itself is  small. Well maintained by the relevant authoraties and kept clean. If your time in Hanoi is limited there are more interesting places like museums by visiting which you learn a  lot about Vietnam and its people.
You can visit this place as a touristy attraction.Nothing much in this place, except Confucius.Not worth visiting if you are familiar with Chinese literature, history, and Confucius teachings.

The Temple of literature is a rather strange tourist attraction. Although the buildings are quite interesting, there is nothing particularly outstanding about them and it seems that an aura of mystery has been built up around this site to encourage visitors.To be honest, I found the site rather uninteresting and apart from some very nice bonsai trees, I very quickly became bored
If you are interested in history, it might be a good place to visit. It tells you the history of the scholar and the 3 kings that have rule Vietnam.

This visit was not for us. A rather complicated explanation failed to convince us. Did we understand that whoever passed the exams here became a mandarin because this position offered opportunities for an important role? We were not impressed, but that is a personal opinion.
Understood more about the historical background of Vietnam and if you plan to enter, its better to bring a guide to know where you're going!
Good for those who likes old building and understand Vietnam history. Good for spending some relaxing moment there.
Enjoyed seeing this, but it was not a place that could take a lot of time to thoroughly visit.  Glad that we went once, wouldn't go back again.

The space is well maintained and a quiet haven from the noise of the city. The pounds at the beginning are beautiful and the last building is truly stunning. We passed up the audio guide and soon realised the error as there was limited English information. Nice visit all in all.

The Guozijian, a shrine honoring Confucius and the equivalent of a university in the past. Vietnam, with its high educational fervor and reverence for doctorates, is similar to us. This seems to be due to Chinese influence.

I visited this place during a day-long walk around Hanoi. It seemed to be a popular spot for Western tourists. It felt like a quiet place within Hanoi. The presence of a Confucius shrine made me realize that there must have been a time when China ruled Vietnam.
This tour gives you a great feel of the influence of particularly China and to a lesser degree France has had in shaping Vietnam’s cultural and religious history.

We went because there weren't as many tourist attractions in Hanoi as we expected, but I don't think it's really necessary to go. The entrance fee was 30,000 VND, so for three people it was 90,000 VND. When we paid 100,000 VND, they gave us change in the form of 10 1,000 VND bills (50 KRW) that even they hardly use.
Beautiful gardens, shame on the lack of English to explain the surroundings.  They have but not enough to cater for international visitors.

We stopped off here today actually with our tour guide and I would have to say this way my favorite of the temples we visited .Lovely gardens which serve to join the separate temple complex’s . Have to pay to get in but as the guide did this I’m not sure how much this was .

We were told it was a must-see, it is not .. like much of older historical value in Vietnam, it is there, but not so much conserved or explained well. It is pleasant, peaceful and has some interesting artifacts, but nothing exists in a context where you can understand it if you come with little knowledge. I stupidly expected it to be a respository of some sort of literature. Oddly it is, but of nothing of any sense that we can understand, it is more that records were made in stone of who (only the powerful) lived when and whom they relied upon. At least that is my understanding .. nothing about literature as we know it. Still as a remnant of the past it exists and may be of interest to some.
All the students come here seeking divine help for their upcoming exams. Worth a visit for a short stay.

If you have travelled over Asia, you can skip this one, Place is very small and nothing phenomenal as compared to structures you see in Japan, China, Korea... This entire place can be covered within 10-15 min.
It's not a particularly charming place, neither good nor bad. It's still interesting to take a short stroll, but don't expect much.
The Temple of Literature was a popular destination for the Vietnamese, especially for school groups. We wandered around here, but didn't get much out of it.

Consists with traditional complex of buildings the Temple of Literature & National University tells us the story of history of educational  level of Vietnam.Also the effectiveness of the Buddhism and Buddhist monks that mixed with traditions tells lots of things. Confuses teachings got priority during the latter period and there is a beautiful separate chamber to pay homage to honorable  Confuses.

Interesting temple and worth a stop. Home of Hanoi's fisrt University. Going with a group was very informative but they also had good signage in Vietnamese, French and English. You can see the Chinese influence on Vietnam culture and architecture

This site is interesting to look at and visit but isn’t particularly well explained or captioned throughout the complex. If you go, read up on the area before hand or have a student/guide accompany you if you want to understand the detailed history.

Very interesting. Lots of buildings still standing and legible write ups explain what you are looking at or where you are standing. They even got a room with portraits of the vice chancellors from centuries ago. Overall gives the sense of a temple rather than a university.
I'm not lying, you can finish the tour in 30 minutes. There really isn't much to see other than the fish and the park. Go if you have time, but if not, you don't have to go.
The audio guide and entrance fee were 80,000 VND haha. It's a bit tough if you go when it's hot haha. Locals were all dressed up taking graduation or wedding photos haha. It's just worth a visit.
Too many people, too much noise...all the students were there for their graduation photo.

During the Qing Dynasty, Vietnam produced a scholar who passed the imperial examination. To commemorate this rare scholar, who is also considered Vietnam's revered teacher, this temple was built. Many students visit the temple before their major examinations to pray for success in their careers. The temple enshrines both the Vietnamese and Chinese versions of Confucius, and the inscriptions throughout are in Chinese characters. Presumably, Vietnam used to use Chinese, but no longer does so because the Vietnamese find it too difficult.
I went as it was part of the itinerary. Took some nice pics, looked around and waited for my tour group and we left.
An interesting visit from a literary and historical perspective. Beautiful courtyards and buildings. Magnificent Buddha statues. Worth seeing.
Meh, this visit isn't essential; the buildings aren't that extraordinary, and there are quite a few shops. It's worth the detour for its bonsai trees.
A rather nice complex with various temples. Worth a visit for 30,000 Dong (a little over €1).
Average not very exciting but good to see the architecture was pretty good. Be prepared to walk a bit. The Guide was good at explaining. English speaking guides are always good to have at thie time.

as it is clear from the name of this temple it is filled in with many symbolic statues and architecture, you would be amazed by Turtle Steles with the names of those successful at the royal exams. i think this temple really worth to visit

It was a very pleasant walk in the morning. It was a place of peace and quiet away from the hustle and noise of busy Hanoi streets. Our guide told us the history of the place as he walked us through. I am quite impressed how much emphasis Vietnamese leaders made out of study to improve their country.

The prototype of Hanoi's city logo is a place of worship for Confucius. The courtyard has many ancient trees and a temple of literature dedicated to Confucius and his disciples. Overall, it has a strong ancient Chinese character, and students can pray for good grades!
If you free, when you in Hanoi, you may visit Per head entrance fee is 30K VND. Easy to come hear by a  taxi Anyway peaceful place

On our tour in Spanish, the guide took us to this temple, which holds great historical significance for the Vietnamese, as it was their first place of learning. Without a guide's explanations, you'd simply wander around without it capturing your attention. But knowing its history and importance to them, you'll understand why a visit is a must.

Visited the complex on my day off. Some nice architecture consisting of s mix of older buildings and reconstructions. The audio guide and signs did not do a very good job of giving a sense of what the place was and how it functioned.
Not bad, but not much to see. Beautiful old trees, a decent temple, and a nice little lake, but the souvenir shops and crowds spoil the visit.
Worth a visit only if you have a lot of time...there are much better places in the city. What I appreciated most was that the bar inside served espresso coffee.
Frankly, I expected more from this temple, given its fame, and yet it's just another temple... although it is big!
Nothing too special as a temple.. perhaps with a tour guide it might be more meaningful for visitors
Built in honor of Confucius in the 11th century, it later became a university. It's one of the few monumental landmarks to see in Hanoi. It's featured on the 100,000 dong banknote.

You expect a peaceful place... Well, no. Huge crowds of tourists with guides shouting into microphones. In short, it's pretty but not at all relaxing. P.S.: The French audio guide is incredibly boring.

This is a fairly well-known historical and cultural site in Hanoi, which I learned a lot about from books when I was a student. This time, I had the opportunity to visit with friends. The location is convenient for transportation, but because Hanoi's streets are very crowded, there is no parking available, making the visit somewhat inconvenient. The services offered are average, nothing particularly special or attractive.

I was really looking forward to this sight, even sprang for the audio tour, & was disappointed. The audio tour was boring & I'm actually usually a big fan of those sorts of things. The temples & altars were beautiful of course so it might be worth checking out just for that.

Came here as it’s one of the stops on the hop on hop off bus.I paid for the audio headset which gave lots of interesting information but there’s also written information. It’s not a very big place so you’ll need 25-35 minutes to visit.The garden is pretty.
Hanoi is overflowing with temples and pagodas. They are all impressive, but eventually they all start to look the same.

We decided to walk from the Old quarter to this and it was pretty underwhelming overall, but very beautiful gardens and pools. Google maps seemed to direct us to a nearby street rather than to the temple itself but it's pretty easy to spot.

Operating as both a Temple and Vietnam's first university then this is a must visit for all tourists that are coming to see the city. Located near the Old Quarter and in the centre of the city then this is somewhere that you should come and see. First built as a temple dedicated to Confucius in 1070 before opening as Vietnam's first university in 1076, this temple features an abundance of history, however, there is a lack of information telling you this at the attraction. If you don't have a good tour guide then this temple will just feel like a nice walk through a park that you have to pay for and a bit of a waste of time which means ensuring you have a guide that speaks a language well that you understand. Expect at busy times that the queue may be quite long in order to get tickets to try and arrive early in the morning or in the evening when the majority of tourists have gone back to their accommodation.

A pleasant museum with beautiful pieces and faithful reconstructions of scenes of daily life, crafts, and dwellings from the provinces. Much less surprising if you've had the pleasure of visiting the provinces!
This is an old place, but there is no renovation or maintenance; there are many services that disturb tourists.

We were keen to visit because of the history and significance of this place but were disappointed with the lack of information on signs. Would have been nice if there were some sort of exhibits around or a more immersive exhibit. We have seen many temple complexes in Vietnam and this one didn’t distinguish too much in terms of immersing visitors into its different significance.Worth a visit but hopefully they do something in the future to improve overall visitor experience.

It's lovely, especially if you have the explanations that go with it: the symbols on the roofs, the types of buildings, the gardens, the stelae, the competitions! However, it's nothing literary, more like a temple of knowledge! You won't find any weapons or the Temple of Confucius there. Worth seeing. At the back, there's a little vendor selling bracelets for luck, intelligence, happiness, prosperity... nice little souvenirs to take home if you don't want to fill up your suitcases.

There's nothing wrong with this site and the entrance fee is fair, but having seen a lot of shrines, temples and ruins in Vietnam, this one felt somehow lacking. The gardens are pleasant and well-kept but there is little here you cannot see for free or a lower price both elsewhere in Hanoi and across Vietnam. If you're passing and have nothing better to do it's worth a look, but I wouldn't make a special trip unless you've got other plans in the area.

For us, the visit there was unnecessary. We have reached saturation with temples and pagodas. It repeats itself. There is also a pagoda there for inspiring figures in the field of education and enlightenment.
An interesting place where the university and culture of this wonderful country were born.

It's similar to a traditional Chinese temple inside; the area isn't large, and you can walk through it quickly. Since it's quite far from the old town, it's worth a visit if you have time, but not worth the trip if you're really looking for something special.

If you're passing by, it's worth a quick walk around as it has some pretty greenery and interesting structures (unless it's raining, the whole place is outdoors). Wouldn't really recommend the audioguide as it was a little slow and dull.

It’s a nice place to stroll around. I’d suggest getting an audio guide as you miss out on a lot by just walking around. The temple itself is beautiful, although we have seen more amazing temples in Vietnam, that you didn’t have to pay for. All in all it’s good for an afternoon, when you don’t know what else to do. If you have seen temples in Cambodia or Thailand before, this one will be very disappointing.

My free guide took me around this strange combination of historical temple and university. Almost 1000 years old, quite impressive condition and history. My guide told me that they mainly was teaching literature, math and ho to make poems

The Temple of Literature is one of the highlights of Hanoi's tourism circuit, and perhaps if you are just in Hanoi it delivers on that promise.  If you are travelling more widely you will find yourself in far more interesting and/or beautiful historic sites.  Perfectly pleasant to visit, but don't expect anything too amazing: it may be ancient and may have a fascinating history, but not much of that is apparent to the visitor.

The park, the setting and the buildings are pretty. Information is very scarce if you do it individually. Very disappointing to read that some of the buildings have actually been built in the 1990s and are not even exact replicas/ restorations, but rather just new buildings made look old and from the time of when the temple was the first university. Go if you need some "green park vibe" and have spare time. Don't go if you have too much on your agenda already. (It's also paked with tourists!)

This is a well-maintained historical site, once dedicated to Confucius Study. Built in 1070, this place hosted the Imperial Academy, which was considered the first national university in Vietnam.  We participated in the self-guided audio tour and found that it primarily focused on the architecture, its significance; and how the building and ground were used back in the days. We enjoyed the beautiful, traditional grounds but felt the guided audio tour could be less drawn out.

With virtually no signage what-so-ever this is only worth going to if you have a tour guide with a wealth of knowledge or you have done ample amounts or research with photos to explain all the historical and traditional points.

We stopped by with a guide. We were told it was a must see destination. I don’t think we would have got as much out of it without a guide, but I think you can also hire an audio tour. It was interesting to hear the history, and probably one of the better tourist sights we visited in Hanoi.

Even though I went to the Temple of Literature, Vietnam's first university, there were a huge number of people early in the morning. Also, they provided audio services in about 10 languages, so it was good for listening to the explanations.

My spouse and I are American university professors who teach in the Humanities, so needless to say that the Temple of Literature was very high on our list of "things to do" in Hanoi. I hate to be "that one dissenting voice" but this was one of the most anti-climactic sites we saw in all of Vietnam. Really. It was fine, but kind of small, kind of dull, and it did not have great signage or a good audioguide, nor could one explore much. We got to the fifth courtyard, and I was like, "Wait, that's it? That's the Temple of Literature?"It may not have helped that we had just come two days earlier from Hue's Imperial Citadel and many tombs, which are gorgeous, but mainly I did not see the big draw. It was interesting to see the Confucian contributions to Vietnamese thinking as well as to see these early educational spaces (although there were few buildings, and the ones that were there were largely gates of the kind that one finds at most temples still, not all that remarkable, in other words). A man doing calligraphy was the highlight for us. But a gift shop selling ancient books of Confucian authors might have added something, and the last space inside was plain old tacky, with Confucius statues surrounded by beer cans for the afterlife and some silly things like Custas. Does Confucius really want Custas and cheap beer in the afterlife?Did I really trek halfway across the city to see this place?Unless you are right in area, I'm going to be "that person" who says to not bother. And if I'm right, you can thank me later. And if I'm wrong, I'm truly happy you got so much more out of it than I did! I noticed it was well-loved, for example, by vast tour groups and school kids. And also, it has some old trees and turtle statues that were the short highlight here, but still, not enough to recommend. Three stars for at least being a.) a university and b.) old.
Ok place to visit, nothing special if you've been to China. But quite well maintained. Huge place and clean

When visiting the Temple of Literature you should expect to be able to see the entire site in less than an hour.  When buying your tickets this place seems like it is in need of funding as things like brochures(free at most attractions) have an additional charge here.  The amount of space taken up by the gift shop area is also disproportionate when compared to nearby attractions.  It is fairly quiet and has some nice places to sit in the shade.

Other than people watching there is very little to see a couple of nicely decorated golden Buddhas, but no sign of literature. If not for the little Vietnamese Denis the menace in traditional costume the trip would have been a bore... No traces of the oldest university in a series of nested courtyards.
I felt like I had done nothing but eat and shop during my stay in Hanoi, so I decided to visit at least one historic site on my last day. It
There was a long queue of students on the day we went. We almost skipped it.It's a pretty nice place and feels full of history.

We visited on an afternoon,when it was less crowded and more quiet.The lush gardens are very pretty to look at.A lot of the architecture is average.The inner sanctum contains an altar for Confucius and his disciples.The best thing is that there are descriptions in English too.
OK. On the slightly tattered side of things but ok for killing some time and learning about a very interesting part of the ancient history of Vietnam.
It's really cheap to enter so it's difficult to get too disappointed but there is very little to see in here. The dress code isn't upheld here.
Nice place and very informative lots to see and do. Make sure you have a travel guide to explain it all
It's a temple; in Vietnam, they're popular to visit because there aren't many. It's good with an audio guide, and both, along with the entrance fee, are inexpensive.

This is a series of structures that formed the first university in Vietnam. We visited on a day when local high school graduates had gathered at the temple to celebrate their recent graduation - very cool to see the enthusiasm of these learners in such a historic site. It seemed as though the non-graduate attendance was pretty sparse.Although there were a few interesting structures and some nice plots of greenery, overall I found the place to be underwhelming. Perhaps we should have sprung for the audio guide to get a greater appreciation of the site. Without a guide, I enjoyed seeing the grounds and the buildings and especially the celebrating students, but I'd have chosen to skip it in favor of other museums and sites if I were visiting for less than three full days.
A learning center dedicated to Confucius; interestingly, the names of the students who completed their studies are written on stone tiles.

This is one of the many spots our group visited.  The Temple of Literature is Vietnam's 1st university originally established to educate royalty.  This site has well preserved buildings & artefacts.  Clean, nice & quiet place to visit.  Students today come here & pray for good marks.  Good photo ops of building architecture & landscaped gardens.  If you don't have a guide, it is recommended to have an audio headset, if available, to help you understand what you are looking at.
30 vnd entrance. I spent 30 min there. Clean toilets and no soap nor paper towels. If it's worth visiting? I am not to sure..It's good for taking a walk in a green area amongst trees:)

We found that, compared to other Museums, there was not so much to see here.  There looked like there was a market opposite the entrance that was very popular with the locals but we didn't stop there.

We were so looking forward to this and we were underwhelmed. The Vietnamese have been at war for 1000 years so they've been too busy to do much landscaping, but more than that we had hoped for more about Confucius.

Traditional Chinese temple complex has been reconstructed. Avoid the tour busses by going there very early or at lunchtime..Why do all the "great teachers" look like Ho Chi Minh ? ... these are not "relics".
This temple is situated right in the city center. It was built between 1070 and 1076 and served as Vietnam's first university.

If you have been traveling for a while in Asia and you have been some tempels before, this is not a very special one. Its very touristy, so you might skip this one if youre planning to visit other tempels.
The temple and grounds are fine.  Nothing too spectacular here and not near anything except the Hanoi Hilton.  It was ok - there are much better temples around Hanoi.
If you are interest with Vietnamese history of eduction, you could go . It’s a small compound 30 walk in enough.
Simple and not too special.Alot of student taking graduation photo there. 2 souvenir shop.Suitable for student praying for success in examination.

It's literally a temple dedicated to education so mainly about the teachers and students that attended the temple. If you're into that, great but if you're on a budget then don't feel bad skipping it!

Affordable tickets for a view of the temple which is segmented into 5 areas.We happened to visit when there were tons of school kids that were brought for an excursion. Their presence helped to create an academic atmosphere otherwise would be a very quiet place without much entertainment value. Pay more for an alternative language audio to understand more of the place.Since it is a Confucius temple, we prayed for good academic results nevertheless.

It's a large temple complex dedicated to Confucius. It's of great importance to the Vietnamese, and the stone stelae in the rear are a UNESCO World Heritage Site. However, in terms of architecture or grandeur, it's nothing particularly special.

The building complex itself is beautiful, with elements of traditional architecture. To enjoy the temple itself, you need a guide to help explain what you're looking at and the temples true purpose and meaning. There are no detailed placs inside written in English to help guide you, so the temples significance is lost on you. I went once without a guide and once with and of course I left with so much more knowledge on the temple and Vietnamese culture with the guide. The guide was part of a tour, I don't believe there are tour guides at the temple.

A must-see in Hanoi. Well-maintained. The only downside is its popularity with tourists. Too many tourists—I'd say hordes—come to see the monuments and stelae of the past. More explanatory signs would be helpful for history buffs.
Pretty temple, but to get more than minimal information you need a guide which is expensive. Serene until you get to the end and then there are loads of school children.
A pleasant visit to the temple dedicated to Confucius, with beautiful statues inside and a water fountain – a nice stop before exploring the old quarter.

...way overcrowded. Interesting, to be sure, but not quite worth the effort. Confucius would be pleased but didn't see nor feel there were a lot of Confucian followers. No rush to see especially on a hot day with arrogant, aggressive nouveau riche Chinese pushing their way.
I often found it understandable when I heard that they used Chinese characters. Vietnamese sounds similar to Chinese, and many of the people I encountered had Chinese-like mannerisms.
Enjoyed the history but not as wonderful as many other Temples and Pagodas visited. Might be more enjoyable if seen early in trip.

Beautiful gardens and is really cool looking back in time with the old stelae that are available to see. Unfortunately this place is packed with tourists and unless you really want to see it not sure if it is worth the 60,000 VND to get in. All depending what you are looking for

Beautiful temple complex with lovely gardens and an ancient history. The arches are beautiful and there is enough information to understand what everything is. It wasn't very crowded despite the nice weather, and because of this, it really felt like an oasis amidst the busy streets surrounding it.
There were explanations in English and Vietnamese, but I stopped reading them halfway through. Inside, there was a pond and stone sculptures. I think it takes about 30 minutes.

If you can read Mandarin you can understand the full meaning of this place of the story, if not you feel it's just a park. Very surprised to see that the Confucius message around the door were partially hidden by 2 statues left and right. Even if the politic now do not respect the great philosopher Confucius, a bit disappointing by that. The place is to be visit in one hour about, entrance is 30 000 VND.

We visited this place & learnt of the history through HanoiKids tour. Interesting but not overwhelmed by this attraction. Felt that there are  better & more worthwhile tourist attractions to visit, for the same (if not less) entrance fee.

The Temple itself is a modern construction based on an old historic Vietnamese cultural site. Pleasant enough to visit and stroll through whilst enjoy the setting. However, if you are not a student of Confucianism or have any interest in this, it's not that interesting. Not what I expected based on other reviews. Luckily I left and from the back road there was a small market with activities related to the Spring festival and New Year (Toi?). By a 'lake'.This ended up being a highlight of my journey and I was quite the mini celebrity as not many foreigners had ventured here.
This temple really immerses you in the atmosphere of the country.

Worth a visit to see, but I can appreciate that it isn’t such a rich experience if you don’t spring for the audio tour (or a guide) to give you the background on what each location is. It’s just a little too bad that the audio tour almost tripled the cost of entry.Ticket entrance: 30,000dngAudio tour: 50,000dng

My expectations of the Temple of literature was one of a beautiful, peaceful area in the middle of a chaotic city. Unfortunately the reality was far different. It was anything but relaxing when we went, in fact extremely busy! We wandered around but as it was so busy it was hard to take in the feel of the place. There was celebrations going on when we were there which was nice. It is only 30 Dong each to get in.

It was very crowded, partly due to the Chin New Year celebrations, but the many worshippers in their colorful Sunday clothes made a magnificent sight. Definitely worth a visit, especially since it's so centrally located!

The Temple of Literature complex is located on Temple of Literature Street in Hanoi. It's not very large, but it attracts many tour groups. Its full name is "Temple of Literature - Imperial College". The temple grounds have many trees and a large pond. If you have time, it's worth a visit, but not going won't be a big loss.

To be honest we weren't overwhelmed by this location. The local students will have more of a connection with the location, they come here to pray for luck with their exams. Historically important as a learning centre, it is a popular spot for tourists. We were shown around it by a couple of university students, they thought students coming and getting their photos taken was tacky as only the rich can afford such luxuries (an interesting perspective).
A beautiful area, with plenty of opportunity to snap some great photos of the decorative buildings and decorations.

It has several temples. Children enter free. They offer paid audio guides and tours. It's a bit far from the Old Quarter, about a 30-minute walk through Hanoi's chaotic streets. We went with my 7-year-old son and he enjoyed it. I would recommend it if you like and are interested in temples.

Loads of students were there in gowns and mortar boards and young ladies in An Do which added to the atmosphere.
