Bizarrely on two separate occasions  young ladies asked to take a selfie with me - not sure why they wanted their photo taken with a white female stranger but I was happy to oblige!I wouldn't say this is a must see place to see in Hanoi.

This place is like a little oasis - quiet and tranquil away from the traffic. The audio guide explains the history of the buildings and and the ideas behind the layout and design.  It is worth purchasing the audio guide! There isn't a lot to see as such but it is worth a visit, especially as it is an easy walk from the Old Quarter and it has longer opening hours than other sites.

The Temple of Literature is actually a series of courtyards separated by carved doorways leading to a two-story building. Entrance fee: 30,000 dong. Open every day (even Sunday, despite what the guidebook says). The place is beautiful and serene. One major drawback: the French audio guide, priced at 50,000 dong, is of very little interest, and the woman you hear reading stumbles over her words!
It would seem that this building is a must-see when visiting Hanoi. The site is indeed pretty, but not very old (19th century). Interesting, but not exceptional!
The best monument of Hanoi just being average might say a lot about the tourist monuments of the city.

Vietnamese scholars studying for government jobs a thousand years ago stayed and hit the books here. It's a nice enough spot, with pleasant gardens, and if you can read a bit of Chinese characters it's kind of neat to see the stone tablets in which every graduate throughout history have been inscribed, but that's about it.The architecture is completely of the standard classical style you'd see throughout all Chinese influenced areas. If you haven't seen that kind of thing before, it's great! If you have, well, no surprises here.Oddly, the gift shops don't sell much in the way of academic supplies (notebooks and such) which would've been a nice themed souvenir, especially if they could actually come up with some pleasant designs. As far as literature goes, they do have some books at the gift shops, but they're incredibly random. I spied Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas, for one.Final note: Technically the standard "temple" dress code applies here, but they seemed pretty relaxed in enforcement.
Came here alone without a guide so I didn’t really understand what most things symbolised. Beautiful architecture and interesting to see all the students posing for graduation photos though.
Spent a relaxing hour here away from the bustle of Hanoi. Interesting tortoise statues and watching Vietnamese people coming to pray for exams and university places.

One of Hanoi's monuments that bears witness to a bygone era. Perhaps it's the guide who "did the job" with the bare minimum, but there wasn't much to sink your teeth into... that said, it's harmonious, well-preserved, and from my perspective, there isn't much literature. If you're expecting to find a collection of works from various periods, with an understanding of the evolution of Vietnamese literature, you'll be surprised ;-)
It would be nice for a very serious history buff. It seemed like the market area was the most popular portion of the tour. Most people ended up congregating there.
A pleasant and quick visit to make after seeing the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum!
Good. It provides an opportunity to learn more about Vietnamese education during the time of the emperors.
Worth a visit if you have time. We were disappointed with the lack of information as to the history and purpose of the Temple. It was 30,000 each to get in.

Nice architecture and helpful signs that indicate the purpose and style of each section. Quite cheap, but it doesn’t take long to walk through it. During off peak it might be nice to sit and stay for longer.

I went mainly to see the topiary I had read about. Those turned out to be across the street and were either poorly sculpted or too immature to determine which of the zodiac signs they represented.  The temple grounds however were quite lovely.  The exterior architecture was interesting and pleasing.  There wasn’t much to the interiors.  We enjoyed the serenity for the 45 minutes we spent there.
A bit too touristy for my taste: I'm not saying I wasn't expecting it, but it lacks solemnity...

Interesting to visit, as it is the site of the first university in Hanoi. It is a series of old open buildings, exhibiting the architecture of the time. A visit to the Temple of Literature can easily be combined with a visit to the Fine Arts Museum, which is virtually across the road.
The area is nice to visit and includes some of the most interesting temples from Vietnam. The visit does not take too long and you can buy cheap tickets directly there.
A pleasant outdoor walk; at the far end is a two-story building with several statues and offerings. Inexpensive, $1. Closes at 6 pm.

A beautiful and well-maintained temple... very cheap entrance fee, worthwhile if you're visiting several places in Hanoi. If you've already been to other temples and don't have time, from my point of view it's not essential.

Dropped by to see the temples which were interesting although partly obscured as some elements are undergoing redevelopment. Very busy site and limited explanation if you do not have a guide. Worth a visit through if you have a couple of hours free in Hanoi.
The building complex is described as the first university of Vietnam.
Very pretty but not too much to walk around. I would recommend no more than an hour here to see all the old buildings and statues

We came because we heard it was a hsitorical university & temple. Bit of a museum and temple. Out whole group were more satisfied using the toilet facilities here. LOLGood to see local graduating students taking photos in local dress as well as wearing their graduation gown.

This was a quiet, peaceful area. But there's not really much to engage with. There's old tablets that you can't read, and a temple with the standard (but cool) alters to look at. The best thing about this area was it's fairly expansive grounds and greenspace that you could stroll through for a reprieve from the city.

This is an old university and has a long history. You can see the entire place in an hour.Other than at the entrance which explains the history of the place, there is no guide or tour for this place which would explain anything else about the place. Perhaps other than it being an old university, there is nothing else to it.

The events taking place added to the temple as it gave an idea of students at work or at study.  A favourite place for university graduates having group photos for their graduation ceremony, nice to see the styles and continuance of culture.  The temple itself is remarkable for its age and worth spending time looking around.

It's said to be a place that brings good fortune in academics, so there were many Vietnamese people there as well as tourists. The statue of Confucius was sparkling and vibrant. Apparently, many students visit during exam season to pray for success, which made me realize that Japan and Vietnam are the same in that respect.

As I have visited hundreds of different kinds of temples in my life, the temple of literature is an average  temple in my standard. Plus the square pond in the middle is under maintenance and tall boards are installed around it, you can skip a visit if you don’t have much time.

Interesting place to visit. You don't need a tour guide and you could possibly see and read it all under 1hour. To study is very important to the Vietnamese and thru are very proud of this temple of literature & national university.
I found this fairly boring so didn't stay long cheap enough and in. the area of other attractions so worth a look only 40000 dong

Another must see attraction when in Hanoi. Need to pay entrance fee. The complex consists of 5 courtyards and each represents an interesting piece of history. Parents and their kids come here to offer prayers to Confucius and rub the head of the stone tortoise structures for luck before an exam. There is a statue of Confucius and his disciples in the inner courtyard where d devotees can pray to. Overall an interesting place.

Although there was construction going on, we were able to visit without any problems. The turtle's head, which is said to bring good luck, was indeed bald from being stroked too much, just as the guidebook had written.

A historic and peaceful place in the middle of the center of Hanoi. A fairly large complex with a number of courtyards and pavilions. Well organized visit and visually beautiful place. The temple with Confucius and four of his disciples was a great sight. Some renovation when we were there so we didn't get to see everything and the peace and tranquility the temple offers.

The Temple of Literature is right next to the National Gallery and can be briefly visited. Entrance fees to attractions in Hanoi are inexpensive. If you're short on time, there's no need to go in; you can't get many good photos from the higher vantage points.

The most interesting part of this attraction is the fact that it is ancient, dates back to 1070 AD, and is still standing!It's a small place with five small courtyards, each with its own little significance. The gates for each of these are old (and yet maintained) and typically Vietnamese in their architecture. Lots of bonsais in one of the courtyards. That's about all!Dr Bhupesh GoyalIndia

The Temple of Literature is a lovely place to visit if you have an hour or so to kill.  It was full of tourists during my visit and photos that simply show pagodas with no people in sight are probably unrealistic.  There are some gift shops that sold relatively good quality souvenirs.  A huge bonus for us was the nice restaurants within the vicinity of the Temple.  It is also about a 15 minute walk from the Mausoleum so may fit in nicely with that trip also.

An old school founded by Confucius. The most interesting aspects are its history and the layout of the inner courtyards. The last building, where the statues of the kings are located, is the most beautiful.

The temple is a symbol of the city and it deserves a visit. For those used to Mandarin architecture, it will surely ring a bell. However the visit is quite quick, the place is crowded and some parts are currently not visible.
Finally, it's quiet here, and you're highly unlikely to get run over by a motorcyclist, which is quite a feat for Hanoi. Cool students!
An interesting temple showcasing the traditional education in Vietnam. However, seems more of a ceremonial place recording past academics.

A temple built to honor Confucius, with several courtyards containing various Chinese structures. It was Vietnam's first university, where mandarins were trained. Students' names were inscribed on the stone stelae and turtles in the courtyards.

We visited on a Friday when most museums were closed. There were lots of groups of school children and graduation photographs being taken. It was very atmospheric. The purchased map and information was really helpful but we still feel like we missed something. At the exit, there is a sign to visit another building a hundred metres or something away. It was very close but we couldn't find it. So we returned and asked, again we were stumped. The only building we could see in the direction was a fine arts museum. Missed the statue of Confusious. I think the admin also covered the entrance to the other building? If anyone has visited, would be great to hear which other building it was and if the admin/entrance fee was included. Thank you.

The Temple of Literature is a complex of temples/buildings.  We didn't use a guide.  We paid entrance of 30,000 dong per person and just entered and walked around the complex.  We spent less than an hour here.
Honestly, it's a place you have to visit, but it's worth getting up early and avoiding the crowds.
Unfortunately, my opinion is not at all objective because I have traveled to China twice and there these types of temples are very common and much larger and better preserved.
The temple is quite beautiful and large. The architecture of some of the buildings is worth seeing. Nice, but that's about it.
Very beautiful however needs a tour guide or most English and signs as to limited information is given.

it was a very  interesting  temple and parks  and worth a few hours of your time.Also nice to get away from the maddening crowd of the old quarters   with  motor bike noise. The  area has large gardens  and some nice temples to wonder around and read in english.

The entry fee is 30k dong and if you want an English pamphlet thay explains about the place then you pay extra 8k dong. When I visited there was some construction taking place but even then I was able to see everything in less than 20min. This place is now very popular for student to take graduation pictures. Can be missed if you do not have enough time.

It is an ancient building. After entering, you will first see the Confucian Temple to worship Confucius and his disciples (Zengzi, Yan Hui, etc.). The statues appear to be modern sculptures. Further back, at the Imperial College, you can see the real old statue of Confucius, as well as old official robes and other cultural relics.

A temple in the middle of the city. According to the guide, it's tradition for students to take their vows in this temple, but that sounds like a guide's tall tale. It's worth a visit because it has some interesting buildings.

The first university of Vietnam way back in 1076, it was established within a temple dedicated to Confucius which itself was established in 1070. Graduated students still come here to take pictures before graduation, its kind of a mixture of worship and tradition. Another name to fame for the place is its feathered on the back of 100000 Dong note. I felt the courtyard after courtyard layout was similar to temple and palace complexes of China only smaller in size. Having visited those, this complex felt like a mediocre architecture site. Come here to know and see the special felling youth have with the site

We have to buy a ticket to enter the temple. Its very crowding at weekend. There are a lot of young students who wear suits. They take photos for their graduation. It ruins my mood to visit a place that is supposed to be historic and cultural.

If you only visit one temple, this is a good one, especially with all the history behind it, but we've seen nicer ones at other places.Unfortunately, there are renovations ongoing, so part of the temple was obscured from view when we visited.
It's nothing extraordinary. Temples with a strong Chinese influence. Rebuilt after the war, it served as a teaching center.
Hanoi's First University. You discover the importance they place on education and respect for their teachers, as our guide explained.

This attraction was included in the organized tour. I found the gate unique and beautiful. Naturally, there were many students walking around the grounds, and together with all the visitors, it was pleasantly lively. So, a nice experience, but this attraction, too, was not very spectacular or special compared to other cities in the region; rather, it was somewhat colorless and simple.
It isn't a very large place, and there were even areas under construction.

Part of the temple is under construction, which might explain why it wasn't as impressive or was a bit disappointing. We've visited other temples, and I think this one is very poorly maintained, with dirty floors and very dark interiors. Be careful with your belongings at the entrance; it gets very crowded.

Nice, what can I say? But nothing special. In fact... expectations were so high that a bit of disappointment was felt in the end. The only thing that remains is that it's very interesting to see the customs of Vietnamese graduates (they come to this temple to have their customary photos taken), and you see how "trained" they are in the culture of photography and imagery (especially the girls) and how everyone generally dresses elegantly for the occasion. I was surprised by the complete absence of parents during the graduates' celebration.

It is a nice temple to visit but I have to admit that I was slightly disappointed with the grounds of the temple and partly it is because I have read too many great reviews about this temple before my visit. The actual place looks quite different from what I have seen on the internet. The lotus pond is not very well maintained and it looks dried up. I suggest visiting with a tour guide would make it more interesting.
I didn't get the impression that it was particularly unique. Perhaps because this is a prime location for learning, the procession of graduates in Ao Dai taking photos stood out.

The temple isn't particularly attractive, but it's historically interesting. We went on a day when it was packed with university graduates taking photos, which I suppose is a tradition. That was interesting to see, although a bit annoying after a few minutes.

I thought this might be a little more interesting than it turned out to be.  It was not very unique, in fact compared to many other temples throughout SE Asia this one was quite average.  The day we were there was a graduation period for many students so lots and lots of graduation pictures being taken.  That actually made the visit most interesting.  Otherwise it is standard gardens with average buildings.
A string of Buddhist temples where Hanoians come to have their photos taken. The spectacle is in the courtyard.
It's located a little ways from the old town.
You walk through the temple complex dedicated to Confucius; there are a few not particularly informative panels in English, a guided tour would probably be helpful here.

A must-visit place when traveling to Hanoi, you'll gain a deeper understanding of the history of Vietnamese education during the feudal era. The architecture is well-preserved, beautiful, spacious, and airy. However, on weekends, it gets quite crowded with students, who are noisy, taking photos, shouting, and running around. The restrooms charge a fee (if they want to charge, they should include it in the entrance fee). More activities like book fairs and calligraphy demonstrations by traditional calligraphers should be added to the Garden of the Imperial Academy. The two rows of buildings on either side of courtyards 4 and 5 should be restored and expanded to enhance the museum's appeal.
It was quite nice to stroll through the grounds, but unfortunately relatively unspectacular compared to temples in Bangkok and the like :(

We were expecting a peaceful and quiet stroll. That wasn't the case at all. We found ourselves in the middle of a graduation ceremony. It was incredibly crowded, making it difficult to appreciate the beauty of the garden and grounds.

Lots of historical papers and excaveted equipment. We did not enjoyed it that much, although I can imagine that for a history lover it might be interesting. Not every description was translated to english.
Just popped in for a wander and escape from motorbikes. Well worth it, especially as there are things to learn.

Confucious is one of my favourite philosophers and i was really surprised that his literary museum was so lacking in his work. The grounds and temple are great but the actual information presented there wasn't too impressive.
We went as part of a tour . Didn’t really understand the concept . I wouldn’t recommend.The people visiting where more interesting to watch.
Difficult to get through this venue if you have a walking disability, but still very interesting for all who attend.

Not a lot to see, the grounds are well kept and the structures are similar in nature, well built, Pagoda style (I think is the term), neat and airy. Some good photo ops. Contrary to what might be said, you can't touch the Statues of the Turtles, so look for good luck elsewhere.
A must-see when visiting Hanoi, it is part of its history and worth visiting to understand certain things and influences.
This was an interesting visit and very informative regarding the history.Worderful buildings and well kept.

It's a beautiful place steeped in Buddhist and Confucianism. It's worth visiting with a Vietnamese person to fully understand its significance and get the most out of the experience, as it's an integral part of the country's beliefs and religion.

I felt the Temple itself  was fairly standard stuff but the gardens are great. The day we visited was just before the festival so the grounds were spectacular with lanterns, floral displays, flags and banners.
Actually one of the few sights in Hanoi. Nothing special, but interesting enough for a visit. Well-maintained site located in the middle of the city.
Saturday afternoon tour. Very interesting VN so long a center for learning & excellence. Place packed with fresh young people all anxious to succeed. Well worth a visit!

It was a bit of a shame I couldn't see the pond because it was under construction, but it is a nice place for a casual stroll. There is an admission fee, but I heard it is free if you have an international student ID! Just keep that in mind.
Very busy and crowded. Place where students graduate for their higher degrees. Walk to the very end to see Confuscious memorial.
Well worth a visit although it is not all that stunning. Didn't know much about Confucianism before my visit, left the place a bit more enlightened.
Beautiful temple and nice to have seen. Beautiful literature and interesting to know. If you are there anyway, you might as well visit it for about 20 minutes.
One of the few places in Hanoi which I felt was very average. Could have been better maintained. Can spend may be 20mins so so max here.
I recommend going with a guide or maybe buying the prospectus along with your entrance tickets since there  are close to zero information about the place, buildings and exhibits. Quite a shame.

Since there aren't many monuments to see in this city, I decided to visit this temple, which had come highly recommended. It's really just a collection of small houses in the traditional Chinese style, which have their charm, but that's about it. Nothing inside is worth seeing. It's mostly the buildings themselves and the peace and quiet (in the gardens) in a city as noisy as Hanoi.
Beautiful estate with lovely gardens in the city center. Everything could be a little better maintained, though. The various buildings are also worth seeing.

The grounds are a nice break from the chaos of Hanoi, but the buildings are not unique.  The story about its founding and tha stelae are interesting.  Don't bother paying 8000 D for the brochure.  Also count your change from a larger bill as my friend was shortchanged.
This is a Confucius temple, but surprisingly, there were few Chinese or overseas Chinese visitors, and many Western tourists!
Literature written on stone. Who knew? Interesting place to visit with a nice flower garden. Good place to take pictures.
Really great interesting place but would most definitely recommend going with a guide as would have no idea what we were looking at if it wasn't for him!Really pretty and historical
It would have been better with a guide who could have explained the history of the place. Without a guide, we found it beautiful and interesting, but it wasn't breathtaking.

Good old temple type - it is said to be first vietnamese university.If you are from indian, you had enough temple, so if you are running short on time, better to skip this.Nothing special here - for asian tourist , surely can skip it.

Our title says it all. :) Maybe because it was so blisteringly hot and muggy when we went, but we thought it was just nice as a historic and tourist destination. We think we would've enjoyed it more had there been placards in English describing what we were all seeing. (And we obviously would have gotten more out of our visit had we had a guide, of course.) It was a nice respite out of the bustle of Hanoi, however, and we had a bunch of school children flag us down and practice their English with us, so that was really fun and super cute. So, we would say that if you have some free time in Hanoi and are in the general area, it is worth a visit, but we would not recommend going way out of your way to see it.
If you're traveling to learn more about the place, a guide is recommended to help you understand better

I was expected to see more about Confucius students during the visit to the Temple of Literature. However, it was not much of that. It is more diverted to a temple visit basically. Only a small part of it has display of those old textbooks and clothing of the students.
I liked to visit it, but it's not a highlight for me. Would have prefer to spend more time in the lovely markets or wondering around hanoi.

This was my third visit to the Temple of Literature and once again the staff tried to short change me when purchasing the tickets. Luckily I counted me change back and the lady quickly produced the missing money - not an innocent mistake because the exact same thing has happened previously. So take care of your money when buying tickets. That aside, it's an interesting attraction and well worth visiting in Hanoi
If you go here, I recommend booking a tour. Looking around on your own is entertaining, but it is all about the history.
An interesting visit to a place that, despite the large crowds, conveys peace, something to be grateful for in Hanoi.

An old institute which served the purpose of its time. Though not on the same scale as the Nalanda University or other renowned ancient institutes it nevertheless reflects on the ancient history of Vietnam and the encouragement given to learning on a large campus by the then kings,Emperotrs/rulers.

Many tourists visit this temple because it enshrines Confucius. It has a charming atmosphere, and I think many people come here to pray for academic success. Personally, I preferred the main hall in the back to the turtle monument.
Peaceful place and great for a nice walk. However there was nothing grand about it. We felt we overpaid as well as we were only there for 30 mins.
IT was nice to see when you have some time to spend in Hanoi. Teenagers weten't very enthousiastic about the place though...We went with free city guide so she gave us the info we needed!
Place was clean. It was raining but still there are a lot of tourist. It's very accessible. Clean WC inside.
The grounds aren't big, so you don't need a lot of time.  This is where the first university was in Vietnam.  You can walk around at your own pace and there are some signs translated.

1. Dress code - no restriction2. Environment - quite a nice view. I guess it built in the influence of china. Therefore gt a long way of garden view before reaching the main building 3. Building - very "history" style. Quite nice actually.4. Ticket -30000dong5. Information - partially provided in english only. However, juz to see the statue /building... no need explanation also can if u don mindIts state the education history in 19century... kind of similar system with china.6. A must to visit?If you gt time, can choose it to kill time!

The temple of literature is of historical interest, but if you don't have a guide to tell you it's story, then it is just an old looking building and grounds. There were not many information boards present. I would suggest you either do it as part of a tour or do a bit of research first so you know what you are looking at.

A splendid temple in the middle of a beautiful area. The statue of Confucius is all dominant and provides a central focus point for historians, tourists and most importantly school children. Schools organise trips which flock to the site to take in all its beauty and teachings....should be on everybody's schedule.
Nice, but nothing special. You can see it all very quickly.

The temple and grounds are very well maintained and a popular tourist spot. While we were there the University was holding graduation and there were hundreds of students there with cap and gown taking pictures. It gave us a glimpse into the life of Hanoi residents and their culture. Very interesting and nice to visit.

This historical landmark didn't even evoke any emotions. It's simply another gathering place for locals and tourists, just for show. There are flower shops, pagodas, and a blooming park. That's all for 30,000 dong. You can walk around it in no more than 1-1.5 minutes.

The Temple of Literature is now a very popular tourist attraction. During our visit we shared it with a number of tourist groups of various nationalities. While it is an elegant temple complex, you will have seen more outstanding temples many times if you have travelled broadly in Asia.The temple consists of a few different courtyards which are marked by ornamental gateways. Inside the courtyards you will find lotus ponds, towering shade trees and various stelae and statues. The main prayer hall is fairly modest but it does have some intricate woodcarving on its beams and pillars. Locals still come here to pray and make offerings. Entrance costs a reasonable 30,000 dong.

Another must-see. There's no information at the temple, so read a guidebook beforehand. Honestly, apart from one of those typical giant drums, in my opinion, there isn't much else of interest. The bonsai trees are beautiful.

If I understand correctly, this is Vietnam's first university? Or perhaps its first educational facility. There isn't anything particularly amazing that will make you go "Wow," but I think it's worth visiting if you're in Hanoi anyway. It's not far from Hoan Kiem Lake, after all. However, in my opinion, if you want to stay elsewhere longer or are weighing your options between this and another place, it isn't a must-visit. It just feels a bit like a traditional Korean Hyanggyo... haha.

When you go to Asia, you're bound to see some temples. And it's probably gonna be more than one. This one is a perfectly fine example of a such, but does not offer any more than any other temple in I have been to. To get the most of this visit, read up on the story of the place. It is quite interesting, even though the place is not itself.

Not really sure what exactly one is supposed to see here. Stone turtles with some faded tombstone looking structures over them / Lotus ponds / a small little temple, which to be honest, is nothing spectacular. Most of the info you get about the place depends on your tour guide so its really up to them to make or break this place for you.

The place is nice but not as enriching experience as expected. You can spend about an hour here to get some insight on the history of education in Vietnam, but spending more time would not be worthwhile the effort. Give it a look if you are passing by.
The interest is primarily for Confucian data. The stelae are very damaged and the writing is practically illegible.
The temple and the buildings are worth a visit, young graduates from Hanoi go to this place for souvenir photographs, near the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum

The military museum was closed in the middle of the day, so we took another walk to the Temple of Literature. It didn't cost much to get in, it was just fine to walk through a quiet park surrounded by busy roads. Some English information boards, but it seems there is more info in Vietnamese. A guided tour might have made this more interesting.

lasted here under an hour as nowhere to get respite from heat and humidity. Lots of people. Ideally a guide is needed to tell you what you are looking at as nothing is in english. Kids again free at this museum- adults 30 K each.
Quite fun to do since it doesn't cost too much effort or money, but it's not very special. Fairly busy and you're through it in about fifteen minutes.
You can visit the citadel, mausoleum, one pillar pagoda and flag tower in a half day walking. Beware of hit stroke

The One Pillar Pagoda , and the Temple of Literature is in one place . You may opt to walk to the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum as well as it is about a kilometer away on foot. If you're taking a taxi  , better have your hotel book a taxi for you or take either Grab or Uber. If you opt to flag down a taxi make sure its the green one , I think they call it Mah Lin taxi. We got ripped by a taxi driver we flagged down going back to our hotel . We later learned its one of the problems in Hanoi , tourists getting ripped by taxi drivers . The hotel front desk warned us to take the taxi name and details and ask for a receipt from the driver next time we flag down a taxi , that way it would be easy to identify the driver and file  the complaint.

This is the biggest temple in Hanoi, so if you'd like to see a temple while you're there this is the one to see. Still, in comparison to temples around southeast Asia, this really isn't very impressive. There isn't much to see unless you have a tour guide to explain it to you.

About a 20-minute walk from the Old Quarter, this temple has the distinction of being the country's first university. A great place to relax after the hustle and bustle of Hanoi. One of the many temples you can visit in the country. Recommended if you have time and are looking for a pleasant place.

This is .now a Confucuan place of worship honouring Vietnam's early kings, restored after war damage. Called university because it was where the first mandarin candidates took their exams but that was centuries ago. Next door is a large Confucian temple that seems to be more a monument than active place of worship. The mist interesting aspect of both is the tiling on the roofs and calligraphy (Chinese).

Out of all the museums in Hanoi, this one is rather uninformative (though to be fair it is not completely a museum). We went with a local vietnamese friend who could explain to us the context the temple was built in and some of the statutes, but otherwise info was in vietnamese and it's not easy to grasp why there is a temple of Chinese-design in Hanoi (Vietnam was conquered by China for 1000 years, hence the Confucianist influence). One highlight was a short performance with traditional instruments that took place near the end of the temple.

This was the first university in Hanoi from ancient times. It's relaxing and calm environment almost transports you to those times. Be conscious and avoid taking pictures of the saints. It's rude. Temple closes at 5.

Entry charge was 30K VND (about £1). It was a very hot/humid day and found that walking around the place was quite uncomfortable, most of the rooms were hot n stuff. It was ok to wander around the complex.

A visit to the temple is quite nice for getting a feel for the old student community. The history behind it is certainly interesting, but unfortunately, you don't learn much without a hired guide. You can only wander around, look at the buildings, and find basic information on two information panels. You'll probably only gain deeper insights with a guide. The grounds themselves are quite beautiful, though.

This is a very interesting place. Popular among young Vietnamese. We saw people taking graduate photos. The lotus flowers are beautiful.  This is the start of the flowering season. Can see the long historical root to Chinese.

The history is good too see. We were lucky to have gotten a private guide with private car which made the experience a lot better.  It got boring after a while due to heat and busy day visiting other places. Recommend having a tour guide went to Kodo restaurant next door and saw pictures of former Australian PM when she visited.
The tour was conducted with an English-speaking guide, making it difficult to understand everything.
I loved the place. We had to walk through very busy streets to get there, but it was worth it.I was impressed with the long history of this first Vietnamese University.
If it's your first time traveling to Asia, you might like it, just like the rest of the country,

I'm not really sure what I expected, but this wasn't it.  The whole place was overwhelmed with graduating high school kids taking photos and tourist shops.  The funny thing, is that this is one place that had some halfway decent stuff to buy. There is very little feel of authenticity here......its kinda sad. Besides it being old, there wasn't much to make you feel like you were in a historically important site.
Better things to do in Hanoi, the architecture is interesting but otherwise pretty boring I think. Just have a stroll around the town-- you will see more interesting stuff

We visited the Temple of Literature with a guide. Many exciting stories about the role this place has played for Vietnam and the leadership of the country. Nice historical buildings even though some of them were built after the war. The Confucius Temple is part of it all and the place appears on one of the banknotes.
Visiting this place makes me understand how china confucianism has guided the vietnamese education and literature system during the time of the emperors'.

They gather outstanding students from all over the country, administer exams, and train future bureaucrats. The certificates of success and the paintings of their triumphant return to their hometowns show just how incredible this achievement is. The lotus flowers in the garden were in bloom, and the place was overflowing with people taking pictures.

It's an iconic place to visit in Hanoi but I frankly grew tired of all the temples. After you've seen a couple, you've pretty much seen them all. My best advice: See a few and skip the others. This one is definitely worth seeing, especially if you like history.

This place still preserves the achievements and names of scholars. The scenery retains its ancient charm, but it has become somewhat commercialized; people come only to take pictures and don't stop to fully appreciate the feeling that this place is a source of pride for the scholars of a bygone era, even though each person has their own perspective.

The Temple of Literature is a set of buildings with the distinct Vietnamese style, it can be nice to walk around in and get a tiny glimpse of the purposes it used to serve. It is a very popular place for university graduates to congregate for speeches and photo opportunities, but not a lot going on here.
The place is the oldest university of vietnam and have an interesting history the temple as such is small and non distinct and take half an hour to visit
Very interesting ancient temple. Beautiful gardens. Former university. There are also some pagodas. Highly recommended.
You can walk through it briefly, but I didn't find it particularly impressive. The temple isn't very large, so a quick look is worthwhile. The entrance fee was 30,000.

The Temple of Literature in Hanoi is a typical Chinese-style building, similar to many other temples of literature in China. Most of the existing buildings were constructed during the Ly Dynasty in the 17th century, covering an area of ​​over 26,000 square meters and facing south. The entire complex consists of five courtyards, with a dismounting stele outside the gate and a still pond in the front courtyard. In the center of the main hall hangs a plaque with the four Chinese characters "万世师表" (Teacher of Ten Thousand Generations), inscribed with the words "Kangxi Emperor's calligraphy." Inside the main hall is an altar for Confucius, flanked by shrines to Confucian scholars from both China and Vietnam. The Temple of Literature is famous for its "Jinshi Stele" (Stele for Successful Candidates in the Imperial Examinations), with lifelike stone tortoises carrying these exquisitely carved steles.

The truth is that very little remains of the original structure, which is understandable after so much war. The reconstructed parts are worth a visit, but the interior is entirely filled with shops, which I think detracts somewhat from the experience.

This is the site of Vietnam's oldest university. The tour begins after passing through the gate; the complex stretches long and narrow, reaching far into the distance. You can feel the history in every corner of the building.
I stopped by simply because it is located in front of the National Museum of Fine Arts

This is a quiet place - unless there is a school group with a teacher yelling in the Public Address system!. While there is a bit to see if you explore, it's a little bit of "Not Another Temple". It gets more interesting the further back you go. While the architecture is fantastic, there is not a lot to see.

One of Hanoi's famous attractions, the Temple of Literature boasts an antique exterior, yet it lacks the essence of history. Perhaps because I'm a foreigner, or perhaps because I've lived in Vietnam for a long time and have been deeply influenced by Chinese culture, it felt like a mere copy. The culture and history embodied by the Vietnamese people seemed completely disconnected. That said, it's better suited for a quick, superficial visit.
A peaceful quite garden with a little history and culture- not much to see , very worn out old writing on the doctorate stones

Visited only because we visited the Museum of Fine Arts just across the street.Need a well informed guide or better read up on the internet about this site before visiting.  The compound is large taking a whole block.

Hyped up as a must-see in Hanoi, this spot has a nice grounds in a pleasant & quiet pocket of the city, and some history to it, though not much else.Pros: accessible (15 min. walk from lake), pretty property, the history.Cons: crowds, lacking info./details.A 'maybe' in my opinion.
Stunning architecture and well-maintained. It's a shame that the dozens of school groups constantly visiting are so noisy; it detracts somewhat from the magic of the place.
The Temple of Literature had some nice China-Vietnamese style buildings to see but it is very touristy and gets boring after 10-20 minutes.
This is a nice place to visit, especially if you're a teacher or are interested in confucious.  It's a nice place to walk around and enjoy a leisurely stroll.

A very old university. It's more of a museum now - but when we visited a lot of students were there taking pictures in graduation attire. So apparently the place is used for some of the ceremonial connected to passing their grades. Some of the buildings in the complex are very old, and so are the tablets where the names of those who passed their exams in the past were written. Some of the buildings however are very new. In one of them they have models of the complex showing how it is today and how it used to be.
The visit is okay but there's not much to see; the most interesting part has been destroyed, I think, because there's a fence, so the place is okay, but there's no need to linger.
It's a beautiful temple, like the rest, with Buddha statues and incense everywhere. The day we went, we happened to be at a school graduation, and it was fun to watch.
The temple is fine to go to if you are passing through but there is nothing special about it .It is a nice temple though.
It was interesting to spend an hour as part of a tour to learn about passing knowledge through the years. Would like to have understood more but found some details vague.
It's certainly beautiful, but only average compared to the praise heaped upon it by the guidebooks. Worth seeing, but don't spend more than 30 minutes there.

but this place had little going for it. there wasn't much to see, it was a tourist trap full of souvenirs (which actually a few were kind of cool and i purchased a few things) but it wasn't worth the money or the walk.  The pagoda in the middle of the lake is free and a much better photo opp.
One of the must see places in Hanoi. It is a peaceful and relaxing place with a history to tell.....
Quiet and peaceful place in the middle of the city. nothing much to do there - you can spend some quiet time, Did not see the part behind the temple, as not clearly marked

As part of the overall Hanoi experience this is worth visiting, if only to see the many graduating students coming here to have photos taken.If you are on limited time visit the old quarter and the mausoleum
We went to see this university. Ok it is peaceful in the middle of noisy hanoi. And has some scenes for beautiful pictures. Thats all..
This place is nice when it's empty of tourists.  There's beautiful architecture and gardens which would be more enjoyable if you had the space to.  Go early in the morning.

We had a walk around and read all the signs we could find but there just wasnt much to see or do. No books either which i found strange for a temple of literature. If you are passing then have a look but dont go out of your way to visit.
Learning about a country's history is always worthwhile.
This is a reasonably big complex of buildings and open squares with some beautiful old Chinese style architecture. Best visited with a guide I suggest.

This was a very interesting place, but it did not look very much like a temple. It was gigantic, and taught you about teaching in Vietnam in the olden days. There were very interesting sections and it was absolutely beautiful throughout. I liked the experience and found it moderately interesting to learn about Vietnam in the old days.

Well, I'm not the most literate person. I enjoyed.. there was a graduation on that day anf the place was full of students.. it was fun because of that. Would recommend if you are literate and/or have time.
It was pleasant and nice to see, but there is not much to the place.  Local people and tour oriented advisers seem unduly high on this location.  We enjoyed a number of other sites in the area more.

Although the temple of Literature is one of the popular sites in Hanoi, I didn't really find it fascinating as it was just a temple with some history inside. It's of walkable distance from Ho Chi Minh Complex, takes about 15mins on foot.
This temple has retained an undeniable authenticity. But, perhaps because of the rainy weather, the tourists, or the proximity of the avenues, I didn't find it very charming.
Lovely set of courtyards, buildings and architecture and worth a visit just to escape the hustle and bustle outside.
It was pleasant walking through the grounds, but unless you are ultra interested in Vietnamese history, this won't mean much.

Upon arrival, the place looks incredible. You have to pay about $2 to enter the temple. Architecturally, the gardens, courtyards, and exterior buildings are very beautiful. Once inside the main temples, there isn't much else to see. If your intention is to spend a quiet moment reading in the gardens, it's highly recommended; however, if you're there to visit a temple, it's not!

Quite a pleasant complex and interesting history but difficult to take photos due to the crowds so I couldn't get too excited about it. I liked the tiled roof. Would be nice to visit when not many people around, but not sure how feasible that would be.
A pleasant and easy place to visit...but not essential for a visit to Hanoi...unless, of course, you're passionate about Vietnamese culture...

We toured the site with a guide who gave additional information on this temple that was originally built as a university in 1070 dedicated to Confucius and scholars. This is the same temple illustrated on the back of the $100,000 Vietnamese dong banknote.  We found the experience enjoyable as we saw several courtyards, trimmed lawns, pavilions, temples and statues devoted to Confucius scholars who had an impact on Vietnamese history.  Of particular note are 82 tombstones with the names of scholars/doctors who excelled at the royal exams from 1442-1779.  Turtles represented longevity and their carved names ensured these scholars will be remembered for one thousand years. Unless you understand Chinese, there is not much translated information about the temple so reading ahead would be advised.

Built more than one thousand years back, it is rather disappointing that there is not many historical relics in the temple. There were not enough English translations for the ancients stone tablets which would explain deep meaning poems or writings from old scholars. The temple is simply more of a place for locals to ask for god or ancient scholars blessings for students success in studies. What is worth to find out would be the old trees around the temple compound. They looked like centuries old and would have seen revolutions and wars in Vietnam. The interesting building design of the temple is so heavily Chinese influence. More could be shared on how Confucius and his disciples teachings influence the ancient kingdom of Vietnam. No much explanation on that.
It's a former university with a temple complex. The history is interesting, and Hanoi doesn't have that many other highlights...
It's a small but beautiful museum inside. Worth a visit if you have time. But it's nothing unmissable.
visited the temple and saw some graduation ceremony going on. probably 3-4 groups of them. interesting place to visit for 30-45mins.
Quite good. Nothing special if you have been to Angkor Wat or similar places before. Nice historical twist though.
The name promises more than the temple actually delivers. But it's still worth a visit for its architecture alone.

Visiting this temple is a must on any trip to Vietnam. Those who have already seen many temples can skip this one. The remaining stone tablets with their inscriptions are quite impressive, especially when you consider the meticulous craftsmanship involved.

If you are interested in the old Vietnamese "royal" history, and morality result in places like this, visit it! For an average interested person like me, It was nice to see the magnificant buildings and hear the story behandling them. A visit that will be remembered, and is perhaps a must for first-time Hanoi visiters.

Interesting place to visit this, of course it has a lot of history and used to be the main university of Hanoi back in the olden day.Each of the sections within the temple of Literature represents something. There are depictions of the holy animals of Vietnamese here, especially the giant tortoise with the books on its back. What is also most interesting is to see the prayer happening inside with the bells tolling and the priest reading out sermons. Several youngsters could be seen here posing as a group in their graduation dresses, which our guide later informed us are rented dresses, and photos are taken whenever students are free irrespective of them having completed their graduation or not. And these were students from all across Vietnam and not just Hanoi, just goes to show the popularity of this temple.Some shops inside sell some touristy items but nothing unusual. Got covered as part of our city tour, otherwise there is nothing unusual to see in this.
Peaceful and a nice place to visit. One can skip this if there's no time left. All in all,a good place.

Is a temple of Confucious with small lakes, nice scenery and historical buildings, some locals said is the first national university there which makes it meaningful for them. Located within walking distance from the masoleum, worth to pay a visit if is nearby. There are graduate students taking graduation photo when I visited there which is quite a view.

You do learn a lot from coming to this place...just stop to read the informaiton on the site .  The buildings are well maintained, the gardens are clean and the whole area is a buzz with excitement.  Sad to know that when the French occupied this holy place they turned it into a military establishment...but that was then and this is now.  It is now glorified and revered by the Vietnamese.
Frankly, not great: old stones that don't really evoke any atmosphere or emotion. Not essential, in my opinion.

This is the Temple of Literature, the oldest university in Southeast Asia. It doesn't really feel like a university because it has a Chinese-style gate, and you walk along a corridor with ponds on both sides, where there are several temple-like buildings. The grounds are long and narrow, so it's quite a walk to the building at the very back.

While it's true that it remains the oldest university in Vietnam where mandarins received their training, it's still just a few reconstructed buildings... personally, it's not something that excites me...

Went earlier in the day than expected but the Ho Chi Minh museum/ mausoleum line was more than a mile long so we decided to skip it and enjoy some time at the temple of literature. Nice place, got to see kids graduate. If you go in the morning there is a place next to craft link that has the best pho I had in Vietnam.
Very peaceful place to visit if you just want to read or collect your thoughts. It is very large inside. I enjoyed the visit and taking photos. Enjoy.

This site has essentially been rebuilt; it has nothing to do with our old universities. The colors here are truly striking, and the rest of the site is interesting. Since Hanoi has essentially been rebuilt, their history is quite recent.
You learn some interesting history associated with the envelopment of Vietnam's religious progression. Well worth a short visit.

This temple was established in 1070 and is dedicated to Confucius and other scholars.  Within the temple complex the first university in Vietnam was established in 1076.  The main Great Gate is lovely. Our guide showed us that the gate is on the $100,000 Vietnamese currency (see my photo). The Stelaes of Doctors are pretty cool. I've never seen anything like it.  They are on carved bluestone turtles.  They contain the names of the graduates of the royal exams some dating back to the 1400s.  The altars to Confucius and his disciples are well done and lovely.  Very colorful.  When we visited there were many recent graduates all dressed up taking pictures.  It was nice to see.

We visited the Temple of Literature during Tet, also known as Chinese New Year. The place is steeped in mystery, a blend of former literature professors, doctors, and many others. The temples are well-maintained and graceful. Enjoy your visit!
We liked the entrance arches and it was it was good to see an actual graduation taking place. It was interesting to learn about the history of the building but not a must see monument for us
Located within walking distance of Hanoi city center, it was bustling with tourists.

The massive amount of people really does make it seem much less like a historical temple. You just wanna get in and leave. I walked in a line of tourists. Like queuing up to buy something. Nothing quite amazing is at the end either.
A peaceful place which replicates earlier buildings on the site. Watch the graduating classes of students take photos to mark the end of their studies and relax in the well tended garden
We made a stop here during our tour. Nice for a short visit. The gardens are beautifully landscaped.

Our tour guide tried to market this place as a sacred and special temple - however we werent really feeling that way after it took us over an hour to walk around it due to the 100,000  tourists inside. The red carpet and plastic print outs of scholars hung up looks tacky. Would have been nice to simply see it how it would have once been without trying to be catered to.
A restored chinese 'university' (Place of learning). OK for what it is. Would be very hot in Summer (we went in Feb)
I understand the history of the place, but as a tourist site it's worth a quick visit and not spending much time on it.

The complex itself is quite pretty, but anyone expecting large libraries (I mean, this is Vietnam's first university) will be disappointed. The rooms mainly contain altars, all of which look the same.

This is one of the most popular place for tourists to visit in Hanoi. U can learn about the 1st university in Vietnam, with model of the original design of the university area. this is also a place for souvenirs.
Probably one of the few places to see in Hanoi, so tourists head there but there's nothng so valuable to see in my opinion

Visitors come from all age groups, from children and students to parents. You can no longer touch the symbolic turtle, but instead, it is said to bring good luck to touch the head (or belly?) of the crane located in front of the main shrine.

It's a historically significant site, but since half the complex has been restored, the sense of authenticity is somewhat lost. You can't tell from the photos, so Instagram will be happy to see you there. The stone steles (or tablets) of doctors deserve special attention; they have been recognized by UNESCO as World Heritage Sites.
The first university in Vietnam. It's a peaceful place where you can sit and observe, escaping the chaos of the city. It has an interesting history; read about it before visiting. Price: 20k
There isn't much to see in the exhibits, but the area is full of trees, making it a refreshing place in Hanoi, a city of honking horns and exhaust fumes.

When visiting temples in Hanoi I was a little bit disappointed because Inalways expected more. True that after two wars that is difficult. Go there keeping that in mind and trying to learn from them all that happened he and you will enjoy it more than if you go expecting something as what you see in Thailand or other Viet cities.
A temple made for Confiscus, a teacher who pushed education and an organised family structure in Vietnam. Huge place, oldest university, 1000 years old. Ok to visit
Not worth the visit or price of admission. Over crowded.  The grounds were not pretty and there wasn't much to see.
After 3 weeks of travelling which included Seim Reap we were literally "templed out" so perhaps a bit unfair here but I found it a little boring, Nothing really that grab us.

It’s quite a simple temple. Believe, it is special place for the students & most of them gather here to get a photo shoot for their graduation. Quite interesting, they also have an area, where the names of the past scholars were imprinted on stone in the local language.

Ancient seat of learning in the centre of bustling Hanoi. Some lovely buildings, statues and artefacts. I was lucky enough to have a guide, without you may struggle to fully appreciate the Temples history due to limited info on site. Should visit if in Hanoi

I paid my entrance fee, walked through well-tended gardens and gilded temples, all very calming and pleasant - and I didn't have much of a clue what it all signified or really cared to find out.  Sorry, just somewhere to go because people recommended it.  I found a nice little cafe for a beef noodle soup lunch afterwards and enjoyed that much more.

This is supposed to be the first university and temple dedicated to Confucius.  Lovely landscape and old buildings but you are not sure what you are really looking at unless you do some reading before hand.  We found out AFTER the visit that the first university is actually the large building in the 5th courtyard.  Suggest you engage a guide to fully appreciate this attraction.
This was Vietnam's first "university," a place of learning that has been transformed into a rudimentary tourist attraction, inferior to those of the past.

Seen many far better both in Vietnam and in other Asian countries.  It's very old around 1000 years in parts and the carvings are excellent.  But I thought it lacked character.  Busy busy too. Expect to be harassed when you leave the exit
Beautiful spot. Whilst I would not recommend a long visit, an hour is defiantly enough, to cannot escape the beauty of the architecture and significance of the temple.
Historic old structure finish has been painted over with new paint out of time with old tiled roofs. Ponds are severely polluted remain untended. What a loss of history and shame on Vietnam.

Interesting buildings of ancient architectural styles and laid out in the style of a Confucian temple.    Besides the various buildings, there are the turtle stelaes with names of the scholars.  Shops inside sell touristy items.
This is one of Hanoi's tourist attractions. There were a lot of Vietnamese tourists.

Interesting and worth a trip, but it seems more space was dedicated to squeezing in spaces selling tacky souvenirs than anything educational. If pushed for time in Hanoi, drop this one off the list and take more time to take in all that happens around Hoan Kiem Lake, then go across town to see Uncle Ho's Mausoleum and the neighbouring One Pillar Pagoda.
Nice with a beautiful garden, but visited in the rain, so the charm was a little diminished.

In context it is probably a bit better than average, but very good is a stretch IMO. Some very old and interesting academic history, average gardens that are fairly well maintained & several souvenir shops that all sell the same fare. All-in-all worth the walk and the very affordable entry fee, but once is enough.
After walking around in hectic Hanoi it was nice to take refuge in a calm and serene place. It's not a must to visit, but it's worth it if you pass by.

Ok it is a nice place to go and see if you have the time - but if you don't, it's not a big deal. If you come when there are hardly any visitors, it is a tranquil experience. We took some good photos upstairs and left after say 15 mins max.
Small museum, interesting for the history of Confucius and literature, I recommend taking an English speaking guide
The way national heritage is kept in Vietnam shocked me. It could greatly improve and offer proper descritpions and so on as it is the case in most countries worldwide.

We thought walking the grounds were beautiful but we had a tour guide and he spent way too much time explaining the history which left my children very bored. We bought them ice cream and let them listen to traditional music while our tour guide explained its history. If you love Confucian history, its worth a visit. If traveling with younger children, skip the history tour and just stroll the pretty grounds. There are a few plaques you can read briefly to understand some of its history. Its essentially the place Vietnamese studied to pass the exams to become government employees--very similar to China's bureaucratic exam system based on confucianism. There you have it; that's it in a nutshell for your average tourist. But you can take pretty photos from here.

Confucius came to teach and impart knowledge to the people, starting from here and spreading throughout the country. This place served as both a temple and the first university in Vietnam. Nearby schools hold ceremonies to present red scarves to honor outstanding students.

Historic. Do see the beautiful Phoenix. Audio tours may help if they were made available here. Read about this place before going.  Without an understanding  of historical context the place itself may seem empty.
A place that can accommodate many people. Great in cool weather. It was the 1st university in Vietnam, and also dedicated to 孔子. Walking around to relax is nice

Actually I want to give score of 4 for this place, but some aspects made me decided to give just score of 3.Actually from the history aspect this place is very good to be visited, to be explored. Unfortunately it will depend on our guide quality. As long as I saw there were no guide in this place and also there were only few sign board that can give us explanation about the history of this place.The size of this place is not too big, there only few objects to be explored but quite nice to see. When we visited here, there were no crowd of tourist, but many young students (looks like elementary students) came in some groups. We found that this place is also becoming a history centre for the students.They were cute. I took a lot of photos of them. But may be not all people like children like me :)The interesting objects are the buildings. Some old and heritage small building that nice to be explored.There were some water pools inside this place. Unfortunately there were no lotus on in. Just a few water with a few leaves of lotus without the flower. This place is clean and looks like the management maintains it well, but as a tourist destination I think it will be better if they maintain lotus flowers grow in the pool. Moreover lotus is a symbol of Vietnam, right?Oh, btw the souvenirs inside this place is quite cheap compare to One Pillar Pagoda.

Having heard a lot about this temple from fellow travellers, we strolled in one afternoon after a long walk from the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. The temple sanctum as such is quite pretty with some elegant handwork and brilliant hues of red and gold all over. The statues are quite life like and have a lot of history to them as do the stone turtles lined outside with the names of those successful at the royal exams. The several courtyards are preserved in average condition and the place is quite full of tourists at most times. Overall, worth a visit for the essence of the values it holds.Highly recommend carrying a bottle of water as you amble inside on a hot day. The exercise can be quite overwhelming if you've been on your feet around Hanoi and then drop in for a look-see.

To my point of view, if you are a history lover and if you have a guide to explain everything to you then you can enjoy this place. I didn't find this place worth to visit. Moreover, it seems to me that I just lost the money and time to include this place in our itinerary.
I went during the New Year's celebration, and the amount of decorations and colors dazzled me.
Definitely worth the visit, to see a one thousand year old temple. The names of "graduates" can be seen on old stone tablets. This doesn't take long to tour through.
It's a place where students would take their examinations a long time ago. There is a small altar where located inside the compound.

Nice isn't my favourite adjective and this place is just nice. If you're doing the walking tour you may as well see it, but it's not hugely interesting. Pretty, it's pretty. And its reason for being is pretty cool.
great for cultural and architecture fanswonderful point for a real touch of asiatic culture recommended bring some water on warm days

It's a beautiful temple, with architecture influenced by Chinese culture, as it was dedicated to the philosopher Confucius and houses Vietnam's first university. When we went, there were many university students taking photos "for posterity," so it was nice to see them in their traditional uniforms. However, it's not a must-see on your visit to Hanoi. Some city tours include it, others don't.

On the outside, it looked like a very interesting and large temple but actually took us no more than 20-30mins to finish walking through the whole thing.  We didn't get a tour guide so perhaps missed some more of the interesting details about the temple.

Didn't feel this is a must see, small temple with many turtle statues. This was supposed to be the place where the royal exam was held. Saw a lot of graduates taking photos which was an interesting sight, but the temple itself is small.

Temple for scholars of Confucius during the Nguyen Dynasty.  82 stone stelles,  similar to those in Hue and I saw 1 such stone stelle in Tran temple,  Ninh Binh. Good for a morning walk amidst the noisy n busy Hanoi traffic.
This was an interesting place to visit. Knowing Confucius used to be here. It's small and shouldn't take more than an hour to see.
Apparently this temple is very famous for both local and foreign tourists. During our visit, we saw a number of local students celebrating their graduation there.
Visiting it, you'll pass through courtyards and avenues

It is a nice enough temple but not really special. There was not a lot of information available about its significance, although this should be the site of the first university in Vietnam. Having been to the Confucius temple in Beijing, this was very small in comparison, and not as informative. But it is still a nice temple complex.

I wouldn't consider this a must see. Unless you're specifically interested in temples. There's nothing special about this one. Of course it's pretty & there's a few different things to see. Large area to walk around take pictures. Crowded of course , just be prepared. Cheap to get in 30k I think.Maybe 20 - 25 mins depending on your pace & interests !
On the day we visited this place was crawling with graduates getting photos - it was impossible to enjoy and I wouldn't be in a hurry to go back and see if it's different on another day.
It's nothing special, but it is part of the city's heritage. With a good guide, it's easy to follow.

We arrived there, seeing many students doing pictures after having graduated.For us this was the most interesting thing, seeing how this ancient place is in used in nowadays.The place itself was overcrowded.The former idea, the reason why it was built is impressive, but it would be enough having read it in a booklet.

Attractive and peaceful compared to the bustle of Hanoi. However is not a great deal to do there and it was very busy with graduating students (I assume?), which meant that we were always walking into someone's shot. Worth a visit if you have the time, there was no queue to get in.
Temple was very nice, I didn't have a guide so I just went at my own pace, the gardens are very pretty and there is a lot of info about the history
Recommended by all the guides because it's historical, but apart from a small temple and a crowd of students there isn't much to see.
A temple worth visiting, but there are others in Hanoi, less famous and just as pleasant; a very touristy and somewhat overrated place in my opinion, difficult to access for wheelchairs.
Dissapointed with our tour of this place as it was overcrowded with graduating students. Could not see a thing.
It's important to prepare or have a good guide to understand the history of this place. Like everywhere in Vietnam, the facilities are in need of renovation.

Oldest university! Quite nice that names of students who passed the exam, the subjects, AND the names of the teachers were engraved in stones that sit on the backs of stone turtles. We witnessed a group of college students who were graduating in education. That timing added to the experience. They were in Vietnamese dress, then put on their caps and gowns. Students still go before the shrine of Confucius to ask favor in their studies. This can be done rather quickly, so why not?
No literature, as I consider what is literature, but it does offer several displays.  Why not stop in and look around.
The Temple of Literature is an interesting place, although after several visits to various temples, it remains just another one.
We took a private tour here as one of our stops. It was kind of boring in my opinion, but my husband and I aren't history buffs.
Lots of young people graduating - very nice to see them dressed up and happy. The temple was like many others but very cheap to visit, they have student discount.
If you have half hour to kill pay it a visit, some good history nice buildings. Nothing to exciting.There is some shops here with the usual items

To be honest, I thought the Temple of Literature would be something like a repository of Vietnamese literary works from the old days. It is, in fact, the first national university in Vietnam where men used to study subjects like philosophy and, I assume, literature. They have replicas of stone slabs inscribed with names of students awarded doctorate degrees by the monarch dating as far back as 14th(?) century. I was here on a Saturday and it was packed with locals and foreigners. Herds of students were still arriving by bus as I left at around 1PM. Students are wearing traditional Vietnamese dresses under black/red toga (gown and mortar board), taking class/solo/group photos. This amused me a bit because there didn't seem to be real graduation ceremonies, only endless photo ops. I was right, according to someone I heard. For reasons I don't understand, the tourists (white, 50+ age group) loved them and snapped photos endlessly. I doubt most visitors realized there is a small museum at the back, which is far more interesting and relevant than the students in togas.
We arrived on the day when students were having their group photos taken, which added to the charm of the place. Interesting architecture to see and small temples to visit.
Aside from the fact that we happened to be there on a graduation day, we found this temple disappointing compared to many others.
It's still very pretty, with large parks and lovely buildings, but there are really too many tourists for what there is to see.
I wasn't impressed of this building, I've seen more beautiful temples in the world. We've seen a lot of students making pictures, that was nice to see.
A very peaceful temple, for a relaxing walk among pagodas and wise turtles, you have to go there by taxi, a few euros, you pay the entrance fee another few euros so it's worth a visit
This temple is an illustration of Vietnamese history. It features some interesting stelae and a hall with a main altar and another altar for the recently deceased.
Was 32 degree day, many school kids graduating ceremonies, was hot and humid = uncomfortable! If you have an interest in literature, go visit! Pick a cooler day if available!

We visited by our own (due to time issues), but for sure I will recommend to visit with a guide who can explain the history of the Temple (we only have our many guides to read about, but it is not the seame). As it was aproaching the end of the school season, many students were there celebrating. Realy nice place to visit and understand the importance of culture and education for Vietnamese people.
A haven of peace in the middle of noisy Hanoi.

Temple is ok but since there are no proper signs searched forever to find it...and not a nice area to get lost these surroundings (very poor and dirty). We thought there would be a parc around it but it was just a little stroke of green. We didn't experience the oasis people wrote about at all...
It's a beautiful place, a haven of peace in Hanoi, but disappointing because of what it contains and the explanations....
A lot of nice relics but could use more narratives. The structures are relatively well maintained. Like many other sight seeing places in Vietnam, it needs clean toilets.

As this is the site of Vietnam's oldest university, it's considered a place that brings good fortune in learning. Therefore, it was visited not only by tourists but also by many young people who looked like students.
A large concentration of interesting buildings that function as cultural or scientific centers. Extremely crowded on weekends. Many venerable sites, but little explanation, so a guide is essential.

This building is built centuries ago, and Vietnam is not really a very strong countries during the time so don't  expect anything huge and great.This "university" is basically prepared for those students centuries ago. Who need to travel thousands of miles to Ancient China for highest imperial examinations. If you are familiar with the exam system in Ancient China, only the top 3 (状元、榜眼、探花) are promised with brighter future. Besides, if you are good enough but not in the top 3, you could be awarded with the title of Jin Shi, Ju Ren or Xiu Cai (进士、举人、秀才), and most of them will end up to be lecturers, teachers etc.You can see some monuments at the entrance which record the name of Jin Shi, Ju Ren or Xiu Cai (进士、举人、秀才) who graduate from this university. Occasionally, there are names for top 3.Some of the monuments are hundreds years old (you can read it from the writing) and fading, a feeling of vicissitudes.
A small, traditional island amidst the chaos of Hanoi. It's pretty, blending in with its surroundings, but it didn't particularly impress me compared to other temples in Southeast Asia.

It was ok , pleasant to walk around and informative with a guide but no information without one. Lots og graduation ceremonies going on so very hectic while we were there and this did detract from the holiness of the site

Perhaps we were a little unlucky with timing, as this Temple was heaving with College graduates in academic dress being photographed throwing mortar boards in the air etc. It was difficult to appreciate this Temple which was very touristic and certainly not an oasis of tranquility!
There were lots of students in the academic-related areas. Apparently, it's good luck to touch the turtles.
If you've been to similar places around the world, this isn't anything new. It's a small, standard complex of buildings and gardens, unremarkable in any way.
The tour of this Temple was okay. If you were short on time I don't see the need to rush in to visit.
Located in the heart of a noisy neighborhood, surrounded by heavy traffic, I didn't find the peace and quiet I was hoping for.

I visited the temple as part of a city tour. The Temple of Literature is the site of Vietnam's first and oldest university, the National Academy, and within its grounds are stone turtle heads and turtles inscribed with the names of those who passed the imperial examinations. This time, I encountered a group of university graduates, who were taking commemorative photos everywhere. The women were wearing ao dai, and they looked beautiful, but the sheer number of people was exhausting.

A massive crowd had gathered; the female students were dressed up in traditional attire, while the male students were neatly dressed in suits. Perhaps because it was graduation season, people were wearing graduation caps and taking group photos, while students who had hired photographers were busy capturing individual shots throughout the Munmyo Shrine. I had expected a quiet place, so I was surprised by the huge crowd.

It is just a temple with big compound. The first university of Vietnam. Good to get a guide to tell you its history. Many locals especially university students go there for photography session in the beautiful compound.

I first knew it in 1989, returning in 2016, the atmosphere that reigned there then is no longer there, you no longer "feel" that ambiance of "thought, of intellectual research" and above all, there is no longer the silence!!!
Need a ticket to enter....30k vndHowever I really didn't get what it was all about. Probably didn't help that it was swarming with students taking photos. Seemed like they had all just graduated.

This is a historical place in Hanoi. Quite famous that's why there are a lot of people here.However, it's a good place to study Vietnam history, also you can find some spot to take a good picture there.

The first two gates of this complex are promising. However, it is extremely crowded with groups of tourists. At the second courtyard, you can see that the old buildings have all been taken over by souvenir shops.

The temple of litreture was our first site to see in Hanoi. it is a nice complex of some gardens and abcient pagodas. Used to be a kind of high school for mandarines. As many places like this one in Vietnam, the lack of guiding/ brochure or signs with information prevents it from reaching its full (big) potential as a touristic interesting site.

Had a nice stroll through the Temple of Literature. Very peaceful feeling despite the traffic noice from the streets. Nice surroundings and buildings, the layout reminded me of the Forbidden City in Beijing, only a much much smaller. Nice to visit and if you do, don't forget to walk around the back of the main temple. There is a big drum on the right side and a huge bell on the left. Not many, if any walked this way. We didn't either but noticed the drum and bell from the outside.

I didn't appreciate this place due to many locals rather students taking a photos for their graduation they occupy almost the whole area. Aside from this, I think this historic place is nice to see by travelers/tourist.
