The Temple of Literature is old buildings looked poorly maintained about 10 minutes away from Hoan Kiem lake. It was constructed in 1070 under Ly Thanh Tong’s dynasty, first to honor Confucius . In 1076, King Ly Nhan Tong continued the work and built Quoc Tu Giam as the first university of VietnamInside you'll find a pond known as the 'Well of Heavenly Clarity', a low-slung pagoda and statues of Confucius and his disciples.The entrance fee is 30,000 VND . Opening hours: 8:30 to 11:30 and 13:30 to 16:30 everyday except Monday and national holidays.
Interesting building, we visited on a Sunday and found it extremely busy with students who had graduated at other universities and came here to have photos taken.
The history of this place was very interesting, and the temples were ok.  It was hard to tell they were recently restored.  We saw a number of classes taking their graduation pictures which was fun.
Interesting place to have a look round with some beautiful buildings. Some kind of graduation happening while we were there so spent our time avoiding getting in their photos!!

It used to be a school for the rich people, but in the end it was opened to public... It isn't a school anymore though... It's said that if u touch the head of the big turtle statues inside then u'll have a good luck, especially kids who do that will pass their exam... Now since they r UNESCO heritage, it's not allowed to do that anymore... It's an interesting place with lots of histories behind it, but make sure u come there with a tour guide, becoz there isn't that much infos on site...

Temple of literature, dedicated to the sages, is located right in the middle of all the chaos of the city, and as such can be a place of refuge for a while. Its relatively quiet an serene, although not more than a 30-minute visit in my view.
I revisited this place on my second trip to Hanoi. The guide was just a quick tour, but I'm glad I came back. The restrooms are not free; it's not a rip-off.
It is nice place but don't expect something special and don't ecpect a library or books to see.It is quiet and gardens are good for relax.

It's a must-see, both for its architecture and the significance of its structure, as well as for its interior. It's a quick visit, but to fully understand many aspects, it's advisable to go with a guide or have read a bit about the temple beforehand.
It is a nice spot to visit....i. However I've been to many other in Japan Cambodia and Thailand that were much much nicer

I expected something totally different. When I saw that it was advertised as the oldest Vietnamese universitiy, I expected rooms with thousands and thousands of books, manuscripts and so on. It actually looks like a temple, statues, gardens and fishponds. I would recommend to pay the entry fee of 30k, and see what it is all about, but do not expect anything WOW.  Enjoy
Coach loads of tourists arriving ensured it was not a tranquil and quiet place to be. The only place in Hanoi where we were pestered by street sellers.

When I was told to go to the Temple of Literature and the National University, I thought it was two different places and I had visions of books being venerated in a modern university: nothing of the sort. It is as much a temple as anything but one which, in the course of its 1000 year history (it was being built as the Normans were compiling the Domesday Book), held a teaching position, overseen by the king, in the literature arts. Not a lot to see but what there is is interesting. Beware the taxi drivers outside who will offer to take you on the meter but then rig the meter to badly overstate the cost of the ride. Practically nowhere should cost more than 40k to 60k dong.
That's pretty much it. The history of the place is very interesting but we read it in advance. The place is pretty but not that interesting to look at.
Its very pretty and beautiful but I hoped something biggest.  There are just five small areas and thats all. But its just 30 000 dng.

The temple is a place of refuge in noisy Hanoi, were it not that it's overrun by tourists. We were there at the moment some Vietnamese classes were taking graduation pictures, which made it special but also even more crowded as tourists would swarm around them to get a picture. Yet the temple as such is worth the visit because of its architecture, the old steeles and the mere idea that being good at poetry would give you the best position in ancient Vietnam. An ideal world!:-)
This beautiful, large temple is worth a visit, divided into three main areas. Oriental scents in the air allow you to escape from the city traffic for an hour.
I was expecting more, but it's quite nice. It's small, not a must-see, really nothing essential to visit.
A cute place to see, but not extraordinary. If you got time it's worth it a visit but if you have a day i suggest you to focus on something else.

It took my girlfriend and i a while to walk to on a very hot day, and of course we had to battle through the millions of bikes. But this place is a small oasis in the city. Although its situated next to busy roads once inside the street noise is all but gone. The temple is interesting with gardens. However the price charged compared to time spent seemed a bit steep.
It's a temple, a beautiful temple, but nothing extraordinary! It's certainly a must-see in Hanoi for most tourists! Nothing exceptional.
For those who have traveled to Asia, another temple. Nothing special. Crowded, a bit run-down... nothing to report...
Maybe this is my "problem", but honestly it's not interesting and there's nothing to watch, but I repeat, maybe it's not interesting to me
Celebrating Confucius' thinking and teaching, along with a short history of the University. Nice gardens. Worth a quick look if you're in the area.

Chinese culture. There are stone monuments to those who passed the imperial examinations, and statues of the "Four Sages"—Yan Hui, Zengzi, Zisi, and Mencius—in addition to Confucius, which suggests a strong influence of Confucianism, particularly Neo-Confucianism.

Slightly disappointing experience considering that the price is 15000 vnd while you can easily visit the entire complex in less than 30 minutes,  anless you wanna stop by for prayers and offerings..Is a nice place but is smaller than i expected and there is no much to see or visit.. apart the temple..
The gardens are really pretty and there's some cool topiary but there's not much else to say about it.
We spent time here that's not having a guy sounds it to be quite confusing as to the actual representation of this temple although we did have a slight idea from the bro sure
It's not worth visiting the place with a tour guide. I would recommend getting a Hanoi guidebook and using it to visit any site, only paying the entrance fee for the attractions.

We had expected a truly restful and relaxing experience and, though is is undoubtedly a fascinating place and we enjoyed reading about the history of it, it was very busy and not restful. We'd still recommend, but don't expect to be sitting enjoying the tranquility during the peak season.

During our visit the place was so crowded it was difficult to even walk through the park. There were many students taking pictures which was fun to see but added to the masses of tourists. Would have been very interesting and nice to see without the crowds.

I visited this place, but the rain lost some of the charm of a place steeped in history. Its interior gardens are beautiful and feature very ancient plants, but as I said, the rain made me miss a lot.

Undoubtedly the Temple of Literature and National University is an extremely important cultural site. It's been here for almost a thousand years and looks like it's been carefully preserved and maintained for the whole of that time. Within its perimeter wall are pagodas, courtyards, small ponds, statues, pavilions, a giant drum and gong (both, alas, unbangable) but, surprisingly for a temple of literature, not a single book. Not even a Dan Brown. Allow 60 minutes, more if you're interested in early Vietmanese architecture and the history of Confucianism.

Even though it's an oasis of calm in this incredibly noisy city, this temple isn't anything exceptional when you've seen so many more beautiful ones in Asia (Thailand, Bali, Cambodia, Singapore...), but hey, you have to find something to do in this city!

Established in 1070 to enshrine Confucius, it is called the 'Confucius Temple.' It is Vietnam's first university, founded in 1076, and the names of those who passed the imperial examinations are inscribed near the entrance. With lush greenery and a quiet, calm atmosphere, it deserves to be called a sanctuary of learning in Vietnam, both in the past and present. Inside the Temple of Literature stands a large stele with a turtle-head pedestal, on which are inscribed the names of those who passed the civil service examinations administered for approximately 300 years, starting from 1442.
It's one of the places to see in Hanoi, quite nice and calm. You can see it all fairly quickly, allow 1 or 2 hours maximum.
The gardens are lovely for a stroll, but the visit is quick and of relatively limited interest.

This was my second visit here after 7 years, and I still was happy to come back and pay the 30k entrance fee to see the stone tortoise tablets again. The shops in the back section take away some of the charm this place could have.

The place was well preservation and worth to be visited.It should be better if there was guide to grouping guests and introducing detaily about history and architecture.Parking was bad. The sign showed price following time 8-18: 3k and 18-20: 5k. But some one erased the number "1" in "18-20" to get 5k.
Unfortunately, there isn't much to see in Hanoi, so it's a must-see, but honestly, you can see it all very quickly and it's not particularly impressive.
Somehow I was expecting a more romantic place. Instead, all you get is some yards, temple buildings and the very ancient (and historically relevant) stone plates.

A protective wall surrounds the complex. However, the roar of traffic is unmistakable. Unfortunately, there are far too many stalls selling merchandise, which seem out of place. There are more beautiful sites in Vietnam. Sadly, it's very touristy, and the true value of this magical place is lost.
In the hustle and bustle of this metropolis, this temple is nice to visit. For 1.5 euros, you'll be entertained for an hour. Beautiful buildings, statues, and courtyards.

Interesting History connections with the Doctors Stone Steale. Lovely ancient temples in a nice surrounding garden. Quite simple explanations, not a huge lot of information but enough to give you an idea of the significance of the place.

There are signs in English with some explanation of the origins and their meanings,  but it was all very dull. The buildings were beautiful,  entrance very reasonable but I'm sure Hanoi holds many more interesting sights
This Confucian temple is not very large, but it is well worth a visit. Admission is €1.20, and children under 12 are free.

I usually don't bother writing reviews but I thought I'd just send a warning to those who attempt to purchase anything (i.e. ponchos) from the people out the front. You pull out 500,000vnd and they will straight up snatch it out of your hand and cause a scene... You won't get your money back, they have a strong grip and they will outnumber you. Other than that, the gardens and temples were lovely :)

Though it's considered to be one of Hanoi's main atractions, we'd expected more of it. In Vietnam we've seen more interesting temples. The steles are worth to have a look at though. Maybe it's better to go there with a guide, who will give some background information on the temple complex.
Pay attention to all the trees in the garden cut to reassemble the 12  sacred animals, and the old turtles with name tableaus on top of the shirls
This temple enshrines Confucius and his disciples.
Not the biggest of sites we visited but still offers a few nice pictures and some interesting readings about Confucius and history of the temple.

Before the Communist Party of Vietnam decided to fully adopt the "National Language" script, there were still a considerable number of intellectuals in Vietnam who could write Chinese characters. This Confucius Temple is a cultural heritage site, a testament to the ancient Vietnamese intellectuals who studied the Four Books and Five Classics and took the imperial examinations. It is basically similar to Confucius Temples around the world. In addition to Confucius, there are also sacrifices to Mencius. In fact, apart from this place, most of the historical sites in Vietnam still show remnants of Chinese characters, which shows that Vietnam was also a member of the ancient Confucian cultural sphere.
I often take my children to visit this place, but the space is cramped and the signage isn't very good, especially the directions to the restrooms.

The temple is a revered part of Vietnamese History and Culture, and if you have done a bit of homework and understand the history behind it, it is a wonderful spot, if you are doing a race through without a guide, go straight on to Ha Long Bay
Nice place to learn a little about Confucianism but not many things to do and see, 10 minutes are more than enough to visit the whole place.
This is where education started in Vietnam, and is a resting house for the intellectual . It has a buddhist temple and surrounded by greenery.

I know the Temple of Literature is a famous tourist destination, especially for foreigners visiting Hanoi. But apart from Khue Van Cac, I don't find anything particularly outstanding about it. I just like the space: large, airy, and with the stone steles. It's for tourists who "truly" want to understand more about Vietnamese history in general.
This could have been a very interesting place, but there is very little information about the site. It would be good to go with a guide or tour, or Hanoi Kids.
Good to see the first university in Hanoi. New did it as part of a private tour. They have clean toilets there for 1000 VND but can be quite crowded

Worth a visit if no other reason than to get off the crazy streets of Hanoi. It is a quiet, sedate stroll thru a well maintained set of gardens and restored architecture dating back to 1000 AD.  Signage is in English and tickets currently 30,000 dong (kids under 15 are free).

Do your reading beforehand so that you get the most out of the temple. It is quite interesting and there are a few informational plaques that are helpful. The gardens are pretty and relaxing in the midst of crazy, busy Hanoi. It was crowded and buzzing with hawkers at the gate, but worth a visit. It is a significant landmark and essentially the first university in Asia.

The grounds are a tranquil setting in the middle of busy Hanoi. The architecture is nice and there are signs I'm English to explain some of the sights. It's not overly big though so don't expect to spend too much time here
As the title suggests, almost everything is described. I had expected more regarding the literature. The site itself is worthwhile. Still, recommended...

For me, it's just a normal temple with quite a history and value for the local people and believers. It's quiet and peaceful with it's vast gardens and views. Great for a quick retreat between the bustle of the city. There were souvenir shops inside the temple area, best get your souvenirs here because they came at a cheaper price than outside in the city. (I checked)

If you have limited time in Hanoi, it might not be a good idea to prioritize a visit here; there are many other more interesting places to see. However, if you have plenty of time, stopping by and learning more about Vietnamese culture and history firsthand is a great option.

This was a basic trip away from the main area we stayed in.Very cheap entry (30k) and a nice stroll through the grounds and some history to read about  Confucius.Certainly not 'exciting' but quiet and easy.I would recommend going here just to get out the hustle of the normal streets.Expect to spend around 30-40 mins

From the view from the street and reviews I'd read previously, this temple is a 'must see'. Maybe I'm missing the point of this building but I didn't see any references to literature. A beautiful temple in equally beautiful grounds. That's all I can say! Was in and out again in 15 minutes.
The entrance fee is reasonable and you get to see the Chinese-style temple. Please bring along an umbrella with you on a sunny day or rainy day.

We visited the Temple of Literature with Hanoi Kids on a half day tour. They made the visit interesting by explaining about the architecture and history of the site but if we had visited on our own I think we'd have been disappointed. It's beautifully built and peaceful but not really much to see or learn.

as the title goes, It's beautiful, but you don't have to go inside. most of the temples in Vietnam has similar features, and this one is not an exception. It is beautiful, but I don't think it's worth going inside if you have visited other temples here.

On the eve of graduation for many universities, this place is often crowded with students taking photos in their graduation gowns, demonstrating the significant influence of Chinese culture on Vietnam at that time.
We visited the temple on the day of the student awards ceremony. I expected to find more documents inside.
Nice to visit. Nice shops. Good to see a the history and the temple and it's garden. Very nice in the middle or the city.
Very peaceful and quite temple in the center of the wild hanoi! Very impressive cultural heritage!!!

This was a really pretty attraction. However, you cannot spend too long there. It was quite small and very similar to other temples (if you have been travelling around Asia). There was also a weird festival going on in the centre courtyard which meant that the whole place was littered with souvenir stalls and other random things which detracted from the beauty of the temple. Nice visit (30K for student)

Went here because of the reviews but if in hindsight I'd probably have gone to the Museum of Ethnology or something else. Moderately interesting but lacking information for travellers about the history of it all.
As we visited at noon so the weather was extremely hot. We didn't even want to stand up and walk. The scene was not that beautiful. And again EXTREMELY HOT!!!!
The Temple of Literature is very ornate and beautiful. There is however, not a lot of literature for English speakers with the tour. Koto restaurant and nice little coffeeshop nearby are good.

Our review had said this was a quiet bastion from the turmoil of the city. Not quite. It was before finals and it appeared every student in Hanoi was there making offerings and sending prayers for good exam results. Besides every tourist had appeared to come that day. The temple was beautiful and the steles and turtles fascinating. I would suggest a weekday visit rather than a weekend.

The Temple of Literature is probably the oldest historical building in Hanoi, dating back 1000 years. Site in a very realising and lovely garden it gives you the opportunity to take break from this chaotic city

It's a temple with some lovely gardens, quite nice if it's not a stinking hot day with a bunch of graduation ceremonies going on (or at least that's what they looked like). I think if you went here as part of a tour, or with someone who knew something about the history of the place, it would be more interesting. Don't go expecting it to be anything other than a  nice garden with some lovely old buildings.
It is Vietnam's first university and the place where Confucianism was systematized. Also known as the Quoc Tzu Gan, it is the equivalent of Korea's Sungkyunkwan.
Nuce and peaceful place except it isnt really spectacular and also very crowded with school groups. If you don't have much time in Hanoi, I wouldn't recommend it.
I went expecting something like Jongmyo Shrine, but it's small—a tiny garden. However, it's nice not having motorcycles. I zipped through on a motorbike, but there wasn't much to see.

The Temple of Literature was not what I thought it would be. While the surrounding gardens are attractive there is not much to learn about the site itself. Maybe some background information before a visit would make it more worthwhile as some others have suggested. I did see some attractive girls being photographed so that was a plus!
Perhaps I saw too many pagodas and structures before getting here. And way too many school kids in tours didn't help matters. Probably nice late in day in lights and no crowds.
I went during the Lunar New Year period, so it was bustling with Vietnamese people. There were especially many students, and it seemed like they were praying for success in their exams.

It was raining when we went there so it was a bit of a hassle. It was also a bit crowded as there were also students taking their graduation pictures (aside from tourists). Nevetheless, we still found the place interesting and good for taking pictures. You can explore the area in less than an hour. Entrance fee is 30,000 dong or roughly 1.5 USD
To enter here, you have to pay 20,000 dong. It is a beautiful courtyard with a temple in itself. However, it is very touristy. Ask yourself if you want to pay for this!

This is an interesting and historical place. Buildings are beautiful and in pretty good condition. I found this place a bit boring though. Also be aware of the taxi drivers outside temple. They are often not confirmed taxis, meters are often fixed and they demand you to pay a lot of money. I recommend to walk a little further from the temple for get an reliable taxi.

The elegant Chinese architecture makes this a lovely place; seeing Chinese buildings in Vietnam feels quite welcoming! Many graduates come here to take photos, and tourists can see scenes like this anytime! Many young women are wearing Vietnamese ao dai (traditional long dresses), which look very beautiful!

A charming place, beautiful gardens. We were fortunate to be there on a graduation day so many students in caps and gowns added excitement and atmosphere . Also saw many younger students praying for knowledge and success at the temple with school principals in support. Only place in Vietnam I had to pay to use the loo and I had no small dong so it was an expensive penny to spend- loos were however clean.
This place was cool and big but it was a little dull in comparison to other things you can see in Hanoi. I'd recommended going to pub street and enjoying yourself and skipping this one.
Nice for a brief visit if you are in the area. Some interesting photo ops. It costs 30k VND for entry. You'll spend maybe 30-40 minutes there.
This temple is located in the heart of bustling, noisy Hanoi. As soon as you enter, you're immediately struck by the tranquility. If you enjoy architectural history, this visit will suit you.
I found the inside of the museum boring, but outside it was nice and interesting to walk around. Definitely worth it if you are in Hanoi.

It's located in the city center and is very popular. There's no entrance fee, and people are always passing by. There are always tributes and ceremonies held there. This is interesting because you get to see their culture and customs. It's a temple with images and gardens. Nothing special for me, but for those who like it!!!
If you have less time for wandering on around vietnam, put this last on your list. Place is a bit small and crowded. Not so much to see except a few altars. Although the garden was good.
Nice place, lovely gardens and some great facts about the University and the culture. A little boring but part of a tour
It's one of the best things Hanoi has to offer, and it's not overly spectacular.

Paid for what I feel should be a free attraction as they make money out of you in the gift shops/food venders inside. If you have free time then go have a look but it's not on my top 10 things to do when in Hanoi

Apparently, this temple was once a university where people prayed for academic success. Without a guide's explanation, it's hard to understand the details. The souvenir prices seem to be inflated for tourists. I recommend buying a good luck charm.
It's a lovely place for a stroll, and it's even more interesting with a guide. It's cheaper with a student card.

A revered historical site over 1000 years old devoted to education and the training and testing of bright students , sourced nationwide.   Not a lot to see than exrtremely old buildings and one modern reconstruction.  Most notable is the relative calm and freedom from traffic noise all pervading in Hanoi.  Major disappointments are that most explanatory displays are only in the Vietnamese language and there are too many gift/souvenir outlets spread throughout the complex, at least 5.  Allow 1 hour to visit. Entry free VD30,00.

It was okay and had some interesting history but overall this is something to do if you can't find anything else to do. To be honest I found the groups of giggling school children more interesting than the location. They had a ball taking pics of me.
Not quite what we expected. Only 30k entrance & nice little area to walk around. Not much shelter from sun, shady bits are great.
Guided group tour to discover the first Vietnamese university, still a destination for young graduates who celebrate their achievement.
The oldest university in Vietnam but reserved for future kings.  Interesting and worth a quick look around the temple and grounds if you have time.
It is very close to the city center and well-maintained like a park.

At 40,000 VND (half price for students), I'm not sure if it's worth it, but it's close to Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum, so if you're passing by, why not? It has a nice garden and a bit of history on display, but don't expect much more.
Vietnam's first university where students were trained. Not much to see. Nice. Nothing more. Mostly vegetation and various sculptures.

I like Confucius, and that's why I wanted to visit this place. It's beautiful, but there aren't any panels with explanations or information about his history, which would be very interesting. If you're nearby, it's worth a visit. But it's not a must-see!
It was very busy when we were walking around this historic place. It was nice and it was interesting to see the students posing for graduation pictures. A guide was helpful to explain the history.

We enjoyed strolling through this quite deceiving large complex - if it's not your thing, you can become over-saturated with history, but for those interested I think you will find it an excellent site.  It is dedicated to scholastic achievements and a good example of traditional Vietnamese architecture.  It's a little oasis away from the hustle and bustle of always busy Hanoi

You do not need a lot of time at this place but it is very interesting.  We had a guide who walked with us and explained the different items on display.  Without a guide you most likely will not understand the history and significance of this place.
Large complex, the size deceiving when you first enter, however can't say that it's a must for an itinerary.  I would have preferred to have spent the time elsewhere.

It's a beautiful place needing a wee bit of a touch up. It would only take 10 minutes to go in and out. Much better things to do. We was lucky to see some school kids to a class but nothing huge to go on about

Beautiful grounds with loads of history. We visited here as part of a private tour. Lots of information and a place good for young and old. My blond kids got a bit mobbed and had fifty photos taken with the randoms in the grounds.
It's a lovely place to visit and take a stroll. Good infrastructure. I recommend stopping by and exploring the surrounding area.
The entry area was visually appealing. However, the site did not have much to offer. Be sure to have money in order to use the bathroom.
Good for a stroll. Unable to grasp the interest of this visit. But not much else to do in Hanoi anyway. Avoid on a rainy day.

The facility was the cornerstone of education from the early days. There is little to guide the visitor as little written in English. On the day that we visited a group of students were graduating and it is presumed that the facility was used as a photo opportunity. Well preserved and centrally located. Note - many of the public exhibits are closed on a Monday.
Not only did we have absolutely no prior knowledge, but since it was a tour, the guide enthusiastically explained everything, which made it easy to understand and very helpful.

Rose was our guide for the morning and this was our first stop. The information was interesting and gave us insight to the Vietnamese culture. The temple was very old and respected by locals and visitors alike. Some maintenance is needed on walkways to assist in safety. But it is accepted there are higher priorities in this city.

This was an interesting temple with lots of local students having graduation photos there. As with others, it is a bigger site than first appears. It is interesting but there are plenty of other similar attractions in Vietnam.
Interesting historical temple. Worth having a guide as little is translated, easy to spend an hour or so here viewing the various elements.
The entrance fee of 30,000 VND is cheap, like almost everything in Hanoi, but there wasn't really anything to see. Perhaps it would have been more interesting with a guide.

Went with my family and got a reduced price as I'm at university. Nothing particularly special if you've been to a temple before but interesting to see what kind of things people studied. Full of your standard American tourists with guides but worth a visit.
It's always cool to see ancient sites and this one is no different. I can't say that it's a must-see, but if you have some time to see the tourist sites then definitely include this one.
I went to Hanoi because there aren't many tourist attractions there, but it was extremely crowded and overwhelming. It has a historical feel, but I also thought it was a bit cheap.

Probably one of the few temples in Hanoi's city center, so it's very, very crowded with tourist groups. It's quite small, with a shop located in one wing of the temple. There's a lovely garden. Best visited outside of peak hours!

If you have some spare time and want to get out of the craziness of Hanoi you can have a stroll around the gardens here for half an hour or so. There are also some decent shops and food options not too far from here.
A place where one can see the local lifestyle and culture of Hanoi. The local fro the age of three to eighty spend their day here.
Definitely worth seeing, preferably with an Italian-speaking guide, given Hanoi's limited attractions. Honestly, we expected more, even just from the care of the gardens.
Did this on a tour with a guide. Happy we had a guide to explain the history, as not much written word around. Very pretty.
Made up of five courtyards separated by well-kept gardens and ponds, it is a site worth visiting for its history and the silence that reigns there.
Well, the weather was bad. And yet there were still many visitors. Visiting the Hanoi Arch is part of the experience, but it's not really exciting.

Nothing special; no signs in English so you really need a tour guide to tell you the significance of each bldg. If you've been touring all day, this may not be the place to go...if you've seen one temple, you've seen them all...
Visit to the temple of literature, cradle of education in Vietnam, visited by tourists and graduates and families who came to take photos of their children graduating from the university.
A guided tour to a well-maintained place with some interesting aspects for tourists. Recommended.
It was crowded when we visited in the late afternoon.

Like most of the signature "places to see" in Hanoi (and in Asia for that matter now) This place was overrun with tourists from buses. Many of the people hop off the bus and begin jostling for "selfies" at the entrance and throughout the park-like setting. This Temple is actually a series of plazas and compounds joined by a central walkway. It is serene without the blast of people---so plan your visit accordingly---otherwise you may just end up walking through and leaving. Sometimes too much of a good thing is a bad thing! Please, people, no more selfies! Is it not possible to simply go and enjoy a place quietly and remember it in peace?

This temple is dedicated to Confucius and is also the oldest university in Vietnam, where the imperial examinations were held. It was heavily influenced by Chinese culture during the thousand years that Vietnam was under Chinese rule, and this influence continues to this day. There were many tourists, but it wasn't exactly a quiet place to visit, as there seemed to be a field trip or extracurricular activity by local elementary school students. However, the names of those who passed the imperial examinations are inscribed on stone monuments, and

Interesting visit. Although the temple itself was unimpressive, learning about the first national university was extremely interesting. The grounds are a popular spot for Vietnamese people to go and take pictures. We spotted many couples taking engagement pictures, young women doing portraits and huge graduating classes taking class pictures! This alone is enough to make the visit worth it.
This place was a school or university for ancient people.The building is old and has very nice architecture. The graduated students were honored in the form of stone steles.
It's really beautiful to look at. Unfortunately, the information panels (except for one) are not translated. Entry costs 15,000 VND for students and 30,000 VND for adults.

Worth seeing for its history and tradition. It wouldn't be so special if it weren't for that. And if you happen to be there when schoolchildren are visiting... it's beautiful for their liveliness, but a bit too crowded.
...but I wouldn't plan/invest too much time here.
It's a very nice place. It's about a 20-minute walk, and if you're passing through or have some time in the city, it's worth it.

We spent an afternoon there, and frankly, after traveling around the country, it's nothing special. The best thing about it was the student atmosphere. The price per person is 30,000 Dong. Much more interesting was walking back to our hotel in the old town.

The most significant aspect of this place is its antiquity, as it appears to be the first known university (run by monks) and library since the city's founding. The buildings and gardens themselves didn't particularly impress us, as what remains is quite austere.
Since my accommodation was nearby, I stopped by here for a walk after dinner.

This was an interesting site to visit but it was packed with tourists and felt a little like a factory line. It might be better to go with a guide who can explain the significance of what you are seeing.

This is a fairly standard temple complex and if you have been to many it would be pretty much the same, but as we hadn't been to any this trip so far it was ok. Being the temple of literature there were a lot of graduating high school students celebrating In the grounds, making a colourful spectacle.The final large building is interesting with a good view from the upper floor.Nice place for a short visit.

It's a beautiful place, but it was quite crowded on the day I went. I'm not sure if it coincided with a graduation ceremony, so you might want to check for any major holidays beforehand, otherwise it will be very crowded.

I realise this is a very important institution for the local people but I found it not very interesting to me. It was Chinese New Year when we visited and it was absolutely packed which didnt help. It was not a highlight of our trip and we would have done without visiting it as part of our tour.

Not a bad site - one of the better sights to see in Hanoi. Sadly the main building and the old dormitories were destroyed by Ho Chi Minh in the 1946 war. Inexplicably, a completely different building was recently built in their place instead of rebuilding the old buildings, so the fifth courtyard no longer has any historical reference.The stone steles were good. Pity that none were translated though - it would have been interesting to know what they said.
It will be perfect if I mustnt pay much for the ticket. I think this is a public place so the price isnt expensive.
Well worth a visit for its architectural value, but you won't find any literature here (which we naively hoped for, haha).. The entry was super cheap (30000 dong)

I visited because it is right in the middle of the city next to a lake, but there isn't much to see. Right in front is a famous water theater, and next to it is a road with a lot of motorcycles, so there are plenty of sights to see in Hanoi.

We went to the literature museum mid morning and there were A LOT of people there. The grounds are very beautiful and the buildings are interesting to look at. However there is not much explanation for each of the areas and hardly any in English, so you won't need much time to see eveything. I would suggest going early in the morning to avoid the crowds and if you are a student bring your student card because you will get in for half price (15,000d instead of 30,000). Also you might find it more interesting if you read about the temple before going, that way you will won't miss the little things.

Quite a few gift shops within the compound and you still gotta pay to use the WC once you've paid the entrance fee. I wonder how much of these buildings are actually from nearly a millennia ago... The stones on the tortoises are obviously original given their state.
Pretty temples but there was not as much information as I would have liked to help us understand its importance. I didn't see any audio guides, but it was very busy so we might have missed them

This place is included in all the local half-day and full-day sightseeing tours, so we decide to pay a visit.  As we found out, there's not that much to see.  The "museum" does not offer any audio aid and there aren't enough English information regarding the displayed artefacts.  The structure of the temple is very old, but don't know if it is the original built in 1053(?)

Visited with the in-laws who were here from the States in October.  Made the mistake of coming out on a Saturday afternoon.  It was wall to wall with young Vietnamese graduates taking photos!  Might come back again one day as it seemed interesting enough to visit, but maybe on some weekday morning!
This place was interesting and had a sense of history. I enjoyed seeing the architecture and carvings.

Like many other countries, Vietnam dedicates absolutely everything to venerating the figure of Ho Chi Minh: the father of the nation, the eminent one, the enlightened one, the leader. And there, in magnificent fashion, stands the mausoleum, which you enter two by two in absolute silence, without being allowed to bring cameras, and passing by what they say is his embalmed body. It could also be a wax replica because from a distance that's what it looks like. If you don't go inside, you're not missing anything.

I suggest you get a guide. Which costs 100,000 Dong as stated by the ticket office. If not, at least read up about this building before hand or at least buy it's info pamphlet for 8000 Dong. If you've not gotten souvenirs, get them here. It's cheaper, and some are unique. Anyway's the university has some dedication to Confucius and his 6 students/deciples.

It was the first university in Hanoi. The building can be visited for 300,000 dong. The tour is pleasant and offers a glimpse into its history. There are explanatory signs inside, but some people hire a guide. It takes no more than an hour and a half to see it all.

We did this tour today and being a Saturday and also the first day it was opened after TET it was extremely busy. It is of interest but on an very hot day it was just too crowded and hard work to really enjoy.

I'm sure the temple of literature itself is great to see, but the massive crowds of people meant that we couldn't actually read any of the signage or get inside several of the buildings to see within. What we did see was very interesting, and the short walk in and out of the temple is very pretty - if you can deal with getting crushed from all angles by people shoving and elbowing their ways through. Not necessarily a location worth the stress of getting around it

The Temple of Literature is a great visual aid to understanding the Confucian system of education and government service.  Most of the other visitors the day I was there were on school field trips.  A few of the students wanted to practice their English and a couple of girls even asked if they could take a photo with me.  Of course I obliged.  If you're looking for souvenirs, the gift shops are cheap and have some more-or-less educational items for sale -- like necklaces with sayings (in Vietnamese characters) such as "attain" and "pass an examination."

I didn't go inside, but listened to the guide's explanation at the gate. It was packed with vendors and tourists. Those who want to go inside have to pay an entrance fee. There are also places to eat nearby.
This place is very, very busy. Without a guide you may wonder what the fuss is about. Some interesting culture and history, very popular with local people who came dressed in their Sunday best.
Not sure whether it is worth a special visit good to see if in Hanoi as interesting how they recorded knowledge on stone etc
A lovely place with beautiful architecture, very well maintained, with ample spaces to view the entire structure, and an interior courtyard that showcases its architecture.

The temple is dedicated to Confucio and literature, academic achievements. Nothing special. If you have time and want to do a quick visit it is ok but if youbare in tight schedule and need to choose few places to visit in Hanoi, then I would not prioritize it.The entrance fee is 30,000 vnd per adult (about 1.5 usd so very cheap ).

Interesting history and nice buildings but rather unsatisfying without a guide. There were a couple of plaques that gave brief outline of the history, but many individual exhibits were not labelled in English. Also it would be good if they could bring in more period furniture and items. I would like to have known what a classroom and desks were like, for instance.

The tour became more interesting when I was able to talk to a girl who understood everything about the buildings and customs inside the temple. For example, people should always face the statues, which is why I saw so many people leaving with their backs to the statues through the small doors.

We had a quick look around but for me it was just another temple. Not that impressive and most of the signs just in Vietnamese.If you have time maybe worth but if not you do not miss anything I would say.Entrance free: 30.000 Dong, Students 15.000

The stone monument atop the turtle has some kind of writing on it that's barely legible. In one corner of the building, there was someone practicing calligraphy. Apparently, people who can write Chinese characters are rare in Vietnam. My friend who went with me is skilled at calligraphy, but whether the person there was good or not... I'll leave that to your imagination.
A nice, serene little place located in Hanoi. If you have time is might be worth coming for a visit to get a good feel for Vietnamese culture

It didn't take us long to look around this attraction, it probably took us longer walking to it from our hotel! It was ok, the grounds were well kept, it was a bit shabby in places, but worth a visit.

A visit to The Temple of Literature by ourselves really only warranted a 3 star review. As there aren't many English explanations it is difficult to know what you are looking at. Beautiful buildings (poorly maintained in my opinion), a dirty pond, and sad looking grass. Perhaps a tour with a guide is warranted for this site.
This is basically a very old University.  There's not a whole lot to see but people who are really into historical old buildings may enjoy seeing it.

This is a beautiful place designed to create space for the mind and body, I can appreciate the elegance and intricacy of every element. However, like any tourist attraction in a big city Is is teeming with ( some quite unnecessarily noisy) visitors. Fittingly there were a large group of young Vietnamese people celebrating their degree success in caps and gowns. I was very pleased to see their joy and pride but my selfish self longed for some contemplative silence. I guess I should get up earlier.
We have already seen many temples in Vietnam, and this one is remarkable to us in terms of content (the many steles), but not in terms of richness and decoration.

The history behind this temple is very intresting. But if you visited a couple of temples before this i just another one. Then spend your time to more uniqe objects. If you never viited a temple or if you are a temple neard, you should definetely go there.

You can feel the depth of Vietnam's history from the gardens and architecture. It's a place where you can experience both the past and present of Vietnam, with many local students as well as foreign tourists.

Really interesting historical site, well worth a visit. We were there about 3 pm and it was teeming with tourists. Mainly the large groups with guides broadcasting via microphone ruins the experience a bit. Maybe better to hear here early if you don't like crowds.

Was not Impressd at all, nice to see just if you have time. There is only a few builidingd to really take a pic of and the rest just same old same old. It is so so. Been there done that won't do it again.

A very pretty and peaceful temple, sheltered from the constant noise of traffic. You'll walk through a garden, then a courtyard with stelae, and then another courtyard to reach the temple. Entry costs 30,000 VND per person, and the booklet costs 8,000 VND. Be careful when getting your change; the woman at the reception desk almost "forgot" to give me a bill.
Imagine stepping into a place built in 1070, with architecture inspired by China. And on top of that, it was the target of French bombing raids during the Indochina War.
It's a very nice park with another cool temple. There isn't much information on site about what exactly it is. Not a waste of time.
There were lots of people who looked like students, and it was quite lively.

The university is hundreds of years old and is a place where students study. There are statues of turtles and cranes in front of the temple which symbolize health and longevity. You can hire a tour guide to explain the history of the temple and university in more depth.
Had a walk through here. Was interesting and definitely recommend you drop in if youre going past, but wouldnt go out of your way especially to see it.

This was added to our day tour itinerary by our travel agent. As it is a temple to Confucious we were expecting a few of his philosophies to be posted around the attraction but were disappoint. Lucky I could look up Brainy Quote on Google to explain to my teenagers who he was. Magnificent gardens and nice historical temple but I wouldn't design your day around it.

A long history, a beautiful pagoda, and interesting statues, but the tour is over in 30 minutes, and there isn't as much to see as in a museum. However, you can stroll through the gardens; a bit of peace and quiet in the middle of Hanoi has a certain charm.

A temple among many others; perhaps it has another story, but ultimately, when you visit, is that the main point? (30,000 VND) The visit is also frequented by many locals in traditional, military, or school uniforms. Make sure you walk all the way around so you don't miss anything.
We've been to a lot of temples and this one was just so-so for us. It was pretty, lots of people visiting. Didn't take long to walk through the area.

We enjoyed strolling around the Temple of Literature but one needs to read up before visiting to enjoy the visit or go with a good tour guide, ours was lousy. It was an interesting experience though, when we were visiting there we a lot of students around who just graduated in full graduation outfit.

Although Vietnam has a history of anti-Chinese sentiment, their ancestors consistently learned from the culture of the Celestial Empire. The dismounting stones at the entrance of the Temple of Literature demonstrate that ancient rulers highly respected Confucian education. Inside the Temple of Literature, one can learn about Vietnam's ancient civil service examination system, making it well worth a visit.
I am glad I saw this and I liked reading the "stiels" (I'm not sure if I have spelt this correctly)   However, I was a little disappointed.

Interesting and peaceful gardens, and a little bit of history. However it is not very informative, and there isn't a massive amount to see (I just had to rely on Google). Not really worth a special trip, only recommend it if you are nearby and need a break from the motorcycles.

The gardens at the entrance of the temple of literature are manicured and pretty.  They have topary animal figures representing the animals of the lunar year.It is worth visiting the temple with a guide so that they are able to explain the significance of the temples points of interest.
Very old university. It is nice to walk around. Beware of the hawkers at the entrance, they created confusion about their prices and then refused to return our money. Worse than elsewhere.

The place is beautiful and well-preserved, but unfortunately, the hustle and bustle of vehicles around the site prevented me from fully appreciating it. However, don't let that deter you from going; it remains a must-see in Hanoi.

It was little over a week ago I visited this temple, and already my memory about what was in this temple is fading. The temple has cheap admission fees and is a nice place to occupy some time. However, the location is quite far from the centre of Hanoi where most accommodation is situated. The items on display were average (in comparison to other museums). If you have extra time, feel free to come here, but if not, you will not be missing out on much!

This is a beautiful temple dedicated to Confucius, his students and a place of learning. There is signage in English, French and Vietnamese keeping you informed about the various statues you see.  There is also limited information about the history of the temple. Our tour guide spoke in Vietnamese, so I'm sure he explained some of the symbolism in more detail. It is still a beautiful site, though. Lots to see, take your time and look around at some of the hidden treasures - door guardians, little lions, clouds on the roof, flags, students taking graduation photos, stellas, etc.
Again, in my view over-rated. We did a half day walk all over central Hanoi & glad we had the option to do our own options.

I first visited the Temple of Literature in 2002. It was a revelation of history and heritage. But I felt that the place was dilapidated. It was ok then as Hanoi was just opening up for tourism. But after 13 years, the place has got worse and there has not been much effort made to ensure the place live up to its heritage. The only change is the new ticket booth to collect toll. There is not much to read or hear about the place as the guides are not readily available. But it is still a good place to go in Hanoi to learn about the literary and educational development and the influence of Confucius in Vietnam, provided you have a tour guide

Fascinating temple dedicated to Confucius, his students and a place of learning. There is signage in English, French and Vietnamese keeping you informed about the various statues you are seeing and the history of the temple. That said the information is limited and there is so much symbolism through out that you could miss it unless you have a local or a tour guide to help explain it for you in more detail. In which case the grading would move to "4 - very good" Lots to see, take your time and look around at some of the hidden treasures - door guardians, little lions, clouds on the roof.

We took photos and bought souveniors. A tour guide was explaining to his guests about touching the phoenix stomach and tortise head for luck. So we did. Some students graduated n were there with their class to take grad photos.

I think most tours will take you here, and it is definitely a place with plenty of history. Shrines and places of worship aren't really my cup of tea and I wouldn't have been sad to have missed this off the list and done some other sight seeing. Going with a guide probably helps, but there are signs in English to assist.

As is the case anywhere, you cannot fully grasp it with just a quick glance. It is a place where one can learn about the perspective on scholarship, as well as etiquette and humility. It is meaningful to look up even a little bit of information beforehand, understand its significance, and then examine it.

I get the historical significance but this isn't an active University or anything so it was not that impressive. It's not a large area and like most places in Vietnam, the English descriptions are severely lacking so it ended up being a quick walk into an old temple on the way to the Hanoi Hilton museum.

Although we were guided by a tour guide we were never Although we were guided by a tour guide we were not clear on what this was. There is a large statue of Confusius but it's not a temple. We were told students come to take pictures here as a group. They also pray before exams. t clear on what this was.
Nice and quiet place. You probably need a guide to really get the most out of it. It was definitely worth a visit though!
Former university, nowadays  monument and nice garden, where the undergraduate students celebrate their successfully finished studies. Quiet place in hectic city.
Great place to find out when the first university of Vietnam , all the literature of the ancient kingdom Ly dynasty. Actually the name is wrongly translated, the right meaning is Confucius temple

The Munmyo, said to enshrine Confucius, can be entered by paying an admission fee. There are buildings resembling temples and shrines, and Chinese characters can be seen here and there. It is fascinating to watch people praying to Liu Bei, Guan Yu, and Zhang Fei. It is a place worth a quick visit.

I read about the Temple beforehand and it was fascinating. However, going there myself wasn't a very fascinating experience. It might've been better if I had a guide to tell me the story while we were roaming around. There was a graduation there when I went, so in a way, it made the trip better and more colourful. Managed to talk to some of the students who felt very honoured to be a part of the Vietnamese tradition and culture.

The most interesting part of this place was to see the temple that is pictured on the back of the VND currency.  I know this place has history but because I'm not a local, it was hard for me to appreciate.

Hanoi isn't a beautiful city; it's shocking, picturesque, and chaotic. The traffic is awful, with motorbikes going the wrong way everywhere. But this temple is the most beautiful thing it has. The gardens are well-maintained and worth seeing.

Not there for very long. Quieter than the surrounding madness of Hanoi. Many groups of graduating students come for photos at this time of year. Try to get a guide for the Confucian temple. 30.000d /pp entry fee.
I was there on the day of the graduation ceremony... and it rained, too.
Good place to go see, however doesn't take very long. If you want it to be more interesting, make sure you get a guide!
We popped in but nothing to write home about.  Nice buildings and temple in nice grounds but would not rush back.  Good for 30 mins visit.

Daily graduations make for great photo opportunities with the temple in the background.  I was confused for the purpose of this location because there was nothing to explain what everything meant and our tour guide just dropped us off.  The buildings were interesting but not on my top of the list for things to see.
Small place.. guide is a must. You will see exciting school graduates coming for pics. Apart from that very little to do.
As other attractions in Hanoi, it did not wowed me. I liked the stones with the names of the graduates

This temple is dedicated to learning.  It was Vietnam's first national university.  There are 5 courtyard, each one hold significant to literatures. On the day of the visit, many graduating students came here to pray for a successful future, going on to universities.
not too much to see here. just neat that it's an ancient spot for confucius teachings. grounds are a large garden-like area. gotta pay to enter. it's fine nothing too exciting.

Some buildings, landscape and a few shops selling handicrafts. The temple itself is like others in Vietnam, now within what seems to be the University campus, so many students around. Can be given a pass on a tight schedule.
Full of excitement, I arrive there and all I see is people, more and more people.

I probably should have gone for a guided tour as those around me with guides in many different languages seemed to be more impressed with the experience. Definitely come to see the graduations if possible. Nice mix of old scholars and new scholars.

Good to visit once! Temple of Literature is a place where the local graduates take their graduation photos as the place is to commemorate Confucius. Entrance fee: VND 30,000 per adult. It is about 20 mins car ride from Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum and taxi fare costs about VND78,000-80,000.

We were lucky, because when we visited this temple, or rather the first university of Vietnam, graduation parties were taking place. Boys and girls in beautiful clothes were having their photos taken with their diplomas. Beautiful stylized gardens, somewhat resembling cloister corridors, for contemplating life.
Worth a visit to see the buildings but not much more. A bit lively when we visited as there were graduation ceremonies taking place but it is no longer used as a uni.

Nice enough for a visit. When we were there, a group of graduates had just arrived, which was very nice to see. It is an important place for Vietnam because of its educational and cultural value to the country, which makes it a special place. The brochure costs 8,000 dong, but it contains very little information, so I wouldn't necessarily buy it.
I had imagined an oasis of calm and peace and soul, but the temple is quite small and doesn't really have anything to offer.
I wasn't particularly drawn to it when I saw it in the guidebook, but since there aren't that many tourist attractions in Hanoi itself, I decided to go.
At the entrance, there were gravestones of those who passed the civil service examinations, and inside, there is a place where Confucius is enshrined.

Nog a Real must see in you ask me. Every single graduate in and around hanoi comes there to celebrate their graduation. Fantastic for thema but not as much for tourists because of the hundreds of students. Some locals told us that this wasnt an extreme busy Day but it happens all the time. Still nice to see and visit but as stated; we were a bit underwelmed

OK it is ancient and the wrtings there are set in stone, but nothing much to write home about after its visit except to say you've been there. It is an area of Hanoi which is renwned for traffic jams, so allow plenty  of time and avoid rush hours.
On a nice sunny day lovely to walk round . On the day we went there were lots f scholars in their robes taking their graduation photos which made a lovely sight

This temple is worth visiting because it's different from the others; you'll see students in traditional student clothes, carrying balloons to celebrate their exam success; it's a very cheerful place and seeing all these Vietnamese students is a sight to behold because you don't see that anywhere else!!
We spent about one hour here while visiting Hanoi.  It's centrally located in a park like setting with beautiful gardens and ornate statues.
It's "nice", but following the ratings I hoped for much more. Plan 1h maximum for this visit & bring your guidebook or else if you want to learn something from it.

Thought we would have lunch close by before going the the university, but the area around there is not very nice (dog poo).  No decent placed to eat.  Were in and out of the university within half an hour.

a warning: be very careful when buying a ticket here. I received the incorrect change and was charged 80,000 instead of 30,000 (handed the woman in the ticket booth a 100,000 note and walked away only to look down and see all I had for change was 20,000). I've heard this has happened to others as well. the temple is lovely, and worth the 30,000, but not if you're getting scammed for almost 3x as much.

The temple of literature is an interesting visit - there's nothing particularly exciting about it, but it's got a nice little history that makes it worthwhile. It comprises various buildings - the majority of which appear to hold shrines which locals seem to visit fairly regularly. The most fascinating part was witnessing the excitement of all the students who still go to the temple when they're graduating from their studies - it was nice to see the grounds still used in line with their original purpose.
Was quite amaze by the temple is actually the first University in Hanoi. We met a bunch of students taking graduation photos over there and guess photos definitely turn out to be great !:)
Entry costs 30,000 VND. It's an option, but not a "must-see." If you're looking for a quiet oasis in the otherwise bustling city of Hanoi, you can enjoy some peace and quiet here.
Again this was part of a walking tour so we had a guide to help as out with some in sights to the place. Not such if I had missed the place I would have been to upset
We struggled to understand this attraction, whihc was probably our fault. An interestimng layout with a series of  Buddhas in the inner courtyard

This was a nice place to walk around. Plenty of beautiful flowers, ponds, and the temple was nice. Lots of graduates posing for photos made it a bit crowded in places. Not an earth shattering attraction but worth a visit.

This is a very interesting attraction. Lots of graduates would go there to have their picture taken with their caps and gown.  The gardens are beautifully decorated with lots of flowers.  It is worth a visit.
If you have some spare time, it's worth a visit. It was the first university in Vietnam. The temple was built in honor of Confucius, his followers, and Van Chuan.
It may well be that this building holds great historical and religious value for the Vietnamese people.

Had a walk around here and read what was there. This would probably have been more interesting had I had a guide explain stuff to me. I would say something to visit to fill in time. Not something you would have "missed out on" if you don't go.
I only spent 10 minutes there because there was nothing more to learn.

Lots of walking paths, some areas were flooded. The temple itself where the main statues were of the teachers were adorned with the splendours fitting for worship. We expressed our desire for wisdom and knowledge and hoped we were blessed. Didn't wash our hands in the pond for creativity as it looked a bit polluted.

Don't expect to spend a whole morning to visit this place, it's a small place to see, story to learn something about vietnamese culture, but it's just a tiny spot, not big deal to see.In the meanwhile ,you could visit the fine art museum and end up at the KOTO restaurant  in the same spot ,then your morning will be done with a finish touch of culture.

We visited during Tet, so it was very busy with many Vietnamese families visiting at the same time.  My children did not appreciate it, and it was raining, but aside from the crowds, it was a rather peaceful place.
Entrance Fee is 30k dong but it is not really worth a visit. You cannot see much in that temple and the greenery is not in best shape. Do not expect to much.
A Temple by any other name is still a temple. Nice grounds, nice temple. A tribute to students being recognised for their educational advancements.
It has very nice and tranquil gardens, not the most impressive place you will see in Hanoi. Worth going.

Not much english or french transation. The building (achitecture) is great, but there is little explanation given in the temple and if you buy a pamphlet for 8000 dong at the entry...I guess a guide or beoing with a local would bring much more interest since the history is quite impressive!

It is said this is the oldest university. But what you will see is a park and a temple, with a lot of stone tablets that has scholars' name (but you can't read it as it is in kanji-like writings. If you haven't seen any temple or pagoda - you will find this place is amusing.

In all the Asia this university was the first. Educational environment was touched in that visit. While our visit, there was some graduated students celebrating there as traditional constitution. Really the Place deserve to visit.
I visited after hearing that it is Vietnam's first university.

It seemed that many graduating students had come to take pictures, as there were many students wearing ao dai. In a way, the female students in ao dai were more impressive than the Temple of Literature itself. There were also souvenir shops inside. There was a statue of someone studying in the past, and many people were bowing to it, showing the depth of their faith.

I came here once when I visit Hanoi for the first time. There are not too much entertaining activities here. The most impressive moment in here is in spring and summer, many students come here to take photos for their yearbooks. Most of them are girls and they wear the "ao dai" - traditional costume of Vietnamese women. So beatiful.

This is great place to see some really gorgeous ancient architecture. The temple itself is beautifully laid out and you can purchase some keepsakes from inside its walls. Look out for the miniatures nestled in the bonsai s. Get some rain boots if you are going in the summer. There is little to no drainage and massive puddles form.
It is a place worth visiting at least once. Honestly, buildings like this are all pretty much the same, but it will be more interesting if you go knowing the history and background behind it.
Perhaps did not really get to enjoy this place to the fullest as hardly could understand my guide. Temple was typical and the turtle stones were interesting.

This attraction didn't exactly blow me away. There are many old stone tablets with inscriptions, some weathered beyond recognition. But even if they had been legible, my lack of knowledge of Vietnamese wouldn't have helped me much.

If you are in the area, it is fine to visit. It is easily doable if you are doing, for example, the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum - Temple of Literature - Hoa Long Prison tour. Perhaps the bad weather played a part as well. If we could give half stars, this would have received 3.5.

Worthwhile visiting to see historical buildings and artefacts that were from 1000 years ago. Garden is lovely and well kept. Watch out for street vendors outside the front gate who are pushing their way to get you to buy their wares.

The gardens are nice and the history interesting. I would say take a visit when you need a break from the busy and loud Hanoi. It's very restful in there so take your time!!! You can even exercise in their if you wish, but it does cost 30,000 dong to enter!!!

The Hanoi Temple has a millennium of history and is officially known as the Temple of the National Guard. It was founded in 1070 by the dynasty of Li and is located on the western side of Hanoi, Vietnam. In 1076 it was established as the First High School of Vietnam. The difference from the ordinary temple is that it is a temple dedicated to Confucius.On both sides of the Skywalk well and east, there are 82 monuments on each side. A living, flexible stone turtle raised its head, attached to this fine sculptural monument. The first monument has been carved for over 500 years. After that, each exam, a stone monument is raised. Turtle Negative Stone is long-lived and expects the growing development of Vietnamese education, while confirming that Vietnamese talent is worthy of respect and memory for generations. The inscriptions on the monument are written in Chinese letters, with different pencil. Each monument is a literary work, and the sculptures on the monument are diverse, reflecting the artistic image that evolves over time. Evidence of its wisdom and sophistication by Vietnamese artists. In 2010, UNESCO included the Temple – National Monument in the World Heritage List.

The Temple of Literature is one of Hanoi’s most iconic and historically rich landmarks. It offers a peaceful escape from the busy city streets and gives you a glimpse into Vietnam’s academic and spiritual past. If you’re visiting, I highly recommend taking a Grab (or taxi) straight there — it’s the most convenient and affordable option, especially if you’re unfamiliar with the area.I visited in June, and the weather was hot and humid, so be prepared. Bringing a hand fan or small towel can make a big difference in staying comfortable as you walk through the large courtyards and historic buildings.The temple grounds are spacious and beautifully maintained, with ancient architecture, bonsai trees, and quiet reflective pools. If you’re planning to step into the actual temple areas, it’s a nice gesture to bring some small change for offerings — not required, but appreciated if you wish to participate in the local customs.Overall, it’s a serene and meaningful spot to learn about Vietnam’s Confucian heritage. Give yourself an hour or so to stroll around and take it all in — and don’t forget water and sun protection if you go in the summer!

Upon entering, you will find five sets of gardens, each with a vertical building, divided by a distinctive gate. One of them, the Kuiwenkaku, is depicted on the 100,000 VND note.

The setting and buildings are nice and change from other buildings in Hanoi but you will find much better and more complete elsewhere in Vietnam, for example in Hue. However, it is a good introduction to Confucianists and examinations.

Van Mieu or Temple of Literature is the oldest and perhaps most beautiful building in Hanoi. The oldest part dates from 1054, modeled on the Confusius temple in his birthplace. For centuries it was an academy or university for mandarins. The temple complex consists of five courtyards, some of which have beautiful gardens, others large ponds. Each courtyard is separated by walls and ornate gates. A central path divides the complex into two symmetrical halves. Under canopies you will find a number of stelae with the names of the graduates. They are carried by turtles. The last courtyard has a smaller building on the left and right with the traditional drum and bronze bell.
