The Temple of Literature is a huge Confucian complex built in 1070 that was built in 1070 by Emperor Ly Thanh Tong. The complex was the site of the first University of Vietnam (1076). Originally it was reserved for the children of noble families, then starting from 1442 the university became the place where students of all social classes, coming from every part of the country, went to learn the principles of Confucianism, as well as the classics of literature and poetry.Starting from 1884, at the behest of Emperor Le Thanh Tong, the names and relative dates of birth of the best students were carved on stone steles and exhibited to the public.The complex is characterized by the Khue Van Pavilion, dating back to 1802, and an artificial basin called the "Well of Divine Clarity".

Where the elites of the kingdom studied, all those called to be leaders of the governments of VietnamThere is a figure of Confucius that is veneratedThere are gardensAnd two men who write in Chinese the word you choose from a notebook they give you. The words are related to Confucianism
Great tour for an afternoon, you get to know a little about the city's ancient history and culture. It's a great place to go and enjoy

We visited using a hop on hop off tour bus. There are two sites at this popular location. The temple itself and a small lake with cultural activities across the very busy road. Entrance to the temple was approx $3 and the lake area was free. Both sites were educational and informative.Heavy traffic around these sites was reduced to a crawl, so plan extra time in your day to get to or leave the site.Incidentally, if you intend using a hop on hop off bus, Hanoi City Tour stopped travelling to its stop at this site, leaving  angry customers behind. City Sightseeing Tour buses picked up its customers - no matter the traffic conditions.

Temple of Literature is set in the center of the capital. It dates back to 11 century when it was built as the first Vietnam University. Today, it's still the place where student celebrate their graduation.Over the centuries a Confucian Temple was built along with living barracks at the end of premises. Beautiful landscaped gardens in between pavilions and Temples.Small entry fee to enter and you need around half an hour.
The temple compound is quite large but the pavilion and temple structures are relatively small. All you need is 15 minutes to cover the site thoroughly.

Visited here on the afternoon of 22-Dec, 2023 and saw several local students in their special ware also offering prayers. Saw the Vietnamese unicorn, phoenix perched on the back of the Vietnamese turtle and also the statues of the 3 kings who encouraged academics with their contributions.

Interesting place. There is an exhibition on the timeline of dynasties in Vietnam, stelaes and areas of worship. You could do this self-guided. Saw many graduating students and girls in the traditional ao dai here taking photos.

Perhaps it is the most important temple in the city, certainly the most famous. When we went there were classes and classes of students who had just graduated who came to be rewarded and to receive their diplomas. Beautiful, young and happy. And the temple also lived a new life.
Very beautiful temple in Hanoi. Very nicely preserved. Well maintained. Truly a must see when you are in Hanoi.

Magnificent temple!! A little calm in this bustling city which is teeming with people, scooters and cars in every direction!! Here we feel peaceful. The building dates from the 19th century and is well preserved! The visit does not last very long, allow 45 minutes and entry is 30,000 dong. (a little more than €1, depending on the course. At the moment, €1 = 250,000 dong)I advise you when leaving to stop at a small café that makes delicious fresh fruit juices. (Shanti coffee)

This is a peaceful manicured garden in the middle of the city and then some courtyards surrounded by small exhibitions. It doesn't take long to walk through. The exhibitions cover the history of the place, where student examinations were held and it is interesting to learn about that. You can also learn in general about the history of Hanoi city, and there are also some shrines dedicated to key rulers of the city. It is nothing groundbreaking and not truly essential, but worth a stop if you are in Hanoi for more than a couple of days.

Beautiful and peaceful! The oldest university in Vietnam, right in the middle of the city and easy to get to. The architecture was great. Lovely pools and plenty of greenery. Give yourself 45 minutes and enjoy. We were there in the middle of the afternoon, so there were a lot of people---it might have been less busy earlier, but it was not over crowded. There are nice shrines and some neat historical stuff to see. Worth a stop.

Peaceful place with steele, tablets of stone, commemorating successful students in national exams to select government officials for 500 years.Interesting bonsai trees with miniature figures. Old temples.
The cultural heritage showing the education practices of Vietnamese scholars. One of the oldest and most credentialed University of its time.

A lovely setting for Vietnam's oldest university. Wanderinf throgh the lovely gardens is peaceful - particularly as once outside the crazy traffic is buzzing continuously. The temple and its history is worth learning about. We were amazed that people tossed coins and some smaller notes onto the roof!

This temple is in the heart of Hanoi, and is really a telling for the city. Beautiful greenery and flowers in bloom, was nice to have a stroll. (Also got to see lots of fish) We spoke to some locals who explained to us how university students come and pray for good grades here. There’s also a small market in the main area next to the museum, where you can buy souvenirs.

This is one of the symbolic places to represent Vietnam. It tells how the country is influenced by Chinese. Many turtle stone statues represent government officials who passed imperial exams and each turtle is different to other. It’s fun to look and enjoy each difference.It’s also a nice place as a park to walk around.We visited Ho chi min memorial and Chùa Một Cột, one pillar pagoda after here.
The place is relatively small but it is certainly the best cultural attention I have seen in Hanoi. Inside there are pleasant oases of peace. The story is also interesting.
You need a guide to explain the interesting details of the place. There are several details to note.

We arrived at 10am and not so many people there by 11.30 when we left heaving with people.  The audio guide was comprehensive and worth the small fee. Beautiful grounds  with magnificent old trees, bonsai and regular and very tranquil considering bustling traffic all around it.
Pleasant place to visit. Beautiful temple garden and interiors . Students visit for good grades in exams

It appears to have been both a Confucius Temple and a testing center for the imperial examinations. This clearly shows that Vietnam, like the Korean Peninsula, was heavily influenced by Chinese culture.
The Temple of Confucius, with its thousand-year history, was built in the 1070s to commemorate Confucius and his four major disciples.

The one star missing is for the TERRIBLE audio guide. Extremely boring and useless. Do yourself a favor and just go enjoy the scenery and wonderful temporary exhibits without being encumbered by a dumb headset.

This was our main focus for Hanoi sightseeing. As coincidence, this day, there were few schools with many kids visiting temple. Normally it is oasis of silence and peace. This time it was huge people traffic, speakers shouting some intro from invited people.  Kids definitely no attention. Otherwise really nice place, old architecture, decoration, green park in downtown and tons of history. Number one in the city. Enjoy the history influenced by Chinese many centuries back

There is a fee to enter, and they offer a headset audio guide, I recommend you get the audio guide. The guide is very informative, and you change tutorial channels according to the numbers on the map. It explains the temple very well as you walk through the area. A very nice well-kept area well worth a look.
It was the first thing I visited in Vietnam and I was immediately pleasantly impressed by both the history and the architecture.

that is nice place for learning more about culture and history of Vietnam.It is nice for walking there.So many young Vietnamese students visiting there and thet are so happy to meet us. However, it maybe too crowed at weekend

Visited the Temple of Literature and University as part of the Hanoi city tour. Found it interesting that the culture was preserved for the next generation. Has a Chinese feel where they follow the teachings of Confucious. Going through the 5 courtyards was interesting. A must visit if culture and traditions interests you. It is better if you visit with a guide.

Popular spot for locals, especially students and those graduating. That alone is lovely to see but to know it was deliberately buried to be preserved during the war shows how much literature and studying is held in high esteem here. Ancient graves of philosophers and doctors line the walls. It's a bit grubby and there are many opportunities to buy touristy things, scrolls depicting academic success for instance, but they are hand painted on the spot and rather lovely.  It's not crisp and organised but interesting to see locals pay homage to Confucius and we enjoyed it. Just DONT get the Tuk-Tuks outside - they were 5 times what we paid elsewhere.

We really enjoyed strolling through the many and varied courtyards that make up the Temple of Literature. It is so spacious and felt like an oasis in the midst of the bustling city. There is a nice cafe tucked away within the grounds and we also purchased a lovely calligraphy painting made to order.

My wife and myself are currently holidaying in Hanoi and earlier today we went to see one of the best known local attraction - the Temple of Literature & National University.It is certainly a popular site with many visiting school groups. Whilst I can see the attraction of visiting the site of Vietnam’s first University, there is not a great deal of interest to hold one’s interest for long!
Nice place to visit on your trip to Hanoi, but you should have a local tour guide to tell you why - what.... about this temple or university. Without tour guides, you can take beautiful photos

Excellently maintained Temple University for Confucius teachings. Awesome banyan tree in the main gardens. Made up of 3 distinct courtyards it honours the scholars who studied Confucius teachings and are recorded as successful. Statues of Confucius adorn the final courtyard with several of his family looking down on him. A golden turtle is also seen in the finalSanctum.

We visited the temple of literature about 20 years ago. What’s new? Not much in the old university dating to the 10th century. They now have a decent audio guide to purchase with the ticket . Some areas now closed off.  It is worth a visit, a fascinating part of Vietnamese historyWhile we were visiting a group of students were graduating. Good to see the historic place still used to promote learning. Was fun to see
We were there with a wonderful guide who explained the entire history of this compound.  It really made the understanding clear.   Beautiful place and very important part of Hanoi history
I had a look around this place today and it was ok.  I got some alright photos and there's abit of information to read about the history.
A lovely estate with a great tour to enjoy. It's beautiful, culturally rich, and steeped in history and typical culture. The tour is well-conducted. Highly recommended.
Interesting part of Hanoi history.  We had a tour guide, so we were told the full story of how it was built and by who in four stages. Along with the education history of Vietnam.
Take a stroll at the Temple of Literature. You will pass by a bridge and you can take pictures there. It is a historical place to visit.
A must-see, we visited it both day and night; the building is beautiful at night, and its history is important.

An ancient building dedicated to Confucius, the temple is composed of five interconnected courtyards that should not be missed. It is best to be accompanied by a guide/interpreter to understand the purpose of the different courtyards.
Another really beautiful historic area in Hanoi. We enjoyed simply wandering around there, it had been very calming as well as the Vietnamese architecture is stunning.

Be warned that there is an entry fee for this attraction, though it is worthwhile. You can spend a number of hours leisurely exploring the temples and gardens spread throughout the considerable grounds or just soak in the restful and relaxing atmosphere of this particular oasis of tranquility and serenity, focused on understanding and learning, situated directly in the center of the bustling city. You will discover places for refreshments and coffee on the premises. An audio guide might be rented for a tiny fee in the entry.
A series of pretty, shaded, flower-filled squares; this place is best visited with a guide or a good written guidebook. It's full of history and features magnificent memorial stones.

This complex is a breath of fresh air in Hanoi - inside its walls, not only will you experience the overwhelming history of the place, but also respite from the hustle and bustle of the city around you. Through several courtyards, boasting lakes and stone structures, you will learn about the temple itself, which dedicated to the teaching on Confucius.This is also the site of the first university of Vietnam, the students and disciples here learned the ways of being a gentleman and living a well-respected life. They wrote poems and essays, and were tested - the most prominent receiving their doctorate and having their names inscribed on large stellae. Presented on the backs of a turtle, there are 42 stellae on site, dating as far back as the 1100s, and all the way up to the 1700s, when the last tests of this temple were conducted.Inside one of the last courtyards, there is a large souvenir shop which also offers snacks and drinks for sale, and there are washrooms available on the grounds as well. There seems to be times when there are live music performances, but I did not see a schedule posted regarding showtimes. A curious observation - watching people tossing coins onto the curved roof tiles from the second story of the main temple building. It's like a mad game of Vietnamese Plinko, and I'm not sure of the significance. Best advice I can give for a great visit? Splurge on the audio-tour, but you only need ONE for 2 people - just remember to bring your headphones with you, as you can plug 2 sets into one base!

This was only a short ride in our guides car,from our hotel. What a peacefull place theTemple of Literature & National University is. It is like you have been transported to the countryside. There is plenty to see, and some good photos to be had. You can have a headset for information, but our guide Khan was very knowledgeable, and so we had a great afternoon learning all about Temple of Literature & National University. It is definitely worth a visit.

Our guide brought us here to learn of the culture and the footsteps of historical Vietnam and how it has become what it is now. I love how the trees survived together with the age-old buildings. One will immediately feel at peace. "Is this a school or is this a temple?" I thought to myself.
One of the city's best cultural attractions.
Loved this insight into old Vietnam. We had an amazing guide, Duy, who talked us through all the symbolism and helped me understand the Vietnamese of today, through their past

It's a truly wonderful experience to be accompanied by a guide who narrates the history (the stories) and ancient traditions associated with this place and the people who have frequented it for centuries. Gardens, statues, plaques, bells, covered spaces—everything harks back to the past and the culture that are at the root of today's Vietnam.

Hanoi's largest temple has celebrated education and the teachings of Confucius since its founding in 1073. It was Vietnam's first university and is now one of Hanoi's most visited monuments. Our guide explained everything well; it was an interesting and great visit.
A peaceful oasis in the city.
Perhaps due to the effects of COVID-19, or because it was early in the morning, there were very few tourists.
A temple not to be missed during your visit to Hanoi. Beautifully maintained courtyards and an unmissable calligraphy workshop.

This is a temple dedicated to Confucius, his disciples and to knowledge. It was also the oldest and most important university of Vietnam from 1076 to 1779. Today it is a venerated place which is visited by students and their families before or after an important examination. The place is huge and consists of five courtyards, each accessed through ceremonial gates. The innermost courtyards have temples dedicated to Confucius and to various monarchs of Vietnam. There are also steles which list down names snd details of hundreds of students who took the imperial examinations. Inspite of the crowds the place has an atmosphere of tranquillity.Once you have finishes your visit, you can also step into the small Ho Van cultural village, located opposite; or step into one of the cafes for a bite and a bit of caffeine. We tried the Ha Noi Cafe and it was quite decent.

Beautiful serene gardens and multiple buildings. Make sure you follow the arrows as there are additional buildings in the back. The Thai Chi practitioners in the adjoining park are a lovely local bonus.
Definitely worth a visit when in Hanoi, lots of info on Confuscious and his teachings, very informative and good for students to visit.
Visited this as part of our tour of Hanoi.  Very interesting and were lucky to be the first visitors that morning, so quite quiet. Beautiful architecture.  Good insight into history of Vietnam.

We found this place surprisingly interesting. At the end of a four week SE Asia trip we'd pretty much 'templed out', and so a visit to the Temple of Literature hardly warmed the cockles of our heart.Well, we got it wrong. It's thousand year history as the first Vietnamese university was actually fascinating and I found some of the architectural details impressive.I'd recommend a visit here.

Young woman selling t shirts pulled a 200,000 dong bill from my wallet and returned a 20,000 when I tried to stop her. Basically stole. Another switched the cards I chose and replaced with smaller cheaper cards. Walk away. Buy nothing from them. Deceptively good looking but very dishonest. “Please help me” they say and swarm you till YOU will be the one who needs help.

Unfortunately, education doesn't seem to be a priority in Vietnam these days, but I could be wrong. I haven't seen the same number of children graduating from primary, middle, and high schools as in the Philippines; it's probably due to budget constraints for families.

These were the prettiest gardens we saw in Ha Noi. In addition, the temple was in very good shape and interesting. Well worth a visit (when one can find the entrance, although that was not made easy by our rickshaw driver dropping us off some distance away - make sure you’re taken right to the door as it’s a long way round the perimeter).

It is always fascinating to see how Confucianism shaped the minds of the Chinese and the major peoples under their influence: Koreans, Indochinese, Japanese, and others. This important center of thought is a clear testament to that.
Lovely area with lots of interesting features, huge drum and  bell and lovely sculptures. We went during Tek very busy as singing competitions going on but still very interesting.
Interesting to see the evolution of the university over the years. Go with a guide to get the history. I loved seeing students getting their grad photos taken there.

While Confucius is undoubtedly the founder of Confucianism and a great thinker, I was surprised to learn that he is still so revered in modern Vietnam. I was also surprised to see numerous artifacts related to the imperial examination system, which was once practiced in Vietnam, revealing a history deeply influenced by Chinese culture.

Naturally, we visited the Temple of Literature. The entrance fee is certainly worth it. However, I do recommend taking an audio tour, as the history of the temple is described in perfect English. It tells a complete story of how formation and education were shaped. It is also beautiful to see what is offered and how people pray. Very interesting and definitely recommended.

Amidst the frenetic pace of the streets near the Old Quarter, this place offers a bit of tranquility and history (over 1000 years!). The temple honors the Chinese philosopher Confucius and preserves the remains of what was a university for approximately 700 years, the first in Vietnam.

We visited the University built in 1070 where the temple was dedicated to Confucius. Beautifully coloured  statues showing Confucius and his four best disciples.Desoite wars and other disasters the temple has survived and holds relics from many past dynasties. Well worth a visit. See also the One pillar Pagoda.
Exploring the whole area makes it a worth while. Easily you can spent 1.5hours here. Glad that we came.

The complex consists of five courtyards where portals, altars, and the Well of Celestial Clarity, a tank with carp, are distributed. Of the 112 turtles, only 82 remain on pedestals; students often pass by before exams to pet their heads, as it is said to bring good luck. It is located near the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, accessible by taxi from anywhere in the city.

A wonderful visit to the Temple of Literature, though it was incredibly crowded as we went during Tet (Chinese New Year). Despite the crowds, it's a place rich in history and beautiful. Don't forget to visit the park opposite as well.
A place that sanctifies education, a place where Hanoi's first university was founded and has been teaching and educating Vietnamese students for centuries.
The temple dedicated to Confucianism is a very popular tourist attraction in the capital.
The Temple of Literature in Vietnam primarily enshrines Confucius, the revered sage. While it differs somewhat from traditional Chinese Confucian temples, they share a common origin.

If your staying in the old quarter the temple of literature is worth a visit. You can combine it with a trip to the citadel and military museum and make a morning of it all. But by itself it is not worth a visit. It was very beautiful, but large crowds from locals make it a challenge to really enjoy the beauty.
A quiet and interesting place, filled with the impressive educational history of Vietnam.  Temples and stelas nicely preserved.

Since it was a traditional persimmon festival, we held events and sold food, so it felt like we did a lot of things. Also, there were events at the lake across the way, with singing and floating lotus flowers, so it was fun.
This is a very beautiful park with five courtyards that correspond to the five elements and a very beautiful building dedicated to the deities; a very worthwhile visit.

I visited without high expectations, but contrary to my predictions, the well-maintained garden and pond were impressive. Just beyond the wall, it is chaotic with a mix of motorcycles and all kinds of cars, but this place is tranquil and offers quite a bit of food for deep thought. There was a description stating that it is Vietnam's first higher education institution, beautifully arranged around a shrine to Confucius. It is worth a visit.
Despite the many years that have passed, we can still see the names of the institute's graduates engraved on the stones. The Temple of Confucius is very beautiful.

It's one of the most visited places in Hanoi, and for good reason, as it's worth seeing the typical traditional Vietnamese architecture, accompanied by small pools filled with water lilies. However, I was disappointed by the interior, as I found it rather cold and lacking clear explanations of what was on display.

Amidst the hustle and bustle of the city, this temple is quite pleasant and a delight to behold. It's even peaceful. The architecture is beautiful, the ornamentation lovely, and the roofs magnificent. Locals often come to have their portraits taken, creating a charming atmosphere. There's a lovely craft shop at the exit that shouldn't be missed.
We were passing through Hanoi and chose this place over many others. We weren't disappointed. Beautiful, heartwarming, and interesting.

Dedicated to the teachings of Confucious, this complex of historical educational significance is a wonderful experience.  An oasis of calm amongst the hustle and bustle of Hanoi, there are lots of beautiful photo opportunities of the gardens, ponds, temples along with students who love to celebrate their graduation here.  It is a vast complex, so be prepared for a bit of a walk, and stepping in and out of temples - there are however places to sit, rest and reflect on the significance and beauty of your surrounds.
Fascinating with all the old buildings, statues of cranes on turtles, inscriptions of names of former students and many fine building details.

It’s very nice to walk around and enjoy very picturesque with the buildings, gardens and temples.Lots of history to temple so I think it’s best to get a guide if available they can explain some of it.Lots of primary school kids visiting as well as graduation classes taking photos near the temple nice to see students/kids exploring there local temples and history.I would certainly put visiting this temple in the top 5 things to see in Hanoi.

Easily accessible as it's close to the old quarter where the hotels are located. The place feels cut off from the world right in the middle of the city. It's essentially a former university that trained the country's educated elite.
As a place dedicated to the god of learning, it has a dignified and tranquil atmosphere, unlike other tourist destinations. The architecture, influenced by Chinese architecture, is also worth seeing.
Nice place for a short falk of 30-60 minutes, good architecture, nice park, interesting temples. Worth visiting.
The temple is located right by the lake. It's well worth a visit. However, we only saw the temple from the outside in the evening.

As part of our trip we visited this cultural icon in Hanoi. For someone from New Zealand the age of these items is amazing. Many are well preserved and there are translations available for the most important. There is also space to be outside and enjoy the gardens in lovely weather - which was nice for families.

It is a little oasis in the middle of a bustling city. Very easy to walk around look inside the temples. The shrines are beautifully presented and have statues of Confucius and scholars that then followed in his footsteps.  We spent  no more than an hour here. Definitely worth a look

It is a Confucian temple, and it was clean and beautiful. It is the most worthwhile place to visit in Hanoi. The landscaping is well done. I heard that Confucian events are still held there every year. It is not very big, so you can see everything in 30 minutes to an hour. It is a small Oriental garden.

A major landmark in Hanoi, it evokes the city's great literary era. A beautiful and authentic monument, it is rich in symbolism. Vietnamese people flock to visit it on weekends. It is best to visit with a guide who can explain the history of this place.

Temple of Literature & Culture is a Temple paying homage to Confucius in Hanoii. The temple hosts the Imperial Academy, Vietnam's first national university. Its a scenic peaceful site giving respect to the chosen students who are listed on Stone Tablets and the Library . The temple was built in 1070 at the time of Emperor Tong. Its artfully carved has various statues and also large Metal Drums and Bells and has a door through which the King could enter and side doors for others to enter.

After exploring the city center by motorbike and sampling the street food, head to the Temple of Literature, a peaceful and beautifully landscaped place. The trees are magnificent, and Confucius reigns supreme.

A nice little temple in the city. You can take a stroll around the gardens and walk around and explore some of the temples. To fully appreciate the experience I would recommend getting an audio book to fully understand the history of the site.
A moment of calm... and a few signs in French. A pleasant place to visit to discover the history of Hanoi.

Nice compound with history and teachings of Confucius. Wished to have more time to roam around in peace rather than a rushed trip offered by the Free Hanoi Tour. It is not necessary to join any tour group, grab in Hanoi is easily accessible and affordable.
Tickets are obtainable in the little kiosk to the right of the main entrance. The temple itself, at the far end, houses an effigy of Confucius. On the way don't miss the turtles in the colonnades.
We arrived on the day of the school awards ceremony for Children's Day: it was very lively and enjoyable.
Well worth a visit to see a bit of the history of Hanoi. Some of it is reconstructed though so not original but still worth visiting  for an hour so.
It's similar to South Korea.
You absolutely must do it, plus there were photo shoots with girls in traditional costumes, it was magnificent... between the history and the architecture... you can't go to Hanoi and not do it...

This is where the emperors came to pay respects to Confucius and his principals. There are real records in stone if all the people from the traditional nation wide tests that passed the exam. I highly recommend getting the audio Guide as it adds a totally new layer of information that I found really interesting. I would recommend bringing your own pair of 3.5mm headphones because the headphones they provide were kind of gross. English and Vietnamese signs on most things, but obviously not the slabs of stone engravings.

The temple is beautiful, a place to enjoy and relax while contemplating this lovely site. Its ponds, parks, and beautiful architecture—courtyards, rooms—sit down and savor the moment. Authentic Vietnamese culture.
A great place to stop and look round it is very serene and peaceful . Lovely water Lilley  ponds . Very interesting - reading material in English

Buildings featuring traditional Vietnamese architecture. The Temple of Literature was founded around 1070 and dedicated to Confucius; it was the first Vietnamese university. In one of the courtyards, the records of the graduates are engraved on large stone turtles.
It must be worth a visit if they have it on their currency. I just loved watching all the new graduates celebrating the hat toss.

Overall, there aren't a lot of attractions in Hanoi, but the Temple of Literature is definitely worth a visit. You walk through the entrance gate and suddenly find yourself in a small oasis in the middle of the city. The noise level drops dramatically. The various temples and the gardens are in quite good condition. We simply strolled around and took it all in. The descriptions of the writings and figures on display are only minimal and incomplete in English. If you want to know more, you'll probably have to book the audio guide, which we didn't.

This Temple was delightful...many classes of (high school?) graduates came in the day I visited to have photos taken. Lots of youngsters studying English came in to converse with English speakers. Grounds are lovely with ponds and a shrine with Buddha statues.

Pleasant architecture and gardens, which was the first university of Vietnam almost a thousand years ago I recommend to visit the place with a guide or someone able to explain the meaning of what is visited

A place where peace reigns, although only a portion of what was the first university remains, it is well worth a visit. With a good guide, it's a fascinating experience. Remember that initially, only the children of wealthy families could attend the university. Later, to allow access to someone without money, a selection process was held for the best students, and only those who passed the exams were admitted. You can wander through its halls and even see a fine arts student drawing.
At almost 1,000 years old this complex is well worth seeing. It is a garden setting chock full of the living history of the country. Go and enjoy it.

Interesting architecture and history. A major drawback, however, is the audio guides. The tour is narrated by French-speaking teenagers, with significant pronunciation errors and data mistakes, which makes the visit quite disappointing considering the beauty of the buildings. Be aware that the audio guides are more expensive than the entrance fees!
This is a Confucius temple built during the Chinese occupation of Vietnam.
Our guide gave us a very thorough history of Temple of Literature; really interesting background and well kept attraction.

Even though I paid the entrance fee to go in, the actual area to look around is quite small. Of course, when viewed as a whole, it is spacious and offers a glimpse of Vietnamese Confucian style, so it is well worth a visit.
It is a green space surrounded by a wall with temples inside to view, ponds and flowers. 35 to 45 minutes to see and packed with tourists on busses.
If you have some extra time up your sleeve then definitely visit this site. It has some great significance to the history of education.

This temple is dedicated to LEARNING and we happened to visit on the National Teachers day, teachers, kids wearing the best outfits singing songs. The architecture is very unique to Vietnam. As in so many temple you need to immerse yourself in the provided information to fully profit of your visit.
Listening to our guide on the writings of Confucius, it's interesting to observe the reactions of the ladies ;)

A beautiful temple that traces the journey of Chinese and Vietnamese intellectuals and the movement of the philosopher Confucius. You can also see their diplomas (a kind of tombstone), the teachers' uniforms, and their notes.

Elegant gardens and groundsImpressive buildings each has meaning and relevance.The audio descriptions add to your understanding of the importance to the Imperial courtMagnificent Confucian temple as the last building
It's wonderful to hear the whole story and stroll through its gardens and admire its buildings. Highly recommended, a must-see when you visit Hanoi.

This place is far more popular than years ago when I first visited. This may be due to the fact that many students now come here to be photographed after their graduation.This provides god photo opportunities and the ones that can speak English are keen to converse with you.Apart from that, there are many interesting buildings, statues and objects in the grounds. Worth reading up about the 'Temple' to know more about it.Recommended.

A quiet oasis from the outside noise. We were lucky to see students celebrate their graduation ceremony with photos etc. Look out for the fish and butterflies, they are beautiful. Be patient to visit everything as it can get very busy.
The temple complex is very beautiful and manageable in size. You can easily see everything in about an hour.
As soon as I arrived in Hanoi and checked into my accommodation, I went straight to the Temple of Literature
A relatively quiet spot in the bustling center; a group of students made for an interesting visit; the temple building is well reproduced.

A museum right in the city center that attracts huge crowds... A very popular spot for wedding photos and other events... A series of small temples without a Garden of Eden... The lotus is present, but not all the ponds... I discovered a different side of Confucius in this museum where tourism mingles with quiet contemplation in front of magnificent hotels... There's something special about seeing young people praying before these sacred places...
We were surprised by the calmness of this place. Many young people were there for a ceremony.Remember to cover arms and legs before you go into the shrine.

It's a complex of buildings with centuries of history, home to the country's first university, with beautiful, well-maintained gardens and religious imagery. A haven of peace in bustling Hanoi, it's well worth a visit.

no tourist group will miss this place, so be prepared for the crowd. however, the gardens are more quite, as people flock to the buildings, especially the Confuzius shrine. some parts (the last one from the entrance) however is very new, done just some 10 years ago. all together a nice and interesting place a bit out of the hectic city traffic.
Lovely buildings but no literature! The first university of Vietnam holds lots of history and it's a interesting walk round in a quiet area.

The grounds are quite extensive, containing gardens, courtyards and temples to Confucius and his disciples. Note that guards here will be more stringent in enforcing the "cover-up" rule on visitors showing too much skin, and provide free robes for offenders (which are actually pretty cool, designed like medieval Vietnamese scholar robes). The Temple of Literature – National University is a place that always evokes very special feelings in me whenever I return. It's not only a famous historical site in Hanoi, but also a place associated with many personal memories.  In my final years of high school, my friends and I came here to take graduation photos. The ancient setting with its moss-covered tiled roofs, old trees, and red brick courtyard created a unique atmosphere, both solemn and peaceful. Even now, looking back at those graduation photos at the Temple of Literature, they still seem beautiful and full of emotion, preserving a carefree period of my youth.  Furthermore, it's also where I went to pray before my university entrance exams. Standing before the doctoral steles and the ancient academic atmosphere of the Temple of Literature, I always felt a surge of motivation to study. I don't know if it was just my psychology, but the feeling at that time was very special: a mix of nervousness, hope, and a determination to strive to achieve my goals.  The Temple of Literature – National University is not only a famous tourist attraction but also a symbol of the Vietnamese tradition of valuing education and respecting knowledge. Every time I return, I always remember the stressful days of exam preparation, the dreams of my school days, and the memorable moments with my friends.  For me, the Temple of Literature is not just a historical site, but also a place that holds so many memories of my youth. Perhaps that's why every time I pass by, I want to stop for a moment to admire it and recall the most beautiful time of my student life.

Returning to Van Mieu – Quoc Tu Giam in early February 2026, after nearly ten years away, felt like stepping back into a familiar dream. The air carried the gentle chill of late winter in Hanoi, mixed with the scent of incense and the quiet excitement of visitors seeking wisdom and good fortune for the new year. I came to xin chữ from an old calligrapher, just as many generations have done, hoping a few elegant strokes of ink might bring patience and clarity for the year ahead.  Walking through the ancient courtyards, I greeted the statues of Confucius and Chu Van An like respected elders. Their presence remains calm and dignified, watching over the stone steles where the names of Vietnam’s scholars are carved. Each turtle carries stories of perseverance, reminding me how education has shaped this country’s spirit for centuries. I also paused at the space where President Ho Chi Minh once spoke about learning and moral character; it was moving to imagine his voice echoing between these red brick walls.  What touched me most was the atmosphere at dawn. Soft light filtered through the old trees, and young people in colorful áo dài took photos among peach blossoms and spring decorations. Their laughter blended with the sound of temple bells, creating a scene both timeless and vividly alive. As someone from the South who rarely visits Hanoi, I felt grateful to witness this harmony between history and modern life.  Van Mieu is more than a tourist site; it is a living symbol of respect for knowledge. Even after a decade, the place has not lost its quiet power. Instead, it welcomed me back like an old friend, reminding me of student days, of dreams once made, and of the long road still ahead. If you come to Hanoi in early spring, give yourself a slow morning here—you may leave with lighter thoughts and a deeper love for Vietnam’s soul.

In 1484, with the aim of promoting Confucianism and honoring Confucian scholars who passed the imperial examinations, King Le Thanh Tong ordered the erection of the first doctoral steles at the Temple of Literature for the imperial examinations held in previous years (from 1442) of the Later Le Dynasty.  The doctoral steles at the Temple of Literature were erected over a period of nearly 300 years (from 1484 to 1780), inscribed with texts listing the names of Vietnamese Confucian scholars who passed the imperial examinations from 1442 to 1779.  In the oldest stele, from the 1442 Nham Tuat examination, Mr. Than Nhan Trung wrote: “Talented people are the lifeblood of the nation; when the lifeblood is strong, the nation is powerful and prospers; when the lifeblood is weak, the nation is weak and declines. Therefore, no wise emperor or king has ever failed to prioritize cultivating talent, selecting scholars, and nurturing the nation's lifeblood. Scholars are so important to the country that their value is immeasurable.”  But that is only half the meaning of erecting the steles. The other half, unfortunately, is rarely mentioned or noticed, thus failing to fully understand the intentions of our ancestors. Besides praising and encouraging, the stele at the Temple of Literature also serves as a strict admonition and reminder to those who have passed examinations and those pursuing further education. On the 1442 Nhâm Tuất stele, Thân Nhân Trung wrote: “Let us review the names of those who passed this examination. Many have contributed to the peace and stability of the state through literature and governance, earning the nation's trust for decades. However, there are also those who have become corrupt due to bribery or fallen into the ranks of evildoers, perhaps because they did not see this stele (as it was erected in 1484 – 42 years after the examination). If they had seen it in their time, their hearts would surely be filled with goodness, and their evil intentions would surely be suppressed; how could they dare to arise in such a way? Thus, the erection of this stele is incredibly beneficial; the wicked will take it as a warning, the virtuous will follow it to strive, it guides the past, shows the way to the future, both to cultivate the integrity of scholars and to strengthen the nation's destiny. Anyone who sees this stele should understand this profound meaning!”  The stele commemorating the doctoral graduates of the Quy Mui year (1463) warns: “Those scholars who wish to have their names inscribed on this stele must strive to ensure their reputation matches their merit, cultivating virtue… so as not to betray the good intentions of the court in bestowing praise, and not to betray the noble aspirations of serving the king and helping the people, leaving behind a lasting legacy… So that future generations, upon seeing this stele, will say: This person was utterly loyal to the country, this person bestowed favors upon the people, this person was upright and righteous, this person upheld virtue and achieved merit. Truly an honor. Otherwise, people will look at it and say: A treacherous villain, a disloyal and cowardly man. Public opinion is clear; how can one not be cautious?!”  All the stele commemorating the doctoral graduates contain similar warnings. “If one merely uses the prestige of passing the examination to seek wealth and comfort, taking that path as a shortcut to becoming an official, then future generations will call them treacherous and wicked, thus bringing disgrace to the scholarly tradition” (Stele of the doctoral graduates of the Dinh Suu year (1577)).  We often criticize the old-fashioned examination system, which heavily emphasized rote memorization and quotations, but the true learning system of the past always valued those who made practical contributions to the country, demanding substance that matched the title.  "If one has only a reputation without substance... then they are merely a rotten scholar, a petty person, condemned by the world, despised by the people, a stain on the reputation of a great scholar" (Inscription of the Quy Hoi Examination, 1623).

The Temple of Literature in Hanoi is truly a place where history, culture, and serenity come together in perfect harmony. Often described as Vietnam’s first national university, this ancient complex offers a deep insight into the country’s scholarly traditions and Confucian values.  As you step through the grand entrance, you’re welcomed by beautifully maintained courtyards, traditional Vietnamese architecture, and peaceful lotus ponds. Each section of the temple feels like a journey back in time. The stone steles, engraved with the names of ancient scholars, stand proudly as symbols of academic excellence and dedication.  What truly impressed me was the calm and spiritual atmosphere within the temple grounds. Despite being a popular tourist attraction, the space retains a sense of purity and quiet dignity. The red wooden pavilions, intricately carved details, and the iconic Khue Van Pavilion make the entire complex visually stunning.  Walking through the corridors, I could almost feel the presence of the scholars who once studied here. The mix of cultural significance and architectural beauty makes the Temple of Literature a must-visit destination in Hanoi. It’s a place where one can slow down, reflect, and appreciate Vietnam’s rich intellectual heritage.  Whether you’re a history enthusiast, a lover of architecture, or simply seeking a peaceful spot in the heart of the city, the Temple of Literature will leave you inspired and enriched.

The Temple of Literature was built in 1070, the second year of the Than Vu era of Ly Thanh Tong. Dai Viet History Chronicle, Social Sciences Publishing House, Hanoi, volume 1, page 234, records: "In the autumn of the tenth month, the Temple of Literature was built, statues of Confucius, Zhou Gong and the Four Disciples were erected, and paintings of the Seventy-Two Sages were made. The Crown Prince came there to study." [2] Thus, besides the function of worshipping the ancient sages and masters of Confucianism, the Temple of Literature also had the function of a royal school, whose first student was Crown Prince Ly Can Duc, son of King Ly Thanh Tong and Empress Y Lan, who was only 5 years old at the time. In 1072, he ascended the throne to become King Ly Nhan Tong.  In 1076, Ly Nhan Tong established the National Academy next to the Temple of Literature, which can be considered the first university in Vietnam. Initially, the school was exclusively for the children of the king and the children of high-ranking officials (hence the name Quoc Tu - National Academy). (Viet Su Thong Giam Cuong Muc, Publishing House of Literature, History and Geography, 1957) records: "In the year of Binh Thin, the first month of the year of Anh Vu Chieu Thang... the Quoc Tu Giam was established; those with literary talent were selected from among the civil officials and appointed to it." In 1156, Ly Anh Tong had the Temple of Literature renovated and only worshipped Confucius.  In 1253, King Tran Thai Tong changed Quoc Tu Giam to Quoc Hoc Vien, expanding it and admitting children of commoners with outstanding academic abilities. The function of the Quoc Hoc school became increasingly prominent, surpassing its function as a place of worship: "In the third year of Quy Suu (1253)... In June, Quoc Hoc Vien was established, statues of Confucius, Zhou Gong and the Second Sage were painted, and statues of 72 virtuous people were drawn for worship... In September, a decree was issued for Confucian scholars throughout the country to come to Quoc Hoc Vien to teach the Four Books and Six Classics" (Dai Viet Su Ky Toan Thu). Pham Ung Than was appointed Minister and concurrently Head of the National Academy to oversee the studies at the National Academy. During the reign of Tran Minh Tong, Chu Van An was appointed as the Headmaster of the National Academy and the direct teacher of the princes. He died in 1370 and was enshrined in the Temple of Literature alongside Confucius by King Tran Nghe Tong. During the Later Le Dynasty, Confucianism flourished. In 1484, Le Thanh Tong erected steles commemorating those who passed the doctoral examinations from the 1442 examination onwards (a plan proposed in 1442 but not yet implemented). Each examination had a stele placed on the back of a turtle. By that year, the Le Dynasty had organized 12 high-level examinations. Le Thanh Tong (1460 – 1497) regularly held them every three years, exactly 12 examinations. Not every examination was immediately inscribed with a stele after completion, and not every stele erected remained intact, undamaged, and unlost. Different periods had their own variations. Major reconstructions and renovations took place, such as in 1653 (Thinh Duc year 1) and 1717 (Vinh Thinh year 13).  During the late Le Dynasty, specifically the Canh Hung era, steles were regularly inscribed. Although not all steles remain, the sculptural works and valuable historical documents are still preserved.  In 1762, Le Hien Tong ordered its renovation into the Quoc Tu Giam (National Academy), a high-level training and education institution of the court. During the Canh Hung era, Vu Mien, Nguyen Nghiem, and Nguyen Le commissioned the casting of the Bich Ung Dai Chung (Bich Ung Great Bell).  During the Nguyen Dynasty, the Quoc Tu Giam was established in Hue. In 1802, Emperor Gia Long designated it as the Hanoi Temple of Literature. The Governor-General of Bac Thanh, Nguyen Van Thanh, had the Khue Van Cac (Khue Van Pavilion) built next to the square well. Thus, at the beginning of the Nguyen Dynasty, the Thang Long Temple of Literature was renovated once, becoming the Temple of Literature of the Bac Thanh region, before being renamed the Hanoi Temple of Literature. The Quoc Tu Giam was later transformed into a school for the Hoai Duc prefecture and subsequently located in the area. This temple was built here to worship Confucius' parents.  In early 1947, the French colonialists shelled the building, causing it to collapse, leaving only the foundation with two stone pillars and four stone inkstones. Today, the entire Thai Hoc complex, built on an area of ​​1530 m² within a total area of ​​6150 m², includes the main architectural structures: the Front Hall, Rear Hall, Left Wing, Right Wing, bell house, and drum house, modeled after traditional architecture on the former site of the National Academy.

The Temple of Literature (Văn Miếu – Quốc Tử Giám) is an absolute must-see in Hanoi, offering a beautiful and tranquil escape from the city's hustle. This isn't just a temple; it is the site of Vietnam's first national university, founded in 1070, and it wonderfully captures the nation's profound and enduring respect for education. ​Walking through the five courtyards, you are treated to stunning, well-preserved traditional Vietnamese architecture, especially the iconic Khue Van Cac (Constellation of Literature Pavilion), which is so significant it's the symbol of Hanoi! The shaded green gardens and the central Well of Heavenly Clarity provide a genuinely peaceful atmosphere. Be sure to see the 82 ancient Doctoral Stelae, set upon stone turtles, which honor the successful graduates of royal exams—a moving testament to centuries of scholarship. ​It is a rich, calming, and deeply meaningful historical site that beautifully tells the story of Vietnam's cultural heritage.

The Temple of Literature - Imperial Academy is a complex of historical and cultural relics in Hanoi. Built in 1070 under the reign of Ly Thanh Tong, it was originally a Temple of Literature, worshiping Confucius. In 1076, the Imperial Academy was established - the first university in Vietnam, for the children of kings and mandarins, and later expanded to talented people from common people. The Temple of Literature - Imperial Academy is about 700m long, divided into 5 consecutive areas along the north-south axis: The Temple of Literature Gate: a three-door gate, traditional style, ancient tiled roof, signboard "The Temple of Literature Gate". The Great Middle Area - the Lake of Literature - the Khue Van Pavilion: a square lake symbolizing the harmony of heaven and earth. The Khue Van Pavilion: a symbol of Hanoi, built in 1805, symbolizing the brilliance of academia. The Doctorate Stele Area: includes 82 stone steles, engraved with the names of 1,307 doctors from the examinations from 1442-1779. each stele is placed on the back of a stone turtle - a symbol of longevity and wisdom. UNESCO recognized it as a world documentary heritage (2011). Dai Thanh Palace: worships Confucius, Tu Phoi (4 excellent students), and Thap Phiet. Thai Hoc area: restored in 2000, displays the history of Vietnamese education, worship statues: Chu Van An, the kings who founded Confucianism: Ly Thanh Tong, Ly Nhan Tong, Le Thanh Tong. Van Mieu - Quoc Tu Giam is a symbol of the tradition of learning, a national monument and an important tourist destination.

The Temple of Literature – National University is one of the most historically and culturally significant sites, embodying the Vietnamese people's spirit of learning. Upon entering, the first impression is one of solemnity, antiquity, and tranquility, completely separate from the hustle and bustle of the city. The architecture is harmonious, weathered by time, with each area clearly arranged, reflecting the depth of its centuries-old history.  The area of ​​the Doctoral Steles is particularly impressive, not only aesthetically pleasing but also deeply meaningful in its tradition of respecting teachers and honoring talent. Strolling through the grounds with its many trees and ponds is very pleasant and relaxing, perfect for sightseeing, taking photos, or simply pausing to reflect.  The preservation and guided tours are well-organized, helping visitors better understand the history and role of the Temple of Literature – National University in ancient Vietnamese education. This is a very worthwhile place to visit, especially for students and those who love culture and history. It absolutely deserves 5 stars.

The Temple of Literature is a unique and meaningful landmark that beautifully reflects the history of Vietnamese civilization, especially the legacy of its kings, scholars, and the importance of education. As Vietnam’s first national university, the site offers a deep cultural narrative through its courtyards, stelae, and traditional architecture.  Visiting near Tết (Lunar New Year) adds a special charm. The atmosphere feels festive and symbolic, with many people—especially young visitors—wearing traditional Vietnamese attire (áo dài). It’s common to see couples, friends, and colleagues taking photos together as part of the tradition to welcome the new year with good hopes, knowledge, and prosperity.  This seasonal moment transforms the Temple of Literature into more than a historical site—it becomes a living cultural space, where history, tradition, and modern life meet. Overall, it’s a must-visit place in Hanoi, particularly around Lunar New Year, to truly experience Vietnam’s respect for education, heritage, and cultural continuity.

It’s a place that invites you to slow down, learn, and simply be present. Definitely one of my favorite spots in Hanoi, especially if you enjoy history, culture, and quiet moments on your own.

Temple which showcases the importance of education in traditional Vietnamese culture. The Temple of Literature is truly one of the most beautiful and peaceful places I visited in Hanoi. Built in 1070, it is known as Vietnam’s first national university, dedicated to Confucius and the values of knowledge, discipline, and moral character. Temple of Literature – Imperial Academy: Where Time Stands Still in the Heart of the Capital Amidst the hustle and bustle of modern Hanoi, there is a space where, upon entering the Temple of Literature Gate, one suddenly feels life slow down. That is the Temple of Literature – Imperial Academy, a sacred place where a thousand years of civilization converge.  1. The Beauty of Serenity and Knowledge Stepping through Khue Van Cac – a symbol of the brilliance of knowledge – we encounter the image of 82 stone steles bearing the names of Doctoral laureates, standing majestically on the backs of stone turtles. Each stele is not just a list, but a story about the efforts and the people who used "scholarship" to serve the nation. Looking at the silent stone turtles, we understand that: "Talented people are the lifeblood of the nation."  2. The Interplay Between Past and Present The beauty of the Imperial Academy lies in the fact that it is not a "dead museum." It remains vibrant and full of life. These are images of students before each exam coming to pray for good luck, and the flowing ao dai dresses of university students on graduation day. The red of the gilded wood, the mossy color of the roof tiles seem to blend with the aspirations of today's young generation.  3. A Quiet Moment for the Soul Strolling beneath the centuries-old trees, inhaling the faint scent of incense from the main hall, we find an extraordinary sense of peace. The Temple of Literature not only teaches us about knowledge, but also about morality, about respecting teachers and elders – core values ​​that remain unchanged in any era.  Conclusion: The Temple of Literature – National University is not just a tourist destination, but a journey back to our roots. Each person, upon leaving, will have a small flame of love for knowledge and national pride ignited in their heart.

The Temple of Literature – National University is a famous historical complex in Hanoi. The complex includes the Văn Lake, the Văn Miếu – National University area, and the Giám Garden. The main structures are the Temple of Literature – dedicated to Confucius, and the National University – Vietnam's first university. From the outside in, the gates are: Văn Miếu Gate, Đại Trung Gate, Khuê Văn Pavilion, Đại Thành Gate, and Thái Học Gate. With over 700 years of operation, it has trained thousands of talented individuals for the country. Today, the Temple of Literature – National University is a popular tourist destination for both domestic and international visitors. It is also a place where outstanding students are honored and where an annual poetry festival is held on the 15th day of the first lunar month. Notably, it is also a place where students come to pray for good luck before important exams.

A Brief Introduction to the Temple of Literature and National University Complex The Temple of Literature and National University is known as one of Vietnam's first universities and the source of the later tradition of studiousness.  "Talented people are the lifeblood of the nation," and true to that saying, in August 1070, during the reign of King Ly Thanh Tong, the Temple of Literature and National University was begun with the initial purpose of worshipping Confucius, the founder of Confucianism, and also as the first school for princes. This school is also associated with the exemplary teacher Chu Van An, a revered figure for generations.  Spreading across an area of ​​over 54,000 square meters, the Temple of Literature includes famous architectural structures such as Ho Van Lake, the Van Mieu Gate, Dai Trung Mon Gate, Khue Van Cac, Thien Quang Well, the area of ​​the Doctoral Stele, Dai Thanh Mon Gate, and the Thai Hoc House. Both the architecture of the Nguyen Dynasty and Eastern culture seem to be miniaturized and contained within this place. Visitors can easily spot images of two dragons flanking the sun and moon, dragons, mythical creatures, and turtles everywhere. And as always, during the Lunar New Year, people flock here to pick lucky branches, ask calligraphers for auspicious blessings, and hope for a year filled with good fortune and prosperity for their families.

As a history enthusiast, I often visit ancient sites of worship, but this complex in the capital offered a genuinely surprising and enriching experience. What initially appears to be a beautifully preserved place dedicated to Confucius quickly reveals itself to be something far more profound: the nation's first national university, established nearly a thousand years ago. Frankly, this fact alone—that this country founded a formal, centralized institution of higher education back in the 11th century—was a moment of genuine awe and admiration. It immediately speaks volumes about the early civilization and intellectual value placed on learning and meritocracy here.  The architecture is serene and balanced, guiding visitors through five distinct courtyards, each representing a step toward scholarly achievement. The most impactful feature for me was the third courtyard, which contains the 82 well-preserved Stone Stelae of Doctors. These giant stone turtles, each carrying a stele inscribed with the names and birthplaces of successful scholars, are not just monuments; they are a tangible, historical ledger of academic success spanning over three centuries. This tradition offers remarkable insight into the rigor and prestige of the ancient examinations. Visiting this location is not merely sightseeing; it is stepping directly into the heart of this nation’s enduring commitment to learning and intellectual merit. It’s an essential stop for anyone wanting to grasp the true historical depth and the sophisticated foundation of this country.

🏛️ Introduction & history • The Temple of Literature – Imperial Academy was built in 1070 under the reign of Ly Thanh Tong — originally a place to worship Confucius and other Confucian sages, and a place of study for princes. ￼ • In 1076, the first academy in Vietnam — the Imperial Academy — was built next to the Temple of Literature, to train princes, children of noble families. ￼ • Through the dynasties — Tran, Le, Nguyen — the Temple of Literature – Imperial Academy played the role of an educational and cultural center: from 1253, it expanded to recruit more talented commoners, and during the Le Dynasty, it erected a doctoral stele (from the 1442 examination) to honor outstanding students. ￼ • This place preserves 82 doctoral stone steles — recording the names and examinations of thousands of doctors — symbolizing the tradition of learning and examinations in ancient Vietnam. ￼  📐 Architecture & structure of the relic  Van Mieu – Quoc Tu Giam — a mostly ancient complex, including many valuable areas & structures: • There are courtyards, gates, stele houses, church buildings, gardens, lakes, trees — creating a solemn, quiet space in the heart of the capital. ￼ • The most prominent is Khue Van Cac — with 8-story roofs, round windows, ancient wooden roof — becoming a cultural symbol, a symbol of Hanoi. ￼ • The stele house area is located next to the lake (Thien Quoc Tu Giam: Symbol of wisdom and tradition of learning Quoc Tu Giam, also known as Van Mieu - Quoc Tu Giam, is not only a historical relic but also a symbol of the spirit of learning and the respect for talents of the Vietnamese people. This is the first university in Vietnam, the birthplace of thousands of talented people for the country through many dynasties.  Space and Architecture Quoc Tu Giam is divided into 5 main areas, each area has its own meaning and architecture:  Ho Van: A clear lake, creating harmony and peace as soon as you enter.  Vuon Giam: A cool green garden, an ideal place to walk and feel the serenity.  Van Mieu Mon, Dai Trung Mon: These are the entrance gates, designed in ancient architecture, showing solemnity and respect.  Khue Van Cac: This is the symbol of Quoc Tu Giam and also the symbol of the capital Hanoi. Unique design with the image of "Khuê star pavilion" symbolizing the brilliance of knowledge. This is a check-in spot not to be missed.  Doctor's Stele House Area: Displaying 82 stone steles recording the names of doctors who passed the exams from the 15th to the 18th century. Each stele is placed on the back of a large stone turtle, symbolizing longevity and sustainability. Admiring these steles helps you better understand Vietnam's tradition of examinations.  Interesting things to do when visiting Learning about history: This is a great place for you to learn about ancient Confucian education. Every corner of the architecture, every stone stele contains meaningful historical stories.  Quiet space: Although it is a famous tourist destination, Quoc Tu Giam still maintains its solemnity and quietness. You can take time to walk, contemplate and feel the ancient atmosphere of this place.  Photo shoot location: With ancient architecture and green campus, this is one of the ideal photo shoot locations, especially during Tet and exam periods.  Advantages and disadvantages Advantages:  Cheap ticket price: The ticket price is only about 30,000 VND, very reasonable.  Central location: Located right in the city center, easy to travel by many means.  Deep historical significance: A place to educate national pride and the tradition of respecting teachers.  Disadvantages:  Crowded: On weekends, holidays or exam season, the number of visitors and students coming to offer incense is very large.  Lack of tour guides: If you want to understand more about each area, you should hire a tour guide or find out in advance online.  Quoc Tu Giam is not only a relic to visit but also an experience for you to meditate and reflect on the good cultural and historical values of the nation.

Review of the Temple of Literature – National University (Hanoi):  The Temple of Literature – National University is a destination with significant historical and cultural value, especially for those interested in learning about Vietnam's ancient education system. Upon arrival, you'll easily spot many students and parents visiting, seeking calligraphy, and praying for good luck in their studies, especially during exam periods.  The ancient atmosphere and spacious grounds, with their doctoral steles, lake, and greenery, create a solemn and peaceful feeling. Without a guide or prior preparation, it might feel a bit monotonous.  The hot weather, especially in summer, is a minor drawback, as the area has many open spaces and few shaded areas. Nevertheless, it's still a place worth visiting to learn more about Vietnam's educational history and knowledge.  Review of the Temple of Literature – Quoc Tu Giam (Hanoi):  The Temple of Literature – Quoc Tu Giam is a historical and cultural site, especially appealing to those interested in Vietnam’s traditional education system. You’ll often see many students and their parents visiting, especially around exam season, to pray for good luck and academic success.  The site offers a peaceful and solemn atmosphere, with ancient architecture, stone steles of doctors, ponds, and shady trees. However, the overall experience may not be very memorable unless you’re particularly keen on history. Without a tour guide or some prior research, the visit might feel a bit plain — mostly walking, sightseeing, and taking photos. **The Temple of Literature – Imperial Academy** (*Temple of Literature – Quoc Tu Giam*) is one of the most significant historical and cultural landmarks of Hanoi and Vietnam.  ✅ **Strengths:**  * **Great historical and cultural value:** The Temple of Literature was built in 1070 during the reign of King Ly Thanh Tong to worship Confucius and respected sages. In 1076, the Imperial Academy was established here, becoming Vietnam's first national university, where many talented scholars were educated. * **Ancient, distinctive architecture:** The complex features many structures, including Khue Van Pavilion, the Well of Heavenly Clarity, Doctor's Stone Steles, courtyards, and worship halls. All are harmoniously arranged in a tranquil space reflecting traditional Vietnamese architecture. * **Educational significance:** This is a symbol of Vietnam’s long-standing respect for learning and moral values. Every year, many students and families come to offer incense and ask for calligraphy blessings before important exams. * **Beautiful setting for sightseeing and photography:** The green gardens, historic buildings, and peaceful atmosphere make it an appealing spot for visitors who love culture, history, and photography.  ✅ **Some limitations:**  * **Can be crowded during peak times:** On holidays, festivals, or exam seasons, the site gets very busy, which can feel noisy and overwhelming. * **Limited explanatory information:** Without a guide, it may be difficult to fully appreciate the historical and cultural significance of each structure.  🎯 **Overall evaluation:** The Temple of Literature – Imperial Academy is an especially important historical and cultural monument, both sacred and a testament to Vietnam's tradition of valuing education. It is a highly recommended destination to explore the culture, education, and history of Hanoi.  ---  Below is an **objective assessment** of the Temple of Literature – Imperial Academy, one of the most representative historical and cultural relics of Hanoi and Vietnam:  ✅ **Advantages:**  * **Immense historical and cultural value:** The Temple of Literature was built in 1070 during the reign of Ly Thanh Tong, initially to worship Confucius and other sages. Not only were there Vietnamese students, but I also saw many foreign tourists eagerly discovering the heritage site. Some visitors were happily engaging in conversations with local students, creating a lively and friendly cultural exchange atmosphere. The Temple of Literature (Văn Miếu) is more than just a monument; it's Hanoi's calming breath amid the chaos of motorized traffic. If you're expecting the gold and opulence of Chinese imperial temples, you'll be surprised by its elegant sobriety and its red bricks that seem to absorb the city's noise.  An Oasis of Geometry and Greenery As soon as you step through the entrance gate, the first thing you notice is the shift in frequency. Hanoi outside screams, while inside the Temple it whispers. It's structured into five straight-line courtyards: a design that almost guides you by the hand toward the heart of wisdom. The gardens are meticulously maintained, with centuries-old trees and ponds that reflect traditional architecture.  The Turtles: Guardians of Knowledge The most striking area is that of the 82 stone steles held up by giant turtles. Engraved above are the names of the doctors who passed the royal examinations between 1442 and 1779. There's something moving about these "monuments to hard work": they remind us that, in Vietnam, culture and study have always been considered the highest form of nobility. ​Fun fact: Students once stroked the heads of turtles for good luck in exams, but today it's forbidden (and rightly so, to preserve them!).  ​Architecture that never ages ​The Pavilion of the Constellation of Literature (Khue Van Cac) is the city's symbol. It's small, made of red wood, with a circular window framing the sky. It's proof that beauty doesn't need colossal proportions to take your breath away. ​ ​If you love history, architectural photography, and want to understand Vietnam's Confucian soul, it's also the perfect place to see local graduates taking photos in traditional dress (Ao Dai).  I liked it very much!!!!!

It's boring if you don't know, but it's fun if you do.  A record of a conversation with Gemini  🏛️ A comprehensive guide to visiting the Hanoi Temple of Literature (Gukjagam) 1. The meaning of water and the pond (Cheonggwangjeong) The square pond in the center symbolizes "land," containing the light of the sky. It embodies the philosophy of purifying knowledge and purifying the mind.  2. Stele on the back of a turtle (Jinsa Jemyeongbi) This is a list of successful candidates for the civil service examination from 1442 to 1779. The turtle symbolizes "eternal wisdom." (※ The turtle's head is currently untouchable for protection, so please only look at it with your eyes!)  3. Statue of a turtle and a crane The two symbolize "eternal friendship." Legend has it that they helped each other during droughts and floods. This embodies the Vietnamese tradition of wishing for academic success and good health.  4. The secret of the high threshold You often bow your head to look down, right? This symbolizes humility before the sages, as well as a practical purpose: preventing flooding.  5. Bell and Drum (Thai Hac District) This bell symbolizes the beginning of class and authority in the past. The "bell on the left, drum on the right" arrangement symbolizes the harmony of the universe. It was magnificently restored in 2000 to commemorate the relocation of the capital to Hanoi.  6. Yellow Flower "Oncidium" (Dancing Lady) Yellow orchids scattered throughout the Temple of Literature symbolize the nobility of the royal family and the integrity of scholars. They are a symbol of good luck and prosperity.  💡 Visiting Tips  Look for "Gyumun Pavilion" on the back of the 100,000 VND note!  The central path was originally the King's Path, so walk along it while feeling the royal aura.  Ascend to the second floor of the Tai Hac Building for a panoramic view of the entire Temple of Literature.

Confucian culture permeates the world, cultivating ethics and benevolence. 2025.10.14 💐Temple of Literature - Imperial College: Considered Vietnam's first university, it is located at the center of four bustling streets in Đống Đa District. Spanning approximately 54,331 square meters, it features lush greenery and dotted with ponds, and its antique architecture creates a tranquil and peaceful space. Nearby are President Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum, Thăng Long Imperial Citadel, the Vietnam Museum of Fine Arts, and Hỏa Lò Prison. 🪷Historical Value: It retains its ancient and solemn appearance after millennia. Constructed in 1070 during the reign of Emperor Lý Thánh Tông, the Temple of Literature houses Confucius, Chu Công, and the Four Companions. In 1076, Emperor Lý Nhân Tông ordered the construction of the Imperial Academy next to the Temple of Literature to educate the emperor's sons and the children of powerful families. During the reign of Emperor Trần Thái Tông, the Imperial Academy was renamed the National Academy, enrolling students of commoner origins but with outstanding academic performance. During the reign of Emperor Lê Thánh Tông, monuments were erected to honor outstanding individuals and distinguished individuals. 🪷 Cultural Value: In 1962, it was designated a national cultural relic. Eighty-two of the monuments have been designated UNESCO Memory of the World Heritage. As a place of veneration for national sages and talents, it was Vietnam's first center for education and the imperial examination system, and a testament to the nation's historical heritage. The preservation and collection of precious cultural relics of our ancestors, such as calligraphy and exquisite carving techniques, are evident in stone tablets, horizontal plaques, and other artifacts. 🪷 Ancient buildings in the center of the street: A complex steeped in traditional influences, built along a north-south axis, imitating the structure of ancient palaces. Adhering to Eastern Feng Shui principles, reflecting the philosophy of Yin and Yang and the Five Elements, the site creates harmony between man and nature. 1. Overall Layout: Divided into five zones, the site reflects the various stages of the imperial examination process during the feudal era. *1: Văn Lake and Tam Quan - Spaces that guide visitors into the complex. *2: Khuê Văn Pavilion - A symbol of Hanoi, a place honoring knowledge. *3: Jinshi Monument Courtyard - Houses 82 stone tablets recording the names of successful candidates. *4: Dacheng Hall - An area dedicated to Confucius and other sages. *5: Taixue District - A place for cultivating and celebrating Confucian education. 2. Yin and Yang Harmony: Văn Lake (water - Yin) combines with the architectural elements (earth - Yang) to create spatial balance and harmony. The Five Elements are: Wood (sandalwood for pillars and beams), Earth (Bát Tràng bricks for walls and paving), Fire (red tile roofs, primary decorative accents), Metal (decorative patterns, bronze bells), and Water (well systems and ponds). 🪷Dacheng Gate: Contains three rooms, painted red with dragon and cloud patterns, with two rows of columns in front and back, and a central row of columns. 🪷Taixue Hall: Once used for lectures and training. Now dedicated to Chu Văn An, it houses valuable educational materials from the feudal era. 🪷Dabai Hall: Constructed with nine rooms, it is flanked by corridors. 🪷Khải Thánh Temple: Located at the rear, it is dedicated to Confucius' parents. It once housed 150 rooms for students. It was completely destroyed by French bombardment in 1946, but was later rebuilt and remains intact to this day. 🫡Note: Dress modestly and conservatively. Avoid wearing clothing that is too short or overly revealing.

A Bubble of Serenity and History in the Heart of Hanoi The Temple of Literature is not just a monument; it's a true encounter with the cultural and spiritual soul of Vietnam. As soon as you step through its gates, the noise and bustle of Hanoi fade away, giving way to a profound peace, a bubble of serenity that invites meditation and exploration. This site, which housed Vietnam's first national university (the Imperial Academy), founded in 1070 to honor Confucius and scholars, testifies to the country's deep respect for knowledge and tradition. The traditional architecture, with its successive courtyards, curved roofs, and finely crafted wooden beams, is absolutely magnificent and remarkably well-preserved. The highlight of the visit is undoubtedly the discovery of the stone stelae, mounted on the backs of sculpted tortoises. These inscriptions honor the graduates of the imperial examinations and serve as a reminder of the crucial importance of education in Vietnamese history, a timeless symbol of perseverance and success. The landscaped gardens, adorned with ponds (including the "Well of Heavenly Clarity") and centuries-old trees, offer an idyllic setting for relaxation. The atmosphere is both solemn and serene, a place where history is palpable at every turn.  In short, a visit to the Temple of Literature is an unforgettable experience that enriches the mind and soothes the soul. It is an absolute must for anyone wishing to understand the essence of Vietnamese heritage. Allow approximately 1.5 hours to fully immerse yourself in this magical place, and remember to dress respectfully, as it remains a sacred site. An exceptional visit, steeped in culture and history, which I highly recommend!

My visit to Hanoi was nothing short of incredible! The city is a perfect mix of old-world charm and modern vibrancy. Walking through the Old Quarter felt like stepping back in time, with its narrow streets, ancient pagodas, and colonial buildings.
