I couldn’t get enough of the street food—pho, bun cha, and the amazing egg coffee were highlights of my trip. Each meal was an adventure, and the locals were always happy to recommend their favorite spots.While the traffic can be overwhelming at first, I quickly adapted and even found it exciting to cross the busy streets alongside the locals. The energy of the city is contagious, and there’s always something happening, whether it’s a bustling market or a peaceful morning by Hoan Kiem Lake. Hanoi left me with unforgettable memories, and I can’t wait to return someday!

After visiting the Temple of Literature (Văn Miếu Quốc Tử Giám), I was deeply impressed by the bustling atmosphere and the ancient beauty of the site. Right from the entrance, there was a large crowd, particularly groups of students from schools inside and outside Hanoi, coming to explore and learn. They gathered in front of the pavilions, attentively listening to the guides introducing the long-standing history of the Temple, once the largest educational center in feudal Vietnam. Not only were there Vietnamese students, but I also saw many foreign tourists eagerly discovering the heritage site. Some visitors were happily engaging in conversations with local students, creating a lively and friendly cultural exchange atmosphere. Within the Temple’s grounds, an art exhibition was being held in celebration of Hanoi's Liberation Day. The paintings vividly depicted the heroic history of Hanoi, from the resistance against the French to the modern era. They were skillfully arranged within the serene, ancient space of the traditional pavilions. The solemn setting, combined with the artful displays, further highlighted the beauty of the Temple, both ancient and modern. With its unique architecture, large shady trees, and the peaceful ambiance of the surroundings, the Temple of Literature is truly a must-visit destination to experience Vietnam's culture and history.

We strolled through the quiet gardens and were exposed to ancient scholarship. I was impressed by the beautiful architecture and took many photos. The Temple of Literature - National University in Hanoi is an important historical site, built in the 11th century to worship Confucius and served as Vietnam's first university. On June 23, 2023, I visited the Temple of Literature and Imperial College in Hanoi, Vietnam. It felt like visiting Chinese cultural architecture; if it weren't for the many Vietnamese students, I would have thought I was in Taiwan. As the tour guide said, Confucius had a great influence on Southeast Asia, especially North Vietnam, which bordered China. During the era when Vietnam had to pay tribute to China, a dominant culture inevitably invaded. Although Vietnam was colonized by France and now has its own written language, the Temple of Literature and Imperial College still exist, and students still come to pray for success in their exams. With the major exams approaching in June, many students come to pray.

The Temple of Literature & National University in Hanoi is a must-visit destination for anyone interested in Vietnamese history and culture. Built in 1070, this ancient temple was dedicated to Confucius and served as Vietnam's first university for over 700 years.Walking through the temple's well-preserved architecture and tranquil gardens, you can't help but feel a sense of awe and reverence for the scholars who once studied here. The stone tablets inscribed with the names of past graduates are a testament to the temple's rich academic history.The temple is also a great spot for taking beautiful photos, especially during the spring when the cherry blossoms are in bloom. Don't forget to visit the gift shop, where you can find unique souvenirs and traditional Vietnamese handicrafts.Overall, the Temple of Literature & National University is an impressive and educational attraction that should not be missed on any trip to Hanoi.

Welcome to the "Temple of Literature" ("Van Miêu") and to the "Imperial Academy" ("Quóc Tù Giám")! The temple dates from 1070, was built during the reign of Emperor Lý Thánh Tông and dedicated to Confucius and famous scholars. The academy dates from 1076 and was the very first and largest university in Vietnam until 1779. Visit its pavilions, gates, courtyards, gardens, ponds and altars! The courtyards are numbered from one to five. The first two ("Dai Trung Môn" and "Khuê Vân Các") include gates and beautiful gardens. At the third courtyard ("Thien Quang"), a nice pond known as the "Well of Heavenly Clarity". The fourth couryard ("Dai Thàn Môn") houses the "Hall of Ceremonies" and the "Sanctuary to Confucious". Finally, at the fifth courtyard, the grounds of the "Imperial Academy" ("Quóc Tù Giám") and altars honoring famous professors (on the ground floor) and ancient kings (at the upper floor). Unmissable at any visit to Hanoi! Belore going, check visiting hours (as they may change seasonally) and dress properly for a temple. Enjoy!

To put it simply — hypocrisy. The great history of Vietnam and the revolutionaries tortured for their ideals in this prison — that's a great achievement, but here they've turned it into a tourist attraction. My grandfather was a prisoner of war at Hoa Lo for five and a half years.  He was a U.S. Air Force pilot, shot down twice during the Vietnam War. While he was held here, my family didn’t know if he was alive. He missed the first five years of my mother’s life—not because he chose to, but because he was imprisoned, tortured, starved, and subjected to relentless psychological abuse. He was never allowed to receive letters or packages, despite what this museum claims.  Growing up, we heard his stories. They weren’t embellished. They were matter-of-fact accounts of survival. After his death 12 years ago, I came here hoping to connect with him by walking through a place that defined so much of his life.  Instead, I felt nothing—because the story told here erases him.  Hoa Lo presents a deeply selective version of history. When Vietnamese prisoners were held here, the museum rightly portrays brutality and cruelty. But when American POWs were imprisoned, the narrative abruptly shifts to one of humane treatment, leisure, and even fondness—as if they were happy guests who later returned out of nostalgia.  That version of history is false.  The treatment did not change based on nationality. The suffering did not soften. To suggest otherwise is not only inaccurate—it is disrespectful to the men who endured years of abuse in this place and to the families who waited, uncertain, for their return.  Museums exist to confront history honestly, even when it is uncomfortable. This one does not. By sanitizing and rewriting the American POW experience, Hoa Lo does a disservice to truth, reconciliation, and its own credibility.  I came here seeking remembrance. What I found instead was propaganda.

visited on Nov 20. the majority of the place is currently under renovation and the main attractions are turned into photo walls for people to stand around and stare at while listening to the audio guide.  the ticket booth charges full price while only informing guests of the construction inside through a piece of A4 paper, with nowhere near enough information to justify the disappointment ahead. the ticketing worker doesn't speak foreign languages but all the staff inside do and have no trouble leading you to the audio guide room to purchase prerecorded tours.  tourists were packed in there like sardines. the live tour guides in different languages literally screamed to reach their group. if you're a solo traveler it'll take you max 20 minutes to withstand this awful experience.  i also couldn't find any easy access info online about this insane renovation especially when it'll end, in both vietnamese and english. at least pin a notice on your Facebook or website like how hard could this be?? this is the cherry on top for feeling cheated as i used to recommend this place as a must see for hanoi.  hoa lo should just close and inform their audience on all online channels properly instead of this money grabbing behavior that's going to make visitors never want to return.

As a museum on the history of Hoa Lo prison, there is a shocking lack of actual history. Instead the museum is dedicated to shameless propaganda for the Communist Party of Vietnam.  The start of the museum showcases the horrors inflicted on Vietnamese prisoners when the museum was run by French colonials. You can walk through the inhumanely cramped rooms and see the barbarism of how prisoners were kept. Clearly the French built a nasty prison and committed numerous attrocities here and it would be a crime to continue it's use.  However, skip forwards a few years, and the prison is now being used to house American POWs. Surely Americans being housed in the same prison must have also faced terrible conditions? They nicknamed the prison "Heartbreak Hotel," but no, according to the museum, American prisoners were treated like honored guests. They were free to enjoy Christmas feasts and games of basketball (as long as a cameraman was nearby to record for propaganda purposes).  The museum that could be used to present history is now just a house of propoganda and untruths. This is somewhat unsurprising, as even during the war they concealed what was occurring here. Admiral Jeremiah Denton had to blink "torture" in Morse code while telling interviewers how great a time he was having for any information to get out.  Tldr; The museum is preserved well and has a good setup, but any attempt at teaching history is overshadowed by posting blatant lies (that can be facts checked with a 10 second google search) all over the information boards

We wanted to learn more about the colonial period and the relationship between French settlers and the Vietnamese. There's very little information in this 'museum'; everything remains very vague. It quickly becomes clear that it's more of a propaganda tool for the Communist Party than a genuine effort to help us understand the lives of prisoners during that time.

The lower section of the museum, which deals with colonial rule, is impressively presented, albeit sketchy, and lacks specific historical events. What made me extremely sad during my visit, however, is that the museum depicts the oppression and torture of communist Vietnamese, while the Vietnamese government is doing exactly the same thing in Vietnam today in 2022. Oppression, arbitrary detention, and torture, even the death penalty, of dissidents are commonplace in this country (see Amnesty International). Vietnam ranks 174th (!) out of 180 in the press freedom rankings (see Reporters Without Borders).  What also shocked me is the upper floor of the museum, where extreme propaganda is carried out, with Happy Hippo images of imprisoned US Army soldiers decorating a Christmas tree or receiving farewell gifts as if they were at a summer camp. They, too, were tortured and subjected to terrible conditions. History is completely distorted here.  I don't want to dismiss what happened during the colonial era in any way. However, Hoa Lo Prison is completely unsuitable as a museum! If children visit this place, the one-sided reporting and inflammatory language of the museum officials will only fuel hatred in a place that should educate so that history never repeats itself.

Is Vietnam being shy towards France and China? Only the surface of its existence is known, and it has become a tourist destination for Westerners. You should go to the "Vietnam Revolutionary Museum". Why don't they say that this place was born from the cruelty of the Westerners, including the French who indirectly ruled Vietnam, and the cruelty of the Hua Qiao? The French, with themselves at the top, made the Hua Qiao and French mixed race people into administrators and middle class, and turned their grudges against them and violently dominated the Vietnamese. They further did not allow the Vietnamese to grow grains, but instead made them grow luxury goods to send to France, imposed heavy taxes, and finally forced the Vietnamese to sell opium and other things to enrich themselves, while completely ignoring and incapacitating the pure-blooded Vietnamese. Hoa Lo Prison was created with the purpose of making an example of the Vietnamese who rebelled against it with indescribable torture and public executions, and to prevent them from rebelling again. Why can't we get a glimpse of the true historical essence of why this place and this act existed at Hoa Lo Prison? Finally, as a Japanese person, I cannot understand how this scene alone can condemn the Japanese military, who were so fundamentally different from the Japanese military's governing methods, which did not allow the local people to be ignorant, and who committed such horrific mass murders that they even used atomic bombs on non-soldier civilians, and even burned them alive in air raids on urban areas.

By all means visit the "Hanoi Hilton" but do your homework beforehand to learn the real stories of what went on in here because you certainly can't rely on the Vietnamese version of the truth being told.Making themselves out as latter-day saints, it's all the fault of the French and the Americans committing atrocities and torture against the valiant native population who were just freedom fighters.There isn't a single mention anywhere of what the Vietnamese did by way of torture to the American pilots who ended up here (do a Google search for "strappado" for one of the torture means, but you won't find a single reference to it in the totally one-sided presentation of history.We left feeling utterly at a loss because of the outright lies being told in this place. As a museum on the history of Hoa Lo prison, there is a shocking lack of actual history. Instead the museum is dedicated to shameless propaganda for the Communist Party of Vietnam.The start of the museum showcases the horrors inflicted on Vietnamese prisoners when the museum was run by French colonials. You can walk through the inhumanely cramped rooms and see the barbarism of how prisoners were kept. Clearly the French built a nasty prison and committed numerous attrocities here and it would be a crime to continue it's use.However, skip forwards a few years, and the prison is now being used to house American POWs. Surely Americans being housed in the same prison must have also faced terrible conditions? They nicknamed the prison "Heartbreak Hotel," but no, according to the museum, American prisoners were treated like honored guests. They were free to enjoy Christmas feasts and games of basketball (as long as a cameraman was nearby to record for propaganda purposes).The museum that could be used to present history is now just a house of propoganda and untruths. This is somewhat unsurprising, as even during the war they concealed what was occurring here. Admiral Jeremiah Denton had to blink "torture" in Morse code while telling interviewers how great a time he was having for any information to get out.Tldr; The museum is preserved well and has a good setup, but any attempt at teaching history is overshadowed by posting blatant lies (that can be facts checked with a 10 second google search) all over the information boards

They spent more time telling us about how the Americans enjoyed basketball here and parading photos of them decorating the prison for Christmas! Not one word about the torture of them at all. I recommend just watching a video on YouTube about this place and the Vietnam war in general! I do not agree with the Vietnam war and the atrocities from both sides but just tell the history how it actually was.

I am beyond surprised that this museum has such high ratings. This only shows the clear lack of knowledge visitors have about the events that took place within the walls of this prison. This museum is a perfect example of spreading historical misinformation and political propaganda. The hellish torment and conditions the American POWs were subjected to can only be compared to the atrocities of the Holocaust. They referred to notable prisoners like John McCain as “special guests“ as if they were celebrities who should be appreciative for their time spent in the prison. It shows photographs of soldiers getting “regular” medical checkups and smiling prisoners of war enjoying a game of basketball and celebrating/decorating for Christmas.I think this direct quote sums up the viewpoint perfectly: “No more flights on B-52s and carpet bombings, only a serene time for these American pilots to think about what happened and the beauty of peaceful life and warm humanity in Hoa Lo Prison.”As an American, I do not condone the USA’s involvement in the Vietnam War and certainly not some of the actions that took place during this time. However, anyone with a basic understanding of the events will know that this is not the type of treatment that took place. Although it’s only a few dollars, I highly recommend saving the money so that this is not further supported. It is especially upsetting if you are a veteran or have a family member who served.This “museum” should be absolutely ashamed.

This is a must visit for people interested in firstly the French occupation and then the American invasion of the late 1960s. I was amazed to see the American fighters so well looked after by the Vietnamese jailerand were allowed to play sports as excretion.

Whilst it's certainly acceptable to contest historical narratives, and indeed, for nations that have suffered under colonialism and invasion, it's essential, the portrayal of the Vietnamese as nothing more than heroic and innocent victims of historical misfortune, who themselves took no part in atrocities, is astounding, hypocritical, and wholly lacking in credibility. The majority of what's left of the prison (rather ironically, the remainder is now a plush 25 story serviced apartment building), is dedicated to the horrendous treatment of Vietnamese dissidents by the French colonialists. This is all done in an unnecessarily hyperbolic manner, that detracts from rather than emphasising the cruelties endured by the prisoners. There is then a large section celebrating the leadership and heroism of the leaders of the CPV who were imprisoned at Hoa Lo, but a lack of similar recognition for any of the more everyday people who entered and never returned. Without wishing to deny or diminish the role of these revolutionaries, narratives of the ordinary fighters involved in the struggle (or even those who were falsely imprisoned), would've added a far more interesting and palpable strata to the museum. The small section dedicated to the female prisoners goes someway to address this, but generally, the tone is very one dimensional, didactic, and context free - nothing is ever put into perspective - a huge failing for a place of remembrance. This second section is quite simply unconscionable propaganda. I'm not American, but I left feeling extremely angry at the manner in which the infamous 'Hanoi Hilton' was presented as a model prison, where the prisoners were treated with great kindness by their hosts, and even educated as to the ways of Vietnamese culture, as they spent their days playing chess, cooking, and whiling away the hours until they could head back home. It seemed surreal, a joke, which I kept expecting to be revealed as comparing the then propaganda against contemporary accounts. But the punchline never came, and the American POWs still face the indignity of not having their experience represented with any degree of accuracy at a monument purporting to be there as an act of remembrance for all of its victims.The final section is perhaps the most interesting part because it doesn't exist: how the CPV treated its dissident prisoners in Hoa Lo right up until the mid-80s.

If you are going with the intention to tour the prison because of it's role during the Vietnam War, you will be disappointed.  The Hoa Lo Prison was built by the French in the early 1900s to imprison Vietnamese revolutionaries, so about 90% of the prison is dedicated to their struggle.  There is 1 room dedicated to the Americans there during the Vietnam War. I have done significant research and study on the American POWs in the Hoa Lo Prison, and unfortunately the Prison does not reflect ANY of the hardships the POWs faced.  Granted, these hardships were in the hands of the Vietnamese.  I understand that the prison is going to show the Vietnamese point of view, but the way they articulated and displayed the American experience in the Hanoi Hilton made it look as though they spent 7 years in a Vietnamese themed Disneyland.  All that to say--make sure you do your homework before you go so that you know what is the truth about what happened there during the Vietnam War, as you will be misled if your only source of information is from the exhibits.
I'd like to preface this by saying that we're very interested in Vietnamese history and know quite a bit about the occupation and the Vietnam War.

Propaganda is always ugly. The assertion that US servicemen were well treated in Hoa Lo is utter nonsense and yet the videos shown depict happy and healthy POW's enjoying Christmas and playing chess. I already knew that I was in Vietnam and had been to museums that are filled with propaganda and are very biased. This was the most telling example of that in all of Vietnam.It starts out by being "greeted" by a soldier just staring us down. Then we walk in and see the horrible treatment by the French of Vietnamese political prisoners. They go into detail how the French mistreated them. Then miraculously, the prison became a Holiday Inn as American prisoners were there. American prisoners were treated to their favorite sporting events such as basketball, volleyball etc... They even allowed them to play chess and make Thanksgiving and Christmas turkey. How nice the Vietnamese were to our American GI's right? Ask John McCain if he was treated so respectably. They have his pictures and uniform as trophies. They are sick! Then you are treated to a nasty little video again filled with such lies. Aren't Vietnamese people embarrassed by this distortion of the truth? A little child can easily see how horribly they present their lies. I'm ashamed I went there!

Not surprised at the propaganda and lack of honesty this prison represents, this country was just full of it. This place will make your stomach turn and really question what is wrong with us all as humans. The ticket ladies just glare at you (you know, how dare you go to their museum) and there is a security guard in the room about American POW. There were basically no other security guards on site, as if they know people are going to get pissed at the blatant lies in that section of the museum OR they just don't trust foreigners to not steal (incredibly ironic in this country)

I was extremely disappointed with this museum, it didn't give much of a feel of how the conditions really used to be, not in comparison with the other war museums I visited in Vietnam. Almost all of the annotations were in Vietnamese, and there were rooms after rooms dedicated to individual people without information about these people! I wished I hadn't bothered. Save the history museums for Ho Cho Minh City!

Our visit to this 'museum' was a total waste of time and money. The building doesn't really give you any insight as to what the prison would have been like as the structure has either been sold-off or modernise, the exhibits are rather poor, most of the descriptions and explanations are in Vietnamese with no English translation, and the entire 'story' of the prison has been blatantly used as a huge propaganda machine. I felt a bit cheated that I had paid money to visit a place which can so shamelessly lie to it's audience. How they can expect anyone to believe their version of how they treated American POWs is beyond me. I think the sole use for this museum must be for the Vietnamese to 'teach' their own about how they have suffered through the hands of various invaders through the years, but this surely shouldn't be for outside visitors to see. I guess it's interesting to see propaganda up so close and personal, that is about the level of the experience. I really wouldn't recommend this so-called 'museum'.

I went to this place without reading much about the background. First it was interesting to look around, study the descriptions and stories of the Vietnamese fighting against French colonists. But when it came to the section where it stated on the entrance "the crime committed by the French" (or something like that), and then was led to a section with pictures and descriptions explaining how American prisoners treated nicely and taught about how to clean and take care of themselves like Vietnamese, it felt to me that it lacks of some consistencies. After the visit, I went online to look for some information of the background, and shocked to find out the American prisoners were in fact tortured badly in the prison, unlike what I read from this place. I feel that I paid to be cheated!

I do not doubt that the french treated the prisoners poorly but there is no way I can believe that the Americans lived it up playing guitar and basketball the entire time that they were there. If you're interested in seeing the propaganda for yourself go, if not then give it a miss.

The Museaum could be very good, but it's just a propaganda museum.You can see how cruel the bad french treated the vietnamese people and on the other side you see how friendly the vietnamese people were with the americans !I imagine that the french colonist weren't very friendly with the prisoners, but you will not make me believe that the Vietnamese let the American prisoners play guitar, basketball and chat with journalist while decorating a christmas tree !!! Too much propaganda ruines the real history of the place !

I was interested in seeing a historical place.  There are always 2 sides to a story.  However the Vietnamese portrayal of their treatmenet of US troops held in prison is shameful.  Read Senator Mc Caisn biography for his narrative on the years of real torture the troops endured.  I feel guilty having paid an entrance fee and supported this site.  Yes, the Vietnamese endured extremed hardships and death during the war but to belittle and lie about the abuses they inflicted on captured soldiers will only make the healing process between the countries people more difficult.  There are many wonderful places to visit while in Hanoi and great native people to interact with visiting this site tainted my perspective on things.

If I'm honest, I'm not entirely sure what I expected but when the guidebook reminds you that previous American prisoners' memories are very different to those reported in the museum, you know to expect a serious amount of propaganda. For those people who believe what they read - the French treated the Vietnamese horrifically, whilst the American POWs had a relatively comfortable stay where they could decorate christmas trees and sing carols around a fireplace that had been drawn on the wall. The photos show those situations, so they happened but there are very few other pictures of the prisoners at any time during their stay. Who knows what is actually true, and I'd imagine that you couldn't put a lot of it in a family museum, but I was shocked by what I read. It was a skewed version of reality with carefully selected photos and no memoirs of POWs. It's worth a visit if you're nearby, but don't take what you read at face value - which I think probably goes for most of Hanoi's museums.

80% of the prison was sold off to developers but the ruling elite decided to keep a small portion for anti-French, anti-American propaganda. There isn't much to see here mostly some pictures and bizarrely quite few shackled Vietnamese 'prisoner' mannequins smiling and looking proudly defiant. The plaques which contain most of the history about the prison reveal very little information but do offer a lot of historical inaccuracies. Basically they tell a story of how peaceful Vietnamese were just living their lives working in the fields then the French invaded turned them all into slaves, imprisoning and torturing all who opposed them. They imply that their was the foundation of a communist rebel party which constantly waged a guerrilla war campaign against the French Imperialist occupation. Then after the Vietnamese defeated the French and drove them from the land the American's (who had used deception to convince the South to attack the North, invaded. Then there is a whole two walls talking about female prisoners who were captured and tortured by Americans at some other prison South of the DMZ. The rest of the exhibit talks about how American's used barbaric torture techniques while the North Vietnamese not only didn't torture their prisoners, they actually treated them really well and bought them gifts, kept them well fed, etc. Of course this is where John McCain was held (who has described his torture in great detail) but there is only one photo of him. The exhibit ends with a wall of photos of all the countries who rallied against the US and sided with the North Vietnamese. Strangely France was North Vietnam's biggest ally in the anti-ward movement even though they were the early Imperialists and still hold vast interests in the country. I loved Vietnam, the country and its people and I felt that this exhibit was a blight on my experience and portrays the country in a bad light. I had always grown up opposed to the American's involvement in the war, I left the exhibit feeling sorry that they lost.
It's a typical kitch propaganda museum. It's only interesting if you want to see a real guillotine (there are only a few left in the world).

Located right in the heart of the city, this site is incredibly easy to reach, and the ticket price is very affordable. However, I must admit I was a bit disappointed with the experience. While the history of the 'Hanoi Hilton' is profound—spanning from French colonial rule to the imprisonment of American pilots like John McCain—the museum itself feels lacking. There are far too many photographs and posters instead of original artifacts or immersive exhibits. It felt more like a gallery than a preserved prison. It’s worth a quick stop for the historical context, but don't expect a deeply detailed or comprehensive tour.

I cannot recommend this museum because of the false narrative and misrepresentation of facts it perpetuates. One would expect a museum to be trustable. This one is clearly not.  There are effectively two main themes to this prison. The first was the early period where the French were holding Vietnamese prisoners. The museum did a very good job of discussing the conditions and atrocities that occurred during that time. This was very good and informative.  Later the prison was used to hold American pilots who had been captured. This is where it's apparent that this museum is little more than propaganda. They make it sound like this prison was a resort. Like the prisoners loved it so much here because of everything that the Vietnamese were giving them, such as tours of the city, fancy dinners, etc.  As an example, I overheard a tour guide stating how John McCain turned down the opportunity to go home so that he could stay with his friends. Anyone with Google can look up the true story of how he was being used as a pawn. He himself has stated that the museum's depictions are factually incorrect.  Anyone visiting should skip the sections discussing the American prisoners because you will walk away with a warped reality.  I can't recommend this prison because of the outright deception they are fabricating. I would have given it one star, but the information about the French holding the Vietnamese was good.
