It's not worth it. In my opinion, the reporting is far too one-sided and rather poor.
The photos are the main focus, with the occasional prison and model. It would be nice to have models or sounds that are a bit different from the photos.

It breaks my heart to see this, as it reminds me of our own Seodaemun Prison. There must be a genuine apology and forgiveness toward the country that suffered the damage. At the very least, like Germany did.
One of the city's main attractions. We found it a bit of a pain. The good thing is it's cheap.
War is brutal and inhumane, and freedom and human rights were out of reach in the context of that time and place.
Letter from the imprisoned German pediatric nurse Monika Schwimm
Only go if you're a fan of sadness: better go elsewhere
It was a bit disappointing as it was different to what I expected.  But it was incredibly realistic to be able to see the concentration camp as it was at the time.
The entrance fee of 30,000 dong was a waste. There wasn't much to see and it was too dark.
That's all. It's not very expensive, but don't expect too much either…
Waste of time
Gives some idea of ​​the experience of the prisoners - local and captured American soldiers
I've been to Hanoi more than 20 times and always get diarrhea.
Honestly, it seems like Koreans don't have much to see and they don't even support Korean language services.
It lets us know that we are countries with similar histories.
If you want to know about Vietnam's independence history, go go go
A prison used to suppress resistance during the French colonial rule of Vietnam
The French are gentlemanly compared to the Japanese.
There are no Japanese guides so I don't really understand.
The day I went... it was closed for renovations.
A bit too much of a garish look for me.
Just so-so Nothing to see One guillotine
There doesn't seem to be a need to go there.
I don't understand socialism.
I would not recommend this hotel.
I thought of sadness.
feel ill
Manageable
terrible
I went to a nearby restaurant
It was just like that
Just time
Would rather do something else. Far too many people and a poor system of how to move through the halls, you stand and wait forever inside the museum to move on. Very propaganda feel to the …
You don't really need to go/Absolutely not recommended  A visit to Seodaemun Prison would be much more ideal.
We didn't get the audio guide after reading the Google reviews, and honestly, the visit was chaotic. …
What a chaotic place to visit. Crowds of people, all coming and going in different directions.

We couldn’t get past the pictures of American soldiers receiving mail, celebrating Christmas, playing sports, getting health checks etc. they tried to make it look like summer camp for captured soldiers. Yes, what Americans did in Vietnam is terrible, but it was difficult to see this propaganda.

The first half focuses on the Vietnamese prisoners under French rule, which was very informative and interesting to learn about. However, don’t expect to learn anything about the treatment of American POWS during the Vietnam War. The American section was extremely biased. The museum mentioned absolutely nothing about how they were tortured, or their living conditions. It showcased propaganda images from the War, which showed prisoners having fun, and engaging willingly in interviews, stating this was their general experience. However this was actually something that prisoners were tortured for until they agreed to take part. I personally don’t agree with many of Americas actions throughout the war, however I felt this exhibition was extremely misleading so I was very disappointed.
The museum is nothing more than the gate to the colonial French
We are divided, because the depictions of the prison are very well made and poignant. The audio guide was also well done.

It is interesting to see the building and the different cells but the information written on the walls is not that informative and I get the impression that it is a rather biased version of history (especially when it is portrayed that the Americans had a luxurious stay involving decorating Christmas trees and playing card games).

Heavy on the history of French colonial rule but scant info about the horrible mistreatment, torture, and death of U.S. prisoners kept here. All that remains is the gatehouse as the prison itself, which took up the block behind, was razed to construct office buildings and other modern structures within the last 20+ yrs. It's safe to say this is a part of history the Vietnamese want to forget as well, but it's unfortunate there's a gross omission of what really happened here. Take it with a grain of salt. Due to some gruesome pictures, best not to bring kids under 12 yrs old here.

Hoa Lo Prison is located about 7 km from the center of Hanoi. From the outside, I found it very sturdy and well-concealed. I've experienced the inside. The prison is built in a two-story, labyrinthine style, so those unfamiliar with it will have trouble finding their way out. In short, the architecture shows how solidly the French colonialists built the prison, making escape very difficult. However, the downside is the people and the way the Vietnamese police manage it. Despite being close to the central government, corruption and bribery are rampant. At night, the guards gamble. The wardens bring contraband into the prison for the inmates to use. In short, it's rotten. Prisoners can get whatever they want as long as they have money.
If you are a history buff then this might be your thing... it wasn't ours. We went with teenagers and they were ready to leave in about 10 minutes... we were ready in 30.

Were excited to visit after visiting Tuol Sleng in Phnom Penh. But this was disappointing. Entrance 40k and then extra 50k for audio guide. The extra for audio guide not advertised at ticket cabin or at audio guide borrowing-place. There is a very little left of original house and building and so the atmosphere of the place can’t be felt. The information provided is full of communist propaganda about the strength of communist fighters tortured by French. They indicate that the American POW that were held here had a great time celebrating Christmas and playing basketball. Suppose that’s how McCain injured his shoulders...
If you want an accurate count of history, don’t follow this tour. Very inaccurate accounts were stated based on what people have said to the public verse what was stated in the prison itself.
The explanations provided and documents displayed reek of communist propaganda...

Only one-third of the original building remains. Much is devoted to how the French treated Vietnamese prisoners, often freedom fighters. Rightly so. However, even today there are still political prisoners in this country, where the death penalty is still in effect. Relatively speaking, very little and one-sided attention is paid to the Americans they imprisoned here. All in all, not very impressive.
Cheap entrance to this small prison, It was interesting to walk through the old cells and read about the history of the place, and the prisoners lives.
Only after visiting the museum did I learn through the internet why the German pediatric nurse Monika Schwinn was imprisoned there and what became of her cat.

Eighty percent of this "museum" is about the French occupation and the imprisonment of the Vietnamese people.  I can't speak about the truthfulness of what happened during that time and the horrors that were imposed on the prisoners, but the exhibits were done well.  The other twenty percent of the museum focused on the "humane" treatment that the American troops received while staying there.  They "prove" this with videos of men playing ping pong and having Christmas service, or being fed good food and drinking tea out of a china cup.  It was INSULTING and full of lies.  Those prisoners were forced to make those videos and I know personally some vets that were there.  LIES are being fed to anyone who walks in this place.  (See my pictures)

One of the few reasons to go here is to say you have been here. I don’t find that to be a reason to see anything. I will give it 2 stars because of the memories of news footage shots and interviews of America POWs who were imprisoned and tortured here, but multiple information boards have a marked opposite view from this reality. There is more interest in the French war when Vietnamese were held and tortured here. It’s a grim place. Sorry but Trip Advisor appears to be allowing only live shots, so no photos.
Caught a team trying to pick my wallet in here! We were in the room with the guillotine. I looked down, I thought I felt a strange movement.
I went into the prison. It was a huge disappointment. All about Vietnam being the bright star on the hill.

I would strongly suggest doing a little bit of research before visiting this museum. The first 3/4 of the museum shows how the Vietnamese communists were imprisoned  by the French and kept in terrible conditions, whilst the final 1/4 demonstrates how the Americans lived in relative ‘luxury’ by comparison.
We don't think it's worth going...especially considering the prisons in our country...

I was looking forward to learning something about the various conflicts this prison has played a part in, but unfortunately through a combination of (1) not much to see, (2) inadequate storytelling and (3) highly biased positionality I came away none the wiser. Cheap (30,000 VND) but for us a bit of a waste of time.

The bad guys, that is, the French and Americans who were "stayed" in this prison, if the photos on display are to be believed, lived like prisoners staying in a luxury hotel: they were looked after by doctors, they did sports, they celebrated Christmas with a large illuminated tree... Meanwhile, the Vietnamese who were locked up in this same prison earlier during the French protectorate, were apparently tortured.

While I would recommend a visit to the Hoa Lo Prison, I was disappointed in the way that the history of the prison was displayed. The atrocities committed against the Vietnamese people under French colonization were indeed terrible and should not be forgotten or dismissed and it was very important to learn about that aspect as it is not as often affiliated with the prison outside of Vietnam. In the same regard the truth should also be told about the atrocities committed against the US POWs during the Vietnam War. Several accounts from former prisoners of war describe the acts committed there in great detail however not a single mention of it is told in the prison displays of the museum. It only emphasizes that the POWs during the 1960s and 1970s were treated very very well. It is so important to be able to acknowledge both the good and the bad from the past so that we can all learn from it. Overall I was very disappointed in the historical fiction created by the museum.

Look, this was an OK experience, if you are eager to see what is left of the prison along with door fragments in glass boxes then check this out.I am glad I did this however I would recommend watching a documentary on this place, it will be cheaper and cleaner.

Honestly felt this was a money grab. They had parts of doors on display? Kind of like they were grasping at straws. It wasn’t very informative. Last year we visited the Cambodian Genocide Museum which was much more informative and interesting. Wouldn’t waste your time/money visiting this place.

It was my first time visiting a prison as a tourist so the place was quite haunting and ghastly to me. Imagine those tortured that those prisoners endured through during their time was unbearable. Nonetheless I went as an eye-opener to how lucky we are to be living in this modern and well structured world. You can take a visit if you have nothing much to do in Hanoi. We just happened to pass by the area and decided to take a look because we had quite plenty of time around.

Learn about how the French were terrible administrators of the prison, and how kind and benevolent the Vietnamese were.  The only problem is that it's a total lie.  The Vietnam government should be honest about their history.

During our visit to Hanoi, the guide took us to see the prison, and well, for me, it's... Inna. A skippable visit if you're short on time; if you're bored, it can be a way to kill time, and the entrance fee isn't expensive.

Not much to say apart from... small prison with a very very skewed view of History. In a nutshell. The French were terrible. The Vietnesme treated American P.OWs like they were at a holiday camp. Insert some photos to prove it and that is the prison.. not worth the 40000 dong. For half a prison and One sided story of the past. Shame really as it could be a very interesting and Informative place.... but as with so many museums here in Vietnam you only ever get 1 side of the story..

The first part of the prison is really interesting and has a lot of information. You can feel how the Vietnamese prisoners must have felt. But as soon as you go the section about the americans imprisoned during the Vietnam war the propaganda starts. They say that the American had good treatment. But if you think about the history you will realize that the American soldiers were the enemy. So it seemed pretty unusual that they had good treatment such as a big Christmas dinner. Just 5 minutes into googling you will read the truth. Very disappointing because it is State museum.....
The museum just didn't communicate the story of the prison very well at all, so i found it rather boring. I believe it could have been so much better.
Not a very impressive display.  Most a photographers display of that era.  Wouldn’t come to Hanoi and not visit, but truly not very impressed

As an American, this was hard to walk through. I understand that Vietnamese have their perspective on the war, and they deserve to have that. However, this goes too far in my opinion. There are examples of the Vietnamese being tortured and pictures of how great Americans were treated. The propaganda was disgusting. If you want to see a part of history, then visit. If you’re American you should expect to be disappointed.

Helt okei å besøke dette. Spennende å ha vært der. Imidlertid fremstilt veldig patriotisk, ble litt for mye. «Alle kjempet til siste sekund». Mens amerikanere som ble fengslet der under Vietnamkrigen spilte fotball og biljard. Holder med 20 min der!
Good

Also called the Hilton of Hanoi, it was the place where political prisoners were held. If you are a history buff, have a great interest in Uncle Ho or American pilots involvement in the Vietnam war, then you mist definitely visit the Hoa Lo Prison...   we found the place depressing and only a fraction of the original prison is left... not much reference to the actual prison. But thats just us.
Nothing much to see. Given that the entrance fee is quite cheap. I am not complaining. If you do not have time. This is one place you can skip with no regrets

It is not my cup of tea, but a male colleague really wanted to see it. It was lacking most of the real prison to make space for a modern high-rise hotel, so I don't feel that I should have to pay. To see it. It was also full of propaganda. That is another reason, I am not interested in such sites.

The entrance fee was 30,000 dong per person, but there was nothing interesting inside. Compared to the prison in Phrae province, Thailand, this place offered a more realistic view of the prison environment.

I was anxious to see the “ Hanoi Hilton” the famed residence of Senator John Mcain. Like most Vietnam museum it is not well cared for. Nevertheless it was a bit interesting. The cost for entry is only 30,000 vnd per person. So perhaps it is worth that. I think some might tell a different story of what it was like to be housed there. But that is true of any two sided story.
Good to have seen and to be aware of the history, but strictly speaking, as an outing, it doesn't offer much. Only for true culture enthusiasts!

This museum was like most prison museums except there was propaganda written all over the experience. I guess it can be fun to get a feeling of just how staged it is, but I didn’t feel like I really learned anything new. Maybe perhaps that this is where John Mcain was a prisoner? The entrance fee is fairly cheap, though
After 2 weeks touring Vietnam the war/museum baseline was high. Its easy to get to, but I would only go if you have time to kill.

Quite small and not that interesting when compared to the War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh. Might have enjoyed this more if I hadn’t visited that one first.I am not taking sides as it’s all history but the information is clearly propaganda. The French as portrayed as callous and cruel but the Vietnamese portrayed themselves in a good light and talked about how well they treated US prisoners. Fact is everyone was horrible and tortured everyone.The exhibits also pale in comparison to the War Remnants Museum, which is much more informative and graphic.Nevertheless, a good way to spend an hour in the afternoon here for 30,000 dong.

The prison is interesting, and you can understand the hard daily life of the prisonners but the presentation is really too biased. For instance there is almost no presentation of the american also imprisoned there except to show how well they were treated. And french treatment is denounced with a specific vocanulary that plays more on the emotions than facts

We were quite disappointed.  The complex has been more than halved for an apartment block development and the whole experience is phenomenally biased.  Terrible things happened here and the Vietnamese were good the the American POWs but unless you are really keen to go there are better things to do.
I was expecting something more realistic, but it was mostly just photos and dolls, which was a bit disappointing. It wasn't that big either, and I was able to see everything in about 15 minutes.

The museum is in good condition and contains numerous descriptions. While the section on French mistreatment is well-developed, the bad faith displayed in the section concerning the detention of American pilots is ludicrous.

Since it is part of America's involvement in the Vietman War, many Americans will want to visit.  A lot of the display describes the French treatment of Vietmanese prisoners.  The prison was built for that purpose.  Towards the end, you come to the section about the Vietmanese's treatment of Americans.  It is really hard to imagine they there were having as good a time as is suggested by the material presented.  My guide was genuinely surprised when I told her that our version of their treatment is very different as compared to what is portrayed

I am fond of visiting historical sites and ensured this was on my list for a short visit to Hanoi. Much of the original site has been torn down to make way for a towering structure, but some history remains. Unfortunately, the history of the site is overshadowed by its unbalanced presentation. The prison’s usage during the French colonial occupation is eye-opening, though details are scant and the propaganda is heavy. Only a small section of the prison is devoted to the war with America; this part is practically skippable, as the propaganda is so thick that there’s no historical value. As they say, history is written by the victors. This prison will explain the horrors suffered by the “patriotic Vietnamese revolutionary comrades” from French jailers, but ignore the horrors inflicted by Vietnamese jailers on American POWs.
An old prison converted into a museum, which recounts some of the hardships endured by prisoners during that era. It didn't offer me anything new, as such hardships occurred in many other prisons.

We decided to visit the prison because we were running out of ideas. It isn't a very recommendable experience as half of the prison is demolished and I don't think they make the most of the remaining part. Additionally it is quite propagandistic regarding the historical information they provide.

I found the visit boring, the buildings are boring. The history of  the struggles for independence is very important, I, on this occasion was not excited by the information or the underlying propaganda.

Interesting visit if you do not know much about the history however inside wasn't as expected. It didn't have the feel of a prison remains more of a museum. The impact was not as hard hitting as other old prisons I have visited. The fake people inside took away from the severity of  the conditions of the prison during its time.

If you are thinking of a concentration camp in Germany or Korea, you are greatly mistaken. It is located right next to a large building, so the space is very cramped, and the recreation is not done well.

I struggle to understand some opinions. Mine is that if you have something not much better to do...do it. They show you a couple of dungeons with mannequins and little else. It's not worth the time wasted on the trip or the visit. Apparently, it's important to them. All due respect.
Too much propaganda and too few documentaries. Not enough historical facts and not that much to see.
Not that interesting, kind of a boring museum, but at least it isn't expensive. Not a must see in my book.
I'm not sure if this is a prison camp or just a building. I didn't go inside, but it was disappointing because it just looked like a Vietnamese building.
The French were terrible, brutal to the Vietnamese and the vietnamese adored the American PoWs they held in the prison. I wouldn't bother visiting. The lauguage did have comedy elements.

It was interesting to walk through the French section of the prison (which is most of it), but the American side was disappointing. There were guitars and medical equipment (for all of the top notch medical care the POWs received while staying at this luxury hotel) and pictures of American soldiers playing volleyball shirtless that looked like a scene from Top Gun. Amongst the stories of French torture were downright laughable lies about how humane the POWs from the other side were treated.
The prison is small and not quite "touching". Most of the cells are empty, so it took us barely 20 minutes to see and read everything possible.

Less than a third remains of the original prison compound. Most halls are used to tell how cruel and horrid the French colonisers were towards the Communist Party subversives in the 1930s and 1940s. A couple of rooms tell of how the prison was subsequently used in the 1960s and early 1970s to hold US prisoners of war, and how fabulously well-treated the Americans were while held in this "Hanoi Hilton".
Some kind of interesting things to see but would not really recommend going.  Can walk through in about 10 min or so.
Worth a visit if there's nothing else to do.

This is a pretty lame place to visit. The exhibits are average, at best. The two video presentations are so appalling (the upstairs one is almost impossible to follow as about 30% of the subtitles are cut off the screen), they're almost comical, which is unfortunate as the subject matter is anything but amusing.

we visited the prison named the Hanoi Hilton during the war. It was where the US POWs were kept, it was interesting to see however our group of friends all thought the guides there were to politically correct and pushed party politics and gave information about prisoner conditions which everyone outside Vietnam knew was untrue, we walked out half way through the tour.

This prison is interesting, though the information is quite questionable.  Most of the exhibit is dedicated to historic rebellion against the French colonization.  I know nothing about this so can't speak to its accuracy.  The stories presented were fascinating though.Once the timeline moved up to the Vietnam War, the legitimacy of everything became questionable.  As an American I know a bit about this part of the story.  The displays were saying that the GIs named it the Hanoi Hilton because it was so nice (um, no), etc.  Enough seemed wrong that I researched a few bits online later and sure enough the museum is a propaganda machine.  The suit displayed as McCain's isn't even his.  Should you go?  Sure, it's not expensive and then you can say you've been there.  But don't believe most of what you read.
There isn't much to see. If you are looking for photos, you absolutely shouldn't go.
I expected more and was disappointed...boring.

The Hanoi Hilton, you figure should be of interest to Americans since this is where John McCain was held and I'm from Phoenix but the Vietnamese removed everything that had to do Americans. This place is all about the days before the American War. Most of the original prison has been removed. So now it's just an old prison building.

It's a waste of time. There aren't many ancient photos or introductions inside. There's only one guillotine worth seeing, and most of the other items are replicas. You can't finish watching it in less than half an hour. It doesn't have much historical value!
Didn't get much out of the flimsy display than by briefly reading about it online. Worth if you're in the (otherwise unremarkable ) area and have a 30k dong surplus

Most of the exhibit is focused on the construction of the jail. by the French and then its use by the Vietnamese against the French. Only 2 rooms relate to US pilot internment. Only costs about US $1 so not a big risk if you don't like it.

Although this clearly has a strong and sad history, there is sadly not much to this museum. It is relatively small and unfortunately it felt a little too much like reading propaganda throughout and therefore some of it, particularly about the American soldiers, didn't feel 100% true.

I can say I went to "Hanoi Hilton" but what a disappointment. I guess I shouldn't have expected it to portray brutal  living conditions.  It was mainly about the Vietnamese that were kept there during the French colonization. Only two rooms were dedicated for paraphernalia and pictures of time inside for the American pilots who were kept there.   Mostly pictures of happy times. Such Propaganda!!

Disappointing museum which repeats information too often for a small exhibition- as such it belittles the importance of the place in Hanoi's history. The information is woefully biased & great swathes of history are omitted to suit national sentiment. The guidebook sold with the ticket just repeats the info inside the museum. As does the final video which I can only assume wasn't originally intended to be shown at the end. Takes about 30 mins if you do read everything. Tickets -20k each

Easy to get to within the city by taxi. We had been very interested to see this site, but found that our time might have been better spent elsewhere. It was not in its original state any more - over half of the prison is now torn down and replaced by high rise hotel and/or residential - very strange to see those buildings overlook the few buildings that remain. I do now understand that it was not just a prison for Americans in the Vietnam war, but also for the Vietnamese people during the time of the French. So, clearly there were many more years of that issue, and I understand the need to cover that. There is still a strong feel of propaganda, though - this is another good time to read what you can ahead of time and then use that, along with what you see and read on site, to get a more rounded picture of history. There are a number of photos taken during the time of American prisoners - most photos made it seem that it was like a holiday for them - that they ate turkey and played ball and cards and other games. If you're interested to see this relative to the Americans' time in the "Hanoi Hilton", don't waste your time. You can see some cells and some barracks where they show how the Vietnamese were held, but I don't think you get much feel for the time and treatment of Americans.

While I am not sure exactly what I had in mind it was not what I expected.  I did not find it well organized or really informative - even with a guide.  The information is more focused on the French period than on the Vietnam War
We went mostly to kill time; it's a skippable excursion but with an interesting ending. It's over in 30 minutes, I don't recommend it.
The old Hoa Lo prison didn't impress me at all; I found it a boring visit where they show you some period artifacts and clothing, a few cells, and very little else.
Interesting in parts, but full of government propaganda about how well they treated American POWs. Don't believe it.

Partially destroyed and transformed into a museum, this prison, built by the French in 1945, was used during the Indochina War. The 45-minute tour offers a glimpse into prison life at the time. Recommended for history buffs.

Small as much of the building is missing. What is left is important to see, but mostly presented as original history of Vietnamese people suffering under French rule. Very sad to see. Largely ignores use as Prisoner of War camp.

It's worth going if you have time. It's centrally located and admission is 30,000 dirhams (15,000 for students). But neither the building is beautiful nor is the story interesting. It's geared towards Vietnamese tourists.
Very little to see as about 8 tenths of the site has been demolished. Still rather interesting though.

I had quite high expectations of this prison museum - "the Hanoi Hilton". They were quickly lowered when I realized that big parts of the museum is empty cells with no further information or text written. The photo exhibition about the american captives (Mr John McCaine one of them) was interesting to see, but you must remember that this museum (as many other in Vietnam!) is highly influenced by communist propaganda. The american version of what happened in Hoa Lo is a different story, and the truth is probably somewhere in between. Spend around 45 minutes in here, you dont need more time. The museum shop is boring and sells nothing except for the ordinary souvenirs you find in the Old town.

I didn't really want to visit this site. I felt ike a traitor doing so. I was traveling with my husband whose father served in Vietnam and I'm French. My father, who did not serve in Vietnam, was nevertheless a French naval officer. As soon as we stepped in, it was overwhelmingly clear how much the French were and are hated and how the historical narrative is skewed to reflect that. I understand I'm in a communist country with no freedom of speech or thought, but nonetheless I was frustrated that the information was so one sided. When we got to the American part of the war, the depictions given were of a 4 star hotel. Photographs of soldiers playing volleyball and enjoying Christmas dinner as if any American isn't stupid to recognize these as the staged picture these are and that most of us haven't learned in our history classes of the gruesome torture experienced in this jail. Funny enough, the same day, we got back to our hotel and there was a documentary on National Geographic channel of John McCain's time in captivity at this jail. I'm not sure how it got past the sensors, but I assure you he did not once mention any volleyball games. I'm not sure I would recommend this to any American. It lines the pockets of the Vietnamese and furthers their propaganda. It just left me feeling angry and sad. This was a stark contrast to the Genocide museum in Phnom Pehn.

This museum has the potential to be really interesting but I think it's current state isn't very engaging. I do realise we are in Vietnam but there was often no more than one line of English to explain what something was. A tour guide or something would be really useful so I could've left feeling like I'd learned a bit more.

My girlfriend loves prison's so we tend to visit historical ones whenever we are in a place and this was one of the worst, the information wasn't very good, just a few stories about people who were held there without much background

I found this museum to be very biased. It portrays how terrible the conditions were for the Vietnamese imprisoned by the French, but how great the conditions were for American POWs. It doesn't add up. Lots of propaganda.
I was expecting an interesting visit.  It just seems that this site is mostly a re-creation of parts of the prison that used to be here.  High expectations.

Like all of the museums I have visited in vietnam the history is a little one sided Obviously the Vietnamese have suffered massively at the hands of their invaders so there's really no need to try and emphasize this through lies.The museum makes it out that the American prisoners of war were treated with kindness in the jail and shows staged photos of them enjoying thanksgiving dinners etc. Reading numerous other sources we know this is not the case. Keep an open mind when visiting this museum and don't believe everything you read. It was also quite hard to navigate and the exhibitions were  carelessly laid out

Het is een museum vol Vietnamese propaganda. Buitenlanders deden slechte dingen en toen zijn de Vietnamese heldhaftig geweest door te vechten en hebben ze de gevangenis omgetoverd tot Hilton hotel voor de Amerikanen waar ze konden lachen en spelletjes konden spelen. Kortom het is een museum met een zeer zwart witte uitleg. Wel interessant als je meer wilt leren over propaganda, maar niet als je meer wilt leren over de rol van de Fransen. De gevangenis zelf is niet indrukwekkend. Gewoon voor die tijd. Als je meer wilt leren en een mooi en vriendelijk museum wilt bezoeken dan verwijs ik naar het politie museum. Veel feitelijker, geeft meer inzicht, mooier, vriendelijke mensen en gratis

Beperkt museum met een klein blik achter de schermen van de gevangenis. Niet heel interessant voor een gezin met tieners. Je bent er binnen een half uur echt doorheen, omdat er niet heel veel is te zien.

Conclusion: interesting for the wrong reason. The history of the prison is very interesting. You get a peak into how the prison was used before actually being a prison, how the French transformed it, and how the Vietnamese drove the French out and took over the prison. Lastly how the Americans were enprisoned. The presentation is however quite one-sided, describing the French in the worst way possible, and praising the time when the Vietnamese run the prison with outmost respect for the prisoners (even though different sources claim that the time there wasn't so great).Then again, you get a feeling of the nationalism of the Vietnamese, presenting the history in this matter. That was actually quite interesting, but at some expense of the history.

The so-called "Hanoi Hilton" informs visitors of the different uses of the prison in different eras, including who the jailers and prisoners were. The first part of the visit details the cruelties the French subjected the Vietnamese prisoners to and the bravery and perseverance of the Communist Vietnamese. Then, the second half of the visit insists that the American POWs held in the same place enjoyed good treatment. While I appreciate history and try to visit historically significant sites, Hoa Lo Prison was a letdown.

Due to a tropical storm, we had to change our plans for Hanoi and decided to visit the prison, as it wasn't too far from the Old Town and we could manage the walk almost without getting wet. However, the exhibition itself was rather dry, presenting only facts. The brief account of Americans' lives in the prison was entertaining, though, as it clearly illustrated the multiple perspectives on history. This section, however, only comprises two rooms displaying exhibits on this topic.

I don't know if there are any, but they probably are, since this "attraction" attracts quite a few tourists. The truth is, it's hard for me to understand why someone who doesn't have to would want to go into a prison and see how people were tortured and killed there. It's not an experience recommended for sensitive people, and certainly not for you to go there with children. For those who feel compelled to learn about Vietnam's history through prisons, if you've been or plan to go to the War Museum in Ho Chi Minh City, you can skip the experience.
I popped in here when i had a spare afternoon. I looked at everything within an hour. Its not a big prison. You could spend longer if you were to read everything.

We've been traveling for a year, and nowhere else have we been so disrespected as in this museum. We arrived 15 minutes before closing, but we weren't informed of this at the ticket office and only found out when we already had our tickets in hand (and a guidebook we bought to understand the history of the place). The guy who took our tickets at the entrance told us to hurry because it would close in half an hour. Five minutes later, the museum lights went out and a security guard came to ask us to leave the room we were in. He waved his arm indicating the exit as if he were shooing away a dog. In each room we entered on the tour, he or one of his colleagues came to hurry us along without any sympathy. We simply couldn't read or understand anything about the history. The impression was that the prison had been deactivated, but the prison-like spirit of the staff who work there still remains. Pay attention to the closing time, because you won't be informed when you buy your ticket!

You can spend about 20 minutes in this museum and see it all. The place is tiny. A few cells and some small descriptions written in awful English. Highlight was a giant guillotines. The place is stuffed to the brim with typical communist revolutionary propaganda. Would not recommend. We paid 30VND a person.
Not much to say about this prison your. Pretty boring. Not much happening. Only takes about 20 minutes. Unless you read all the info which a lot of it is fairly full on. Not at lot happening here

It's tiny and not much there is actually from the prison.  Lots of modifications. Also the information is poorly written in English and sometimes did not make sense. I have been to many prisons preserved a lot better.
This was once a prison, but not really much to see other than cells that were once occupied by prisoners.

The first part of the exhibition was interesting; it showed how the Vietnamese were imprisoned by the French. After that, we searched almost in vain for information about what happened with the Americans. The exhibition only showed how wonderful their time was, talking about Christmas and farewell gifts. Conversely, it only focused on how badly the Vietnamese were treated. Pure propaganda with many historical gaps. Disappointing...

I'm sorry to give just two stars, but there really wasn't much to see here. Lots of plaques to read with some reappearing photos, a main room with shackled mannequins (the highlight), a smaller  room with two cells and a few artifacts. I just expected more and was disappointed.
They portrayed the treatment of American prisoners as if they always treated with great with living conditions like a nice hotel.

Our visit to the prison was very disappointing. Much of the information presented is incomplete, and the strong ideological bias is evident in every explanatory text—especially in the final paragraph. Furthermore, the focus is almost exclusively on how the Vietnamese were treated by the French. The periods when Americans were imprisoned and tortured there are almost completely ignored or glossed over. In this way, the prison primarily serves as a propaganda tool and unintentionally demonstrates that those in charge are apparently still unable to critically examine their own actions. It cannot compare in any way to the War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City.

Typically political, with some nice spooky features like a model guillotine & statues of pretend prisoners.Took about 30 mins. to get around - a reasonable boredom killer. Doesn't tell a story like it should - such an interesting history in this country, its fair to expect more.Could be a lot better, hard to recommend as is.

We left the museum after a very short time. A load of propaganda..it is probably true that the life in prison was horrible for the Vietnamese inmates but it is very hard to belive that the Vietnamese were so nice and lovely to the American inmates..Sorry but we did not buy that

a museum with very little inside of it. cool that its in the actual prison but there aren't any cool artifacts and the narrative doesn't dive into any specific (interesting) stories.essentially a wikipedia page IRL.
But it's designed to bring the visitor closer to the Communist Party than to explain the injustices of the French. A must-see, but with a good dose of humor.
They focus on the place being a French prison. Most of it torn down for an office building. Not worth the time unless you want a picture in front of the gate.
Not very big or much to see. Luckily had  our own free tour guide to explain  things . Hard to see the conditions they were forced to live in and the treatment .
Was pretty interesting to see the French guillotine and John McCain's flight suit, that being said the majority of the information is historically inaccurate and mostly communist propaganda

The museum was clean and in very good condition but the viewpoint was  clearly one-sided (of course). The first half was on the French occupation and how horrible they were treated as prisoners (very sad if the information was true) and the second half was on how great they treated the US POWs. Having read and heard the stories of the US POWs held there...this portion showed footage of media manipulation and was designed to show them in the best light possible. Another note, the data at the prison didn't match the data showed at the Vietnam Military museum...at least make sure your stories match. History is history and it's disgraceful to try and spin any of it to ones benefit. It's worth a visit but only if with the understanding and open mind that it is very one-sided.

The only positive point is learning a little more about the Indochina War... otherwise, the prison is completely unauthentic, apart from a few cells, but everything else has been renovated. And barely a quarter of the prison remains; the rest has been destroyed. What a shame.

I am not a big fan of prisons as tourist attractions. It is certainly true that the Vietnamese have, for centuries, suffered greatly at the hands of outsiders but the exhibits here are so transparently propaganda that it hard to know what is true.
Quite nice and informative about the history of political prisoners. However, rather bare and dull.

I did learn something about the harsh treatment of Vietnamese political prisoners by the French colonialists and the formation of the Socialist party and that was interesting.Although i am not American, i had read John McCaine's biography and was interested to see the Vietnamese perspective on the war and their treatment of American soldiers. Ok it was a stupid war and America started it but how can they dare to pretend that they treated US pow's really well at that prison. It's sad that many people might believe their lies.

Most of the prison has been knocked down but a little has been kept. You can visit the cells and see a guillotine used by the French up to the 30's and photos of the results! US pow's were detained here during the war and there is photo evidence of how well they were treated, unfortunately it is the communist version, please google for the truth. You will only need half an hour here.

very 1 sided view on the history, french occupation shown as oppressors and the prison as a worst place on earth, same prison during Vietnam war with us supposedly turned into prison friendly .. a lot of propaganda not entirely in line with the historical truth
It quickly becomes clear that conditions in the prison were not good, regardless of the era in which it was established.
A great tour option to understand what happened many years ago in the Vietnam War... Photos, videos and models of everything that happened and
Rather touristy, hardly looks or feels like a prison anymore.  Instead, donate your money to Amnesty International or a Vietnam Vet fun of your choosing.

I was expecting way more from the prison, I expected it to have been maintained almost exactly as it had been originally so you could really get a feel of what the inmates went through. It was badly organised, I often ended up in rooms I had already been in by accident. And it's portrayed incredibly one sidedly.
Shows a decent amount of history but doesn't have a 'prison' feelNewly painted etc, more like a museumIf you have the time head over, short for time don't stress
Not objective.

Emphasis on French colonial time there & a little about the Vietnam ("American) war. Heavily biased of course, & not reflective of the history as i know it. Did not really like it , although some of our co-travellers did so.

A prison, a historical prison ok. Depressing like all prisons I visited. I am somehow happy I have seen it but was a bit disappointed by the lack of interesting information there. Not sure how accurate the info is either.... So after wards to lift up my spirit, I went for lunch at Amato, a small restaurant serving european food on 1a Trang Tien, right next to the back entrance of the Hilton Hanoi Opera Hotel (not the prison the real hotel! ;)

While the prison has a long history, including the internment of the Vietnamese by the French, the depictions of the US war ( as it is called in Vietnam) are completely bias and pure propaganda. Only a portion of the prison remains as most has been demolished for a new development, but the remaining portion appears to be representative depiction of the prison. Only two rooms are associated with the US war. One is a collection of photographs and news articles depicting demonstrations against the war from around the world and the second room includes photographs of US prisoners that show the prison as if it were a summer camp. There are pictures of men playing cards, playing volleyball and decorating a Christmas tree. All of which is totally propaganda and untrue.

The museum's collection is not very well organized. There is a lack of explanation regarding the context, colonialism, the Second World War, the development of communism... ultimately, it could also be called the propaganda museum.

A shrine to the heroes of the Vietnamese liberation struggle. Man, did these people suffer. To be approached respectfully. A small area commemorates some Americans who were imprisoned here, too. Bizarrely, most of the prison site is now a luxury housing development. Would you want to live here?
2015/05/24 Viewed from the car window. I was told I couldn't go inside.

Vast majority of it is devoted to the horrors of the French occupation and not to the war. he U.S. section was relatively small, and filled with propaganda. They've completely misunderstood the aim of a museum, to tell history from an objective point of view. Sad in a way because they could have made this really interesting and told a bit of their history as it was.
It is a creepy and sad experience to look at the exhibits. After the visit there it became clear once more that history books are written by the victor.

We walked and walked to get to Hoa Lo and frankly we needn't have bothered. Aside from a few prison cell doors and some accidentally lovely lighting, it was a wasted trip. There is no real signage. The literature on the walls goes on and on about everything but the facility itself. Even coming from India, where museums are monumentally badly done, this was a disappointment. As for the guillotine that's supposedly somewhere on the premises, don't even try to find it. We tried and failed, and ended up in a random room on the upper level, watching a vague documentary on the revolution in a red-carpeted space that looked like it had been done up by Kim Jong-Un.
We found it overpriced and poorly laid out.  The exhibits are poor quality.  We preferred the War Museum in Ho Chi Minh much more.

The story of Hoa Lo Prison is a story of poorly treated people, regardless of nationality. Horror from all sides in all wars, and an important place to visit in that regard. After the visit there it became clear once more that history books are written by the victor.
10 minutes in there are enough and the things you see could even be googled. In my opinion not worth to spend 30.000 VND

Hard to tell what was lies and what was truth. Also difficult to see what was actual prison and what has been renovated over for the tourists. All in all a disappointing visit, no need to make it a priority if you're in Hanoi.

This is one of those must see monuments in Hanoi but it is not well presented at all. Apart from the complete lack of information for the exhibits, there is nothing to guide you around the museum. Would you believe it, I missed seeing the infamous guillotine and a couple of the dioramas on the premises. Comsidering the prison is steeped in so very much history, it is a big letdown.

Exhibits were well sign posted with explanations written in different languages including English. The best part were the cells where death row inmates were held and reading about escapes from the prison. More photos than anything.
Everything is well organized and clearly labeled but the message that the French were evil and the Vietnamese were remarkably kind to American POWs was a little overdone.
Not really a place to visit if you have only 2-3 days in Hanoi.. the city is full of other great places, lakes, restaurants, beer corners and other sites...

I was disappointed in 'Hanoi Hilton'. However, entry was only 30,000 VND each (about $2 AUS), so no real harm done if you don't enjoy it.It is filled with propaganda. They present a very one-sided view of the Vietnamese 'revolutionary fighters' and American prisoners of war who were imprisoned there. It didn't feel authentic at all - the interior looked like it had just been repainted and looked clean and tidy. There are very few artefacts on display, and those that are, are in pristine condition. We also purchased the tour guide booklet upon entry (only 20,000 VND), which contained interesting and insightful information. However, most of the content in the booklet is displayed on signs in the prison.

I grew up in a communist Czechoslovakia. As a kid, I was fed the same propaganda stuff that is all over the walls in this museum. The language & choice of words are all vs imperialist enemies of the comrades & heroes & political prisoners under the French occupancy and American invasion etc etc. There are two sides of the story of course and in here you'll only find the one that is preached by Vietnamese government comrades. I once run away from the communist rule, while this was the first time since 1989 that I was again in a communist country. Made me feel awful & I struggled to keep quiet & polite. I'm not saying don't go though. If nothing else, it may make you realise how far certain countries moved on since the fall of Iron Curtain in Europe and how things are still going strong in Vietnam.

Whilst the majority of reviews consider the prison a interesting and educational experience, I found myself frustrated with both the poorly written English and the one sided (and somewhat distorted) view of what happened in the prison. There is no denying it is a harrowing and interesting place but I left feeling that the prison was simply a propoganda front. Wasn't overly impressed but would still recommend going, you just have to know what to expect.

History is always interpretive so I entered Hoa Lo expecting some "interpretation".  While the exhibits for the pre-1960's period were very well done, the imbalanced nature of how the stories were told made me skeptical and annoyed by the end of the visit.

I had higher expectations for this place. It was almost as though they were only telling one side of what happened in this prison. You can't tell me that the American Pilots imprisoned there were having fun and playing games all day and not once questioned for information.....

Probably worth a visit for historical buffs but it is dark and sad.  Do recommend a guide as this was a French Prison before it held the American Prisoners during the Vietnam Conflict. The guide will give you a better perspective.
A part from two rooms remaining from the old prison, there is not much of historic value and not much information in English.
As of January 17, 2015, the entrance fee had increased to 30,000 dong.

Not much information available or many cells to see. I thought I would be seeing a historic jail but instead it looks repossessed as a Museum and most cells are bordered up and used as offices or storage spaces for tools. The education was a little weak on detail. Still for 20 minutes of entertainment for $30,000 Dong (US$1.50) it was a time filler.

History is written by the winner so understand the party line of how good the North Vietnamese were and how bad the United States was.  As opposed to John Mccain's story, the treatment of US POWs is shown to be kind, almost fun-filled with plenty of food and good treatment. the US is the "imperialist."  Get over any outrage and absorb another side of history. To accent the horrid nature of the West there is depiction in great detail of how horridly the French treated the Vietnamese during colonial days. That takes more study on this reviewers part but does. It does give perspective to the fierce resistance against the French and then the US.

Their really isn't much worth visiting here for. The place's history has been completely whitewashed and, in the process, been destroyed.Don't bother going in - the doorway is the most interesting part.

I love Vietnam and its people. In no way is my review a reflection on their culture. I guess I had a bias (much like the prison itself) when going to the Hoa Lo Prison. After endeavors at the Killing Fields and S-21 in Cambodia a few weeks ago, from the hype I was hearing, I was expecting something hard hitting. Not the case. Seeing an old prison is neat in itself, but a good portion of it was sold or blocked off. The museum plays up the torture elements a lot more than what it appears as. The prison was used by French colonialists and this prison really is just like any other French prison during that time period. It's really nothing new or shocking that their prisons were notoriously uncomfortable and well beyond humane standards. Read the novel Papillion. But the prison really tries to paint a picture about the evil Frenchman targeting Vietnamese people. I don't think anyone condones the French attempts in colonizing Vietnam and imprisoning those who wanted independence, but something about it felt exaggerated and heavy on the propaganda. Once you get deeper into the war between Vietnam and the US, it becomes more prevalent. As a US citizen, it was an interesting experience seeing the war from the other side. It was humbling and embarrassing at the same time, but an experience worth going through. But then you get to the propaganda elements. They portrayed themselves as humane captors when imprisoning US soldiers, and they show footage of soldiers playing volleyball and just having a great time. They ignore the fact that John McCain has become somewhat popular for being a tortured Vietnam war vet who ran from president. As well, it doesn't take much research to see how many accounts there are about US soldiers being beaten and tortured at this prison. It's a little inaccurate on how they present the situation; near humorous. Overall, it was interesting to see, be it a little underwhelming. I wish it was a little more historically accurate and not a painted picture.

This side trip was a dud.  The guide spoke only of the atrocities committed by the French vs Vietnamese "rebels" between WWII and 1954.  Very little mention of what transpired there from '67 to '73.  A small room with photos of US military personnel in obviously staged propaganda situations gives a "Hogan's Hero's" type of existence when in actuality life at the Hanoi Hilton was horrific for physically and mentally abused American POW's.
This attraction would do better to reflect history in a more rounded review of realistic events. The attraction may not have represented the overall history from a balanced objective.

We heard from a tour guide that they had to renovate when the USA prisoners where brought there to make it a 5 star resort.  Where the audio tape stated they increased the size for more prisoners.  At the end of the museum they should a bunch of pictures of the Americans receiving medical attention playing chess and laughing.   Take it with a grain of salt.

I know that we shall not forget part of history (and even more the worst one to avoid it happening again), but this prison/museum is pointless. Cheap, disordered, shabby and creepy.. the only feeling you'll get from there is willing to escape as soon as possible. Only the small yard in the back looks more spiritual and respectful to me. I would not recommend this place.

It's just 20.000 dong so not even a dollar. For maybe an hour of pleasure. I almost walked away without seeing the American war prisoners section , sign posts are poor. Items are shown used by prisoners a lot spoons cups so avoidable.... I god some good titles to read from the book store Stanley karnow- Vietnam a history Robert Mason- chicken hawk Knowing you are standing in a place with so much history is worth the dollar but info is poor. Do not buy from the souvenir shop it's a rip off with items priced in dollars. The ticket to get in vs the prices are insane and are average lots of Vietnam and some hanoi items you could get in the streets for a better price ...

This really wasn't all that interesting. More than half of the prison is now a massive hotel / apartment block.The part that is left has been changed significantly from its original interior. The artefacts on show aren't all that interesting and all of the letters and a lot of the explanations are written in French or English.The place was very small and not very well represented (especially when you compare that to War Remnants museum in HCMC) and having spent a whole dollar and half an hour inside I don't feel that I know any more about the place than I did before hand.It's a shame this is so poor.

The Hanoi Hilton was a propaganda prison during the Vietnam War (or American War depending on where you are from).  And it still is.  Although the vast majority of it is devoted to the horrors of the French occupation and not to the war.  I'm glad I went but it wasn't a highlight.

The French imprisonment of the Vietnamese was displayed emphasizing the atrocities committed.  The Vietnamese imprisonment of the Americans was displayed as if the prisoners were attending some private, vacation getaway:  basketball, movies, chess, happy times.  This traveler hopes that someday a more factual display will be presented.

We were very keen to visit - but it was disappointing.  The only interactive item has two headpieces in English - one of which was broken and the translation was almost incomprehensible.  For not much effort on behalf of the museum, this could have been a fascinating insight into this rich historical museum.  Yes, yes, its skewed to one view point but you expect that and cant care about that.  I had hoped for a few more photographs and 'fact' ,and less clay models and questionable painting of walls.  I didn't learn much or get much of a sense of the history at all.

Was surprised to see Trip Advisor ask me to review this.  When I was in Hanoi, the tour guide I hired said "Oh, that building no longer there.  Torn down."  Now I see differently.  Hmm...  Now I'm kind of bummed.
I didn't like it at all; it's a bad museum, and it's one of those places where you can still feel the negative energy. Honestly, it's definitely not a "must-see."

What you will see is a small part of the original prison, the majority of which was demolished in the 1990s. The most interesting part of the "prison" today is the propaganda you will see that emphasizes how poorly the incarcerated Vietnamese were treated under the French, and how well the Americans were treated by the Vietnamese when they were held here. Incredibly, there is a very old guillotine on display from the French period.

I was pretty disappointed with the prison. The place is down right depressing and boring even for someone who genuinely cares about historical significance and has some reverence for the past. The exhibits are all disjointed and have very little explanation of what they are or how they fit together historically. Focuses more on displaying a collection of random artifacts than it does on giving an understandable chronological history of what happened at the prison over the course of history (Other than the fact that it was generally a terrible place to visit at any point in time including the present). Half the displays at the prison show artifacts indicating the horrors of the French colonial period, and then the next exhibit is memorabilia from the Vietnam war and celebrates the French as moral allies and supporters against the US. No explanation of how things changed. I would read up on your history before coming here otherwise you will find it just as boring, depressing and confusing as I did. There are much better ways to spend your time in Hanoi.

I was really wanting to see the prison, but I was disappointed when I got there.  My poor husband was dragged along and hated that it was worse than he was anticipating.  The propaganda on the Vietnamese side is a joke.  Honestly, it wasn't worth my time.

Me, the Canadian, was the one who wanted to see Hoa Lo Prison, not my husband who is American. I wish we had skipped it.  While both of us have a strong anti-war sentiment, the dose of Vietnamese propaganda was repulsive, ie. their displays of "kindness to POW's"  "we the Vietnamese treated our uninvited guests well".  Whatever happened to the fact it was "the Vietnamese against the Americans" it was also the North against the South Vietnam?     I went back to our hotel and read Senator John McCain's writing about his reality of time as a POW in Hoa Lo.  If they are going to post so many photos of him in the Prison and his jump suit and personal effects on full display, he deserves to be able to share about his torture at their hands, no?  Nothing is good about war, but I can't get over how Vietnam is so comfortable still spewing what is obviously Communist lies and propaganda.  I regret spending money for the entrance fee.

The Hoa Lo prison was built by the French to house Vietnamese political prisoners. It was then used by the Vietnamese for American POW's during the Vietnam War.  Not much of the expansive original grounds/buildings remain, but a scaled down model inside shows what it used to be. The museum is mostly to showcase torture/brutality of the Vietnamese political prisoners by the French, including a guillotine.  A very small room is dedicated to the American POWs and shows games of basketball and happy Christmas celebrations.  We were even told that the prison was a place to keep the POWs fed and healthy for the duration of the war. Having read a number of accounts written by these POWs which detail instances of serious brutality and torture, something does not jive.  Those inaccuracies (which then led us to question the accuracy of the rest of the museum), coupled with the small size / dim lighting (it was a prison, so not surprising), I wouldn't say that this is a must visit in Hanoi.  However, entrance fee is minimal, approximately 1 USD equivalent, and it's easy to get through in under an hour.

I found the prison to be informative and it is horrible to hear of the atrocities that happened there. However some of the pictures and information are lacking  descriptions and English translations  and if I hadn't purchased the guidebook along with my ticket I would probably still be a little lost.

There was not a lot of prison left as big Singapore company had bought most of land and built huge luxury hotel next to it.   What was left seemed very unreal and tacky.  Plenty of photos of the Americans held there but all looked too staged.   Lots of gruesome things from when French ruled.  I expected much more and would not put this on my list of things to see

Went with my wife who isn't really into this sort of thing, but being a bit of a military history buff I really wanted to go.  Only about 1/4 of the actual prison remains however, so the displays are few in number.  But that doesn't take away from the historical value when it comes to talking about the revolution during the French occupation, where a number of Vietnamese patriots were imprisoned and/or executed here (this section saved me from giving only 1 star).  The section about American pilots during the war is extremely laden with propaganda.  They have a video playing showing and talking about how the prisoners were very luck to have been here (as opposed to other prisons).  Saying things such as they were treated with the utmost respect and fed very well (while the normal Vietnamese citizen didn't have half the food, as they point out).  Having read a few books on the war this is a load of crap and simply disinformation/propaganda.  The video playing showing the prisoners eating large meals, playing volleyball, smoking, watching films, & listening to the guards play music for them was essentially shot in one day...then the malnutrition, beatings, & torture resumed.  This was unfortunate knowing that a large number of people didn't have 1/10th of the knowledge of the war as I did and would go away from there thinking "hey, these guys were pretty lucky".  As William Tecumseh Sherman put it, "war is hell" and these guys were not treated with a lick of respect or anything for that matter...The Vietnamese frequently pointed out that the Americans were committing war crimes but failed to tell the truth about the atrocities.  I wouldn't waste my time here unless you personally knew someone imprisoned or are a military history freak like me.
I know this is something you should see but it was difficult to see the conditions and the pictures of what so many people endured. You must make up your own mind.

After visiting the prison and museums in cambodia and Saigon, this museum was disappointing. It lacked creativity and often the letters and explanations were not translated in English which left me feeling uninformed.

Hey its a prison right? Shouldn't be enjoyable. But it was very small, no tour guide was present. I was in and out in 5 minutes. Watch the great underrated movie Hanoi Hilton with Micheal Moriarty and Paul LeMat instead.

It was a lot smaller than we expected.  We did not enjoy it, and felt that most of things that were posted up around the museum were lies.  The words are very much Viet Kong words, and say that the people starved because the Americans took all of the food.  My father is a Vietnam vet, and did not appreciate what was said.  Will not visit again.

Honestly, this was terrible in my oppinion. I have visited many old prisons before and this has to be the worst. The information was terrible and the way they have "done it up" is ridiculous, it doesnt even feel like it was ever a prision. By all means go and visit, but remember- whatever you think you are going to see.... You wont.

With the history that abounds in the "Hanoi Hilton", we were very disappointed with the actual site.  The U.S. section was relatively small, and filled with propaganda.  Granted there has been so much propaganda on both sides that it is hard to know the facts, but the pictures and descriptions paint the prison as a happy place where US prisoners were treated very well.  Of particular interest was the flight suit of Senator John McCain.

Not much to see and no explanations in French. The few cells offer a glimpse into the practices of the colonizers. Furthermore, it's such a shame knowing that three-quarters of the prison was demolished to make way for two office towers...

Glad that this was five minutes way from our hotel, as would have been classed as a wasted journey. Quite small exhibit, although some of the War info was interesting but just not enough. then again, i dont think i would lile it presented in a glossy,western way either..

I went here today based on a few recommendations. Its was so boring. The best thing were the old cells and the creepy music they played. That made the atmosphere interesting at times. There is only a tiny section of the old prison complex left. The knocked the rest to build a silly modern tower block. From the model in the museum it seems as though they knocked down the more interesting parts. The memorial wall in nice however.
It's only part of the prison now; there's not much to see. There are many photos that don't necessarily have anything to do with the prison.
The truth is, very little or nothing remains of the prison, and the information they provide is scarce. It can easily be ignored.
While the historical significance of the place might make you want to visit, the truth is it lacks any real charm.

For us visiting this "museum" was a total waste of time. I sympathize with the people imprisoned here as it must have been a terrible time for them. Still it's so obviously communist propaganda throughout the whole place that there is just no point visiting. They've completely misunderstood the aim of a museum, to tell history from an objective point of view. Sad in a way because they could have made this really interesting and told a bit of their history as it was. Go if you're into communist propaganda and skip it if you get annoyed when historical facts are grossly distorted. Entrance is 20.000VND per person.

This place was full of propaganda. It was not worth the price.  There was creepy music playing like I was in a haunted house or something. It does not even compare to the museums in Cambodia about war. They tried way to hard to make it sad and it came out really hokey and over the top.
Cost 20,000 VND...if you have been to the Tuol Sleng Museum in Cambodia...I wouldn't bother with this...!  Would learn more off the internet.
We went to see it but it's not really worth it; if you're short on time, it's better to see other things.
The prison is of course of great historic importance. But there's little that remains of the original prison and the story told is too one sited.

I am all for hearing the story of a country's history, but this museum did a great job of telling its story. The French used it as a central hotel building back in the day, then it was used as a prison for 'rebels' to Vietnam. Basically the whole thing is about how Vietnam was killing its own people as rebels before the Americans came there and killed the Vietnamese themselves. Interesting that it was a place for downed US pilots during the 70's. Didn't really enjoy this museum.

how well they were treated. Not as interesting as I had hoped. A lot about the french and how they imprisoned the locals. A really bad place to be locked up but not as interesting as I thought it would be

Most of the proson is gone with just a small part left which was not very interesting.  Most is about the French colonial days with only a small part relating to the barbarian Americans playing ping pong.  I would skip it.

Very small bit left mostly devoted to french imperialists and the noble Vietnamese Communist warriors. Heavily skewed to pro Vietnamese interpretation of US prisoners.. so much so made one wonder why they didnt ask to stay on after peace.

Most of the information in this prison seems to concentrate on the French colonial era up to 1954, telling how awful the French were and how patriotic and heroic the Vietnamese were. It significantly fails to address how the prison was used from 1954 when it was under Vietnamese control, when there were a number of American POW's held here. I was hugely disappointed when I visited Hoa Lo Prison.  I normally have a thing for prisons - just really enjoy visiting and hearing the tales of famous prisoners and relevant conflicts.  But Hoa Lo Prison (better known as the "Hanoi Hilton" in the West) was a bit of a letdown.Just to be clear: I don't have a problem at all with the Vietnamese take on history, although I could imagine some patriotic Americans getting upset.  Most of the prison is dedicated to talking about the horrors undergone by Vietnamese freedom fighters at the hands of their French colonial masters in the prison.  However, after independence we are told that the gaolers suddenly became considerably more enlightened in their sensitive and caring treatment of American GIs during that unfortunate period of conflict.  I fully expected that, and usually hearing the "other" side of things makes it more interesting, not less.But it is still a poor tour.  Firstly, the majority of the prison was torn down to make way for the massive Hanoi Towers next door (which, on the plus side, makes it easy to find...).  So you only really get to tour about a quarter of the old prison.  Secondly, the tour is massively random - you just keep circling back over yourself in a demented figure of eight.  It really did feel like following breadcrumbs back and forth.  Lastly, there isn't very much to it.  It is small - there are very few relics left from olden times, and you are hard pressed to spend more than half an hour there.Most of the tour focuses (understandably) on the Vietnamese war of independence.  Only two rooms are really dedicated to the "American war".  Perhaps not surprising, those two rooms were fairly full of American tourists absolutely absorbed, and the rest of the tour was sparsely populated.  Then at the end of the tour, if you want to visit the gift shop, you have to work your way back across the prison, but, unfortunately, it is filled with very banal standard Vietnam tourist souvenirs - generic T-shirts, carvings and paintings.  Absolutely nothing specific to the prison (I have a friend who knew people who were incarcerated in the prison during the conflict, and I wanted to bring back something for him - but there is absolutely nothing specific to the site - not even a key chain, far less an amusing "I survived the Hanoi Hilton" T-shirt - I guess it is all still a bit raw for the Vietnamese).To be completely fair, it is only 10,000 Dong (US$0.50), so it is still a place I would suggest people pop in and visit.  But for me overall it was a bit of a disappointment.

I understand that all museums and memorials have a built in bias, but saying that the American fliers spent their time here playing basketball and ping pong, and cooking chickens, is like saying that the Nazi concentration camp inmates went sailing and ate bon-bons all day.  This was the one place in Vietnam that I felt animosity towards Americans.

The prison dates to 1896 when it was used to hold and torture anyone (local Vietnamese) who opposed the French government (Back then the French held power in Vietnam as THEIR colonial interest).   In the mid to late 20th century (US War), Hoa Lo was then used by Vietnamese locals to imprison American POWs who gave the site the nickname “Hanoi Hilton.” Most of the prison was demolished in 1990 to make room for a high rise (Hanoi Towers).  A small corner of the original prison still exists dedicated to the history of the prison’s past.   There was lots of propaganda media, but mainly about how the French used the prison in the first half of the 1900s as a place of incarceration and torture for anyone that opposed the French governing.  Why there is even a Guillotine on display and pictures of severed heads.Only as a side feature is there information about the 600 American prisoners of war that were held there (some over 9 years), during the Vietnam / American War.Besides the citadel this was the only other location worth historical viewing in this very crowded and trash covered city.   Yes there are some sights and experiences in this city but based on the pollution and many motor bikes we have no desire to visit the area again.

Built in 1896 by the French colonizer with the name: 'Maison Centrale', for 450 prisoners, that number increased to 2000 around 1930. Mostly political prisoners and opponents. During the American war (we call it the Vietnam War), Hoa Lo became infamous as a prison for downed American pilots, who gave their place of incarceration the ironic nickname Hanoi Hilton. The original nameplate still hangs above the entrance, but a large part of the cell complex was demolished in 1997. The preserved part is furnished as a museum, which mainly refers to the colonization period. The museum exhibits consist of chains, whips and other torture instruments and some preserved cramped isolation cells. In a small section there are some photos and material from the American period. The whole thing actually doesn't amount to much.

Okay place to visit. Gives a person a good understanding of the poor conditions of prisoners. Also of the long history of the prison from when the French built it in the late 1890’s. One of the sites included by our guide. The original prison dates back to the French occupation of Vietnam and the imprisonment of political prisoners seeking Vietnamese freedom. It is much smaller than you think with only two blocks of the original prison still standing. That said it gives you an insight into the terrible conditions experienced by the prisoners. It also is renowned for being the prison for American airmen during the Vietnam War. While their conditions were much better it was still bad. Be prepared for the propaganda video!
Interesting for learning about local history and the people who were imprisoned in these facilities in the center of Hanoi. A must-see for history buffs.

The original Prison was built by the French in 1896 to hold Vietnamese patriotic & revolutionary fighters. What remains of the original Prison is interesting as is the way they have staged the exhibits. It does contains historical photos and documents from the turn of the 20th century. If you are in to prisons and in particular political prisons then you’ll enjoy this if not if not skip it or view it from the outside.

The exhibit was done very well.  The history was interesting.  However, if you are miltary buff and wanted a perspective of the american side it was very little.  Most of the exhibit was dedicated to  the imprisonment of the vietnamese during the French control.

Interesting exhibits that focuses more on the French imprisonment of Vietnam people rather then how the prison was used for POW during the war whilst there is some information available they have left out key parts of the prisons history. I left feeling as though I hadn’t got the who history of this place.

You will leave here thinking the French and Americans were the only barbarians connected to this place. The shocking conditions, torcher and malfeasance towards the revolutionary prisons by the French is described in great and shocking detail. The treatment of the US prisons by contrast is critiqued from the perspective of compassion towards the captives!  Nothing on the treatment of POWs. Nothing. Disappointing.
Pros- interesting to be able to walk in the prison and great English explanations. We also used the audio headphones.Cons- super biased and a little inaccurate.

Interesting museum but too full of propaganda! Pictures of American prisoners enjoying putting up a Christmas tree and playing pool did not sit easy.  I certainly did not agree with the Americans getting involved with the war in any way but like to see how they were treated!
