I decided to research on my return to the UK and this prison, known as the Hanoi Hilton, was the worst POW camp - and the Americans were not treated well at all.  I prefer the truth when visiting a museum and this was not!

Highly recommend buying the audiobook, it definitely gave some extra information that was really useful. However, we found only the first half was informative. From there, you get expected propaganda on US POWs. I do not condone either side of the war.. but it's far from an accurate rendition. Disappointing.

I'd say this is a site worth visiting, but keep in mind its purpose is Communist propaganda.  It's divided roughly into 2 sections:  Vietnamese prisoners under the French who were treated abominably, and U.S. prisoners under the Vietnamese who were treated with the utmost compassion and respect.I learned a lot about resistance against the French.The section on U.S. POWs was a horrific whitewash.  The photos and narration show them being treated incredibly well, and the uneducated visitor might swallow this.  The reality was starvation, torture, and outright murder were the norm for most of the time there, until the fear of bad publicity forced a change.I of course do not mind, and even welcomed, reading the Vietnamese view of the war.  I do mind propaganda and outright lies to cover up brutality.
One hot afternoon, after months of planning, I finally decided to go to Hoa Lo Prison. And it was a fascinating experience.

Actually the building now was only maintained part from its original which is now opened as a museum. When walked further inside you will see those horrible rooms that really make you scare and crazy. Can't imagine those prisoners lived there as being buckled in the bed. I don't like the feeling being there because I really felt sad to see the people being tortured and how many lucky one can get out from there alive!

Although somewhat interesting, for the most part a bit boring! Seventy five percent of the original prison building has been torn down. A few interesting artifacts are displayed but very few. If you decide to go it’s ok but, if you decide to skip it you aren’t missing much!

How do you rate a prison museum?  The audio tour had a lot of information, especially about the earlier history where the French held the Vietnamese, but I found myself just wanting to see where John McCain was held for five years.  There is a display case just about him, with English descriptions of how he was "saved" from Truc Bach Lake and given medical care.  The actual wing where Americans were held is no longer standing, but you can get an idea of how grim, depressing, and deplorable the conditions had to have been.  Group detention and single cells in the remaining part, at the front, have been filled with life-size mannequins.   There is a gift shop.
the prison since the France be in North Vietnam and later this place use for USA soldiers, place which call "Hilton Hanoi" . It is not simple to see what was there - but it must to visit.
Very basic museum with not much information around though it is interesting and there are some noteworthy displays. Worth a visit if you have time to kill.

Although the ‘Hanoi Hilton’ is a must visit, it’s really just for the name these days, as the majority of the place has been built over. Nevertheless, there are some interesting exhibits and it’s worth taking 30/40 minutes to take a look round and try to get a feel of what it was like to be taken there in the war.

Hoa Lo Prison is a facility where the French imprisoned Vietnamese, and later the Vietnamese imprisoned Americans. It's a true reflection of the oppression and cruelty that one person can inflict on another. A place for those interested in history.

A symbol of oppression, like any prison, here also a symbol of dehumanization and crime. The inmates divide the suffering into their own, which symbolizes their own courage and the crimes of others, and the suffering of others—in their case, it continues to be their own courage and the crimes of others. This museum is terrifying because it proves that, some 50 years after the war, the authorities are repeating a narrative based on selected facts.
An interesting and historical tour. There is a lot of reading and if you read it all it will take a long time. But a good break from the busy streets of Hanoi.

The original prison was demolished in 1990, and today there is a replica where visitors can tour the cells, interrogation room, guillotine, and see the conditions of the prisoners there. Tickets can be purchased in advance online.

The history and Vietnamese perspective are very interesting and informative.  Much of how modern Vietnam has come to be is apparent... The museum/prison itself however isn’t really all that riveting.  Definitely worth a visit but temper your expectations.

This facility shows how the Vietnamese Communist Party was organized during the French colonial era. The exhibits were far more intense than I expected, and I was struck by the terrifying power of the colonial power, France. It
It could have been interesting, but the explanations are far too brief and lack any historical context.

We spent 60-70 minutes and had the audio guide but it was hard to hear the voice over the ‘background’ music. Definitely interesting to see how the French Colonialists mistreated the Vietnamese during their occupation, and the Americans who were captured and held here appear to have had good conditions for a prison. I wouldn’t recommend this as a must do in Hanoi.

The audio was a little wordy and not really sure it was exactly necessary. Tip: if you have an extra set of earbuds that fit standard phones (not the new iphone ear buds) you can connect to the audio set and only pay for one. There’s an extra outlet which we noticed after the fact.
A visit isn't a must. Interesting to see and hear the stories, but the design is somewhat lackluster in places.

Interesting visit, but the focus is overwhelmingly on the experience of Vietnamese prisoners under the French. The small section about US prisoners is whitewashed. Skip the audio tour, which is informative but loaded with propaganda.

If you have interest in the American Vietnam war this has a lot of interesting history. In some ways even more interesting than the Vietnam war was the French occupation and some of those stories. Frankly it’s interesting enough but not a highlight or a must see.

Interesting take on some parts of history but goid none the less, more interesting to see visitors of a certain age from certain countries fighting with their memories. Must of been so difficult. Place worth a visit.

It provides an interesting history of the country and the POWs that were imprisoned there during the Vietnam war.  There is plenary to read in English without the need for the audio tour.  I am not a huge fan of museums and $13 USD for an hour tour seems high.

First the good. It’s easy to find. We were told it was free but it was only 10K VD. The museum is logically laid out and is easy to follow. Displays have English entries. Audio is offered for 50K VD. Interested perspective on how the French and US plays in Vietnamese history. Down side. It’s a lot of propaganda but once you realize what the underlying themes are it’s easy to get by it all. It is worth the visit. Just realize that the victors get to write their history.

Inside, there were exhibits of the cells and guillotine tools used at the time, and it was so sad that it left me feeling very dark and heavy-hearted. Entering the cells gave me the chills of being in a haunted place.
Not that exciting really. Audio guide was poor. Could have been good if items had been left in original place, and the main prison kept.

Unfortunately, not much of the prison complex remains, so you can get a relatively quick overview (about an hour). The prison gives a grim impression and shows the conditions under which, among others, the French imprisoned people there.

Interesting and distressing at times to see the rooms/cells and learn about prisoners life here in the Hanoi Hilton. Display told us how good life was here for the American soldiers - am fairly sure they would  not  have felt that way all of the time. Worth seeing.

I would say you get some insight into how the prisoners lived day to day. I personally don’t know if the political information provided is unbiased. Overall, I enjoyed walking the streets of Hanoi. Stopping in at the prison wasn’t a highlight. It nonetheless is not a regret.

The building is clean because it has been renovated. While it was once used to house American prisoners of war during the Vietnam War, it originally held Vietnamese political prisoners. You can learn a lot about its history here.

It really had 2 lives.  One as a prison and torture center run by the French for Vietnamese people who protested. Once as a cushy jail where American fighter pilots captured in the war had everything but freedom. Recordings of how Vietnamese people "saved"him from the lake.  Christmas dinner letters and momentos from home basket ball games...you get it.  Except i seem to remember that John McCains time there was all but cushy. Having seen John McCains interviews about this time I know he was beaten, tortured and kept in solitary Ha Lo is largely self serving propaganda.   The tour seems honest pre war but not so much post war. Keep in mind while touring.  Do you need the audio meh I got it but could have just read and looked had I known the stories would be largely BS.

Touring any prison is a bit horrific. The rosy picture of how the enemy American prisoners were treated here is an interesting take. I'm sure the American Senator John McCain has a very different take of his 5.5 years here. Worth going to.

Came away feeling that it was an important part of our tour around Hanoi, but the exhibition is small.  Much of the prison building itself has been sold off to property developers, so only a small section is left.  That being said, it is a sobering place, and an important part of the city's history.  Rose coloured specs version of treatment of the US prisoners at the end of the tour.
This is where France captured and imprisoned Vietnamese independence activists.

It was okay. It was interesting to see the history , sad about the torture. entry is reasonable but then they try and sell you the audio part , approx 100,000 dong which makes the tour not overly economical. It doesnt engross you for hours.  The gift shop is reasonably priced, I thought I got ripped off with my wooden box with chopsticks but overall it was okay. The drink vending machine was well priced. It took us 30mins to see it.
If you have some time or are in the area, it's worth visiting the prison. However, if you've already visited another prison in Vietnam, it's not worth it.

There's not much left of the 'Hanoi Hilton' now , as only the gatehouse remains. It's worth a visit though. Interesting to find out how cruel the French were when they occupied Vietnam as normally just associate this prison with American prisoners during the Vietnam/American war.

There was not much to see in the prison. Most of the original structure has been demolished, replaced by the tower right behind. Guessing because it's an infamous reminder of the Communist military's past. Only worth a visit if you are bored.
If you're in Hanoi, it's definitely worth a visit. Entry is cheap, and you can spend some time exploring the old prison, first used by the French and then by the Vietnamese.
Yes, interesting story about colonial oppression, but how about add in the bit about communist oppression post colonial period
A grim experience, but unfortunately a true story. Nicely done and good orientation in English. Beautiful outdoor areas.
A historical site that hints at the horrors and cruelties of its operators.

This place is not the greatest tourist attraction in the world, but then again that’s interesting enough to spend an hour or so in. This is mainly focused around how the French use the prison to keep local Vietnamese in 100 years ago. Minimal focus on post 1960’s prisoners etc, i.e. where Westerners would know the prison as the Hanoi Hilton. There was only a small portion of the original prison retained, but it certainly gives you the feel for what it was. Only about one dollar to get in, and you can guide yourself around very easily. If you have a free hour, it is definitely worth doing, but if you’re trying to maximise your time and see the sights, this is something that you could miss without too much regret.

Certainly worth the visit very informative and thought-provoking on the inhumane conditions of the Vietnamese politcal prisoners. However, the American POW section presents their time in the prison as somekind of a happy summer camp, which I certainly don't believe. I am not American so nothing personal, but it seems clear you don't get the whole truth on the treatment of the American POW. Because of this only 3 stars.
Interesting and important part of French Colonial Vietnamese history.We got very bad vibes here - reflecting past events in this place with Madam Guillotine still in situ.

Fairly interesting tour which details the imprisonment of the Vietnamese by colonial French forces as well as the later imprisonment of American pilots during the Vietnam war. I wouldn't place much credence on its factual accuracy. The prison is described as "Hell of hell" when run by the French, but suddenly turns into a happy summer camp for Americans when imprisoned by the Vietnamese (photos of smiling Americans and alleged doctors). The propaganda is rife throughout, which I found quite amusing. Other than some interesting photos there isn't a great deal to view here. It's a time filler, not a "must-see".

If you're to believe everything at the Hoa Lo Prison, you would believe that the Vietnamese were always persecuted, but they were good to their captives.  (I think John McCain recalled otherwise).   I know that the winners always get to write history, but I don't really believe that POWs were only "temporarily detained"

The structure and organization of the prison, as well as the cruel conditions of detention endured by Vietnamese people during French colonization, are well presented. However, the decrees detailing the horrors perpetrated by the French state are not translated into English and are therefore inaccessible to English speakers. Regarding the conditions of detention for American soldiers, the descriptions are beyond comprehension. It's a shame that the visit is conducted against a backdrop of communist propaganda.

Tickets were cheap to get in, from what remember roughly around 30,000 Dong (around a dollar 50 American). Do not fall for paying to rent the earphone system, you dont need it as long as you speak English. Most, if not all, of the signs in the old prison have English included on them explaining what went on in the different locations of the prison. We had our 2 year old son with us and had no issues pushing him around in a travel stroller. Might have to lift it up a few times here and there but nothing a strong buck cant handle. The reason I gave it a lower scoring is because I found out that about 70% (if not more) of the prison was not the original. So basically you are on the same grounds of all that torture but the majority of the building itself was not the original and felt a little disappointed. Call me petty I suppose but all in all it was a great experience for the low cost.
There wasn't much to see.

I am the anti-French overseas Vietnamese, when I visited Lo I was almost crying when I recalled the days of suffering the aggression. I really don't want to use the Orpheo audio guide when knowing where it came from. You should use a device that does not remind others of the past
A good place to explore the sad lives of the prisoners that had to experience hardships. They have a nice display of prisoners full of sad and distressed expressions. A sad piece of history

The former prison complex for Hanoi gives a historical journey through it's working life and a good understanding of the conditions in which the prisoners were kept. It costs only VND30k (approx. 1£) for an adult. You can pay extra for an audio tour, but the info boards do have English descriptions.

Hoa Li prison was good. Nothing over the top that makes it fascinating. Walk yourself through in about 40 min as it’s not that big. 40k dong per person to enter. You can also get a audio tour with headphones for a additional 50k dong per person if you want.  We didn’t get it as every sign had English on it. It’s a pretty cheap way to kill a hour and learn a little about the prison and some history plus it air conditioned which was a bonus when it’s 34 degrees out.
Definitely worth a look, doesn’t take long to go through... had a bit of trouble finding each point on the Audible tour as many numbers not in sight.

The Prison was an interesting place to visit, however has a very narrow focus and really hammers home a few key pointers. These are the injustice faced by Vietnamese prisoners under the french rule, and the above average treatment received by American POWs. A little more variety in information would really benefit the place, and the English is almost non existent unless you buy a guidebook.

Provided a good insight into the prisons history during French occupation and the revolution period. Touched briefly on American POWs and had a memorial too. It would of been good to have more detail into the day to day life of a prisoner. It gives you a feel on how they were treated by the guards but not so much life in pristine.Instead it is quite heavy on the injustices against the pro-revolution Vietnamese prisoners there which is understandable of course. It is a memorial as well as a museum for them. It outlines the resolve of the people and how they took secretive measures to provide political teaching in the prison. Focuses mainly on the organisation of a political party inside the prison and how they remained focused on independence and overcoming French rule.
On the other hand, logically, considering what it's about, I didn't like that the audio guides were only available in English and French.

Most of the original prison has been torn down, like 90 percent. Has a couple jail cells to look at with some wax like prisoners on display for effect. Has some newspaper articles on display but all in all it was very boring and staged.
Well presented historical viewpoint on Vietnam and the French and American occupations. Chilling reminder of days gone by
An insight into the history of French settlement and rule in Vietnam along with some history of the Vietnam War .
The prison is quite small, and personally, I didn't find it very engaging. So, only visit if you have the time; it's not a must-see.

The prison is quite interesting. You can learn a lot about its history and how prisoners were treated over time. You can see and read about how the prisoners "lived" and were treated there. If you're in Hanoi, you should definitely visit the prison. Like almost all museums in Vietnam, the entrance fee is very cheap (around €1), so you can't really go wrong.
An old prison. The history is interesting, but there's not much to see. You can see the cells and some photographs. The rest isn't interesting.
Interesting! Good way to kill time if your bored. Staff are friendly and the audio guide is worth purchasing
We were aware when going that the story would be told from one perspective and this certainly was the case. Nevertheless an interesting hour on a rainy afternoon .
The visit was a bit disappointing. The space is small in proportion to what you have to pay. They somehow forget that they themselves also did terrible things during the war.

We went here after going to the one in Cambodia- the Museum of Genocidal Crime (S21). In our opinion you can skip this and go to Ha Long Bay, Sapa, or other sightseeing spots in Hanoi. We used the audio tour and it really wasn't what we were expecting. We ended up walking out before finishing up the prison. If you're in Hanoi and have nothing to do then sure go for an hour but I wouldn't recommended it as a must do.
Like all prisons: inhumane and degrading living conditions. Annoying communist propaganda: they treated American prisoners well, and what a coincidence, they even showed photos to prove it...

Visited with a tour group. There is only about 15% of the original prison left. Land no longer part of the prison used for commercial businesses including the Somerset Grand Hanoi Hotel. Museum and displays more anti-French and the French Colonial period, that were very trying times. Very little regarding the imprisonment and treatment of US service personnel. There are a few pictures that implied good treatment, which was not true. Consider saving the price of admission by reading up on the history of the prison online, hopefully a balanced history, and walking around what remains of the original building which is a little more than the front façade. What I found most interesting were the historical photos and diagrams.

Small part of the old prison can be walked through, shows some cells, and memorial. Good information (in English) given on critical events. The place can get crowded with a lot of people moving between rooms, try to avoid following a large group.

My husband wanted to see it, but I'm afraid of places like that. Younger generations shouldn't come see it because it shows how terrifying the crimes of people in the past were. If you disregard the confinement aspect, it was a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity to see it.
The old Hilton Hanoi is worth visiting but the negative propaganda gets a bit tiring, A lot of the original prison no longer exists. Well worth visiting, if you have time

The Hoa Lo Prison was built by the French and mainly housed Vietnamese political prisoners.The stories are interesting, the visual displays at times disturbing, but I also found they very much glossed over its use during the Vietnam War (American or Civil War as it’s known in Vietnam) as a POW prison where it became known as the Hanoi Hilton.Worth a quick visit.
This is definitely worth seeing and interesting but I don’t think it’s the best historical prison exhibition I have ever seen. Worth a visit but not the highlight of the things we saw in Hanoi.
What was on display?! However, it remains a historically significant place. A place for learning about the negative aspects of history. It gave me a lot to think about.

Going through the Hoa Lo Prison, one gets a good sense of the injustice and desperation experienced by the inmates. The display rooms are cramped, the halls are dark, and the general atmosphere is cold. I suppose this is the main intention of the attraction, and it accomplishes it well. However, the presentation and layout of some of the exhibits could have been better. Descriptions and explanations could have been better. Unlike some other attractions, the history could have been made to come alive. Similar to the Conciergerie in Paris (although a bit better), it was overall a bit drab, and one could easily get tired of  all the lifelessness.

My friends and I visited the Hao Lo Prison during our stay in Hanoi because we wanted to visit the infamous prison used by the French and Vietnamese.The exhibition was pretty decent; they had a nice layout and did a good job of keeping the prison in tact. They had English signs so we were all able to follow along.The first half of the prison is used to cover the French treatment of local Vietnamese prisoners, which we found quite interesting.The second half is all about how the Vietnamese used it during the Vietnam War with the United States. Not to be insensitive but the exhibit was laughably biased. The museum talked about how great the American prisoners were treated and how they were even given gift bags when released. they had several staged photos of the prisoners writing letters home to their families and playing volleyball in the yard. As people who are very familiar with the atrocities committed by both the Americans and Vietnamese during this brutal war, we were amazed how blatantly bias this museum was.However I would still recommend going if for no other reason than to see the famous Hanoi Hilton. I would encourage any visitors to do some research on their own before going so that you are not sold into believing their propaganda.

The museum is set in the small remaining section of the prison as the rest has been knocked down for redevelopment.There are some graphic images that depict how Vietnamese prisoners were tortured and the place has an eerie feeling from knowing how many people died within the prison. There appears to be one main theme of the prison and that is how badly the political Vietnamese prisoners were treated whilst they were there. There are also small sections in how well they treated the American pilots who were there in the 70’s. It gives a good insight to the prisons history albeit from a rather biased viewpoint.
Not a ton to see here, but some great stories to read thru on the walls and it is cheap if you need to kill time. But don't put high on the list.

Found the prison and exhibitions very interesting, but extremely biased. Obviously this is to be expected, but I was still rather shocked by the portrayal of the prison as some sort of holiday camp for American POW's. I found the stories about the Vietnamese prisoners while under French rule quite moving.

Sad place with a grim history and I suspect that what visitors are told is filtered through a particular lens. It is sad that the gift shop did not have more of substance - especially on the history-  rather than the usual trinkets.
Very sobering visit. A must for those who are interested in military history.  Easy walking from Old Quarter.

The brutal treatment of Vietnamese under French rule is not something not known widely internationally, this is done quite well. However, depiction of the internment of US pow was done through staged photos, footage and cutesy artifacts, leading one to think they were in a Boy Scouts camp having a great old time. Fair amount of govt propaganda which is almost to be expected here.
Not the best designed visitor attraction ever but helps to bring to life to some degree an important part of history that is best not forgotten. Quite quick to get around and relatively good value

Worth a visit if you have time in Hanoi. There is a bit of over dramatisation going on with some of the videos and exhibits but the old prison site and some of the photos are still interesting. Strong nationalist overtones can get repetitive as you read about the strong willed Vietnamese overcoming their imperialist oppressors for the 100th time.

Having visited Tuol Sleng in Phnom Penh was expecting another harrowing but enlightening visit to the Hanoi Hilton. Unfortunately most of the prison had been demolished to make way for a large high rise but for VND30k its not an expensive trip and still very interesting.

We had high hopes of a visit to the Hoa Loa Prison museum and it was an interesting place to walk around, but we didn't find it particularly informative. It was mainly focused on the era where Vietnamese people were imprisoned by the French, with a very small exhibit based around the American soldiers' confinement (with lots of pictures of them playing basketball, having dinner and reading letters!!!)It was interesting (if a bit gory) to see the guillotine and solitary confinement cells.

The French built the prison in their then colony and treated the Vietnamese very badly, which displays of prisoners in shackles, torture and a guillotine. The Vietnamese themselves provided supposedly a kind of holiday resort for the American war prisoners, where they played games, planted gardens and were taken care of by private doctors. Sounds all a bit too much like propaganda to me.
Not much to see as most of it was demolished and is now a hotel, but if you like looking around prisons it’s worth visiting....

Kind of a must see, if you have never been.  About a third of the original prison remains as the museum, the rest having been torn down for modernization of the area.  It is part of history and the perspective presented is certainly interesting.  The horrors of the prison were all inflicted by the French upon the Vietnamese while the Vietcong treated their American prisoners with the utmost dignity.  The cells and displays that remain were certainly shown to support that point of view, with a couple of galleries, not cells, documenting the American POWs.

HANOI HILTON used to be much larger and what is left of it tells the story of a terrible and famous place during the Vietnamese war. The exhibits contain prison chambers and an original guillotine, plus many original photos.
PositivesNot too busy, a gently hour or soSome authenticityCheapNegativesNot hugeDon’t base a day around it

This museum is only a remaining section of the original prision, since the majority was demolished to make way for a shopping mall and condos.  It takes you through displays, photographs and artifacts of both the time of the French occupation of Vietnam and also the more recent American/Vietnam war.  Some of the photographs are disturbing but it serves as a reminder of the human tragedy of political conflicts and war.
Cheap entrance to this small prison, where we spent about 30 minutes. It was interesting to walk through the old cells and read about the history of the place, and the prisoners lives.

Well worth the 30000 dong entry fee and kids under 15 are free. My son enjoyed the walk around which took under an hour. Gives a hands on glimpse on the toughness of this gaol and what life must have been like there.

A good short visit to get some background on the history of this prison. It was used by the French and eventually by the Vier Cong to house US POWs, including the late US senator John McCain.Good short visit but a pity only a small remnant of the place remained; a big chunk of the land has been torn down to make way for the Hanoi Tower.

A visit to this former prison is almost essential if you're spending a few days in Hanoi, but be aware that it's not really for the buildings themselves, as only a very small part remains. Rather, it's to learn more about the history of French colonialism and the Vietnam War a few decades ago.

I went there twice when friends visited me. I recommend visiting with a guide to give you more insights and details. If you go free and easy, do read up on the history of Vietnam so that you can appreciate what happened. Otherwise you will not enjoy this prison tour much.
Interesting, but the regime is using it as an opportunity for propaganda... Here, only others are bad, but what can you do, that's just how it is.

Fascinating to see the two parts to the prison. One part presented where the French held the Vietnamese and the other part where the Vietnamese held the Americans. You can see and hear the narrative that they are telling - they looked after their prisoners fine. So do go and get a context on the history BUT don’t stop there. Make sure you read other sources of information about the treatment of prisoners there for a fully rounded view of the history.  One thing that is incredibly clear to me though is that war brings out the worst in many people.
Worth a visit. Mostly concerned with Vietnamese prisoners during French occupation. Small section dedicated to American POWs. An hour would be sufficient.

This is the site of a prison camp built during the French colonial era. Initially, it housed political prisoners, and during the Vietnam War, it appears to have held prisoners of war. The narrow, dimly lit cells and the guillotine used for executions are on display.
Museum about the prison system during the French colonization and subsequently during the Vietnam War. Both exhibitions overlap, making the stories sometimes difficult to separate.
We were with a guide who explained the harsh reality of this prison. The exhibit vividly depicts the lives of prisoners during the war.

95% of this museum is dedicated to the Vietnamese peoples struggle during French occupation. They have done a good job recreating an idea of how things were at that time.  The 5% of the museum that reflects the war with the United States shows a very different experience for those incarcerated than what we, in North America, were told.  This history was written by the victor. Still worth visiting.  It can get a little overwhelming when large groups are visiting as space in most exhibits is limited.
Nice exhibition which shows the conditions at Hanoi  Hilton . Reasonably priced I believe it was around 40,000dong each ( just over a pound )

Went to learn more about history from the Vietnamese perspective.  Many cells with mannequins in shackles. The exhibit shows how the prison was used when the French imprisoned the Vietnamese.  Here there are descriptions of horrific treatment of prisoners where they were tortured, starved and died in many numbers.  Later the prison was used to hold American fighter pilots who were captured during the war with America.  They portrayed the treatment of the Americans as practically benevolent.  They show the Americans playing basketball and volleyball. This doesn’t quite fit with some of the stories told by former prisoners of war.  Wish there had been a bit more balance.

This museum is very worthy of a visit.  Today it is much smaller than the original prison but some parts of it remain, enough to gie you a flavour of the conditions.  The prison has a long history beyond the american war.  Although very interesting and quite frank at times, it is still difficult to to fully believe everything you see.  There is a certain degreeof whitewashing.

Information was available in French throughout our visit, but our impression was mixed. We were presented with a contrast between the villainous French and the virtuous Americans, who were treated like royalty (even though they were essentially bombing the locals). Add a touch of communism, a few undated photos that seem to have appeared out of nowhere, a hint of heroism, a good dose of resilience, mix it all together with the absence of a timeline, and you get an interesting museum, but not exactly informative.

Interesting to see a part of history walking through a prison that even put in place by the French and was then used as a prison for POWs during the Vietnam.   A history of the French mistreatment of political prisoners is the overriding theme with cells, and chambers and an original guillotine.  What's missing is the reality of how US prisoners were treated. Anger against the us bombing comes through loud and clear.  But the exhibit tries to paint a picture of a life of leisure for US pows and badly misses the boat.

I was expecting bit more from this place but I thought it falls flat with displays and story.Most of the now museum is about French trying to over take Vietnam and somewhat small piece of Americans captured during Vietnam war. Most stuff is nothing but communist propaganda. Its not gruesome as per some comments here at all
... but not too interesting. Now probably less than 20% of its original size, focus is mostly on Vietnamese prisoners. Conditions were terrible there...

This prison is known in the US as the Hanoi Hilton. The slant in the prison is that the French, when they conquered Vietnam they treated the Vietnamese prisoners horribly, shackled all the time. Large rooms where they were all shackled. The spin on the US POWs was they were treated well, got to play sports in an outdoor area. There were pictures of the US Presidents visiting. A completely different take on the place.

Day off in Hanoi so this  visit with a local colleague was a must.Ok, so some of the history may be amended/ altered but this is what I enjoyed....the alternative view.Can’t say that it is half as good as the superb Robben Island in South Africa but it is still worth an hour of your time.What always strikes a chord is how cruel mankind can be historically and we don’t learn from it even in these supposed more enlightened times.It is a shame that much of the prison was demolished and there is the usual souvenir shop but overall I would recommend it.

The propaganda in the whole story they tell is absurd and laughable. In this century all information can be found on the internet; One has to be very simple of mind to believe all these obvious nonsense. All communists are noble and the rest of the world are all criminals. With this I am not stating that the French and later the Americans did the right things. They had no business there. Their behavior was shameful. But lets not pretend that the commies are all saints. A museum would do well to put everything in a historical perspective and to rely more on the facts.The museum would become much more interesting.It is not expensive so the visit is worth a visit for sure, and a must do while in Hanoi. The 3 star rating is just for the propaganda nonsense.

It was mostly propaganda museum to promote communism and how their comrades worked to liberate Vietnam from their French oppressor.  They also showcased how well the captured American Pilots were treated nick naming the prison Hanoi Hilton. I am assuming that they are saying the prison condition was for the Americans like staying in a hotel. It was interesting.
With dioramas depicting the conditions of the prison at the time and displays of torture devices, this might be a tourist spot that's not for everyone.

Hoa Lo Prison (Hanoi Hilton) was an interesting historical site to visit. Very much pro Vietnam and mixed reviews about America’s role in the so called “American War”. Having experienced the Vietnam War as the sibling of an active duty military man, it pained me to read some of the descriptive narratives. Reality is painted by our experiences. There are no real victories when lives are lost. Hoa Lo Prison is worth the time and entrance fee.

The prison was interesting to visit.  It was very focused on the Vietnamese war prisoners and how they were vital to over throwing the French.  The visit was filled with propaganda and focused on the hospitable treatment the Vietnamese gave the American pilots during their time at the prison.
Not all that exciting, however there probably aren't too many other historically significant places to visit. At approx US$2.50, its still worth a visit.
Too much information to take in for an ordinary person, you would be better to read up beforehand..... would be great if they did an audio tour

This is a worthwhile destination because it has a longer history to convey than its modern notoriety as the Hanoi Hilton of the Vietnam War. The museum is new, the entrance fee is very reasonable and the facilities are  good. The exhibits are well described and presented in logical order commencing with the French colonialists who built the prison to incarcerate and torture local people who fought for liberation. Exhibits include a guillotine and accompanying evidence of its use.Americans expecting to discover details of horrible conditions encountered by POWs will be surprised to see photos of smiling, healthy guys playing basketball and receiving care packages posted from home. Half the site was demolished for a huge tower block that looms overhead, but the museum is cleverly architecturally designed to ignore this and a generously spaced memorial within the site to those who suffered there provides a place for contemplation.

The reconstruction of the prison is truly well done. The cells are still preserved with original parts, anklets, and even a guillotine. In the upper rooms, the entire history of the war is displayed, while in the lower part, near the exit, there are photos of the American presidents at certain historic moments of the various negotiations.
I t was ok to see it and interesting to read about the history of the prison. Not so much to see in my opinion but still nice to go by if time for it.
Had to visit this historic prison to fully understand Vietnam’s colonial past

Walking through the dark hallways, peering into mannequin occupied cells, seeing the happy faces of US prisoners, and finally the shackled Vietnamese from the time of the French,  I had enough...Man's inhumanity to man...

Interesting to see the history of the revolutionary people of Vietnam and how they struggled for independence from the French. But also very sad to see the conditions for those American pilots imprisoned during the war with America.  Some of the photos are deceiving and do not tell the whole story.
We visited this ‘museum’ and fortunately had done some reading beforehand.

Formerly known as the Hanoi Hilton during the American war, this famous prison has been mostly torn down. A museum now occupies the remaining structure, dedicated primarily to the horrific conditions during the imprisonment of the Vietnamese communists by the colonial French from 1896-1945. Peculiarly contrasted with the apparently luxurious conditions of the captured and imprisoned US airmen during the bombing campaigns, including photos of them playing basketball, smoking and eating Christmas dinner! Former senator John McCain was held captive here for a few months, but he tells a different story. We struggled with many museums in Vietnam portraying such biased (and blatantly false) histories... Possibly we weren't in the right frame of mind for the visit, but we only had a quick look around here - as was very very crowded mid-afternoon and most of the information was only available in french with very little information in English, possibly we would have enjoyed the experience more if had been part of a guided tour - but the museum was absolutely jam-packed with such tours and we couldn't wait to get out to be honest.
If you're staying nearby, why not visit? It's dark, and you can't see half the exhibits. Price for two adults is 60,000 rubles.

I visited this place during a day-long walk around Hanoi, and took my time to look around. It's a former internment camp from the French colonial era, and a guillotine was on display. I was able to see everything at a leisurely pace in a little over an hour. The restrooms were located to the right and at the back of the entrance.

So while it was not a “bad” tour it, it seems the American POW were a minor foot note compared to some of the other locations we have visited...ie German history museum or the concentration camps. Tickets were cheap and kids under 15 were free.  Glad we did it, although my 11 year old daughter was sometimes grossed out, but I would not go back.
Saw the Hanoi Hilton yesterday. It was a prison. Different feeling. Not much else I can say. It was a nice piece of history to see and explore
This was right next to our hotel so was an easy choice to visit. Most of the setup showcases the brutality of colonial era and how colonial subjects were often treated to achieve domination.

For an American, this place is notorious for mistreatment of POW's .   There is much more history than that span of time for this place and they let you know it.  Most of the faciltily has been demolished and replaced with a real hotel. What remains is the story of imprisonment and torture of Vietnamese by the French.  Ok, that is true.  But, what I take exception with is the misrepresentation of how the Vietnamese treated their American guests.  There were atrocities committed on both sides, however this place is the poster child of prisons.

Worth a visit while in Hanoi, but don’t expect too much. It cost 20,000 VND per person. Only a small part of the prison has been retained and converted to a museum. Most of the original structures were bulldozed for “revolutionary development”. I lost interest after a while as the entire narrative was extremely one sided ...

Overall a good showcase but some videos/panel texts reeked of biased one-sided perspectives. Dark and cold especially in the dungeons. Best part was the prison escape stories through the sewers. Decent gift shop at the end.

Most of it had been torn down, the only thing we really saw was the gate.  It was kind of a disappointment. Although it was really just the gate, learned more then you could imagine.  Like the stories that people went through. It was very sad, but it is a very big part of vietnamese history.

I went here because it was "The Hanoi Hilton" but in realty 95% of the what remains is dedicated to the cruelty inflicted on the Vietnamese people by the French occupation. A small room contained info on the US war prisoners (Sen. John McCain) with photos showing Birthday parties and holiday celebrations indicating it was a wonderful experience for our guys! NOT! Again this is the Vietnamese perspective.

We stopped off here after several temples and made a nice change especially as the kids were done with temples.The prison has been mostly torn down now and only a 5th survives but it’s still very interesting and shows you how life used to be back then. Worth a visit to make you appreciate the struggles of the Vietnamese people throughout history.

The prison itself is quite bare, which I suppose was how the prison looked back in the day. You walk through the different rooms and are shown various torture methods and conditions are described - it must have been very bleak but a lot is left to the imagination.The highlight comes in the courtyard though, where a comprehensive history is given of Hanoi's involvement in ridding Vietnam of the French (1945-54). I would warn that the history is not very objective, although Vietnamese may say this about Western accounts of the conflict. There are a couple of interesting rooms at the end where they examine the conditions American PoWs were kept in.We only had 2 days in Hanoi, this was the only museum we went to and it certainly gave us enough information
The prison is interesting, but unfortunately very little of it remains, so you don't need to set aside much time for a visit (about 1 hour).

A deserted prison that turned into tourist attraction.Not very big place, able to finish within an hour.Required ticket, non air-conditioning.Quite a sad place to visit a place where locked up, torturing and executed of prisoners.

We went there when we wre stroling through the streets of Hanoi.Also, most of the illustrations are somewhat biassed, it is worthwile to be visited. No matter on what side the prisoners where on, they went through hell.Staff is very friendly and competent.

The entrance fee is 30,000 Vietnamese Dong. Many foreigners go in to see it. Inside, there are exhibits documenting historical events such as how prisoners were held, as well as some US military equipment and videos. I didn't take many photos; personally, I think half an hour is enough.

There is an admission fee, and although it isn't large, it is a place worth visiting at least once. It is a faithful recreation of a POW camp; it is a heartbreaking place. There is also a souvenir shop at the end!
not a huge exhibit, we spent less than an hour here. visit if you are keen on understanding the country's POV
The prison is just a fraction of what it used to be, you get to see about 45% of what is left as they removed most for a new building.  It was quite an interesting viewpoint of how things were.

This appears to be the place known as the Hanoi Hilton, famous from novels and other media. There are significant differences between what is written in American-leaning sources and the description here.

We enjoyed our visit to Hoa Lo prison, however the exhibits were slightly confusingly put together and not curated particularly well, you end up reading a lot of detailed information about not much. Plenty of propaganda as you might expect too, make sure you read about it on Wikipedia too to get a more rounded view

This was a museum about a prison that was used in the eary 20th century which imprisoned political activists. Gives you a feel of what it was like and you can go into a few of the cells that were used. Lots of information and has a memorial. It was quite interesting. We went round it in about 30min. Entrance fee about 30-40 dong
This was a very interesting prison to visit. Dating way back before the wold wars right up till the Vietnam war. Pretty sad seeing how the prisoners were held captive. Kind of a creepy place.

Not much remained, most of prison it’s been sold and destroyed, but if you’re interested in Vietnam war you have to go. What you find is a lot of pictures, stories, videos... all celebrating Vietnam state goodness, for example pictures representing american prisoners celebrating christmas, nothing about tortures inflicted on them.You will see.
The propoganda slant detracts from this exhibit. A factual account and left at that would enhance effort. Good thing it's only a couple of dollars admission.
Enjoyable, but get all the information from a decent history book and don’t rely on the commentary as it is pure propaganda.

We enjoyed our visit to Hoa Lo Prison - we loved the exhibition on the American Pilots who were kept there as POWs. The majority of the museum was about the communists who resisted the French which we didn’t know so much about but still found it quite interesting. Also a lot of the original prison has been built on so you don’t get a full picture of what it would have been like. Don’t expect to be there all day - we did the whole thing pretty quickly. Worth a visit if you have a few days in Hanoi but wouldn’t be top of the list!

This prison is basically propaganda, but worth the inexpensive fee to see the “Hanoi Hilton”.  Except for a tiny room, everything is focused on before 1945. It is powerful to see the photos of the American prisoners, and especially Sen McCain.

It’s a very surest experience to tour a prison that was so recently used.Due t urban development a large part of the prison has been demolished which is a shame for a site with so much potential.The power of the building as you enter is very significant and you can feel the history, however, there is no need for the bad manikins around the prison which are used excessively. The prison is split in to two wings. The first is not so interesting with limited information. However in the second wing there is more interesting, detailed information about what happened in the prison throughout it’s history with well structured exhibits (even if sometimes it feels a little propaganda like).

This was a pretty interesting way to send a few hours. Rather harrowing going into the dark dingy cells and imagining spending year upon year in there. But the display they had up showing their lovely treatment towards the American prisoners is very contractictory to the actual truth. They show the American POW’s playing chess, making Christmas displays, decent food and cigarettes on tap. They say in one display the those American prisoners had a better quality of life than the average Vietnamese man at that time. However that is far from the truth and I find it slightly disturbing that a museum would give false facts.

This was an interesting experience.  It highlighted the brutality of the French regime and the inhumane treatment of Vietnamese prisoners whilst stressing the "caring" way that American prisoners of war were treated.

This is more of a guy thing. My husband was pretty happy there reading and looking into the articles and artifacts found. It was more knowledge for me as I was not aware that there was a prison such not for convicts but for political crimes and surrendered soldiers.
A nice small museum but quite sparse with displays and predominantly focused on french occupation. You will only need 30 minutes to cover all displays.
The prison is reduced to just a part... I didn't find it too interesting compared to other prisons, but it does give you an idea of ​​the last years of Vietnam's history.

Terrible things took place here too. Lots of text and explanation. It's a shame they tore down half of it to build an apartment block. Entrance is very cheap. Buy a small booklet with it for explanations.
Interesting from the days of the French colonisation to the American /Vietnam war. The souvenir shop is good no hassle value.

I recommend it if you are interested in history and want to leave a trace of having been somewhere, but the quality wasn't good enough to make you wonder if it was really worth visiting. If you are expecting something like Korea's Seodaemun Prison, you will be very disappointed.
Worth popping into, but do your own research, as it’s a fairly lazy take on the subject matter. Much of the original is gone.

It was cool to take a quick walkthrough of the Hanoi Hilton as this prison was known.  Unbeknownst to me previously this was the prison where former Senator John McCain was held at.  If you enjoy cool parts of history I would suggest visiting here.  It can be done with relative quickness, the fee is not exorbitant, and it’s a good experience.
First of all, if you're going to visit a country, you should go if you want to learn about its history and pay your respects.

A prison in the city center, famous for housing American pilots, including McCain. It's not a must-see, and there's not much to see. It's divided into two sections: one about the conditions endured by Vietnamese prisoners and the other about the conditions of American prisoners.

A stern and sobering reminder of the horrors of war and colonialism. A must visit in order to understand the history and idiosyncrasy of Vietnamese people. A little on the nose on the propaganda at some sections but everybody gets to play the game. Raw and in your face perspective.
worth seeing indeed yet very bias against the Americans and not 100% honest about their side of the story

I think maybe I expected too much . I mean it was informational but just not realistic to me at all.  I know they had to rebuild it obviously but I guess it just wasn’t what I expected. They did have some really cheap souvenirs that I got so that’s a plus.

Vietnamese history is interesring there is no arguing with that. Prison was okay, but iI lef wondering how much of the things were original and real. I guess we would have gotten more out of it if we would have had audioguide or real guide. This museum have really patriotic attitude and strong opinion about history.
Not far form the Church and worth the visit. Interesting to see Vietnam’s take on certain aspects of the Vietnam war. Maybe not for young children.

The prison "Hanoi Hilton" is basically a summary of the dreadful things the French put the Vietnamese through the displays are quite graphic.There is an area dedicated to the wonderful treatment  the North Vietnamese gave to the American pilot captives obviously propaganda  so don't get upset it is still worth a visit.

A quiet and calm visit to Hoa Lo Prison on a raining day. It is always good to know more about the past, glad that we didn't experience war period. Information is stated in Vietnamese, English and French.
Got to see the prison the day John McCain died. Worth 30 min visit but more is not necessary. Plenty of placards written in English were very helpful.

A good little activity to pass on a short time! Not too expensive and gives a good insight into the conditions for prisoners kept here over the years! Most of the prison has been knocked down so just a few rooms and a shrine to see. Kids enjoyed the cells on death row and the guillotine!!

This is an old prison area. It feels gloomy and chilling to enter. There are many statues of prisoners shackled, chained, and brutally tortured. Be careful when walking around here because it's quite well-preserved.
One of the most important landmarks of Hanoi is the old prison that housed Vietnamese revolutionaries during the French occupation... The place is good for learning and taking some souvenir photos.
It is a place where you can catch a glimpse of the painful history of the past. Taking this opportunity at Hoa Lo Prison, I thought that I should also visit Seodaemun Prison in Korea.
If you have only a couple of days, skip it. However, it was interesting to see if you have time to kill in Hanoi. Price is reasonable and some of what you can see is terrifying in a good way.

Interesting museum mostly concentrating on the prison during French occupation, but also the use of the site during the Vietnam War to house American pilots.  Certainly looks at things from a different angle to the Western perspective of that period.
An interesting visit from a historical perspective. Part of the prison remains in its original state, and the buildings are still standing.

The prison is interesting to see as it has been used by so many, and you can get an insight into this. What I did not like was the language used about each of the countries who used the prison. But I guess you have to expect that. Just try to ignore the emotive language and observe it's history.
The staging was sometimes too theatrical. I didn't feel any emotional connection to this place, despite its profound symbolic significance and overwhelming history.
I have visited Alcatraz and the Eastern State Penitentiary in Philadelphia, so this prison by comparison is nowhere close, but it is still worth seeing if you have the time in Hanoi.

It was a relatively short visit as much of the original prison had been demolished to accommodate the expanding city which you can see in the back yard. There were some good artefacts to look at and an informative video which explained a lot about the prison transformations over the years.

The prison building itself was a haunting structure and eerie but definitely immersed you into it. The exhibit was quite good and the information offered was interesting HOWEVER  approach this with an open mind or prior research. The history offered whilst mainly true is very one-sided and there are aspects that are blatant deniers of truth ( such as the treatment of Americans imprisoned in the prison, and lack of mention to Australian, NZ soldiers). The history must be considered in the context of crimes each side perpetrated. My partner and I found observing there propoganda very interesting but I do worry of people who believe the information offered to them blindly so if you visit here PLEASE DO YOUR OWN RESEARCH AS WELL.When we went there was an exhibit remembering patriots which was a nice place of remembrance and respect for the Vietnamese people.

I was looked forward to this visit and came away a bit disappointed. Only a small part of the original prison remains so the tour does not take long. Exhibits focus almost exclusively on the suffering of the Vietnamese “patriots” under the French occupation. One exhibit talked about the War of American aggression and the American pilots held captive. Apparently their treatment by the Vietnamese was so good that the Americans affectionately called Hoa Lo prison the “Hanoi Hilton” presumably because it was like a holiday camp. I know history is written by the victors but this was stretching it a bit. Having said that, some of the exhibits of personal stories of prisoners were very good, and you did get a good impression of what the prison was like. Worth a trip if you can cope with the propaganda.

It’s an interesting place to visit even if you are not familiar with the history of Vietnam. My 3 year old son asked a lot of questions about the photos and art but got scared of the dark cellar/dungeon rooms (they were creepy). It was a good way to while away 1 hour.

The museum itself is small. We got there after being at the Vietnam War Museum in Saigon (which is much larger and more interesting). It gave another perspective. Mainly about the American pilots. Nice but not a must if you don't have time.
A must do if you ask me. Low price to get in. Not too many people when we were here. Quite small and doesn’t take long time to cover.

Some of the original prison still stands, most was torn down to make way for new buildings. The walking tour gives insight on the French treatment of prisoners through the 1950s. There is a solemn memorial to the people killed in there fight for independence. They also have a couple of rooms dedicated to the American prisoners of war. The description of the treatment of there POWs is somewhat different than what was reported by the returning Americans.
Glad I went to learn how French oppression cataclysmically altered Vietnam's history and spirit. The American representation is .... let's just say a different perspective than I understood.

The museums in Ho Chi Minh are much better. The prison had no real direction, was interesting in parts but mostly difficult to follow which part of their history you were meant to be observing. No disrespect intended at all, interesting but not my personal favourite.

We visited with a guide. For this tourist stop a guide may not be necessary. There were plenty of signs to read and videos to watch. It’s not a huge place as most of the original building is no longer there. There was definitely a heavy feeling in many parts of the prison. Stop by if you’re looking for something to do, maybe allow up to an hour.
It’s an interesting hour wandering around, only £1 entry per person. Quite depressing, but that’s expected from a historic prison....

It was interesting and fascinating to look around a place which is obviously fairly important historically, for the imprisonment of Vietnamese revolutionaries and of US PoWs.However, given the little research I did following my visit, it seems that a lot of how they present the treatment of US Prisoners of war is incredibly twisted. They present it as if the US PoWs had a great stay and thoroughly enjoyed their stay, ignoring the many claims of torture and mistreatment.That aside, it was a good way to spend an hour or so.
Entry was 40k dong. Nice exhibits, very small. Saigon Remnant museum much better and bigger. Can see the whole thing in about 30 mins

A visit to this place is part of any Hanoi tour package. Having been briefed about the place by the guide and from various brochures, you enter with a mixed sense - one of revisiting history, but dominantly, with a sense of the meaningless of it all - the war. Looking at the photos and the articles used for torture etc, as you leave, you can sense the aura of gloom, sadness and grief from the expression in every visitor's face. You visit a prison to leave hoping that no human being ever gets into one!

The prison can be self-toured in 1-2 hours depending on how much reading you do about the many displays. It costs 30k VND to enter, a very modest sum. The prison focuses primarily on the French Colonial period but the American War is afforded some attention too. Seeing how the prisoners were housed and treated is very disturbing.  Videos are available in a couple of rooms. The place is mostly not air-conditioned but with ventilation provided by fans. I think the prison is a must-visit to put the impact of colonialism in perspective.
The entrance fee was nominal. I wouldn’t advise buying the booklet with the ticket. The same information is provided throughout the museum. My visit took about an hour.

This museum was interesting. Although, We have recently visited Tuol Sleng prison in Cambodia which seemed a lot more informative and emotive, so we were a bit underwhelmed. We are not history experts,however, it did seem the Hoa Lo had a political agenda which was repeated time and again. So bear that in mind when visiting
I don't know what to feel about this place because of what happened there during the war.  Lots of cruelty for sure,  but If you love history, you'd learn a lot from visiting this place.
Nice to have seen, but not really special. Just a terrible prison in a big city. Important for the history of Hanoi/Vietnam, though.

This museum tour was informative about the Hanoi Hilton. They didn’t touch very much on the POW camp that was here during the Vietnam War instead it was geared more towards the French prison before that time. It was interesting to see the cells that house American POW’s though

I visited hoa lo prison today. It was creepy visit to historic place and to cruel human mind. There were couple cells where you could visit and see the reality of inmates life. Guidance letters on the walls told the story about inmates everyday life. For small children this might be too scary but for 10 years and up this could be interesting (also depends on the child). It's quite small size for museum so the person who isn't very interested in history can visit here. It won't be too long museum tour. There were some videos with english subtitles where you could catch up your historic information. Entrance fee is 30 000vnd for 1 adult.

I think I was expecting the visit to be a bit longer. The displays are quite respectful to the individuals who were incarcerated there. It is a very sober reminder of the Vietnam's past. Worth the visit.
Compared to other places we visited in Hanoi this was a bit boring to be honest. The exhibits are quite limited. Very cheap to get in though so you can't really complain too much.
It's really cheap to enter so it's difficult to get too disappointed but there is very little to see in here.

Wife and I decided to stop in and walk around the prison while touring the city. Cost 30000 VND per ticket and probably took about 40 minutes to tour the place. Interesting spot considering its history, my thai wife was really shocked by the guillotine and prisoner depictions. I was familiar with the history of the prison but found its use as patriotic propaganda fascinating. Worth a drop in if you’re walking close by.

Good exhibits gives the visitor a stepback to French occupation. The guilitine shocks the vistor though.The USA POW section is not accurate though as when I investigated after the visit I was shocked to find that the POWs were treated exactly the same by the Vietnamese as the Vietnamese were by the French.

Admission fee - 30k DongPartial of the prison had been demolished for urban development, remains worth the time to explore. Videos in English subtitle available to allow you to understand more about the time in the prison back in those days. You would approximately need 1-1.5 hours to complete the tour. Only the level 1 worth the visit as it brings you closer to the prison life back then.
Built by the French during their colonial era, the rooms where the Vietnamese were crammed together are still visible. It served as a prison for American pilots during the war.

Interesting place to visit in Hanoi, let’s you see how the French treated Vietnamese Political prisoners and also the “Hilton” that the American pilots stayed in. Valuable history here but some of it could be more clearly represented.The complex isn’t too big and the visit can be completed in 1-2 hours.

kind of interesting is the best way to put it. I would give it more stars if it wasn't filled with propaganda. I gave noticed a huge theme in all museums here of "poor us" aside "look how great WE treated POWs". It's interesting and not a huge investment (30k) but definitely over the obvious propaganda.

Difficult to find if you're on foot. The visit is deeply moving. Don't go with children. The end of the visit glorifies the party, and nothing is written in French because they were the torturers! Everyone will form their own opinion on this. Worth seeing if you've already seen everything else in Hanoi.
Not much to see, especially with a kid. But there is rather cheap souvenirs store inside. It does not hide from rain.
If you want to learn some past history, u can try that one. It give some views about the important milestones in vietnamese history

Visited this on our last day in the country as our flight wasn't until the evening. There was no audio guide of any sort for this, so you had to rely on the plaques dotted around the prison. There were a few video sequences in a couple of rooms, which were English subtitled. I enjoyed the visit, however it did not wow me in anyway, though it was humbling to see the conditions the locals would have been kept in and what they endured. Overall it was very cheap entry and a good way to spend a few hours if you're at a lose end.
Nice for a rainy day. We had a guide with us; you get a reasonable impression of what goes on here, but North Vietnam is still glorified, even though we know better now.

Please know history before going there.  They should have bulldozed the whole place (I realize half of it is gone).  Terrible what happened in there to everyone.  I didn't even bother to enter the rooms about the American side of the war as it proved to be total propaganda.  It has a very evil energy in most of the rooms, for good reasons.

Interesting place and history but nothing special as such or if you have few days and need to prioritize I don't think you should prioritize it. Otherwise well maintained, good information and interesting. Not particularly big but still takes a while to go through.

If you're an impressionable person, this place isn't for you. You can see the cramped cells where prisoners lived, the guillotine they were executed with, and very lifelike mannequins with ankles shackled with iron yokes. Videos and photographs of Vietnamese prisoners (under French rule) and Americans during the US-Vietnam War.
Well some interesting infos. I think it wasn't that different from other prison museums that I have went. It's small so good for stopping by.

Not sure that any visit to a prison can be described as enjoyable, particularly in view of its gruesome history.  Nevertheless, it is part of Vietnamese history and should be visited if you want to obtain a better understanding of the country.

It was not very expensive to see the prison, 30k VND per ticket. The prison is small, and they’ve put some effort into trying to give you an idea of what it was like to be imprisoned there. There are some mannequins shackled up and some interesting stories about how prisoners escaped. There is a full sized guillotine and some gory photos. Good for a quick walk through, the whole tour will take you less than an hour.

This place dates back a long way and was used by the French and communists before the Vietnam American war where it became known as the Hanoi Hilton.It’s interesting but be ready for the propaganda about how wonderfully they treated American POW’s etc. It’s pretty in your face.  If you can take that with a pinch of salt, it’s an interesting place to see.

not much left of the prison but a few cells - a giant hotel dominates the former site and overshadows the remnants of the former prison.  most dedicated to the atrocities of the former french colonialists - and a small section to the american war.  govt propaganda included free of charge

I had to go there with my interests in the 20th century, and whilst the early part of that century, with the French occupation was well represented and described, and interesting, the latter was badly portrayed and disappointing. Like a lot in Vietnam the story has a very biased position that detracts from the facts

There isn't much information available, just a few panels in English, French, and Vietnamese. Much could be explained with more background information. There's practically no information at all in the outdoor area.
Seeing the Vietnamese version of the American war was very interesting. A fair amount of propaganda, and perhaps stretched truth.  Be prepared for a very somber experience.
The visit was depressing to see how Vietnamese went thru war in modern time. It’s a small prison took us 30mins to finish. Lots of tourist .
Was here on a rainy day so we weren't alone. It was great to see/read a bit about the conditions the prisoners had to suffer. Everything is in several languages.

The first part of the prison focuses mainly on the struggle for independence against the French. It is very extensive and nationalistic/patriotic. The second, much smaller section deals with the Americans who were imprisoned there during the Vietnam War. According to the stories in this museum, they were treated excellently, which is, of course, utter nonsense. So, it is a monotonous story, but it is nice to see the older items, such as the pilot's suit of an imprisoned American.

I am not sure what I expected, but as a Vietnam vet who had friends incarcerated there, it was a disappointment.  Only the oldest part of the prison is still there, and it does not include the area where US pilots were locked up.  There are 2 display rooms with pictures of how well the US pilots were treated during their stay there!

This is a walk through tour, with many exhibits depicting the horrible life the prisoners faced,  The displays were generally informative and some of the exhibits were very emotional to look at.  The overall atmosphere was somber. One thing I found particularly tacky and insensitive was the souvenir gift shop in the middle of the tour route.  Overall, I'd say this was worth a visit from a historical perspective, but don't plan on spending a lot of time there.

Worth visiting but not a "wow"-experience. As earlier reviews has mentioned it was easy to summarise: the French were awful and they were so nice with the Americans. Didn't feel portrayed objectively and totally truthful and a bit repetitive.
Everyone visits . A history of the French, Vietnam and American troubles in Vietnam.Worth a visit as it has many exhibits, photos and a learning experience.

Small but interesting museum about the French built prison that eventually was used to house the American Aircrew during the Vietnam War. Expect it to take around 2 hours to complete. Top tip: Buy the museum visitor book.
It's a horrific place in history, whether you find that beautiful or not is beside the point. How anyone could endure it for seven years (John McCain) is beyond my comprehension.

Hoa Lo Prison, also known as the Hanoi Hilton back in the day is worth a visit to get some of the flavour of some of its historical past. Actually, a lot of the prison has already disappeared having been demolished in the 1990s so there is not that much left to see. The entrance fee is inexpensive at 30,000 VND (less that £1) and for that you get some interesting exhibits and a somewhat slanted historical prospective about the treatment of the Vietnamese by the French and the Americans and on the other hand how well foreign prisoners were treated by the Vietnamese. It is an interesting place to go and see and I am glad I did.

Enjoyed our visit here but couldn't help feeling that it could be so much more.  The primary focus is on the Indochina wars and showing the conditions that the prisoners were kept in.  The prison is quite small and some of it is being renovated but it was still an educational visit.The exhibits covering the American war were interesting and it would have been good to see more of this

Again, not a whole lot to see inside alot of the prison was demolished in the 90s. If your going because of an interest in the Vietnam war and the Hanoi Hilton stuff, there's not a lot to see. There is a section dedicated to American prisoners but it's more about how well the soldiers were treated and reconciliation. The stuff about the French colonial period is interesting and definitely worth a look but it's more mannequins than actual artifacts.
The museum is informative, but it gets tired of the look how bad we were treated and how well we treat others. As we all know the truth is always in the middle.Still, an interning visit.
I guess that is it an important landmark for history. However it is relatively morbid and there is not much to see.

Interesting to see the Vietnamese spin on US POWs.  Pictures of holiday meals, basketball games, and playing cards.  Hardly the true experience of our Naval officers held captive there during the war.

The Hanoi Hilton is a required stop for any American who live through the Vietnam War era. Only about 15% of the original facility is still intact. Interestingly, the other 85% has been replaced with a high-end office building. A little ironic. It takes about an hour to go through the facility and most of the displays are in english. Obviously the content was not written from an American perspective. Obviously the content was not written from an American perspective. The display are a little disappointing but none the less I would highly recommended given it's historic significance.
My boyfriend loved this, found it very interesting and moving! It was nice to have. a bit of a change from just temples he found! Good to break the day up!

Like all Vietnamese museums, this one is dripping with Vietnamese propaganda and incitement against the evil enemy! Presented in a more objective manner, this attraction might be somewhat more appealing, but constantly having to fight off the rising nausea from all the propaganda drivel barely allows for a 3/5 rating.

Hoa Lu Prison was built by the French during their colonization of Vietnam. It was named after the village that was originally a pottery kiln manufacturing center. Unfortunately, two-thirds of the prison was demolished to make way for a five-star hotel.

It is worth a visit. Learn something of the history of the French imprisonment of the Vietnamese in the early ‘30s and then read the stories of some of the Americans held prisoner during the Vietnam war
Interesting to see. Beautiful old building and impressive to see how people were treated in the prison. Nice to visit.

Fascinating to visit and worth seeing but not a "must do".I felt that the information could be presented better. For example there was a lot of heavy reading which could have been exhibited in better and more engaging or moving ways.It certainly is a very important historical monument/memorial.I know westerners like me (from New Zealand) are exposed to a substantial amount of American propaganda but I have a strong feeling that there was a lot of political spin from the Vietnam government in how the "Hanoi Hilton" was operated during the Vietnam/American War. But that's normal throughout the country because the patriotic cause for independence is inseperable from broader politics.
Worth a visit if you have time, but not as good as the women's museum. Lack of context surrounding the war which is understandable and not unusual, but overall a fairly old school museum.
VND 30,000 per person. Is pretty small area, probably took about 20mins you could finish explored the whole area.
Well maintained attraction. Displays the past living conditions of the prisoners and how they escaped. Interesting facts but small area, able to cover within 30 mins. Visit when you have extra time.

This is where a number of American pilots and servicemen spent their time when captured by the North Vietnamese. Much of the site has been redeveloped for apartments but the main facade and some of the buildings have be retained in order to tell more of the story of the recent conflict.You do learn some of the stories of the prisoners and a little about the conditions but it's probably a museum that won't stick in your mind for long (although it helps to broaden your knowledge of the war).30 minutes is probably sufficient and it does get a little crowded.
A short Sunday outing; some of the photos are hard to look at, but unfortunately, they reflect reality.
Definitely worth a visit, for a very modest entrance fee. There are interesting displays, excellent restoration and is a great way of learning more about Vietnam
