A visit that takes about an hour to discover this place, which has evolved over time. A place of remembrance which, depending on the room, leaves one speechless, bearing witness to so much suffering within these walls.

The museum is interesting because it's a prime example of propaganda and one-sided information. You get the impression that the American prisoners of war were treated like guests and had a wonderful time at the "Hanoi Hilton." This is even stated in black and white on the numerous display panels at the end of the exhibition. This biased and simply false portrayal makes you seriously question the veracity of other information in the museum.

It is not expensive to gain access to the prison. It is pretty much a self tour. They have a small area that has been reserved for tours to walk around and look at holding cells and some informational displays. All in all I was disappointed in what I saw. It is worth going only to say you were there.

Walking distance from the old quarter.   good value for the ticket price.   Displays offered a sense of the torture and confinement of prisoners.   Not up lifting but essential to understand the history of vietnam.

Not quite sure what we were expecting but this is basically a few rooms showing how completely horrific the French imprisoned the communists back in the day and how well the Vietnamese looked after the Americans so well during the Vietnam war. Highlight was an actual guillotine

This institution has a checkered history to be sure.  That history is presented in part by a strong emphasis on the torture, sufferings, and unity experienced by many of the early Vietnamese nationalists under French rule.  Little mention is made of the same things experienced by captive American servicemen after defeat of the French.  Much is claimed about humanitarian treatment of prisoners and post-war reconciliation efforts.  Its remaining structures are a dark place with a dark history that a visitor should explore to better appreciate the price paid by many in the unification of Vietnam.
A thought-provoking experience if you are interested in a bit of history. However, there is not as many things to see as you might have hoped for.

Historical place worth a visit. Bear in mind there is more to the story being told. The highlight for me was discreetly watching an old Vietnamese person pay his respect at the shrine and watch his ritual of respect. I wish I could have engaged him for his history.
Hoa Lo Prison is a very sad but historical place to visit.  It is a part of our history which we should never forget.

It's a small part of once was a larger complex but the government tore it down to build an ugly hotel/building. The info is depressing (obviously) but informational. Go if you are into Vietnam history, but don't expect much on the American war. It is propgandized and just a small portion of the experience.
A lot of history in this old building. You can discover why this was build and by whom. Very interesting. Not that big (anymore), but worth a visit.

There isn't a huge amount to see but some of it is quite moving.  There is a bit of propaganda to deal with but it's easy to perceive and set aside. The mural is a moving tribute to lives lost and people tortured.

I’m a veteran, who was stationed in south Vietnam in 1971-72.  I visited the War museum in Saigon last year, but missed Hoa Lo prison while in Hanoi .  I recommend this museum to families with teens.  Let them see what war does!

As any history enthusiast know, VietNam had been into war and it was divided into North and South Vietnam.This museum depicts the VietNam war in an Hanoian/Vietnamese point of view - the gruesome image of the war and its lasting effects on its history and present culture.From Hoan Kiem Lake, this is some walking distance (at least for us). Entry fee is around 30,000 VND per person.

While there were some good historical pieces and you could see the cells themselves showing how bad things must have been, this was a very skewed picture of history. There is a long documented history of mistreatment of American POWs in Hoa Lo but they describe it as ‘humane’ and ‘a serene time’ for the pilots...making it like they were thankful to be imprisoned rather than having to participate in the war. It calls into question the depiction of the French here too when you realize how inaccurate it is for the Vietnam/US war period. So while it’s worth a visit, take it with a grain of salt knowing it’s full of propaganda.
Interesting history of prisons in Vietnam Including an area dedicated to American prisoners. You do get the feeling that this part has been heavily sanitised.

Well. It's inexpensive, and it was on my way. But the museum desperately needs a revamp. Unfortunately, the official stance of the Vietnamese leadership is still that only Americans committed war crimes and atrocities. It becomes truly ridiculous when they display pictures of McCain visiting the museum—a man who wrote a book about the torture and mutilation he suffered in captivity. At least they didn't exhibit the letters extracted under torture from prisoners, which were presented at the time as proof of good treatment.

This is historically a famous place to see and visit - especially for Americans, but the interior attractions are very nominal and minimal. Its very interesting historically, but most of the displays are pictures, maps and videos. There are not many actual artifacts.

If you have time in your schedule then give this place a visit.  Sorry cannot comment on price as it was part if a planned city tour.  The Museam is about a 20min walk(good pace) from Hanoi, Old quarter.
Attended with another family (4 adults and 4 kids). It is worth going if you are in the city and like delving into history.
You can learn about history from the French colonial era to the Vietnam War. There is also a guillotine. There is no Japanese explanation.
It might not be one of Hanoi's must-see attractions, but I think it's worth a visit if you have the time. It's not expensive, and you don't need to spend much time there.

Most of the old prison has been demolished. What is left contains a museum which concentrates on the French use of the prison in the the Vietnamese struggle for independence. There is a small section on use during the US war “Hanoi Hilton “ which has limited information except for highlighting what a great time the American prisoners had at the hands of their “enlightened” Viet Kong captors! Believe it or believe it not.
Shows a lot of the history of the prison when France colonized Vietnam.  Some of the American prisoners, but really not particularly interesting.

It is interesting to go and read about the prisons history but overall I didn’t find it overly interactive. I would still recommend you go especially if you are unaware of Vietnam’s history as it does open your eyes as does the other Museums such as the ones in Southern Vietnam etc.

I was really looking forward to seeing this museum as I'm a Vietnam War buff, but not much to it, had some interesting items, but think it could have been so much more, as the "Hanoi Hilton " played a huge role during the Vietnam War. The War museum in HCMC is much more interesting.

This is a tourist attraction I specifically visited in Hanoi. It's a bit somber, and you don't need a whole day to see it. The area isn't large; you can walk around and learn about the history of this place.

Would recommend visiting Hao Lo prison when visiting Hanoi. Costing only 30,000 VND, you can make your way through the museum in 30-60 minutes. The museumit self is fairly decent with interesting exhibits and show pieces. I would also recommend that before going keep an open mind and read about the history before entering as it is very subjective and biased to shine the Vietnamese is a positive light. They describe the American PoW as having a 'Holiday' within the prison.  A lot of propaganda.
But since there isn't much to see in downtown Hanoi anyway, I ended up going. It was cheap, so I don't think it was a waste of money. Just go on another day tour instead.

Situated next to Hanoi Towers, is Maison Centrale in bright yellow. If you have time, you can visit this place and learn about the sufferings of the Hanoi people during the time where they were colonised by the French and the Americans. entrance fee is VND30k.

Another example of the shocking ways people treated each other during the war and terribly sad how they were treated. The information regarding the French use of the prison was shocking to say the least and seemed to demonstrate what really happened to Vietnamese revolutionaries during French colonial rule. HOWEVER the museum claims that when American POW were kept there it was a place to reflect and relax in a serene place. This is completely untrue, simple google hanoi Hilton American pow and you will see that it was no holiday and Americans were brutally tortured just as the Vietnamese were years before. The term is ironic!Please take that part of the museum with a pinch of salt as clearly the people who did the exhibits were attempting to portray the vietnamese soldiers as people who treated the Americans with mercy! They were not treated any different...you will see letters there which clearly look as if they were coerced into writing about how well they were treated.Good museum to get an idea about vietnams history but don't believe everything you read, do your research.

Compared to prisons in other countries with similar historical backgrounds, this one feels rather rudimentary. There's no detailed explanation of the inmates' lifestyles, only simple statues and textual descriptions, making it difficult to understand the lives of people at that time.

I am Australian and I couldn't believe how skewed and downright untruths that are presented here, absolutely no comments or recognition of the history beyond 1960. The museum focuses entirely on shaming the French colonials from the 1930s onwards. NO mention of American treatment or a chance to examine the history from both sides. The signage blatantly suggests Americans who were held there were treated with 'love and peace'. BS.  Ashamed I put my dollar to this propaganda machine.

I thought it was a smart facility, conveying the deplorable conditions the prisoners were kept in without being overly cruel. I appreciated that the exhibits also covered the subsequent reconciliation and improvement of relations with former enemy countries. I also sensed some communist propaganda aspects, but that too is interesting when viewed as part of this country.

Only part of the original prison exists.  The other part has been replaced by... a Hilton hotel.Nice evocation of the inmates life but unfortunately the explanation was way too much (red) coloured.  Apparently very difficult to put things in perspective.Nevertheless glad I saw it.

Worth a visit, especially for those familiar with Vietnam War.  Interesting view of French treatment of Vietnamese dissidents. View of Hanoi Hilton for American prisoners interesting, but filled with propaganda, light on all the truth.

Worth a trip to see how the the Vietnamese political prisoners were treated by French during their rule.  We were unaware of this part of Vietnam history and found it very informative to see and read about the way political prisoners were treated.
This facility, dedicated to the memory of victims who were imprisoned, abused, and tortured, may not be a large museum, but it allows us to imagine the enormity of the suffering.
The stories told here seemed rather lifeless and one sided.  Most of the old prison no longer exist. Only the front building remains.
Worth doing and inexpensive to visit. Learn about brutal history of prison during French occupation and read a perspective of it’s use holding US POWs in the 70s.

It is an interesting place but it hits you hard in the face how superficial some of the Information is. Prison conditions for US prisoners glamourised and ill treatment not acknowledged. It all feels a little contrived by the Vietnamese.
The prison interior is grim and creates an unpleasant impression. The propaganda texts seem ludicrous when one considers the ordeal suffered by French prisoners of war during the Indochina War.
Rather depressing, but important to remember the history.  Much more of the Vietnamese that we held here over the years than the Americans. Actually the part were the Americans were held is now gone.
You will catch a glimpse of Vietnam's dark history. The exhibits are not large, but there are still some artifacts and documents that depict what happened at that time.

Small and good if your interested in the many wars in Vietnam and the devices of punishment! It wasn’t really our cup of tea! Too much focus on punishment and torture, although semi-factual we didn’t really enjoy it. Lots of the information seemed quite one sided too which left you wondering how accurate it all was? Unless you have a fascination with war and punishment you don’t need to see this place.

Visiting the prison is exactly what I expected it to be. The architecture and history of the building is quite interesting. I recommend keeping an open mind and visiting it. It will only take about 30 minutes to walk through. There is an entrance fee, but it's not much.

A relatively small place to visit. Suggest to allocate not more than 1.5 hours to visit the prision entirely.Gives a good history lesson on the use of prision for political prisoners and for POWs during Vietnam War.
This museum simply conveys without gimmicks the history and suffering through the years. Worth a visit to help understand the historical context and perspective.
Interesting, although there's very little to see. Only a small part of what was once the entire prison remains, it's rather poor, and most of it consists of photographs.

Being an American, it was very interesting experience to see the Vietnamese perspective on this. The tour is self guided, so I would recommend purchasing the guide book so you can get some added explanation as to the history of what you are seeing
Perhaps because it is a country with a history similar to ours, there were many similarities.
Small entrance fee, rather oppressive atmosphere inside. If you're in the area, you can drop by.

The Prison is small as half of the prison has been demolished! Nothing much left to see other than some of the remaining sections of the prison such as the torture chamber and a few prison room. However, it was interesting to visit a prison for the first time.
Interesting to walk through. The death cells, in particular, gave me a claustrophobic impression. The mannequins placed in the cells are also frighteningly realistic.

I paid the Hoa Lo Prison a visit while in Hanoi for 3 days. It was very cheap to enter (around 1€), but was slightly underwhelming on the inside. Most of the original prison has been knocked down and you are therefore left with only a few rooms to visit. we did not use a guide so maybe that was part of the problem. Overall not worth prioritising over other sites.
I don't know if it because I have visited many old prisons but I felt that this place did not really make the most of the history and stories that it could have told.

You have expectations to see more of the old prison complex but straight after starting the guide tells us that most of the old prison had been pulled down and replaced with an office complex.What left is interesting and probably enough to get the feel of the conditions but it's hard to say especially with some of the "propaganda" on display. Probably to be expected. Still worth a visit.

While the prison exhibit is somewhat interesting there is very little left of the original site.  Certainly some interesting exhibits especially if you are French or American but as far as a rating is concerned, we felt it was only average.
Interesting to see, but could be more written info. I specially missed in depth stories about the prisoners to get a more personal touch.

Not very interesting place due poor  items at the exposition, just watching TVs or reading documents, things you can do via internet. You can finish your visit in 15 minutes. The positive point is you help the city to grow up tourists places.

The prison gives a good picture of what the French did to the Vietnamese, and frankly, it is quite severe. The fact that Americans were also imprisoned here is almost forgotten, and what is visible of them gives the impression that they didn't have it so bad. I don't know what the reality was.
Vietnam is good and immersing you in the moment and this is also the case at Hoa Lo PrisonWell worth a visit to understand the history of this Prison

I enjoyed the visit if added historical context to my awareness of this very interesting country. There were lots of articles over the various times that the prison was being used. Translations into 3 languages assists the whole experience.Overall the building was well appointed with good wide clear walking paths. There are some stairs to climb as a part of the tour.  There is a gift shop too.All in all a great afternoon

We enjoyed this Prison visit, it had well presented information regarding the history of the place. The prison is only a small place compared to its days of operation, this is due to the city expanding.
Interesting museum, but not much information available. Probably better with a guide. Missing an audio guide option.
Very small in comparison to the original size. Some great features but missing some important information. A audio guide may improve the experience. A good hours entertainment if in the city.

A respectful and well presented museum to the history of the prison up to its contemporary use in the Vietnam war for detention of captured US pilots. Some interesting material, tv footage and reconstructions of its older past.

The history of the Vietnamese prisons, both moving and horrific, is recounted, highlighting the war. Why this constant propaganda and the horrors of war, regardless of whether the perpetrators are Vietnamese, French, or American?

Although I knew from the name of the facility that it wouldn't be a particularly fun place to visit, seeing the panels, reconstructed figures, and solitary confinement cells and learning about the past history made me think more deeply than I expected. I
It isn't a place many people visit, but it is a good spot to stop by briefly before dinner while checking into a nearby hotel. It is similar to Seodaemun Prison in Korea, but on a smaller scale.

It's a visit that, if you know English, you can understand something about the history of the prison; otherwise, you can see some cells and some torture methods and so on, but it's not that important, really.

I could have done without visiting here but it was part of my tour. If you are in to horrendous treatment of prisoners and torture or you have a connection with the prison then go but otherwise give it a miss. Most of the prison has gone now, the bit that's left is a museum.
If you have already been to the war museum in HCMC then you can skip this one. It's quite a dark place, made me feel gloomy and uncomfortable.

Cheap attraction to learn some of the history of Hanoi.  It was only 30,000 VD to enter and took about an hour or so to go through all the exhibits.  These seemed to have a very one sided slant.  All about the terrible treatment of the Vietnamese prisoners by the French and the exceptionally good treatment of the American prisoners by the Vietnamese.  Nothing there would give nightmares to young children, but they might be a bit bored.  Lots of the exhibits were photos of the meetings of the political heads of the time, or documents (in Vietnamese) of what was discussed at these meetings but being as we couldn't understand it, it wasn't particularly informative for us.  Interesting exhibits regarding the escape through the sewers and the death cells, guillotine, etc.  Lots of history of the place but got a bit repetitive when extolling the virtues of "the struggle" and "our comarades" etc. Would still recommend it as I believe you should endeavour to learn something of the history of a place, not just go for shopping and scenery.  Was a quick and cheap way to spend an hour or two but be prepared to read between the lines sometimes.
It is interesting to learn about a historical period concerning both us French and the Vietnamese who welcome us today...

Interesting to stroll through briefly. The cells and photos are impressive and give an idea of ​​what it must have been like back then. However, I found the accompanying texts and other information very one-sided (lots of oppressed Vietnamese and little general info about the war) and nationalistic. All in all, certainly worth a visit.

Getting here was very easy and we found it no problem. I would suggest finding out information about it prior to coming as when you get there they charge you for an informational booklet. It's such a sad place to go because you really feel the loss that those affected by the prison felt. If you have a free day in Hanoi, I would suggest making a stop here if you're a history buff.

I visited with a uni student guide a few days ago. I normally like to visit museums on my own so that I can go at my own pace and absorb all the info... I didn't really get much out of visiting the prision, I found it reasonably small and not that much going on. Most of the info was about the Indochina war and there were 2 small rooms about American pilots in the American war. Was good to have a (semi) guide to get her opinion on some things.
we have been there because it was included in our tourism tour, but for me it is not very interesting...

We walked through this museum on our own with the guidebook. Perhaps it would have been better with a tour guide because there is not must information about the exhibits and I would not visit here again. Was disappointing because there is so much history about Vietnam that could have been covered!

It is a well run museum plenty of information . The part about the torture by the French was very informative a true . However,they claim that US prisoners lived in hotel conditions and are never harmed. That is a bit far fetched for any prison holding war prisoners.i did learn about French occupation. It was informative but westerners will find the information about US prisoners  a bit skewed especially when McCain and others prisoners spoke if their treatment and some showed signs of being beaten.

This is worth a visit (30K Dong) - but don't spend too much time here. For all of it's hype, it's pretty over-rated. The majority of the prison has been demolished and sold off for some horrendous tower block built next door. For all of the supposed sacredness of the site to the Vietnamese - they didn't build something very sacred at all in it's place. There are a few interesting bits, like the size of the cells and the guillotine. You do get a strong sense for how badly the Vietnamese were treated by the French in this prison - and you really do get a sense of how hard life must have been for the prisoners. The plaques to the 'matyrs' too is pretty moving - most of those executed were so young, handsome and passionate. Still, you can get a good sense of it in 30 minutes. Don't buy the guide book - complete waste of money.

This was interesting place to visit particularity if you are a history buff.  Be prepared for an unrealistic description of how US pilots were treated while imprisoned there.  The most interesting part of the visit was to better understand the determination of the people to govern themselves. We  were able to visit for about 2 hours and that was enough to see what was on display.
I went there because I wanted to learn more about Vietnam, but the explanations and narration were in English, so I didn't understand much. I just felt like I got a glimpse into the past.

This museum tells a story of French brutality.  However, don't know how factual, however, lacks info on the Mc Cain period of the U.S. involvement in the Vietnam war.  Depressing place and certainly the representation of a sad period in Vietnam history.  However, must see and you draw your own conclusions.

An interesting perspective on French colonization and an insight into the lives of prisoners. The visit has the merit of raising awareness about the plight of political prisoners and confronting France with its past. The entire visit is translated into French.

There were mostly Westerners there. It was quite difficult because everything was written in English. My child was scared of the dark room with mannequins and didn't want to go in. But I think they got a sense of the history behind it. There's also a famous chocolate shop nearby, so it's worth a visit.
The guillotine, gates, and other original artifacts are on display, making for a rather visceral experience.
Don't expect too much; it is certainly not the most impressive museum you will ever see, but popping in certainly won't hurt. The entrance fee won't stop you from going inside either. 30,000 VND

Unfortunately most the original prison is goneThis place is full of local propaganda, having read a few books from POW point of view where they explained how they were made to pose in favourable conditions seeing the said photos made me chuckle

Upon entering, from what I could see in the model, only certain parts remained. I relied more on watching the videos they provided for each section. Some parts seemed to be the original, but they were still chilling enough to give me goosebumps.
Worth visiting when in the French Quarter, gives an insight into the troubled history of Hanoi. Cheap entrance fee, worth getting the guide book to give greater depth of description.

This facility, built at the end of the 19th century, is said to have housed political prisoners (independence and nationalists) during the French Indochina era. The facility itself remained standing until the end of the 20th century, but because the neighboring Hanoi Tower was built on the redeveloped site of the former Hoa Lo Prison, only a portion of the prison remains today as a museum. It's very close to the Old Quarter, making it an easily accessible spot for a visit. The exhibit descriptions were in English and Vietnamese. Understanding English would deepen your understanding, but even if you don't, I don't think a tour guide is necessary.

If you have time, you can visit this place. Generally they do not show such things to tourist but here you get a chance to see how Vietnamese​  suffered for freedom.It will give u message - Freedom is not free, people died for it.If you are with children, you can skip, it is bit dark and depressing.

Worth a trip just so you can see how cruel some people can be.The Prison is not as large as it once was but you still get a feel of how life behind bars was all those years ago.There are still the death cells and an old Guillotine, this area is where many died, it's a somber atmosphere in there.

The cells and facilities are quite striking. There's free motorcycle parking at the entrance if you buy a ticket. You can easily visit in 30-45 minutes. It's nothing extraordinary, but it's worth a visit if you have some extra time.
If you really interested in history, you may visit this prison. You can see the prisoners life back then. The place gave my friends a creepy feeling since the place is quite gloomy and dark.
I was frankly a bit disappointed with this visit; two-thirds of the prison was destroyed and therefore inaccessible. Otherwise, nothing exceptional.
Very little to see here. Some of the old black and white videos are interesting but the set up is not that great. Spent about an hour. No need to stay any money than that.

If you just glance through it, it should only take about 30 minutes. The air conditioning wasn't very effective, and it was hot, so 30 minutes was about my limit. The photos of the guillotine and severed heads used during the French colonial era were so graphic that they've remained vividly in my mind.

We had the chance to visit this museum but I was not so satisfied. It's interesting but at the end is only a prison. We missed the opportunity to visit the Ho Chi Minh mausoleum and for this I was disappointed.
A very touching visit. Worth a visit to get an idea of ​​living conditions during the war.
We visited here on our tour and it was an interesting look at part of the history of Vietnam. I am glad I went there.

The Hoa Lo Prison, just located outside the Old Quarter is a place full of history but did not managed to convey this historical story to me personally. This site should be one of the main attractions of the city, but just like some of the other historical sites, it is quite boring. It feels like they filled the rooms with some random items used during that time. I think they could have done a lot more to this place as there are some really interesting things to show. Also, what happened in there, must have been horrific and inhuman. The TV's which are telling the stories take way to long, and subtitles only appear 1-2 seconds, which is not enough to read it. If you are in the neighbourhood I would visit this place. It would take around 40 mins to see everything.

The museum tells the history of the prison from French colonialism to Vietnam war days. However, could be a bit more detailed with more interactive tools. It also isn't very big, walking through and reading could be done in about 30min. They tell a lot about the Vietnamese resistance and their fight for communism and equality, which I'm not very sympathetic with, which might be the reason why I find it a bit boring. Amazing representation of Vietnamese character, and fight for independence though. In a nutshell , go if you have time to spare, but I wouldn't say it is a must do.

Good old piece of history in Vietnam and Hanoi. Takes about 20 mins to walk around and costs 30,000 VND each which is probably a fair amount.Gives more of an insight into pre-Vietnam war history but is worth a visit if you can fit it in.

Interesting visitYou don't Need a guide as all the info is well provided in English, French and VietnameseI wouldn't take small children there tho. Tickets were 30,000 vnd each. I do suggest you do some history reading beforehand, to get a balanced overview of all that happened :)
Interesting to see, and the illustrations are good. You don't get the hole picture becs it's not that huge and good information.
It's an activity that's decent for the price. They offer a Vietnamese perspective on the war, portraying the French as the culprits, but it's interesting to see that viewpoint.
Most of the original prison has been demolished so there isn't that much left to see. Emphasis on how badly the French treated prisoners and how well the Americans were treated while there.

This place is worthwhile to get a sense of the horrors of twentieth century Vietnamese history. It is strongest on the colonial period where it shows the apparatus of repression the French used to suppress nationalist and Communist campaigns for self-rule. The bare, morose cells in which political prisoners were kept shows the dark side of colonialism. The guillotine which was used for beheadings brings the point home even more strongly.Where the museum is more contentious is in its treatment of the Vietnam War. While it is correct surely that American carpet bombing of Hanoi caused huge suffering, the pictures of American POWs decorating the Xmas tree and receiving excellent medical treatment is surely a very one-sided presentation of history. That's why this place is only three stars for me.

Having been to this it is clear of the brutality that occurred within the walls and the exhibits do demonstrate the horrors. It is quiet creepy when looking through into the isolation rooms that there are mannequins within the chains, it does show the tightness of the space whilst giving it a realism.One has to be careful of the language however, it is very biased even still and shows the Vietnamese as heroic for fighting back, whilst their captives were simply terrorists. So because of this I do have doubts about some of the accuracies portrayed.It was still interesting and fairly inexpensive to go do.We spent about an hour here

The prison was worth the 1.5 dollars for sure. I liked learning something about the imprisonment of the Vietnamese and americans. I must say that i think it was a little minimal, i would have liked to see more so i would have gotten a better impression of the life of a prisoner at the Hanoi prison. But, overall, definitely worth the time and money!
We had to stop just for it's historical significance. Must remember that this is Vietnam's version of the story.

I went with some friends and a young child (a friend's younger sibling) who was very scared to go there. Although I know that this is evidence of the crimes of French colonialism left behind in Vietnam and its historical value always reminds us to remember the sacrifices of the heroes who died, I advise against bringing children along.

After visiting Saigon, we went to the Hanoi Hilton Prison because our hotel was nearby. Unfortunately, after what we'd seen at the Saigon War Museum, this prison didn't surprise us with anything new. It was much smaller and had much less to offer. The price was twice as high as in Saigon, but it offered much less history. For those who won't have the opportunity to visit Saigon, it might be okay.

The prison is only about 30% left as it has been mostly replaced by an office block. What remains is well laid out and clearly messaged and described throughout.The American section was very interesting and the display of torture methods of the french was also interesting.My teenage girls were prompted to a discussion on the Vietnam war and its merits that was most interest A must visit although the prison is now quiet small.

This place is definitely worth a visit. Very interesting and a lot to see and read. Eerie place. The prison is big, two floors. You get to see cells, a death row concept and death rituals. The have re-created the cells with 'wax' people which is very effective. The price is 30,000 Dong. And is around a 25 min walk from the Old Quarter. The majority of the prison is inside, so it is possible to do on a rainy day.

This place was not very busy in the morning. Tickets were super cheap and bought at the location at the time. English translations for everything. Spent max. 1- 1.5 hours. Worth seeing before you leave Hanoi.

An OK museum for Hanoi if I'm honest, not a great deal of info about how the prison was run and let's just say it was a little bias about how Americans were treated there. Go in with an open mind and learn a little more about communism but take it with a pinch of salt.
The museum is medium in size but not spectacular. While it is still a good experience to be able to walk and explore the actual exhibits themselves are not moving for some reason.

It's worth a visit and the 30,000 price, but it's also not THAT interesting. It's basically a prison built by the French in their colonial time on Vietnam. The prison exhibition tells the story of how badly the French treated Vietnamese political prisoners, and how well Vietnam treated American POWs later. Interesting but not a must see if you only have a short time in Hanoi.
The prison is cheap enough and sheds some light on the conditions of prisoners held there - not as interesting as other war museums / memorials I've visited.

We had just arrived in Hanoi so jet lag was definitely a factor. This was the first place our son took us since it was not a long taxi ride from our hotel in the Old Quarter. It was something to see, for sure.
It's a small museum of the old Vietnam prison; if you have the opportunity to go with a guide, they can explain the facilities inside, otherwise it's a themed museum.

The place is interesting, but swallowing all the hype they try to shove down your throat is a bit much. The explanations given make it seem like the prison was horrible when Vietnamese people were held there by the French during the colonial era, but magically the prison transforms into a nice place when you visit the wing where the Vietnamese held Americans during the Vietnam War. It's kind of hard to believe.

The prison museum is interesting and does give you a flavour of what happened and what it must have been like, but the information there is pretty poor, at least in English. They told you what things were and what had happened, but didn't explain things. For example there was a picture of some women and a caption saying 'imprisoned for their part in the Hanoi poisoning'. Well what was the Hanoi poisoning?! Their information needs to be improved I would say, as it has the potential to be fantastic. I think they just need to get a native speaker in there with a good understanding of Vietnamese who would be able to write more interesting information signs.

First of all, since it is located in an accessible area, I hope travelers will stop by. If you study Vietnamese history beforehand, you will feel the horrors even more deeply. Even if you go without prior knowledge, the interior is cool, and it is open to visitors, so I recommend stopping by.
I had the review before so thought I knew what to expect. It was OK but needs to develop what it has.

I think most people of herd of this place Hanoi Hilton its not a long visit because not a lot of it left, its informative with some good displays, I would of liked a bit more of the place standing to give a bit more of the experience
Interesting museum, but quite small and the English translations really need improvement! Clearly the information was not translated by a native English speaker and is hard to follow.

A little difficult to find but a rickshaw ride is probably the best way to get there. We arrived with about an hour to spare before it closed at 5pm and the place was very quiet with few tourists, so quite a good time to visit. Tells the storey of the French occupation of Hanoi and the terrible treatment of Vietnamese people. Hard hitting and cruel insight of life under the French rule.

This is an interesting visit and will take you about 90 minutes to do.  The story told is very much from a single perspective.  As a consequence it feels a little like propaganda which is understandable.  Would have been more interesting with a more balanced perspective and narrative
This was an interesting visit. Learnt about the history however as the people didnt speak much english it was a little hard to fully understand everything that happened.

It isn't particularly great for Koreans unfamiliar with Vietnamese history, but it is worth visiting at least once if time permits. However, despite being a building, the air conditioning wasn't properly maintained, so it was quite hot; although I wanted to look around more, I ended up leaving in a hurry. I don't remember the exact amount, but there is a small entrance fee, though it is inexpensive so it isn't a burden.
Interesting and informative if you like this sort of thing. Dosent take too long to look around and cheap admission. This was included in a "Walking Tour"  in our package.

I did go, but the experience was so dark that I don't really feel like recommending it. I think they were trying to show how badly the Vietnamese were mistreated during the French colonial period, but the exhibits were poorly made mannequins and lacked impact.
A reasonably good musuem about the horrors of wartime prisons and the brave people that lived/died there in Vietnam.  Great on your first day in Hanoi.

Slanted is generous. Some of the information presented is flat out false. Do your own research ahead of time about the imprisonment of Vietnamese under French occupation and imprisonment of US POWs during the Vietnam/American war. Then go and think critically about the way the museum tells this story.Facts aside, the museum is well curated with signs in Vietnamese, French and English.

Lots of history behind this place, pre-dating the vietnam war. Worth a quick pop-in, cheap admission. I'll echo what others have mentioned though, as it is a bit of an eye-roll of how the treatment of American soldiers was spun. You'd think they were at summer camp or something like that...
To better understand the recent history of Vietnam. The locations are historical. The perspective on events related to the war and the lives of prisoners is recounted with precision.

Visited the Hoa Lo Prison otherwise known as the Hanoi Hilton,  most of which has now been demolished , which gave you a very one sided view of its history suggesting that during the Vietnam war all the prisoners who were mainly downed American pilots, we treated with dignity and respect !!!!!  Yeah right . It is what it is and I suppose that every country wants to portray it people in the right Light but I know a fair bit about the war and not much of what I saw reflected what I had read and seen ! Maybe I've been reading all the wrong books ?
When you visit Hanoi, this is certainly one of the must-see remnants of the Vietnam war. It manages to visually show the terrors, but it is a bit static.

This is a really quick stop. For those unfamiliar with the Indochina Wars, it is portrayed much differently in Vietnam than perhaps elsewhere. War is told by the victor and their is stark belief that the Vietnamese people were victorious over the French and the American "invaders."That being said, this is an important piece of the country's history. Many leaders and icons spent time in what he affectionately refer to as the "Hanoi Hilton."  The exhibition vividly displays the gut wrenching conditions. A must see, to pay respects at the very least.
Be before warned when visiting this museum: The exhibitions and sketches give an insight into how history can be used/abused to convey a certain historical and ideological perspective.

My partner and I are very interested in the history of Vietnam and the struggles they have been through over the years. This prison was a good incite into the "hanoi Hilton" and how it was used initially by the French and then during the Vietnam War. It was a quick walk through, probably only took us 45 minutes and it costs around probably $6AUD including booklet.
Enjoyed finding out about the history but not a lot do do there.  Small exhibit and not something I would return to.

Without being disrespectful, I found this museum a tad propagandarish for my liking. Otherwise it was a museum where one could feel the frustration and anger of those implicated during the war, and perhaps this was the purpose after all. The entrance isn't exorbitant so it won't hit too hard if one decided to drop by. The room with the guillotine and video with disembodied heads was the highlight.
It would be a good idea to familiarize yourself with the prison camp online before visiting.

Spent 1 hour here. The museum provided information about how the french treated the vietnamese in this war time prison, and how the vietnamese treated the americans, although I'm not too sure about the accuracy because it seemed to be presented in a biased, propaganda-ish way. Entrance fee was only 30,000 so you can go if you have nothing better to do. Some mannequins along the way in the cells which were a little creepy and shocked a little boy who was not expecting it. Parents should look into cells before showing children.

Very few things we saw in a dozen or so tourist venues in Hanoi carried the feel of "theme park" exploitation.  The Hoa Lo Prison comes very close.  It has a worthy primary theme -- the struggle for independence from French colonial domination -- but the diorama approach gives it a feel of the theme being pushed too hard  Likewise, the chronicling of escapes through the sewer system is interesting, but using salvaged sections of the sewers as the focus of the exhibits seems curious.For Americans, the 1967-1973 use of the prison as the "Hanoi Hilton" for captured American pilots is certainly a draw ( as evidenced by the tour buses lined up on the street outside).  Still, that era is given only brief coverage, with the primary exhibits seemingly the same press photos of capture American pilots playing ping-pong.  The post-colonial utilization of the prison may not warrant the full attention of the Vietnamese, but I think it will provide a "wake up" moment for American visitors.  John McCain would be among the most notable pilot prisoners held here (for most Americans), but he receives only a passing mention as one of the pilots who went on serve as an American MP.The Vietnamese sense of perspective offered here--French Colonialism vs. the American involvements in Vietnam--make the visit worthwhile. It is important to understand the Vietnamese perspective on their own history, but that message is actually muted here.  The day we visited, the museum was so overrun by tour groups that I completely missed the exhibit on the American pilots.  My wife did catch it led me back to it.  It was so jammed with assembly line tourists, however, that I couldn't do anything more than pass by the displays.My advice:  ry to figure out an off time to visit.  It was worthwhile, perhaps mostly because it was not at all what I expected.

Worth a quick visit. Only 30k VND to get into. Most of the prison has been replaced with an apartment block so is fairly small and can be round it, reading everything in c.30-60mins, so only a small attraction.Worth a visit if you have some time, but not a must-see.

For Americans this prison will always be the Hanoi Hilton, but the colonial history is interesting.  Much of the complex has been destroyed, but a small portion is set up as a museum.  One wonders if the history of the French colonial period is distorted to the degree that the history of the "American War" is.  Nevertheless, for military buffs, this is a "must see".
As other reviews have mentioned, I think it's best for women not to go alone. The atmosphere is heavy.

The actual visit took place in May 2014. The exterior and interior remained as they were at the time, revealing the gloomy atmosphere of the prison and the unsettling living conditions of the inmates. Perhaps this serves as a stark reminder that one should not choose a life of crime.

Entry ticket was inexpensive and worth going to see. We just happened to walk past and thought why not have a look. Commentary was heavily biased. We did our own tour was definitely a sombre feeling as you walked around. Very interesting to see from a historical point of view.

There is no way to "rate" a visit to the Hanoi Hilton that is going to be very meaningful to others, as what you think of your time there is pretty much determined by your motive in going.As an American veteran of the Vietnam War, I wanted to see where some of my heroes like John McCain had been held, and to get a feeling for what they endured.First surprise: most of the prison has been demolished and only a portion remains, for museum purposes.Second surprise: a lot of the exhibit has to do (and understandably so) with the long history of Vietnam conflict that doesn't even involve Americans.  That makes sense, from a Vietnam historical perspective; I just wasn't aware of it, so was surprised.Not a surprise: some (much?) of what was said in the exhibits having to do with "the American war" was, to be polite, propaganda. OK, lies.So if you are looking for an unbiased presentation of America's involvement in the war and the life of Americans held prisoner here, forget about it.If you are looking for a glimpse of that sorry chapter in the war, or if you go to pay homage to the men who were held there, it could be worth your while.

It's a prison. There you'll see how prisoners (both men and women) were held, and there are mannequins representing the people and how they were mistreated. But I don't really consider it a must-see; it's something you could do if you have some extra time in Hanoi.
Could not say much on this because we did not go inside.  We just checked it out and took pictures from the outside area. Perhaps next time if we had more time to visit.

Our family went to visit the prison and enjoyed a few hours looking around. There were some interesting videos being shown that told the story of the prisoners experiences and pictures of many prisoners and their stories. A lot of the memorabilia was written in Vietnamese so we couldn't really take in too much detail.It is more like a gallery now so you don't really get the 'real gaol' vibe too much apart from a section which houses a few original cells. We were definitely glad we visited and was probably one of the more interesting museum type experiences for the kids particularly.Worth a visit!
Not too much of any real interest here , worth a visit to but don't expect too much , I was in and out in about 30 minutes

Small museum primarily focused on the struggle for independence from French rule. The American prisoners (Hanoi Hilton) are mentioned only minimally, in just two rooms. However, there is a clear picture of the horrors endured by the Vietnamese prisoners.
It's an interesting place only if you visit with a guide because you might miss a lot of valuable information.

I'm not the kind of tourist who likes to visit former prisons or concentration camps, however, for this one I was with a group and basically had to join in.  It's an interesting enough attraction, the information you get is shocking and eye opening. Nevertheless, the visit is very brief and we ended up spending more time in the museum's stores.

We walked from St Joseph's Cathedral area to prison, enjoying seeing a bit of the French Quarter of Hanoi.  Spent 60-90 min there and bought the English guide book for 20.000 VND which had many photos in it.  Ticket admission reasonable @ 30.000 VND.  Sadly reflected on the fact that "water boarding" is still a torture used today.
Decided to go here as close to hotel and police museum. Interesting history with the French and how political prisoners were treated. I would not make it a must see, but informative.
A brutal look at how the Vietnamese people were treated by the French. The section detailing how the American POW were treated seemed very biased though.

Over half of the prison has been replaced with skyscraper buildings.  Small museum.  Most of the museum presents the treatment of Vietnam prisoners during the French occupation.  Not an honest representation of how the American POW's were treated.  Signage and pictures of American POW's very limited.  Not worth the 8 hour bus ride, 4 hours each way,

This is 100% propaganda museum. Everything is portrayed and presented from the communist perspective. If you are French or American this might stir your blood a bit so be prepared. As is said, "the victor writes the history."  Still, I recommend visit. Plan about 1 hour. Turn left at entrance and find the American items. Only two small rooms of American. Rest is French.

The information provided focuses primarily on Vietnamese prisoners; American prisoners are barely mentioned. The visit lasts about an hour but can be longer if you enjoy reading all the information panels. The death row corridor is unsettling; go to the very end and look at each cell as you walk, and you'll understand everything. This place made me want to watch classic war movies.

It was interesting to see Hoa Lo Prison (also known as the Hanoi Hilton).  It was informative regarding the terrible things that have occurred over the years to the Vietnamese people.  There are two parts to the museum.  One focuses on the actions taken during the French rule and war in Vietnam.  The other focuses on the time that the facility was used to house American pilots during the Vietnam War (the Vietnamese call it the American War).One needs to read the history books but it certainly seems that the American pilots were treated much better than the Vietnamese prisoners.It represents dark times from the history of Vietnam.

This used to be a prison for Vietnamese people during the war with the French. Then captive American pilots were here during the war with USA. Well maintained and lots of info ..Some gruesome.However, fair bit of propaganda in terms of how people were treated etc..So take it with a pinch of salt.
Parts of the museum is very moving with powerful exhibits, parts were a bit laboured and dull.The room with models shackled was shocking, and the memorial wall was incredible.

Scary place created by the French to break the will of unruly subjects. It did the opposite. It´s now a memorial to the revolutionaries who stayed firm in their beliefs. It would be an even greater testament if the whole story of the suffering of American POWs were told too.

When touring around hanoi a very historic and eerie place, with some of the original building still in tact. Quite a bit larger than thought upon arrival and very cheap entry for an attraction especially being in the capital!

This gives you good insight into the French and Vietnamese relationship in the 40 and 50 The section on the American prisoners takes all your ability to suspend belief Please note I am or never have been supporter of war however  and little less bias would be appreciated

Fairly interesting but you really need a tour guide with you if you want more information. Quite confronting for young children as they have models placed in cells etc demonstrating what it was really like. You only see 1/5 of the original prison, which is more than enough.

Learnt a lot about how the prison ran and got an idea of how prisoners were treated. Great thing to do in wet weather. Wouldn't recommend for families with young children as they'd get bored. Majority of the prison has been knocked down and a lot is just displays that have been recreated. A cheap and interesting experience.

Expected this to be better than it was, basically a few boards and pictures on wall then some of the cells. Probably only hour- hour and half worth of your time. I expected more to be honest and thought it was a very average experience
It looks like they've recreated one or two things at the camp site, but there isn't anything special.
We did not like the difference in the view of the very nasty French and the very kind Vietnamese towards the prisoners.

The Hoa Lo Prison is also known by American as the Hanoi Hilton. This prison was used by the French to jail the Vietnamese political prisoner. Only 2 rooms show pictures of the captured American pilots. A disappointment.
It is quite nice to see how the Americans were imprisoned here. Watch the history video first.

The legendary 'Hanoi Hilton'. Worth a look? I guess so. Interesting relic which has been left intact along side some high rise apartments.  As with all museums i have visited in Vietnam, it would benefit from not being a propaganda tool to indoctrinate it's masses. A balanced view of things would make it a more interesting place to visit.
Interesting for those who want to learn about the country's history, but the images are very graphic; perhaps necessary, but not suitable for sensitive viewers.

Having visited a couple other prisons from the French colonial era, this was nothing new for us. The Vietnamese love to highlight how terrible the French were to the prisoners, which we understand... it was a prison after all.

This is OK to see though much of the prison is no longer there. Though it addresses the Vietnam war, much of the material there is focused on the history of the prison from the time the French built it.

My guess is that appeal of this place to American  visitors depends on their feelings about  the Vietnam War or, as the Vietnamese call it, the American War. I visited with 3 or 4 people who were in that war and  the son of a former U. S. prisoner with the man who  guarded him happened to be there. This was apparently not  planned. The American  had his father's diary with him. The historical orientation of  the  prison is more  anti-French. This was a French  prison and  the treatment  of Vietnamese prisoners is emphasized. This is only a small part of the  original prison the main  part is  now a large, modern  hotel which  looms over this  old structure.

What I understood was interesting, but I could have used some more backstory on the Vietnamese revolutionaries and what they were fighting for. Overall I found certain displays great, but as a whole the museum wasn't super cohesive.

Is place was really interesting to look at but would have been much better is you could find out more of the history.  They have an information booklet you can buy but after a while stopped reading and just walked round.  They should have an audio guide which would be easier.
Good way to spend 60_90 minutes. They sell a book upon entrance- it is very cheap but the information inside three prison is good, so purchase it if you want to re read the history afterwards.

I liked this prison museum a reasonable amount, as it taught you a considerable amount about prison in the olden times. However, it was a bit gruesome, as in there were model people everywhere, and they all looked terrified, but the worst bit was that there was an actual guillotine in the room, and you could see how terrified the people in prison must have been.

The former French prison was largely destroyed for a new building. What remains is just enough to give one an impression of what it was like to be a prisoner in the French colony. Not a nice regime... The part where the use as a prison for the US prisoners of war is shown looks a little too optimistic.It is worth a visit but just for an hour.

If you read about Vietnam's history, you know that Vietnam went through a lot. However, it's a different kind of feeling visiting the "Hilton Prison", also known as Hoa Lu Prison. Some of the pictures and sculptures paint a gruesome picture, not for the faint hearted. I'd recommend going on a tour to find out more and you'll also get to understand it on a deeper level. Won't reveal too much, you have to go there to find out more!

As a prison officer myself I am always interested in visiting prisons around the world and the 'Hanoi Hilton' is definitely worth a visit. It is nothing spectacular but it gives you an insight into life in the prison under French control. Like the War Remnents museum in HCMC, it is extremely biased (to be expected obviously) so go in with an objective mind.

This is a must visit on the tourist circuit of Hanoi, but it's hard to believe anything of what is written. The information boards are incredibly biased and leave you wondering what, if anything, is historically factual. For example, one board states that despite the Vietnamese government being at war and having a shortage of money, the US prisoners were treated "as best as they could be", and you'll see plenty of photographs of smiling American inmates playing pool, cards etc. Everything here needs to be taken with a pinch of salt. It's an exhibit that's essential to visit and to see the conditions for yourself, particularly the death row dungeons used by the French colonists, but do your own research to make sense of it all. I would rate higher if there wasn't so much propaganda

Very similar in its message to the War Remnants Museum in HCMC.  30,000 VND grants admission to the gatehouse of 'Hanoi Hilton'. Used in the colonial period for political prisoners and later for US POWs in the Vietnam War.In my opinion, there is much more to this beautiful country and bustling city.

Built by the French to hold Vietnamese dissidents it gives you a picture of the fight for freedom - where the prisoners were the heroes. That way it's similar to many others (ex in Phnom Penh). But part of it's history is also as "Hanoi Hilton", the prison for many American prisoners of war. Telling both these stories makes the impressions a bit mixed and more complex than for most similar places.

Obviously there is lot of history here, but after going through what is left of the whole facility, I found it difficult to know what to believe.  Some of the photos don't lie, but some do, and you are left to make your own conclusoins since there is no way of knowing when the truth ends and the propaganda begins.  And make no mistake, there is no shortage of propaganda when visiting these Vietnamese war memorial sites.  It was even worse at the prison we visited on Phu Quoc Island.

It was interesting to see where the American POWs were held during the conflict in the war with Vietnam which was relatively recent history. The place was purpose built by the French as a prison and I was interested to see the guillotine there which I had not associated with Vietnam previously. Good to see but not outstanding.

This prison is worth a visit but you must realize that the government has the ultimate say over the content and therefor things seem a bit slanted. For example, it provides details of the brutality the French used over the Vietnamese kept in the prison; but during the Vietnam war the prison was then used to House US POW's and the content in the museum makes it seem like the POW's were living in a resort,  not locked in prison. So just know that going in. They do have a lot of interesting artifacts and just the overall history of the place is fascinating!

Worth spending like an hour or so in there  Too much useless  details But when you get to the cells you feel the shiver down your spine Watch out in there even the ticket comptoir tried to rip me offThey demolished a big part of the prison to build a big tower The small left part is definitely must do

In Vietnam, the "Vietnam war" is understandably called "the American war."  I get that.  It distinguishes that war from the many others in Vietnamese history.  They have fought the Chinese, the French, the Cham, the Cambodians...they have a long history of war.Entrance to the prison is a paltry 20,000 dong, or about one US dollar.  Not too much to shell out to visit an icon of history that most Americans my age have heard about.  The brutal torture of US servicemen at this prison was renowned after the war.  John McCain among the many airmen who did time at that facility.I was not aware that Hoa Lo (known to us Americans as the "Hanoi Hilton") was built by the French as a prison to house and mistreat the Vietnamese dissidents of the period prior to the American/Vietnamese war.  That history was amply presented, taking up the majority of the exhibits.  The Vietnamese were truly mistreated by the French, even executed by guillotine, the original device still displayed for visitors to see.The exhibits are decent, using a variety of methods to tell the story of the French occupation and brutality.  I was glad to visit the prison to broaden my own knowledge on the pre-American history there.  However, this prison is renowned to the "West" as the Hanoi Hilton.  That portion of the history is condensed to two small rooms near the end of the exhibit.  Far less space than even the reflection garden on the grounds.  And the propaganda is disconcerting.  It diminished the story that the prison tells about the abuses against the Vietnamese.  The exhibits go to great lengths to explain how well US servicemen were treated, even using the sarcastic "Hanoi Hilton" as a propaganda tool, explaining that the US Servicemen gave this prison that name because it was so comfortable.  The photos show captured pilots playing pool, smoking cigarettes, smiling and many of the other photos that were staged by the North during their captivity.  I don't have a problem with propaganda, per se.  I understand, particularly in a communist country, you are going to get some propaganda.  But the convergence of history and propaganda in a museum was a bit much for me to take.  As I said, lying about the treatment of US servicemen diminished my view of the French portion of the exhibit.  It caused me to doubt the allegations.  If you will lie about one thing...wouldn't you lie about the other.  So I left dissatisfied.  Hoping that the government will one day understand how this undermines their own credibility.  Hoping that they will eventually correct these exhibits and tell the truth.  It is okay for them to tell it from their perspective.  It is okay that they say the US invaded Vietnam (and deny the fact that the south invited us there).  It is even okay to say that the US was attempting to exploit Vietnam.  That is all fair game.  But lying about facts that have never been in dispute in a memorial museum is very disappointing.  Yes, visit.  It is worth the meager dollar admission fee.  Just understand that propaganda is alive and well.

Only a 4/5 of the original prison remained for the visit. 30,000vnd each visitor. All explaination are available in Vietnamese, French and English. Some part are quite impressive. especially the death sentenced cells and the guillotine.Don't forget to visit the upper part. 30-45 min will be enough for a full visit. Very central close to Melia hotel.
Definitely something to see. Interesting perspective on how much "fun" the American soldiers had while they were being held here...

Fängelset byggdes runt 1890 och hade fångar fram på 50- talet och var mycket otrevligt för de som satt där. Vi hade inte så mycket att göra för dagen så därför blev det ett besök där. Det är ingen större sevärdhet men vi fick se hur illa de inspärrade hade det.
Ein Besuch ist schon interessant und lehrreich, aber man darf nicht zu viel erwarten. Das Kriegsopfermuseum in Saigon ist jedenfalls umfangreicher und vielfältiger.

Kann man sich ansehen, muss man jedoch nicht. Es existiert lediglich noch ein kleiner Teil des Gefängnisses. Wirkt irgendwie etwas lieblos zusammengeschustert. Alles in allem hat es uns nicht sehr gut gefallen. Für 30.000 VND Eintritt pro Person jedoch zu verkraften. Lediglich die investierte Zeit wäre wo anders besser aufgehoben.

This is the notorious prison used by the French to subjugate the Vietnamese, and later used by the Viet Cong to imprison American pilots during the Vietnam War. Unfortunately more than 2/3 of this prison was demolished in the 1990's to make way for a high rise construction, and only the main entrance and some of the cell blocks remain. A number of displays and video clips show the history of the prison, some of which in a decidedly anti-French way. Worth a visit if you have a few days in Hanoi and done with the usual touristy stuff in Old Quarter, but maybe skip it if you're short on time.

We went with the family to see the prison. The place was worth a visit, although we quickly went around it. It doesn't offer the most revolutionary prison description in the world, however. You can go around with a stroller, except for the short last part, when you can leave the stroller at the foot of the stairs. Of course, there are several small stairs and door lifts.

A part of the prison was demolished to build Hanoi Tower and there were not many things to see. We finished in less than 2 hours, which was fast.I was expecting more torture tools exhibits used on the prisoners, but there are not many. The mock up did not look real at all. There are not many cells and an area was set-up to show well treatment to American POWS, it was a surprise. Don't go with high expectations.

I took a walk to here from my hotel in the old quarter , its a nice place with some good exhibits but I was expecting more for some reason , cant really complain as cost was minimal and was well kept place

If you are not usually a museum-goer i would not recommend Hoa Lo, I think its more for people who have a special interest for these kinds of things. It was cheap tickets and you get a lot of history and learn a lot of information, although I wish they could have written more in english. Worth the ticket fee, maybe not worth your time if you don't have a long stay in Hanoi.
Self guided tour. Interesting mix of information. I stopped off to kill a few hours. A good rainy day activity.
It's not a must visit to Hoa Lo Prison. But if you have free time & wanted to know more about Hanoi City's history back in years, this is the place.
As the name suggests its a prison and its very touristy. Walking distance from the hoan kiem lake!!!
Perhaps more interest to the Americans who spent the war behind the walls. The guillotine stands proud and worth a room of it's own. Worth 30-40 mins
A grim reminder of war and some rosy depiction of American POW life which I can't quite get my head around, not a place I would recommend except to remind oneself of the unnecessary pain of strife.

But really there are so many of these kinds of attractions and they all have the same despicable tale to tell of how cruel man can be.I don't think there is a jail museum in the world that hasn't got a story to tell about past degradation, violence and mistreatment of political and non political prisoners.

Apparently american POWs lived like kings while they stayed in the "Hanoi Hilton" Museum did reveal some interesting history but also continues with propaganda for the state. Must see tour stop for anyone interested in the Vietnam War.
If you have some extra time, it's worth going. The place is small, so you can go quickly just to see it.
The study of the spaces and especially the explanations described therein provide a good overview of the suffering and the will to live of this admirable people.
The prison was definitely interesting. Unfortunately a large portion of the original prison was demolished to make way for a tower block of apartments.

We spontaneously went here with out planning to , a local told us it's good when we got there Didn't cost must for entry which is good , very interest to see such a eye opener to see how the prisoners were treated I would recommend going.

I was not sure if I should go in or not, but did anyway. I wouldn't say that I enjoyed the visit, but it was educational. It simply amazes me what the human species does to one another for land, natural resources, religion, money and power.

Hoa Loa Prison - well presented, educational but a pretty grim place. Gives you a good perspective on the Vietnamese struggle and the horrible conditions they were subjected too by the French. The Vietnamese treatment of the American POWs seems to be too good to be believable. Lots of displays and details.  Only a small part of the original prison remains. Takes about 1-2 hrs depending on how much time you spend
However, the theme could have been explored more thoroughly. It's one of those places you have to go, but it's nothing surprising.

This was interesting, what there was of it. The vast majority of the prison was torn down to make way for a hi rise building. What remains is a very small section with on,y a few things to see. I was a bit disappointed since there really was not a lot else to see in the neighborhood.  The story is repetitive and unbalanced historically.

The visit to this prison is quite worthwhile. The "story" is told entirely from the Vietnamese perspective. We usually hear it from the other side. That makes it quite interesting. The exhibition is fairly basic, not exactly great. Fortunately, there are usually English "subtitles".

Interesting museum with a good insight to the activities of the French then the Vietnamese. The latter will tell you that during the Vietnam War the prisoners nicknamed it the Hanoi Hilton as they were looked after so well. Suspect a bit of political intervention here.

It makes no impression on us as Israelis or Jews. Unfortunately, we know of museums or institutions of this type that are presented in a more tangible way. And the fact that McCain, with all due respect, was there makes no impression on me. The museum is only a small part of the original prison.

Entry is 30 000 and an additional booklet regarding the history is available for another 20 000 dong. The vast majority of this site was knocked down in favour of tower blocks and this is basically just the gate house - very little seems to be original. It took me less than one hour to tour the whole site. Furthermore, you should know that the majority of the information relates to the treatment of the Vietnamese at the hands of the French and their own fellow countrymen (political prisoners). One small room shows items relating to the US pilots who were held here - this is mainly (very obvious) propaganda - lol the jackets / leather satchels they were presented with on release : ))) It was interesting to visit to say you've been, but certainly not a true reflection of the truth or even original artefacts.
They demolished 80% of the real prison to build a new building. Only a small part remains to be seen. Despite this, it's cheap and worth visiting if you're passing through.

Basically all that is left is the Gatehouse which is now a museum. Easy to get to and only 20 minutes walk from the Old Quarter and 10 minutes from the Lake. Interesting but concentrates in the main on the time under the French, rather than the 'hospitality' given to the American Prisoners of War. The communist version and its gloss over seems so dated and quite pathetic. Fortunately it seems since 1986 the country as far as tourism is concerned has been given its freedom, making Hanoi a great place to stay and enjoy.

Very cheap to enter.  Some english is written to help you understand the exhibits.  There are interesting things to see - but so much of the prison was demolished to make way for the new buildings.  It's worth a look if you're in Hanoi :)
The most fascinating part is the blatant propaganda and 'Alternate Truths'. What I could not find out (but wanted to) is how the prisoners that escaped via the sewer (!!) cut through the metal bars>?

It's a place you can visit or not; it's quite small and doesn't have any particularly memorable exhibits. Compared to the S21 concentration camp in Phnom Penh, it's much simpler. If you don't have much time in Hanoi, I wouldn't recommend going.

I think it was much smaller than I expected. Maybe it was because I went with high expectations, but I found it just okay. Still, I think it would be more informative if you read the descriptions and studied the historical background beforehand.

What remains of the prison that was called the "Hanoi Hilton" during the Vietnam War is quite small but we found this to be a worthwhile visit.  This small prison museum focuses mostly on the history during the French era.  It gives a good perspective of what the conditions must have been like during the time it was a prison. It also includes a section with photographs and some clothing from the Vietnam War showing the captured American pilots including Senator John McCain.  Our guide did remind us that the museum presents the Vietnam perspective some of which is propaganda such as the treatment of the American prisoners. We know he still suffers from the injuries from his treatment.
It was good to understand what had happened historically at this prison.  It was rather small and focused on what happened under the French occupation of Vietnam.

The museum seemed to repeat itself re facts and details on the signage.  The info on US pilots captured and held there was interesting but some wording seemed like propaganda.  However, it was an interesting viewpoint.

This prison was built by the French at the end of 19th century. It tries to give an impression of what conditions would have been like, including one room with life size figures of inmates. The cells used for punishing one or two prisoners conveyed more of a sense of the brutality of the place. The genuine guillotine is pretty scary.After the French colonial period, during the Vietnam war (or American war, if you are Vietnamese) the prison housed captured American pilots. A gallery of photographs shows the excellent treatment that they received. It's all a question of balance and perspective.Not a very big site - apparently most of the prison was demolished to provide land for the adjacent office block. But worth a short visit.

I don't really like this kind of place, they are somehow sad, but despite my personal opinion, I really do think worth visiting. Is obviously an historical place where the main topic is Vietnam war in the 60s.Touching
There's not a vast amount left, but what is there is interesting and reasonably well displayed (as long as you can see beyond the propaganda speak). Definitely worth a visit.
Tourist attractions in Vietnam are generally poorly documented. This one has numerous information panels in three languages, a big plus. The entrance fee of 20,000 dong (80 cents) is reasonable.
Not much to see but the old photos of American pilots are classic.  The staged exhibits with statutes can be missed. You can see what you're Ed to see here in about ten minutes.
Although built by the French and eventually housed American prisoners it was a disturbing and thought provoking visit.
Was expecting more. Was ok but nothing special Was more about the prison from the old days. Wanted more about the Vietnam war.  Particularly more info about John McCain

This museum can be visited in the span of 45 minutes. The most interesting part was the propaganda that said American prisoners were treated better than the average Vietnamese person. That is completely false.
This prison tells something about history of Vietnam. The prison is small and one hour is enough for visit but it will definitely give chills.

Tour the remaining part of the prison and witness the Colonial brutality inflicted on the Vietnamese. During the "American War", John McCain was here in the Hanoi Hilton.  Hear their side of the story.
The history of the way of imprisonment in colonial times is not amusing, it reminds us of the dark side of mankind.
Wife and I finished the entire tour and did not really think it was information or interesting.  I would not recommend this place.

The prison museum is a bit out of the way and doesn't really offer anything you wouldn't expect. It also had virtually no information on how the prison held American PoWs and simply concentrated on the French colonial period.

It was interesting to read and learn about what happened here, but it was a long tour and very pricey for how cheap everything is in Hanoi. I love history, but I'm more of an ancient history buff and love old architecture and culture. This bored me a little, I'm ashamed to say bc what happened here is an incredible story, but I could have gone without seeing it. My fiancé thoroughly enjoyed it though.

My son (14) and I visited here in Jan17It is worth the visit, and reasonably pricedUnsurprising, the history is very weighted in favour of Vietnam, so the happy smiling pictures of the American POWs seem incongruent with the reports that the POWs brought back

This was a facinating prison, with a spooky atmosphere, and there was half priced entry for students which is always a bonus. However the information inside was quite one sided so I recommend reading up on the history beforehand or bringing a guide book with you
This is worth checking out to understand the history of the area. You can walk around without a guide as the signs are in english, french and Vietnamese; but the guides do add background context.

This place is worth a visit when u are at Hanoi. We walked from old quarter and it took us 25 minutes to reach there. It denotes the hardship faces by Vietnamese against the imperialist powers of the time. It is quite enlightening and the place can be covered in a short span of time.
