We visited this place out of curiosity and it was very average. We learnt new stuffs about Vietnam war and the hardship they went through. Place was clean and well maintained but only for historical buffs. Other than that, nothing spectacular.

80% of the prison has been redeveloped so not a lot to see. Also there is no map or guide so you get a bit lost as to what you have or have not seen. Vietnamese history told from their point of view. Worth the visit of you have the time.

Most of the prison is long gone.  Torn down in the 1990's to make way for a large office building.  The main guardhouse and some cells are all that's left.Some of the cells have their original leg irons and the big cell block is populated with sculptures of men in varying degrees of distress..  Historical displays regarding the prison's past before Vietnam.  An amusing video on how well the American POW's were treated.A good rainy day activity if you want to kill an hour or two but once is enough.

The Hoa Lo ("Hanoi Hilton") museum is a must-see stop for most tourists visiting Hanoi, and it's worth seeing simply for the complicated history found within.The prison is centuries old, and while most of it has been torn down and replaced with a residential high-rise, the corner that remains has been transformed into an exhibit with photographs and accounts of the prison's history.Perhaps most famous (to Americans) for the photo of John McCain being tended to on a hospital bed by a Vietnamese doctor, the museum also offers remnants of the prison's cells, the guillotine used for certain prisoners, and the horrific 'tiger cages' used to keep several prisoners inside a confined space barely large enough for one person (let alone 3-4).Typically crowded with the tour bus visitors that flock here on their circuit, the jail is a worthy stop if you can beat the crowds early in the morning to explore the small grounds in peace and quiet. It should not take more than an hour to walk through and get a sense of the exhibit's main attractions and captions.

I visited this prison and recalled it being really good - I have just visited and it seems parts of it have been closed off - I don't know if thats true or not or my imagination??  I found it interesting to visit but only spent about 30 minutes here.
Definitely worth the entrance fee..just a couple dollars and there was a lot to see. The building is very well preserved.

This museum offers a chance to learn more about the country's colonial past and the recent war with the USA. It's interesting, but we still saw everything quite quickly. Finally, at 30,000 dinars for entry, why not?

This is (part of) a historic prison, a product of French colonialism. "Part of" because most of it has been destroyed to make way for real estate development.The remaining portion focuses primarily on French rule over Vietnam and the mistreatment of locals who raised up against them. That really is what the focus is on, not the Vietnam war component.In more modern times, it was the site of where USA POW's were held, but much of that area is no longer in existence. There are photos (primarily the old propaganda shots) and Senator McCain's uniform and helmet (his cell no longer exists)  but not much else regarding what would be of interest to most Western baby-boomers.It has a historical value so you may want to visit...but know that the Hanoi Hilton aspect is very limited.
A prison museum that opens up a perspective on the background of the Vietnam War, perhaps a bit one-sided, but with some really interesting objects on display, including a real guillotine.
A good historical place to see when considering the Vietnamese resistance against the French. However, one needs to disregard the communist propaganda which was rampant and laughable.

We stopped in for an hour not expecting too much.It's actually an interesting account of the Vietnamese communist struggle against the French colonialists, but unfortunately veers into the realms of nationalist propaganda when talking about its use to house American airman as POWs. Apparently they were treated to better food and clothes than the local Vietnamese population...for reasons never really made clear.That said, it's an interesting example of the winner writing the history books and I found it good value for the entry fee (if I remember rightly, 30k vnd).
The Hoa Lo prison was interesting. A lot of the exhibits seemed to repeat themselves. Wouldn't go back for a second visit.
Not what I expected as most of the prison is gone and the remaining is pretty sterile and focuses on the atrocities the french did to the vietnamese.

this is the prison used during different Vietnamese wars , they have retained a small area , to show the barracks , the history and the way to escape , this was last use to keep the pilot of American air force, that is why this is also called as the hotel hilton/ maison.

We were interested in this visit and since the prison is primarily focused on political prisoners during the French conflicts, we thought it would be a good learning experience. While it was somewhat informative about the Vietnamese prisoners, everything we "learned" was called into question when we entered the rooms regarding the American conflict. We are open to the idea that there are two sides to every story but the videos in the two rooms on this era were grossly inaccurate and offensive. I realize the Vietnam government churns out a lot of propaganda but the over-the-top video of prisoners seeming to enjoy the cultural exchange when, in fact, they were tortured and abused was horrible. It called into question the accuracy of every other aspect of the museum. So I can't say that we really learned a lot but it was informative in the sense of truly understanding how much government propaganda permeates the culture.
It's a prison where many explanations are in French... a place where you ultimately see very little except for some of the conditions of detention for political prisoners or women!
A Vietnam War detention center. The aim is to see the former prison facilities.

A museum in three parts with a clear and easy-to-follow layout. Explanations in French and English, and plenty of concrete information without requiring too much reading. The section on the conditions of Vietnamese detention is quite graphic and may not shock the more sensitive visitors... however, the section on American prisoners is perplexing: it's like something out of a Disney movie, it was so idyllic for them... where does the truth lie? Isn't the plight of the Vietnamese exaggerated while the fate of the Americans is idealized? We'll never know, but form your own opinion...

Hoa Lo Prison originally built by the French in 1896. During the Vietnam war, it was a place for incarceration for downed US pilots. Most of the prison complex was demolished in 1997 for the development of a high rise building complex , with a small section of the former prison remained which house the current museum.
An interesting, cheap thing to do while visiting! Lots of history and facts that we never knew about before visiting.
Only a small complex, whilst somewhat interesting, it was clear the treatment of prisoners was horrific.  Not our cup of tea but a real history buff may find it more interesting.

Only a small part of the original prison remains and most of the exhibits are not great. It started as a colonial prison and no surprise this was not a pleasant place. Most of the site is dedicated to the overthrow of the colonial yoke and the suffering the Vietnamese went through to gain independence from the French. There is a small section dedicated to the American pilots who were captured during the Vietnam war and fortunate enough to be so well treated in this facility (somehow I became a little sceptical about the wonderful experience this site afforded them?). There is death row including the working guillotine - this was truly horrific in every aspect. It was good to see the Vietnamese women recognized as equals in this place, as they too were imprisoned here. It is worth the two hour visit!

The explanation in English was very helpful. The location is easy to find. It took us 15 minutes to walk from Old Quarter. The videos were helpful in understanding the past. The map is on the wall at the entrance so you can take a picture though it is not necessary. The route is well designed and sign post to lead you walk from one exhibition hall to another. They provide drinking water too.

It's a little interesting to see some shackles and prison cells. But not a must-see. The propaganda, about the brave communists suffering under the evil French, and then about the compassionate communists treating Americans like guests, was so ridiculous that it's hard to know what if anything is true here. If it makes people more sympathetic to the plight of people in prisons, then it might be worth a gander, but I doubt it.

This museum had a lot of information that was fascinating when looked at through a discerning lens. The section on Americans in the prison acted as though life was perfect, and even showed images of McCain visiting with a cheerful caption. Here is an excerpt from POW Sam Johnson, who is a Congressman now "During a routine torture session with the hook, the Vietnamese tied a prisoner’s hands and feet, then bound his hands to his ankles—sometimes behind the back, sometimes in front. The ropes were tightened to the point that you couldn’t breathe. Then, bowed or bent in half, the prisoner was hoisted up onto the hook to hang by ropes. Guards would return at intervals to tighten them until all feeling was gone, and the prisoner’s limbs turned purple and swelled to twice their normal size. This would go on for hours, sometimes even days on end. Aside from leg irons and leg stocks—both of which were used on me for months and years on end—the meat hook was a favorite instrument of torture at the Hanoi Hilton." Take everything you read with the understanding it is propaganda and take it as a testament to the power of history.
Reasonably entertaining if one can say that about such a place. Nicely set up and worth a visit. Easy to find and navigate.

Good way to spend half an hour but not amazing. Cheap entry so may as well take a look if you're nearby. Much of it has been demolished to make way for new buildings but what's left is still in original state.

Read up on Vietnamese history with the French and the US war before you go if you want to get the most out of the prison.  It is mostly displays now with a few remaining cells because most of it is a new building, but if you keep an open kind worth seeing how the Vietnamese feel about overcoming French colonialism and US presence in the South.
Very interesting for the war history of Vietnam! Even for our teenagers. Especially since we are also going there in Ho Chi Minh City for the contrast.

Little remains of the Central Prison. The descriptions and descriptions are rather sparse. A shame, because a great deal happened there. One more point for the authors of the Rough Guide: the tree so beloved by the inmates is a Badamier, not an Almond tree...

An interesting place to visit with some great information on the horrors of the prison but very slanted on the Vietnamese suffering and nothing about what they did to the French or the Americans to balance it

It was built by the French colonialists in 1896 for the purpose of imprisoning anti french elements in Vietnam.After independence, most of it was demolished to make way for the commerical Hanoi Towers. Just a small part of it is left as an exhibit.

Interesting but limited information about what you were looking at. Old style museum for what is a great story. Worth going to if you are hitting other museums or places in the area, but not somewhere to trek to.

This museum aims to highlight that the French held their political prisoners in inhumane conditions, but that this did not prevent them from continuing their activism. In contrast, American prisoners were reportedly treated very well. Otherwise, the buildings are interesting.

The communist  propaganda is alive and well in this place where so many prisoners were held, esp American pilots.  John McCain was held here for 7 years and his version of how things were here vs what you read and see in pictures at this place are no where close to each other .  The western visitor needs to understand and know this ahead of the visit and just take in that perspective.  I would recommend the visit To see a part of recent history from the Vietnam war

It could be so much better. More telling of the history would be good. It just kind of feels like a government propaganda machine created it. Like, oooh look how dreadful the French colonists were when they used it as a prison, but then look how well we treated the American soldiers when we used it as a prison. I wanted to really understand what happened but I didn't find answers here.
Has an interesting story, and a deep history, but I suppose I found it 3/5 on the intrigue scale. Some pretty rough stuff went down at this place!
As the title says - a prison. Good exhibitions, and fascinating pictures. Not a place you have to visit, rather spend your time on all the wonderful food!

If you have ever seen a guillotine here is one. And it will make you wonder about the history of the french invention and invasion - and then there was the americans who called this place Hanoi Hilton at times.

Nice place. You will get to see the history of the prison, the way they showcasing the history with the realistic statues will make you feel standing there back then. Well preserved and details written everywhere. Even without the audio guide also you will understand everything.
Interesting place, but at the time of our visit the main exhibitions were closed. so we saw more than a few photos. But definitely a good visit, it is Vietnamese history worth visiting.

Stepping into Hoa Lo Prison was like walking back in time. The dark cells, heavy doors, and detailed exhibits created a somber atmosphere that really conveyed the weight of its history. The information throughout was clear and engaging, …
You can buy tickets at the main gate. You can choose with audio guide or without. We opt for no audio guide. The exhibition has english description anyways. A good 30 min to an hour is enough. …
Undergoing a lot of renovations currently so many areas can't be seen. Can get crowded when the massive tour groups come through. But overall a nice,  unadulterated museum.

The ticketing staff were upfront and informed me that about 40% of the prison was closed for renovation and replaced with exhibits, leaving the decision to visit up to me. Unfortunately, there was no crowd control, so it became extremely …

We were told not all the museum was open as restoration work taking place - should be finished May 2026. It was very busy and difficult to get down to the cells. However, useful information about the prison.

A historic prison in Hanoi, Vietnam. The informations are very informative and the audio guide is very helpful. There are more tourists during our visit, and its kind a crowded that time. There is a souvenir shop inside too.

Great displays, factual historic exhibits, artifacts, movies on screens, lovely memorial, and there is areas closed at the moment but we were told at the start 30% unavailable to view. Still a great museum with wonderful staff.
Entrance 50, audio guide 100 Very very dark stories. Why only 4 star review? …
PLEASE BE AWARE 40% OF MUSEUM CLOSED UNTIL APRIL 2026. Despite this ticket price is still full price... However the museum is still amazing and interesting. Strongly recommend the …
The infamous "Hanoi Hilton". A grim piece of Vietnamese history. A place to say you've visited but to be honest, I couldn't spend too long there, it was very depressing.

Highly recommend coming here if you are at all interested in war/criminal history. We went around 1 pm on a Friday and it was pretty busy. I saw a few English speaking visitors being disrespectful which was pretty upsetting. There is a sign outside of the building stating some of the exhibits are closed so be aware of that.

Even with ongoing restoration, Hoa Lò remains a "must-visit" in Hanoi. The use of infographics ensures that the educational value is not lost, turning a potentially disappointing "construction zone" into an insightful deep dive into the …
An important part of Vietnam’s history 🇻🇳 The museum offers a good overview, and about half an hour is enough for a short visit. The entrance fee is 50,000 VND. …
Very good tour as it takes you into life inside the prison and shows you conditions during that period. …
An eye opening experience and a sad reminder of what we can do to each other. Exhibition was smaller than we expected but the cover charge was also less in comparison with other such exhibits.
Fascinating museum explaining the history.  It's 50,000 D for entry. The exhibition gives you a hint of what life may have been like The memorial garden I found emotional
Very centrally located, easy to use museum audio guide, but museum is reduced as 40-50% is being renovated.

We used the audio guide (extra but cheap) we thought it was worth it and gave way more context/ info. There was quite a few exhibit pieces not there when we went (as they are being restored) not sure how long they won’t be there for
Nice place to learn about Vietnam's history. We visited just in time as the country celebrated its 80th independence day. Although humid and smell inside not that well maintained
Nice experience. Part of the museum was under construction so part of the last displays were a bit cramped. The audio aid is a must to get the full experience.
Hoa Lo Prison is a deeply moving and educational site, offering a sobering look into Vietnam’s complex history. …

If you’re looking for something to do that involves history, I’d recommend coming here. I chose to walk through and read everything myself but I think for the full immersive experience, you should get one of those audio devices (though, it …
Visiting Hỏa Lò Prison, often called the “Hanoi Hilton,” is a deeply moving experience. The museum offers a raw and honest look into Vietnam’s turbulent past. …

Walking into Hoa Lo prison, you can almost feel the ghosts of despair walking alongside you. Unfortunately, when I visited 50% of the prison was closed for renovations. It cost 50,000 dong for the ticket and another 100,000, dong for the …
Hoa Lo Prison – A Place of Profound Historical Significance  Hoa Lo Prison, often referred to as the “Hanoi Hilton” during the Vietnam War, …
Can know more about the history of Vietnam. But it is under renovation and only 60% of the museum is opened
50k ticket Good but now close for male prison and escape way

Great museum, quite heavy at parts though. The exhibition is set up nicely, taking you first through some of the old prison blocks, after to a memorial and finalices at a section with a more overarching story of Vietnam’s political state. Would highly recommend if you’re interested in the political dynamics that unfolded here.

The ticket for children under 16 is free. They have audio device to hear the story of the prison. It is quite well managed with lots of displays and pictures. The path to follow inside the prison is clear and easy. They have souvenir shops inside too
Under construction so not all areas are accessible. They will tell you when entering. Still worth while
The place is historic and worth to go, but you wont understand the exhibits unless you pay for the audio guide (not expensive though). It is a nice place to spend an hour or two.
A journey through the French history of the prison. The American history (which I'm sure most people are here to see) is captured by two small hallways of photos based around the bombing of Hanoi.
It could get crowded but having a booked tour makes the whole thing faster. Inside, it is amazing how they were able to preserve a rich part of their history. …

I visited this museum during my Hanoi visit. This museum used to be an actual prison. It now showcases the hardships Vietnamese freedom fighters had to endure when they were put in prison. If someone is a history buff, they should definitely check it out.
This place is definitely worth visiting. It’s always good to see some of the history of places we visit. Some exhibits could be a little disturbing but overall, it’s a must see venue. …
If you have nothing to do can go visit. paid total of 100,000 for 2pax

Learn about the history of imprisonment of resistance during the French colonial period
Interesting, but not much explanation, so I recommend buying the audio guide. It was cool, which helped with the heat this time of year.

Very informative and well worth a visit. The history of the prison is particularly interesting. There's also a section on the Vietnam War. The explanations regarding the latter may be somewhat one-sided, so be aware of that.
If you haven't been here before, you should check it out. I spent almost 90 minutes seriously exploring the place and even rented a pretty expensive translation device.

I took the audio guide; I'm not sure if it was worth the cost, as the information panels seemed quite extensive, but it was very good for setting the mood with the background music. The tour is a good length, although the rooms are rather …
Very nice tour! A little short but really enjoyable. 50,000 VND/person.  Note: the audio guide costs 100,000 VND/person, even though the signs are in French.

Interesting, but you'll need to get the audio guide because there aren't many detailed descriptions. It's a shame that it goes from the French period to the American War and then back to the French period.

The museum, housed in a former prison, is located within walking distance of the Old Town. Admission is 50,000 DNG. Several interesting rooms are spread over two floors, and an audio guide is available. I enjoyed the engaging presentation on the Vietnam War with the United States.

The Prison Museum in Hanoi offers inexpensive admission, and children under 15 are free. The museum offers numerous historical stories, guidebooks, and audio guides. Numerous ancient relics and a glimpse into actual prison conditions are available.
This is a museum dedicated to the Vietnam War. There aren't any special facilities or attractions, so it's pretty average. It's easily accessible from the downtown area, so it's worth a stop. …
It was a good visit, although there were too many people and large groups that sometimes made it difficult to move around, but otherwise it's worth stopping by if you have the time. Entry costs 50k.
Worth seeing.  I took the audio guide in French. The commentary is rather biased, but who could …

This is a prison (actually a detention center) built during the French colonial rule of Vietnam. It's a must-see facility for learning about Vietnamese history. The admission fee was 30,000 VND for adults.
Definitely worth a visit, this prison has a lot of history behind it.

Anyone with any knowledge of what is understandably referred to here as the "American War" will have heard of the infamous "Hanoi Hilton", but it seems somewhat strange and a surprise that in the middle of this bustling, modern city it is still here, albeit as a museum. However, if you are in Hanoi, you really should come here and even if you don't necessarily agree with the historical narrative put forward, be grateful that you can now visit this beautiful country and wonderful people.

Visiting Hoa Lo Prison is a deeply poignant experience. We plunge into the dark hours of colonial history and war, with a staging that makes the atmosphere particularly heavy. Despite the gravity of the place, access remains very affordable with a low entry fee, making it an essential and accessible step to understand Vietnam's past. A necessary place of memory that marks the mind long after the release.

It was very busy when we visited which is a pity but I think we saw most things.We learnt a lot about the welfare of the prisoners although the section that dealt with the American POW made it sound like a holiday camp so I don’t think this was a true reflection of their imprisonment.  Senator John McCain spent some time here.Overall it is a sobering experience because being held there must have been a living hell.

Hoa Lo Prison is a must-visit when in Hanoi. The site recounts several harrowing episodes in Vietnamese history, from the French colonial period to the Vietnam War.The exhibition is clear and thoughtfully organized, making it easy to grasp the significance of what you are seeing. The museum effectively recreates the atmosphere experienced by prisoners, notably through the use of life-sized figures placed in reconstructed detention areas. The artifacts themselves are presented with restraint and honesty — few words are needed to convey the reality of their use.The captions accompanying the photographs are equally informative and provide helpful context throughout the visit.That said, I would approach the section on American imprisonment with some caution. Looking more closely into the background of certain prisoners suggests that parts of the narrative may reflect a degree of bias.Even so, this remains an essential visit for anyone wishing to better understand Vietnam’s complex history.

Only a section of the large, notorious prison remains, but it packs a lot of information though signs and exhibits about the captured and tortured political prisoners, as well as the physical conditions.  An important, historical place to visit and a good place to learn about the effect of French colonialism.

Definitely worth a visit, especially to learn about the French colonists and what they did to political prisoners. When we visited it was undergoing renovations, but a staff member informed us of this before we purchased the tickets, so we could decide whether to go in or not. We didn’t do an audio tour, so I feel we missed out on a lot of information.
Admission 50,000 dong, audio guide 100,000 dong.According to our knowledge, representations are somewhat one-sided perhaps precisely for this reason a good experience.
Insightful Vietnam War history site—partly under renovation (disclosed in advance). Audio guide recommended; English labels are available, but crowds can obstruct views.

One of the safest prisons in Asia from 1893 to 1953 during the French colonial period.Today's museum tells harsh stories about how prisoners were treated with torture and neckline in the guillotine.During the Vietnam War, defeated American pilots were placed here, and they named the Hanoi Hilton prison.Although it was one of the safest prisons, there are stories of fatal escape attempts.A symbol of how humanity has treated each other, even in our time count.

Fascinating, though a significant section was closed for renovations—and they somewhat downplayed the harsh treatment of American POWs. You’ll get a vivid glimpse into the prison’s history—the lifelike statues bring the past to life so convincingly, it almost feels like you’re standing there in that era. The site is exceptionally well-preserved, with informative details displayed throughout.
Very clean and organised. Sequentially good with the narrative. Very helpful staff. Would be amazing if there were an app/digital guide to use. But overall very good!
It was interesting - but I fear we missed a lot by not spending the extra money on the audio tour. I found the captions to the photos superficial.

We were charged twice. Once for entry another for headphones. No refund on headphones. Some sections are small so you to wait for entry. Very educational, about the local political prisoners. Once again show the resilience of the local people through historical hardships.

Former prison with personalized status and photos of former prisoners documentary about political prisoners who suffered terrible living conditions photos of Vietnamese revolutionaries in hoa lu prison
This was very educational and visceral reminder of war. The audio in English was exceptional. Seeing an actual guillotine and pictures of victims is confronting. Would recommend to all.

Interesting museum and worth a visit when in Hanoi. Highly recommend you purchase an audio guide to understand what you are seeing. There are in depth stories around the whole museum. Some of the text is extremely small and even more difficult to read when there are larger crowds.

We toured the remains of the prison with audio guides that give a lot of context (I strongly advise them). The history of the prison is traced from colonial times to the Vietnam War. However, the influence of pro-Vietnamese propaganda is felt. Prisoners under the colonial regime are shown as examples of bravery locked up for their opinions while the Processing of American pilots has passed under a candy pink filter that totally ignores the war crimes and torture of the time.

Visiting Hỏa Lò Prison is a sobering but very insightful experience. The museum gives a glimpse into Vietnam’s complex history—from colonial resistance against the French to the Vietnam War era when American POWs were held here.Highly recommend taking the audio headset, especially if you’re in a couple or small group. It’s well-organized and adds a lot of valuable context as you move through the exhibits. The narration really helps bring the stories to life and makes the visit much more meaningful.It takes about 1 to 2 hours to go through the museum depending on how much time you spend at each section. Some parts can be quite moving, especially the preserved prison cells and the personal stories.If you’re interested in history and want to understand more about Vietnam’s past, this is definitely worth a visit.

An interesting read when walking through prison. Quite a humbling and impactful time to reflect on the resilience of the human spirit to resist, survive, and the complex nature of human experience due to colonisation, occupation, and wars in general. We were a bit rushed. Lady at the door rude re the ages of our children (one I wouldn’t even call tall) and us needing proof to be carried on us. Other than that, quite enjoyable and some undercover if you get rain. Worth it visit IMO.

This facility was established during French rule to house Vietnamese political prisoners and activists. It was used to house American prisoners of war during the Vietnam War, and was apparently referred to by American soldiers at the time as the Hanoi Hilton. The exhibition is mainly colonial, with instruments used for torture and a guillotine table with a blade attached, and there is a rather heavy atmosphere. It is impressive that the air conditioning crisis is bad for a lot of tourists, and it is so hot and humid that you might think it is a production.

(Cash only) Ticket: 50,000 VND; Guided tour: 100,000 VNDIt takes about two hours to complete the tour with a guide, and there are chairs along the way to sit and listenYou can better understand the history of Huolu Prison through pictures and sounds, recommended👍It's very crowded on Monday afternoon, but you can see the exhibits by waiting for each otherIncredibly informative and interesting exhibition of the sufferings endured by the Vietnamese people. I wish there is more staff around as some visitors were being insensitive and disrespectful.
Relatively small but interesting museum, providing an insight primarily into the French Colonization period but also Vietnam War. Disturbing and moving, well worth a detour and visit

Interesting museum, well done, informative, friendly, audio guide Charging stations...Covers colonial times and American War.The American War is probably not entirely objective. Definitely worth seeing

Ashamed to say I don't really know the history of Vietnam, and I thought this prison was only for American Pilots from the Vietnam war.It was actually built by the French, an interesting piece of history and well set out museum. It was the entrance fee, plus an additional fee of the headphones.It will take you approximately 90 minutes with the headphones on, to get round.

Most western visitors will gravitate here due to its place in fairly recent history. Overall it is very well done, giving a grim sense of the experience of the inmates. History is written by the victors, and I suspect this is the case with a some of the description of the treatment of the US pilots. It was not a hotel.Everything is in immaculate condition and treated with reverence by the staff and visitors. Even without the audio guide there was plenty of information available. You will spend at least an hour here and possibly twice that.

Another opportunity to see a different view of Vietnams colonial and wartime history.  Compare how the French treated prisoners to how the Vietnamese treats POWs.  Well worth a visit but take your time, get an audio guide and expect lots of people
Decent and cheap. The hard set was cheap and easy and explained things well. Not heaps to see. Pretty basic but some interesting.

Came across the prison by chance whilst walking around the old and french quarters. There are plaques around the prison in English and French that help guide you around the prison.It would appear that it was an exceedingly tough life / death if for detainees.US pilots were apparently treated quite well whilst they stayed at the Hanoi Hilton.I'd definitely suggest visiting.
It is worthwhile to see this prison that American POWs had to endures as well as Vietnamese political prisoners of the French.

Hoa Lo prison is within walking distance of the old quarter. It’s really done well and gives a good sense of what life must have been like for the prisoners. The audio guide costs about double what the entry fee is (and is on top of entry).  I think it would be worth it if you are interested in much more detailed history. The exhibits do have short explanations in Vietnamese and English and actually that would have been enough for me as I found the audio guide didn’t move at my pace and really detracted from me being present in the moment but I think this is just down to how we individually interact with these things.

This was an interesting, pro-Vietnamese/pro-Communist story of Hoa Lo prison. Most of the original building has been destroyed, but you can still walk through parts of the original prison. The audio guide was well worth it. I spent an hour and a half there and it was the perfect amount of time.

One of the key places to visit, there's much to see about the (very bad) treatment of political prisoners by the French. Not so much about how the American POWs were treated. Lots of historical displays and information -- again, mostly on Vietnamese prisoners. Not a big place. A visit takes an hour.
Very interesting to walk around. I would recommend the ear phone information pack for a little extra cost, you get so much more interesting facts about the prison. Well worth a visit.
We took our kids around - and they enjoyed it as much as we did - well worth a visit if you find yourself in this part of the city and don't have other plans

A must to see the prison where US pilots were held in very difficult circumstances. If the place is busy the corridors can be difficult as the in is also the out route.A courtyard with memorial is a relief to get to after the cramped conditions
The visit is recommended. The whole prison story. From when it was founded to when a group of prisoners escaped.I recommend taking the audio guide to understand each room

We visited mid-morning to avoid the tour crowds, and I would suggest the earlier the better, so as to experience the displays with the level of solitude it requires.I was astounded by the number of people who wouldn't pay a paltry fee for the self guided audio headset as it added so much clarity to the experience.It was shocking to see how a supposedly civilised country such as France treated their fellow humans, but then again, how many empires behaved in such a manor ? and how many countries still are today ?The displays were well laid out and numbered for ease of understanding with the audio  headset.I realise that this is Vietnam's story to tell, however, I thought it was a little cheapened by the constant maintenance of the party line through the French area when human emotion must have been to the fore of the poor Vietnamese, and the assertion that, in general, the American POWs were treated with such gentleness and compassion during that conflict. Obviously, the pictures and displays are authentic, however, I imagine selective portrayal was extensively used.I thought the narrative was weigted unnecessarily heavily in one direction, as what I assume would be a more accurate portrayal, would certainly be understandable to most people today.For me, both the story and site were lessened a little by such.I'm glad I went and would  recommend a visit to anyone visiting Ha Noi.Also, maybe it's just me, but I noticed a few people openly sobbing ??? Whilst its eye opening for sure, I certainly didn't achieve such emotion.

A little out of the way but if you have time and are into history I would put this on the to do list. Entrance fee is around $4 AUD and the audio guide is another $4 per person. We had read the audio guide was worth while but didn’t really want to spend the extra so just got one guide and shared a pair of headphones! Worked out fine - the museum is quite busy but still enjoyable and not too crowded. You could still enjoy the museum without the audio guide but the guide did provide access to some great stories throughout. If I were to go again I would still only purchase the 1 guide but I would only bother listening to the audio attached to the personal story pictures these are easily earmarked and I would also listen to the guide around all the torture devices. Some of the information about the treatment of American soldiers was definitely brushed over/ selectively included - as an Australian teacher who has taught Year 10 history the perspective was interesting however, selective.Would recommend going especially if you like prisons or war!
Very informative, though suspect based on the info provided around the American POWs treatment that it’s very one sided. That said, the French treatment of the Vietnamese people was horrible.

We paid for an audio guide and, although pricey relative to the entrance ticket, it was easy to use with clear language, and provided lots of extra detail that wasn’t available within the museum. Of particular interest were the personal accounts portrayed. The museum was very interesting, detailing numerous atrocities that were bestowed upon political prisoners that ended up here. As mentioned before, the American POW section did present a very positive perspective of how they were treated. Was this the case? Maybe? One thing I loved about this museum was the numerous benches provided to sit on, everywhere! Very nice to be able to sit and listen to the audio guide in peace as people filtered past.
This prison is near to Hanoi towers which is actually built on some the site as well. Get the audio guide to listen to the stories to get a look of it from colonial times to the war times.

Not the most cheerful tourist spot, but we felt it was important to see. The exhibits about Vietnamese prisoners were extensive, while the American POW section seemed selective in its narrative. The guillotine display was particularly chilling. Informative but heavy experience.
Great visit, all very interesting.I think the price of the audio guide is not justified (it tells nothing more than information signs).

Modern bright building (nice) on the outside. In the inside, it’s gloomy and filled with dark glimpse of Vietnamese history. Great place to learn a little bit about Vietnamese history, Indowar and American pilots who were prisoned there
It is smaller then it was on my last visit as most of land sold to developers But still a good visit Good to see the Hanoi Hilton and the good french construction
Very interesting stories and descriptions of the treatment of prisoners, war captives and political prisoners.Well worth the visit.
This museum, in Hanoi city, is definitely worth a visit.  It gives some insight into the history of Vietnam during the French colonial days.

We are on a 23 day OAT tour of Vietnam.The prison is only one of three location in Hanoi worth visiting.The prison dates to 1896 when it was used to hold and torture anyone (local Vietnamese) who opposed the French government (Back then the French held power in Vietnam as THEIR colonial interest).Most of the prison was demolished in 1990 to make room for a high rise (Hanoi Towers). Asmall corner of the original prison still exists dedicated to the history of the prison’s past.There was lots of propaganda media, but mainly about how the French used the prison in the firsthalf of the 1900s as a place of incarceration and torture for anyone that opposed the Frenchgoverning. Why there is even a Guillotine on display and pictures of severed heads.Only as a side feature is there information about the 600 American prisoners of war that wereheld there (some over 9 years), during the Vietnam / American War.A dreadful place and we only went there because we heard so much about it during the war.
Interesting visit from the point of view of history. The living conditions imposed by the French were poor and the interiors prove this fact

Honestly, I can only think of war and colonial rule as something that happened somewhere unrelated to me, but that is why I thought it was so meaningful to be exposed to the reality of our institution.Listening to the audio Guide's explanations of living in poor conditions, torture and executions there, inside the actual facility, makes you think about how much sacrifice was made and how far away it was to get a peaceful life today.It was as if the monuments and the trees that had been there since then were telling us not only about the cruelty and the misery, but that everything was connected to the peace that we have today and we must continue to do so.
Former Hoa Lo Prison turned museum and nicknamed "Hanoi Hilton" by American POW's. I recommend this museum to everyone who wants to learn from history so the horrors of the past may not be repeated.

I went here on a full day city tour.  I spent about an hour inside.  Interesting to see how the French treated the prisoners.  By contrast the Vietnamese were quite accommodating to the Americans in the 60s and 70s.

This little museum has a lot packed into it. There are plenty of displays which are well labelled. It's quite popular and there were a lot of people crammed in. There are also the courtyard, guillotine and cells to explore. Definitely a visit as it displays early Hanoi history under the French rule.

I visited this museum during my Hanoi visit. This museum used to be an actual prison. It now showcases the hardships Vietnamese freedom fighters had to endure when they were put in prison. If someone is a history buff, they should definitely check it out.

We went to see Hoa Lo prison with very limited knowledge around the Vietnam war. We found the beginning parts very interesting and thought there was enough information on the placards (as we opted not to go for a guided audio tour). The walkthrough offers a lot to look at and read, and depicts what it was probably like to have been a prisoner of war in those times.

Maison Centrale was a horrific prison, and it’s worth a visit (but the communist propaganda should be taken with a grain of salt). It was opened by the French during colonial times to imprison and punish those who rose up against the French regime. Inmates had their legs bolted to wooden beds, where they could not move position, or even move to use the toilet. Mothers along with their children were also imprisoned here. It was a brutal place. The propaganda, however, is outrageous. Yes, after the French pulled out of Vietnam, the Americans believed that supporting the South Vietnamese in their fight for a democratic republic against the Communist North Vietnamese was the right thing to do. Yes, it all went pear-shaped, and in retrospect, perhaps the US shouldn’t have gotten involved and instead allowed the South to be taken over by the North (which is what happened at the end of the war anyway). The museum purports that the American prisoners of war were basically in a good-time summer camp, complete with a full medical team and a Christmas feast. The reality, however, is that when Jeremiah Denton was forced to make a propaganda video, he secretly blinked “TORTURE” in Morse Code. Inmates were not allowed to speak with each other, so they used a tapping code to communicate. The museum message is that that John McCain was treated well. However, he was in solitary confinement for 2 of his 5.5 years here, lost 50 pounds in weight, his hair went completely grey very quickly (likely due to his trauma), was tortured, was forced to make false confessions, and was denied proper medical treatment and nutrition. He was never again able to raise his hands above shoulder height, and Hillary Clinton from the opposition once famously helped him fix his hair before an appearance because he was unable to reach it himself. All in all, it’s a sobering visit. It was an inhumane place for any poor soul to spend their days. This is true for both those imprisoned by the French colonialists and those imprisoned by the North Vietnamese, despite the museum’s message that it was fun for the US POWs.

Visiting Hoa Lo Prison in Hanoi is a profoundly moving experience. Walking through the grim, high-walled corridors and peering into the small, stark cells gives a tangible sense of the harsh conditions faced by prisoners. The exhibits, filled with personal stories and historical artifacts, evoke a deep empathy and connection to those who suffered there. The somber atmosphere and the historical weight of the site leave a lasting impression, making the visit both unsettling and enlightening.
While only a fraction of the original structure remains, the section open to visitors provides a stark view of justice under French colonial and Vietnamese rule.
A very informative tour on the difficult history of Vietnam. Makes it possible to understand the conditions of detention during and after French colonization

Remarkable experience, very hot and sweaty, but exhibits are informative in English, French and Vietnamese. We used an audio tour guide, but everything they say in the audio guide, that you pay 100k VND for, is on the signs infront of the exhibits

Really interesting museum! They give you headphones to walk around and listen to, however they are quite time consuming and can get a little boring. The experience was really cool and I learned a lot whilst being here!
I am speechless after having the opportunity to visit this place and witness firsthand a small part of Vietnam's dark history. I highly recommend everyone to come here.

Impressive to see what took place in Hanoi. It is not expensive 50,000 per person and children under 16 are allowed free of charge on presentation of ID. There are audio recordings in different languages that take you on a journey through the prison.  Definitely worth a look.
Went as a family. Bigger than expected and worth the visit. 50000 dong per person entrance fees.  They did not charge my sonwho is 6yrs old.

There is a lot you can learn about the treatment of Vietnamese political prisoners during the French Colonization. You can also rent an audio device that I believe explains the details. However I didn’t rent the audio device. They are numbers mentioned on each section and I believe you can play the audio according to the number. There are lot of fans and coolers placed throughout the area but it was still a bit hot for me. There are a couple of souvenir shops inside the prison as well.

The Hoa Lo prison was an eye opener for the treatment of criminals and political prisoners during the French colonisation of Vietnam .  This is an era of which I knew very little, except that France was going to great lengths to suppress a population deserving of independence.  It’s not an uncommon story of western colonisation/suppression in general.  The museum is well done - I highly recommend the audio guide; a bargain at 100 dong; and if the topic interests you, you’ll find the audio compelling, especially the inmates stories.  The ‘comradeship’ is pretty central to each display.  It’s ironic when you consider how horrendous the French treatment of the Vietnamese was that they should ignore the true conditions in which American POWs were held.  Make no mistake, the US involvement in the Vietnam War was a dark chapter in its history and the destruction it ‘rained’ down upon  the Vietnamese people in chemicals and weaponry was horrific.  But to suggest POWs were treated humanely with respect, allowing them to mingle together and enjoy holiday dinners isn’t even close to accurate.  It’s a shame as this  inaccuracy undermines the truth of all the stories. Still, it’s well worth a visit.

Prison built by the French to detain Vietnamese political prisoners, then used to detain American soldiers who sarcastically named it “Hanoi Hilton”: the American prisoners were subjected to terrible torture that the Vietnamese government still denies today. During the visit, in fact, it was clear that they wanted to make it appear that the American "guests" were treated in a more than humane way.Explanatory signs in English and French.Interesting visit.

I visited this place because it was recommended in the guidebook. The exhibits are so painful and shocking that you can't help but look away when you enter. Facilities like this should be preserved for future generations. Many visitors stopped to look at the exhibits. They seem to have earphones for foreign languages, so why not use them? As an aside, I was surprised to see that the Ho Chi Minh sandals I had been looking for were on sale at the shop, and although they were the regular price, they were cheaper than I expected, so I bought them. Also, while there are many tourists from Western countries and Asia, such as France and the United States, in Vietnam, I saw many young Japanese tourists here.I went there as a set with a famous and popular Asian restaurant and chocolate shop nearby.

I visted the Hoa Lo Prison. First, the good. I learned so much about the French occupation and the following repression of the Vietnamese Rebles. Now the bad. The American Vietnam War section was not balanced. I would say the museum did not address the harsh treatment of American POWs, but nor did I expect it to shine a light on that dark history.
This is a must visit place for history aficionados. Also known as the Hanoi Hilton by the American prisoners' of war captured by the NVA.

This museum gives an inside view of the life of the prisioners - where they lived, how they were treated etc. It has the orginal prision gate  & the guillotene on display. It has nice pictures & other items thru which visitors can experience the prision life.NIce museum worth visiting once.

Interesting site to visit.  While there is a fair amount of info on the Vietnam (American) War, the education is rightly focused on the entire history of the prison, built by the French, which includes significant mistreatment of Vietnamese political prisoners by the French prior to liberation from being part of French Indochina. Other reviewers comment on the propaganda around how well cared for the American POWs were while in custody.  It’s true that it is definitely prominently displayed and is hard to read; however. It should be kept in mind that this is told from the Vietnam government’s point of view.  The information is highly patriotic in nature as a site like this in any country would be.  As an American, if you don’t remember that cultural context, you will be irritated.  You are in another country after all, and one that has significant restrictions on free speech and free press to boot.One thing is clear, anyone who spent time as a prisoner here over the approximately 100 year history of the prison lived a nightmare in awful physical and  psychological conditions.

We came to the "Hanoi Hilton" on a drizzly afternoon. The audio guide is well worth doing to provide some insights as you tour around. Some of what occurred during the French occupation was sadistic, the models and exhibits help you to imagine the conditions.  Of particular interest to me was the prison use during the Vietnam/American War, probably as it is recent history within my lifetime, as expected there is a good section dedicated to this and it was interesting to hear how well American prisoners of war were treated, so much so I wonder if they were sad to return to the US (sarcasm). This said it's worth a visit and easy enough to get to and from using Grab.

The historical dates. timelines and general accounts are valuable, especially since most people under age 60 don’t really know much about the Vietnam wars. I agree with so many previous reviews about making the treatment of the Americans seem like a resort and not a “hell on Earth” as it really was. Leave a couple of hours to go through it thoroughly.Howard S.

Super cool to see and don't miss but the propaganda was a joke. Showed two chunks of history... when the French imprisoned the Vietnamese resistance and when the Vietnamese imprisoned American POWs. Showed the French as ruthless torturers but presented the way the Americans were treated like they were on a vacation. Sitting in the cell was chilling.

Outstanding museum. Very highly recommended. The torture of Vietnamese by the French appears well documented.  However, the treatment of USA pilot POWs is not presented accurately. They were tortured and treated quite badly. It is a shame that the Vietnamese government leadership can't admit the truth about the treatment of the American POWs.
Good for someone that really love to study about Vietnam history. Need to pay more if you want to hear audio for explanation. Or else a little bit less information from the poster. Somehow creepy.

Audio tour (100,000 vnd) was well worth it. Some sections of the exhibition were outside and it was raining when we went (though there were umbrellas provided) so byo jumper! Took about 2hrs to listen to all 30 audio chapters.

It was very crowded when I was there. War sites always leave one heavy-hearted but this was an eye-opening walk if you are a war buff. There are audio-guides so you can DIY and learn about the oppression faced by the Vietnamese on your own.

Very interesting visit giving insights to the French colonial period and of course the Vietnam war. Well worth s couple of hours. Every story has two sides - interesting to see the Vietnamese view of the imprisonment of Vietnamese during the 1930s as well as their view of the US inmates during the 1960s/1970s - suspect that the museum authorities might have a generous view of how the US servicemen were treated.
Very much worth the visit here. Used for political prisoners during the french occupation, and later for american prisoners during the war.
Very cost effective museum tour.  Good to see inside the cell blocks, experience the conditions and be educated about recent history

Very interesting place. We paid the 50,000vnd for the audio guide and found it very helpful. Signs do have English translations but I think without the audio guide I would have been a bit confused so definitely worth hiring.

Interesting to see. Partly scary, for example the dead cells. As long as you are aware that the story is quite lopsided (French who condemned Vietnamese = evil. Vietnamese who held American prisoners of war = kind) our family appreciated the museum.

A prison is rarely a positive experience and even here you get to experience an extremely frightening handling of convicted persons. At the same time, it is useful to see how it can be as there are still today prisons with equivalent standards and management.The conditions are well described but also show the prisoners' creativity in order to have a slightly easier life and also continue to communicate internally and externally.To get the most out of the visit, you should hire an audio guide.The prison is a gruesome monument well worth a visit.
A very interesting look at history and the difference between how the Vietnamese prisoners were treated compared to the American. Worth getting the audio guide!

For anyone with even a passing interest in history. Sad and sombre, it's an important site to visit, to truly understand what the Vietnamese were fighting, to see France's near 70-year 'colonialisation' legacy and what a western country was willing to do, even up to the 1950s. The brutality, even against teenage resistance fighters, and the young toddler children of female prisoners, is awful to grasp. It puts some things into perspective.

This museum is worth visiting. It tell you the story of how brutal the French were. It is informative but also sad. Looking at the guillotine in the execution room is very dramatic, especially together with the photos of actual people who has been executed. This is just a reminder how cruel humans can be.

I visited Hoa Lo Prison during the week in the morning when they opened and it was not busy. I say, plan an hour max to visit the prison. There are many interesting exhibits here with information that includes English text. This prison has a lot of history, unfortunately nothing good. Life was rough here, especially during the French occupation.

It was a great experience to see what the poor people of Vietnam went through. When Americans thought they were being helpful. At that time. It gives you a greater understanding and respect for such an amazing culture in Vietnam today. We thank you for your amazing hospitality and your kindness I was enjoying your stay here.

Fascinating prison that shows the struggles the Vietnamese people went thru under various colonial oppressors. Some of the exhibits (the ones about American POWS) are laughable propaganda though and need to be taken with a grain of salt. Still well worth a visit.

My boyfriend and I visited the “Hanoi Hilton” for around an hour during our stay in Hanoi. I really enjoyed our visit as a learned a lot about not only the use of this building in WWII, but also before that when Vietnamese prisoners were held here.  I recommend getting an audio guide, to at least listen to exhibits that interest you, as they provided some great information. Like other reviewers here, the stories about how WWII prisoners were treated is just one side of the story, but still an interesting and educational stop.
This is an old prison built by French while occupying Vietnam. It shows how the locals were kept in confinement and tortured by the French police. There is an audio guide and an entrance ticket.

After reading “things to do” in Hanoi we asked our tour guide to accommodate a stop to the prison. We are so happy we did! Great experience and helps understand part of history. Chilling and en lighting at the same time. Would strongly recommend making time
A well presented prison museum with lots of interesting things to see and read. Some chilling moments as you see the reality of living conditions and learn a little about the history of the place.

My family enjoyed the museum.  You have to look past the nationalistic propaganda (Vietnamese prisoners were tortured and treated horribly but American prisoners were treated really well and played basketball and volleyball all day; not quite how the American POW's describe it).  Worth going for sure, however.

As many have written. This museum is from the Vietnamese perspective. It was very interesting and a sad reminder of the original use of it by the French to imprison the Vietnamese.Before we saw the the 'American' side, our tour guide thankfully warned us, that what we were about to see was prob not an accurate portrayal of how the Americans POW's were treated. I did spend some time after I left the prison reading the stories of those who were held there.

What a place to make you realize how hard was life way back then, how the France colonization pretty much just make Vietnam and its people suffering. Can't say much, as the chill of the prison, the history of the people who trying to protect their country and what was happend with all of that torture just make you in silence, you can not even imagine!

As with every location in Vietnam the story presented is quite one-sided (their home, their right to present their version of the story!) but I thought this was a well laid out museum and larger than I was expecting. Found it interesting and would love to give a massive thank you to the visitor that found my husband’s money belt he had dropped, and handed it into security. We are incredibly grateful and delighted to see there are still lovely people out there!
Good to be reminded of the horrors of war. Very realistic displays and lots of information boards. Bigger than expected.

We read that this was a worthwhile thing to do whilst in Hanoi. It was interesting to visit this place to see what the prison was like before the Vietnam war. It was also harrowing to see how some prisoners were treated and to see what incarceration was like for them. After walking through the prison, there is a final section focused on the Vietnam war and whilst it was interesting, we found it was content heavy with boards and boards of text and it could have been more succinct; especially as this area was outside with no air con.  It was an interesting visit and good to get some history, and it was reasonably priced to get in. Do bring water and a fan if visiting this attraction as it takes a good 1 - 2 hours. You also don’t need the audio guide for your visit as there is plenty of text to provide context.

Definitely get the audio guide, you get more in depth stories of what happens at each place and a better understanding of the horrific conditions the prisoners were in.Whilst the prison was very interesting and it was awful hearing the treatment the prisoners had, please note that the information presented is VERY biased. All of the information is about Vietnam vs the French or the Americans, with very little mentioned about the actual divide between North and South Vietnam.Some of the accounts which were presented regarding how they treated US prisoners was also very selective, showing them in a favourable humane light, but when. you read the actual accounts online it's drastically different.The tour wasn't in chronological order, and went back and forwards in time which made it quite confusing at times.It's still definitely worth a visit though.
Really interesting and well done. Make sure you get the audio tour and headphones for the best experience. Really reasonable entry and audio tour- a must if you’re visiting Hanoi.

Wir waren positiv überrascht vom Museum und haben einiges über die Geschichte Vietnams und des Gefängnisses dazulernen können. Aufgrund der Bewertungen haben wir uns gegen einen Audioguide entschieden. Durch englische Infotafeln konnten wir aber genug verstehen. Zu den ausgestellten Dokumenten (Briefe etc.) gab es jedoch keine Übersetzung

It was one of those things that we felt like we had to do.   Very little information about Vietnam War role during first part of tour.    Mostly information about the prison's French past.    Later, when the Vietnam war is mentioned, it shows American prisoners celebrating Christmas.    Obviously, North Vietnam won the war, and it gets to present the narrative it wants.    But, it does not take much imagination to realize the prison was not a pleasant place.   Less than an hour.   Audio tour is not necessary.

Worth getting the audio guide. Good museum / exhibit and still has ~1/5th of the original buildings. Took around 2 hours. Covers the prison during French colonisation  (Vietnamese prisoners) and during the war (American prisoners); take the latter period with a pinch of salt in terms of the conditions the Americans were imprisoned in…

I had done a bit of research before going here, even reading the book by John Mc Cain.  It was still an interesting visit and whilst not entirely accurate, still had a lot of information and worthy of a visit.  Quite creepy in places and I wouldn't take small children to a place like that.  I liked the motorbike parking valet had towels placed on all the seats of the bikes, so owners wont get burnt butt, thought that was a nice touch.  Was easy to find and you had to pay in VND, didn't seem to accept cards for payment.  We spent an hour walking around and that was enough time.
Centrally located and worth a visit. Some parts of the story certainly missing but interesting all the same. Went with 13 and 10 year olds and they enjoyed it, took around an hour.

Interesting exhibition of prison and its history as there were many different kind of prisoners over the years started with French occupation to Vietnamese war. English audio guide is for extra price, still basic descriptions are in English language for admission price.

Thorough about the horrors of the French occupation and the US bombing.  They describe a bit out of context the horrors of the S VN “tiger cage” system.   However they barely mention any hardships endured by the US aviators.   They say the aviators were provided food that reflected the poverty of the North then but fail to even mention the allegations of torture by the North’s prison guards, which were convincingly made IMOQuite unbalanced IMO.
Decent museum. Can be completed quite quickly. I’d say go because it’s cheap and there’s something to learn for sure. I would’ve given it a 3.5 if that was an option.

An interesting place to visit with lots of history.  It is worth visiting to get more of the history of the country.  It helps you to understand the war time not just the war against the Americans but also the French - who often seem to be forgotten about.  There is a very tasteful memorial on the site.We chose not to have an audio guide and there are English signs and information throughout the museum

What an interesting place. It tells the history of the time of the French occupation and shows how prisoners were treated. It was also used for US POWs during the Vietnam war but their “stay” seems to have been less violent than the Vietnamese people’s “stay” under the French. It is well worth a visit but be prepared for the heat inside the buildings.
Dramatic prison museum dating back to the French occupation. The rooms have been restored to their original appearance with reconstructions. An interesting way to understand Vietnamese history.
Very interesting. Lots of history, including American prisoners here. There's propaganda for the visitor to deal with. Not crowded when I was here.
Good place to visit when you are in Hanoi. Many objects there and a lot of info we get. Just 15 minutes for walking from Hanoi Old Quarter

An informative, interesting place to visit.  We all took away a lot of information about the history of the prison.  Plenty of varied information about the Vietnamese prisoners & then later the US pilot prisoners. The place is well maintained, information in multiple languages.   No need to purchase the audio, everything has written information.   Entry was 30k per adult, well worth it.
Seemed alright, just I hadn’t showered for a few days and a serious bout of itchy balls. Didn’t really get what it was about, any suggestions on how to catch up?

Well preserved and presented, this is an interesting visit.The commentary was clear throughout the exhibits. It was interesting to note that the French tortured and brutalised the Vietnamese nationalist insurgents, but the VC supported their US prisoners by letting them play volleyball and basketball.That aside, this is well worth a visit and why shouldn’t the victors rewrite history? 😂

The museum is very centrally located in Hanoi. The audio guide is recommended, even though it gets a bit long-winded towards the end. The first two parts are very interesting, well-explained, and illustrated. However, the captivity of the American pilots is somewhat romanticized and idealized. The final part is a paean to the Communist Party and the achievements of the imprisoned party members.

This is the so called Hanoi Hilton with interesting history about French colonialism and USA POWs. The entry fee was 30,000ḏ for adults and 15,000ḏ for students. She accepted my Australian student card after a bit of scrutiny!Please don’t worry with the audio guide. They tell you at the entrance that you need it as there is limited information inside, but we found there was more than enough info in English and French to meet the needs of most. The audio guide is 100,000ḏ so I really would not bother.An interesting place to visit and inexpensive without the audio guide.

This is an interesting and heartbreaking place. Used for political prisoners from 1886 to 1990s, this place has stories and artifacts from a dark time within these prison walls. It was worth spending time at.

Enjoyed an hour’s visit here. Exhibition is well presented. I quite liked the way that the French are portrayed as the really bad guys when they ran the prison but when the Americans were held there it was all volleyball, letters from home and medical treatment! Nothing wrong with a bit of patriotism…
The information online still shows a 45-minute evening trip costing 300,000 VND. Please have a staff member check and correct this.

Good place to visit with a real insight to Vietnam history. Good value for money and would recommend the audial tour. It doesn't cost much and worth it. To walk around the museum takes about 2hrs. If in Hanoi -worth a visit.

Definitely a sobering testament to history. Whitewashed by the Vietnamese as these magnanimous victors, completely neglecting the fact that John McCain could never raise his arms high enough to comb his own hair.
Well curated museum. Place-cards on exhibits in English. You don’t have to buy the audio tour if you’re on a budget. The information on the exhibits can suffice. Spend 1-2 hours here.

If you enjoy 20th Century History you will like this museum. Entrance is only 30,000 but the audio guide is 100,000; we found it worth it for the extra information, although we tired a little towards the end.  The exhibits are all very well presented, and you can enter the former prison cells.

Well maintained, informative and best enjoyed with the audio guide. Ticket is 30,000 vnd for the foreigners and audio guide is 100,000 vnd but recommended. Gives a factual overview of the darker colonial past that many Asian countries had to endure and one really feels for the tortured, oppressed political prisoners when you get to exhibit 21 which celebrates  the undaunted vietnamese spirit.

Quite a bizarre yet harrowing experience. The information was limited so I would advise getting the audio guide to give you more insight (we were googling things on our phones). Also, very very biased side of history depicting the American prisoners of war attending a ‘holiday camp’ and saying that they did not want to leave. Altogether, still very interesting.
Very interesting museum documenting Vietnam's difficult but inspiring history. Definitely get the audio guide, I wouldn't have learned much without it. You can easily spend a few hours here.

If you are in Hanoi I think you should try to fit this in. Coming from Australia I was well aware of the brutality of European colonialism in times gone by. I was not aware of the actions of the French in Vietnam such a short time ago.

What initially seems to be an intimate account of the horrors experienced by individuals held captive here unravels into a national history much wider in scope. The freedom fighters and patriots once imprisoned and tortured by the French colonialists would become government and military figures; the very things they stood against were encapsulated within the confines of the prison.

I believe this is an important cultural stop while visiting Hanoi. Although it is difficult to imagine the war years, this building and its history can serve as a helpful reminder of what occurred, who was involved, and other pertinent details.

Even though many parts of this prison are disturbing, Hoa Lo Prison, a.k.a. "Hanoi Hilton", is historically important and a recommended stop on any Hanoi itinerary.  The displays are well done with good English descriptions on labelling.  There is a small section regarding the time frame when it was used to house American POWs, such as John McCain.  There are both interior and exterior displays.

This gives a very good history of Vietnam's fight for independence.  The prison was originally built by the French and used to imprison Vietnamese "freedom fighters".    The first part of the tour focuses on this part of Vietnam's history.

I didn't rent the audio guide and felt the English explanations were sufficient for my understanfing about the dark history. It reminded me of how recent our "civilization" is and there is no lack of evidence of how evil human beings are.
As tourists, I believe its always important to be interested in a country’s history. Hoa Lo Prison was an excellent visit for this. Very extensive and informative visit.

Interesting to see the famous Hanoi Hilton. No need to hire a headset as all the information boards are in English, Vietnamese and French. Lots of other visitors there. Facts are slanted against the French who imprisoned the Vietnamese communists and make the American Vietnamese war pilots look like they had a great time in the prison! 30,000 dong per adult.
At only 30k VND entry (50k VND for the audio guide, which is very good) this is definitely worth a trip to experience some of the horrors faced by Vietnamese prisoners during colonial rule.

Seeing first hand the Hanoi Hilton was very eye opening. You experience the brutal lifestyle of the people serving their time in Hoa Lo Prison makes you realize how much better life in the modern world really is.

A sobering visit to the Hoa Lo Prison is in order when visiting Hanoi. Although the original buildings have long since been demolished, the museum is a harrowing reminder of the atrocities that have taken place here. I would recommend getting an audio guide when visiting.

With the history behind it related to the "American War"...it was a site I wanted to visit. People of a certain age (me) know the story of the "Hanoi Hilton" as it pertained to captured US serviceman and their time spent here. I had no idea about the previous history when it was under French rule. Lots of history. Lots of sadness and pain for so many.
We spent about an hour here which cost 30,000D a person which is about $1.75CAD. More if you want a guide or head set. Everything was labeled so I wouldn’t recommend the headset.

Yes, it's among the obligatory tourist sites in Hanoi, this remnant of the old prison now turned into a museum/propaganda house. We visited partly out of curiosity--to see how things were represented--and partly out of respect for the US prisoners housed there, not least the late Senator and patriot from Arizona. It's an emotional experience, so much suffering, especially under the French; and also the accounts of the American war and its prisoners...very much contested ground, historically. Much food for thought here, but not a fun place.

This prison of horrors was built by the French in 1886. Originally a prison for Vietnamese by the French and then for Americans by the Vietnamese. Many, many American combat soldiers such as John McCain were imprisoned here. Here is where torture was commonplace throughout the history of this Prison.

It's bizarre to read that American prisons of war were apparently kept quite comfortable and treated well... in a way almost offensive but then who's to say what's correct and what's not?! The prison has been well kept and reflected the entire history from the point of view of the Vietnamese. It's quite eye opening and well worth a visit. I'd say an important part of the countries history.I'd highly recommend spending the little extra on an audio guide to enhance the experience and clarify everything you are looking at.

Only one wing of the original prison complex, dating back to the French occupation, remains. An audio guide provides background information on the French period and the Vietnam War of the 1960s and 70s. It offers a unique perspective and should be supplemented with other information, literature, and visits. For history buffs, it's certainly a must-see, despite the somber atmosphere.

Visited Hanoi "Hilton" which was only partially remaining, The visit was eye opening as it showed how the prisoners were kept in cramped quarters. John McCain was also kept as a prisoner here. Worth a visit.

If you don’t get the audio tour your kind of waiting your time. This is an interesting way to spend a half day at most. It explains not just about the prison but will gives you a little insight into what the Vietnamese people have gone through.

According to what I learnt from the exhibition, the prison mostly showcases the French colonization time in Vietnam. It was only at the end of the tour did I know about the US pilots involvement, which was pretty interesting but rather a bit biased and propaganda-like. It was the exhibitions that struck me with the huge amount of information. At the end, the visit is totally worth it. Would definitely recommend it to my friends

Quite interesting. The focus is mainly on what the French did to the locals. It is only after you finish the tour that you get to see the American involvement. I would have like to see how they treated the American POWs.

A very interesting and informative tour giving the history of the prison from the time of French colonialism. Audio tour is full of propaganda and I felt like I was listening to a communist era speech about the evil of the western world and the glory of communism but atrocities were committed by both sides in that prison and the presentation was pretty one sided. It is a reminder of how horrifically man can treat their fellow man and if you can look past the propaganda, it’s a must visit.

In my opinion, there isn't a great deal to see in Hanoi. However, I found the prison very interesting and would recommend it. You should definitely take the tour with an audio guide. It took me two hours. At times, there were too many stories, and it dragged on a bit towards the end. But you can always skip chapters. If I'm not mistaken, the entrance fee was 30,000 VND and the audio guide was 50,000 VND.

Hoa Lo Prison aka  The Hanoi Hilton was started back during French colonial occupation and presents you with a history that is intense and brutal.   For the baby boomer generation this is a part of history that is deeply ingrained in your psyche and when there brings you back to the time when you saw pilots being held and when that wench Hanoi Jane (Jane Fonda) betrayed our American pilots when she handed over the secret notes to the prison guards.    What is important, had but important is to step back and look at this place through the historical lenses  and then you can begin to slowly separate your personal feelings and then understand why the Vietnamese fought like they did to throw off French oppression.    This place was in operation for more than 120 years and it was a place that was feared by everyone.   Today it is museum and it does a very good job of presenting its history and the stories of those people that came through it and the stories of those who didn't.
