If you are going to Hanoi this is a must see destination.  It is somber and sobering but it is history and it gives you some serious insight as to why they fought so hard. Have a good tour.

A glimpse into the horrible brutality of the french on the Vietnamese and later the prison for the US military. It is very well organised with so much information. It shows how long the Vietnam people have been at war!

Hoa Lo prison was built by the French by taking over and displacing an entire village community. Over the years it was the place where Vietnamese freedom fighters were interred in the most inhuman conditions possible. There are lots of stories here; stories of brutality and death; and stories of heroism, like those who escaped through the sewers. The prison was also used to house American PoWs. This prison museum is open till 5 pm. Photography is allowed; however out of respect for those who suffered here I took only 3 - one being the shadow of the guillotine.
I had a look around here and found it vaguely interesting. I didn't bother with the audio tape as I just wanted to read and go at own pace. For 30000 dong it's worth a look
A very good self tour, that shows you the history of the Prison. Recommend a couple of hours to look at all the Prison has to offer.

I wish that we'd had time to go inside as I'd heard good things about it but time didn't allow.We were however able to look around the exterior and I'd recommend the very informative display of the history of Vietnam and Hanoi that lines the walls and is free for all.

Very interesting and moving, particularly of the prisoners held here in such terrible conditions.  Need more than an hour here to take in all the exhibits.  Wish we’d visited separately from our tour to have more time here.

Now a place of peace, this is a useful insight into the political imprisonment in days gone by.Its a shame much of the original building js no longer there. Some of the printed information is a little confusing \ contradictory but I found no one available to discuss this with. All in this was s good experience.

A well presented representation of the history of this prison, built by French to house political prisoners who opposed their occupation.They had actually brought a guillotine with them before the construction of the prison.The treatment of the prisoners was barbaric and continued well after the atrocities of the Second World War that France underwent!Cost 30,000dong (£1) excellent value. There is an audio guide, but if you choose not to use one there is plenty of information in the various stages, these are within the remaining cellsHighly recommended
Impressive museum about this originally French prison. During the Vietnam War, American prisoners of war were interned here.

We hadn't planned on going here, but our 13-year-old daughter chose it to fill an afternoon. She got into the audio tour while the rest of the family took in the atmosphere of the harsh prison and whatever printed English stories were available. Halfway through our 10-year-old was quite upset by the nature of the content. It was one of the most emotionally challenging historic sites I've been to, but reality is like that.
Worth a visit so to remind us how cruel humans can be to each other. The whole tour is bias and a lot of the stated information has to be taken with a pick of salt. Still worth a visit.

When you have heard of a place so often the actual experience of visiting can be a let down, but not here, this was just as sad as we expected. The prison elements made you realise what life and death had been like there, and then the images and statistics and general horror of war and conflict made us sad, and very quiet as we spent a long time taking it all in.The most memorable was actually seeing the messages and coverage from around the world showing how people at the time felt about the Vietnam/American war.Has to be visited, look out also for the stories of the escapes from this Hilton.

A great place to visit to understand how prisoners (both Vietnamese and Americans) were treated during their incarceration at this prison. The propaganda is interesting and pictures are moving. For an entrance fee of 30,000 VDN it’s worth it. You can also hire an audio guide for an extra 50,000 VDN.

Cheap to enter, will not take long, interesting and insightful ! A view to not letting past history repeat its self ever !! Taken by two FREE tour guides, to young students who spoke very good English...100 of them in the city membership.....please look the college scheme up !!
This museum is not too big but its filled with important information and if you love history it's worth a visit.

Important history and interesting information, definitely buy the audio otherwise it will be difficult to get background facts and the history will not be told quite as well. The audio tour is 50,000 dong per person.

It was interesting to see the remaining portion of the prison that the French built to hold Vietnamese political prisoners and then used by the Vietnamese to hold Americans during the war. Harrowing conditions. At the end there is a Garden of Peace that is full of Vietnamese propaganda claiming that all the nations in the world were against "imperialist American aggression" Audio guide is extra cost.
Interesting but biased presentation of the treatment of American prisoners. French treatment if Vietnamese prisoners was the focus however.

We visited this as part of a walking tour with Hanoi Kids. Helped having a guide to explain a little about the place. Having said that lots of signs on the exhibits . It whiles away a couple of hours.

A scary place ( as you'd expect), the broken glass topped walls give an initial taste in what is to come.Intimidating conditions from the French rule through it's time as the Hanoi Hilton.Never seen a guillotine before!

This was worth a visit,  wasn't to expensive.  Wish they had tour guides to explain it more as all of the areas need to be read about.  They did have an audio guide but there was 4 of us & didn't want us all to be walking around with head phones in.  Had it been more expensive I might not have been so impressed but for the price we paid it was interesting. It doesn't take very long to go around,  no more than an hour.  Which is a good time frame with kids.

Another tourist spot with a rich history and a Vietnamese perspective, focusing on the French colonial experience and the war against the United States. It's also close to other places worth visiting, such as the Temple of Literature.

A visit to the ‘Hanoi Hilton’ is almost a must when visiting Hanoi.  Most of the displays are about the French period, but there are a couple regarding the ‘guests’ that stayed there during the Vietnam War.  I think it was quite eye-opening for our kids (11 and 14).
The audio guide is a bit long winded....but it's good information much more than the signs alone. Don't buy the paper guidebook.  Good information and it doesn't feel like propaganda.
An iconic Hanoi landmark steeped in French colonial history and the war with the USA. A glimpse into what the colonial empire was like, and what France did in this country.

In spirit this is an ugly place. Of course crimes are ugly but political repression and retribution are uglier. The focus was on a time when the French were incarcerating political prisoners. There was also some information on the American pilots shot down in 1972 but mostly on the day they were released after their six years of “temporary” and indefinite stay. A lot of propaganda on how kind the Vietnamese were shown by staged pictures of happy American prisoners. Hmm.

The Hoa Lo prison is easy to access, well organized and easy to grasp. Used during both French and Vietnamese control, the prison holds a certain political role. Well worth an hour or a little more. (Get the audio tour - $2.5 - with 35 pre-recorded tracks for each exhibit in the prison.)(Our seven year-old was a little intimidated by the prison, but there are no strong graphics etc.)
Very interesting. I learned a lot about the vietnamese war. However, I reccomend before going to read up about the basics of the war if you are quite clueless about the topic as you may get confused.
This was simply £1 pp to get in, well worth it. A lot of history attached to the prison, it is an interesting place I enjoyed it.

This unique and interesting place to visit for tourists interested in Hanoi's history. This location was once a prison that now functions as a museum commemorating the city's history. Children under 15 years old enter free. Admission is affordable, at 30,000 Vietnamese dong (approximately US$0.30) for adults. Tour guides, books, and digital versions are also available. Entering here allows you to experience the authentic atmosphere of a prison. It's a great place to bring children to learn about the conditions faced by those imprisoned.
Nice and exciting tourist attraction. Very moving and thought-provoking. Central location. Can be seen in about an hour. Without audio guide.

We knew this would be a difficult place, but the stories at the end of the exhibit about the Viet Nam war were so difficult to read.  I highly recommend a visit here, but be prepared to feel some pain.

This former prison is now a museum that is pretty much dedicated to show how badly the Vietnamese prisoners were treated by the French during colonial times (I don't know much about what the French did) and how well the American prisoners were treated by the communists during the Vietnam war.  You'll see pictures of US prisoners smiling, playing ping pong, going to church, eating well, etc. - the world knows US prisoners were tortured, not well taken care of but the Vietnam government propananda machine is still putting out the lies and propaganda.   Overall, it's worth a visit but I think that most people about 1 hour is good.  If your the type of person that reads everything (some stuff in English) and wants to do the audio guide then plan on 2 hours.

We did the prison on our last day in Hanoi to fill in the day before leaving for home.  We all hired the headsets for a small fee, I feel you get a lot more out of the experience if you do.  There is a lot of information in English you can read but listening allows you to walk around & observe a lot more, took us a few hours to get around, no food options available within other than vending machines so take it with you.  My 20 & 16 year old kids got a lot out of it but I wouldn’t take small children.

We travelled for the full length of Vietnam from Hanoi to Saigon, via Danang, taken in most of the 'War' history. Hoa Lo Prison a.k.a Hanoi Hilton proved to be the most friendly and non-biased war associated site we visited. The museum mainly concentrates on the French occupation, guillotine provided, and also how the museum has been used by a prison. All the information is well presented.
Great museum. I spent 3 hours there!!! Grab the audio tour as it adds a lot to the displays. Great personal stories that really bring it to life.

Representative of the damage inflicted by colonial powers, both psychological and physical. It's shameful and a real eye-opener to experience. I think the audio guide is helpful, and you can take your time. It's well-presented without being too intense. But be prepared for a strong stomach. The "American" section is a little less compelling but still worth visiting. An hour and a half to two hours is necessary, hopefully avoiding the hordes of tourists who photograph absolutely everything.
We spent a good hour here exploring the prison and imagining the horrors that had taken place. Good to get some history and fill in an hour or more in Hanoi.
Prison was smaller than I imagined. Was plenty to read and see but it seemed to repeat alot of it. Definitely a place  you need to visit In Hanoi.

If you have expectations before visiting this site, you will come away with a new perspective of the many conflicts in Vietnam's history.  Much of the prison is now gone, but the displays in the prison which is now a museum are thought provoking and could be hard to look at. It could be depressing, but there are displays providing hope for the future.

Two admissions + two audio guides (worth it!) = 160,000 VND, which is a small price to pay for two hours spent in this historical site. Seeing the guillotine used there by the French up close is disturbing.

I think many people think this is going to be all about Vietnam war and it’s not. It covers centuries of troubled times and harsh conflicts, its a must if you are in Hanoi. Very interesting when you see  what this country has been though in the last 2000 years. I did feel at the end there was a lot of propaganda especially with the relationship with Russians, but all in all it was very interesting trip
Very interesting and memorable. However, most of the information is very scewed in favor of the Vietnamiese.
If you have some time, it's a highly recommended visit. The tour through the prison allows you to experience firsthand the conditions to which prisoners were subjected during the war.

The prison highlights the suffering primarily of the Vietnamese under French rule.  The resilience of the prisons was astounding, given the torture and conditions.  While some reviewers were offended in how American prisoners were portrayed, remember that this is the Vietnamese narrative of the war.  For them, the American were just another invading foreign force to be repelled.  I think the museum allows empathy for all those prisons who suffered.

I wasn’t going to visit the prison as I didn’t think it would appeal to me, but went and enjoyed it. I too, the hop on hop off bus tour and visited late morning. I found the history interesting and the stories about how it was used to house American pilots during the Vietnam war very factual. Like any site associated with war, it was grim, but I thought it was well preserved and definitely worth visiting.

This museum shows how the French treated political prisoners during colonialism. The prison is very somber, and you can tour the premises with an audio guide, also available in German. This provides a wealth of information. Inside, you can see how prisoners were incarcerated and chained.
We got a slice of history first thing in 2020 when we visited the prison. The facility has been maintained fairly well and the audio guide has loads of information about the place.

We found the museum to be very informational and moving. The audio sets are definitely recommended, they really add to the experience and are easy to use. They cost 50,000 dong extra ($2), but it's worth it.There are plenty of benches to sit and rest / listen to the audio guide.The only disappointment was that the room about American prisoners of war was entirely propaganda. I hoped that they would be a little more honest in their depictions of American imprisonment. It is interesting to see the Vietnamese perspective, but I would recommend educating yourself further on the subject after your visit.

Great tour with good guidance. Not the usual America war style prison that u might think. More backdated to French colonial times and only a small section for America war but still a fantastic insight to what the jail would have been like for prisoners

A tour of the prison provides a great opportunity to learn more about the fascinating history of Hanoi.  Especially the French occupation and the treatment of the political prisoners.Well worth a visit.

We spent an interesting afternoon doing the audio tour and found it very interesting and emotional.You learn the history of the conditions the Vietnamese were kept in under the French rule and the way the Americans were treated after being captured during the vietnam war when it was referred to as the Hanoi Hilton.A must see activity in Hanoi.

Very interesting part of Vietnamese history. Would have liked more information on the American servicemen held here. But interesting to read about the ‘ comrades’ held here and the conditions they endured. Quite horrific .
I found the prison interesting, shame that most of it was knocked down for development. There are a lot of informative displays. I’m not sure how biased the information is though.
We got the audio guide and found it very interesting. Took about 1.5hours to go through with the guide. We went at about 9am and it was very quite and enjoyable.

Our visit to Ho Loa gave us a lot to think about. The exhibition presented a graphical representation of the horrors the Vietnamese political activists had to endure as they fought for the independence of their country. We'll worth the visit.

First of all I need to say that this place is not family friendly, the place contains scary pictures and the tool that the French use to torture the proud Vietnamese peace fighters, do not bring young toddlers with you on your trip:Lets start at the front, you will see a yellow gate that says “Maison Centralle” which is French for  The Central House ( I guess ). You can take some pictures at the gate. Next, you must buy the ticket at the ticket booth, a black little building at the left of the entrance. Then you will follow the directions to the prisoner’s cell. You will see life sized statue around the room. Afterwards, you will enter the Caqchot or the Dungeon. Prisoners are locked with their feet positioned higher than their head to create stress, not only that, they are torture in an inhumane ways such as Cold Water torture in the winter, fatal diseases, and extreme hunger from the French. Walking out, you will see the jailyard or garden. There are displays of monuments and relics that are too large to fit inside. Walking out to the second floor, you will see the list of Vietnamese fighters, politicians, and civilians that have died or extremely tortured here in Hanoi Hilton ( another term for the place ). If you read this review, I didn’t wrote all I could but I wanted you to be surprised because there are about 60% of this prison for you to discover! Bye and have a nice trip.( If you read to this part of the review, you are so good at reading)

Given the moniker of "Hanoi Hilton" by the relatively few American prisoners kept here when captured by the North Vietnamese during the Vietnamese war (mostly notably later Senator McCain), this prison is actually a brutal place used to house, torture and kill thousands of Vietnamese political prisoners and freedom fighters by the French colonial army.  It is a shame that Western visitors focus so much on the dozens or so US prisoners there and not on the immense tragedy of the Vietnamese people.  Walk around and pray in your heart.

We visited this museum on a walking tour with a university student, so were treated to additional information by our guide. The price point was fantastic (less than $2 CAD per person), and there was signage throughout the museum. Only a small part of the prison is preserved for the museum, but told a chilling tale.Great exhibition looking forward to peace at the end. The museum is worth a visit to learn about the prisoners (Vietnamese by the French, Americans by the Vietnamese), terrible conditions and history.

Known as the Hanoi Hilton from the "American" war, as the locals call it.  I was unaware that before the American pilots were held there, it was a French prison for Viet Namese, and  not a very nice place.  As others have said, it is a good place to go to be reminded of man's cruelty to man.  It has a fascinating history and lots of interesting information, definitely worth a visit

A sad reminder of the cruelty humans are comfortable inflicting on one another.  Worth a visit if only to remind ourselves never to allow these kinds of horrors to happen again.  This isn't a particularly large museum but they've left enough of the original nightmarishness intact to drive the point home.

I had no expectations. We paid 30,000vnd each and another 50,000vnd each for audio tour. Very informative and raw. The way they created realistic conditions with sculptures made it seem very eerie. The tour took about 1.5 - 2 hours.

Like any other prison in a colony, this one also has many stories of human rights violations and atrocities  of the rulers over the common people. Depressing but important to see and appreciate the efforts of those who fought against the colonial rule to over turn it and attain freedom for their nation.

Did not have any expectation when we visited the place, but turns out to be one of the places in Hanoi city that left an impression. Well-organised museum with good information about the history and exhibits. Worth a visit if you like history and museums.
Really enjoyed this visit to the Hanoi Hilton. It’s small but captures a lot about times past and the terrible conditions. Would recommend.

We were worried that the old prison would only be featuring the story of US prisoners of war but no, it tells the story of the prison and its prisoners from a long time ago under both Vietnamese and French control. Audio guides available. Basic WC.
The place is very educational and I’ve learned a lot about the history of the revolution in Vietnam. It is also a very inspiring place.
The prison was easy to wonder through, displays were in Vietnamese and English and it was not crowded. I found some of it hard to take due to the topic but interesting none the less.
The prison is interesting & tells the history from  perspective that people in the western world don't normally get. Well worth visiting.

This place will take about 1-1.5 hour to visit. I suggest getting an audio guide (50K VND plus another 50K VND as a deposit) to get more detailed information about the place. They actually have a lot of signages and explanations in English already, so you can just read it at your own pace, especially if you just want to go through some areas faster than the others. Overall, good place to learn about their history.

It is good to see these things, but be prepared, that it is a bit depressing to see the conditions in which the prisoners have to live. It took ua sbout 45 minutes to see the expositions and it was not crowded - only few other visitors were there.

Went to visit the Hoa Lo Prison or Maison Central it is a nice interesting museum and it shows what the prisoners went through.  Very sad to see the awful treatment that they had to endure.  War is awful Never again should anyone experience this.

Only part of the prison has been preserved. Rest has given way to urban renewal. They show some of the conditions that existed but speak mostly of its time as a prison the French used against the Vietnamese. There is a small section that talks about the Vietnam war but it is kind of glossed over and there are propaganda photos of American pilots playing basketball, chess, and appearing to be having a comfortable captivity. Still a place that needs to be seen.

This prison is a good visit if you are in the area. The audio guide was good and very informative if at times repetitive. The museum itself is well presented and has good artefacts. They have presented the cells well within the prison so that you get a real feel for how they were back then. It is hard to navigate around the audio tour as some bits end and there are no signs to the next stop. Inexpensive for entry and the audio tour.

Entering inside, you can't help but be moved by all that these people went through! For the thousands of political prisoners and ordinary citizens who were imprisoned, tortured and ultimately died either from the hardships or from the guillotine they were led through in this prison.

Only a small part of the original prison remains as a museum. The displays do not hold back on the brutality inflicted on Vietnamese political prisoners by the French colonial regime. From the punishment cells where prisoners were forced to sleep on a sloping concrete floor with their heads lower than their shackled feet, to the guillotine which claimed many victims, it's all there.A further exhibit paints a rosy picture of life for captured airmen shot down in the American war in this, the Hanoi Hilton.A temporary exhibition at the time of my visit honoured peace activists from all over the world who protested against the war.This is not a comfortable place to visit, but it is rewarding.
This place is good to visit because you can get a new experience here.the entry ticket also really cheap.

A little hard to get past the history of this place, in order to give an honest review.This was a part of history that redefined the previous glory of war.When we visited Vietnam, most of the local people told us they just wanted to work on what they were doing.  Vietnam has been invaded so many times, by so many countries/kingdoms, the people just want to be able to make a living, and provide for their family.Vietnam is still a communist country, but only approx 20 percent of the people are part of the communist party.  You have to apply to get in, and if anyone in your family past committed any previous affront to the party, you will not get in.This prison museum depicts the prisoner conditions, including photos of previous prisoners, how well they were treated, and how they lived while in prison.The photos included shots of John McCain, and his history in the prison.  How he refused to be released from prison, until all prisoners were released.There is a souvenir shop, restrooms/WC onsite, and the souvenir shop takes credit cards.The museum is a political statement on how well the prisoners were treated, and knowing that the internet in Vietnam is still controlled by the government, to this day, it is a sad state of affairs. Our intent was not to make this review a political statement.  We lost the war, but left them with generations of physical deformation from using Agent Orange....who really won, and who really lost.This is an inexpensive look into the war, and the reality that if a government/nation controls the flow of information, they control the perception of reality for the population.We heartily suggest visiting this museum.Mahalo

I visited Hanoi primarily for sightseeing, but I was also able to experience its history. Although there are no Japanese signs, I think it's worth seeing the exhibits alone. I was deeply struck by the tragic past that shaped Vietnam.

This is a really interesting place to visit as it highlights the brutality of war and has plenty of information and artefacts. We didn't use the audio service and we still had a good experience and learnt a lot. The prison is also quite open in that you can walk into the cells and torture rooms etc. which makes the experience more moving. It is also close to Hoan Kiem lake (around a 15 minute walk) and the tickets are very cheap. They also do discounts for students and elderly people. You could spend 1-3 hours here depending on your interest and attention to detail. The only negative is that some of the displays did seem a bit biased; implying American POWs had a great time in Vietnam and went home with nice souvenirs... we weren't entirely sure on the authenticity of that.Overall, it was really interesting and eye-opening, I would definitely recommend!

Worth seeing. Only a small part of this prison remains but it’s enough for you to try to imagine what it must have been like. You really get an idea of how it was and the hardships that were endured. Give yourself time to read the many moving stories.

On Sunday, while visiting the book street with my family, we stopped by to visit Hoa Lo Prison. We were impressed by the friendly staff and the cleanliness of the site. The directional signs were quite clear.

This prison was originally built by the French to imprison Vietnamese revolutionists, some of which later became founders of the Vietnam communist party. It was also a prison for USAF POWs, who referred this prison as Hanoi Hilton. The Vietnamese government depicts the Hanoi Hilton as a great place for American POWs, where they were treated with respect. Of course the Americans will tell you a different story. The late John McCain was here when he was a POW.

This is the instant pick-me -up Go to prison + then back into the sunlight3 minutes walk back to Lake ,sunshine and freedom All things you appreciate more after seeing the conditions and lives of the prisoners

Where Senator John McCain was allegedly incarcerated for 5 years. Originally built by the French when Vietnam was owned by them to imprison any militants. Then used to take care of shot down US pilots and was coined Hanoi Hilton. Gruesome place but interesting to see. Worth a visit for the £1 entrance fee(30,000 VD)

A large part of the prison was destroyed to build buildings, what remains is interesting, you can see dungeons, there are many documents from when this prison was used for the Vietnam-US war, and on the other hand, we don't talk much about this prison while the French had colonized Vietnam.
It is a fairly old prison, where most guards and is quite large and certainly. It was fixed a long time ago and looked old.

The old prison is almost gone but the recreation about the atrocities perpetrated in the prison does evoke strong emotions. Do spend 2-3 hours here and reflect on the spirit of those that were at war against mighty powers. Must do in Hanoi especially if you are interested in history.

We utilised the hop on and off bus service which stops here. It is a very interesting place to visit... very sobering to take in the history of the prison. Westerners really don't understand what human rights we have in comparison to places like this.. food for thought
A fascinating place to learn party of the history that took place in Vietnam, very interesting with some great displays, not for the faint hearted

It is located south of Phen Kiem Lake and is within walking distance. I heard it was a place where nationalists who resisted the French were imprisoned, and since various torture instruments are also on display, you can get a sense of the Vietnamese people's history, even if only a little. However, the facilities are somewhat shabby for a museum.

We visited the museum in November 2019. The museum was very informative and gave a great insight into the conditions and the initial use of the Prison by the French during the colonial times. The museum then has a small section detailing the American pilot POW’s who stayed at the prison. I would recommend visiting Hoa Lo Prison.

Hoa Lo Prison Historical Relic is located at no. 1 Hoa Lo street.  It was built in 1896 by the French when they still the colonial rule of Vietnam, to hold thousands of Vietnamese patriotic and revolutionary fighters.  Even though now only 1/5 of the original area of the prison are still there, here we can learn how the prison was built, the rooms and how the prisoners were treated. It’s an interesting place if you like history.

A lot of history in this place. Apparently John McCain was in here for a while. Entry is cheap, worth a visit for an hour or two. Central location so you can visit en route to seeing other places like 'train street', the Ho Chi Minh mausoleum or the French Quarter.

I spent around 1.5 hours here fully immersed in the historical atmosphere. Although some rooms were under renovation during my visit, there was still plenty to explore.  The museum provides a powerful and eye-opening look into Vietnamese history. It was inspiring to learn about the resilience and strength of the Vietnamese people. Audio guides are available for an additional fee, and some sections feature interactive videos with English subtitles. There is also a souvenir shop at the end of the exhibition route. A very meaningful and educational experience!

We visited Hoa Lo Prison in Hanoi, and it was an interesting historical stop. The site is open daily from 8:00 AM to 5:00 PM, and the ticket price was 50,000 VND per person.  The ticket queue moved quickly on the weekday we went, and the staff informed us right away that part of the prison was under renovation. Around 30% of the complex was closed, with replacement posters in some sections, but we chose to enter anyway. We didn’t rent the audio guide, but there were a moderate number of visitors, and several local guides were explaining the exhibits to their groups. We ended up overhearing some useful information as we walked through, which made it easy to follow along. The prison’s history is powerful and sobering. Originally built by the French colonial government to detain Vietnamese political prisoners, it later held American POWs during the Vietnam War. The exhibits illustrate both periods, giving a clear sense of how life was for prisoners during those difficult times.  For convenience, toilets are available in the outdoor central area and also near the merchandise shop by the exit gate. Overall, it was a meaningful and educational visit—especially for those interested in Vietnam’s history. Thank you — cảm ơn!

This was an outstanding visit. Admission was 50,000 vnd and I strongly recommend the audio guide headset for an additional 100,000 vnd - it had so much more context than just touring through via the wall exhibits.  I also appreciated learning the history of the prison during the French colonial period, as well as the Vietnamese side of the story of the Hanoi Hilton that they did not teach in my US history textbooks.  Well worth the visit.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Hỏa Lò Prison  A powerful and emotional place to visit. Hỏa Lò Prison offers an eye-opening look into Vietnam’s history, with detailed exhibits, original structures and deeply moving stories from both the French colonial era and the Vietnam War period. The museum is very well presented, with clear explanations, artifacts, photos and personal accounts that help you understand what prisoners went through.  It’s not an easy visit — some sections are confronting — but it’s incredibly important. The atmosphere is respectful and reflective, and it gives visitors a much deeper appreciation of the resilience and struggles of those who were held here.  Allow at least an hour to take it all in properly. A meaningful and memorable part of any trip to Hanoi, and a must-see for anyone wanting to understand the country’s past.

From my experience at Sacred Night 2: Living Like Flowers - honoring female heroes and warriors.  - Ticket price: 399k VND/person. 50% deposit required via bank transfer or 100% payment. Just pick up your ticket upon arrival.  I had to wait and arrange my time for a long time to have the opportunity to experience this. Tickets are usually booked 3-4 months in advance, or even more. It's only on weekends: Saturday and Sunday. So I was constantly worried about cancellations or unexpected events.  - The ticket price for Sacred Night 3 is 499k VND. I'll have to wait a few more months. Because right after attending Night 2, I immediately messaged them about Night 3. Night 1 is no longer being performed.  - Before entering, there will be an information session and a light refreshments area. Sweets, tea, and snacks are provided thoughtfully. It's best to arrive a little early to enjoy the tea and take photos. Wearing an ao dai (traditional Vietnamese dress) is also very appropriate.  - Each person was given their own pair of headphones, though they were a little loose. The volume could be adjusted. - The acting, role-playing, and experiential segments were amazing. You have to go, sit, and listen to truly feel the atmosphere. It was breathtaking. Even though I had visited Hoa Lo Prison during the day before, attending this private performance was completely different.  - At the end of the program, there was tea, jelly, and cakes made from banh betel leaves (like banh xu xe). They were delicious!  - Throughout the program, there was supposed to be a photographer capturing every moment and uploading it to the fan page, but unfortunately, I didn't get one.

Hoa Lo Prison turned out to be one of the most striking places I visited in Hanoi. The moment I walked in, the atmosphere felt heavy — the small rooms, stark displays, and preserved details offer a raw look into the lives of those who were once held there. The museum presents its history in a clear and compelling way, making it easy to understand the events that took place without feeling overwhelmed.  I happened to go at a quiet time, which made it easier to explore slowly and absorb everything; about an hour was enough for a thorough visit. The ticket price is quite low considering how meaningful the experience is. Be prepared for warm temperatures, as part of the site is outdoors and the indoor areas aren’t well-cooled.  Overall, it’s a powerful and unforgettable landmark that leaves you thinking long after you leave.

The prison holds deep historical significance, first during French colonial rule and later during the Vietnam War. The exhibits clearly show how extremely difficult daily life was. Prisoners were kept in cramped, overcrowded cells with limited light and ventilation. Many were shackled together, forced to sleep on hard floors, and given minimal food. Seeing the preserved iron shackles and narrow stone sleeping platforms made the suffering feel very real and personal. The museum does a strong job of recreating the atmosphere of that time. The life-size models and preserved cells help visitors imagine what prisoners experienced physically and emotionally. It was shocking to see how resilience and determination helped many of them survive such conditions.  Strongly recommend renting the audio guide during your visit. Without it, the information displayed on the panels is quite limited and not very detailed. The audio guide provides much deeper explanations, historical background, and personal stories that greatly enhance the overall experience and understanding of the prison’s history.  Some areas were closed for renovation during my vist. While this limited access to certain sections, there was still plenty to explore and learn from. Overall, it is still worth buying a ticket, as the experience remains meaningful and impactful. Visiting Hoa Lo Prison was both educational and emotional. It gave me a deeper appreciation of history and the strength of the people who endured such hardship.

A Powerful and Thought-Provoking Visit to Hoa Lo Prison  My recent visit to Hoa Lo Prison in Vietnam was a deeply moving and eye-opening experience. The prison stands as a stark reminder of Vietnam’s long and painful struggle under French colonial rule and later during the Vietnam War.  The museum is very well maintained and thoughtfully curated, with clear explanations, photographs, and preserved cells that help visitors understand the harsh realities faced by prisoners. Walking through the corridors evokes strong emotions and offers a sobering insight into the resilience, courage, and sacrifices of those who were imprisoned here. What impressed me most was how the history is presented in a clear and organized manner, making it easy to follow even for first-time visitors. The visit encourages reflection and leaves a lasting impact, reminding us of the value of freedom and human dignity.  Hoa Lo Prison is not just a historical site but a powerful place of learning and remembrance. I would highly recommend this visit to anyone interested in history, culture, and understanding Vietnam’s past on a deeper level.

This is a must visit if you have interest in history and want to know more about how prisoners were kept during French times in Vietnam. It also covers some details about Vietnam and US war. Eventhough there are some details mentioned in each display but I would recommend to get an Audio guide as it really helps to understand the displays in detail. This is surely 1.5+ hrs but it again depends on ones speed to finish the displays. Overall, a really nice experience and worth spending both time and money.

February 2026 - MUST SEE  I visited the Prison on a Monday afternoon. It was quite crowded but I just had to wait about two minutes to get an entry ticket (50k, cash) + 100k for an audio-guide, which is a must in my opinion.  I arrived around 15h45. They stop selling tickets at 17h but the prison is open until 18h. If you wanna see and read everything, and listen to the full audio-guide, it requires at least two hours. I wasn't able to listen to the 3 last stops as the place was closing.  This is the best attraction I've seen during my week in Hanoi. Provide with many information regarding the creation of the prison, its use by the French colonialists, the emprisonment of American pilots and so much more. When I was there, there was also an temporary exhibition about the American war in Vietnam. I learned a lot.  The only minus is the behaviour of some tourists, very noisy. Some group book one audio-guide for 3-6 people, and were repeating out loud what the guide say, disturbing the other tourists. Many tourists also have inappropriate behaviours (very loud, laughing, here for pictures only) and many men wear above the knee shorts on the sacral part when it is specified that it is prohibited.  Beside this aspect, the visit is a truly overwhelming moment and an absolute must-see.

Hoa Lo Prison Museum in Hanoi is an interesting and emotional place to visit. It shows how prisoners lived during the French colonial time and the Vietnam War. You can see old prison cells, photos, and real stories that explain the history in a simple way. The museum is clean and well arranged, so it is easy to understand. It can feel a little sad, but it is an important place to learn about Vietnam’s history.

Originally built by the French in 1896 as Maison Centrale, Hoa Lo Prison was a high-security detention facility for Vietnamese revolutionary fighters under colonial rule. The structure was designed to be robust, aiming to suppress and instill fear in those who opposed the colonial government. The conditions of imprisonment were notoriously harsh, with cramped cells, a rigid regime, and severe punishments, making it one of the most infamous prisons in Indochina at the time.  After 1954, when Vietnam gained independence, the prison was taken over and used for other purposes. During the Vietnam War, it held many captured American pilots, including Senator John McCain, who nicknamed it the "Hanoi Hilton"—a sarcastic remark reflecting the harsh realities of war. In 1993, most of the prison complex was demolished to make way for urban development, with only a portion preserved as a museum. Today, this site is an important historical attraction, helping visitors better understand Vietnam's struggle for independence and the major upheavals of the 20th century.

For obvious reasons the "Hanoi Hilton" has significant historical value to anyone who studies Vietnamese or United States history.  I am greatful to have had the experience to tour it.  The exhibits were presented well and the venue was well maintained.  If in Hanoi take the time to visit.  Highly recommended.

Five stars - but not for comfort.  Hoa Lo Prison is a dark mirror held up to humanity. It honors the courage of Vietnamese revolutionary fighters, yes, but it does not allow heroism to exist without context, without cost, without blood. This place forces you to walk through layers of cruelty inflicted by empires that spoke of civilization while practicing domination - the cold violence of French colonial rule, the industrialized annihilation brought by American carpet bombings, the millions of lives crushed beneath ideology, strategy, and indifference.  What makes Hoa Lo unbearable - and essential - is that it refuses abstraction. History here is not numbers, flags, or slogans. It is the fate of the individual. A body. A mind. A broken life. And in that single fate, the destiny of all humanity becomes visible. The prison walls do not scream only of Vietnamese suffering, but of a universal pattern - how easily humans dehumanize, how efficiently systems turn cruelty into routine.  And yet, this is not a place of pure despair. Paradoxically, amid the darkness, something else survives. A quiet, stubborn presence of human dignity. The capacity to endure. The potential for good that still slumbers within us, fragile but not extinguished. That final spark - not naive optimism, but hard-earned hope - is what makes Hoa Lo more than a memorial of horror.  In times that feel increasingly heavy, uncertain, and violent, this place stands as a warning and as a last hopeful anchor. It reminds us what we are capable of at our worst - and what we must choose, consciously and relentlessly, to become at our best.

A great museum experience and very rich content. Learnt about how vietnamese resistance was mistreated during french colonialism and how US pilots were held as POW. Must get the audio player to fully immerse in the storytelling. The site is not big but the amount of tourist/locals are huge. It is semi outdoor so do expect sweat and mosquito.

Hoa Lo Prison is a haunting yet incredibly valuable destination in Hanoi. Here are some brief observations about this place: • Historical significance: Often described as "Hell on Earth" in the heart of the capital, it preserves vivid evidence of the unwavering and indomitable spirit of Vietnamese revolutionary soldiers in the face of brutal torture by French colonialists.  • Tourist experience: Hoa Lo is currently one of the best sites in Vietnam for content and media promotion. Its modern displays, combined with sound and light, create a realistic and moving experience.  • Special highlight: The "Sacred Night" night tour is an unmissable experience, allowing visitors to delve deeper into the emotions and heroic historical stories through theatrical performances.

This is a great memorial to the victims of this place. Vietnam has done a good job of representing what took place here and the efforts made to move past it.  There are audio guides and tours in multiple languages that visit this facility. Be warned the vibe here is dark and if you're sensitive to such things, you'll probably not feel great when you're done.

Hoa Lo was interesting and sad at the same. Highlights a lot of the history of the vitamins occupation specially in Hanoi. Story's of the heroes and activists men and women who fought back in various ways. I've seen the same things in different places I visited, history repeats itself in different parts of the world. It was 50 or 60 dongs and 100k for the self guided audio device. Took me about 1 and half to finish. They close at 6pm. Definitely worth a visit if you're in Hanoi.

Plan for enough time allocate up to 2–3 hours.Use the audio guide for a more engaging and contextual experience. Visit early in the day to avoid crowds and navigate the exhibits more easily. The museum does a good job telling the stories of prisoners, including both Vietnamese revolutionaries and American POWs. However, some parts emotionally intense or even shocking, especially the darker prison cells and personal stories.

I participated in the Sacred Night Tour 3: Fire of Youth organized by the company for free viewing. The actors' performances were good, recreating some heroes in history, who had to sacrifice when they were very young, touching, proud and admired how resilient our ancestors were in the past. The tour has situations for the audience to play the role, to experience some of the difficulties of our ancestors in the past.  I have to shout out to the boss and the company for always spreading good values, from patriotism, inspirational stories of everyday life, good things that can be done for guests... very happy to go see such a good night tour under the sponsorship of the boss

Hỏa Lò Prison is not a long visit, but it is a deeply affecting one.  As we moved through the exhibits, my daughter quietly became teary-eyed reading the panels. The storytelling is restrained yet emotionally honest — not designed to shock or dramatize, but to bear witness. What stayed with us most was the clear reminder that in war, there is no true victor. The heaviest and most painful cost is borne by young soldiers whose lives were cut short, and by the families who carried that loss long after the fighting ended.  Like many museums in Hanoi, this is not a place for quick glancing or surface appreciation. It carries depth and heart. The panels don’t try to impress; they aim to impart understanding, reflection, and empathy. That quiet approach makes the experience more powerful.  Hỏa Lò is brief in time, but lasting in impact. It leaves you with a sober appreciation of history as lived human experience — marked by suffering, resilience, and memory carefully preserved.  A thoughtful, moving site that encourages reflection rather than spectacle.

Hoa Lo Prison, also known as Hoa Lo Prison, is one of Hanoi's historical sites, built by the French colonialists in 1896. It was once a "hell on earth," where thousands of Vietnamese revolutionary fighters were imprisoned and tortured. Originally covering over 12,000 square meters, the prison was solidly constructed with stone walls 4 meters high and 0.5 meters thick, surrounded by barbed wire. Currently, the preserved site covers approximately 2,434 square meters, becoming a popular destination for both domestic and international tourists.

Hoa Lo Prison is one of Hanoi's most iconic historical sites, attracting numerous domestic and international visitors. It was once a prison built by the French colonialists, holding many Vietnamese revolutionary fighters. The exhibition space is scientifically arranged, realistically recreating the lives and indomitable fighting spirit of the prisoners. The artifacts, images, and historical documents evoke deep emotions and offer profound educational value. This is a meaningful destination that helps the younger generation better understand the nation's history.

A must for Americans. Recommend the audio tour and you can get a thorough review in 90 minutes - in and out. Some of the museum is under renovation - maybe 50%, but what is available is well worth it.

I have to give 10/10 praise to the Hoa Lo content team! From the fan page to the display at the site, everything is very youthful and accessible without losing its solemn atmosphere.  • Plus point: They have a very convenient automatic audio guide with an expressive voice. You should definitely visit when traveling to Hanoi!

Visiting the Hoa Lo Prison historical site was truly moving and inspiring, filled with admiration for the resilience, bravery, and indomitable spirit of the revolutionary soldiers, and a sense of pride in being Vietnamese 😊 …

I visited Hoa Lo Prison in Hanoi and paid 50,000 VND for the entrance. I didn’t get the audio guide since many of the exhibits already have clear explanations in English. It was very interesting to see and learn about the history of the prison and what life was like for both Vietnamese and American prisoners. The displays are well organized and give a deep look into an important part of Vietnam’s past. Definitely worth visiting if you enjoy history.

HOA LO (HANOI HILTON) prison will be of great interest for anyone who has taken the time to research Vietnam's history, and it's struggles with transient foreign powers.  The setting underlines the incredible will power of the Vietnamese people to endure inhuman treatment at the hands of external aggressor's. In recent years the prison was synonymous for detaining American pilots, downed during 'missions'.  The facility is well visited by the local citizens and tourists alike. 🏛️ Hoa Lo Prison Relic: Where Time Stands Still Hoa Lo Prison is not just a historical relic; it is a symbol of unwavering will. From the moment you step through the heavy, black wooden gate, the atmosphere changes – solemn, majestic, and somewhat chilling.  Emotional Touchpoints • Audio Guide: This is the "soul" of the experience. Stories of faith, sacrifice, and suffering are vividly recounted through headphones, making each step heavier and more meaningful.  • Artifact Displays: From the giant guillotine to the worn-out prisoner uniforms, everything evokes a brutal yet heroic period of history.  🏗️ A Touch of Regret Amidst Unfinished Things Although still open to visitors, the fact that the prison is undergoing renovations will certainly prevent your experience from being 100% complete.  • Limited space: Some exhibition areas or characteristic corridors may be covered with tarpaulins or temporarily closed for preservation. This detracts from the overall view of the prison's original "U"-shaped architecture.  • Construction noise: Sometimes, the sounds of chiseling or construction unintentionally disrupt the inherent quiet and sacred atmosphere – a place where one should ideally only hear the soft footsteps and the gentle voice of the audio guide.  • "Heritage" photo opportunities: The characteristic moss-covered yellow walls are sometimes obscured by scaffolding, leaving young photography enthusiasts feeling a little disappointed when trying to capture nostalgic images.  ✅ Advice for you Despite some regrets about its imperfect appearance, Hoa Lo Prison is still well worth a visit. Renovations are necessary to protect the monument from the ravages of time, helping this "red address" continue to tell its story to future generations.

Hoa Lo Prison, stands as a stark and sobering historical site in central Hanoi. Originally constructed by the French colonial authorities between 1896 and 1901 as Maison Centrale, it was designed to detain Vietnamese political prisoners fighting for independence. The prison earned a grim reputation for brutal conditions, overcrowding, torture, and executions—evidenced today by the preserved French guillotine, leg shackles, dark solitary confinement cells (Cachot), and recreated scenes of chained inmates enduring horrific treatment. After Vietnam's independence in 1954, the facility transitioned to North Vietnamese control and housed captured U.S. pilots during the Vietnam War (1964–1973), including notable figures like Senator John McCain, whose flight suit and parachute are displayed. The ironic nickname "Hanoi Hilton" reflected the POWs' sarcasm amid poor conditions, though the museum emphasizes Vietnamese resilience and downplays certain aspects of that era from a national perspective. Today, only a portion of the original complex remains as a museum (much was demolished in the 1990s for urban development). Exhibits include artifacts, photographs, prisoner letters, and mannequins illustrating daily hardships, offering powerful insight into colonial oppression and wartime captivity. The atmosphere is heavy and emotional—visitors often describe it as eye-opening, disturbing, yet essential for understanding Vietnam's history. An audio guide (extra fee) enhances the experience with detailed narration. Allow 1–2 hours; it's affordable and easily walkable from the Old Quarter. While some find the presentation biased, it's a compelling, must-visit for history enthusiasts seeking reflection on sacrifice and endurance.

🔥 Hoa Lo Prison – A place that preserves the tragic memories of Hanoi  In the heart of modern Hanoi, there is a quiet and haunting place – Hoa Lo Prison, also known as Maison Centrale, located at 1 Hoa Lo Street, Hoan Kiem District.  This place used to be “hell on earth” under the French colonial period, and is now a historical museum that helps today’s generation better understand the resilient fighting spirit of the Vietnamese people.  The prison was built by the French in 1896, with the purpose of detaining patriots and Vietnamese revolutionary soldiers. The name “Hoa Lo” originated from the neighborhood specializing in pottery production, coal burning and brick and tile, but when it entered history, it became a symbol of pain, courage and indomitable will.  Passing through the heavy iron gate, visitors will encounter dark cells, cold chains, artifacts and models recreating the harsh lives of prisoners in the past.   In the darkness of the thick walls, there is still a shining spirit of optimism, faith and aspiration for freedom - which helped them overcome all the brutal beatings and tortures.   Another part of the exhibition area marks the period after 1954, when this place was used to detain American pilots captured during the Vietnam War, including Senator John McCain - who later became a symbol of reconciliation between the two countries.   Today, Hoa Lo Prison is not only a famous historical tourist destination, but also a place to educate about patriotic traditions, a place to remind everyone of the value of peace and freedom.

Hoa Lo Prison Visiting Guide: Getting There Hoa Lo Prison is located in the city center, so getting there isn't too difficult. It's easy to find by following Google Maps directions if you're traveling by private vehicle. From Hoan Kiem Lake, follow Le Thai To Street towards Hang Trong. Continue past Ba Trieu Street and turn right onto Hai Ba Trung Street. From there, go another 500 meters and turn left onto Hoa Lo Street; the prison is located at 1 Hoa Lo Street.  Alternatively, you can take a ride-hailing service or a taxi. Bus routes: Bus No. 02: Bac Co - Yen Nghia Bus Station Bus No. 32: Giap Bat Bus Station - Nho Son Bus No. 34: My Dinh Bus Station - Gia Lam Bus Station Bus No. 38: Nam Thang Long Bus Station - Mai Dong Bus No. 45: Times City - Nam Thang Long Bus Station  Opening Hours and Entrance Fees for Hoa Lo Prison - Opening Hours: 8:00 AM to 5:00 PM every day of the week, except for certain holidays or other special occasions.  - Entrance Fees:  Adults and children 15 years and older: 30,000 VND/person.  Students, people 60 years and older, and those receiving social welfare benefits: 15,000 VND/person.  Children under 15 years old, people with disabilities, and those who have contributed to the revolution: Free.

Hoa Lo Prison, built by the French colonial government in 1896, apparently housed Vietnamese revolutionaries and soldiers. It offers a glimpse into a hellish era, unimaginable from present-day Hanoi. 🕊️ My Visit to Hỏa Lò Prison – “The Hanoi Hilton” 🇻🇳  Walking through Hỏa Lò Prison was a powerful and emotional experience. Hoa Lo Prison – a return to the pages of living history in the heart of the capital I visited Hoa Lo Prison in 2022, and it is truly one of the most emotional places to visit in Hanoi. Passing through that black gate, it feels like touching real history. The stories of revolutionary soldiers, the cramped cells, the remaining artifacts… all make the viewer silent. Although it is a place of imprisonment, the spirit of freedom and indomitable will are present in every corner. If you come to Hanoi, you should definitely spend at least 1 hour to visit Hoa Lo. One of the experiences I looked forward to the most before going to Hanoi was visiting Hoa Lo Prison, and it didn't disappoint. The museum is thoroughly educational and really gives you a sense of what life was like there, and shares a lot about those who were imprisoned there from French colonial rule, until during the duration of the Vietnamese-American War.  I have to say that the Vietnamese truly do have deep commitment to and extreme talent in preserving these historical artifacts and turning them into an educational experience. Not all countries or cultures are this invested in taking the trauma and turmoil from their past and turning it into a lesson. It's been two weeks since we came home from Vietnam and I still find myself thinking about the displays there: the clothes used by prisoners, the torture items, the stories we read. It was extremely eye-opening and reminded me of the effects of war on us all.  At the end of the exhibit, you can light an incense stick for the soul of those who passed during the war, and those who passed in Hoa Lo Prison.  There is an option to get a human guide or an audio guide, but we opted to go without either. It was still an educational experience. Tickets were 50k dong I think, and you would need an hour and a half to soak up the whole exhibit.

Dong Song ❤️  Go to see how the Father and Brother generation fought for Independence - Freedom!  There are still many "people" stuck there after hundreds of years and have not returned or have no place to return to ❤️  Hoa Lo Prison is one of the special historical relics in Hanoi, associated with many ups and downs of the nation. I will summarize for you according to the timeline:  ⸻  1. Construction and original purpose • In 1896, the French colonial government built Hoa Lo Prison in the center of Hanoi (now on Hoa Lo Street, Hoan Kiem District). • The name "Hoa Lo" originally came from the neighborhood that specialized in selling coal stoves and clay ovens before. • This is one of the largest and most solid prisons in Indochina, initially holding about 450 prisoners, but later it was often overloaded, at times holding up to 2,000 people. • Main purpose: to detain patriots, anti-French revolutionaries, and also to detain criminal prisoners.  ⸻  2. French colonial period • The prison had a brutal detention system: solitary confinement, iron shackles, chains, and torture methods. • Many famous revolutionaries were imprisoned here: Phan Boi Chau, Nguyen Luong Bang, Truong Chinh, Le Duan, Nguyen Van Cu... • Also from Hoa Lo, many daring prison breaks took place, the most famous of which was the Tet 1945 prison break.  ⸻  3. Anti-American resistance period • After 1954, when the North was liberated, Hoa Lo became a place to detain criminal prisoners and some political prisoners. • In particular, during the Vietnam War (1964–1973), many American pilots who were shot down were detained here, including John McCain (later a US Senator).  • American prisoners of war called this place “Hanoi Hilton”, both satirical and reflecting the harshness of being detained far from home.  ⸻  4. Present • Most of Hoa Lo Prison has been demolished, leaving only a small area that is preserved as a historical relic and museum.  • Inside are displayed: • Torture instruments, iron shackles, solitary confinement cells. • Artifacts and images of imprisoned revolutionary soldiers. • Documents about American pilot prisoners of war during the war. • This is a famous tourist destination, attracting both domestic and foreign tourists, both for historical education and as a testament to the resilient spirit of the Vietnamese people.

I've been to Hoa Lo Prison a total of two times. The first time was before the fan page went viral, and the second time was recently at the end of 2025. A Profound Journey Through Vietnam’s History  Visiting Hoa Lo Prison was a deeply moving experience. The museum offers a stark and powerful glimpse into Vietnam’s turbulent past, from its days under French colonial rule to the Vietnam War. Walking through the preserved cells and exhibits, I was struck by the resilience and courage of those who endured unimaginable hardships within these walls. The displays are informative and thought-provoking, providing insight into the struggles for independence and the complexities of war. It’s a sobering reminder of the cost of freedom and the strength of the human spirit. A must-visit for anyone seeking to understand Vietnam’s rich and challenging history.

Visiting Hoa Lo Prison is not merely a historical excursion — it is an emotional reckoning. The moment I stepped inside, a chilling silence wrapped around me. It wasn’t the air that made me shiver, but the weight of suffering, resistance, and unspoken screams that still linger within these cold, oppressive walls.Built by the French colonists to incarcerate and break the will of Vietnamese patriots, Hoa Lo was designed as a living hell. Narrow, airless cells, heavy wooden shackles, and rusting iron bars speak of the torment inflicted. Instruments of torture — including the infamous guillotine — are preserved like grim witnesses to the brutality of a regime that sought to crush the human spirit through pain and fear.Yet amidst that horror, what struck me deepest was not just the cruelty, but the courage.As a traveler representing Xemsomenh.com, where we explore the fate, soul, and resilience of the Vietnamese people, I was deeply moved by how the revolutionaries transformed this prison into a cradle of strength and ideals. Names like Phan Boi Chau, Nguyen Duc Canh, and Nguyen Van Cu echo through the halls — not as victims, but as symbols of heroic resistance. Within these bleak cells, they turned darkness into light, turning torture chambers into classrooms, and nurturing hope in places meant to extinguish it.Hoa Lo is no longer just a museum. It is a place of remembrance — a shrine to sacrifice, and a testament to the iron will of those who laid the foundation for our independence. It reminds us that while colonists may have wielded chains and swords, they could not imprison the human spirit nor destroy the dream of freedom.Walking through these corridors, it is impossible not to feel humbled. You don’t just see history — you feel it. The pain is palpable, but so is the pride. Each rusted chain, each crude wooden door, tells a story that demands not only respect, but also reflection.At Xemsomenh.com, we believe that destiny is shaped not only by stars and numbers, but by the courage of those who dared to change their fate. Hoa Lo Prison is living proof. Here, fate was rewritten — not by surrender, but by sacrifice.If you come to Hanoi, don’t skip this place. Come with an open heart. Leave with a deeper understanding — not just of Vietnam’s painful past, but of the extraordinary spirit that rose from it.

Sarcastically known as "Hanoi Hilton," this infamous prison was renowned for its harsh treatment of prisoners. French built in 1896; it is now mostly torn down and transformed into a museum with stark reminders of the era of its prominence. It was hard walking through there knowing some of the history which included the incarceration of Senator John McCain.  Originally the prison had 4 areas (A-D). Within those areas were the cramped cells, torture devices and the Guillotine Room. Today we see articles of oppression, the sewer used to escape and the POW exhibition area. Significant artifacts including haunting photos and prison objects. There’s newly created artwork and sculptures of prisoners. You can light an incense stick to hopefully inspire a more peaceful future. I did.

Recently, I had the opportunity to join the tour “Hanoi Essence: 6 Hours of Magic” – a short but emotional and memorable journey to discover Hanoi. As someone who has participated in many tours, I was really impressed by the meticulousness and scientific arrangement of the schedule as well as the quality of service that the organizer provided.The tour started at 13:20 at Hoa Lo Prison – a famous historical site in the heart of Hanoi. Right from the gathering, I felt the professionalism when the tour guide briefly introduced the schedule and important notes. I was quite impressed with the way the staff took care of the tourists, from ensuring drinking water to welcoming them warmly. Hoa Lo Prison carries within it painful but proud historical stories. Under the guidance of the tour guide, each story and each artifact seemed to come alive before my eyes. Leaving Hoa Lo, the group quickly moved to Thang Long Imperial Citadel by comfortable and cool means of transport. The shuttle bus was very punctual, clean and fully equipped with drinking water for each passenger. At Thang Long Imperial Citadel, I felt like I was stepping into a space of the past, where the vestiges of ancient dynasties are still imprinted through each brick and each gate. The tour guide not only introduced historical information but also told interesting stories, making the tour not dry at all. After hours of exploring history, the group moved to Hanoi Old Quarter to enjoy the typical cuisine here. The time for cuisine was arranged reasonably, not too rushed. The tour suggested a few famous restaurants in the Old Quarter and I chose a small, pretty restaurant with typical dishes such as pho, bun, and spring rolls. Each dish had a strong Hanoi flavor, was fresh and delicious, and was served very attentively.In the late afternoon, the group experienced the feeling of sitting on a cyclo around Hoan Kiem Lake. This was probably the most romantic and memorable moment of the trip. When the city began to light up, Hoan Kiem Lake appeared sparkling with Turtle Tower and the bright red The Huc Bridge. Sitting on a cyclo, watching the narrow old streets, the street stalls, and the flow of people created a peaceful picture of Hanoi amidst the hustle and bustle of life.Next, the group stopped by Thang Long Water Puppet Theater to enjoy a traditional art performance. The wooden puppets were skillfully manipulated under the water, combined with traditional music to create an artistic space imbued with Vietnamese identity. The performances tell familiar yet captivating folk tales. The last stop on the journey is Indochine restaurant. The restaurant space is luxurious, cozy and the menu is diverse and attractive. I really like the attentive service of the staff here, always ready to meet all the guests' requests.Dinner took place in a comfortable, cheerful atmosphere. Delicious food, beautifully presented and especially a relaxing time after a long day of exploration.The tour ended at 19:30 and the tour guide did not forget to say goodbye and introduce other tours for those who want to explore Hanoi more deeply. Hanoi Essence: 6 Hours of Magic" is truly a short but extremely complete journey. From historical relics, cultural spaces to cuisine and art, all are arranged harmoniously and carefully. If you are looking for a tour to explore Hanoi in a short time but still full of experiences, do not hesitate to book this tour right away.

Visiting Hỏa Lò Prison in Hanoi was a truly eye-opening experience. As a tourist, I appreciated the well-preserved historical artifacts and the detailed information provided throughout the museum.What I liked:- The museum offers a balanced narrative about the prison's history, from its use during the French colonial period to its role in the Vietnam War.- The exhibits are engaging and immersive, with a mix of photographs, documents, and reconstructed prison cells that give a vivid sense of the harsh conditions faced by prisoners.- The storytelling was powerful, particularly the section on the resilience and determination of Vietnamese revolutionaries held there.- The air-conditioned interiors provided a comfortable environment for learning, even on a hot day in Hanoi.But I didn’t like something as:- Some parts of the museum felt a bit crowded, especially during peak visiting hours, which made it challenging to fully enjoy the exhibits.- While the displays were informative, I felt that certain narratives were a bit one-sided, particularly in the section dedicated to American POWs during the Vietnam War. Adding more perspectives would enrich the storytelling. Overall, Hỏa Lò Prison is a must-visit for history enthusiasts and anyone interested in understanding Vietnam’s complex past. It’s a sobering reminder of the struggles and sacrifices made during pivotal moments in history.
