It was totally worth it. Since it was my first time visiting and I was traveling alone, I rented a recording device to listen to the stories about this prison. As I visited each area, observing and listening to the stories behind the artifacts, I grew to admire the revolutionary soldiers of the past even more, and especially, my love for my homeland, Vietnam, deepened!

I can’t believe that Hoa Lo prison is (currently) only rated at #10 for things to do in Hanoi. This is easily a top #5 experience. The prison is such a grounding experience. It tells the tales of countless Vietnamese men and women who were imprisoned and/or killed due to their political and spiritual beliefs. Despite this, the prisoners never gave up hope, and many even escaped the horrifying conditions they were exposed to. You get chills as you pass through the prison cells and learn about what happened inside these walls.

Hoa Lo Prison is a must-see historical site in Hanoi. It offers a powerful and emotional experience, recreating the dark periods of the nation's struggle for independence. The exhibits and stories are clearly and vividly displayed, helping visitors gain a deeper understanding of the resilience of the revolutionary fighters imprisoned there. However, the English information could be improved to better serve international visitors. This is a wonderful place to explore and learn about Vietnamese history.

I didn't really want to visit this prison, but had booked a 'Tour with a Local' who was a bit dogmatic about the itinerary (but she was right!). It is easy to get to, with plenty of info/signage in English. It was really interesting to hear about the French colonization of Vietnam and the history of the prison, and to see artefacts and history from the 'American War'. This was obviously from the Vietnamese experience and point of view, and most illuminating and fascinating. Seeing a guillotine was gruesome but also interesting (don't think I've seen one before despite being to France on many occasions).

My family esp. our 14 year old son is a history buff.  He took his time here, listening to the audio headset guide we rented.  It was very interesting and sad.  The materials or displays was awesome.  Some are realistic which made it sadder yet very interesting for history sake.  Lots of emotions here while we go through the tour.  It's a must visit when visiting Hanoi.  The museum over all was well-kept, clean and organized.  Staff or volunteers running the place speaks English.  They are also friendly and accommodating.  The museum have vending machine, gift shops available.  Restroom is also clean.  There's no time constraint so you can go through the museum at leisure or at your own pace.  We are specially thankful for the A/C cool atmosphere.  It was a reprieve from Hanoi's summer heat.  Truly an amazing and historical museum full of interesting displays.

Hoa Lo Prison, or "Hoa Lo Prison," is a haunting historical landmark in the heart of Hanoi. Originally built by the French colonialists to imprison Vietnamese patriots, it later served as a detention center for American prisoners of war during the Vietnam War. Today, Hoa Lo is a museum, recounting stories of courage and resilience during the most difficult periods of Vietnamese history. A small place, yet one filled with profound meaning, it leaves visitors with much to ponder.
A place steeped in spirituality and the historical legends of our ancestors throughout the nation's difficult times. I was deeply moved and gained much inspiration to live and work even better.
The Hoa Lo Night Show is truly exceptional. It was so emotional and moving that it brought me to tears. The performers are incredibly talented and made the experience unforgettable. Highly recommend!

This is a place you absolutely must visit if you're in Hanoi. Partly because it's near the Old Quarter, and partly because it's conveniently located for experiencing other things in Hanoi. If possible, going on a date here would also be a great option.

Hoa Lo Prison offers a sobering look at Vietnam's history, particularly its French colonial period.  We were moved by the prisoner artwork and personal stories.  While somber, it's a chance for reflection and understanding.

For what it cost, it’s worth it. Very informative. Kinda graphic at one point, but loved all the history and explanations behind everything. We didnt do the audio tour, there is plenty to read. It was easy to enjoy because we went at opening and basically went ahead of everyone else.
One of the many must-see attractions when visiting Hanoi. It offers a realistic portrayal of the suffering caused by the war. The entrance fee is reasonable for tourists...

The prison is fascinating and informative. We did the Audio Tour of the prison which is available for 100k VND (per person). The audio tour provides narration through headphones as you walk through the prison and key in exhibit numbers into the device they give you. The narration includes personal stories of people kept in the prison, and explanations of objects in the exhibits and their significance. Hoa Lo prison is full of historical objects and insane stories of escape and determination. I highly recommend going and doing the audio tour.
The place woth to visit.You will learning much about Vietnam War, seeing how did they can fight again 2 big countries...Vietnamese should pround of their country.We love Vietnam.

Before going to Hanoi, I’ve been eyeing to visit this museum because it’s part of the history of Vietnam. The museum was very immersive and well-presented. You can avail the audiobook to deeply understand each representation per room. Overall, it was a great experience to know this part of Hanoi.

This is the famous Hanoi Hilton. It is very moving and the audio tour is really really good. While seeing the artifacts of American POW’s (including John McCain) is moving, it is most important to be here because the long struggle and bravery depicted against the French colonialists helps you understand how very very long the Vietnamese struggled for self determination

A must do in Hanoi. I got the audio tour (highly recommend) and my wife and daughters (18, 15), didn’t get the audio. The audio was great, adding prisoners’ stories to the tour and getting a true sense of what it was like. At time it was devastating and other times uplifting with the courage of the prisoners. American part showed well looked after prisoners, so take that how you want to due to the two different sides of the story. Whole family appreciated the information presented in this well set out tour.
We learnt so much going through here.  Would def put it on your to do list.  A very good experience.  We did not get the audio - but rather read our way through which we were more than happy with.
Fascinating history.  A larger touring experience than I expected.  I rented the audio guide and was glad I did.  Interesting insight into the Vietnamese perspective on the Vietnamese/American war.
This place is really cool. Easily my favourite paid tourist attraction in Hanoi. Would recommend. I didn't get the audio guide but heard from others that it's worth it.

A visit to Hoa Lo Prison is a must. The prison, or what remains of the original structure, was built by the French (who ruled Indochina-Vietnam, Laos and Cambodia-up until 1954) in the late 19th century and was used to incarcerate mostly political prisoners who lived under inhuman conditions. The appalling conditions used by the French are evident in the various displays throughout this museum . Even if the story may be "one-sided" one cannot help but despair at the thought that human beings can be so cruel to others. The prison was also used by the North Vietnamese  during the Vietnam War, to hold enemy prisoners, mostly Americans. During that time it came to be known as the "Hanoi Hilton"..... However not much is displayed about the Vietnam War years.

A visit not to be missed for its historical weight, but ALSO FOR THE EMOTIONAL IMPACT..The entire exhibition is done with very impactful realism. There is no chance of not being affected. It is common to find former prisoners visiting with their families, which further reinforces the emotional nature that the visit imposes.The exhibition respects the temporal evolution of the prison and the most relevant events that took place there.
Must Do !!! This is so interesting and thought provoking. some real insights into how life would have been like (and its brutal).
This is a must visit place for history aficionados in Hanoi.  Good for someone that really love to study about Vietnam history. So proud to be vietnamese when you see this place.

Very informative visit.  Individual headset to hear the commentary for each exhibit.  The information in the rooms were well presented.  It was busy, but we were able to get around easily and see everything.  Some of the rooms had seats so we were not constantly standing.  There was a shop on site although we didn't buy anything.  Although not air conditioned, there were air fans in most of the rooms.
Impressive prison museum in Hanoi. You will find out a lot of details about the history of Vietnam. It's definitely worth a visit!

It was truly a meaningful place where I could learn a lot about Vietnamese history!!! If I have another opportunity in the future, I will definitely come with other friends and family to share what I felt and increase the value of the trip. Hanoi, the center of Vietnam, is the best!
Had a great time and there was an outstanding exhibition there, documenting the history of the prison. I would advise anyone visiting Hanoi to go

We had an enjoyable morning at the museum, but highly recommend. The only thing I would suggest is that you bring insect repellent with you as sometimes the bugs are quite bad I got about 30 bites during my visit.

I went with my parents who are super interested in history- me, not so much, however I had a fantastic time. We walked through individually, without a tour or the audio and loved it. Extremely touching and really glad I saw it. No line for the tickets, just buy them outside the prison on the day.

This was our number one half-day out in Vietnam (along with the water puppets).  You had audio in several languages and easy to find your way around.  It is not for the faint hearted though, the conditions that the Vietnamese and US prisoners experienced was horrific.

It's a wonderful place to learn about Vietnamese history. It's heartbreaking to think of how cruelly the prisoners were treated. After seeing the actual size, darkness, cruel torture and execution equipment of the cells, I'm impressed that they were able to build a facility called the Hanoi Hilton.
So impressed with the audio guide service at Hoa Lo Prison! They had multilingual options that made the history accessible to international visitors. A great touch to enhance the overall experience!
It is worth visiting to learn about the history of Vietnam and its occupation by the French. We did it on our own, and with the audio guide you are well informed.

Great immersive spaces that gives great insights of life as a prisoner. The area can be covered in under an hour but for audio tours which I would recommend might take much longer due to the variety of content. Highly recommended.

Hỏa Lò Prison was a prison in Hanoi originally used by the French colonists in Indochina for political prisoners, and later by North Vietnam for U.S. prisoners of war during the Vietnam War.During French era Hỏa Lò was known as Maison Centrale. During Vietnam War, it's was known as Hanoi Hilton from the film The "Hanoi Hilton".
Fire Prison is one of many must-visit places when you travel to Hanoi. There is a new night tour open to everyone but the ticket price is not worth it.
Impressive, sickening and respectful of everyone who has been imprisoned and tortured here. What hell.Still good to see.
A very interesting look at history . This is an old prison built by French while occupying Vietnam. Great audio tour at a cost of $100,000 dong.
A place worth experiencing when you travel to Hanoi. Currently there are also very interesting night tours. Ticket prices are a bit more expensive but still reasonable.
The museum, history, and collections are rich. The prison and their events were beyond barbaric- evil. Emotional experience.

I loved this visit, it was fascinating! Terrible to see the life of these prisoners in this prison and the survival in these dungeons!! I learned a lot of things that I didn't know... the whole story is explained on panels but you also have the possibility of taking audio (for a fee) very nice too, that's what I did in French .I really recommend this visit, you will not regret it!
It was a very informative and moving experience about the history of Hanoi and what took place here for many prisoners of war.  Definitely worth a visit.

Not to be missed to remember the horrors of colonialism (here French) and part of the sad history of the country, the sewers which allowed certain prisoners to escape and change history. Well documented and well done. The building was built by the French by razing a village that lived on crafts.

This relic is part of Vietnam's painful past and exploring it helped me understand more deeply the history and spirit of the Vietnamese people. Detailed stories, especially together with the photos of actual people who has been executed. I think it is an interesting historical place and worth experiencing for not only history lover.

The Hanoi Hilton, is possibly one of the hardest museums I have ever visited. This establishment has seen many deaths, hardships, and torture. It is important to remember the history despite how painful it is. The museum is huge and not even the full size as it once was. Visit on a weekday, the weekends are quite busy and crowded.

Feels wrong to say I enjoyed the visit given the site's historical purpose, but it does capture Vietnam's history dating back before colonization. The site is only around half what it once was, but does a good job at capturing the experience of the prison as well as the purpose. Do keep in mind it presents a certain historical perspective, but nonetheless a must visit in Hanoi.

The museum is well worth seeing. You get a good impression of the conditions of the past. The many information boards in Vietnamese, French and English tell you a lot and you don't necessarily need the expensive audio guide. The entrance fee is 30,000 dong. Only recommended to a limited extent for children.
tbh, i was exploded with tears and emotions after visiting this place, so proud to be vietnamese!!!!

This is the prison known to Americans as the Hanoi Hilton when US airmen were detained there and there is an exhibit on that period.  But far more interesting is the way the struggle for independence from the French is depicted through the prisoners detained during the French occupation and their stories.

This is one of the best place to be in hanoi. You get to relive the struggle of the Vietnamese people against French and Americans.It makes you remember about the heroes of the country. It is must to be in here as understanding a country's history is the best way to start your trip.The place is at walking distance from st Joseph's cathedral.
A really important place to visit. Very informative and gives those who visit an insight into the history of the prison and the conditions that the prisoners endured.

I was truly impressed by the comprehensive exhibits and the haunting stories they tell. The prison's transformation from a place of suffering during the French colonial to a poignant reminder of resilience and determination during the American War is striking. The attention to detail in the exhibits and the informative audio guide made my experience much more worth it. Walking through the solemn halls and cells, I feel a sense of reverence for the strength of the Vietnamese people!!! Hoa Lo Prison is an essential stop for history buffs and curious travelers alike!
Loved it and wave scary then port Arthur in Tasmania Australia. I loved the death cell and the women one. Great audio tour at a cost of $100,000 dong. Lovely staff and also great gift shop.
It’s an amazing tour very detailed and a great guide in assistance learning about the history of the people and Vietnam War.

The colonizing power or not puts its intelligence, its methodology and its rigor to create the perfect place to lock up its political opponents. In this respect, this prison is a success. Buildings still standing that seem to defy the years. The visit is fascinating, we opted for the audio guide and the explanations are an undeniable plus. We must recognize that the French authorities have great contempt for the Vietnamese people and it is incredible to see that the scandalous conditions continued after the Second World War. This prison was also used by American soldiers. Obviously, as in other places of history in Vietnam, the historical facts are reviewed according to the Vietnamese prism and certain assertions should be more nuanced but the fact remains that it is an essential visit which sets a framework history and allows you to acquire new knowledge.
Definitely worth a visit if you’re in the area! The museum is laid out really well and is very informative and interesting

This was my second visit to Hoa Lo Prison but the first for my husband. It was open on Independence Day but was incredibly busy because of that. Last time I came away with an understanding of the horrible conditions Vietnamese people suffered under French colonialism but this time the actual prison area was so crowded it was difficult to stop and read anything. It was less crowded in the section about the American pilot prisoners and in the outside area. There were two new displays there that replaced the ones I had seen last time: one on the 1972 bombing of Hanoi and one on Vietnamese youth involvement in the resistance. These were primarily photos and written material (including English) an both contained quite graphic material.
Excellent exhibit on the infamous prison and what life was line in it. Expect to get a healthy dose of propaganda but still worth the visit. Need at least an hour.
A place full of heroism and showing the fighting spirit of the Vietnamese people and a lesson for everyone in the world to appreciate peace more.

Put it on the list of things to do in Hanoi. We didn't participate in the audio guide and it took us 1-2 hours to tour the museum. There are many signs explaining the history of the museum and exhibits. You are allowed to move freely around the prison and cells and it is both interesting and challenging. There are stories of some of the people held at the prison and they have been presented in a way that will hold your attention and make you want to read everything. We had a really good time for 30,000 VND, definitely worth the trip, it's also within walking distance of the city so easy to get to on a day trip to Hanoi.
Visiting Hoa Lo Prison is a must when visiting Hanoi. It is a very important historical relic where you can learn more about the stories of the struggle of the Vietnamese people

The existing museum is located in the remaining sections of the prison. Entry price is pretty cheap, as is the audioguides, which are available in several languages. On google maps, it says the museum closes at 5pm, but we learned that it is open until 6pm, but the last entrance is 5pm.  It was an interesting and informative museum.

Chilling and fascinating experience. The prison has lots of different sections with plenty of information and artifacts. Highly recommend getting the audio headset for more in-depth knowledge of the prison and its history.

Went to see the infamous " Hanoi Hilton", whilst only a small part remains, there was enough to see the conditions etc they had to endure. We used the audio tour for about $6, found this very informative and easier than reading all the info on site.Highly recommended.

Best tourist attraction in Hanoi! The museum is well-designed, where you can learn the history of Vietnam from the French colonial period till the end of the Vietnam War. There's a lot to see in the prison, with clear description. There's also sufficient space to sit and enjoy the AC throughout the entire visit which is very nice during summer. There's also a small shopping mall and supermarket beside the prison which you can grab some snacks after the visit.
Great museum- if you want to see and read everything you may want to do it over two days as it is so interesting and there is so much info.  We took two teenagers who both enjoyed the experience.
Definitely a must-see, both with and without a guide. We were very excited and it would be a shame not to visit such an iconic place, where they have transformed it into a really good museum.
I didn’t realise how good it was until we visited it, really captures the horrors of the prison. Some of the torture techniques were grizzly.

This is a must visit in Hanoi and I was glad to have read the suggestion to get the audio tour. It was informative and interesting. As many others have noted it is very much from the Vietnamese perspective. It is their right to tell their story. It is good to have this in mind as you listen to the stories. There is information about the construction of the prison, use by the French to house political prisoners and then by the Vietnamese to house American pilots.

This is a must when in Hanoi it is  so so interesting .I read a review before we went and they said to do the Audio tour and they were not wrong makes the whole place come to life in your head would recommend at a drop of a hat as we had been to Saigon first it gives a great perspective on the North & South  views

The audio guide may seem expensive (compared to the ticket price) but is well worth the money.  The introductions to each part of the prison is clear and informative whilst being complimented with personal accounts of inmates.   It is a pity that barely 4/10ths of the prison remain -- how the rest "disappeared" under the neighbouring hotel remains a mystery!
Very interesting museum, describing the history of the war in great detail. Audio guide was very worthwhile otherwise it would have been difficult to follow.
Our tour of the Hoa Lo Prison was a deeply impacting experience. We gained understanding of Vietnam's history and the beautiful people of this country.

This was so worth visiting to witness and see the oppression by French Colonists and the atrocities committed by the Americans. One can see and visualise what the wonderful, kind Vietnamese people have had to endure and yet have emerged as a united nation moving forward and without any ongoing adverse feelings towards either of the latter , with a forward positive mindset .One can see and hear the narrative  of the cells , oppression and suffering , yet see the positives whereby American ex soldiers have met and seen their previous victims, captors and indeed saviours . One can see how Senior John McCain was recused from Truc Bach  lake , and although imprisoned and tortured , came to meet his rescuers and ended up making friends and ended up being a benefactor for the Vietnamese people

When you consider all the horrors that went on here during the French colonial period, discussed in great detail in the audioguide, it really starts to sink in how determined the Vietnamese people have been in their struggle for freedom and independence. Definitely get the audioguide, btw, because without it you miss a lot. Unfortunately the struggle had to continue against the Americans, some of whom served time within its walls after being shot down while bombing their cities, and there's an exhibit on that as well. Americans tend to complain that the information presented here is biased. No doubt all countries try to put themselves in a favorable light. How well or badly the Americans were treated, is in the eye of the beholder, but some facts remain facts. American planes were bombing Vietnamese cities, not the other way around. Vietnamese civilians were getting torn to shreds by American bombs, not the other way around. Take the exhibit on John McCain. McCain wound up here because he was bombing civilian infrastructure -whether a power plant or a light bulb factory. For this act, a group of Vietnamese civilians pulled him out of a lake after his plane was shot down, saving his life at the risk of their own. There's a moving photo of that event on display. Many of the Vietnamese who were here under colonial occupation, were here for much less, and received a fate that was much worse.

Walked from Military History Museum to Hoa Lo Prison (Entry 30KVND Open 8AM - 5PM). Very interesting to learn the Prison's history. And the significance of the location.BTW - there is a VP Bank No Fee ATM nearby. Walked back to my hotel (Hanoi Flora).

The audio tour was very impressive. Hỏa Lò was originally built to serve as a prison under French rule. When the Americans invaded, the North Vietnamese established a special section specifically for prisoners of war. The American soldiers sarcastically called the prison 'Hanoi Hilton'. The American prisoners were mostly Air Force pilots, shot down and retrieved from the jungle.

Well worth a visit. We did the audio tour with our 8 and 10 yr old kids. They got through 2/3 before getting bored which was pretty good going.Highly informative and interesting tour. A great way to spend an hour or two learning about Vietnam’s history.We took cyclos there. If coming from the old town there is a big and very busy road to cross and we wouldn’t have fancied doing that as a pedestrian.

It's a place you should visit to relive a historical era. I truly admire the willpower of the politicians. In fact, their unwavering determination stems from their belief that justice is on their side and their faith in victory.
Just spent a couple of hours here which is enough time. Great place to go to find out the history of the prison from when the French ruled it to the time it was the Hanoi Hilton
Worth visiting and spending some time here. The condition of the prison and the prisoners who lived there during their imprionment is well depicted. The entrance fee is also very reasonable
Glad we’ve seen this place! So much history and so sad. I was shocked actually how Vietnamese prisoners were kept. Very awful conditions! So cruel.
It is part of Vietnam's history and is one of those places to visit to understand how much the Vietnamese people had to suffer to gain their freedom.
Very good place to visit in Hanoi to understand about history of Vietnam. It is not so far from Hanoi Old Quarter so we can get a taxi easiy.
A fascinating exploration of how this building became a prison throughout French occupation and the American war. Well worth a visit.
This is a museum that was once a French colonial prison, and later a Vietnamese one, which played a fundamental role in the country's recent history.

Impressive and a must see as long as people understand that 1964 - 1973 period is 100 % propaganda narrative!For example a statement that US prisoners of war had more food than they could eat is obvious disinformation.I took audio guide which is 100 000 VND extra (ticket is 30 000 VND). I recommend to take it. Without you would loose a lot of information at least from the earlier French occupation time

Visited the Hoa Lo Prison during a city tour of Hanoi. Commonly known as Hanoi Hilton. Historic prison where political prisoners were kept during the French colonisation and the American/Vietnam war. Vividly depicts the harsh conditions that Vietnamese political prisoners were kept. I found it interesting and painful. A must visit as it forms a crucial part of Vietnam's history. Well documented and preserved for the next generation.
Here are my recommendations . Hoa Lo museum is intersting if you like history. We had an amazing time in Hanoi

Hoa Lo Prison, also known as the Hanoi Hilton, is a historic prison complex in Hanoi that was used for political prisoners during the French colonial era and later for American prisoners of war during the Vietnam War. Today, it serves as a museum and a reminder of Vietnam's turbulent history.

When visiting the vibrant city of Hanoi it is easy to overlook the history of the country, from French colonisation to the Vietnam War. A visit to the prison explains this well, and vividly depicts what life in the prison was like.

My wife and myself are currently holidaying in Hanoi staying at the excellent Boutique Hotel Lasiesta (see separate Review) and earlier today visited Hoa alo Prison/Museum - better known as the Hanoi Hilton.It is an old property opened by the French but our main interest was to learn more about the US pilots who were held captive there during the Vietnam (or American - depending on who you’re talking to!) War. In this regard, the services of a good guide are a must accompanied by a keen eye for details!All in all, it was a most informative visit and one that I would strongly recommend to any visitors looking to learn more about Hanoi’s history.
Very good place to understand about history of Vietnam. We learn a lot from guide Thang from Vietnam Escape Tours Travel. Thank you so muvh
Must visit during Hanoi visit for little bit of history. A lot to learn about some of the history. Expect to spend around 1 to 2 hours.
Very interesting museum. We learnt a lot. We didn’t get the audio guide and still found it very informative. Highly recommend!
Nice place to understand about history of Vietnam. Very amazing place and we could not understand why people could live there. Had a great time trip arranged by Vietnam Escape Tours.

If you're over the age of 55, you can probably relate to a visit here.  I went early in the day, just as they opened, and it was nice that there were no crowds and I had plenty of time to process all the displays.  When I was leaving, there was a long line waiting to enter the prison.

The exhibits offer really interesting interpretation of this prison, ranging from French colonization to American POWs. Lots of interesting displays on the history of this site from the perspective of Vietnam and how different world events have impacted the local community.

This is a very worthwhile place to spend an hour and understand how the Vietnamese were treated in the colonial times and what drove them in their long  fight for freedom. It’s separated into two conflicts really, the early colonial period and then the US/Vietnam war. This does have a slight propaganda twist to it but nevertheless this all happened and the bombing happened and atrocities happened. every country engages in some propaganda and as I write this Ukrain has been  been invaded by Russia , all repeating itself again. You cannot fail to learn something about Vietnam from this place.

Entry is 30,000 I think from memory, but the key to having the best experience here is by getting the audio guide for 100,000. There are many signs around the prison but the audio guide goes into so much more detail, which really makes the experience much more chilling and real. There are even accounts from former prisoners on the audio guide.We spent around 2 hours here in total although we took our time.This prison is well worth visiting if in Hanoi, highly recommend.
Part of the history of prisoners in Vietnam. I recommend that if you go in a group, even if it's just one person, you rent the translation headphones to understand the stories.

The former French prison is a perfect place to understand more about colonial rule. The informative museum provides an often-graphic overview of French suppression of the local independence movement as well as a platform for patriotic learning. Each prison cell is well-maintained to re-create the horror of inmate life. The part on American POW is also remarkable. Do devote enough time to explore.

I was able to relive historical moments and recall the people who sacrificed themselves without hesitation for the independence and freedom of the Vietnamese nation. I will have to come here many more times.

Spent a couple of hours visiting the old prison in Hanoi Paid to hire headphones with audio description of exhibits which was well worth it and very well doneA really well put together museum explaining how the prison was used first by the French and later by Vietnamese to house prisoners including political prisoners and later captured American airmenWell worth a visit

A former prison now a museum, featuring a variety of rooms and exhibits, recreating the conditions of a Hanoi prison where both Vietnamese men and women were held during French rule, including the guillotine.
A must to see place. Difficult not to be upset after a visit to this historical place but important for a better understanding of Vietnam and its recent past.

An eye opener and must see visit in Hanoi. Very good site to revisit the past and learned from it. So heartbreaking to see the pain of the past. Would recommend to visit this in Hanoi. Only VD 30k or USD1.2 for admission.

A popular attraction kept in good condition. Don't pay much attention to the message that they are trying to convey. I think it's very tongue in cheek, they know, they are just having fun with us tourists especially French and US. See it for the construction, size of cells and how prisoners were kept. Their main message is anti-French. If you want to know what it was really like, buy a book, written overseas.
Very interesting to visit and learn more about the history of the prison over the years. Lots of information provided as well as being able to look in cells. Definitely worth visiting!

This was a great tour. It was sad because so many American pilots were there. There were numerous pictures in a flight suit. They revere John McCain especially since he came back to Vietnam in 96 and re establish relations with America which dramatically improved Vietnamese life.

It was great that the gallery was presented in the way how it was like for the prisoners so that visitors can have an experience of it. The exhibits were well presented with sufficient information in Vietnamese, English and French. Recommended time to be there 90 minutes.

This is really worth a visit. Rent the audio guide. Walk through the rooms and listen to the horrific conditions and torture of what the prisoners went through.  Very sad but you learn about the courage and patriotism of the Vietnamese. At the end of the journey, I felt like waving their flag!
An interesting visit, though as French people our ears were ringing quite a bit (that said, it was an active colonial prison before the war, so a fundamentally brutal and inhospitable place).

Historical landmarks are filled with interesting facts & relics. You will learn a lot about the history & struggles of the people for both French, American & locals. It is an excellent place to spend an hour (or two). Highly recommended! Tickets are required for anyone above 15 years old.Openining hour: All week (8 AM - 5 PM)

The torture and deplorable conditions during the French colonial era are unimaginable. The mannequins of people in shackles convey the suffering they endured, but at the same time, they seem to be an expression of the will to honor and pass on the spirit and pride of those who persevered. It seems that
A real history, we respect much our peace today. The audio guide in many languages can help us to know other stories, well presented.
Even now, I'm still moved when I think back on my journey on the "Sacred Night" tour – exploring Hoa Lo Prison.
Hoa Lo Prison has been renovated and beautifully decorated, making the tour very logical.

It was truly moving; Hoa Lo Prison vividly portrays the brutal history, leaving visitors with a pang of sadness. Each story, each image, seemed to pierce the heart like a knife. I was deeply moved by this visit. Next time, I will definitely return to Hoa Lo and participate in the night tour.

I really did not have much of an idea of what happened in Vietnam with the French. But this prison gives such a clear picture as to what happened all those years ago which when you put into perspective, really was not long ago. The treatment that those prisoners went through to try and get independence was so tragic.Prepare yourself for an emotional experience, it is something which really will hit you. A very good visit and very well explained. You can have a tour guide or you can read all the plaques on the wall which are in English and watch the videos throughout which explain it all perfectly. Fantastic trip and for 30000 Vietnamese dong it's well worth it.

A very sad moment in Vietnamese History, of the events of the war and what went on in Hoa Lo Prison. Arrested US pilots called it the 'Hilton Hanoi'. You can see how prisoners were treated and how they lived in the prison cells. A good morale lesson on why we don't need wars and the why we need the whole world to leave in harmony. The tickets are 30.000 VND for entry. You can also purchase the 'Hao Lo Prison: Historical Relic' publication for just 20.000 VND. The publication contains interesting facts and pictures. There is a souvenir shop in the prison, the souvenir there cost cheaper than compared to official souvenir shops in the city.
For first-time visitors, I recommend the audio guide.
Came here after a tour of Vietnam finished. This was our spare day. Interesting history and sad. Not sure if small children should see this.

The Hoa Lo Prison is a must-see on any Hanoi tour. Although two-thirds of the complex was demolished for redevelopment, the remaining area is still quite interesting. The building, constructed by the French, is architecturally striking. Inmates were never treated well here. The exhibition, however, primarily focuses on the misdeeds of the French builders, while the treatment of later (French, then American) inmates is presented in a rather euphemistic light. This imbalance is just about tolerable, though, and the exhibition, with its artifacts including a guillotine and photographs, certainly makes this "Hanoi Hilton" or "Heartbreak Hotel" worth a visit.

This memorial prison has been well conserved and gives u a glimpse into Vietnam s struggle for freedom under the French and the atrocities suffered by them. Hoa Lu Prison is also called Hilton Prison because during the South/North Vietnam War , American pilots who were captured were prisoned here. But they were not treated as prisoners,rather they lived a comfortable life here,with every facility available to them. U can spend an hour here easily. There’s a lot to see and observe. And a plethora of knowledge about the French Rule and Vietnam s Independence. Some sections are quite disturbing about how the Vietnamese suffered under the French. Entrance is 30,000 VND. Must Visit

Hoa Lo Prison is a testimony to the countless innocent Vietnamese souls who wanted nothing but to lead their lives with independence and respect without any colonial intrusion. The prison cells show the brutality of the colonial administration in the treatment meted out to those who raised their voices against the injustice carried on by the French. A serious visitor could spend hours and end up with tears in their eyes! A must visit when in Hanoi!
Very interesting and impressive museum, showing the different ways in which the prison was used. The section dedicated to peace was a nice touch

Very well organised and discover many things there .   Firts time we saw a real guilotine . Impressive and the way it has been presented is really great . Learnt a lot of things with the exhibition path .

Put it on the list of things to do whilst Hanoi. We didn’t partake in the audio guide and it took us 1-2Hours to get round the museum. There are plenty of boards explaining the history behind the museum and the exhibits. You are allowed free roam on the prison & the cells and it’s both interesting and thought provoking. There are stories of some of the people held at the prison and it has been presented in a way that keeps your attention and makes you wanted to read it all.We really enjoyed ourself at for 30,000 VND it’s definitely worth a look it’s also in walking distance to the town so easy to get to during a day tour of Hanoi.

Probably more known by westerners as the POW camp for the American airmen during the Vietnam war but this is much more. This tells of the rough times that the Vietnamese people had to endure before that period. Well worth the visit
The former prison, which has now been largely demolished, now houses a museum.
Visiting the prison was one part of a three week trip. Guide was great and informative. Thanks Linh for putting together a great trip.
A very chilling place to walk around and see and read how the Vietnamese people have put up with many hardships, especially during the French time.
Fascinating and confronting experience of a very cruel past. Insight into the harsh conditions for prisoners male and female. Stories of escape was incredible.

We spent a very informative morning visiting the Hoa Lo prison, dubbed the Hanoi Hilton by the American POWs who were imprisoned there during the Vietnam war. The prison is an easy place to visit, the flow of the exhibits is very good, and it is a bit of an eye opener when you realise that although there was a lot of criticism aimed at Vietnam for there I’ll treatment of some prisoners, they suffered just as badly under the French regime when the prison was called Maison Centrale. Well worth a visit if you are in Hanoi.
Great tour and a little confronting in some parts. Get the Audio headphones. Loads of information and stories from inmates. Well worth it to visit.

We had a free day , and as it was raining I looked on trip advisor to see if there was something close by our hotel to fill in a couple of  hours, which is when I came across Hoa Lo Prison... it was about a 10 minute walk but once inside you actually feel the oppression these poor prisoners must  have suffered, the absolute inhumane conditions in which they were held  and in many cases lost their lives is felt as you walk through and read the way they  treated.   Very sad but I’m glad I had the experience

Written above the entry to Hoa Lo Prison are the words: "Maison Centrale", or Central House, however Vietnamese prisoners referred to it as "the Monster's Mouth". This is but the small southern section of the remaining prison more commonly referred to as the Hanoi Hilton by American POW's (prisoners of war), during the Vietnam War.  In 1990, the missing portion of the prison was demolished to make way for the Hanoi Towers hotel and offices. Built by the French Colonialists in 1886-1901, the most striking sense of brutality are the scenes and literature revealing torment and torture of the Vietnamese by the French Government. The period of American captivity is "whitewashed", when the Vietnamese were in control of the prison. The visitor will feel humbled, upset and confused; nonetheless, I highly recommend a tour of this historic prison.

An informative history lesson. Everything is well-described in English. The audio guide is very interesting, featuring recorded stories from real-life characters, but unfortunately, the equipment is disappointing, interrupting frequently. There are no graphic photos, so it's suitable for visiting with children.

I am so thankful the city left part of this prison standing so visitors can witness a piece of History. This exhibit is very well done and contains much information. The recorded walking tour is excellent. So thankful I visited and step back in time to feel and see what the prisoners experienced in this place.
Recommend this prison museum for introduction to neo colonial and 20th century Vietnamese history. Well curated and visual.
Eventhough the visit to the prison was a heavy afternoon, it's an amazing piece of history.  The documentation was was very well done with many photos as well.  Plan for about two hours to see all.
The history of the Vietnam War. A must-see museum in Hanoi. Interesting and realistic. It leaves a lasting impression.
Here you will see the prison where American pilots shot down in North Vietnam were held during the escalating US war in the North from 1965 to 1973. It's known as the "Hinton Hotel" of Vietnam.
Definitely a place you should stop in Hanoi.....doesn’t take long, but very impactful.  Gives a real view of what ‘colonialism’ really meant to many in VietNam.
This is a good tour offering some interesting insight into the history of the Hanoi Hilton.I thought the audio tour was well worth the extra couple of pounds to get a full understanding.

We asked our tour guide to put this on our itinerary, and we are so glad we did. It’s not just about the Americans that were incarcerated here, it’s so much more than that. Please make sure that you get the audio guide at the start of the tour, as this explains things that you will miss without it. Allow yourself around 2 hours for the tour. Please be aware that it was a prison for political prisoners and some of the pictures and audio descriptions are not for the squeamish.
Former prison in the centre oh Hanoi built by the french when Vietnam was a French colony, later used during the Vietnam war to captured American pilots well worth a visit .

This prison visit truly give me a deep impression about how the Vietnamese people suffered during the French period & wars. So sad how the prisoners been tortured in the cell. The dark spirit in the prison was devastated for freedom and new life! This is how I learnt more about Vietnam history! No more war!! Freedom & Peace for Vietnam!!

We have been here twice. We went too late the first time and had to leave before going upstairs. It was so interesting and we went back today. I may have overthought it all but it took me 4 to 5 hours total. I wouldn’t have missed the Hanoi Hilton. This historical place was top on our trip to Hanoi.

I would recommend getting the headphones, which cost 50,000VND per person, as the commentary is really informative. 30,000VND per person entry fee, which is very good value for money considering, what you get to see.

A must go to when in Hanoi. Really had my eyes open to the history of Vietnam the occupation of the French and of course the American war. Being a child of the 70’s and growing up with the Vietnam war on the news daily I found this place fascinating, although strange to say a really enjoyable visit.
A must see place, interesting to get another perspective of the war!  Horror that people endured!  It was an eye opening experience,!  The details of the cell and how they were kept in irons.

This museum really did a great job telling the story of this prison during the French colonial times and the horrors that took place. It is worth the trip and is located near many different other areas of note, such as St. Joseph cathedral and the women's history museum. So create a nap and spend the day exploring.
This cozy establishment is located in the very center of Hanoi. These guys had interesting living conditions. It's worth a visit.
A sobering reminder of the past, however it’s essential to visit. I didn’t feel that the stories portrayed a balanced reflection of the history behind the war though
Exciting set up at the museum, recommend taking a headset, costs only 50,000 VND. They have done a good job of bringing to life how the prison worked.
This is a must see... the conditions, the treatment and the fortitude of these men.... you have to see the deplorable conditions and the limited amount of freedom they had
This prison, built by the French, reveals the places, tools, and methods used to torture the poor Vietnamese in the most horrific ways, and how this people suffered at the hands of these criminals.
Please note: you cannot enter in shorts. Lots of regulations to be aware of.   You must use the gate otherwise security will grab you.

While the place is great, on the entrance at security gate they confiscate your lighters and don’t want to give it back once you want to leave the place. I understand the respect and preventing people from smoking inside. However I don’t understand not returning them and I find it as just being mean.

Unprofessional and abusive security  After nine weeks of travelling around Vietnam, this was easily my most negative experience.  At the baggage holding area, a security guard found my electronic gimbal in my bag. Without explaining anything to me or my Vietnamese partner, she grabbed it and examined it like I’d smuggled in a medieval weapon. I tried to calmly show her what it was, at which point she slapped my hand away. Only after that did she tell my Vietnamese partner, in Vietnamese, that it couldn’t come in.  She then placed it on an open shelf, within arm’s reach of other visitors. It didn’t feel secure at all, but we continued inside anyway, trying to ignore how unprofessional that moment was.  When we returned to collect our belongings, things somehow got worse. The man handing items back was completely disorganised, picking things up and handing them to whoever reached out first. On more than one occasion, he gave the wrong item to the wrong person, then took it back and put it back on the shelf. Watching this unfold, it was very clear that my gimbal could easily have disappeared, which was exactly why I didn’t want to hand it over in the first place.  After this experience, I checked the Google reviews of this place and was not surprised to see many people describing the exact same behaviour, the same rudeness, and the same lack of care for visitors’ belongings. Clearly, no one working here has learned anything from years of public feedback. Frankly, people who treat visitors this way should not be in public-facing roles at a site of this significance.  PS: After being so concerned about losing my gimbal, two hours later it dropped into a lake while paddling around on swan boats. The difference is that at least now it’s securely stored, unlike earlier when it was left sitting on an open shelf for anyone to take.

I had a very disappointing experience with the staff responsible for handling personal belongings. The employee displayed an unacceptable attitude, handling my camera and microphone roughly and carelessly, as if they had no value. Additionally, their manner of speaking was extremely rude and harsh, showing a complete lack of respect and professionalism. Such treatment is not what I expected, and it certainly left a negative impression.

We found out the hard way that no babies are allowed in the masoleum. Not something I would've thought to check prior to visiting because the policy doesn't make any sense. Our baby was sitting peacefully in a sling, and arguably a more respectful guest than the hoards of noisy school children. As a great believer in the youth, I don't think Uncle Ho would have approved of any of this.

We got there at 10:45 am and were denied entry. Guards don't speak any English at all. One was able to explain through Google translate that this particular day they closed at 11 instead of 11:30. On and btw people wearing shorts above the kneecap are not allowed in.

Vietnam itself is a country of contrast. Same can be said about the people. Some are the most warm others hostile and constantly trying to rip you off. Buddhists would be ashamed. That being said i was refused entry because i had a JBL speaker in my backpack. I was rudely told to leave. Listen guys and listen well. If you want people to visit your country your attitude has to change. Im not going to get political but Nha Trang a good example of why i wouldnt visit again. Blue and Yellow all the way my friends

Not worth going, Ho Chi Minh didn't even want to be there himself (he wished to be cremated). It's just a communist looking building that's almost never open for entry anyway.  ♿ Accessibility: The security guards won't let you in even on the outside grounds if you have an electric wheelchair, because they are scared of electricity apparently

dress code very strict only to adults. as long as u can pull your shorts down till below your knee u r allowed in. kids can wear shorts and still allowed in. did not manage to go in in the end because one of us was wearing shorts.

The Mausoleum management was very rude, impolite, and unprofessional, especially towards foreigners, failing to inform them of any rules. They then rudely and offensively snatched away their microphones and earphones. One baggage checker even insulted a foreign visitor, calling them a "crazy woman" to their face!?!? The queuing system was chaotic and disorganized, resulting in queues stretching for kilometers, with people pushing and shoving.

Could not visit as the opening hours mentioned here are wrong. You can only visit the mausoleum between 8 to 11am if you wish to see body of president Ho Chi Minh. Staff is pretty rude and would stop entry from 4pm onwards in the premises. Any sleeveless or above knees dress is not allowed. You need to rent sarong/skirt/shirt from hawkers outside. There are limited entry/exit points, thus one has to do a long long walk, whole of the perimeter. Overall, not worth the pain, money and sweat.

We didnt have issues with dress code or belongings but they didn’t allow kids under 2 (the rule is, they should be able to walk). We asked for explanations for the rule, but they said to go ask the policymakers (??). Incredibly rude staff, which is unfortunate for such a significant historical site. Interesting to see Ho Chi Minh though. Free entry.
They try to take your lighter away for some unknown reason... so if you want to get in be prepared to say goodbye to your lighter. Paranoid much?
In Google Maps it says it's open 07:30-11:30. However,  we had to WAIT until it opened at 11:30.

Very disappointed with the way the mausoleum management board works. The One Pillar Pagoda area and the toilet area behind the mausoleum are very dirty. Construction waste is not collected. Many large broken flower pots are still piled up around the old trees. The soldiers guarding the mausoleum are joking and swearing (at the gate from the stilt house). In the museum, they sell ready-to-eat food, canned drinks and there are no trash cans. At the museum exit, there is a stall selling green bean cakes (unrelated to the museum's content). In addition, I also want to contribute comments about the information about the mausoleum's opening and maintenance dates that have not been widely announced, causing many tourists from far away to visit but because they do not know the schedule, they cannot visit the mausoleum => an app should be created to provide information about the mausoleum for visitors to easily grasp information about the schedule, map, etc. In general, I visited the mausoleum 20 years ago and 20 years later it is still the same. No improvement, no change. The garbage is still the same. So boring, luckily I was still able to take a few pictures.

Worst place to visit and learn the history of Ho Chi Minh. The staff there are horrible, mostly the ones thinking they’re the “force” and only show interest for Vietnamese-looking people. Regret paying to enter. Whoever thinks to go, please, think twice.

I had a very disappointing experience trying to enter the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. The staff at the entrance seemed to apply the dress code policy inconsistently; I was denied entry while other visitors wearing similar attire were allowed in. The communication was difficult, and I felt the guards were disrespectful.  This was a surprising and unfortunate contrast to the incredibly warm and welcoming hospitality I have experienced throughout the rest of Vietnam. It's a shame that such a historically significant site is managed in a way that can make visitors feel so unwelcome.
Points deducted by rude unreasonable local police, yelling at foreigners & tourists who do not speak Vietnamese. Unforgettable bad experience. You should change a job if u dislike your life
A disgrace to Ho Chi Minh who wanted to be cremated and his ashes spread across Vietnam. The fake body doesn’t even look like him.

Basically I couldn’t see Ho chi Ming’s body at all. The staff told me that it’s only open for visit on August (and it was June at the time I visited). There’s nothing special at all. And the museum charges and extra VND40000 to visit.

The guards are rude and can become friendly when you ask them simple questions for directions. Not friendly to Indian tourists. Love from India 🇮🇳 ... and they were usually coughing around me as if I were some sort of disgusting human being.  The guards are rude and can become friendly when you ask them simple questions. Not friendly to Indian tourists. Love from India 🇮🇳

Security on a power trip; even items that are not listed among the prohibited items list prevent you from entering. In my case it was binoculars, which cannot be found anywhere on the list, but they would not let me in because of them. Of course they also don’t let you deposit them etc.  All of that might still be understandable if the personnel would not be so super rude and ill-mannered. Leaving a permanent stain on my image of Vietnam and as other reviews seem to suggest, I am not the only one.

Staff were very rude to me and yell at many other tourists when they couldn’t come in wearing short (that’s higher than their knees) or dress. At least provide some free skirt to cover our skin. I wasted taxi money to come here and they told me I have to buy a skirt to cover my leg. it is not worth it. I already wasted taxi money, and I’m not spending money on the skirt just to come in. They wouldn’t even let you in on the outside even if you are wearing short or dress.

Crazy snaking queue to even enter during my visit there on 20Aug. Walked on the outskirts of the place then saw the queue was insanely long. Probably around 2km.  Don’t wanna waste time to queue for so long.
You cannot wear shorts to go in. It's 32°C in Hanoi, I'm sweating a lot, and in order to enter you need to wear pants. Makes no sense.

I am not sure why would the security check asking me to pay 150,000 for the skirt to cover my knees temporarily while the ticket is only 40,000. I can totally understand that the rule and expecting to pay for it but the price for a 15 minutes at museum did not make any sense.

As soon as I entered the security check area on Hung Vuong Street, I wanted to turn back immediately, even though my first destination in Hanoi, coming from Ho Chi Minh City, was the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. The attitude of the two female security officers in bright white shirts on June 21, 2025, was extremely impolite; they only knew how to yell instead of guiding people to the appropriate area to get their trays. It was a very negative experience.

Staff were extremely rude , and I find the dress code rule very stupid . Lots of tourists were really mad when they couldn't come in wearing dress or short (that's above their knees) . They wasted their taxi money to come here just to not come here because theyre wearing short or dress ? And also, it is very hot during summer. You can't even enter the outside if you are wearing a short or dress too. I find dress code to men or women very stupid . I get it that dress code is fine when it come to profanity or inappropriate image . It's literally just skin, we were born with skin just to cover some part that's not necessary to cover. That's how we were born!!!

DISCRIMINATION! If you are wearing shorts you can’t even walk in a park in front of mausoleum Of course men in shorts were jogging inside with same length And of course in pajama is ok Very hospitable thanks!!

Such a double standard place, they let some people with short pants in but don’t let us in. The staffs are not kind enough. Worst experience in Vietnam from north to south ever. The entrance is not clear we walked around for about 15mins to find it and then just got banned from visit.
Customs officers' arrogant attitude and unfair treatment of foreigners and Vietnamese

Today I came to film to get material for my English homework. I know that the management is just a salaried employee. But they kept beeping and shouting even though everyone was moving. I told them not to beep anymore and everyone was moving. Then they turned around and asked if they liked the idea. :))))))

Meeting the security check guide who talks like a father of the world, Uncle Ho taught you to talk to people like that.  I know it's just a minority but I hope the management comrades pay more attention.

They really need to update their hours and days. I went today Friday 2/2 in the morning and I was told that the mausoleum is closed and won’t open until tomorrow (apparently they close on Mondays & Fridays). No signs or anything.

Visited on the morning of  July 9th 25 Arrive at mausoleum and told a tank top singlet was inappropriate and shirt we to short  normal length walking shorts!! Was then sold a strong to cover me up for $100000 dong which didn’t fit so had to buy a second smaller one for $50000dg Planned rip off as there had been no recommendation to wear tee shirts and long pants that I’d seen Went to see the testing place of Ho Chi Minh in state and its closed for renovations for 2 months no signage !! Very disappointing tour

IMPORTANT NOTE: ENTRY = EXIT, YOU CANT EXIT ANYWHERE ELSE One of the Worst and rudest experiences so far in vietnam. We finished the tour in the park and we were only 5 meters away from the grab car but we weren’t allowed to access it on the road even tho we showed them the car. I dont understand the problem for the people to leave the area! We had to walk the whole way back to the entry!! We dont understand at all!!! Makes no sense

Would give zero stars if possible. Blatant discrimination against particular ethnic groups upon entry. My partner is Indian and I am Caucasian, upon arrival we were not aware of dress code, which we could understand….However Caucasian groups were let in wearing singlets and same length shorts as us!!! During the space of 5 minutes 2 other ethnic groups (brown) wearing more cover than white people were let in in-front of us. We tried to ask the man at the front desk who dismissed us and gave us the same BS excuse that out shorts were “too short”, but clearly same rules don’t apply for other Caucasians or other groups.  Really disappointed and quite disgusted about the blatant racism.

Extremely rude security guards using power play just because they can. One man was so awful to us. They confiscated my expensive and sentimental Thai lighter on arrival (understandable as lighters aren't allowed on the premisis), but when asked if I could collect it after visiting, they said no. We were told to either leave, or throw it away. They also made me put my film for my camera through the X-ray which I told them could damage it. Not happy with the way they treated the visitors here.  All i'm going to say is Ho Chi Minh wouldn't have been happy with that🤷‍♀️

Very frustrating experience due to lack of information on what is open & when. Went early afternoon on Thursday & Mausoleum & museum closed but palace open. This was not displayed on their website, google or entry gate. Complete waste of time to enter as may as well look at mausoleum from across the street. Once into this large area can only back track & leave be entry area. Really can’t enjoy anywhere in most surrounding areas due to high security where police keep telling you no entry
rude staff, while he said close at 11:00 am but the time I arrived was 10:45

This place itself is good.But the security check staffs r so rude and lack of education and quality. I have a small flashlight they said no entry, then I gave it to staffs,one said to me go out quickly! I asked why? She said go out now! I just go inside and said plz stop your rude behavior! This is the worst experience in Vietnam so far.

Not really worth a visit. Firstly it open only up to 11.30am. The Q on Sunday was 45mins. You cannot bring any food and drinks not electronics so have to leave at counter. Then you walk with the rest in single file, and just catch a glimpse of HCM for less than a min as they rush you through. No photo taking allowed. Really don’t see the point as a foreigner to do this….

The site itself is nice, it's a quiet little park area, and the soldiers all around the perimeter were super friendly and nice, the security workers who run the x-ray, however, are horrible. Not only were they super rude and overly hostile for no reason, but they stole my property and when I confronted them about it they told me to get lost. Luckily it wasn't valuable, but still it's crazy that such things could happen from government employees. I still recommend going to check the place out, just be prepared.

Security at the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum was unfriendly. During security check we had to surrender a small commemorative Vietnamese flag purchased outside, while local visitors were allowed to keep theirs. Our flag was not returned afterwards (even though we requested it and it was just laying there).

We came to Vietnam from Greece, all the way around the globe, we tried to get inside and we had our film cameras in our bags since we're walking around all day and they didn't allow us to come in. We asked them if we can leave the cameras outside and get them when we leave and they said no, everyone of them was super rude and their behaviour was terrible. Also there is no info of film cameras not allowed with you. Terrible experience

This morning, August 26, 2024, I visited Uncle Ho's Mausoleum and received an extremely unpleasant feeling. The security guard at the Hung Vuong gate (specifically the guy with the thick lips) had an attitude of chasing people straight out. Instead of explaining to the people, the guy showed an attitude. I hope the Mausoleum management board will remind the staff so that people visiting Uncle Ho's Mausoleum have a better experience.

Ridiculous!! You have to cover your knees and shoulders, but how they decide, who is allowed to go inside and whose pants are "too short" is super random and subjective. If they just don't like what you look like you probably are not getting inside with the same clothes as others, who are just passing you. And also the people who are working at the security check are super unfriendly!!! Never experienced something like this in Asia before and I have been travelling around here for 8 months already. One woman just yelled at us because we were about to take a second box to get our bags screened, so definitely no reason for screaming!!!  I personally had the feeling they felt as something better and wanted to show their power just because they were wearing uniforms. Hated it!  And all of this for not seeing ho chi Minhs body in the end, because you are actually not allowed to enter the mausoleum. Definitely not recommend going there!
Staffs were unfriendly. After security check? one guy staff pushed me from behind Also nothing much to do here

Very confusing for tourists. Randomly shutting during the day, random entrances and ‘do not go this way’ signs. No signs or communication. The One Pillar Pagoda was cool but otherwise we couldn’t get into the rest for unknown reasons.

The parking lot is too far from the security gate. Why not add another gate near the parking lot so that visitors traveling by private vehicle can easily access the site? The management should pay more attention to this issue to provide convenience for both visitors and the local community.

This place requires long pants and cover shower clothes, otherwise you need to buy a cover outside which will cost you 5 dollars, maybe people are selling this outside. I feel pretty funny because this is not a Muslim mosque and has to be fully covered.

Absolutely no information given about not being able to wear shorts or T-shirts - we came on our last morning and we were refused entry. No scarfs or anything provided like at other places, just asked to leave the line. Very disappointed and unhappy about the lack of information or awareness given if you don’t visit with a tour guide.

Everything was okay. Except for the lady in the middle room, who was very unpleasant and yelled at guests for not knowing how to put their belongings properly. I had to ask her to lower her voice. I hope the manager will review this. It's unacceptable for a place that's supposed to be dignified to have such staff.

Waiting times can be very long here and the dress code is very strict. If you are vietnamese you most likely will be allowed to enter in short pants. If you are a westerner (not in a guided tour) you most likely won’t be allowed to go in so I recommend wearing long pants and going early If you want to visit this place.

On October 13, 2024, we lined up from early morning, but the instructions let the later groups go first. The service was unprofessional. Waiting felt suffocating in the row of tiled-roof buildings. Extremely disappointed.

The attitude of the security staff at the checkpoint at the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is unacceptable. I politely asked if I could enter with my family, knowing it would be difficult. They refused, and I agreed not to go in. However, my family asked me to hold their umbrella because it had stopped raining, and I was in the line, so I had to go back to ask for it. They thought I was being impolite and trying to cut in line. Excuse me, but if I wanted to cut in line, I wouldn't have politely asked like that. When they finally let me in to get my umbrella, they called out over the microphone, "Ma'am, please stand in line." But then they mocked me in front of thousands of people, saying, "SHE IS A SPECIAL PERSON." I can't understand what they were thinking to say that. As a Hanoi resident, having visited the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum hundreds of times since I was a child, this is the first time I've been spoken to like that by the staff. In short, it's unacceptable, as if I were openly cutting in line.

Disgraceful! Security let tourists in shorts in front of me... and refused me entry because I was wearing shorts. The same rules for everyone ! Instead of letting tourists discover your history, you disgust them ! Don't loose your Time here.
No reason to come here. Especially not with kids

Nothing to see or do. It's just a mausoleum. If you wear shorts they won't let you in, so you gotta buy from the street vendor conveniently located right next to the entrance. Whole thing feels like a scam and waste of time

It was nice to come here, but be careful of proactive taxi drivers outside this place (especially if you are a tourist), the group I was with ended up getting scammed on our taxi fair to our hotel from this location. The two star isn’t about this site but for travellers (non-locals) to be careful of scamming taxi drivers.

Sadly despite researching this attraction on various websites there was no mention of it being closed for maintenance mid June until mid August 2022?. There were   at least 4 guards outside the gate but none spoke enough English to inform the large group of tourists outside why they couldn’t go in. After much more googling I came across an article that explained the problem. There should be better signage and updating of the website. I am hoping to save someone else the trouble I had after walking a half hour there in the heat and humidity. I was very disappointed as it was one of the main attractions I wanted to visit in the city.
Finding the route is too slow.
The mausoleum is closed until further notice due to the works on the body. Don't waste time getting there in July 2025

The doorman was rude to me. He asked me and my friend to cover our shoulders, and after I put on a scarf, he did a gesture to ask me to cover more skins.  I tried to cover more, and he did a gesture to make me tie it up… I didn’t quite get it but still followed his order, but he ignored me and then let a huge line of men enter first. I was so confused…and I finally asked if I was good, but he didn’t say anything at all. I waited for 10 more seconds, and asked again. He finally showed a very subtle nodding.  I really hope it’s not because I am a woman, that he treated me so differently.

The female guard at Hung Vuong gate was extremely rude to guests. Her behavior was uncultured. I don't understand why she was hired to work at a place that is the face of the country. I suggest retraining her. It's too offensive!

Went Monday to find the museum and mausoleum closed so returned Tuesday only to find it xloses for some political event  No notice on the website or at the location Would it kill to inform the public?

10/8/23 at 9am, I went through the baggage control area and the staff yelled at the customer and chased them out. When I asked, they didn't give me an explanation and turned away, ignoring the customer.

Unfortunately my experience here was ruined by the overly pushy security guards who tried rushing you through the park. Considering I had purchased the audio tour and was ushered through without being able to actually experience what I was listening to…

wore a tank top and covered my shoulders with a sweatshirt I have thrown over my shoulders. Was permitted and then saw to Western guys wearing shorts who got in without issues. This is sexist and also why wear overly modest fashion for a „hero“? Happy to do so in a Buddhist temple, but I struggle with a human being deified. Not annoyed that the mausoleum was closed anyways, too much trouble for what?

DISCRIMINATION! We saw Vietnamese men entering with shorts (same length as mine), but when we tried to enter (wife fully covered and me shorts) we weren’t allowed because of my shorts. Really strange.

The no shorts rule applies to whoever the guard decided on the spot apparently. Was refused entry and had to buy pants outside from a lovely lady, but once inside I see many tourists wearing shorts above the knee 🤷‍♂️ …
Not only is the entrance at an odd location, it is also closed at 10.30am on Sunday

We were not allowed to go in in short trousers. And like almost everywhere nobody speaks english. They just let us buy everything for nothing. Very dissapointing.
Great place, but uncle Ho's shrine cannot be entered. No explanation or information is given. According to another review, the shrine is closed for maintenance, re-opening on 8/15/23.

The staff are quite rude. After getting our tickets, I decided to go to the toilet first. I was washing my hands and one of the lady that cleans the toilet started speaking in her language assuming Vietnamese that I couldn’t understand. I tried to dry my hands with the fan because the driers were unplugged. She then came over to me and started screaming at me and indicating for me to leave so I did. I then found a tissue roll outside the door so I took some and the other lady outside that cleans with her also started screaming then she came out and screamed at me more and indicated for me to leave. Extremely rude for a tourist spot if foreigners are not welcome then it shouldn’t be open for them in the first place. We rather spend the 80k entrance fee somewhere else where we would be treated properly. Very disappointing!

This place is extremely sexist!!! They do not let you in if your shorts are not covering your knees. This would be fine if they asked men the same. But men were walking around with short pants!! Staff is extremely rude

Have zero respect for their inconsistencies in their training. Please don't make same mistake and just wear long pants if possible.

Came heren, it was stil closed. Came back after one hour and suddenly i wasnt dressed properly. My shoulders had to be covered. The attitude of the staff in the luggage room should be reviewed. They shout at people and have inappropriate attitudes. Working at Uncle Ho's mausoleum is the face of the country, not a place for girls to speak in a tone like selling fish at the market.
They don't allow people to take pictures and there's no place to store things. Is Uncle Ho's mausoleum only for Hanoians or something? I don't understand.
If the staff and guards hate their life and tourists that much maybe they should consider a different career.

Very poor exhibition. Seems like they just put everything they had in the exhibition room. No priorities or overview, just more and more details.. assuming visitors would know everything politian. I went out the ho chi Minh without know when he was born, when he actually ruled and when he died... Everything got lost in the massive amount of unimportant details.. not worth it.

the staff were rude it just totally kills your mood and everything becomes terrible. They could’ve talked more humanely, not just shout and gave you an order when you’ve done nothing wrong but just new to the place. just close up if you’re not 100% welcome

The toilets are very dirty and smelly. ALL SOUVENIR STORES SHOULD BE CLOSE UP BECAUSE THEY ARE TOO RUDE AND POLITE TOWARDS CUSTOMERS. SELLING GOODS WITHOUT LETTING CUSTOMERS LOOK AT THE GOODS AND CHASE AWAY CUSTOMERS IF THEY STAND LOOKING AT THE GOODS BUT HAVEN'T BUYED THEM.

The museum itself is nice but the staff is very unfriendly. They dont speak english and dont act nice. We had to leave at once without any warning because the staff had a break.

Very shameful to turn your national hero into a scam. If you want to leave during opening hours of the mausoleum, you can only do so after paying a fee. Of course, this only applies to foreigners. Just charge an entrance fee or leave people alone. Rude and unprofessional staff
Seen better changing of the guards routines... But a 2 hour line to shuffle through with 20000 others in order see a dead guy who specifically said in his will he didn’t want to be put on display?

Please update opening hours as it was closed when we arrived, and a couple more signs outside to lead the way where to go would be really helpful.
Not worth visiting at all unless you have a lot of time and energy to waste

This place on Saturday is really bad.Too many people and have many people cut in to the line not follow the rules.The soldier do nothing with that people.Some solder help Vietnamese people to cut in to the line. It's worst place in hanoi.

not welcoming of the tourists. Skip it

This was a pretty frustrating experience. The queue was long and slow, but what really stood out was the staff. They were quite rude and very pushy, constantly rushing people along without much respect. When I finally got inside, it didn’t get any better. You barely have a second to look around before being hurried through. The whole thing felt disorganized and stressful, not something I’d want to do again.

The security guards were extremely rude and ill-mannered. They said I couldn't bring water in. Since my bottle was reusable, I drank all the water and the bottle was empty. Even so, a stupid employee wouldn't let me in with the empty bottle. She wanted to confiscate it. I left. Not to mention they closed before 11 am (they don't have a set schedule for closing time). Worst experience in Vietnam.

You can see it from the outside. They don't let you in wearing shorts, which is normal, and you also can't bring in tripods even if you're not going to use them and they're small like this one. Very rude; the security guard pushed me back for having the tripod in my backpack. There's no entrance fee.

Criticism and Advice:  I strongly advise against visiting for several reasons.  Firstly, the layout of the space around the Mausoleum is extremely poor, reminiscent of the Soviet era. It's impossible to tell where the active entrance is and where it's closed. The area is vast, and visitors are forced to wander pointlessly back and forth.
