Secondly, there are strange and absurd restrictions. Visitors are not allowed to enter the site with bare shoulders or legs—requirements that seem downright archaic. These restrictions also apply to technology: it's recommended to minimize the number of items you bring (you won't be allowed in with laptops, drones, tripods, and other equipment), as the security check at the entrance is excessively strict. The reasons for these measures are not explained, and one gets the impression that they are more of a ostentatious control measure, in the spirit of cheap communist pathos.  It seems as if the main goal is not to ensure the comfort of visitors, but to demonstrate the sheer number of people eager to see the lifeless body of their leader. Meanwhile, if access were more open—for example, even just for half a day—the lines would simply disappear.  Good luck to those who decide to go.  P.S. For reference: the entrance to the Mausoleum is in the south, near the Trueong Legend Café. Access is available from 7:00 AM to 10:15 AM. First, go through the security gate, then join the waiting line.  The Mausoleum is closed on Mondays and Fridays. Information on Sundays is conflicting: check in advance, as there are reports that it may be closed on Sundays as well. Visiting on weekends and holidays is highly discouraged. On these days, crowds of schoolchildren and even kindergarten children flock to the site.

I came here to see Ho Chi Minh City, but I didn't see him even though I came on a weekday morning. They confiscated all lighters at the entrance, and when I asked for them upon leaving, they wouldn't return them. There's no shade at all. Be sure to bring an umbrella to protect yourself from the sun.

Very beautiful exterior, but to enter the mausoleum, the times indicated are wrong. In the afternoon, you cannot visit the mausoleum (only the palace and its gardens and that costs 40,000 dong). In addition, regarding the location of the entrance, we are made to go around in circles all around the huge exterior enclosure: we arrive at the right place (ticket office) which sends us 700 m further, to be turned away by the soldiers, who send us elsewhere, then other soldiers send us back to the first place. Horrible, we give up. Advice: come at 7:00 a.m., for the 7:30 a.m. opening to avoid a huge crowd.
After arriving, I was told by the staff that I could not enter and was discriminated against tourists.

Run away. They don't speak well and have a sullen face. I have a Swiss Army knife in my pocket, but I can't enter. I asked if I could leave the knife there, but I couldn't. In the end, I couldn't enter. There are too many stupid rules. I don't know if it's a soldier or a guard, but their manners are very bad.
The people at the entrance were so shameless. They got angry at me for having a lighter in my pocket because I was a communist, and I ended up fighting with them by patted their pockets.
The biggest lie is that it's open until 11:30. We were there at 10:45 and couldn't get in, even though there was no queue.  Just write the correct opening hours then.

You'll definitely want to come here. But then know that you won’t be allowed in with your shoulders or knees exposed, and in a rude manner. You can't leave your bike anywhere nearby. Nobody! doesn’t speak English, and they won’t want to wait for your translator either. You cannot stand next to a gate or fence for more than 2 seconds. Complete disappointment, and more likely in culture.

There's a very strict security check; you must be fully clothed (cover your shoulders and knees). It's best to leave anything in your pockets somewhere, as you won't be allowed in. There are long lines at the entrance. The mausoleum itself is open for a couple of hours at most, and there's not much to do on the grounds. If you want to see the mausoleum, it's better to go to Moscow; the atmosphere is much nicer.
There is a security check at the entrance. Lighters will be confiscated, so smokers should be careful. There is nothing particularly interesting to see.

This monument was included in our tour of Hanoi. When we arrived, there was already a very long, snaking line, made up of not only tourists but also locals, but also dozens of schools that come as part of the indoctrination of the country's youth. We waited an hour and a half in line (with a break because our guide used our senior status as an excuse). They confiscated your camera and forbade you from taking photos with your phone while you were in line. At the entrance to the mausoleum, a soldier forced my friend to keep her arms close to her body. And then a few seconds to see a mummy that was responsible for the deaths of thousands of people for its ideology. Not worth it. Perhaps a stroll through the gardens in the afternoon, no more.

Don't waste your time! Everything costs money, and you must be covered from shoulders to knees (men and women, no exceptions), or you won't get through. They force you to buy skirts (even for men) to hide your knees. Swim shorts (even if they're well-dressed) are forbidden. Don't go. You can't even see the mausoleum.

The entrance was extremely difficult to find, with no clear signs and numerous restrictions and requirements. I never imagined such a landmark would be so disappointing. The staff checking your belongings didn't speak English well; if they didn't understand English, they would simply tell you to leave. If you want to get in, insist on communicating with a staff member who speaks English.

You used to be able to come to the square and just stroll around at any time, but now things have gotten worse, and it's unclear why they did it this way. There's a check of your belongings and a dress code at the entrance. Moreover, the attitude towards tourists is truly rude, unlike anywhere else in Vietnam; we've truly never encountered anything like this. No one explains anything, they just tell you to get lost with sneers. We weren't allowed in shorts just above the knees, and then some vendors ran up and offered to sell us a cape for 200,000 dong. But as we later discovered, locals are allowed in without any problem in much shorter shorts and skirts. Finally, there used to be storage lockers, but here at security, we were turned away because we had a penknife in our first aid kit. Again, all this is done in a rude manner: "Turn around and get out of here," and when we asked if there was a storage locker (there used to be one), they simply waved us off, "Get out of here," and that's it.

Pure discrimination. They let Asians in wearing shorts, but white people are given a hard time. They tried everything to cover us up, but nothing was good enough. They literally laughed in our faces. Once the guard let us in, we were stopped at the security check because I was wearing a rain poncho?? A very strange vibe, I don't recommend it. They clearly only want to show power here; it's sickening!

One might think the guards at the entrance are a bit racist; they let Asians in wearing shorts but give everyone else trouble. Also, the website says the mausoleum closes at 5:00 PM when it actually closes at 4:00 PM, so we couldn't get inside.
If you're wearing shorts, you can't get in, not even if you put a scarf around your legs... crazy! They obviously don't speak English, so you can't even understand what they want exactly.

The mausoleum can only be visited from 8 a.m. to 11 a.m., a fact only stated at the ticket office. The entire complex is open longer, however. We only wanted to visit the mausoleum, but it was closed because we arrived at 3 p.m. Apparently, shorts are not allowed for men. We were asked to leave, changed our trousers, but once we were on the grounds, there were many tourists wearing shorts. If this rule is enforced, then everyone should be required to wear long trousers!
Terrible experience. Not because of the place, but because of the people working there. They treat you poorly; they don't even try to make you understand them. They ignore you. Terrible.

A woman in a knee-length dress must cover her calves, but a man in mid-thigh shorts isn't a problem. Besides, the mausoleum was closed. Don't bother, just look from a distance and continue on your way.

We were refused entry because we had a small pair of binoculars in our bag... There were no signs indicating prohibited items! An endless queue only to be turned away for something so trivial. People with cameras and lenses get in without any problem; it makes no sense. Binoculars are even allowed on the plane. There was no baggage check offered, and the staff were extremely rude, yelling at us when we politely asked if they had a solution. A very bad experience.
Remember to cover your arms and legs; we can't get in otherwise.

If you want to be treated with rudeness, this is the place for you! You can even skip the mausoleum and just go through the square with the mausoleum—you'll get treated rudely there too. My route to the shopping center led through the square, where I was rudely turned away without any explanation, only told to leave, even though people were walking around peacefully (not that it was closed). When I asked, "How do I get through? Which way?" they simply ignored me. Only at the end did an employee deign to tell me I couldn't because I was wearing shorts. You can't even just walk THROUGH this square to do your business in shorts. It's very strange. Ask your employees to be polite and explain the reason, not just rudely kick you out!
Access is closed to tourists, and the guards' willingness to help them is completely lacking. They're too busy watching TikTok on their phones; keep it up. Shameful.

Worst museum experience ever. Women must cover their shoulders. Shorts are prohibited. Security guards are aggressive. We waited 20 minutes to get in, supposedly for free. We were barely inside when we were told the museum charged admission. We entered at 11:40 AM only to be asked to leave at 11:55 AM because the museum closes at noon.

Discriminatory guards. We come dressed in long-sleeved shirts and knee-length shorts. The guard says we are not allowed in because of the shorts. At the same time, we see lots of people walking around with shorts and t-shirts inside and who are let in while we are sent away. Bad, unprofessional and a pure disappointment.

The security guards are extremely rude and incompetent. They denied us entry for having bare shoulders, so we went outside, put on a piece of clothing that covered our shoulders, and tried again. The second time, they denied us entry for carrying a camera tripod. We left it outside and tried again, and they denied us entry again because we weren't wearing LONG SLEEVES!!  I highly do not recommend this visit.

The staff's attitude was horrible. I waited for at least 40 minutes! I went through security with my monocular, but the female security officer sternly told me I couldn't enter without giving any reason. She turned away and refused to communicate. There was no storage nearby. Even Egypt in Africa is better than Vietnam! I can't believe this is a capital city. I tried to ask if there were lockers, but the security officer just pushed me out! The bureaucratic inaction made me sick. Ho Chi Minh City is much better than Hanoi! I'll never go back to Hanoi again. If Ho Chi Minh knew, he'd probably jump out of his coffin.
The deceased's will was not fulfilled.

Very crowded, hot, and stagnant for a long time before being herded through the mausoleum by the staff. Afterwards, you enter the park where only foreigners have to pay admission. Beyond the park is the museum, where a second entrance fee is charged. There is no information about the entire site or any background information available until then.

The queue on Saturdays is 45 minutes long. There's a different dress code for men and women. It's best to arrive empty-handed; cameras must be deposited, phones are not, but photos are prohibited anyway. It's best not to risk taking a photo inside; the soldiers are very serious. Inside, in the semi-darkness and silence, you can see Ho Chi Minh. School trips, ordinary people, and plenty of ordinary Vietnamese still religiously worship the man who was supposed to bring about the unification of the country, but instead delivered a disgraced communism, re-education camps, lack of freedom, corruption, and poverty. Besides Ho Chi Minh, styled after Lenin, you can also admire one of the former palaces and two workers' houses where he lived, a park, and the One Pillar Pagoda. Clean, tidy, with restrooms and souvenir shops.

Extremely unfriendly security personnel! It was raining, and I was wearing my long black poncho. During the security check, I had to take it off and wore shorts underneath, and they immediately yelled at me! I explained that I had long clothes in my backpack and could wear them, but they didn't listen and just kept shouting at me. I've never encountered such unfriendly people anywhere in the world as in Hanoi; it was simply disappointing.

Terrible staff! Security was like being at the airport! I put my handbag, cap, and prescription sunglasses on the conveyor belt... my glasses never reappeared on the other side of security... vanished as if by magic, and I was told to get lost! Strict dress code required even when the mausoleum itself is closed! Not worth the trip, especially to be treated like dirt.

This place sucks.  Every single employee is rude.  There are no warning signs or information boards about prohibited items at security checkpoints or clothing restrictions.  I wonder if they only hire people who don't speak English, or if they're deliberately ignoring them.  It's a far cry from the typical tourist destination.  They seem to want to build a sense of prestige, but the management doesn't seem to realize that this is not how prestige is built. It just makes people feel worse, contributing to a 10% drop in return visits. I can only say it's truly incredible...  Every single person is stiff and doesn't care whether you call them or not, but they gather around to squabble, making it feel like they're deliberately trying to fool tourists. If you want to feel bad in Vietnam, I recommend coming here and experiencing the most unfriendly experience possible.

The mausoleum is beautiful but the entire security service needs to be reviewed. They shout, they use whistles, they call you for everything and they don't understand English. At the entrance they searched our backpacks and confiscated two lighters that they told us would be returned after the visit, we took absurd walks under the sun, where they even call you back if you lean against the wall and once at the exit we ask for the lighters back, the boy and the girl yell that they won't give them back to us, they don't listen to reason, with the phone they flash in my face the message "the lighters are mine I won't give them back to you, go away" I ask for help from the soldiers around but they laugh or look away, very rude, I couldn't see anymore I started yelling too that they don't explain anything, there are no signs that warn what you can bring and what not and not even that personal things won't be returned, I made such a mess that the boy pushed me and the girl took a basket full of lighters and I mean full hundreds and rudely told me to take mine back and get out of there. This is not how you treat visitors.

The security was aggressive for no reason. I preferred to turn back after a 30-minute wait. Being spoken to like that by security for no reason, when I came to discover the country with a smile, was unacceptable. My wife and I didn't …

It would be nice to see it, but they won't let you in if you're wearing a tank top or shorts, and you can't get in if you have a camera tripod. I asked if I could leave it there at security, but I was denied entry.
No point, waiting in line for half an hour, and the staff is very rude, even aggressive!!! You end up outside the mausoleum with no way to get back inside.

Be careful on the detour; you have to enter from the south. Security is tighter. They confiscated our lighter and incense. It was a real hassle to find our way from the mausoleum to the botanical garden! Security comes first. The only positive point is realizing the fragility of a regime with the enormous Ministry of Defense right next door...
The mausoleum was closed, but there was absolutely no sign outside indicating this. In short, we couldn't see Uncle Ho. This is certainly not in keeping with his wishes. I'm disappointed.
The security staff's treatment is beyond appalling. They don't speak English and simply cause problems, with a rude attitude. There are places in Hanoi where you'll have a much better experience.
Waiting an hour in line only to be told it's closed during opening hours. // Someone was visiting and they decided not to tell you, knowing they'd say no. With small children and in the sun.

Very bad experience. It's totally racial and sexist.  I was refused entry because my trousers were just a few centimeters above my knees. The trousers of the men in my group were the same length, but they could still go inside! The other Vietnamese women wore tight skirts that were shorter than my trousers, but they could still go inside.

The dress code is absolutely questionable. Some people are turned away for wearing shorts, while others are allowed in. It's all very arbitrary.  And the staff is incredibly unfriendly. It's a shame to treat visitors like that. A definite no from us!

A disgraceful place. They make you cover up when you walk around. The police themselves sell you scarves at a ridiculous price to make a fortune. They search you when you enter, keep your lighters and keep them for themselves, and then sell everything inside. On top of that, everything is closed and they don't say anything to you. A walk you could have avoided.

Closed for several days. No information on Google (noted "open until 5 p.m.") or on site. We waited on the esplanade, in the blazing sun (not a tree), for nothing :-( Then we wanted to go visit his houses, but all the direct accesses indicated by Google were closed, we had to make a huge detour while the buildings were in front of us. Finally, we gave up after several attempts in terrible heat. All this is badly organized :-(

I couldn't access the venue because there's an entrance checkpoint, and they turned me away for carrying a small tripod in my backpack (a cheap gorilla-style one). I told them I'd leave the tripod at the checkpoint and pick it up later, and they said no, there was no way I was getting in. 😧 …
I understand not being able to bring in lighters, but why don't they return them? Those damn things.

Western tourists, beware, there's clearly racism towards Westerners. Local men walk in wearing shorts without any problem, but foreigners don't... Several times, you're ordered not to speak while the locals are talking very loudly next to you, and you don't say anything to them... As a foreigner, it's impossible to bring a bottle of water and leave it in the locker, while locals can...  Furthermore, the tour isn't anything special. The route is imposed. Very disappointing and a stark contrast to the rest of Vietnam!
Staff, not friendly, shorts, not accepted, monuments closed most of the time I do not recommend this place tourists are treated like ……

Dress codes are all well and good. They're understandable at a mausoleum or burial site, but being turned away because of visible tattoos is blatantly arbitrary, even when we offered to cover them. A resounding no from us.

Just fooled by the opening. They couldn't get in. After a thorough security check, they only let us walk around once. They tried it for several days. Never again!!! In Beijing, with Mao, that's no problem.
I arrived around 1 p.m. on June 24, 2024. I specifically changed into long sleeves because I couldn't go inside to see the remains, but I could see the entire fire!

The staff's treatment was shameful—rude and arrogant. They don't allow semi-professional cameras inside the temple... Canon EOS 5D Mark IV, which they think tourists carry... but they only tell you this after you've been queuing for an hour to go through security.

First, they told my wife had to cover her knees and shoulders. Then, after a long search, we bought something, but apparently, she wasn't allowed to cover them either; only proper trousers/a t-shirt counted (which no one had mentioned before). With men, it was arbitrarily decided whether their knees were showing too much or not. All this just to walk past the building, not even to go inside. An impossible procedure because no one even said anything except "No, no."
Closed on Fridays and Mondays. They didn't explain anything to us.

Once you pass the security checkpoint and go in, you can't go anywhere~ You have to be patient~ I passed by my group, so they went in first, but I had to wait outside for an hour and a half. I had to contact them, but the soldiers in uniforms were on all fours~ They wouldn't let me make a phone call either. If you pass by them, you just have to go out and wait for a long time~ No answer~~~

There is no clear information about the mausoleum's opening hours. The guards give conflicting information: some say 7:30 a.m. to 10:30 a.m. (except Mondays and Fridays when it's closed all day), others say 7:30 a.m. to 12:00 p.m., and still others say 2:00 p.m. to 5:00 p.m. Every time I've come, it's been closed.
Nope, the mausoleum is closed. There's no sign of it. People are allowed to pay their entrance fee!

The mausoleum is closed until August 15th for maintenance, so if you visit before then, you'll only be able to see it from the outside.  It's quite questionable that they're trying to track down foreigners at the entrance and make them pay 40,000k for the grounds. If you're Vietnamese, admission is completely free...
It was a Monday morning, but there was a long line at the entrance security, so I gave up.
A huge dump! I don't recommend it.
It's ridiculous that you can't even bring a lighter in! Handing it in isn't possible either.  Everyone was incredibly unfriendly!

Very inappropriate behavior of the staff towards tourists. The security staff is not sufficiently equipped with the language, so they just shout and make inappropriate gestures. Supposedly, women are not allowed to have their knees and shoulders exposed. The reason is not entirely clear. In any case, it is a good business plan for the scarf seller right at the check point. I will not support this.
The place closes every day at 11:30 We arrived at 10:00 and the gates were closed on us, the guards and service providers are very unfriendly and disrespectful
We came prepared to go in with the appropriate clothing, but they told us to leave because of the selfie stick. We asked about lockers, and they just told us to leave.

Annoying... the wait... the crowds... the ridiculous rules imposed as if you were entering the Pentagon or a secret Russian base... no photos in the queue outside... but a few meters further on, behind the perimeter and a few meters from …

Having traveled to over 500 cities worldwide, I haven't seen such rude and insane staff in a long time. They yell at people and often refuse entry to Europeans, according to some rules and regulations of their own.
I didn't come in because they wanted to steal my lighter... I found it absurd, you have to go through a scanner, I didn't feel free.
It's an obstacle course to get in and the visit is limited to a photo of the facade (which is nothing exceptional). A waste of time.
The guards force you to wear short sleeves and long pants for women. TO ENTER A MUSEUM. SEE YOU LATER
I came here because it said it was open on Friday. It's closed on Mondays and Fridays.
There's not much to see. You have to wear clothes that cover your shoulders and knees, depending on how friendly you are to the guard. Everything's been renovated and has little charm.
Unpleasant staff Not worth visiting Required to have shoulders and legs covered A waste of time Worse than the airport, they confiscate your lighters, etc.
Avoid. The timetables are wrong.
Entry was denied because of shorts. Meanwhile, everyone else is walking around in shorts. Very strange.

It reminded me a bit of the White House in Washington, militarized and somewhat unapproachable (obviously I'm exaggerating, nothing is as bad as Washington). But maybe I just happened to be there at the wrong time.
Ugly and useless, it looks like a Soviet military base. The staff at the entrance are really rude and unpleasant to tourists.
If you wear shorts, they won't let you in. With the heat and humidity, it's normal to wear shorts.
They take things from you at the entrance that they then sell inside, a rip-off, and the worst part is, you can't visit the mausoleum in the afternoon, they don't warn you about this.
The access conditions are far too strict; it's worse than flying. Ridiculous. I don't recommend it.
Tank tops and/or shorts are not allowed inside (especially if you're a woman). There's a man selling sarongs before you enter.
Girls need to wear knee-length pants or skirts to enter.
You won't lose anything even if you don't visit the queue... It's unnecessary for soldiers to line up in twos in that vast environment.
Schedules are out of date. We arrived at 11:00. They told us the hours were 6-10:00, but nothing is certain.
Long pants for both men and women. Covering both shoulders, otherwise they won't let you in.
Boring, surrounded by armed guards. One hour in line, two minutes Ho chi minh. Waste of time!
They didn't let me in with a tripod, and I can't leave it there either. They have a disdainful attitude towards tourists.
Treating tourists poorly... Choiik... I think I saw the height of rudeness in Hanoi...
Door policy is tougher than at Berghain. Very arbitrary, extremely unfriendly.
A terrifying place. If someone is not a fan of communism, they probably won't like it.
They didn't let me in because I was at the top. I didn't really want to.
This is just the tomb of a Vietnamese leader.
pay full price for entry 10 minutes later they closed and threw us out
I would avoid
Men are allowed in shorts, but women are not.
Arbitrary checks/rules. Very unfriendly!
The terrible lime tree on Red Square in Moscow
I couldn't go inside, so I just walked around the square in front.
I saw it from the outside, with security all around, you can't even get close, nothing worthwhile
Mausoleum closed until August 15th
Skip the visit; there are more interesting things to see on any street in the city.
Good clean, magnificent, the place is very 3
poorly indicated
Unnecessary. Long and tedious lines.
I don't like studying history
Closed until December grrrr.
A Buddhist temple that I do not recommend
There's not much to see, more than I expected
A churro
I don't empathize. Why should I watch this?
Where the Ho Chi Minh mummy is.
I didn't like it
meaningless!
It's just a square
Dead person??

When you enter the (vast) area of the Mausoleum, you must abide by a set of rules that would be indigestible in most of our maximum security prisons.For heaven's sake, maximum compared to Ho Chi Ming: but if I enter the mausoleum where the body is exposed! If instead I visit the gardens, the pond, the wooden house where it turned off, etc. (with humidity at 90% and a perceived temperature of 45 degrees centigrade), someone wants to explain why I have to wear long pants?!?Moreover, you can not approach the sidewalk leading to the stairs of the mausoleum (you are first reprimanded and then rejected by the unfriendly military of Giardia), just as you can not smoke in the gardens: in short, the classic place where you observe the most rigorous "personality cult" typical of totalitarian regimes.Personally, visiting Hanoi again, I would not return.

Terrible. In the afterlife I think it was a total waste of time to see a dead dictator. This is communism at its worst. The soldiers there and the security guards are treating you like a criminal. The place is full of propaganda, and it was all just awful. How we had to stand in line and get crash orders. Have little sense for the whole experience. Steer away, read the bad comments.

We were not impressed, it was a lot of employees who were angry and did not bother to help us. They gave us more, wrong information and wasted our time. I would recommend doing something else. Or get out of town as soon as possible. Definitely the city we least liked being in.

Came heren, it was stil closed.Came back after one hour and suddenly i wasnt dressed properly. My shoulders had to be covered.I bought a scarf around the corner nobody could see my arms and shoulders anymore. But the unfriendly men said it was stil not good enough while i saw people entering with the same scarf wrapt around their body.Went back to the sales man, bought a T-shirt and went back AGAIN. And now the rude men starts complaining about the short jeans of my husband, hè didnt say a word about this before!!! This all happend within 5 minutes so he knew what he said before. Very rude very unfriendly never come back would not recommend going here. They treath you like a criminal
Was denied the entry because I was using shorts just above the knee. At the same time there were male tourist inside with shorts the same length.
Your website said it was open, after a 2.2 km walk l arrive at 12.30 and it’s closed, took a couple of pictures and left, very disappointing.

Do not bother, guards are too strict, outside if you walk too close to line in allowed path, because you want to get past a group walking too slow, you are told off by guards using sign language. Also if inside building walking upstairs to view Ho Chin Ming dummy body(he is cremated), when l stopped to wait for my wife again l was told off.
A visit to a behemoth that is not very interesting, especially when you know that it was done against the wishes of Ho Chi Minh.

When you had been complex, don't move, don't shake, do not wear sunglass, do not wear hat even the sun is like fire, do not speak each other. The guards check and look to  see  to visitors like as a terrorist. I am going to think about never visit to there.
After waiting for hours, under surveillance, you are herded past HCM. Cameras and other recordings are forbidden.

I don't know if it was the tiredness or the heat, but it was my least favorite thing in Hanoi. Located on a giant esplanade, this monument with communist overtones stands there. Far too large (I don't even want to imagine the cost) to pay homage to a leader.
They didn't let us in cause we had a baby with us, which would have been ok except I saw others with same age babies going in. The guard clearly had his own interpretation.
Nobody knows when it's open.
Go there to take a look at what Communism values.... Himt: Not freedom.An exact copy from"mother" Russia Lenin's mausoleum

Please note that the mausoleum is closed for renovations for several months, I believe. Check before you travel, and also be sure to check the museum's opening hours, as it is closed between noon and 2 pm.

The guide said that we should meet in 15 minutes inside the Ho Cho Min eztate, but I decided to go to the bus stop. When I realised that the group stayed inside I decided to go back. There was no way I could explain the guards that I needed help. The did not speak English and were extremely useless. The group lost 20 minutes and I lost a lot of nerves.Awful experience.Socialism sucks.

Technically, we didn’t get past the security checkpoint, so I can’t say much about the mausoleum itself.But the security gate was nice: modern, clean, and inconsistent.I heard that some wheelchair users on TripAdivsor were able to get in with the mausoleum’s wheelchair, but we weren’t given that option. If Vietnam want to modernize, she needs to work on communication and get rid of the inconsistency — build a protocol and follow it! As a suggestion, the staff at HCM Mausoleum should put more effort into accommodating for people with mobility issues rather than outright barring people interested in the revolutionary leader who created the country we know. In any case, powered wheelchair users should just avoid this place, don’t play by others’ whims and do something more worthy of your time.
The visit begins with a security check where, among other things, your lighters are confiscated (?!).

Visiting Ho Chi Minh's mausoleum is pointless! Firstly, the construction of this mausoleum doesn't respect the deceased's last wishes. Its architecture is...horrible, and on top of that, the Vietnamese people are forced to pay for its upkeep!

I have read about uncle ho and ho chi minh mausoleum alot before travel to Hanoi but i had very bad experience when i was around there. The guards around there treated the guests / foreigners very bad. Thats something to be condemn badly. I do not wish to comment more on it and i would like to forget these bad memory in my trip in Hanoi eventhough i have great respect on Uncle Ho

This is a nasty place to visit. There is little to see and it is mostly closed - it closes for three hours in the middle of the day, ffs. This was an unpleasant experience and a waste of half a day. We are trying not to let it spoil our impression of Vietnam, however. Here is a list of issues, in no particular order:- The entrance is unfathomable difficult to find. - It is closed for half the day and no one seemed to know the opening times. - The guards are unfriendly. - The mausoleum is ugly. - There are no signposts and the site is huge so you end up walking in circles a lot- The museum is worse than useless: just a load of communist propaganda trying to justify the regime. - The one pillar pagoda is deeply unimpressive. - The botanical garden entrance is not at all close and is hard to find, despite being next door. And it is the worst botanical garden I've been to. There is an unnecessary dress code (long trousers). A waste of time - half a day of my life that I won't get back.

Do not go by any of the times listed anywhere, ask a local when it's open. We went on a Sunday.... mausoleum not open, museum not open, no signs up giving any information. We went back at 7.45 a.m. on a Wednesday with intentions of queuing up to see Ho...... buses parked up, lots of people around..... this is better than Sunday.... 8 a.m. when you're meant to get access to the viewing of Ho, no doors opening, no queues, ask a helpful guard only to be told it's under maintenance for another 15 days. 'Totally read that - no where!'Wandered to the museum to be abruptly pointed at to buy tickets but no one else was, went through the security check, headed upstairs to check out Ho's life. I had high hopes of learning more of the famous leader. Minimal English, only a single sentence explaining an entire document or important time factor. Everything seemed quite interesting but we got bored with guessing so we turned back to leave and got yelled at. It seems there's only one way around, felt like IKEA in a way but less fun, found the exit - down four flights of stairs out a dingy back door. The gardens are lovely, the pagoda didn't seem like a special place with a coffee shop playing music next door, the drink vending machine doesn't work but again, the gardens are lovely. Up to you if you want to visit, hope you have better luck than we did.

For V it is a holy place, deep respect and immense love for uncle is omnipresent. For someone who experienced communism it gives goosebumps. I don't see a single reason to go inside..however, watching the visitors and the security is interesting, the natives are enthusiastic, the tourists sometimes look in amazement...A tour of the private residence is interesting..e. a collection of cars.. However, in H. there are more interesting - truly historical places.

We visited this museum with high expectations considering how loved Ho Chi Mihn is in Vietnam. This museum was very disappointing. It is so hard to follow. The staff are no help and there are many things that I wonder how they had any relevance to the man himself. Not worth the walk or money. Read everything you want to know about this amazing man online or in books.
There are tons of places to visit in Hanoi.  Limit on carrying belongings and dress appropriately. Not worth the time to spend 1/2 day there.

Completely disorganized and full of tour groups. If you're there alone and have a question, you get rudely answered. Everyone is unfriendly. A huge hassle. I didn't even want to see the house, but because of the poor signage, I didn't know where to go at first. Then I had to buy a map for 40,000 dong, which nobody seemed to care about anyway, and I just looked at the house from the outside. It's not worth it at all. I would have been better off going to the museum, which costs another 40,000 dong. I'd really had enough and left afterward. You have to leave your backpacks at the front, and photography isn't allowed. You should wear long clothing.

I have been here twice, at different times on different days, but still, the mausoleum was closed. I have no idea why. Did I come at the wrong time on both days? Because I know people are able to see him. Anyhow, the second time I went, I was yelled at by one of the guards. Apparently, I was standing somewhere on the pavement I wasn't supposed to be. There were no signs, though, saying you were not allowed to stand there. He motioned for me to move, so I moved a few steps in the direction he was motioning . But he kept yelling at me and motioning me to move. So I moved a few more steps in the direction he was motioning. When he yelled at me again, I lost it and yelled back, "What do you want me to do?" Probably not the smartest thing to do, as a) he had a gun and b) it's not like he would have understood English. But that just serves to show what you might encounter if you come. Not much to see. Mausoleum closed. Angry, rude guards that speak no English. I think you might have better luck going elsewhere.

Arrived here in the afternoon and there was nothing much here to see other than the outside of the mausoleum. and there is a yellow line on the pavement that you are not allowed to step over. Apparently if you arrive in the morning then you can go inside, but for afternoon visitors there is just a yellow line on the pavement.  There are of course flowers by the entrance and honor guards.
I went at 10 AM, but they told me to turn around and go to a spot quite a distance past. When I tried to enter again, I had to

I'd like to point out that my assessment is purely aesthetic, regarding the building's architecture, and has nothing to do with the profound ethical significance and everything the person buried there represents for the Vietnamese people.

I couldn't bear having to wait for hours in the scorching sun. I looked at it from a distance and gave up on going inside. Even so, I was surprised to see the Vietnamese people patiently waiting in the seemingly endless queue.
It's impossible to see it since there was a line that stretched for miles and miles. It's absurd not to offer a priority ticket.
This is not something you should have seen. A long line just to walk past a 50-year-old corpse.
Because in the afternoon, when we were there, it was closed, which we only found out from a local at the locked door.
If you're a socialist, you should stop leaving human remains and turning them into tourist attractions.

It has nothing more than a very large mausoleum where foreigners are not allowed to enter; you can only see the changing of the guard, which is nothing special... on the esplanade, the only thing I enjoyed was seeing the bonsai trees that abound in the streets of almost all of Southeast Asia.

I did not expected that it was just a walk pass the external mausoleum.  We had to go throught the security check w our bag and u need to walk forward/ pass the mausoleum without stepping into the yellow marking infront of the mausoleium. Tried to combine the visit to Hanoi Military Museum which is located opposite the mausoleium on the same day.

This is ridiculously! So a baby under 2 years old is not allowed to enter for what reason? What the heck is this ? I have attached the picture of the regulations at the entrance of the Mausoleum and there is nothing saying that! So I have spend 1 hour waiting my city tour for this stupidity! I would no recommend visit that the security were very unfriendly saying baby not cannot wall? What does this means ? You are working in a mausoleum and is unable to speak international language English ? What the heck !!!! Families do give a try to this thing it’s a complete waste of times ! Cheers!

By the time we arrived at 11:30 the mausoleum was closed for the day.  We spent too much time at the Military War Museum.  We found that one has to stand in a long queue and move, carefully watched by the honor guard,  by the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh encased in glass for a 30 second viewing.  So I don't think it worth the effort even more so in inclement weather.  The grounds are immaculate and I think the museum is worth a visit, but again it was closed for the lunch break as are all the museums.   The one pillar pagoda is a small structure hardly twenty feet high.
Nothing out of the ordinary,too many restrictions on where to walk,what to photograph etc.I didn't enjoyed it at all,waste of time only

I didn't like it. It's a guided tour between barriers, supervised by uniformed soldiers who aren't messing around. Between warnings, you're asked to contemplate the fake remains of Uncle Ho, his office, his goldfish... in short, I didn't enjoy it.

We went there my wife need to by cover scarf because they didn't let her get in.Lot of guards you go by walking after a guide from place you can not speak you can not photo ho chi min the area around is nice you need not more than hour to this activity

We didn't understand a thing about this museum: there's no logical thread, you can find everything inside (just like at the night market, in fact). There are contemporary works of art, photos, panels written only in Vietnamese, articles about the war. We can't understand anything, and the museum doesn't provide guides.
Sad place, queues of servile people, morning hours ending at 10 or 11. A cult of personality that even Hochimin himself wouldn't have wanted.
The place opens only till 12 pm I guess. But the line starts pretty early and is notoriously long. Doesn't make sense for one to wait to see Uncle Ho for just a few seconds.

One room has translated information/facts but the information itself is very limited and as a result,  you learn very very little about Ho Chi Minh himself. I recommend a visit to the Vietnamese Women's Museum- very interesting and well presented!
Hard to gain access to and generally not open to the public. Didn’t get to see the body. The surrounding area is nice but this place is boring and not worth visiting.
The mausoleum has been closed for the last 25 days and even though it isn’t written online it will remain closed until the 15th of August

It was a blistering hot day, combine that with long lines to see the body in the mausoleum, made for a really bad experience. We waited about an hour, according to our tour guide, the lines can be as long as a 3 hour wait. The tour inside the mausoleum was 30 seconds around the open casket with HCM’s body in it. The best part about the trip was the AC in the mausoleum cooling you down after sweating like a pig outside, until you are marched out of the sweet sweet AC room because the 2 km of people behind you needs to cool down too.

First I wasn't allowed to enter the grounda because my shoulders were not covered. Then I went to buy a t-shirt with sleeves so I can enter and then everything was closes except the one pillar pagoda. And to top it off there were other women in the grounds with singlets , so why couldn't I go in with my racerback shirt? Really nothing to see
This building is very ugly and stands out in the city.

I visited this place because it is famous for the fact that Ho Chi Minh is fully reflected on Vietnamese currency. The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum was located in a large square; although there were many tourists, the square was so spacious that it did not feel crowded.

We tried twice with no luck:  once because the line was so long, once because it was closed even though the website I checked said it was open.  I'm not sure if there is a good site to have accurate information on opening hours.  But from the outside, it is a cool looking building and grounds.  Good luck.
This is really terrible, so many people in the que. We tried it twice, booth times where crowded. You have to wait a very very very long time before you can see Ho Chi Minh.
Don’t bother,filled with field trip of local kids. can’t even take picture from outside. We just wasted our time . Student guide brought walk around outside the complex.....

Wish I could write a positive review. Caught a cab from my old quarter digs before 8am and arrived at the Mausoleum about 820 upon arrival a police would not let us enter the forcourt and motioned for us to walk around. At this point our cab driver had done a u turn and seen we had an issue and took instructions from the police and kindly motioned for us to hop back into the cab and drove us to where he was directed which was at least 2 kilometres from the attraction Full marks to this driver from Hanoi Cab company as he refused to take money for the second trip. The line was so long and snaked around several corners and was over 1km long. After a wait of over 2 hours and the mausoleum just coming into view it was obvious we were not going to get to view the Great Man before the 11am curfew. I am not sure why the queue was this long or if indeed this is normal but to say I am disappointed would be a complete understatement.

This is a visitor’s nightmare.  The line is horrendous.  Takes one half day or more to wait in line to see a mausoleum that is just plain not worth it.  You will thank me for suggesting you visit other sites that are more worthy of your time and more interesting.

Went there today in the morning. The queue went all around the square and it took us 15 minutes walking from where the taxi driver dropped us until we made it to the end of the queue. But we were told that we shouldn't be put off by it as however long it moves steadily. Except it didn't. There were thousands of kids there probably on school trips from provinces and their organised groups would form a queue parallel to the main one and it would be the only queue moving too. In an hour we made 30 meters out of 300meters we had left to the entrance alone (from there to the entrance to the mausoleum there was another 800m). I'm only giving it one star for the review to get attention and work as a warning for other travellers. Shame that all major guidebook don't mention that.

The day we visited was shortly after the death of a previous president. Police had barricaded the road, hundreds of buses, thousands of people. Basically we just did not have the luxury of waiting in line for what was most likely more than three plus hours. We left and went to the museum of national art.

Seriously, the enviroment around the mausoleum is very nice. But beware of the crowded tourists, as some of them are pretty impolite.  As for the museum itself... skip it, it’s poorly curated and the presentation won’t help you a bit in knowing the vietnamese culture or history, totally a waste of time and money for the museum.

Went early on Saturday morning and couldn't believe the queues.  Very little help trying to find out how to get a ticket. Gave up in the end .Imagine it would take hours to get in. Surely not worth wasting so much time.
Not worth the visit, nothing to see. Saved by the nice lake inside, otherwise waste of time and money.

Really long queues. If you decide to go, best to go early to make sure you will get in. Really not much to see. Although this was included on our trip, I felt it was more for Vietnamese people to pay their respects. No photos allowed past a certain point , not that I would have taken any. I did , however, take some pics of the queues! They really don’t do it justice.

There are long lines to visit this temple to a dead man whose body has been carefully preserved.  He is guarded by soldiers who enforce a dress code and correct demeanor.  I went simply because it is expected to pay respects to a cultural icon, but I certainly would have been happy to skip the experience.
Crowded and pricey. Long line with unfriendly  line custodiansLineup did move but when you get to uncle ho 8it is for 15 seconds only

The security checks at the entrance were completely disproportionate, it was ridiculous: confiscation of bottles, prohibition of bags, a marching tour. We refused to enter and caused an unbelievable commotion.

I'm not a fan of visiting contemporary funerary monuments, but this one was included in the city tour. With its classical lines, it's very close to the Presidential Palace and the National Assembly. Access isn't guaranteed; I understood that it's only available on certain dates.

I came here as part of a G Adventures tour (Indochina Encompassed).  We arrived in good time to enter the mausoleum, but waited...and waited...to be told that too many people had arrived today (it was 10.30am).A major disappointment.

My review refers solely to the Mausoleum and not to the nearby Presidential Palace and Ho Chi Minh's house, which I reviewed separately. It's currently closed for renovations, but we wouldn't have entered anyway. The Mausoleum is an ugly cube-shaped structure surrounded by columns of no artistic interest.

I don't like cults for anyone, so I can't understand why nations spend so many space and money on mausolea. But if they do, they better build sometthing interesting or daring or - why not - beautifull. This Mausoleum is really boring. You probably don't spend weeks in Hanoi, and you'll lose a lot of time visting this one. If you're not a diehard communist, don't.
Nothing interesting, really, at least for foreigners. The park has no appeal in its gardens, nor do the artifacts that once belonged to Ho Chi Minh.
It's closed for restoration. No point visiting to this monument. Just at the two minute walking distance is the one pillar pagoda. Nothing spectacular and can be given a miss

We were very dissapointed to find it closed on the only day we could go and to then research and find that the other days it is open , it is only open for 3 hours a day except tuesday when it is open all day 😢
In my opinion, if you don't have a deep admiration for Ho Chi Minh, it's not worth going; it's simply about going in and seeing the man turned into a mummy sleeping in a glass coffin, nothing more.

An endless queue, mostly of Vietnamese civilians and military personnel, to see what they say are the remains of their leader, is not worth it, we went on the recommendation of the Agency as a must-see.

What ever ylour hotel staff or anyone else says it is going to be unbearably packed with horrendous lines on the weekend even soon after opening, all very unpleasant. Make sure you go very early in the morning on a weekday.

You stand in line for 30 minutes with a billion others as an annoyingly large amount of vietnamese people cut in line in front of you, then you get into the mausoleum and see the plastic-looking, preserved dead body of a man who specifically said he wanted to be cremated and didn't want a large complex built in his name. Seems disrespectful. I'm sure he would be rolling over in his grave at what this place is, if he had a grave to roll over in.

A complete waste of time. But it gave a good sense of what it would be like with fullblown communism. Endless waiting in lines, and at the end of the line we received a boring glimpse of a wax doll in a tomb. Pretty good copy of Lenin's little commie box 😂 The museum itself was confusing. Seems like they ran out of Ho Chi Minh memorabilia, and just threw in stuff. So a bit of comedy in the museum, which was nice, since the atmosphere was pretty uptight and just a joke when the guards were shouting at people for just about anything. And foreigners have to pay. So, not in line with the commie ideology. It should be available for all I would think. Anyway, don't go!
Went to the Ho Chi Minh mausoleum, lined up for hours and shuffled through toe to heel with visitors.  No good for impatient children, or elderly. There are far better attractions around Hanoi.

Vietnamese will hype over this as it is their national hero. Reality is that you wait for hours and under the watchful eye of the police can walk for 1 minute trough the tomb and take a quick glance at the holy lighted body. Which is clearly a copy.

I arrived at 8am on a sunday morning and there was no way I could enter the mausoleum site. The queue stretched for over a mile and was full of school children. Apparently once you are inside the gates, the queue still continues and you are ushered past the mausoleum and not allowed to stop for more than a minute to take a picture.

Apparently they close the doors at 11, even if you're waiting in the queue you can't see him. We went today at 7:30, and didn't get in until 9:15. The queue moves quickly but is incredibly long. It is very hot too - I'd recommend taking an umbrella! You're also not allowed water or food in the queue. I'd recommend getting there early if you want to avoid the heat, and it seemed incredibly busy on both weekend days that we went. When you get in, you have about 30 seconds to view him as you're hurried and pushed along by the guards. Although he was a legendary man, and I fully respect a lot of the things he's done for Vietnam, I did not think that it was worth the wait at all.We were some of the only Westerners there, I feel like this is something you should do only if you worship Ho Chi Minh.

I couldn't see the inside because it was closed the day I went to visit. From the outside, it's like almost all the monuments erected to communist leaders in a country with a communist regime: a huge concrete structure, or something similar, with absolutely no architectural merit for me.
Cement, cement, and more cement. It's true that visiting it in 39-degree heat isn't exactly pleasant. We didn't get anything out of the museum.

We were in this day trip package. It's on our list.  Not that keen to see a body in a glass coffin. We all voted out not to go in,  specially the quequeing time was 2 hours!  But!  We still had to queque to get in the campus and walked around..You decide!
I must have chosen the wrong day, it was the weekend...after waiting an hour in line, I gave up because the line was several hundred meters long...what a shame...Check the least busy times.
Not a strategic place nor organize area. We do not know where is the entrance. We try to get information by the police, they do not understand basic english.It was very frustrating for us.
You wait a long time in line only to see a doll of the uncle they idolize so much there... I don't recommend it, I suggest the Old Quarter or even the Museum of Literature.

Firstly, the cult of personality is, of course, unfamiliar in Western countries. But apart from that, there's not much to see and do after about a 90-minute wait. The organization of the queues is also only average, unless you're willing to engage in a battle with the locals – because brazenly pushing in line is perfectly acceptable there :)

This is about my fourth time staying in Hanoi in the last five years, but I think everyone should visit at least once! In a way, seeing the peaceful face of President Ho Chi Minh, the foundation of this country, gives you a sense of the country's future potential. And even the Vietnamese people, who in a way (this is just my personal opinion...) seem rather rough around the edges, are solemnly and respectfully paying their respects (only inside the building, though 😃)!
I really enjoyed my time there. Photos are nice. I read the poems of Ho Chi Minh written in the prison and I was thinking visiting here. Now one of my dream became true. Wish to visit again!

Poor Uncle Ho, who wanted to be cremated to avoid the "cult of personality," ask the official guide to go in the afternoon so you can avoid the queue of more than an hour to see the mummy of the man responsible for the exodus of 1.5 million boat people in 1975.
No need to queue for two hours, the exterior is more than enough.

We were interested in seeing Ho Chi Minh lying in state but when we got there found the line to get in was at least two hours long. So we went into the grounds to see and there really wasn't much of interest and the crowds made it uncomfortable. Many groups actually push and shove so beware. Only go if this is really really on your bucket list - and don't go on a weekend, our only available time but also that of the rest of Vietnam!
At least it wasn't in March 2017. It's on a main avenue closed to traffic and honestly... a real dump. If you have days to spare, go, but if not, don't waste your time on something so pointless.
There's a few dudes in white military uniform standing in front if a locked door and plenty ofrestricted signs. Fairly pointless place.
I didn't visit it when I saw the long queue outside of the Mausoleum. My guide told me that people queue half a day under the sun and only pay respect for 2 minutes.
You have to stand in line after having your bag checked at a large building.

Just not really my thing. Especially considering it was never the wish to exhibit the man here. He himself wished to be scattered over South, Central, and North Vietnam. The government, however, felt that things had to be different.

The idea was that it would represent a lotus flower. Instead, it is a Soviet hulk. Foreboding, cold, impersonal. Lines to enter and visit are long when Ho is in town. Ho is often in Moscow getting a face lift from Lenin's keepers. Drive by, look at it, and keep going.

The entrance is horribly placed. There's confusion where to enter and the guards keep telling you to go in one direction so you end up going in a big circle. Once in they stopped letting infront of the mausoleum. Nothing to see, skip it if you don't have much time.

We turned up with a tour guide to Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum on a Monday to find it was shut to the public. We didn't initially think to check opening times as we had paid for a day city tour with a company, expecting that they would have known this, they either didn't or didn't care as we were just visiting tourist.So sadly we only got to see the changing of the guards.

I visited because it was included in the tour. Ho Chi Minh may be a hero of Vietnam, but I wonder what he would think of his body being laid to rest in this way. The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is magnificent, but the uneven sidewalks of Hanoi reminded me once again that this is a sign of communism. I also felt sorry for the soldiers on guard duty. Standing in an overly air-conditioned place for so long can't be good for your health.
We toured the mausoleum to the inner room, where the body of the deceased political leader can be seen.
Not that impressive and quite bored during this visit, don't do it unless you have nothing else to do

On Sunday we couldn't even get close to entering the site so we went on Monday and we're in and out without much trouble.  The only reason we decided to go was because HCM liberated Vietnam from the French in 1946.  This is a poignant fact in that Ha Loa prison artifact demonstrates the Vietnamese are far more willing to discuss the brutal  treatment of Vietnamese by the French than war with China and the US.

This is not a place for kids and something I would not do again. The building is large and so are the grounds, but that's about all I like about it. The entry restrictions and security is prohibitive. It's like entering a bank within a bank ... and this is to see a tomb. Kids will get bored walking through all the hoops and red tape, with nothing else to play or do. I got bored after the first 10 minutes walking to the tomb

The complex itself is extremely impressive, not only the mausoleum, which unfortunately houses Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body, but also his modest home and the well-kept garden that surrounded it, a place not to be missed.

It's very nice from the outside, but we had hoped to go inside too. It's closed 3 months a year, but there was nothing on their website about it, so we only found out when we got there. A bit of a bummer.

Walked to monument to find out it was closed (on a Saturday morning) heaps of tourists wondering around out the front wondering what to do as it's just a massive concrete courtyard more like a runway and a boring monolithic square box housing ho chi minh. With nothing to do my wife noticed some nice topiary and wondered what type of plant it was I put one foot up onto the footpath just to tilt the small label to read it and heard screaming coming from the monument. Looked over and there was this skinny military clad Vietnamese guy screaming at me and waving his hands. I stepped back and the crying stopped. Another tourist told me we can't walk on the footpath.There were no signs that I saw telling me not to walk on the footpath and we left.

Whether this mausoleum is architecturally successful or not is debatable. No one needs this kind of personality cult; only totalitarian systems have such things. For a citizen of a democratic state, it's unacceptable.
A cold monument. Clearly not worth the trip for so little. Backlit most of the time.

This tour is part of the mandatory sites that the Vietnamese tour guide take you. But it is to show who is Ho Ci Main and who he live, it was as always a hot day and we walk a lot in the sun hear some stores about Ho Ci Main and it was waist of time. Skip this tour if you can.

After getting up very early and walking our one-and-a-half-year-old son in the blazing sun to get to the mausoleum, they wouldn't let us in! The reason was that our son wasn't yet walking independently. The poor little guy was asleep in the stroller, and even so, they saw him as a threat and kicked us out of line.

Was disappointed visiting this place. Waited for long q for more than 20 mins just to get in. Although I wasn't enjoying it but be very respectful to those local on their respect to their legacy leader.

It's a mausoleum like those of any communist dictatorship: they glorify the leader, who lies embalmed in a refrigerated room, and little else. There's nothing interesting to see, except for the devotion a segment of the Vietnamese people feel for this leader. Neither the architecture, nor the gardens, nor the history of the mausoleum make it worth a visit. Furthermore, there's an entrance fee, long queues, and three checkpoints where you're required to leave your camera.

The place where Ho Chi Minh's mummified remains rest. Unless you like mummies, don't go. He looks like a wax figure and is guarded by very serious guards. They don't allow photos or videos. For me, a waste of time.
A total drag. In a massive building almost entirely dedicated to Uncle Ho. The best thing about it is the air conditioning, which is saying something.
It may be essential for the Vietnamese people as a reference point for their history, but to a foreigner it's just a big, ugly thing with no charm whatsoever.

Skippable. Unless you're really keen to see it. It looks fantastic, it's very well preserved, but the queue was incredibly long, the heat was unbearable, the security was excessive, and to top it all off, you leave your backpack on one side, your camera on another, and to collect everything they make you walk all the way back to the entrance.

I was so looking forward to seeing the resting place of Ho Chi Minh but the whole experience is so regimented and you feel like you are being processed and checked as an enemy. It was a very uncomfortable experience and if you really wanted to take a long look forget it you are ushered in and out at military precision pace.

what a travesty of a museum. there was one wall with the history of ho chi minh and another room with weird art that had nothing to do with him at all. don't waste your time. wikipedia would be more informational.

We went to visit here on a Sunday and we just couldn't get inside, the line was non ending, just to reach end of the line took us over 20 minutes and it was still further.  It is open just for a few hours, hence we wouldn't manage to reach inside and it was peak summer and didn't have patience to wait in the heat.  Requested one of the police to guide us to the museum and it had a detailed exhibition about Ho Chi Minh, hence got a good view of his life and struggles (though not all was mentioned in English).  It was crowded and most of them were really noisy, there should be silence so that the visitors can absorb the atmosphere but the cacophony spoilt the complete visit.  They need to get a little strict and make the visitors realise it is not a picnic spot as was being treated.
Once dead you have no say anymore. To be put into this mausoleum, being flown to Russia every so often to 'renew' the body with chemicals is something that Ho Chi Minh certainly did not want.

Ho Chi Minh did a lot for Vietnam, but this mausoleum shows that even today, he continues to be excessively idolized. Lines of people, whose cameras are confiscated, forced to remain silent, just to see for 20 seconds the embalmed body of their leader in an unnecessary mega-building, surrounded by guards in immaculate white uniforms.
It's a long walk for how unpleasant the experience is. The queue is enormous. This is an important visit for the Vietnamese.

A building where Ho Chi Minh's mummy rests, surrounded by gardens that aren't much to write home about, with guards who won't even let you step on the yellow line in front of the building, and little else... I think that to appreciate what Ho Chi Minh meant, it's not necessary to visit his mausoleum, much less see him embalmed... Another overrated attraction, like, in my opinion, all of Vietnam.
I wouldn't recommend this building-very pompous and harsh building with huge grounds and nothing around.

We walked a half hour to get there only to find bus load upon bus loads of people. There are thousands of people in different lineups all over the place. We, along with lots of other people said forget it! We didn't see Ho Chi Minh but apparently we could have visited the grounds.

I expected to learn much more about Ho Chi Minh's life, and I went with a great guide who explained a lot more about him. I was more impressed by the architecture of the plaza than by actually entering the museum; I recommend visiting the surrounding area first.
Long wait, crowded wouldnt say it is a place to go. Lots of people, very strict and couldnt even reay see the body when we went by.

Such a horrible place to visit. The guards were rude and makes you feel like you're in prison. They shout at people when they're walking across the field and to those sitting on the curbs, but they were too lazy to shout at those who was sitting at the curbs 20 meters away.

It's a strange experience to go and glimpse Uncle Ho under his glass coffin. Even just to reach the entrance, you have to go around two wide avenues leading directly to it, but they're deserted. Don't even try to cross them; one of the many soldiers present will quickly and firmly put you in your place.
A true Stalinist institution, closed on Fridays but with two guards on duty; nothing to see except the vegetation at the entrance and the single-pillar pagoda.
Lines were 2-3 hours long to visit this small mausoleum with nothing noteworthy to mention,  Just a waste of time, and the rest of Hanoi is also not worth the trip.
Perhaps should have read the tour guide more carefully, I didn'tint expect to see a dead person lying there who had expressly asked to be cremated, poor old chap, long, long queues, sad place.
One of the most obscene and pointless visits of the entire trip. I tried in vain to avoid this completely meaningless ritual obligation. To be forgotten.

We never made it.into the mausoleum but trying to get into it has left a sour feeling for us. After waking up early and quick breakfast, a taxi ride and queuing for depositing our rucksacks we were told we couldn't enter as we had a baby. One has to be three or older to enter the mausoleum. No one.could explain why. They only said it was a rule! My son was in a carrier and asleep! So we all decided not to go. We also noticed that the place is full of unfriendly officials who love to shoo people off and direct them to a random locations

The taxi in the picture (that later we discovered was a FAKE taxi) had a meter running ten times faster than it should. He was waiting just outside the mausoleum to catch tourists. He tried to charge us 600.000 for a ride worth 70.000. We realized something was wrong and bargained 200.000 (still a lot more than we should have paid). It's called taxi meter and it's a small car. Be careful!!!
My sense is you'll enjoy if you are Vietnamese rather than American.  The "party line" is espoused.  American POWs are described as being well-treated.  Confused me!

A sinister, 1960s-style Moscow mausoleum: hideous. Located in a sprawling square dedicated to military parades in honor of Ho Chi Minh (there's an embarrassing presence of photos, quotes, and posters throughout the country). Only for fans of embalmed dictators...
If it was about another person it could be ok, but I can´t find anything interesting about this person no matter his importance in history.

Not a place to visit with children at all. When I tried to visit this place I had my three year old daughter on my back in a child Carrier as she was tired. I asked at the front gate if it was OK to have her on my back while on the tour I was approached by a guard to say that you can not visit the masalium with your child on your back. I said I spoke with the staff at the entrance and they said it was ok to do so. As I went to keep up with the tour the same guard approached me from behind and tried to take my daughter out of the carrier she was in and in the process scratched her eye. By this time my daughter was historical and frightened. After I exchanged a few words that I will not repeat here I headed for the exit where I was escorted from the grounds. Not a very pleasant experience for us at all, considering we found the Vietnamese to be very friendly elsewhere.
A died skeleton in cold room, it is not good for children, good for communist who love or want to know something about Ho Chi Minh
Lining up and streamline to get a few seconds view of wax works Ho Chi Minh was not the best place to visit in Hanoi.

I visited to Ho Chi Minh mausoleum on 27th December 2015 at around 10am to pay tribute to the revolutionary leader of Vietnam.I am really shocked and sad to see that ,when I kept my baggage at the security counter in the deposit baggage area, the officer on duty opened my bag and has stole my RayBan sunglasses.I went to the place with lot of respect to Sir Ho Chi Minh and was really felt good when I see the way the complete ceremony was organised,but when I returned back,I found my glasses missing from the bag.I have only bad memories of Ho Chi Minh mausoleum where the pious body of Sir Ho Chi Minh is kept.People in Vietnam not good.
Just a waste of time queuing in the long line, not much things impressive enough here. A big waste of money also
Nothing special to see. A monument to a communist leader. Better to walk along lakes and water.

The queue for this was enormous and we waited for about 30 mins while the guards were doing something. It was hot and water was not allowed. All you see is a body laid out! Would never have come had I realised what it was.
I can't quite remember the details, but I think the mausoleum was only open 3 out of 7 days (at least in October).

Just go since you're already here. If you don't want to waste your plane ticket, you should go. Otherwise, I wouldn't recommend it. Honestly, there's really nothing to see. You'll understand once you go. Trust me.
It's a large square, the kind of place you'd come to on a tour; it's a waste of time and effort for independent travelers. It's located northwest of Hoan Kiem Lake.
This place is not worth going and waste of time. We can't go in as it is close for renovation. Skip this place.
My rating is irrelevant to this place. I just want people to he aware that it is closed on Sundays. I did not know this so I missed my opportunity to visit.

Second time visit to Hanoi to see Unkle Ho with my friend. How unlucky we are.We did some research online before the trip about where Hochimin's body is. Obviously we didn't do proper research.3 days after Vietnam Independence day(2/Sep) Hochimin leaving to Russia for make up as per our tour guide. HUE travel agent assured us we could see Hochimin body yesterday before booking day tour. Obviously he didn't watch the news. So check with Hanoi people whether he is here or not unless you wanna see only giant building.

In typical communist style, cult of personality. A 30-minute queue to see the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh, the first historic president of liberated Vietnam. I wouldn't recommend it unless you have a lot of free time.
Not worthy going! There are so many lines! And they are very restricted with everything (cameras cellphones water). Very annoying! People.that work there are so rude!!!!Not a beautiful place!

After several attempts to find the entrance, we finally arrived at the guard booths where you have to leave your backpack, and at 10:30 am they told us the mausoleum was already closed. According to their information, the opening hours are 9-11 am...although seeing it wasn't a priority if we had gone in. On the way around, you encounter quite unpleasant and rude police officers (no older than 18). Having read some reviews on TripAdvisor, we don't think we missed anything.

We were advised to visit here by other people we met on holiday. What a waste of time we learned it would be. The only remotely interesting item was the HCM statue. There were no other interesting artefacts. Not a lot of English translations either and the toilets were the dirtiest I've seen in Vietnam. Don't bother.

Really wish I hadn't bothered. Walked 50 mins from hotel, got there and queues were enormous. Found out it closes at 11am and it was already 10.30am. Got in queue to be yelled at by a Japanese tourist who accused us wrongfully of jumping the queue (being British we are used to queues and would never dream of jumping in!!!)Waited in line for a while only to be pulled out of the queue with other female tourists to be told we couldn't go in because we were wearing shorts. What a complete waste of half a day in ha Noi. Having visited lots of Wats etc in Thailand they at least understand tourists may not be suitably dressed and supply robes. I think these guys had way more Vietnamese tourists than they can handle so don't care if the tourists get in or not!Sorry if this is a bit of a rant but I wasn't impressed at all!

When we were dropped off at the mausoleum, we were surprised to see that there was a long queue. We waited for a long time before we were ushered in, and we were expected to place our bags at a station near the entrance. The staff at this station were rude and brusque towards me -- while I understand that it was a hot Saturday and she had to combat the crowd, there was no need to glare at me after giving me the luggage tag, or act like repeating oneself is a chore when I asked her to. This trip was simply an incessant wait to see one of the most overrated attractions. After queueing for an incredibly long time, at times under the hot sun, we entered the mausoleum to see the body of Ho Chi Minh. It was a complete waste of time and we wanted unnecessarily long just to see his body, and I was incredibly uncomfortable with this extravagant display. I understand why the Vietnamese people venerate Ho Chi Minh, but such an ostentatious display of his preserved body served to undermine his wishes and values, and this makes everything feel exploitative. Getting my bag after this encounter was a hassle because I had to walk all the way back to the entrance to get my bag in the heat, and two security guards gave me contradictory directions so I didn't know if I should continue on the demarcated path or retrace my steps back to the entrance. At the end, there was also a pathway for locals and foreigners, and I wondered why this was so, and that everyone's movement had to be so restructed. I also got yelled at a few times, and the language barrier served to exacerbate the situation. In fact, I was in such a bad mood that I did not want to see the other attractions in the area, like Ho Chi Minh's Presidential house. It was infuriating to get questioned everywhere I went -- I understood why, but this bordered on paranoia and greatly hampered me as I made my way to get my bag. The upside was that some guards were helpful and pointed the way, but most of the time, I felt like I was being questioned for no reason. I also thought I got lost in this large compound, and worried that I wouldn't get my bag back. I was also falsely led to another baggage station that didn't have my bag, and it was frustrating to finally obtain it. The long queues, walking time and getting fried under the hot sun was not worth it just to see the President's dead body. Do not bother with this attraction at all, visit the museums, cafes, or whatever else tickles your fancy.

The strangest museum I have ever visited! Nothing really made sense to me with lots of abstract art which appeared to have a very loose link to Ho Chi Minh, all very surreal!  The layout appeared to be very haphazard with no real flow.  There was also very few English translations so overall a disappointing experience and one place to be avoided if you don't have much time in Hanoi.  The sign posting outside is very poor which meant we were unable to find Uncle Ho's Stilt House

Actually you will see a copy in wax since the real one is reserved for someone else. It is a must in Hanoi with some precondition. Everyone thought them will see the real one not knowing that they got to see the double in wax.

Honestly go there if you are passionate about Uncle Ho. Yes he was a n inspired leader, but the mausoleum is honestly not a place you would find neither beautiful, interesting, inspiring or anything else. Once you queued for at least 1 hour you will walk by a window and a gard is pressing you to get out. 1 hour for few seconds, honestly not worth it according to me...

This was included as part of a tour and the guide implied it was a highlight and  not to be missed by anyone.  There was a huge queue and we were shuffled from pillar to post before being given special access.  Joining the queue we slowly traipsed past the embalmed corpse of Australia's wartime enemy under the supervision of armed guards, acutely aware that HCM had specifically requested that no mausoleum be built and that his body be cremated and scattered over the various provinces.  I was disgusted.The rest of the exhibits were in better taste (especially his well preserved Peugeot 404 and his chopsticks and bowl) but only raise the rating to one star.  Otherwise it would have been negative!
Worth seeing to please our guests, otherwise there's no point wasting time in front of this monster worthy of communist tyrannies of any country.

Not sure whether it is the HCM house (old house) or presidential palace, but the one I went is just beside the HCM mausoleum.
