The mausoleum is fantastic, gave me goose bumps and eerier feelings to see how they can preserved president HCM body perfectly! The President seems like resting/sleeping peacefully.What i want to comment here is during my visit to the presidential (old house) where when i just walked in and strolling over the house area, trying to read and learn about HCM history, furniture, etc. suddenly me and my family were chased away/OUT from that area just because it's their lunch time, what?? Seriously, lunch time at 11:00 a.m??And how can a tourist spot can be closed for lunch time?? What???We paid 20K VD just to enter the HCM old house, yes it's very little money but it's NOT free!! But the mausoleum is free.At the end of my trip, especially at the HCM Square I can say I learned NOTHING!It seems that this place is 'NOT READY' to 'share' their history stories to the tourist yet. I was very disappointed during my whole visit. I was waiting to come to this place months and months before, and what I expect from this place maybe little higher than what they can give, probably. But, lunch time at tourist stop? hmm..Hopefully they will improved.My advice, just come very early, meaning 08:00 AM you have to be there at the Square already.

I'm not sure about other days, but on Sundays, this place makes lining up for iPhones or Justin Bieber signings look meagre. Thousands upon thousands of Vietnamese descend and line up to see their leader. It is almost worth going for that spectacle alone I suppose. It is bizarre. Needless to say we didn't see the mausoleum or Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body. Disappointed.

No access as the embalmed body had been removed for its annual maintenance and makeover. Given a brief history of the facility and how the Vietnamese Government did not comply with the simple wishes of HCM. Typical communist morbid edifice

We went to visit Mausoleum on Tues 28th April!  Arrived at 9 30 only to find queue streets and streets long!  After trying to join the line at about 10am and to find out it was Independence Day and line moving very slowly we decided not to bother!Maybe wrong day to see this - we may have been lined up all day!  Sorry not that dedicated, although would have been nice to experience!  Maybe they can organize this a little better!S
it's all about HCM so if you are interested in his legacy and what he did than its for you if not, don't bother.

Not much to write in this review . There is nothing except a long queue in hot weather .....all to see a fake dead body ( good air conditioning inside ) . The queue is for 2 hours and you will be able to spend about 2 minutes inside the mausoleum .

Unless you enjoy looking at dead people this is a place to avoid at all costs.  The lineups to get in are LONG as the Vietnamese people love venerating their dead, and it is just not worth the time it takes. Go to the Vietnamese Museum of Ethnology or the History Museum instead.

Ho Chi Minh is a greatly loved man by many Vietnamese. He brought peace back to the people of Vietnam after the north and south divide. He asked that when he dies he gets cremated and his ashes spread in the north, middle and south of the country as a symbol of this. When he died his wishes were not met and his body is now preserved and put on display to tourists. For a few months a year his body is shipped to Moscow for fixing. It is really quite sad. Just something to think about before visiting.
After queing for an hour or more the sight of Ho Chi Mihn was an anticlimax which could well be missed if our guide did not impiy that it was mandator to visit.
Very well presented; the wait and then the circuitous route to the Mausoleum prepare you for a sombre, but moving, experience.

We went to HCM Mausoleum and Museum in Nov 2014 and asked if the ticket price included entry into the Mausoleum and was told yes but when we exited the Museum we were told the Mausoleum was closed for renovations until December. Ummm.... total waste of money as we were not interested in the museum and walked straight through!

Perhaps I am being harsh. I was rushed through place by an attendant as it was closing time.  I couldn't appreciate how this portrayed a great man, full of modern gimmicks.  Perhaps Vietnamese may appreciate this.
No where to sit and admire the gardens, and the whole thing was a one way system to walk through - men blow whistles at you if you stop to look at the amazing fish in the pond.
How can we still, at the dawn of the 21st century, maintain such a morbid ceremony centered around the personality cult so dear to red fascist regimes!

This experience was terrible for the following reasons:*You had to leave all your belonging including you valuables in the massively disorganised luggage counter. You could take your camera with you only to have it taken ten minutes later by another person at another kiosk and there was absolutely nothing to photograph between these two kiosks.*The queue was unbearably long and hot with no fans to keep guests cool as there are in other attractions within Vietnam.*My boyfriend and I only wanted to see the museum (having very little interest in seeing a dead body) but had to go through the queuing rigmarole anyways. *It was packed, I don't just mean it was a busy tourist attraction, I mean it was quite literally CRAMMED full of people.*The One Pillar Pagoda was very nice but has some heavy, messy and noisy construction work going on around it, totally ruining the peaceful atmosphere you usually have at a pagoda.*There apparently was a ticket price admission for the museum but no one seemed to be paying and just walked in?  Overall the disorganisation and discomfort of the whole affair meant that we didn't go inside the Mausoleum or the museum and saw the pagoda for all of two seconds.I really wouldn't bother.

We waited in a very long line for over 2 hours in the sweltering heat for a 2 minutes walk-through of the mausoleum.  While this is a place of reverence for the Vietnamese, tourists would be better off skipping the mausoleum and just visiting the museum and Ho Chi Minh's residence which are right next door and give history and context to his life.

Lining up 1 hour under a killer sun by 36°C should not an option for anyone, I discovered a polished corpse preserved with help of soviet and perhaps chinese specialists.Awful copy of the Lenine's mausoleum, which is not a masterpiece in architecture.

Nothing went right for our visit to this complex. The opening times were not well advertised and we happened to arrive just before the midday close. We had not been warned that women must have a covering of their shoulders in order to go into the mausoleum. The One Pillar Pagoda was being re-worked and therefore not accessible. Visible directions and explanations within the complex (at least in English) were non-existent.

If you don't want to be herded like a sheep to see a corpse that even the Vietnamese themselves say isn't Ho Chi Minh, don't go. The mausoleum is aesthetically horrifying. The only thing worth seeing are the gardens.

I know it's a mausoleum but how they love to throw their weight around and treat everyone like school children. Of course we should respect the place, but it was overdone. Reading about Ho Chi Min's life, he was a simple guy and would have hated what they have done in his "so called honour". we were glad to get away and breath some fresh air, and enjoy the rest of the city. Not an enjoyable experience!
The founder of this great nation deserves something more attractive than this stark concert  block.  Also, to us,viewing a body that has been dead as long as he has is creepy.

Queues were incredibly long. Make sure to wear respectful clothes that cover your knee. Extremely regimented. Felt like we were on a conveyor belt. Everyone had to conform to single file, no speaking, no water, no cameras, no phones, no big bags...The actual museum was very disappointing. No translations into english for a lot of sections so we didnt know what we were looking at. pretty structures and surroundings.
There was nothing worth seeing and an absolute waste of time.Included by most tour operators as a way of providing maintenance of the same thru ticket entry...

Neat orderly lines move slowly for what seemed like blocks. Take off you hat, hands out of pockets, dress respectively, no photos, no videos, ... To see the mummy of Ho Chi Minh. One big plus lots of government employees keeping the line on good behavior. Not worth the time.

I've never seen more boring museum. I was born in socialistic times and I remember how such museums looked like, however I believed last 30 years even Vietnam could learn something about publicity, culture and art. The visitors in the museum are mainly children from various schools, actually it is full of them, so my advice is to avoid the visit to museum and to spare the time for better activity.

Ho Chi Minh has not been in the mausoleum on the 3 separate trips I have been to Hanoi. The website didnt say anything and my hotel didn't know - but when I got there the guards told me he has been in Russia getting fixed for over 3 months. Dont bother going to see the site as your time is better spent elsewhere.

Unfortunately, we were unable to pay an honorable visit to this great leader of Vietnam. Due to a sleeping baby, we were denied entry to the mausoleum. Sadly, this was done in a very unfriendly tone. We were simply told "No Babies" in a surly tone, without any further explanation, while in the background, Vietnamese people with toddlers who had just learned to walk were allowed in.

Went here on a Sunday which in itself was probably a mistake, but it made Disney queues look pitiful.  There were several entrances for pre-booked appointments, VIPs etc, but the main queue was in excess of a mile long and that's what we could see.  Very little shade and zigzagged across several roads.  Met people who had bothered to queue and said they found it worth it with people very moved by the experience. May be go during the week.

I wouldn't stay in a line to see it.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum boasts crowds that would rival most 300 Spartan fight scenes. Long crowds who's vision of personal space rivals most competitive tango dance lessons. Several security check's which were conducted frequently that featured gawking at my partner, who mind you was almost totally covered. They allowed some cameras to be taken through but not my shiny SLR. We were removed from the line and requested surrender my camera, only after to find staff going through the contents of its bag. Upon stating I didn't feel this treatment was fair (expecting to see my camera on eBay) we were spoken to as if we had violently sneezed in the face of security. We followed all the guides on being respectful to the Tomb but were subjected to constant disrespect and ultimately were escorted out of the premise. It took almost an hour to retrieve our gear. The tour is free however sneaky tickets are needed once you compete the tomb visit.
Went here early in morning as advised but queuing was so long that we came away. We're told later that others had queued for over 2 hours.

We didn't realise we were staying just 2 kms from the mausoleum but the taxi metre went so fast it almost spun off the dashboard!  VN 1,630,,000 (USD 55) later and a grand tour of a few blocks twice over, we arrived to find the huge queue.  We didn't bother in the end and walked back to our hotel for FREE!
We will gladly delve into the history of Vietnam; the visit was very interesting, a quiet sadness came over us, it is admirable what the people have achieved.

We were all glad to get out of there, not very friendly people.

In his last will and testament Ho Chi Minh asked that when he died he be cremated. His ashes should be scattered on a peaceful hillside out in the Vietnamese countryside. A small rest-house could be constructed for those who wished to visit the site. He'd use the palace for ceremonial purposes and large meetings, but he actually lived in this beautiful and humble little house.

The day I went they weren't letting people in to see it.
A tomb built with complete disregard for Ho Chi Minh's will.
Hmm. Especially.
An uncomfortable place that doesn't even let you sit
A place without history is meaningless
That's all.
A super brainwashing place for creating gods
For Russian style. Sadness.
Not very interesting
It won't open until August.
Just one word...hot
The queue is too big, ugh
Hot and boring
.General attractions
not really something to see
So boring~
There's nothing to see...
I came with a tour group.
It was closed
Very cold
Rather boring
Just don't expect anything.
I don't need that again...
It doesn't tell me anything
Nothing to see
Little information about
It's hot
frankly it's not very interesting, to enter the mausoleum a route without stopping monitored by the military, entering think about taking off sunglasses and caps, not talking, no photo, we stop...
We arrived on n a Friday afternoon to find point the mausoleum was closed, which was a shame as we would have liked to have seen inside.

Undoubtedly very important for the Vietnamese for the fundamental role of Ho Chi Minh in the history of the country.That said, to visit the interior you have to queue for over an hour (if there is unpleasant sun), airport style checks, absolute prohibition of photographs with a camera deposited at the entrance.The visit is a line up to Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body inside an imposing building but of zero artistic value.In my opinion, apart from being able to say you have been there, in the economy of travel it is better to spend a couple of hours more in Hanoi's old quarter, what is worth seeing of the Mausoleum can be seen from the street outside in front of the Parliament

While there is tight security and therefore a long queue, what you get in return for the visit is marginal. You see a Soviet-style building from the outside and several buildings, where Ho-Chi-Minh is supposed to have sat, eaten, slept etc. Nothing important worth seeing - unless you are a real Uncle Ho fan. Otherwise, save the time.

Stopover more than useless, horrible Soviet-style building not very different from our fascist monuments, you can not do anything, you can not take pictures, monstrous queue, guards staring at you and whistling at all those who handle the cellphone, in front of a useless square all fenced.

The mausoleum itself is impressive to look at but you have to wait about 3 hours to get inside. The other stuff in the park isn't particularly impressive and just not worth the investment of time. When I went it was super super busy, disgustingly so, which may have negatively impacted my view. I would advise people to skip this attraction if you are short on time in Hanoi. If you have the time or really want to go just have a look at mausoleum from the outside, do a quick lap around the park, and get out of there. Don't bother entering any of the paid attractions like the house or museum.

We were not allowed to enter the mausoleum, because our youngest son was too young (ten month old). The "official rule" is that kids under three are not allowed to enter the mausoleum. I'm ok with that rule, but it should be applied to all families then. Several families waiting with us brought kids below the age of three. When I asked the staff why some are allowed to go in and some not, they made up new rules. First additional rule, it's too cold for our kid. Second additional rule, the kid needs to be able to walk on its own. We were not the only ones that had to stay out. I'm really ok if there are clear rules, but from my point of view, the staff arbitrarily selects who is allowed to go in and who needs to stay out. In the end we visited the mausoleum only from the outside.

Interesting to walk around the tomb and explore the gardens / surrounding buildings.  Got a bit confused about the one way system.Extremely bad experience when leaving.  Beware taxi scammers waiting outside the exit for the unwary.  The authorities need to deal better with taxi scams as they seem commonplace.

I'm writing the review even though I wasn't able to visit the interior. The reason is that at the entrance, during the check, not only did they want to confiscate my lighter but also the pack of cigarettes without returning anything at the exit. I understand that you can't smoke, but this seems excessive to me. Furthermore, it was impossible to visit even the one-column pagoda without going through the same security checkpoint. The visit probably could have been interesting but I decided not to do it for the reason stated above.
Interesting to watch if you arrive there when they change the guard. The place itself does not offer much to look at except a big grey building, and the guards defending it.

The staff were rude and hostile, which unfortunately is a common theme in most places in Hanoi.The mausoleum itself is quite quick and I’m not sure what the interest is with seeing a dead body.The surrounding areas were a bit more interesting with displays of how Ho Chi Minh lived “as it was” in the past, but I find it hard to believe it’s not highly staged.This places feels like one big piece of propaganda for north Vietnamese people.Definitely worth skipping if you don’t have lots of time.

We waited for 45 minutes, unsure if the site would even open, despite arriving right at opening time. The impassive guards said nothing. Once inside the park, there wasn't much we could see; the house and the pagoda were closed, even though we were there during opening hours. Finding a seat was impossible, the guards were intimidating, in short, move along, there's nothing to see, and the park itself is nothing special.

A visit to Hanoi wouldn't be complete without a shuffle round Uncle Ho's body.The rather surly soldiers order you into a single file procession and you're in and out in a minute.Then to walk around the grounds đ50,000 where you're made to walk without stopping and view his house on stilts, cars ans so forth.It's  not a bad couple of hours.WATCH OUT FOR ENTRANCE TIMES. THEY VARY.
The cult of personality is truly one of the hallmarks of totalitarian regimes.
Just a big, guarded building, nothing special to see or do in the surrounding park either. Been there, done that.

I did not enjoy this experience, as you begin with having to check your belongings including your camera and water. Make sure ladies, you are covered or you will be denied entry. You are then lead in 2 single file lines in silence for over 1km in the direct sun. As you go there are several armed guards around and will whistle at you if your caught taking photos of the grounds. You are then taken into the mausoleum, again several armed guards and get to do one pass around Ho Chi Minh. I totally understand the guards and being silent but you don't even get to stop and pay respect to Ho Chi Minh, which was what I was there for. Then you get pushed out to the gardens to buy water as by this time you're super thirsty and souvenirs. The grounds are very large and the majority of it was off limits to the public. The areas that were open were very lovely and it was the only place I saw free birds flying around. The museum of Ho Chi Minh is also on the grounds and I'm sure it has some amazing things in it, however we did not go through it.
It was an impressive building with a large structure, but I just looked at it and that was it. Partly due to my own lack of knowledge, but I'd say it's just a place I saw briefly.
Not very interesting. A long wait to see a mummy from afar... the only thing that might be interesting is the smallest pagoda next to the mausoleum, but we can't go inside.

...but everyone goes there. I was there with a group as part of a city tour. Watching the staff's clear instructions in a line of two – which can take a while depending on how crowded it is – is quite unpleasant if you're coming from a Western country. And then all that just to walk around an embalmed corpse in two minutes... I should have had a coffee outside...

Don’t waste your time on this alleged attraction. You have to slowly march in long lines to get to the Mausoleum, and when you finally get there you’ll get a two second glimpse of his dead body. There is no history or information poster anywhere. Then you are shepherded to his house viewing which costs 40,000 Duong per person just to view a few random rooms from the outside.The Hoa Lu Prison on the other hand is well worth the visit- there are excellent preserved areas of the original prison, many letters , statues of prisoners in the cells, letters and many photos.There is an excellent audio guide available for a small fee which tells you many stories of prisoners experiences and takes about two hours.
Closed at certain times of the year. No information from staff; everything had to be found out thanks to Google.
Very formal and institutional. We happened upon it while walking. It's a huge space, and it feels very cold.
A completely uninteresting building. Furthermore, it was closed on Mondays, the day of our visit. Not worth going.

I really can't grasp that someone who did so much good for the country had one last wish and that it wasn't granted. Hi Chi Minh wanted to be cremated and his ashes scattered over three parts of Vietnam, as a symbol of connection. He didn't want a museum or a mausoleum. To see him lying there, embalmed and all, and to walk past him in a procession therefore feels bitter and unfair. Furthermore, it is super crowded there, but it does move very quickly. It is unclear where the exit is and where to retrieve your dropped-off items.

You need to be properly attired, no sphagetti straps, no shorts, flip flops.You need to drop your bags at a security counter.Long queue... unfriendly staff & guards.Visit to HCM Mausoleum spoiled by very rude security at the baggage drop area and unfriendly guards who seemed to discriminate against foreighers and were surly & unfriendly.At the Security counter baggage drop windows, the women security staff were shouting instead of telling us nicely to go to a different window counter. A local Vietnamese girl (who spoke English)   & who were trying to help us was also scolded by the security staff!The reason they were shouting at us, because we were at the foreigner window counter to drop our bags.  They shouted at us because they assume we were Vietnamese because to them we do not look like foreigners and my brother in law who is English and also at the same counter was not scolded bcoz he obviously looks like a foreigner!At the Mausoleum grounds, one of the guards was posing with a local Vietnamese kid and family for photos...and we witness a foreigner asking them politely if they could also take photo with him, he curtly refused and looked so fierce and unfriendly.Hmm...maybe they feel that we are intruders in the sacred HCM Masouleum ...that is why they were so  unfriendly & surly.

Whilst I'm sure this is a meaningful visit for Vietnamese people, to stand in the heat for over an hour to walk past the glass tomb for 1 minute was not worth the time. We got the diostinct impression that this was  a requirement of our tour company since, unlike other excursions, we were not given the option of skipping it for something else. If this is important for you to do, plan to stand in line for 1 - 2 hours in hte heat. The tomb itself is air conditioned.

Visited the Mausoleum and it reminded me of institutional behaviour. The grounds are amazing but the staff are serious and strict. Do not attempt to visit without complying to the regulations. It’s an interesting place but in hindsight I would not have bothered had I known the regime.

After a security check at the entrance to the site, we can discover on this large, austere square the mausoleum of Ho Chi Minh. To the right of this mausoleum, you can enter the gardens of the current presidential residence, but above all, discover the house on stilts and learn about its history. A beautiful discovery. Please note: avoid bringing lighters and chewing gum, and smoking is prohibited.
Went to go in 2018...closed.  Went again, at opening time in the am, line ups over 2 blocks, then more line ups inside!

This site shreaks out communism, the colours, architecture, signs and uniforms.  Very atmospheric.The main man wasn't in residency, he was back in Russia to have his embarlming refreshed.Worth a visit, particularly if you also stop by the Tran Quoc Pagoda in the same vicinity.
A typically Soviet place: immense, empty, and devoid of any charm, accessed through a security checkpoint 😕. One wonders why...
Not worth the time in line. No sense of the person. More like visiting a wax museum with armed guards. Spend time at the citadel and ethnographic museum.

Only got to see it from the outside as we were ushered away by officials and the opening times are very rigid. Hard to walk to from the old quarter and fences make it hard to get closer to see anything. Nicely maintained from what we could see.

Visited late morning. We were allowed in to the complex only to find that the Mausoleum, Museum and Presidential home were randomly closed with no information on opening times. We got waved away by guards when we tried to read information panels, and sent out of the pagoda by monks who were busy on their smart phones inside the temple. The complex is clean and interesting but we were disappointed and a little perplexed as to why everything was closed. A little information would go a long way!

We were there before 11:00 so that we could also enter the mausoleum. The guard observed my girlfriend (regarding dress codes) and let us through, but upon entering, another guard sent us back. My girlfriend was wearing jumpsuit shorts, but according to the other guard, they were too short. We even bought a scarf to put around our waist, but then we were immediately refused entry by the guard who let us through first (with an arrogant look on his face). We went into the park after 11:00 because the mausoleum is closed then and you can walk right in. But it is a boring city park with a mausoleum on it; in my opinion, nothing special.

Despite asking the guards patrolling the area, who told us it was open, we queued for quite a while only to find it closed. We weren't the only ones; we had friends visiting the city a few weeks earlier and the same thing happened to them. This symbol of outdated communism, where someone who probably never wanted to be there lies buried, is guarded by soldiers. They allow you to take photos of the exterior, but you can't get close. I don't doubt that it holds special symbolic meaning for them, but I don't see it as essential.
Too hot in the sun and too many rules and the entrance is not sign posted so a long walk was necessary. You even have to buy your toilet paper. Avoid.
The stage design is impressive, but the show itself leaves much to be desired. I don't want to give too much away, but basically he just lies around and doesn't move at all.
The visit is conducted from a great distance. The guards are vigilant and quick to reprimand. It's just a simple concrete block. There's practically nothing to see. We didn't visit the museum.

Yes- probably a place to visit- but I felt over saturated by the militia watching every step. We were marched through with not a minute to stop and appreciate the history and integrity of the place. Don’t bother going - and if you do- take a water bottle, a hat, an umbrella and sunscreen- you’ll need it.
A very long wait for a 20 second peak at Ho Chi Minh. To me it wasn't worth the wait. For others it might.

The 2 stars rating is only because there is no information about the closure for maintenance. We were aware of the place being closed on Mondays and Fridays, but there was no information about it being closed for longer. Disappointed that we couldn't go into the mausoleum, but his museum and the Pagoda were open. Entry for the museum is only free for locals.

Has anyone actually seen Ho Chi Minh? I’ve been twice in 10 years and he wasn’t there. The guide said he gets more embalming done each year from June 15-August 15 so I guess that’s why. The mausoleum itself is huge but that’s all that is there.
To visit you must find out whether it's off peak otherwise you have to wait for hours to get in. The purpose is to view the body of their Great Leader Ho Chin Minh.
It didn't particularly impress us. We had to go through a security check to access the mausoleum, which didn't seem very logical.
Don't bother visiting the museum; it's empty. The entrance fee is far too expensive for what it is. I suggest you stay outside and see the mausoleum.
large building on an uninviting square. very bombastic. more for the Vietnamese population. was also very quiet
Wasn't really worth it, as you spend so much time waiting in line to see embalmed Ho Chi Minh. Staff didn't speak English and wasn't helpful to wander around

The mausoleum is quite imposing as a structure. Its design is a replica of Lenin’s in Russia. What is great about the landmark is it is spacious and is surrounded by a lot of greens (trees, grass, shrubs), thus, making it a nice place to spend a quiet day. What was a but traumatic is how the guards at the reception/entrance area conducted themselves. We saw them rudely “manhandle” a Vietnamese woman. While we couldn’t understand what the conversation was and if indeed she had broken a law/policy, it is still a big no-no to grab a female like that. One of the female guards was also shooing us away not knowing we were asked by our guide to wait in that place. She just kept shouting at us (and others). While we know we must respect the culture of every country,  we would not also definitely agree to be treated this way. We calmly told her that she does not need to shout because we can hear her. If this is supposed to be a “sacred” landmark and a tourist place, perhaps the reception guards (or military or whoever they are), should be the first to make it as such. People will respect the landmark if the reception-people assigned there are better educated, trained and are oriented in proper behavior.

It was crowded because it coincided with a Vietnamese holiday. I was told there was a two-hour wait, so I gave up on going in. Apparently, foreigners have to pay, but it's free for Vietnamese citizens. I'd like to go in next time.
We were there on May 3rd and wonder if it was closed due to mourning for President X who had died. I didn't get any information, both the mausoleum and museum were closed.
Just wandered through this massive space.  Had no wish to see the body. Be aware that security will check you even if walking in the grounds.

I'm sure Ho Chi Minh would not approve of thousands of people a day walking past his dead body. Vietnam is a country divided the North worship him and people in the South detest him. We waited an hour and a half in the baking heat with thousands of extremely rude Chinese people to be herded like cattle through the mausoleum. To some he is a hero to others a terrorist, visit the whole of the country and then make your own desicion.
A lot of people have a long queue. In the shorts are not allowed. Admission is free of charge. It was really hot.

The fuss they make here just to run past a wax figure in five seconds is insane. If you try to talk, you get dirty looks, and stopping is completely out of the question. It's totally over the top. Even the tour of Ho Chi Minh's former apartment wasn't really interesting. It seemed like every kindergarten and school in Vietnam was there that day. It was incredibly crowded and, unfortunately, uninteresting to me.

There are more interesting things to see in Hanoi than this Mausoleum; the changing of the guard doesn't add anything. Go see the One Pillar Pagoda instead, but above all, and I insist, the Temple of Literature, and the must-see for me, the water puppet theater, a curiosity of Hanoi. The population living near the railway line is where you can enjoy an egg coffee if you feel like it.
Unless you're a staunch communist, I wouldn't spend more than 10 minutes there.

Took nearly an hour to file past a mummified body that they allow you about 1 minute to see. The gaurds are a very unhappy looking lot! The just order the crowds to keep in line and keep moving.Must say the grounds & gardens were very nice..

Nice district with many embassies and official buildings. The temple is extremely inaccessible to the public with only 2 hours of opening time - and heavily guarded with whistling guards and a ban on lighters and chewing gum. Decent walk if you like to walk a bit, but you can just as easily walk somewhere else - or drive past in a taxi.

Our travel group attempted to visit the complex twice during a weekend in early March. Both times, whether very early or somewhat later, there were endlessly long lines (consisting mainly of school classes) waiting or shuffling for entry.
It wasn't particularly interesting, but our guide was determined that we visit this site. Many Vietnamese people come here to take photos, but apart from a large building, there's nothing to see.
Not really of interest except for a very large esplanade and the handsome uniforms of the guards... And a beautiful garden!

We got to our hotel and were talked into a Hanoi City tour for what I thought was a price of $54 (including lunch). It ended up being $54 per person so total of $108. When the guide got to our hotel , he asked what we wanted to see. I gave him a list of 3 or sights and he kind of pushed me into seeing the mausoleum first. On the way over he gave us some good insights into Vietnam and Hanoi although his English was a little hard to follow. Arriving in the area of the mausoleum I couldn't believe the line. It was a Sunday and lots of people from the countryside come into Hanoi on buses to see this site. We were probably in line 2 to 3 hours. It does move fast but I bet it was the longest line I've ever been in. During the time in line it was cool seeing people doing group exercise classes in the parks -  never saw anything like that - it was like a taste of local normal life.During the time in line I got the whole spiel on Uncle Ho and how great communism is. (Really , cause the only reason your country is doing so well is the market reforms you instituted in the 90s). So I listened politely and wondered how long this would take. Fortunately it was March and the weather was pleasant but mild.Even once you get to the entry gate and have your bags checked, it still takes 45 minutes to get to the mausoleum. As I said the line moves fast , you keep moving as you go past the body, you probably only actually view it for 60 seconds max. It is almost a religious experience for the native born people, not so much for me.Once we were done, we were taken to a great Vietnamese restaurant for a lunch which was excellent. After that our half day tour was over. We had the guide drop us at Ho Loa prison which we toured on our own. All in all, I wish I had insisted on seeing other sights in Hanoi instead of this one.

I got the impression that all Vietnamese guides are required to bring you to Ho Chi Minh's memorial. We were on a tour, but had a local guide in Hanoi. Nobody asked us if we wanted to see this. We were lucky - the mausoleum is closed on Monday so we didn't have to wait in a long line to view Ho Chi Minh's body, which I thought would be pretty gruesome anyway. We were told that a few months out of the year he's sent to Russia for a little tune up and then they use a wax replica in his place, so who knows what we would have been treated to anyway..

We got up early as we were told to get there early as the queues could be very long. We arrived at 07:20 to find the queue stretched forever and there were no tourist signs to help the traveller understand the process.We finally got to the back of the queue to find that queuing isn’t a Vietnamese custom and found ourselves going backwards after 1:30 hours!!We gave up and went to the pool

We have to check out bag and pram which is fine, however we were not able to walk back through the grounds the quick way as we were the last entry for the day, we had to exit the grounds walk 1.3km AROUND the outside back to the bag check location. Absolute disgrace we had to carry our daughter while navigating streets. No staff inside could understand that we wanted to get out bags and just ignored us or pointed us to the road!
Like the presidential palace, worth a visit if you're in the area, but not particularly interesting. A concrete block in front of a square...
If you're passing by, make a detour! You'll be able to say "I saw it"... Apart from that, there's not much to see except for the exterior architecture... and the changing of the guard ceremony...

I am in a wheelchair and was allowed entry into the mausoleum but had to use their manual wheelchair. Four men in military uniform carried me up the multitude of steps to see Ho Chi Minh. Like HannoH3 said, visitors are not allowed to take pictures, our backpacks and iPads had to be checked in.  We were also asked not to talk while in line and a senior staff member accompanied me and my family group of 4 plus a tour guide the entire time.  The entire viewing time took about 1 minute.After the visit to see HCM, we weren't allowed to use the Mausoleum's wheelchair to go to the Museum which was in the same complex only a few hundred feet away.  We were required to return it IMMEDIATELY to the entrance from where we left my personal wheelchair, accompanied by a staff member.My tour guide asked if we could use my own wheelchair to visit the other buildings in the complex, such as the Museum.  We were told we could but we had to come back to the complex after the Mausoleum was closed at 12 noon.  When we returned, we were not allowed to enter.  They kept pointing at my wheelchair.  It didn't matter even when my tour guide explained what their superior had said that morning because by the afternoon, another superior was in charge.  We left and tried another entrance with the same result.  My tour guide wasn't ready to give up so we tried a 3rd entrance. He spoke to a man in military uniform of senior rank if we could enter using my manual wheelchair.  The man assured us we could, so we line up again.  But when we got to the security checkpoint we were denied entry again.  The military officer who said we could pass through was informed by someone higher in rank, whom we never saw, that we couldn't enter because of my wheelchair.  The military officer took pictures of my wheelchair to show that it was a harmless, folding wheelchair but I was still denied entry.  The tour guide said, if you don't trust my wheelchair, provide me one of the museum's but there was none they could provide.  Apparently, all they had was the one by the Mausoleum.I was naturally disappointed because I was interested in knowing about Vietnam's history and seeing her historical places not just from what you can read online. I didn't want to just see the preserved body of HCM but wanted to see the other buildings that all visitors were allowed to see at the complex, especially of his life and works. But because I am disabled and in a wheelchair, I was discriminated against.  Sad but true.

Here too, it's free, you enter a large square, well kept, beautiful flowers etc etc the mausoleum is right in front but you can't go in so you take a photo from outside and go home... if you have nothing to do I recommend you take a healthy walk 😂
The monument is worth seeing, but nothing more. Visits to the tomb are only in the morning. We were lucky enough to be there during the changing of the guard, which was interesting to see!

Disappointed is an understatement. From 7:20 until 10:10, when we finally entered the mausoleum, the queue was utterly unreasonable. We arrived knowing that we might have to wait around 45 minutes at that time, but not this barbaric wait to actually see... nothing? The only truly nice thing was seeing the guards' military uniforms and the ceremonial event we witnessed during one of the marches.
We had time to spare and after checking the Hanoi Old Quarter, we decided to walk there to take a look.Interesting scenario for pictures, not so much for anything else.

One of must-see locations in Hanoi. Open only for few hours on Friday so mind that. As I didn't get inside, I would say that this is a nice walk in a park.I met grumpy guardians and I had a feeling that they were unpleasant just because they wanted to show me a power they have. I dislike that a lot.
It wasn't to my taste. I think I've seen it once and that's enough! It might have meaning for Vietnamese people, but... I wasn't moved.

If you only have a couple of days, save your time and skip this one. Visit if you have lots of time. The check in process is more than painful. Made us leave our iPads and computers with them, but allows smart phones inside, but They don’t allow you to take any pictures inside the mausoleum. The grounds are beautiful, well tended.
We went with excitement and curiosity, but it was closed. They didn't give any explanation. We returned disappointed. We had to be content with seeing the building from the outside.
...a stroll across the square is quite enough... let's move on! We're not interested in places like this. Ho Chi Minh didn't want this kind of monument. Period.
The mausoleum was a stop on our tour.

Our visit to this site was disappointing as we arrived a bit late in the morning and the viewing was closed for the day.The point of the visit isn’t so much to see an embalmed (and supposedly decaying) body but to get some sense about how venerated “Uncle Ho” is - especially among many respectful young and old folks in the North. We enjoyed listening to our guide tearfully describe his improbable ascent and why reunification was so key to his strategic thinking.A visit to Mr. Minh’s compound was more interesting.  There you can see where he spent his last days and many key artifacts such as his office, cars and beloved koi (who, as legend has it, climb onto the steps of the sprawling pond to be fed upon hearing his clapping hands).

the guards are hella entitled and perverted i couldn't enjoy even walking around without a single one every few feet making lewd comments at me and my friend. we were next to the buddhist temples as wellnice architecture tho i guess

President Ho Chi Minh has played important role in current Vietnam’s history and therefore his mausoleum and museum is point to visit.The Ho Chi Minh museum wasnt that nice and there were lots of English language explanations missing.

It's not worth it. Spending hours in line to see the embalmed body and then the house where he lived is definitely not worth it. It's interesting to see the ostentatious security, a remnant of a closed socialist regime (for us in Brazil who have never experienced anything like this), but that's all. It's safe to go in the afternoon (visiting the body is only allowed in the morning) when there are far fewer people.
You can see it from the road. But it you are a real glutton for punishment tour the whole HCM complex. The mausoleum bears a string resemblance to Lenin’s tomb - which is hardly a coincidence.
A big building and not much else.  Can't see anything other than the outside.  Not something I would go out of my way to see.
After queuing up in a military-style line, guided by guards, you go up into the mausoleum, walk around the sarcophagus, and then you're back down!

A gigantic building completely against Ho Chi Minh's own will who would rather have money spent on schools. Hard to understand how/why it was built. Emotional and impersonal place. We didn't go in out of respect for Ho Chi Minh himself.

We arrived at around 10am and the queue went round the complex. It was easily a 2-3 hour wait to get in, and the mausoleum closes at 10:30. If you are not bothered about seeing the inside of mausoleum, go in the afternoon and the whole complex is almost empty. It is calm and tranquil and a nice change from the busy traffic outside.
Not much to see, it’s open from 7-11am. Looks much better at night with lights on. Okay for a quick few snaps.

Tried the first day to visit and were sent around the block when a clear pathway to the mausoleum lay in from of us. Crossed forearms said no entry yet not a single sign said so. Next day we went 2 hours earlier than opening time to be first in queue, that should work. Err, no. The queue ran out the park, down the street and for at least 2.5 k's with 4 visitors per line, thousands upon thousands. So we gave up. No signs in other than Vietnamese to tell us anything and the previous day also nothing! Suggest use a guide, I cannot recommend this attraction based on this.
There is always long standing line.  Once inside several minutes is all given to view ho chi minh.  It is a mystery if indeed it is REALLY ho chi minh or a wax figure.   Personally I would no bother.
I arrived around 8:20 AM by taxi from the Old Quarter, which cost about 50,000 dong.

Tried to visit twice on our few days spent in Hanoi. Both days authorities had blockaded a large area surrounding the mausoleum so we had to take a big detour which wasn't ideal in the heat. The first time we went the whole area wasn't accessible to anyone. The second time the queue was at least 2 km long and that wasn't even including entering the complex and this was at 8.30am. Would have liked to have experienced this place but they made it so difficult to do so we had to skip it.

We got to the Mausoleum at 745 and waited almost 1.5 hours just to get through security, we then waited a further 45 minutes to get into the actual building only to get told afterwards that Ho Chi Minh didn’t even want this, he wished to be cremated in his will! Otherwise to see how he lived and the history was quite interesting, but the site was very busy!

This is literally 2 hours of your day to see Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body for 1 minute. The rules are strict going in, you have to deposit your bags and cameras, and cover your knees and elbows. There is a Ho Chi Minh museum to visit afterwards. Personally I do not think this is worth your time in Hanoi. Spending 2 hours queuing and being bossed around is not justified by quickly seeing Uncle How's body (and as people have pointed out, he looks too perfect to be real considering he died in 1969 and was embalmed a bit after this) The museum is inferior to the other military museums in the area and I think your time would be better spent in these.

I totally agree with the following reviews :1 .  "....There was indeed a line when we arrived, but it moved quickly, mainly because no one is allowed to stop moving ever. You shuffle around the compound from the entrance to the mausoleum without stopping or moving fast enough to walk with a normal stride length.That pace continues through the mausoleum past Ho Chi Minh's body and out the other door. The AC is a bit of a shock after the heat outside, but apparently, rubbing your arms is disrespectful-- a soldier told me to keep my arms down by my sides....."  -  (by TeachT2 ) .2. "......Nothing out of the ordinary,too many restrictions on where to walk,what to photograph etc.I didn't enjoyed it at all,waste of time only...."  -  (by Antonoiu A) .3.  ".....To get to him you have to walk for 2 to 3 klms, up stairs and through the main mausoleum for a 1 minute glance at his body with soldiers keeping you moving.No chance to stop and reflect...."  -  (John B) .4.   "....Consequently, walked directly in with no waiting. When you go into the mausoleum, just make certain you do not walk with the hands of yours behind the back of yours...."  -  (Kwanjun Y) .And so on .Architecturally - the Mausoleum structure is very impressive . Towering severely over the grounds and the tourists/local crowds , making you feel really small . Some may call it interesting . Some may call it beautiful .Myself , I don't mind crowds . Really . Other people want to see/visit same places you want to see .  Also , I DO RESPECT the story and history of HCM  and the Vietnamese people . But I do not like to be pushed around like goats being herded ; I don't like to be told to take off my optical sunglasses and my cap ; I don't like it when they "confiscate " your cameras and small personal mineral water bottle . No , I don't . So , if you are like me  - avoid the Mausoleum . If you are already in the area - do visit  HCM Presidential Palace Historical Site (See separate review) .
Little English, not so interesting.Free English speaking guides may not!Choose carefully.Ho may not be home so ask before going to see his embalmed body.Closed Monday and afternoons

Of course, this place holds great significance for Vietnamese people, but the explanations in English were poor, and some areas requiring an entrance fee were off-limits, so I wondered if it was really worth paying to go inside. Perhaps I felt that way because it was extremely hot.
You probably have to be a communist to appreciate this mausoleum. If you're French, you should also spare a thought for our young people who gave their lives in this country to defend democracy.

I lived in Europe during the iron curtain times when people lined to buy basics in the Eastern European countries. I was always fascinated about why people wanted to see Lenin tomb despite their daily straggles. So I was biased when I visit Ho Chi Ming embalmed body and not in a positive way.I think that the place was creepy as there is more solemnity in a tomb than is a wax figure.Laura
Very long line so we opted not to go. I think waiting time is more than an hour and you are not allowed to take pictures inside.
If you are not fascinated by the history of Vietnam, you won't enjoy this place.Parts of the exhibition didn't even have any English translation.
I couldn't help but visit the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum.

This was my included in our tour, the line wasn’t too long, it was a bit if a walk and yes the mausoleum was air conditioned but not sure why anyone wants to see a mummified dead person, even someone important.

Maybe it was because of the weather, but it wasn't that great... It was too hot, and the timing didn't work out, so I couldn't even go inside. If you're planning to visit, make sure to check the opening hours carefully, and I recommend going in the morning to avoid the hot midday heat!

Ho Chi Minh is a historical figure of immense importance in Vietnam, and locals pay him various tributes, such as bringing flowers to the mausoleum or queuing for hours to enter and venerate his embalmed body (inside the mausoleum). For us, who didn't feel that devotion to the figure, it left us a little cold. The mausoleum is a very large (and not particularly beautiful) building that you can't approach; there are soldiers at the entrance making sure of that. It stands in the middle of a gigantic square reminiscent of Havana's Revolution Square. In the center of the square is a huge Vietnamese flag that is lowered every day at 9:00 a.m., and Vietnamese people passing by must remain silent and stop before it as a sign of mourning for Ho Chi Minh.
Very disappointed with this "visit," rushed, poorly received, and with very little to see apart from an untranslated quote and the man's remains...

Disappointing... we were expecting a must-see in Hanoi and were ultimately very disappointed. The staff is very unpleasant to foreigners, the visit to the mausoleum is rushed, and finding the start of the queue is a nightmare. On top of that, there are huge crowds for barely 5 minutes inside the mausoleum.
The place is spectacular but we couldn't go inside, so it was a bit disappointing 😞 but maybe it was because of the dates or the time we went

10 years ago I come to visit and it was closed for maintenance, I finally made it back and wouldn’t you know it closed again for maintenance!!!I was so disappointed and can’t believe everyone I spoke about it to didn’t let me know before I went out there. Really upset but I suppose it has to be done on a regular basis 😢

We went there out of curiosity, despite our dislike for the leader. We bought inexpensive tickets and... accidentally found ourselves in the museum. It turned out the mausoleum was closed for unspecified reasons. Note! On some days, it's open for short periods, only in the mornings.
Monument is closed now so you stand in the sun and look from the outside. More interesting is the house and gardens where he lived although the grand house is also closed to the public
Basically we didn’t get to see much of anything because it was closed off and the guards kept telling us to walk in different directions just like if they were mocking us. We did not feel welcome.
Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum has a small indoor museum, while the mausoleum itself is outdoors.

It's not worth wasting a minute on it when you have all the natural wonders Vietnam has to offer. It's a building with nothing special about it, no decoration, nothing. In Hanoi, experiencing the city's hustle and bustle and seeing the Temple of Literature is enough; everything else is just about adequate.

If you've been to Washington, don't expect anything similar; it's not really a place to visit. Basically, you take a photo at the turnstile and leave. If you have extra time and nothing to do in Hanoi, you can go there, but otherwise, it's not recommended.

My partner and I stopped here, hoping to get out of the heat for a bit while walking around and visiting the sights. This museum is not air conditioned. It seemed more like an art gallery than a museum. I was expecting to find more information about Ho Chi Minh, but didn’t find much here, mostly abstract art and various information about Vietnam politics.
We tried twice to enter during the posted hours. Neither time was it open. More annoying was that none of the security officers working were willing to help visitors.
I’m not sure if we were badly informed before going here, but it was disappointing. Just a building with an armed guard that you couldn’t get near.
In October 2017, when I went to Hanoi, the museum was closed, so my "visit" was limited to walking around the grounds. The square in front of it is very impressive.

While visiting the mausoleum of Ho Chi Minh, Presidential Palace, and One Pilllar Pagoda may be on your list while visiting Hanoi, plan ahead.  If you do not get here very early, there will be a line stretching around the corner and down the block that would make the Rolling Stones jealous.  Maybe a tour package would allow you to bypass some of this drudgery, but trying to do this by showing up at 0900 will result in a several hour wait with a number of vendors trying to sell you things you don't need/want.  It wasn't worth the effort, time, or expense in our opinion as other attractions are nearby.

Like all mummies (Padre Pio, Popes, and the like), it's a monument to idolatry that leaves things as they are, a gigantic construction with a long, forced route through soldiers in full uniform to see a mummy that actually looks like wax. Its validity is only for history books.
The grounds are very beautiful and there are many tourists, but be aware that it's closed on Mondays and you can't go inside. That's a shame.
Can’t go inside. Nothing much to see but a large building with honor guards and a large plaza outside. Other than to say you were there there’s not really much to see or do.

Had to stand in orderly queues for ages, strictly in twos, (just like the local school children visiting at the time) with guards ordering the tourist visitors to stop talking, remove hats and sunglasses, even when we we still outside in the sun, but ignoring the behaviour of local visitors.  All this to file past the  body of a president who wanted to be cremated rather than to have his body on public display.  Not worth it just to see the rest of the former president’s palace.
This visit isn't that important, honestly, I don't recommend it at all; it's just seeing the corpse of the socialist leader.

Please prepare to wait in a line - if there are large groups of school children they skip the line and it can take 2 hours to get to view his body. If you are prepared for this, then you will not be quite as crabby as I was as I was unprepared and had no idea there would be a line many blocks long! It was an interesting experience, nonetheless. No chewing gum, no sunglasses, hands out, move along. And, his body, hard to believe it’s real.

A visit to the mausoleum of the iconic Ho Chi Minh, his most famous alias, is a must-do on organized tourist tours (most of which are Chinese, by the way). This does lead to some limitations. There are months like October and November when the body is stored away for restoration. Also, entry is only permitted in the mornings, and since work stops at 11:30 in Vietnam, everyone arrives at the same time, resulting in queues. I waited in line for about an hour, and the visit itself only lasted a few minutes. It's really not worth the wait. A more interesting option is the subsequent visit to the park surrounding the old presidential villa from the French era. This leads to the motor pool with the state limousine, his first residence, and the stilt house with its attached bunker and infirmary by the lake. This year, however, these sights were only accessible in the mornings via the mausoleum entrance. I therefore recommend visiting the mausoleum area in the afternoon, viewing the mausoleum only from the outside, and then going into the park with its other attractions. The park also has the character of a botanical garden. However, admission requirements can change at any time, so it's advisable to check beforehand.

You have to wait in line for a long time to see a dead person. That's rather boring. The whole experience and the surrounding circumstances are more entertaining. The queue itself is usually quite long. Stepping out of line is punished by whistling attendants. They meticulously ensure that the rules are followed. After entering the mausoleum, you walk in line the entire time, and very importantly, you're not allowed to stop. So, during the tour, you glance to your left at the deceased in a glass case, for about 30 seconds, and then you're back outside.
Putting aside the 40,000 VND entrance fee, all I could think was, "I should have just taken a picture outside and left."

Think carefully if you want to go in here. Maybe leave it for the fans. There was a two hour line at 8.30am. It is only open for viewing in the mornings. Our tour group  decided to skip it and came back to look around the grounds later in the afternoon which was interesting enough.
I have a hard time understanding this cult place for a man who let about 3 million people die to fulfill his ambitions for a united Vietnam.
See the soldiers parade and the grandness of the mausoleum. Not too far walk from the old quarter. Probably about 20mins walk

Wish our guide had warned us about the 1and half hour queues. Would probably have given this a miss. Significant to the history but too controlled .visitors are he added to the body  and spend about 30 seconds filing past in silence. Would have preferred to just stroll around the rest of the site which was very pretty and included his house and other interesting sights.

I was there in 1997 - HCM was in Beijing. My wife was in Hanoi several months before we visited, he wasn't there and when we went, closed, despite being told it was open!I have grave doubts (no pun intended) that HCM is actually there or, they know I am coming and move him. However, they really should have the promulgation of opening times sorted by now. So do check before you go if the mausoleum is open, and don't be too disappointed if it's shut when you get there.Still remains an impressive building....from outside.That said, HCM wanted to be buried in the north, central and south, so not sure this is what he would want. But I guess in Vietnamese history, he is worth honouring.
Due to the long waiting lines (+100m) and fact that the Mausoleum was not open in the afternoon, we were not able to visit.

It represents an interesting part of history and the open area is pleasant. It was not possible to enter the building and the changing of the guard is nothing special. I may be that tourists are brought there out of a sense of patriotism or duty. You're not missing much if you don't go.
Large building on the very large square near the presidential palace. Open to visitors only on Sundays.
A cubic building, gray concrete with no frills. For a bit of fun, you have to do the changing of the guard... without crossing the yellow line.
It’s a mausoleum.thats it.we spent 1 minute  in front of the mausoleum then we left.you cant walk around it.only the front is kind of open for the public.
Rather classical communist era massive mausoleum.Open a few hours a day, we did not see the embalmed body which we are told is due for some restoration in the near future.
A long queue. And a solemnly walk. Paying respects to Ho chi min. You must do it! An experience. Hat off,no talking,solemn face, showing respect. Not convinced.be respectful to the guards.

If you go on a day when the line is short, you may enjoy seeing HCM embalmed in a glass coffin (in the same way as Lenin). As far as the pomp and circumstance goes, I was not impressed with the soldiers in ill-fitting uniforms and dull Soviet architecture of the mausoleum itself. If you’re going to embalm and display and old leader, you gotta go all out! This display is pretty lame.
I visited it and in my opinion it's not even worth the time I wasted, it's a mausoleum like Lenin's in Moscow. Stop.

I queued for more than an hour on the weekend. My original plan was to skip the mausoleum and visit the former residence and square next door, but I was told that I had to queue up to pay my respects at the mausoleum first. I was not allowed to take pictures or move around freely. I was even allowed to use my phone while queuing!

A paid museum offering access to a wealth of information about Ho Chi Minh, as well as the garden and his former residence. It explains how he grew up and became an important figure in Vietnamese history and the end of the war. I recommend this place for history buffs. However, families with children might find it a bit boring as there isn't much to see.

we did not visit, the hour were only from 7:30am-10:30am and we arrived at 11 the first day. we returned he following day at 8:30am and the lines were very long. so we moved on.  figured we could always see a photo online sometime.entrance gate on far side from Old Quarter, a 20+ minute walk.we went to the Citidale instead, with lots of young school children

The place is big and impressive, hourly change of guards. Haft of the year Mr Ho not even in his residence for face lift in Russia. Inside is freaky feeling and total silent. Under a dim light, two guards stand to guarding the body of Mr Ho if, Mr Ho is in. Outside,The locals enjoy jogging in cool evening after sunset. One positive thing in here, there is no traffic and honking noise. Not much to see, except taking picture and post on FB to tell all your friend “been there”.

Ok we happened to strike a particularly hot week when we were in Hanoi. temps of around 43 degrees!!! ouch so waiting in line for close to 3 hours and having all waterbottles  taken from you at the gate ( to preserve site without litter ) meant things were never going to end well. half the tour party we were with were just about hallucinating with dehydration. Advice would be to get there super early to avoid the worst queues know to mankind. once inside ( with air-conditioning ) you can appreciate the effort they have gone to in regard to preserving his body. you don't get to stand long tho as guards are pushing you along. in and out in less than 10 mins hardly seemed worth the pain of the queues and heat. But not something you would see every day is it....just pick the right day is all im saying

This is just a large formal area with a monolithic brutalist building at its centre containing Ho Chi Minh’ body, contrary to his explicit wishes. The greater complex contains two of Ho’s houses, one colonial French, the other Vietnamese, which are marginally more interesting.

We walked to the monument from Chua Tran Quoc, and the first shock was seeing the long line of Sunday visitors waiting to get in. The second surprise was that there was no access from Hung Vuong street. We had to walk a long way around the grassy area which was guarded and barricaded off to the single entrance on the opposite side. Then there was a security check and more lines. We finally abandoned our mission to pay our respects and settled for a visit to the one pillar pagoda. Since some reviews say that there were no lines I imagine weekdays are better.
Not my cuppa tea.... long queues with police/soldiers demanding you tke your hands out of your pockets and telling you not to walk on the footpath. It was bizarre

After long que , but moves quickly , you enter the mausoleum , rushes past the body and out , no one tells you to visit his house before you go to museum ( for which you pay ) and then can’t go back to his house unless you leave and renter. Was Dis pointed at the lack of organization.

Arrived as part of a booked tour to find 2 hour queue ,in the rain. Although an extremely important statesman and 'Father' of modern Communist Vietnam Ho Chi Minh's body laying in state was really not of interest to me and certainly not worth the extensive wait.

Nothing much to do here but to be able to say yes visited it while in Hanoi. Walked up from Old quarters to this place n then just walked to the lake near it, sat for a while n took a 'grab' bike back to the happening old quarters which is fun. This is more a touristy thing to do. There was something next to it where people were buying tickets (not sure what it was but looked like a park), the palace was also next to it. Had lost interest by the time so just took a photo n walked by. Well can say paid my respect to Ho Chi Minh n had a nice walk through a lot of quarters of Hanoi
... the postcard would be sufficient. One cannot come close to the mausoleum,  guards every few meters are watching that no one crosses the line.
A communist attraction, looking at this place one can only conclude one thing, how great leaders are having fun and people are starving.

We spent a while trying to find this after reading reviews on TA and what a disappointment it was.No access inside and could only look at a cold, bleak concrete monument.We missed seeing other attractions after trying to find this and we wish we hadn't bothered.The are a lot of much better attractions than this in this wonderful city so don't waste your time...download a picture instead if you need to then go look at something else...

It was closed to visitors for refubishment which probably means they are dreaming up a bunch of new propaganda to embelish their history and encourage their people.It is interesting because you can appreciate the approach of the Communist government
He was unquestionably Vietnam revolutionary face and remarkable leader.  The moselium is a bleak stalinist edifice.  Spend your time elsewhere unless you are into this kind of thing.
There isn't much to see here. The exterior looks good and can make for food photos with its well maintained gardens. But that's about it.

Just a brick and mortar structure with lot of grandeur but no beauty built denying a dying man's wish.if you do go there, try to catch the change of guard ceremony for a few minutes of entertainment in an otherwise boring visit.

In terms of architecture, I found it ugly – monolithic. The surrounding park, however, does have beautiful plants, especially bonsai, next to a temple whose name I don't recall, but it's near the mausoleum.

The main Vietnamese monuments are located in a large square/street. This is the first one you come across. From the outside, it's nothing special, but the Governor's Palace, a little further down, is very beautiful. Unfortunately, we weren't able to go inside; it's under restoration.
A calm and green space in the heart of this bustling city!
So, yeah. Located in a large square to house the mummy of Ho Chi Minh, in the style of a Lenin cult, etc. A robotic changing of the guard and all those things typical of bygone eras.
We paid for a tour and Mo Chi Minh Mausoleum was closed for renovations.  although the walk in the square is interesting, not being able to access to the Mausoleum was disappointing.
It was unique because I could feel a different atmosphere from the downtown area of ​​Vietnam with

Went to see the mausoleum in October and unfortunately it was boarded up for annual maintenance. The grounds are very interesting though and was good to see where he lived and worked. I also really enjoyed seeing his cars. However we were rushed through everything and couldn't stop or pause to look at things properly given the number of other visitors, some of whom could learn better manners. An interesting insight into the life of 'Uncle Ho'.
I went there but it was closed. Ho Chi Minh was taken away to have some work done. Hilarious. Maybe a good place to go when he returns.

To be honest, it was just a visit I took after going to a nearby museum. I didn't really feel anything special here. It's just a large tomb, and it makes me think that the person inside must have been an extraordinary individual.
Ho Chi Minh certainly played a pivotal role in the history of independence, but the monument itself is uninteresting.

At this time of year (OCT) the body of HCM is sent to Russia for maintenance purposes (???). So the structure is closed up tight. You can walk around, within bounded zones, and watch the changing of the guard, otherwise not much to see.
We had it on the tour, but it's nothing special. The park is beautiful, but I recommend getting lost in the streets of the old city or taking a nice rickshaw ride.
I expected to see something super unusual, but it turned out to be pretty banal and simple. There's nothing to do there for more than 10 minutes.
I think it would be good to visit after learning a bit more about Vietnamese history

We got there in the morning ons Sunday I believe but the lines oh my was incredibly long, we waited with my niece who was only 1.5yrs old for what seemed like a lifetime... probably around 2.5 hrs We finally got in through the doors and guess what more lines and more linesWe got to see the king or whatever at this point I didn't really care who it was... and it was literally you wait in like for hours to have a minute to walk around and see this dead body being preserved It was interesting before lining up for hrs but by the time we got there we were all grumpy for waiting so long to actually see something that it was a disappointment I wouldn't recommend it for young kids especially with the smells around and people staring.Unfortunately it wasn't the best day out for us
I couldn't understand why this place is topmost attraction in Hanoi. Not a must visit place . It takes around 20 mins and many things were beyond my comprehension.
It’s not like I respect Ho Chi Minh in the first place, so what’s the point?

All organised  tours take you here, but it's basically a waste of time.  The outside, which is much more impressive than Lenin's tomb in Red Square, is interesting to look at for about 2 minutes, but it's definitely not worth spending ages in a queue to go inside .. unless you like being in an endless line with chattering school kids.  Much better to wait for your group in a nearby café and watch the world go by.
It was like India gate of India, bit open place but nothing much to see or do.Better to spend time in some good cafe in city.
Was looking forward to the visit to this historical site - but when we got there - well within the advertised hours it was closed. So check your info before you go.

On the positive side, it is impressive to see and to experience. But what a hassle. We couldn't even find the entrance. Once at the entrance, I wasn't allowed inside because I was wearing a dress that was too short and had to buy a scarf to tie around it. Once in line, there was a woman in front of me wearing an even shorter dress who was allowed in without any problems. After that, you have to go through the metal detector, hand over your bag, and take out your camera. Then 'the line' begins. A long, steady line to get to the building. Halfway through the line, I have to hand over my camera. You are allowed to keep your phone and wallet, though. Upon arriving at the ugly building, you also have to walk two abreast and you aren't allowed to say anything. After 30 seconds, you are back outside.
We went with high expectations because of the love people have for it, but honestly, it wasn't worth it. It's free, but women have to cover their elbows and knees.

If one is wiĺling to buy a hat similar to the one used by Ho Chi Min, this is the place. Otherwise it is a waste of time waiting three hours in Hanoi's unmercifull wheather to have a glimpse of the embalmed leader.

A 45-minute queue to see the embalmed Vietnamese Savior...??? I personally found it very uninteresting. Driving past the mausoleum would have seemed perfectly sufficient, so as not to have missed the monument.
My family and I went to visit it after reading in the guidebook that it was one of the most beautiful and interesting places to visit in Hanoi. It was really ugly. Nothing original.
With the title of this review in mind, it is worth a visit. However, you do need to be there early in the morning and be prepared to join a longer queue.
I think there is a lack of audio information options in different languages ​​to learn more about the history of Vietnam and the life of Ho Chi Minh.

Breakfast at 8 then taxi and then the queue went on and on and on and onHe had not seen it as long and said it was at least 3 hoursMuch respect for Uncle Ho but three hours in the sun on a very hot day we thought another time

Wouldn't particularly recommend this museum. Not much to see. Perhaps influenced by the fact we got completely soaked in a tropical downpour. Not friendly. Kept getting moved on by officials. Interesting to get an experience of the regime.

Large cubic building where you generally have to queue for at least an hour for the "chance" of catching a glimpse of Ho Chi Minh's mummy for 2 minutes... It's not worth it, despite the building's amazing architecture.

We went in the afternoon; it was very hot, and the lack of vegetation made it even worse. The guards at the entrance to the streets leading to the mausoleum didn't exactly encourage us to venture in. The view is beautiful, but there's not much to do there.

Interesting but couldn't get in due to time we went- read before you go on entry times! Our fault! Got a fright when accidentally stepped over the yellow line and lots of shouting occurred, didn't see the signs that said stay behind the line. We weren't the only ones. Missed any interpretive signs, if they were there, so read before you go. Massive structure.

We arrived quite early and found that there was a queue of over 1 km. A guide said that we should expect to wait in line for about 4 hours from that point. We gave up but enjoyed the building from the outside.

They'll recommend it to you, and you'd be right to go. Keep your expectations low. You'll be pleasantly surprised by the enthusiasm of hundreds of Vietnamese schoolchildren, who are thrilled to rediscover their past.
The mausoleum was clean and was located very close to the presidential palace. However, there is nothing much to see.

It's essentially a communist mausoleum. A huge plaza with tons of concrete and nothing remarkable about it. The park behind it, where Ho Chi Minh's house is located, is nicer, but it's a pretty garden. I'd only go to this place if you're going to be in Hanoi for more than three days.
The place is beautiful, but there's a huge list of rules to follow (you can't enter with a cell phone, or wearing shorts, etc.). Not very practical.

The building is only impressive in its size.  It is completely graceless.  We saw it from the outside and waiting in line to get in would not have been a choice.  There were many more places of much more interest.  On the positive side, there were many school children visiting and it was fun to interact with them as they were very friendly and interested in our group of Westerners.

Arrived here at the Mausoleum at about 8:30 AM. Dropped off and immediately thought we are in for a long long wait. The line stretched about 2-3 kms and when we finally got to the entrance we asked the security where to go and were told to keep walking so we did and eventually got to the back of the line which was in another two streets from the main entrance. (GET HERE EARLY)Very rude, very rushed and mostly locals wanting to see Ho Chi Minh's body. Not very tourist friendly and I'd advise the tourists to get here at about 6 AM considering we waited in line from 8:30 AM and only got in to see Ho Chi Minh at 10:30 AM for about 1 minute and that was it!Oh, and don't even consider taking a bottle of water or a snack in line because your not allowed. And if your carrying a back pack expect it to be checked a few times by the security. Very tough waiting in line without a sip of water for two hours in 35-40 degree heat. Another tip, shoulders and knees to be covered otherwise you'll have to pay 100,000 VND for a scarf at the front of the line which is thick and extremely uncomfortable. Although I did see the locals not adhering to this policy at all and the tourists mostly were the ones singled out. Happy to say we've done, but as I said earlier you may have a better experience if you get in early and beat the long wait.
Not a must-see, endless queues, then you're rushed through at breakneck speed, a complete waste of time.

This must see takes up a lot of time. With security and waiting in line. And then you are inside, you can see the grounds and you find out the mousoleum itself is closed. (...) It really is just a big square with that mausoleum on it.
Visited the Mousoleum. It was a hot and humid day and the queues were very long. It was not worth it.

I respect the reverence the Vietnamese have for Ho Chi Minh, and perhaps we should leave the inside of the mausoleum just for them to pay their respects.  Because, frankly, queuing in the heat for over half an hour (even with a jump-the-queue-pass that all the guides seem to be able to get) to shuffle past an embalmed body in a darkly lit room seems rather pointless without a strong cultural affiliation.  It's neither moving nor awe-inspiring.From the outside, the mausoleum is hardly attractive....grand, yes, but in that rather dour 1960's communist style that fills you with gloom rather than admiration.As for the hordes of martinets guarding and organising the site who glory in their ability to order their fellow citizens (and foreign tourists) around, they just offer an example of why communism failed and why most modern Vietnamese despise the party that still calls the shots.
We waited in very long queue for this and it lasts only 30 seconds once in. Not worth the visit unless you are a local.

Long queue, hot sun, along with some barbaric tourists who jumped the queue shamelessly marred this visit for me. You'll spend maybe ten seconds in the room after the 45 minute queue. School kids and some tour groups got priority, so technically the queue looked shorter than it really was -- these people with priority kept entering before us.
