Go somewher else, unless you really wanna see Vietnam's leader.

When we arrived we thought it must have been a special occasion or something. Turned out it wasn't, just a normal day with a four and a half hour queue to see Ho Chi Minh....so we didn't see him. Sorry, not waiting that long!

We believed this was a must see sight in Hanoi, but on arrival we found massive queues which are guide estimated were 2 hours long. We decided to forgo the Mausoleum, and only visited Ho Chi Minh's 2 nearby homes. Again queues but somewhat smaller.We have given the attraction 2 stars as obviously it is of significant cultural importance  to the Vietnamese themselves.

Against the very wishes of Ho Chi Minh City—which wanted its memorial centers dispersed across the north, center, and south to symbolize the unity of Vietnam—people queue for at least half an hour among dozens of young schoolchildren, barely old enough to understand what they're doing, just to see the remains of a man who died almost 50 years ago for about 30 seconds! The faint smell of rancidity in the room makes one wonder how much longer this ceremony will continue.
Unless, you just love Uncle Ho or Re visiting to view the "body" make this last on your list. Be prepared for 4 hour wait to view body Tuesday-Thurs only. Time is better spent at Ethnicity Museum.
Extremely long queue to enter mauseoleum if you visit on a weekend. Estimated waiting time will be 3hrs. Visit on Tuesday/Thursday as advised by tour guide for less crowd.

We have visited this part of city tour in Hanoi and it was not a valuable thing since its not a must see site and lots of people coming around there and we have to kept all the bags outside and walk there so it's kind difficult for us since we have traveled with the baby.
The mausoleum is exactly how it appears on photos.  Nothing more and nothing less.  The royal palace and gardens are underwhelming.
You have come out this far therefore shame not to see .The reverence shown this leader is amazing Meusem right next door is interesting if you want to better understand Ho chi minh

One can understand the Vietnamese people's feelings towards Ho Chi Minh, but as a monument, it's nothing special. The size of the square is impressive, and perhaps the changing of the guard, but I think there are much more interesting places to visit.
There wasn't anything to see as it was closed when I visited - not sure why as it was a weekday morning?It was boring - just a quick photo stop and go.

If you really have 2+ hours that you want to spend queuing in hot and humid conditions and really do we want to see Ho Chi Minh (who actually requested a simple cremation upon his death rather than being displayed as a tourist attraction), then go ahead. Personally I wouldn't recommend it.

Aside from the monstrous queue, it's also a destination for Vietnamese tourists and schools. There's nothing interesting there, simply the embalmed national hero Ho Chi Min, which frankly isn't a pretty sight. It's interesting to see his simple home, the used cars, and the gardens behind the mausoleum.

No need to actually walk all this distance. You can see it from the road via car. It's no biggie- just a hunk of stone with a lot of grass and walkways. We would never recommend it - unless you could go inside and see something!
Forget going inside here  the queue on a Saturday is bonkers. Best to go outside around the grounds which are interesting and the wait to get in is much less

No queues compared to Sunday but pretty boring on a Monday when it's closed! We at least saw the changing of the guard which was short and sweet. The building itself is a pretty impressive structure - very Russian looking and made of different coloured marble.

Didn't queue to get into the mausoleum. Queue was crazily long at 9.45am. Also, just to get into bai dinh square to view it from outside, need to pass through security checks. Cannot anyhow walk as well. Security guards will blow their whistle to signal you to the side

This mausoleum is imposing but frankly ugly... there's nothing Vietnamese about it... it's amusing to see the uniformed soldiers standing guard in front of this tomb... as if it were a bunker containing weapons... but anyway, go there, the embassy district which is very close by is worth seeing....
Make sure you really want to visit this.  There are thousands of school children and tour parties and the queues snake around inside and along streets outside for miles

when i reach in Ho Chi Mausoleum it was lot of local and tourist i can guess around 1000 long cue i can stay waiting 4 to 5 hours until i enter inside my opinion if they can fixed day for local people and tourist to give chance for tourist ti visit this place

I was wrong to not have done my homework beforehand, thinking the mausoleum was in a public square where you'd have to wait in line if you wanted to enter. Instead, we found ourselves standing in a line that was literally miles long, asking the guards how long the wait would be. "30 minutes," was the reply, twice, 45 minutes apart. After an hour of queuing, we returned to the old quarter by taxi, having barely made it halfway.

Well, of course you want to visit this place, and I don't regret it either. However, if you expect this to be a beautiful place, you will be disappointed. It is certainly atmospheric, but not really the atmosphere you are looking for for coziness or fun.

Here, the personality cult is taken to extremes. Almost everything is forbidden within the complex. Even your water bottles are confiscated in the sweltering heat. And uniformed personnel are stationed at every corner to ensure you don't speak too loudly. A visit is only worthwhile for Ho Chi Minh enthusiasts.
You cannot visit the mausoleum: you can only walk past and take a photo of your partner in front of it. Be sure to walk on to the HCM museum.

Not much here for a tourist, certainly not something I would wait in a long line to visit again.  Nice to see locals paying their respects but that rather than the building is the main reason to make the trip.

As expected the visit to the Ho Chi Min Mausoleum presented very little interest and was very crowded. This was a propaganda tour: fortunately, the body was not visible that day. Poor man, he wanted to be incinerated and his body is exhibited against his will! The  only interest was the view of the adjacent garden which is magnificent with all its giant banyan trees.

I made a special trip to visit the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, but entry was not permitted. I was curious because I couldn't communicate with anyone when I asked for the reason, but later I found out through a blog that it is closed to visitors between October and December. I was very disappointed.
Noting great. Just like any other government building, not that great or impressive. However, our experience was eclipsed by the fact that we couldn't go inside to see.

All my reviews of our Vietnam tours are very positive...but this one...come on...Looking back on this tour, the strict military pomp as you proceed slowly thru the thirty minute line-up, up three flights of stairs, past the body and then down and out,  is laughable...Ultra strict dress code....NO TALKING!    Show Respect!   Stay in line!   Stop here!  You! ...Wait!  This three storey solid marble structure on acres and acres of prime, manicured parkland houses the preserved body of a political, historical figure who lived out of the country for most of his life, was a ruler for a very short period of time, yet is given more honor than the Budda ... His written will, which is on display, says his wish was to be cremated...does honor translate into something different in Vietnamese?
Unless you have a thing for dead frozen bodies in glass coffin, the place is not interesting. Better the nearby museum.

The only reason to go here is to get a pic of the building because that's all you are allowed to do. The building is permanently closed and you can't even walk close to it because of the guards. If you are going to the One-pillar pagoda then you might as well swing by and take a pic because it's just around the corner. The one thing I noted in walking around this area is how different it was from the rest of Vietnam, almost like you were back in America. Big, open, clean streets, with no traffic and pristine lawns with spacious lawns. You could definitely tell the government used the money on fronting rather than basic things the country needed.
A vast, icy avenue and a sinister monument dedicated to the cult of personality. It's best avoided unless you're truly nostalgic.

To get to see Ho Chi Min , you have to go through a fair bit of annoying and nit picky security . When you get into the mausoleum they lead you through in groups where you literally walk past him in 30 seconds. Was interesting but not worth the time spent .

I wouldn't advise anyone to go out of their way to come here. Having said that the ride out from the Old Quarter was interesting and very different from the Old Quarter. Watch the taxis as many will try to rip you off. Our return taxi was 3x more expensive in just minutes. I started to complain so he kicked us out!

When you pass by this mausoleum, you quickly understand that you are in a communist country because this site resembles everything you have seen in the former USSR, that is to say: a mausoleum, large grandstands around it and a huge avenue in front allowing tanks etc. to parade.

If viewing dead Stalinist leaders is your thing, Ho Chi Minh, who has been dead since 1969 is embalmed and his body is stored with this mausoleum.  We decided not to bother. The changing of the guard was (marginally) more interesting.

Visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a must when visiting Hanoi. As you walk towards it, you get the feeling of taking a peek behind the Iron Curtain once again. A large square suitable for a military parade, with the stately cube of the Mausoleum in the background. We were there at a time when we were unable to enter for a tribute to Ho Chi Minh. We could only 'admire' the hideous building from the outside. Do not step over the yellow line, or you will be called back.

This place is of great historical importance for the Vietnemese people and many of them make the visit. For non-Vietnamese it is a long wait in a queue to see the body of Ho Chi Minh, who of course has less significance. The guards there are overly rigid in enforcing the rules, of which you don't know about until you transgress one. Not a place to spend time if you are short on it.
For those who are passionate about the man's history beyond that of his Vietnamese ancestors...

We couldn't understand the point of looking at a concrete block with a column-like appearance from 40 meters away. The area in front of it is very large and there's a lawn...all in all, unremarkable...

We went on a tour to the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. Somehow, we weren't allowed in because of our shorts, while others were admitted. We were directed to a shop to buy a scarf to wear as a wrap. We decided against it.....

The walk to finding the entrance was long...then the queues were so much longer.... lots of tour buses and school children lining up for an hour or so.... finally the long queue again to gain entrance to the mausoleum.... then a minute it was over....certainly a place for respect and dignity but unsure if we saw a copy of the man himself...?!
Nothing to see unless you are willing to queue for an hour to see what is in the museum base on schedules.
The main mausoleum was shut when we arrived! We had no clue about the place being shut! There are no signage/boards also that mention it...the adjacent museum and president's house were open though

A pilgrimage site for the Vietnamese. It was packed with groups of Vietnamese tourists and their guides, each one shouting louder than the last. People everywhere. The site itself is nothing special; if you have extra time in Hanoi, you can visit it. Otherwise, it's completely skippable. It's a place dedicated solely to Ho Chi Minh.

Unfortunately the main attraction was closed for "maintenance" when we attended. Otherwise a significant Vietnameses attraction to be treated with reverence. You can always do some research on the Internet to see the works of Ho Chi Minh, the so called father of modern Vietnam.

This is deemed a must see for visitors to Hanoi. Set on one side of a huge square opposite the parliament building, it is notable for its solemnity and formal guard of honour dressed in white uniforms. Entrance to the mausoleum itself is restricted to certain times but probably only of interest to real devotees of Ho Chi Minh.

The opening hours are short, so you need to check carefully. I couldn't go inside, but the people guarding the entrance and the atmosphere of the building were solemn. It is a good place to take a break at the nearby square.

We visited it in October 2016 and were only able to see the Museum... the mausoleum is closed and inaccessible. We were told that the repairs would take 3 months. For us, this circumstance reduced its appeal by 50%.
The mausoleum was closed in October, so we didn't see the inside. The avenue in front of it is the only car-free area in Hanoi. The architecture is very "Soviet-Sino-Communist," massive and imposing.

There is a museum about HCM but thats about it. Not much to see and most of the shops outside are closed. A few shops inside are closed. Was told that the body is in Russia for maintenance until December.

From September 3rd to December 3rd, you won't be able to see Hồ Chí Minh himself – conservation work is underway. The museum (a separate building) is three stories high, with two rather crudely presented floors depicting the timeline of Hồ Chí Minh's visits, the highest being by far the most interesting. Overall, however, it's safe to skip it. Admission is 40,000 vnd.
We went out this way to see this. Waste of time really. You cant go up to it. You basixally take a photo and leave.
I guess everyone needs to go but really not my cup of tea.  Lines area long and tightly controlled by the Gov.  The atmosphere is repressive

I went there to pay my respects to the founding father... but after waiting in a ridiculously long line to get in, I was rushed along with instructions not to stop in front of the frozen objects, which I couldn't tell were real or just mannequins, and the tour was over in no time. I would recommend it if you have a lot of time to spare.
I've tried to visit this point of interest three times. Either because of the opening hours or because of construction, I've left without being able to see it. What a shame.

It is important to find out whether the body is there. There are about two months a year when Ho Chi Minh's body is transported to Russia for preservation. During this time, there is no entry to the Mausoleum and there is nothing to do except stand in the square without shade and take pictures of the building from the outside.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is an esplanade where the Mausoleum is located on one side and the Parliament building on the other. In the center of the esplanade is a huge Vietnamese flag. There isn't much else to the place. You can take photos as long as you stay within a yellow line marked on the ground. If you cross the line, some soldiers will reprimand you. It's a place worth visiting, but nothing extraordinary.

The main attraction of the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is being able to enter the mausoleum and see the preserved body of the founder of Vietnam Ho Chi Minh. However, during the period we were there, they were doing some restoration to the body. As such, being unable see the body was a great disappointment to all of us. We however manage to witness the changing of the guards.So importantly before visiting the mausoleum, do check whether it is open or not. Or you will be left disappointed.

It's currently under renovation and you can't see inside. Please check how long it will be closed. However, it's still fun to take pictures in front of the building, watch the changing of the guard ceremony, and look around at the surrounding facilities.
Been here, it is a large square. Apparently the Mausoleum is open on weekends. Too bad I didn't go inside, it's less impressive then. Didn't find it particularly special to see.🤐
The fussiness of the guards around the tomb and its Hitlerian arrogance of construction make it amusing. Check out the charming Buddhist temple beside the museum (often closed).
It wasn't very impressive or inspiring. I don't think it's worth going out of your way to visit. There's just a building and a lawn... that's all.

There was a huge queue even before we entered. The security checks were stricter than at an airport. The staff were unfriendly and arrogant, wielding the privileges of a socialist bureaucrat. They had absolutely no sense of service.

I deeply admire President Ho Chi Minh – but it's so tedious and boring every time I have to visit this place. The organization of visits here is so disorganized; a bunch of people are working, but they're doing it according to their own whims! There are too many complicated rules for visiting; when I visit, I feel like those regulations have taken away all my human rights in front of such a solemn place. I wish this area were open free of charge to foreigners, open on Mondays, Fridays, and afternoons, and that they would operate during regular office hours....
In short, the thing about mausoleums is that for the locals they're places with a lot of emotional weight... especially when it's a figure like Ho Chi Minh, whom they adore.

Our tour included this stop. The site was closed on Fridays and Mondays. An imposing structure in a vast square used for parades. September 2nd is a national holiday. Everyone is buzzing with preparations.

A monument dedicated to a figure important to the Vietnamese people. A visit with a local guide and a refresher on history are recommended beforehand to fully understand what led the people to build this mausoleum.

The opening hours are very limited and when we visited following advice from the hotel, this important attraction was closed - when it was meant to be still open. We were very disappointed. There needs to be more reliable opening hours so that tourists can plan their visits & the opening hours should be managed closely. Fortunately we could still view the museum and the Uncle Ho historical homes and gardens which are separate to the mausoleum.

The mausoleum itself is just a walk around the preserved body. It is worth of visiting for history lovers, if you also plan to see the palace area or the museum in the same complex. Visiting the mausoleum alone is just the lose of time.
It was closed, so I couldn't go inside. The view from the outside was breathtaking.
Nice to know about it but better to imagine that to see. Not much to do and too much time to get there. 5 minutes visit.

Arrived at around 1045, to find it already closed.  No signs to work out when it would open. No information. Poor effort by whoever runs it. Gardens are ok to walk around though. Museum is a little informative, but also a little bizarre.

Dressed appropriately, no cameras, no water bottles, in silence, waiting in a long, albeit quick, queue to see Ho Chi Minh's body for 30 seconds in an over-air-conditioned room. Not great, but the palace and his former residence are nice to see for 40k admission.
Magnificent site, impressive building. Strict security. Not worth queuing for hours to get inside: corridors that lead to the main room where Ho Chi Minh's body lies.

I am happy I went to hear and see the propaganda based around this fallen leader. But be prepared for LONG lines. The line moves quickly and there is a covered sidewalk that attempts to reduce the pain of the merciless sun. We waited just about one hour or two bottles of water! You will go through security midway and they take backpacks, cameras and iPhones. I was allowed to keep my purse but not my cellphone. The military keeps the crowd moving in two lines to the mausoleum (it's airconditioned). The line moves so there is no pause to spend looking at the body. It is somewhat surreal or I was suffering from dehydration! The tour of the presidential palace grounds was a wonderful example of propaganda. I highly recommend it for only that reason.

It was mostly photos with explanations alongside them; I think I could have found the same thing on Google. Anyway, it helped me remember Vietnam's history chronologically, and it only cost me 15,000 dong.

We visited this early to avoid queues and hot weather. Bags, cameras and water were confiscated although many Vietnamese tourists were allowed through with theirs which disappointed us. We were filed through a long line to see the corpse of President Ho Chi Minh for a mere 30 seconds. No information was provided to visitors.

If you like seeing people who have been dead 50 years then this could be your thing. "Uncle Ho" was certainly the least unpleasant of those that are "immortal."The queue kept moving but you only get to walk round the old codger for about thirty seconds after you have queued for qbout 45 minutes.When I visited the gardens first in 2013 I was just about the only person there. I guess that there were 20,000 (mostly Vietnamese) there the day we went.
The place made me think on east germany in the 1970s. We didnt tried to see Ho Chi Minh because it was steaming hot and there was 1000 people standing in line.
You have to pay attention to the opening dates because the monument is not visible every day and you have to dress as if you were in church because there are long queues to enter.

Not many other people seem to have had this issue but we couldn't work out how you got in. Maybe we were lacking a few brain cells that morning or maybe it was closed but either way we only saw the building from the outside 😞. Would suggest getting some advice from your hotel before heading over there, as it was difficult to get information once there.

You are not allowed to take anything with you so you either put your things in a locker or your tour guide holds them. No cameras is important.I felt very much like I was a beef cow walking along the ramp waiting to be stunned and killed. We were ordered to walk in pairs and very slowly, in a trancelike state, in a maze formation to get to the mausoleum. Along the pathway there are men in army uniform holding their guns. It was horrifying! After the 20 minute or so walk to get to the building you are told "no talking, no stopping" as you get about 1 minute to walk past the wax Ho Chi Minh. Then you are outside and you can talk and take photographs and buy soft drink and all the usual. It was just plain strange.The rest of the complex has other government and official buildings as well as the little wooden hut that Ho Chi Minh lived in.
The memorial hall is best viewed from a distance, as it cannot be visited inside.

There are so many beautiful things to see in Hanoi that this monument pales in comparison. In fact, we couldn't even get close; we had to stay off to the side with our cameras. If you don't have time to go, you're not missing anything.

I went to the mausoleum today and don't think it was worth the trip. (Disclaimer: I went mid-morning, on a Saturday, during the Vietnamese school holidays - not a good idea!)I arrived at about 9.30 to find thousands of people in a huge, snaking queue out in the sun. I joined the line, not wanting to miss out, but I really regret it.The kilometres-long line (no exaggeration) moved decently quickly, but it was still about an hour until I got to enter the mausoleum. Bring a hat and sunscreen.You need to deposit any bags and cameras before entering the mausoleum - the signage is very clear and you don't really lose any progress in the line. It is free of charge.Seeing Ho Chi Minh was pretty cool, although you're whisked along very quickly, so you spend less than a minute in the mausoleum itself. You enter the room in two lines, and the left line gets a better view, so aim to be in that one.After exiting the mausoleum, you end up in a park area (forgive my ignorance; I don't know what it's called or what its purpose is). A long pathway leads you through the park, souvenir shops, cafes, etc. After about a kilometre you may reunite with your bag +/- camera!Overall, it was an ok experience. I definitely recommend going early in the morning (apparently it opens at 8am), on a weekday, when it's not the school holidays.P.S. Don't believe Wikipedia when it says to wear long pants/trousers! Barely anyone wore long pants. I did wear them and sweated so much! Just dress sensibly with shorts/skirt at knee level and shoulders covered.

We got there thinking that we could go in. Wrong. You stand outside in the blistering heat, behind a line on the road infront of the guards on the steps to stare at a closed door. Looking at this picture is sufficient.

This is one of the most iconic structures in whole Hanoi. The complex is in a huge square in the middle of the city. There is the mausoleum itself, which is not accessible for the public to see Ho Chi Minh's body, and the his house and a …

Not sure why nobody mentioned the hours listed here is not accurate. It is the hours for the outside part. If you want to see inside (the tomb of Ho Chi Minh), you need to be there before 11am. After that, you will only see the outside of …

the inside of the Mausoleum is cool; the marble wall insignia (beautiful gold and deep red sickle hammer and star) is very impressive. but, as other reviews noted, it’s a confusing place to navigate. I just walked in at 7am (google maps …

Mid at most, what with the attitude of the staff and all. The venue itself is nice and pleasant though; I just wish the main owner of this property is justly laid to eternal rest in a more dignified manner.

I couldn't go in past security check point because I had a selfie stick on me. I totally understand and respect this as a rule in today's selfie culture. I tried telling security I wouldn't use it and would keep it in my pocket, or leave it …

Nice park in to memorialize Ho Chi Minh. There are several people walking around for exercising. There is a security checkpoint before entering. Bring minimal items (phone keys wallet) dress conservatively.

Not much of a history lesson. Recommend to read up before coming. Great display of the peoples affection for HCM. Magnificent structure. Take note of their hours and they close during lunchtime (I had to wait in the unbearable weather for an hour).

It is said to be the shrine of Hi Chi Minh. But the entry was closed when we visited. Check the timings for entry to the shrine. Other than that we have a few temples, shops and a big garden area. There was no entry fee.

Only for the most dedicated. The wait is at least an hour, and that's when the line is moving quickly. You are not allowed to take photos inside of the mausoleum but once you are out you can take all the photos you want grom the parade …

Pro tip: go to the entrance next to the presidential palace. No line there at all, when the one on the other side has 1h wait. Also, shorts/skirts must cover knees (stricter for women of course 😒). Grounds seen easily from the outside though (I couldn’t get in but saw it well enough) …

You can see from afar in a fenced area and is enough. You could pay extra to get closer, but not much difference. Worth the walk around, including the more modern government building in the surroundings.
It is a nice place to walk around and see, there are really no many things to see or anything very interesting. It’s just a place you have to visit in Hanoi.

Felt pretty restricted. I get that it's a mausoleum but the entire massive place felt stricter than it had to be especially when it comes to photos and walking paths. Go there, walk through it and you're done.

How can we review? Says it opens at 730,  but when arriving at 1100 and proceeding through security, you  are not told that it is only open between, 730-10 am. Then you just get to see a big building and surrounding gardens! Go early if you want to go into the mausoleum!!
Alright experience. Long queue, very big area, could not go into the mausoleum. Nothing special. Have to go to another place, too, the Hao Lo prison.
Not sure if this is typical, but you could not enter the Mausoleum when I went there. Only walk around and observe from the outside.

Didn’t go inside the mausoleum but I wouldn’t say it’s very inviting when passing by. The reviews prove what I expected as there are such strict and unpredictable rules that you don’t feel like you are welcome there. We just passed by which …

Things to note: there is security check way before the entrance. For ladies strictly no sleeveless. I was denied entrance at the security because I had a small torchlight in my bag. Whose handphone does not have torchlight nowadays! Why ain’t all denied access? Can take some photos from the perimeter.

If you have time free than you can spend it for a visit. Waiting time might be long and might be hot on the huge square in front of the mausoleum. No beverages allowed in the area no mobile or camera for taking photos inside. Only few seconds to see Ho Chi Minh in the glass sarkopharg.

Went here on a Saturday morning, as part of a free walking tour, with our two kids. You have to hand in bags before entering, and water isn't allowed on site either. Females have to be dressed conservatively, difficult in the heat of …

Huge , beautiful and with historic importance. It is a glimpse into the great leader ho chi minhs life during the Vietnam war. It is a stark contrast to the huge independence palace used by south Vietnam's …

Definitely a landmark in Hanoi that is by far worth a visit; Expect this to take most if not an entire day; it can get extremely busy. I would suggest going early as the line for entry can get very long and access …

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is closed on Monday and Friday. Unfortunately we went on Friday so could only view from the outside open ground area. So we went to the neaby Ho Chi Monh's Vestige in the Presidential Place area.

No entry fee but there are security checks where your water bottles are kept. Queued for about 45min before actually getting to enter the mausoleum. Went there on a Wed morning but there were loads of school children & veterans queueing …
I didn’t really know what to make of this but I suppose that it’s something you have to do if you visit Hanoi. …

MAUSOLEUM CLOSES AT 11.30AM. The whole place is not very well sign posted for the mausoleum side of things. I get that it closes early but really I just wanted a pic in front of the building but couldn’t, as it’s closed off everywhere. …

We went in July and wandered round the gardens and exterior of the mausoleum - the actual mausoleum museum is closed until August. It’s set in a large area that is pleasant to walk around so is still worth going to even with the museum …

Although the entire "square" may be open during the hours listed on Google Maps, the entrance to the Mausoleum itself is only open until 10:30am or something like that.  I went with a small group of fellow tourists and I think I was …
It's vast, large and stunning. There was a weapon scanning before entry.
It's a nice building for sure  But Nov. 8, whole area was blocked of by military, couldn't even get a good picture from distance
Strange kind of place but a good area to go for walks and training. No tickets required but there is a security check to enter the square.

Might worth it for fans, as a tourist not really. Mausoleum is for free, museum costs a small fee. Quite long waiting time and you have to walk quite a while until you reach it - including security check. A …

It was close when I was there. My friend/guide did not check the hours so we were not able to see his preserved body. No wonder there's not so many tourist on the grounds. Weather was humid and very hot. It's another reason to go back to  Hanoi. Surroundings are clean and lots of securities.  Nice facade.
Small museum. Took 15~20min to see  all o ject. But not much object to see because Vietnam's history is short.

As my fellow traveller said they treat you like sheep and he stormed of before seeing ho chi minh. Place is very heavily guarded and strict. No bags no cameras. But once through the cattle market it becomes a beautiful park with plenty to see.
It was interesting to see some parts of Ho Chi Minh s life but I thought there would be more details, like in the woman s museum. It is good when you love history
Didn't visit it. Only outside. It is the place where they worship the country hero. So it's a special atmosphere...you have the one pillar pagoda just behind
Loads of old people going for a run. Good opportunity for a little walk or jog. Would recommend going in the evening. Don't plan too much time tho. Was okay.

don't cross the yellow line. that is a visitor-free area. you are can only approach from the front building up to the yellow line. to enter it, there must be a special agreement. you are also not allowed to bring a camera or other recording …
Nice place. But it’s a mess, no map, no one speaking English, randomly closed to the public, mandatory way with military guards not allowing you back.
We got there on a Friday and it was closed but there were alot of people walking around watching the changing of the guard.

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is one of the first stops of our trip around Hanoi. It comes highly recommended by the local travel agents. It was quite a journey to get to the location. You had to walk and walk and walk and walk and walk and walk …

Got here at 11:40am and found out you can only enter the mausoleum to see Ho Chi Minh's body before 10:30am. The museum also closes between 12-2pm and costs 40k dong, and since there was only 20mins to see it, we decided to skip. Really beautiful grounds though and would recommend coming at a better time!

It was pretty strange, the mausoleum is only open until 10:30 (depending on time) so I could not see inside. The guards are very restrictive to dress code even if can't go in. The museum is also closed around the middle of the day. So I could not say it is positive more of a let down.

Point of going there was to see Uncle Ho but on the day was only taking viewings up till 1130, not sure if it's a daily routine because Google says viewing was open all day. Anyway the rest of the complex is big and very informative and very busy.

The lines are really really long in mid morning around 10am.  There is also just onr entrance so make sure you go to the right place.  Our taxi dropped us off by the presidential palace but you cant go in from there and it was a really long …

We visited around 10 AM on a weekday and the lines were a bit crazy. There was a lot of pushing, shoving, and cutting in line. Security did not intervene to help, nor to enforce the no-camera policy. Overall this was a chaotic experience …
They don’t let u in if showing your arms, knees or any non conservative clothing, also HCM Mausoleum is CLOSED, will be for a month they told me, you can still walk through the park tho $40K

Report as of Monday, 3rd of June 2019. Arrived at 8am and the mausoleum is closed. More than one guard told me Monday and Friday the mausoleum is closed. Hope this helps other from wasting a trip and to come back again. Good luck.
Bring sleeves shirts as they will not allow you to enter without
Nothing to Wright home about. Yet it is one of those things that you cannot miss to visit.

I respect the place and the name of the man who rest in peace here. but the attitude of the security lady in the check-in office was very very rude to all of us and the other groups and with our tour guides!! shame on her this type of people should not be working in these places and destroy the reputation of Vietnamese.
Tips for visiting Mausoluem:  1 / Do not!… go at Mid day, we went at 10am got straight in, 2-4PM there is a …

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is an important historical site, offering insight into Vietnam's past. The complex's architecture and gardens are impressive. However, the experience feels somewhat formal and rigid, with strict rules and long queues. Additionally, the display of Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body may not appeal to everyone.

The mausoleum is only open between 7.30am and 10.30am, and it’s closed completely on Mondays and Fridays. Please keep this in mind. The grounds are nice and we had a stroll round (twice 😅) but yeah, keep in mind the opening hours are …
Built with Russian hekp after the passing of the much loved Uncle Ho Chi Minh, it stands solemn and defiant in a large square. …
To clarify for anyone who is confused by the information online as to when you can actualy enter the mausoleum: …
The place is closed for viewing. We only got photos of the structure itself. You need to walk right Ba Dinh Square in order to get up close the mausoleum.
Free. Pretty average. Security checkpoint. No pocket knife, no lighter, etc...
Free entrance for vietnamese, ticket price 40k/ foreigner open time: 8-11am on Tue, Wed, Thu 8-11:30am on Sat and Sun …
Beautiful building but entry only between 7-11am according to a guard there. Best to look up the dress code (i.e. no shorts or sleeveless tops) before going.
The lines are insanely long as there is a lot of people wanting to pay their respects, unless you are wanting to do that same it is just as good to view from the outside and not line up
No photos allowed in respect of the great leader.

Quite a surreal experience. You're in and out in 30 seconds and the guards shout at you if you walk too slow (even if it's quiet). Also the opening times on Google are wrong. You can only visit inside the Mausoleum between 7:30am and …

We enjoyed the museum but this was only possible with a free museum tour guide. The information around the museum was displayed in an old fashioned way so it wasn't very engaging at all. I would come here only if you have some time to kill - the air conditioning is a real plus when it's a hot day.

the Mausoleum is closed for two months for cleaning. By mid august it will be open again for public viewing. ladies are not allowed to enter wearing shorts, mini skirts. Kids are allowed to wear short pants. June is very warm and humid.
There’s not much to see, just a wide open space with lots of closed of areas. Be aware they will turn you away if your shoulders and knees are not completely covered.

I didn’t get the go the Mausoleum today because it was closed — a detail that wasn’t updated on Google. Else I’m sure it would’ve been a great experience as the place was large and seemed promising. The Mausoleum is closed every Monday and Friday so avoid coming here on these two days.

So many taxi scam in front of the musoleum. Don't use them!! Very scary. Got angry when I didnt want to pay the taximetre. Obviously it was a scam 770000 dong for 2 km. Tourists are happy to come and enjoy another culture. But …

Nice place but I’m not sure about the opening time - we were there on a Sunday around 3pm (google says it would be opened) and the mausoleum was closed. I mean, I can get in the “park” but not enter the chamber. Quite frustrating.
Only saw it from the outside but it's a nice place

The entrance to the mausoleum was closed when we went. Not much else to look at. The overall atmosphere has a distinctly Soviet feel, solemn and sterile, which is somewhat incongruous with the liveliness of the rest of the city.

The mausoleum is closed for two months. But you can still visit the museum and there’s also a mini puppet theater. The latter costs 70K for adults,  there’s a fixed schedule though I only remember there’s one at 10:10 am. The museum itself …

Very beautiful building and location, especially the gardens and square. However opening times aren’t clear- mausoleum ink open in mornings, guards are rude and directions are non-existent — forced to walk around the whole square just to get in and it had closed by tht time. Could use an upgrade for sure.

Worth visiting. Remember to dress properly. (No sleevless shirts/no miniskirts. Knees need to be covered/half covered.) No cameras allowed inside the mausoleum. Visiting mausoleum is free, but you need to pay to enter the museum (if you want to visit it).

You have to enter into this weird military area to get here. Shoulder covering and masks were mandatory. (Bizarre why masks were since it was all outside!)  Not much to see aside from the outside of the musoleum building. Lots of signs …

Impressive and very solemn atmosphere. No phones or bags allowed. Honor guards inside and outside the tomb. Oncle Hô wanted his ashes dispersed over north and south Vietnam. Instead you can admire its remains through a glass. This is really a cult.

What you see in the photo is what you get. If you get closer than 100 yards, the guards will blow whistles and tell you to get back. IMO, the should let you be able to go up to it and walk around more; in Hue, you can walk inside the Tomb of the king (emperor?) which was really cool.

Getting to the correct entrance is very difficult. We walked around nearly the entire complex before we found it. They also are doing construction so the signs for the entrance were not there. You kind of had to just figure it out. Also the …

Building not really spectacular (did not go inside, might be better on the inside). Opening hours very inconvenient. Looks more like a place for holding military parades. Yet interesting to see this important monument.

Not much to see on the outside. May be better if you get there in the morning when they allow visitors in. The museum next door was very interesting and had a lot of contemporary art along with historical artifacts.

The guards here were shoving us around and were overtly agressive to us in particular although we were following all the rules to the letter.  You really feel a difference in the atmosphere in Hanoi compared to the rest of the country.... …
This place is open on Tuesday to Thursday and Saturday sunday Friday and Monday are holidays Expect a long queue here …
It's closed. A guy told for 3 months.  Maybe under contractions. It's not allowed to go to mausoleum. Many guards
Large Crowds – Do not get LOST A group of seven of us arrived here 1n the morning on 17th January 2018.  You have to leave your hand bags etc. before you enter.  No photographs permitted …

Wonderful gardens and again history of Vietnam, and interesting exhibition in the exhibition hall on ho chi min giving perspective from a Vietnamese culture. Didn't enter the memorial itself but enjoyed the cultural visit
Very unfriendly police/staff

Just a slab of concrete and some young kids in uniform that want to show off by whistling at people who get too close to the monument. Great area, Ho Chi Minh's house is much more interesting to visit.

It is very hard to navigate in the park and trying to find Museum, Mausoleum and How Chi Minh house ... Mausoleum is guarded and strictly marked, if you go just few steps over the line officer is warning you. I understand importance of Hi Chi Minh for the Vietnam but some reactions are just too much ...

It was closed for viewing. It's my understanding he's stuffed and on display. A very nice area in Ha Noi Vietnam. It was built for Ho Chi Minh or Uncle Ho as his people called him. The body is on display for the world. Rated one of The Ho …
Dress appropriately, because no shorts and sleeveless top allowed to enter the area. Already reach there but stopped by the guard so avoid the same mistake like me.
The place is undoubtedly beautiful and packed with history. However, a very negative point of this place is the staff at the security : very rude.
Nice to pass in front. You cannot enter or anything. It’s more a place to pass by when you’re going to Ho Chi Minh museum or the palace.

Just to remind everyone that this and the museum close on both Saturday and Sunday. As it involves a lot of walking and the sun is scorching, bring an umbrella and sunscreen lotion. You need to drink a lot of water too.

No shorts, skirts, or something revealing your skins. I got rejected at the gate and had to buy a pair of pants from street. It is cheap tho. Then I saw quite a few people inside wearing shorts, skirt. I got treated differently because I am tourist?
Too long queue and no water bottle allowed. How can you survive this boiling heat without water? The queue may take 2 hours or more! Not an experience.
Was closed when I went here. Most times it's closed so you have to plan ahead if you wanna enter. It was pretty on the outside. The guards looked interesting when they changed shifts.

Would avoid when it is raining and when it is hot. Children found it incredibly boring. I was not far off that myself. Very long queues and you have to follow the path. It has made my global top 10 boring list.. Zzzzzz
Absolutely packed with people. Know that you will have to wait in a long, very long line of people just to go in and see it for so brief an amount of time. Get there early if you want to avoid heat.
Don't go on hot days. Also the view is great but the mausoleum is closed most of the time I go
Closed for some weeks and even when open, it seems you can only visit for a couple of hours per day which are not well advertised.
It's okay. Nice structure. Not much to do after you pass security. I didn't manage to see it open and I was there before 1030am.
Pretty interesting site to see, but very disappointed in the fact that Ho Chi Minh wanted his ashes scattered across Vietnam and not left in a mausoleum. …
Good experience but didn't get to see uncle Ho. Some people are not officers. They are just as normal as us but acted like they had the power. Uneducated Northern

Not much information I could find about Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum on site or online. When we arrived the lady’s have to have their legs covered up but not Male legs which I found little strange. We had to pop out the exit again and luckily …
Interesting place, unfortunately the mausoleum was closed during my visit. Nonetheless a good insight into where one of the most poignant figures in modern Vietnamese history lived and worked.

We weren't able to enter because of the very very long queue. Aside from the queue outside (which was 2-3 streets away from the entrance) there was also a very long zigzag queue inside. We came at the wrong time since it was a festival there.
Nice area to walk around, but there is no entry as it is under renovation till December 5th 2017 (as I was told) entry and everything around has been changed to a restricted area.
You get to experience what the Communist country feel like here. Everything is taken away before you enter, your iPad, your camera. So of course, you can take any picture of it.

I understand the reverence that the Vietnamese have for Ho Chi Minh as he wrestled more than one great power to the ground.  He is their Abraham Lincoln and deserving of respect.  But the mausoluem was little more than propaganda.
Couldn't get in due to dress code as berms were too short. Please "Wear something that covers your knees and shoulders, which means no shorts, sleeveless tops or miniskirts. "
The Mausoleum where Ho Chi Minh lies is CLOSED until AUGUST. But the park is open. No need to go there early the park is also open in the afternoon 13-16.
Too much to walk nice clean place and historic place in Hanoi there is grave yard of ho chi Minh and museum must visit for tourist
The actual mausoleum is impossible to actually get anywhere near to. Impressive in a way but I never got the feeling of history from it.
Only if you have nothung else to do. Nice to hear the story from your tour guidr, if you have one
You gotta love the man, or this will be a really boring visit.  This is the one place in Vietnam I felt the presence of the police state.
It looks great from outside. but our bad luck that we couldnt reach in time to go inside to see Ho chi minh's mortal remains.
Ho Chi was not there when we visited his body and gone to Russia for maintenance not sure it would have been worth queuing for 2hrs if he had unless you worship him.
Mausoleum only open in the morning so google maps is wrong
Gives some good insight into the making f vietnam of today but is quite repetitive. Some excellent photographic history
Didn't go in because apparently it closes at 11:00 for reasons unknown to me. You would expect something like this to be open during the day.
Something different to do in Hanoi and something you'd never experience in the states. Just make sure you queue up at 8am.
Long, long queues - ladies need to cover their legs, so be prepared to buy a scarf or cloth if you're wearing shorts that day.
Interesting place. Doubt it's really ho chi Minh. It's a big deal though. Be prepared to mind your manners.
Pretty much what you expect.
Don't expect to take your camera in. Don't smile, talk, look suspicious or show lack of respect
You can't miss this place if you are in Hanoi. It's a landmark but i didn't quite need to go in to see....
Interesting space, but the exhibit was a bit weird and disjointed, hard to follow. Nevertheless quite interesting.
Mausoleum in front of an independence square that is watched by armed guards 24/7...
If you don't have much time better skip this place but worth come here take a few pictures from outside

We couldn't get in causeit was closed but there was a great atmosphere
Was closed because of tet but seemed cool

Though impressive, not much to see there.
Same feeling like you visit the cemetery.
Interesting but not that special
too difficult to get in
Unfriendly staff
A place to visit

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, located in the center of Ba Dinh Square in Hanoi, the capital of Vietnam, is the final resting place of Ho Chi Minh, the beloved revolutionary leader of the Vietnamese people. It is not only an important …
The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a symbolic site for understanding modern Vietnamese history, and it's worth a visit. The building and surrounding area maintain a solemn and orderly atmosphere, …

The area is quite peaceful but very touristy, located in the district around the embassies. What sets it apart from the rest of the city is the expansive green space, offering a welcome respite. The monument itself is nothing exceptional; it's rather solemn.

This is a large mausoleum containing the remains of Ho Chi Minh. There is a security check and bag scan at the entrance. Video recording is prohibited, but photos are allowed. There is no entrance fee. There is only one entrance, but all exits are accessible.

May 4, 2025, I was passing by the Mausoleum in the evening, coming from the Presidential Palace. There's a security checkpoint at the entrance to the square in front of the Mausoleum. But I didn't have a bag or any other belongings, so they …

Very popular with Vietnamese obviously, so arrive early to avoid queues. Everyone must dress modestly and even men cannot wear shorts…unless they are knee length at least. Not a big problem as there are enterprising locals on hand selling wraps and light trousers to solve the problem for 100000 Dong!Worth waiting for the change of guard. Only about a 5 minute ceremony.Also worth soaking up the atmosphere in the parade square.The mausoleum itself is very large and impressive and is a huge symbol for the Vietnamese.It is free to enter, but you have to go through security to get into the immediate square which causes delays at busy times.

Unless you are a history buff like me, you might find this a bit underwhelming.  I grew up during the Vietnam War, and he was the great hero of my childhood, so for me it was an emotional experience, BUT....for the average person who doesn't care much for history, this may not be all that good.  The queues are long, and it could be quite tiring if it is a hot day.  The buildings are impressive, especially the main building.  Also, Ho's embalmed body looks very very lifelike.  There are other buildings on the premises (Ho's residence during the war, etc....)  We went because it was part of our itinerary, we were on a conducted group tour. But if you are on your own, you might give this a miss if you are pressed for time, and if you are NOT a history buff.

This mausoleum houses the remains of Ho Chi Minh, the Founding Father, who was given a permanent preservation measure. The inside of the mausoleum is cumbersome to secure and the entrance time is quite short, which limits the sightseeing of the surrounding facilities, so we only saw the exterior.

Turned up two days in a row but uncle ho wasn’t taking visitors and the mausoleum was shut. You could view the building from the outside. It was on days advertised as open on the website and in the morning. Disappointing.
Nothing to see except the closed mausoleum and a large flag pole when we went there. Maybe if there is a nice ceremony or event then it will be worth going to. Visit the nearby temples instead.

For the northern Vietnamese a place of pilgrimage and a very spacious site where these people come to honor their idol. In order to fully understand this, you should read through the history of Vietnam. The building is 21.6 meters high and has a length of over 41 meters. Ho's dying wish was to be cremated has not been fulfilled. Hồ wanted his ashes to be spread throughout Vietnam. Very strict rules are set here. No photography or filming is allowed. There are also rules on clothing and behavior. For example, the legs should be covered, in both men and women. There is also a change of guard, less spectacular than in Greece, for example.

I personally was not impressed.You can't take pictures, you can't talk. Throughout the tour you are escorted with young armed soldiers.Although I understand that you have to have respect and recognition for what you did for your country I did not like the way we had to do the tour.
Very interesting and educational having an Australian back ground . It is part of our both country's history..........

At first, I was going to try to get into the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, but to be honest, I gave up because I didn't have that much passion. But when I got on the open-top bus, I passed by on the circuit course and was able to see the huge buildings, only from the car. It gave me a mixed impression that the bodies were actually laid to rest so that I could see them.

The opening hours of the Mausoleum are a bit confusing. We thought it was until 11 o'clock in the morning, but already at 10.20 o'clock we could no longer visit the mausoleum. After a 40 minute wait we were able to view the square in front of the Mausoleum and the house where he lived. You will be strictly checked (backpack had to go through the scanner).

I visited on a tour of Hanoi city. I was told that there would be a long queue, but I entered through the gate and walked along the long corridor without stopping, and entered the huge building where Ho Chi Minh's body was kept in a refrigerated container, guarded by many guards. I was told that stopping, taking pictures, and talking were prohibited inside the building. Most of the people in line looked like they were Vietnamese. I wanted to see the changing of the guard.

This was ok.  Nothing exciting but if you’re a history buff you might enjoy this. Keep in mind you can’t go in here wearing shorts. We tried to and were turned away and then had to buy some dead sexy pants from a street vendor so we could get in.

The mausoleum of Ho Chi Min who was president from 1945 until his death in 1969 is definitely a destination worth visiting. There is a long queue to do some kind of check at the metal detector. They put my camera in a red bag.We pay the ticket 40,000 Dong each. The guards have a particular all-white uniform because on Saturdays and Tuesdays there is a kind of special ceremony. the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a complex that includes the monumental tomb, the house, the Presidential Palace and the Museum dedicated to Ho Chi Minh. It is now considered a sacred place for all Vietnamese.The complex was built in the period from 1973 to 1975

We are glad we had a  guide when we visited in order to navigate the security and adhere to the strict deferential protocols. The queues appear long, however, move swiftly, particularly in the mausoleum. This is on the list of things to do in Hanoi, and certainly, there was lots of respect and reverence by the Vietnamese visitors.

Definitely need a guide to explain the significance of Ho Chi Minh to the Vietnamese people as there’s no information boards or other materials. We were lucky to visit when the guard was changing, otherwise not really a lot to see. One lady in our party was wearing shorts and had to ‘hire’ a skirt from a vendor near the security gate otherwise she wasn’t going to be allowed in.

Large square with a security entrance into it. Keep in mind the restricted area markings, it's easy to miss as it's marked along the ground on footpaths. Recommend coming when they raise the flag but other than that this site is pretty odd.

The mausoleum of Ho Chi Ming, the national hero of the Vietnamese, a palace built by the French during the colonial period, and the outbuilding where Ho Chi Ming, who refused to live in this palace after independence, stayed in the same garden and grew different plants in the huge garden around it, are among the places to visit.

Visiting this monument you realize something that you don't notice in the rest of the city and the nation: that you are in a dictatorship! You queue and they shout at you if you go a millimeter out of the queue, they shout at you if you turn your head behind you and don't look forward, if you stop for a second longer than necessary, they take your hat off your head and take their hands away badly from your pockets when you enter the room where Ho Chi Minh's mummy is. All the soldiers are mean and frowning. I don't know if the hero of the Vietnam War would have wanted all this, but you really understand the weight of dictatorship in the life and society of men. Bad experience from this point of view.

We figured a trip to Hanoi would not be complete without seeing Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum, so we had a look. The streets around it are closed off, so our Grab driver dropped us off a couple blocks away and we walked over. It was nothing very exciting, but it didn't take much time either. We didn't go inside---we didn't want to be bothered with the dress code or the line. I wouldn't make this one a high priority, but I'm glad we saw it.
Look very impressive with the big open grounds in front of it and the ceremonial guards standing guard. If history is your thing then this maybe of interest. For me it was not very interesting.

A dive into the contemporary history of Vietnam. A tribute to the father of the nation. We learn little there. Attention ladies, bring "a decent outfit", women's knees must not be visible, a police officer checks and refuses the recalcitrant who are reduced to buying pants either from street vendors or in shops a little further away, cheaper. As you leave, the street vendor offers you to buy the pants at half price.

Not much more than an interesting memorial to Ho Chi Minh. It’s one to ‘tick off’ when visiting Hanoi provided the queues to get in aren’t ridiculous. Fascinating to see the cross section of people - Vietnamese nationals and foreign tourists who visit and their differing reactions.
Long lines and a long walk to see the actual body of Ho Chi Min - interesting experience but not a must see. Expect to spend over an hour here.
At the time we were there, we could only look at the mausoleum from the outside. The dress code had to be correct and you had to go through a baggage check before you were even allowed to go near it.

Not very exciting for non-communists or non-Vietnamese, as we don't celebrate the cult of Ho Chi Minh to the same extent; lots of people and quite crowded; if you go, then definitely with a guide to understand the background.

Entry to the Ho Chi Minh mausoleum is not permitted and the time I spent standing and observing the place was only worth it because I was accompanied by a Vietnamese guide who knew a lot about the country's history and was able to explain many things to me.

We had wanted to walk in the square, not so keen to actually go in to be honest.  Seemed a lot of guards around the day we were there, they were all armed which was a bit creepy.  I wasnt allowed to walk in the square because I had shorts on, thought that was a bit strange, but respected the guard and walked around.  It was 36 degrees!

It is only open in the morning, and you are not allowed to take anything in with you, no camera or bags. It was closed when we went. It is a very impressive building, and we watched the changing of the Guard at the entrance door while we were there. It is a lovely area to walk around however the attitude of some of the guards, if you ask them for directions needs a bit of adjusting, as it is a tourist area.

We didn't go inside the tomb as we thought there was some official event going on, thousands of people in white and red queuing in the square in front. We later found out this is an everyday occurrence as only open on a morning until 1130. We went to the grounds and presidential palace. Very strict dress code ( no bare shoulders, no shorts) and security, through scanners ,armed soldiers everywhere. We walked around the grounds and saw the house "Uncle Ho" used instead of the old colonial palace. All in all very interesting.
Not being able to visit such a unique place because it is closed every Friday through Monday was a disappointment. Fortunately, there was the changing of the guard.

do not be scared off by the line.  It was unbelievably long when we were there (holiday for locals) but it moved very quickly  We had an English speaking guide so got a lot more out of it than if we went on our own
The mausoleum is closed until August 15th. Very unclear information from our hotel and when we searched online.Still worth while visiting the grounds and premises where Ho Chi Min lived and worked.

Everywhere I read that the mausoleum is open, but in fact closed to the public from June 13 to August 15 (2022) for annual maintenance, no clear information about that 😅 you can walk around, and continue with the presidential palace and Hô Chi Minh house (not free) and finish by the one Pilar pagoda.

The Mausoleum in Ba Dinh Square is the resting place of Ho Chi Minh, Vietnam's most popular leader. The site is very heavily guarded and when entering the Mausoleum there is no opportunity to stop and linger as the visitors must continuously "flow" through. It is important that you dress with respect and cameras are forbidden. The Mausoleum is closed to the public  between September and December and also some days of the week.

Didn’t want to go into the actual mausoleum and anyway it was either closed due to coronavirus or due to time/ day we visited . Cant’t quite understand why foreign tourists would want to actually view the body. Impressive building and grounds, rather ‘ Stalinist’ looking. The main guards changed whilst we were there, very smart all in white uniforms and precise marching etc. The soldiers/guards were all, or seemed to be, much taller than your average Vietnamese! A must see in Hanoi but not that memorable.

The mausoleum has a very grand appearance from the outside, with a large square for ceremonies at the front. The guards that patrol the area around the mausoleum are very regimented and quite hostile. Seeing Ho Chi Minh’s embalmed dead body was quite a strange feeling, as he looked just like a Madam Tussauds wax work. Worth a quick walk by the outside of the mausoleum, but that’s it in my opinion.
It is part of a visit to Hanoi. When we went, it was very quiet. But I wouldn't stand in line for it...
Since my visit was after closing time, I was able to wander around freely. Admission was also free. In front of the temple is a vast, beautifully cleaned plaza.
Visited the Mausoleum and it was a site to see. It was on huge grounds and I am unsure if we allowed in as it was closed. Worth a quick visit as not much to see.

My friends and I toured Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum during the Corona Virus outbreak which meant that it was fairly empty. Even with it being so empty it wasn't that impressive. If you have some free time, it's worth a visit. But it's not something you need to see.

A must do to see guarded body of revered leader Ho Chi Minh. Been embalmed since 1972. Told to wear masks although not everyone wore them. No queues due to impact of corona virus. Glad we didn’t queue for this.

Very strict entry, currently must wear a mask for Coronavirus,but we did remove it once we were inside the compound as it was empty of people and therefore were not going to catch or pass anything on. The building itself was quite plain, we had expected lavish decorations and fold etc, but I guess as a place of mourning, plain is better? We were probably here no longer than 5 minutes.at least it was free!
This is literally a place where Ho Chi Minh City sleeps.
It's a very pretty place. The monument is imposing. We discovered it during a bike ride. The square is large and beautiful. I recommend at least a short detour.
The Mausoleum is definitely a must-see. An impressive building that is well guarded on all sides.

A vast sprawl of a place and the mausoleum itself is huge!  Ho Chi Min is treated like a god with dozens of guards patrolling and managing visitors.  Cameras and sunglasses aren't allowed - I even had to remove my prescription "reactolite" glasses which is a trifle over-the-top.

walking down from Tran Quoc pagoda/west lake allows views of outside of the Mausoleum and royal palace.I am not sure what the bag policy is and this made my visit uncomfortable.  After the initial security screening I was told to check in my small back pack.  (many people were carrying much larger bags inside).  At the luggage window they said I should take my camera, phone and wallet with me.  This was actually all that was really in my bag.  I then went through in the queue to see Ho Chi Minh.  At the electronics window they did not want to take in my camera as it was too small.  So i ended up carrying in my hands all my stuff.  I put my wallet in my pocket and was a bit worried about pickpockets in the queue but was then told off for putting my hand  in/near my pocket which is not allowed by Ho Chi Minh.  so I am very unclear what the policy is and what to advise people planning a visit.  I get the impression they wanted to identify anyone with a camera/electronic device at initial security and then store these at the camera storage window later on but clearly the different teams are not working to the same policy?Overall it is very interesting to go to this complex but due to all the confusion about the bag policy, difficulty carrying my stuff in my hands rather than conveniently in my bag and attitude of the staff/guards it was not a reverent experience - lacks reflection and peace I expected for a final resting place.

We were lucky and didn't have to wait. The grounds are spacious and everything is very well organized. The guards didn't make a convincing impression. Given the importance of the place, chatting and such right in front of the mausoleum seemed odd. It's nothing like the guards in London, for example. Photography inside is forbidden, but it's tolerated on the grounds.

When I visited there were long queues to get into the Mausoleum, so we were advised by our tour guide to give it a miss as it would impact on the rest of the sightseeing we could do that day.  So if visiting leave plenty of time.  All I  managed was to get some photos of the building when visiting the square.

Uncle Ho was an extraordinary figure in the fight for his country's freedom, and his mausoleum perfectly reflects his legacy. However, I was disappointed by the visit because almost everything is forbidden... Photography, etc. In short, respect is only right for this great man, but the security measures are excessive. An average visit overall.

It's an interesting place to visit, where you can reflect on Ho Chi Minh's life, be surprised by his sleeping-like appearance, and feel the socialist atmosphere in the magnificent buildings and security measures. However, we are, after all, foreign tourists. I also felt a sense of guilt for peeking into a place that is sacred to the Vietnamese out of mere curiosity.

From the moment we entered the park, we were put in line. Cameras were checked in. Then, in pairs, we lined up single file. During the walk to the building, we weren't allowed to talk or laugh; the guards kept reminding us of the rules. Hands out of pockets, no hats or caps... A guard every two meters. You could feel the communist atmosphere.

There is some controversy here, as Ho Chi Minh's wish was to be cremated, not embalmed. Every year between October and December, his body is taken to Russia for preservation. Photography is prohibited inside the building, but there is a free cloakroom where you can leave your camera and cell phones while you visit, or you can carry your belongings in your backpack, but don't try to take them out inside. If you have food and drinks, you must also leave them in the cloakroom.
We decided against the long wait to enter. The mausoleum itself is a beauty from the outside probably not worth waiting to see a dead body. Go see HCM's house nearby instead

Unless you're a history buff, I wouldn't recommend it. It's a historical site, a plaza that looks better at night for a few photos, and that's about it. There are more interesting things to see nearby.
Obviously, this is a place worth visiting for its interest in Vietnamese history.

Nothing particularly remarkable is visible; the mausoleum and the plaza are quite large. It's worth visiting to understand the mindset of the communist era, characterized by massive buildings with little architectural merit.

When we entered, we were told we couldn't bring in our backpacks.  The unsmiling guards shoved our belongings into a bag and shooed us away, handing us a tag to claim our belongings later.  As we followed the other people, walking forward into the open space in front, we were yelled at to retrace our steps, to enter through another way.  And yet, those others could do it ...?Ok, so we obeyed and walked into the other pathway with a canopy overhead. Further on, we saw the toilet sign and moved to visit it.  The guard there stopped us and signaled us to keep walking, with a cold expression on his face. We were bewildered at all the unfriendly treatment.  If we had done anything wrong, at least we should be accorded some politeness being shown.  Body language and facial expression can really hurt.We decided to leave.

Ho Chi Minh City is a simple and quiet place that doesn't take much time and is free to enter. The best time to visit is in the evening when the lights are beautiful and the area around the shrine is large.
At this site, the austerity of the regime is fully felt. We proceed in strict single file and in silence until we reach the shrine containing Ho Chi Minh's body.

It is interesting to see once.  It is a long process to get through the lines and into the mausoleum - took us about 30 minutes or so and it didn't seem to be that busy.  You are directed by stoic soldiers who scold you for taking photos or talking very loud as you are walking - one must be reverent!!  Once inside you keep moving slowing around HCM's body and out - about 2-3 minutes walking around the case where his body is - much like Lenin's mausoleum in Moscow.  Worth a visit.
Many guards, which gave a safe impression. Otherwise more for Vietnamese than for tourists from Europe.
Nice walk but we couldn't come in. Good to know it's part of the Hanoi visit because that area worth the visit.

Massive space and lawn area that you can’t enter not sure why!!! For me the strict rules, regulations and armed guards are a complete over kill and dampens your experience.Remembering he’s not a king, for the Vietnamese people a great warrior from past history and should be remembered but to this scale!!!!For the wait and how uncomfortable security makes you for me not a must do in Hanoi.
The mausoleum is not open to tourists.  The area does contain Ho Chi Minh's house and other government buildings.
This is an important historical site that also contains gardens and the living quarters of Ho Chi Minh.
They are closed from 12pm to 2pm and the museum was also closed at different times. Be aware to avoid disappointment

I went there on a tour from Japan. It's a mausoleum dedicated to Ho Chi Minh. His remains are enshrined there, and apparently they are open to the public on designated days, allowing people to see them. However, there are long lines, so it's best to go with plenty of time to spare.

The Mausoleum was the first sight we were to visit today, however even before 9 am the line was winding around the corner, I think we would have to have edited already an hour to get in. Decided to miss to mausoleum and comeback latter just to visit Ho Chi Minh palace. The mausoleum closes at 10 am. So we just got to see the outside, I was ok with that.

This was a good experience to do. We saw the mausoleum and visited the garden.To enter the main grounds is free, but none Vietnamese have to pay a small amount to view the garden and memorabilia of Ho Chi Minh.Worth doing if you are in the city.
The mausoleum is located in a big area where you can find the one pillar paogda and also the presidential Palace.

This is a must see if you are in Hanoi although there is actually not much to see apart from Ho Chi Minh’s body. There was a long line of visitors when I went and you have to go through in two lines so it takes a while to go through the mausoleum.
Went there to check out the body of Ho chi Min which is supposed to be kept inside. Closed Monday evening, so be careful!
The Mausoleum is very dominant along the strip. Always being watched by the guards. Just looking at it from outside wasn’t very exciting.
This Mausoleum Ho Chi Minh had a unique style of building and located same with One Pillar Pagoda in the center of Hanoi.The ambience is very calm and we can get relaxing time in the park.

Not what you’d call a fun day out and definitely didn’t expect the view inside, the whole experience was like a day out in the USSR in the 1970s. But historical and part of the reason we visit foreign countries.

The line was very long, but tolerable. This is likely due to going on a weekend, so going during a weekday would probably be preferable. The viewing of Ho Chi Minh was very short, we were probably in there for 30-60 seconds. Afterward, there are some pagodas, vendors, and a museum that is an extra charge.
An imposing gray building houses the remains of President Ho Chi Minh.

The mausoleum has remains of the most iconic leader of Vietnam. It's a vast space, green and well organized. You have to deposit your bags at the entry. You can't take pictures inside the mausoleum. Expect to spend about 45 minutes here. You can proceed to the One Pillar Pagoda from here. Tip: please check the time and day of mausoleum open schedule. The mausoleum is closed on Monday by the way.
You can only enter the mausoleum in the mornings, except on Mondays and Fridays.

We were primarily there to see Ho Chi Minh’s house and go to the mausoleum but the way-finding was non existent and the grounds are large. Even the brochure hasn’t a map. When we asked for directions we were sent to the Ho Chi Minh Museum which was interesting but not where we intended to go. By the time we found his house, we couldn’t find the entrance so we wandered around the mausoleum only to discover it wasn’t open. There was no information online to indicate this. We still enjoyed it but it wasn’t what we had hoped it to be.
It's quite nice, you should definitely do it, but I preferred the palace gardens to the 20th-century mummy... to each their own... You be the judge.
In my opinion, it's a visit that not everyone will enjoy; it doesn't resonate with me much. It's more about what it signifies, or the value it holds for the Vietnamese people.
Worth’s visit , guards are very smart and gardens are beautiful . Bit creepy though especially as it wasn’t what he wanted
Unfortunately the mausoleum was closed the day that we visited...I would have thought that our guide would have known in advance...very disappointed.

I've visited the tomb of Lenin in Moscow as well. These men had powerful feelings, society would have more harmony without disparity. The reality is, it doesn't work. Basic human nature dictates we want more, we what what other's have...in all these socialist societies, it ends this way. They become market societies again.

Booked by agency with full all day program we visited HCM mausoleum. We weren't very interested in seeing his tomb to tell the truth, we went early so people was not quite a lot. Lines went quick so didnt have to wait long time. So many guards and deadly silence inside. We really liked the beautiful gardens and the guards shifting! It was just just good compared to  the museum that was amazing! No photos are allowed inside!
Picture paints a thousand words, all could only be experience from the outside. There was a museum but it was closed on a Saturday, 30 Nov while I was there.
A sort of copy of the Lenin maussoleum for the local communist heroIn terms of architecture there is nothing special in this monument

We were ushered through queues of hundreds of people. Our belongings were scanned and we had to walk through a body scanner also. All water bottles and food were removed and thrown out of our bags.  We had to walk from station to station with cameras in coloured bags or tags to collect our cameras at certain stops. A tab but annoying. The thing that did not impress us on this tour is that NO-ONE was allowed to use the toilets unless the tour was over. A bit over the top if you ask me. We had a  teenager and older people on our tour who came away with with a disappointing impression of this only this particular part of our tour. Otherwise okay as this tour is free and the Moselium is open 3 days a week to see "Uncle Ho" as per the brochure and website.
We managed to get there but the lines were long. So we walked around the block and had a perfect photo op of the outside. To be honest the only attraction inside is tomb

I was pretty nonplussed by the viewing of the chalky looking corpse of Uncle Ho as he is known. Most interesting to me was the experience of watching the Vietnamese citizenry as they came to pay homage to their dead leader.

A gloomy mausoleum worth seeing, even just from the outside under the gaze of the stiff-necked soldiers on guard, in an imposing and very spacious setting. Overall, it's worth a visit, even if it's brief. There are much better things in the city.

It is big for sure but a view face on is all one gets for the price of admission.  The narrative from our guide was good and that was the value for me.  I would put this towards the bottom of the list of things to do, especially on a busy tourist day.
Please check the time carefully before you go.
Honorable and memorable tribute to Ho Chi Minh provided an opportunity for many to take time to reflect on a man who contributed much to the people of Vietnam.

I couldn't go in because I wasn't wearing long clothes 😭 But I was still able to look around Ba Dinh Square via a side path :) There were so many police officers standing around that I was a bit nervous lol.
