I was loitering around in short clothes when some guy approached me selling long clothes or scarves, but I gave up on visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum heh.
Respect for the site is mandatory, and rightly so. The mausoleum faces the National Assembly, and the Communist Party building is nearby... ;)
The hotel receptionist told us it was open at midday, which wasn't true.
Very touristy. Masses of people are herded past the body of Ho Chi Minh. It's something you have to see if you're in Vietnam. But that's about it.

I believe that one cannot ignore the history and political regime in Vietnam, which is why it is interesting and almost a must-see to explore and visit this entire site, culminating with a visit to the mummified figure of Ho Chi Minh.

A very popular destination for tourists, but once you're queued up, walked past the body and left, you may feel underwhelmed. I was when I did this on a previous visit.On this visit, it was during the two months each year that the mausoleum is closed to the public.As you walk by, there are some interesting trees and gardens to be seen, plus the many white-clad guards stopping tourists from getting too close.The building itself is minimalist.Visit it if you wish, but if you do want to go inside, make sure it's open when you're there.
Unfortunately, didn't get to go into the mausoleum as went during afternoon closing time. Still recommend to go as you can see local activities and laid-back lifestyle. Wide land space and windy.
The complex (which includes the mausoleum and the museum) is a must-see for tourists, but it should be better maintained. Too much time is spent in queues.
I went on a Friday afternoon, but apparently, entry to the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is only permitted in the morning.
It is one of the great monuments dedicated to important people in communist countries.
Beautiful area with interesting architecture, the queues though can be two or more hours long unless you have pre booked for fast access.

As with similar monuments in Moscow or Beijing, its importance must be assessed in light of the respect and devotion the country's inhabitants have for their leader, remaining somewhat distant for the rest of the world. There is nothing noteworthy from an architectural or any other perspective.

A huge grey building that holds ho chi minh's body. You can view his body but in Oct /November he goes to Russia for keeping him looking good so not avaliable to view when I was there. Security at gates to check bags and appropriate dress only, no bare arms etc. You will here whistles blow when people step on the grass.

Was there but didn’t manage to enter. The building itself is guarded by 2 guard at the door and the whole premise is huge, lots of empty space  prob 2- 4 Football field? Please be there early. It only open to visitor in the morning and certain day is closed to public too. If you dont mind just admire the structure of the grey building and the surrounding, it is open till evening.
I was left wondering if it's possible to enter the mausoleum building. As soon as we got close, the guards would tell us to turn back!
A square with an imposing mausoleum dedicated to Ho Chi Minh, but I must say the reality didn't give me any pleasant sensations or emotions.
Visit to the changing of the guard, a highly restricted and controlled area. A place of political importance during Ho Chi Minh's era. Close to the parliament and ministries.

An imposing place due to the mausoleum. The visit allows you to discover the park gardens located behind it. The propaganda panels on the principles of life are revealing of the culture. A few shops and a small temple in the middle of a pretty pond. A peaceful place.

Found it odd to see a preserved dead body laying in a bed in a huge mausoleum built in his honour. The line up to get in is massive every day of the week which goes to show the respect the Viets have for the man. Worth a look.

Having not search information about the place, actually the place is last rest place for Uncle ho, thats why the line is very huge because we go there on sunday, theres no ticket fee, but if you want to go around the palace the ticket fee is VND 40.000, not much to see, since many place that we can only see from distance, and crowded that we hard to get a photo

It's located in the embassy section of town. So things are a little bit quieter than the old quarter. Where to visit if you have time but if you didn't go I don't think you're missing much. If you do go for fun step a little bit over the yellow line the guards a whistle at you.

It's a place of worship for the Vietnamese, extremely guarded, but it's worth visiting to be in tune with them. I don't recommend going before 12 noon, it's too hot and there's nowhere to take shelter. You walk a lot and to get to the entrance you have to go around, but access is easy.

If you're someone who doesn't want to miss a thing, visiting the coffin inside is a must-do experience. They don't allow entry in shorts, low-cut tops, etc., and they might even reprimand you inside if you have your arms crossed or in your pockets; they consider that disrespectful. Be careful about taking photos, even with your phone (they keep DSLRs and small cameras at the entrance and return them on the way out). Depending on the day, there can be long lines, but even then, they move you very quickly. If you want to avoid all that hassle, take a walk around the outside and take some photos of the surroundings.

This is a special place of honor for one of the truly great individuals who attempted—and ultimately succeeded—in achieving independence for Vietnam after a lifelong struggle against colonial powers. Americans should take time to enter the long, but well organized lines to view Ho Chi Minh’s remains but should also learn this man’s indomitable spirit. There is an especially tragic cast to Ho’s attempts, after WWI in Paris, to obtain the aid of the US in escaping his country’s subjugation by France. President Wilson’s aides brushed this effort aside, and thus foiled what could have meant the avoidance of what Vietnamese call the American War.
A quick visit, almost at a run, just enough time to walk around Uncle Ho's coffin after queuing for about twenty minutes. For those interested in Vietnamese history, it's fascinating!

We visited the Mausoleum as a starter to our trip.  The lines were manageable and the walk by was enough.  To get your bags out of the “free” storage you had to pay to go through the museum or walk a mile around the area to get back to the front.Taxis are available at the entrance but be careful because they will rip you off.  There are plenty of scammers in Hanoi taxi ranks.  It was very hot and rainy which added to the unpleasantness.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is located not far from the Old Quarter and consists of Ho Chi Minh's tomb, the Museum, the Presidential Palace, and Ho Chi Minh's house. It is a popular destination for Vietnamese and tourists alike. The tomb is a monumental structure, and inside is a sarcophagus containing Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body. Cameras or video cameras are not permitted. Not far away is the Museum, which houses an exhibition dedicated to Ho Chi Minh's life. Then there is the Presidential Palace and the house where Ho Chi Minh lived for more than 10 years. It is a must-visit to learn more about Vietnam.

Even though I did spend a couple of hours here and some of it was quite interesting the experience as a whole was quiet disappointing i couldn't see the body which was probably the only reason I went there I kept asking the guards and ticket counter as to which way to the body either they didn't know themselves or they provided me with wrong infoNot a great visit and yeah the tickets were 80,000 VND (3.5$)
It's a free facility, but there is a security check. It's

My husband and older child enjoyed the mausoleum although it was very turisty and they felt like cattle being driven.. My 8 months old daugther was denied entry, without any explanation. So we had to sit outside and wait.

It is a nice space and a building, but this spot look like you are in china,they keep it nicely, but Nothing much to see.It is a busy area and not much information of what to do and how to get in and etc.
We arrived at the Ho Chi Minh site in Hanoi for a visit.

More than just a mausoleum as there are large grounds gardens and buildings of Vietnamese history. Immaculately laid grounds enclosing historical places and artifacts. We spent several hours here with our well informed guide.
It was crowded because it was the weekend, but
A symbol of the esteem in which this leader is held. Tight dress restrictions - no bare shoulders. Tight control of where you can walk.Definitely worth visiting.

May after visiting Halong bay and Ninh Binh, we were looking for something special and We felt there was not much here. We would not regret if we didn't have a photo in front of it. If would are desperate for a photo,  you can have it from roadside and it would be the same.
The mausoleum was not open so we saw the outside and Ho's house. Worth a look, nothing very interesting there and was free.  It is not far from the Old Quarter.

This was a great sightseeing activity. Photo opportunity but that’s about it We walked there from around the lake area. Was interesting seeing the sights along the wayGet a cab to or from there and expect to be ripped offWalking for public transport best

You can't miss this place if you are in Hanoi. It's a landmark but i didn't quite need to go in to see.... The locals here idolises HCM. You have to walk through security at the parameters. No singlets allowed.

The entrance is a journey in itself. No lighters allowed inside. No food or drink past the entrance although they are available just before the exit  (lol)  Allow a minimum of 30 minutes in the sun get to any of the buildings and /or information. Once inside information and buildings are nothing to write about.

I had originally planned to visit Ho Chi Minh's birthplace and memorial hall, but hadn't intended to visit the Presidential Mausoleum because I felt a bit uneasy about it. However, when I asked soldiers for directions, they all pointed me in one direction, and eventually I had to pass by the Presidential Mausoleum. There was a queue to enter, bags were searched, and water and food had to be stored. Inside, silence was required, and the lighting was dim. President Ho was placed in the center. After leaving the mausoleum, I followed the signs to another tourist spot: Ho Chi Minh's office.

A long walk from the old quarter but worth the health outcomes.....once at the Mausoleum it’s huge and striking and the history was engaging and interesting. The WC for females was poor and not clean.

I came in the middle of the day and by that time the mausoleum was already closed. Because it was meant to be a family trip, I didn't get the chance to visit on the other days in Hanoi. So I just have to be content that I already visited this about 10 years back on my last visit to Hanoi. The opening times are quite restricted so make sure you check in advance if you actually want to go inside the mausoleum.

We only saw it from the outside and you can't even get close. A very Soviet, communist-style building. It's worth visiting the pagoda on a single pillar in the same area. You need to be covered up, with no shoulders exposed.

A large modern air conditioned building is home to the body. A very impressive marble building built by the Russians in a Russian style. Lots of guards in their formal whites add to the spectacle. Quite a process & walk to access it but all flat & under cover. Very hot in sun.One pillar Pagoda within the same complex. seemed to be quite a bit of transport in the area - we were on a bus trip.Security is tight - no big bags or big cameras or food/fluids. No photos inside.

It was a quick trip because:-temperature was mid 30 celcius-not sheltered walkway-ho chi minh's tomb was closed (Mondays and Fridays) -attraction closes by middayPositives- nice gardens- good display of cars and buildings used by ho chi minh- costs on 40k dong per person
It was incredibly crowded, with visitors coming from all over Vietnam. You can't bring in luggage other than valuables, so you need to leave it at the entrance. It's best to visit empty-handed.

Quite frustrating to miss the chance going inside this deeply historical place in a political sense. Lines are long that we forego the wait since we need to visit some sights.  Next time we visit Hanoi I’ll be ready.
I think if u interested in history you should visit this mausoleum. Pay for 40.000 each person to visit this complex

We came here on our first day and whilst it was relatively interesting to read more about Vietnamese history, it was more interesting to see how biased everything was! Essentially every museum in Hanoi is an appreciation society of the former leader, Ho Chi Minh. Slightly propaganda-like from our view. I still would actually recommend visiting the museums as it is an insight into the culture but probably would take it with a pinch of salt!

It's a mausoleum; you enter and are always watched by guards, and you pass by Ho Chi Minh's sarcophagus where you see his embalmed body. Beforehand, you leave your camera and pick it up on the way out.

I only saw the mausoleum from the outside during the changing of the guard. That was quite nice to see, but the building itself is large and therefore imposing, though nothing particularly beautiful. The surrounding garden, however, with the buildings where Ho Chi Minh stayed, is certainly worth a look. You can see everything in about an hour. The guards are very strict and blow their whistles if you get too close to a line.
While its a highly respected place, there is not much to see. And my guide was no help at all as he was in a rush to get us out of there

Most of the former residences can only be viewed from the outside, not inside, so the tour was quick. If you want to see the remains, you need to check the opening hours first, as they are not open all year round; they were closed when we went.
The story might be exciting and interesting, but the mausoleum itself isn't really that impressive. Better to stroll through the park afterwards.

The complex is enormous. Special paths have been created for tourists to follow so they don't stray from the route. The Ho Chi Minh Dam is well-preserved, but it's difficult to get a proper look, as you can't stop near it.

This place is very significant for the vietnamese people and has a big square in front of it. Nothing much to see in it but still has a nice surrounding. The guards stand there for hours without moving an inch.
Unfortunately, closed during summer, but still worth seeing. The are is very pretty, lots of walking, but well worth the visit.

There is a flag ceremony at 6 am in the morning.  The only reason we were there at this time was we arrived on the night train from Sa Pa early in the morning.  So it was interesting looking at the many many people doing their morning sport activity.  We were surprised at the number of people doing that.... They all stopped what the do when the flags where up and the national song was playing.  These people are very proud of their country and their culture

Mausoleum is apparently shut in July and August due to preservation work on uncle Ho, so you can just walk around the building and the presidential palace gardens. Loads of local tourist. Women should wear “respectable clothes “ - this means no naked shoulders, short skirts or shorts. You tick the box and move onto another sight...
Its rigid architecture is striking. The empty spaces around it enhance its presence. It is undoubtedly an example of the Soviet megalithic style used to amplify its message.

Go early in the morning. Prepared to be screened like going through airport security. Then there maybe a long line to wait and shuffle  towards the mausoleum. Seems like there are guards at almost every turn. You are being watched through the entire time from entrance to exit. The ground are beautifully kept. But you are not allowed to walk around the ground.Once reached the inner chamber, everyone must keep moving with the line. You are not allowed to pause to view. You probably have 15-25 seconds to look as you must keep moving along with the line.

Most Important. GET YOUR TIMINGS RIGHT. This place works on a timing of its own. No site on the web can give you an accurate schedule and no local tour operator will give you accurate details as they want your business and rather you take a tour with them. Get there nice and early. Before 8am and you will be lucky enough to go inside. We go there late. At 945am and they didn’t let us in. So we had to settle for the photos on the outside and could not go inside to see the body. Dress well as they are strict about the attire if you go inside to view the embalmed body. We missed out, hope you get to see it.
We didn't arrive in time to see the inside, and we could have saved ourselves the visit. It was incredibly hot, and it was just a huge open space with no shade.

It is a historically important place to visit in Ha Noi.  However, I would say that it was not easy to find the entrance.  Also, keep in mind that it is closed during a long lunch break from 10:30 or 11:00 (not sure, pls check) until 2pm.  Also, afternoon visit does not last long either.If you plan well, I believe it worth visiting.

There's nothing there except for the large temple building. There are many places you shouldn't go or enter, and police officers are constantly blowing their whistles to warn people. If you think of it as a place you come to just to say "I've been there," then I guess it's tolerable.
We couldn't visit the mausoleum because the body is being restored. It's a shame: I would have liked to see it. We had to settle for a souvenir photo.

The mausoleum is in an open area with not much else to see...watch where you walk, as there's a yellow zone and if you step on it, the guards will whistle at you until you leave...the only interesting thing is that at the end of the mausoleum, turning right, you can find a park and admire the one-pillar pagoda...there's not much else to see

I didn't have much time, so I only saw the exterior. I spent too much time at the nearby World Heritage site, Thang Long Imperial Citadel. I heard it's about a 15-minute walk from Thang Long Imperial Citadel, but it was too hot to walk. I took a Grab (ride-hailing service). I really wanted to go to the museum as well.
It's a big space with Ho Chiinh's mausalium. It wasn't open when we went. Not much to see but our guide gave us a great history which made it all the more interesting

Security check quick and easy.Very beautiful gardens and temple behind the mausoleum itself -- worth a visit for these alone. Refreshments available.We didn't explore the whole site, so perhaps even more further in. Perhaps more on the life of the Bringer of Light?
I'm glad that I got to see the mausoleum and the Ho Chi Minh area of Hanoi. I knew before I left that the mausoleum would be closed, so, not a problem
Yes, although it's simply a gigantic building that you can't get closer than 25 meters or more because there's more to see in the area, you can't miss it.
Entrance fee 40k dong each, park with 4 buildings where it is indicated that Ho Chi Minh lived or worked there... nothing particularly interesting
Wish they had had more English so we could have understood.  Thank goodness we read up on it prior to our visit.   Warning!!!   Closes early in the day.

The mausoleum is the appreciation and respect prof of the Vietnamese people for Uncle Ho, the founder of independent Viet Nam. It was build in cooperation with Russia, being inspired by the Lenin Mausoleum.It is in the political and administrative quarter of Ha Noi. There is also a large square where the parades take place on National Day.It is closed in June and July for maintenance.
The only real interesting part of this stop on our Hanoi Tour was the changing of the guard. The Mausoleum was large but lacked the style of the other monuments in Hanoi.
You need to have your shoulders covered to go through security. You can look... but only from a distance. A shame. Otherwise, the monument is nice to see.
We didn’t Get to go inside. We were told that Mr Ho sometimes needs to go to Russia for a touch up. Check out if he’s there or not before you go.

Uncle Ho was under restoration at the time of our visit so we couldn't get into the mausoleum.  We visited the museum next door but to be fair, it lacked in substance and there was little in the way of text for the foreign visitor to read and understand.  It would have been nice to have entered the mausoleum but circumstance prevented it.  Check before you head out to visit.  The museum ism't worth the journey from the city centre alone.

The museum itself is relatively large and of course dedicated to Uncle Ho. My experience was too much emphasis on propaganda rather than the man. For example, a whole section was dedicated to celebrating his birthday up until 2018. A bit heavy to get through. All in all, a bit of a thin cup of tea. It must be said, however, that we did not have a guide and therefore got through quite quickly. We would have liked to have visited his Mausoleum. It was closed and I have also been told by other travelers that you cannot count on the opening hours. Remember to have your shoulders covered or you will not be able to enter the area!!

I did not know before I went here that the mausoleum undergoes maintenance every summer between June and August, so only the outside could be viewed. The museum was still interesting, although a bit abstract.

Mausoleum closed but good to see from outside. Cloudy morning so not hot. Would be terrible if sunny. Museum not that informative as very little English or French signage. Historical photos interesting. Headphones available but we didn’t purchase. May have helped. Displays very “ modernistic” and “symbolic” style. Not enough actual historical artefacts.
This is the resting place of Uncle Ho. Visits to the remains are only permitted in the morning.

The mausoleum is closed during the summer months for maintenance, therefore you could not get into the building to look at HCM dead body now. You can spend time wandering around the compound and One Pillar Pagoda, it is free of charge. You will need to go through a security check to get into the compound. You can also pay to get into the museum which is just beside the mausoleum.
Interesting visit. Sadly not able to go into mausoleum but the grounds were impressive and interesting.  Very imposing and revealing as to the importance of this leader.
Interesting place in memory of hcm. Can not visit all places i July August but it’s a must see place in Hanoi

Went there with the understanding that it was open but when we arrived we learned otherwise.  Despite this we see that the grounds are beautiful and it was interesting, albeit quite the propaganda lesson, to learn about Uncle Ho and to see where he lived. The party is on overload here with pushing how great he was.  If you take it in good fun it will be worthwhile. Reminds me of trips to Soviet satellite back in the day...

A typical tourist attraction in socialist countries, a cult of the national hero who led the revolution and transformed Vietnam into a country free from the domination of France and the United States...

1st we have to walk through the parade square. Then we will reach the mausoleum but the time we came, it was closed due to annual restoration so we couldnt see Sir Ho Chi Minh body. We went to this huge park with many houses where he used to stay and his lifestyle. There is a big pond. Good place to take pictures but very warm during the day.
Unfortunately it was closed during our visit and the dress code to enter the park was stricked. We decided to just stroll around the perimeter and appreciate masoulem from a polite distance.
It was Sunday, so perhaps the mausoleum was closed. Therefore, it didn't really add any value. However, the park was beautifully landscaped.

You can still go in and visit ‘uncle Ho’s’ house, car collection and stilt house. Of the three his house is the most interesting - the exhibit currently showcases a huge number of photographs of dignitaries he entertained and of his assembled government plotting various movements in the very rooms you can view inside.Don’t plan for a peaceful visit, absolutely packed with other tourists so that you move between and around the 3 sights in the same way as you might queue to get into a music festival. Beautiful grounds but unfortunately not much to learn here

The tour brought us here and we queued for more than one hour just to view the embalmed body of their leader Mr Ho Chi Minh. Well worth the long wait and upon entering no cameras, handphone or wearing of hats are allowed. No noise or talking either, the mood was very solemn and respectful. Guards are strict and they made sure you line up two by two. Upon exit you can visit the grounds which is huge and beautiful, well kept and simply amazing.
This visit is included in all travel packages; however, apart from the grandeur of the place, it hasn't been of any interest to me personally.

Huge complex, went there twice to look at HO Chi Minh's mummy but they hardly open that area, it get closed early may be they want to avoid tourist's heat. Outer are well planned and maintained. If you want to visit go early by 8 AM.

Went to see the outside but from other tourists who went inside, you can’t take photos, you can’t stop and there are horrendous lines. Even our tour guide said the same thing. Some people told me it would be only good if you see a Vietnamese citizen and were really in love with hi chi Minh; otherwise, as a tourist, probably would just skip this.
The visit was impressive and I would recommend it. We arrived at 8:30 am and there was already quite a long queue.
I suppose if you are in Hanoi I guess you should visit but it is just a big building in a big square and if you are not into political history it means very little
Nice to see, one sees respect from the visitors.........However, we could not go inside due to a VIP visit.......

The mausoleum is located in a square with a guarded entrance; to enter, you must pass through security. The mausoleum itself is enormous. Overall, it's worth visiting for no more than a one-time visit.
I am glad I did this, (ticked off the list) but after querying in the heat for 2 hours, I won't be going again.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum Complex is comprised of the mausoleum, stilt house, Presidential Palace, Museum, and One Pillar Pagoda.What we didn’t know even after looking up the website was that you can only go into the mausoleum in the morning, so we missed the boat on that one.The complex is open, but you only walk past the mausoleum and some of the other attractions there were also closed.Disappointed but not crushed.

We’ve been here at 9 am and there were too many people waiting just to get inside and que at least half an hour to see Ho Chi Minh. Native people have entrance for free, tourists have to pay for every entrance (garden, museum). You have to wear long pants, otherwise you won’t get inside. There’s a lot to see. I think visiting Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum Complex is a must.

Typical Communist mausoleum that was shut when we visited.  Yes, large, grey, imposing, sombre, dull.  No doubt impressive if you get entry to see HCM.  I've seen the Kims' so had no special regret at missing his.

The mausoleum was closed when we arrived after a long march and maybe that was just as well. Well, if you are in Hanoi it is one of the things you should see. But you can see it just as well, and get a picture of it, from a taxi or a bus too (as you can see from the pictures).

We only walked around the park because the tours are in the morning and there's a long wait to get into the mausoleum, so we went in the afternoon. We saw the changing of the guard in goose-step. A very formal place.
We were on a private tour and unfortunately it was closed. When it is open our guide said the lines takes hours to get in, but they do have movable walkways that cover the queues.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is an ode to the opulence of communists who pretend to be humble but then enjoy pharaonic projects. There's not much to see since we didn't go inside with Catai, the group we went with. The only interesting thing is a changing of the guard, not very spectacular, but it breaks the routine of a giant esplanade.
You had to queue for over an hour to go inside.  Our tour guide suggested we go back later when the mausoleum was closed, which we did.  Then it was only the outside of the building.
Enormous mausoleum on a very large square. Serious-looking soldiers in front of the building. We did not go inside.
Certainly interesting for the history that surrounds it, but from the architectural point of view and the visit offer, it lends itself to a quick passage

The Mausoleum houses the remains of the Vietnamese president of the same name, who led the Vietnamese revolution. It's a must-see if you're in Hanoi and doesn't take long. You can witness the changing of the guard ceremony if you're lucky enough to arrive on time.
In a vast square stands a mausoleum in a very martial style, with communist-era architecture. Nevertheless, it's an interesting site, one we're not used to.

It seems that the latest city tours don't include the mausoleum, even though it's on the itinerary. Sometimes tickets couldn't be arranged in advance, and the line was too long, or, as happened to us, it was simply closed. I spoke with other travelers who took a different city tour, and it was always the same story. Of course, I don't know if there are exceptions. Usually, you're taken to the Ethnological Museum instead.

I'm sure it's great... But when we went on a Sunday morning there must have been 300000 people queuing...It went right round the outside and all the way inside.Heaps and heaps of children and groups of Vietnamese people.It was 32 degrees and far too long to stay in the queue.

A walk past the (supposed) Ho Chi Min mummified corpse. This is one of those sights of Hanoi to be checked off the list. The gardens are quite beautiful and it was interesting to observe the reverence of the masses.
The veneration shown to this communist revolutionary is unbelievable.
After the security checks, you enter the mausoleum grounds. There's a strict fence around the mausoleum, and it's very nice to stroll through the grounds to reach the one-pillar pagoda.

Probably a must see when visiting Hanoi and the history of how it came to be is interesting.  Quite stunned to learn that President Ho Chi Minh can be viewed for 2 hours per day however we were not there at the right times to do this so simply a look and learn from afar, the temples beside are worth a quick look while you are there.
I think you should definitely visit if you go to Hanoi. Ho Chi Minh is buried there. I believe it's

When the sun is strong, a hat or similar is essential. There is no shade until you reach the building. Since it is a tourist spot a little far from the city center, taxi touts can be persistent. Be careful of taxis that will overcharge you.

We were going to see this as part of our tour, however when we were given the option to miss this out due to the 3 - 4  hours plus that we would queue for..... our vote was unanimous ....no queue. There were lots of school children visiting as part of graduation celebrations, so usually the wait may be less......

If you are in Hanoi you cannot miss going to see it, even if we did not enter where the body of Uncle Ho rests, as they call him, but a visit to the park with its residences as well as the experience of the changing of the guard are definitely worth the trip.
It provides a glimpse into how the country was run during the communist era: limited access without cameras, standing in pairs but with shoulders and legs covered.

Of course, we visited this during the Vietnamese holiday week, so after 2.5 hours we were finally allowed into the mausoleum. It was bizarre how crowded it was. But definitely worth it. Do make sure you go before 11:00 and wear appropriate clothing.
You are not allowed to bring your bag here. You leave it at the entrance.

We went to the mausoleum and it was a long walk because it was in the middle of a big square. The building was big, imposing and iconic. However because of the holidays it was closed to the public. A big letdown for us.

I hesitate to leave this review, but I have to say I kind of wish I'd skipped this. The display of him in that massive place, with a parade of people walking by. . . It's pretty common knowledge that this was not in accordance to his wishes for his burial. To be turned into a tourist attraction after accomplishing so much in life. . . I rather wish they'd made a memorial or a shrine. I find the display of him against his desires in such a manner really uncomfortable.I will say that the way he's laid out and guarded by the Vietnamese does display a lot of respect and reverence.
One of the main attractions, but there's not much to see. You have to follow a designated path.
A somber, martial atmosphere.

A typical Soviet construction with timeless security restrictions. A simple changing of the guard, with two guards in white uniforms standing motionless, watched closely by a superior who takes care to blow a whistle if anyone crosses a yellow line for a selfie. Important for those nostalgic for the 1968 demonstrations.

We arrived at the mausoleum and there was an incredibly long line in the scorching sun. We therefore asked the guide if we could skip this. We felt hesitant to ask because Ho Chi Minh City is very important. The guide was understanding and flexible and rearranged the itinerary for us so that we could return in the afternoon and visit the area without the line. This meant not entering the mausoleum, but it was fine for us.

Went through strict security(apparently, you are supposed to wear clothing covering your shoulders and knees) and walked in the humid heat and didn't even get to go inside to see anything in the afternoon. Witnessed first hand a female security staff yelling at a young slim built chinese women in short shorts and texting on her mobile phone to not come in. Yet there were young girl in short skin tight shorts walking around. We were told that in the morning there will be an hour line queue to view Ho Chi Minh's body.
On a very hot and humid day, this should not be your most important task, if you are not that keen on queuing for almost an hour, just to see a mummy or a wax image of uncle Ho.
Not of great architectural interest. It's impossible to visit during the day; you have to come very early in the morning... we saw it, it's fine.
What a " religion"! It's incredible what they profess to the leader of the Vietnamese revolution. Schools and people who seem well indoctrinated.

This site is closed on Tuesdays and Fridays; the place is immense and a bit chilly. Nearby, but not easily visible and poorly signposted, is the O-Pillar Pagoda, and a little further on, the West Lake. You can feel the communist atmosphere already experienced in Beijing.
Personally, I would bypass this. You queue for well over an hour to be herded into the mausoleum where you spend no more than 15 seconds.
Unfortunately we could not visit inside. As it closes 11am. So if you want to see Ho Chi Minh s body be early Nd be ready for long cue. No camera or metal allowed inside. Not even bags.

Ho Chi Minh is central to the people of Vietnam and his mausoleum is a place of pilgrimage. It's free to enter and don't be put off by the sight of long queues as the line moves very quickly. Oddly it all felt rather rushed and despite warnings to be respectful both in dress and behaviour, the hasty way we were marched through the room where his body lies in a glass case seemed rather DISrespectful to 'Uncle Ho'.The mausoleum isn't open all day every day so check the opening times to avoid disappointment.

1 hour and 40 minutes in the queue to pay 1 minute of respect to Uncle Ho. Only if you can bear the queue jumping, soldiers barking at everyone and depending on the time of year, the heat or rain (we had both). HCM himself looks well-preserved and is on display in a similar setting as VI Lenin in Moscow.
This was very large. Was interesting to hear the history behind this. Their ex ray  scanners were not working so we could not enter  to get a closer look.

We were the group. Our guide, Tang, explained a bit about Ho Chi Minh's life. You have to go through security to access the square where the Mausoleum is located. It was designed and built by the Russians, modeled after their own in Russia. Strict and understated. You can't go inside, only to take a few photos. Opposite is the Vietnamese parliament, where the only party in the country, the communist-capitalist party, is represented.

I came here with a group of travelers, and did not have a real desire to see HCM's mummified remains, but be aware that, IF you want to see his remains, you have to go on a day that he's *out* for visitation and it's regularly closed, and when not closed, it's packed. We did see the changing of the guard, which is always nice. The communist nature of the country is evident from the square, as the country's "legislature" is viewable from the square, and the statue of Lenin stands proudly on a little nook. The square itself is a required visit if you are in Hanoi, however, as it shows

Get there early as his Mausoleum closes at 11 AM and that line takes forever. I would forego the Mausoleum and just stroll through the complex, tour the Museum, and get out of there. You can take pictures all over but you can't even move your arms inside his tomb. Reminder, no bare shoulders allowed at all!
Very secure place...well-maintained gardens...but don't go before 2pm...otherwise everything is closed!

I made the walk here on a Tuesday morning. Contrary to other visitors reviews, there were not many people waiting for entry to the site, probably because there was little for me to see. I was not alone in experiencing some trouble in locating the main entrance but at least there was no entry charge. It was probably so quiet because the Mausoleum was closed for the day and the museum was not opening until later. I was continually pestered by an old man who wanted to tell me of his life as a Vietcong soldier but in reality wanted to be an unofficial tour guide. He kept waving his notebook at me containing written testimonials from previous ‘customers’. It was at least good to see the soldiers marching in front of the Mausoleum. In the event I had to admit I was not too bothered that it was not open.
An interesting stroll around the large buildings.Monday is a great day to go if you don’t want to see the actual body of HCM - as Mausoleum is closed that day it means the crowds are way less

We stopped by the Ho Chi Minh mausoleum twice for brief visits during separate tours. Our first visit was watch the nightly flag lowering ceremony at approximately 9:00pm. We did that with the Paloma motorbike tour. It was worth doing, as it is neat ceremony and good for a few pictures. We had booked another tour of the mausoleum assuming there would be a museum about his life. Our guide described that there would be a long line to get in to view his body in a glass coffin. We were not interested in this, so we just visited the exterior. The grounds are beautiful, and again definitely worth a stop for pictures. We can offer no opinion on the interior as we were a bit put-off by the idea of viewing a dead body... even if it is a major historical figure.

Completed in 1975, it houses the permanently preserved remains of Ho Chi Minh, who died in 1969. Visits are only permitted in the morning, and although there is a 1-2 hour wait to enter, the tour itself only lasts about 5 minutes.

Only worth visiting if you're staying in Hanoi for a while. The queue might seem daunting, but it moves very quickly. Everything is designed to keep people from lingering. Inside, you'll be reminded of the rules for even the slightest deviation. It's quite exceptional to be able to see someone embalmed.
We suggest taking the red bus op on op off which in about an hour allows you to see the main places of the city without tiring yourself.
We found this an interesting place to visit. We arrived after 11am so too late to view Ho Chi Minh but enjoyed the grounds, museum and pagoda.
There was a 2-3 hour wait, so I only viewed it from the outside. It was nice and clean, but didn't offer much. I took 2-3 photos from the outside and that was it.

I originally only planned to visit Ho Chi Minh's mausoleum, but when I arrived at 8:00 AM, I saw a long queue. I later learned that people were queuing to pay their respects to President Ho's remains. Security was tight throughout the queue; remember to wear shorts or skirts that cover your knees, otherwise you'll be forced to buy long pants at a nearby stall. People occasionally tried to cut in line, but thankfully, the police were there to maintain order. After security checks, you're not allowed to bring water or sharp objects; otherwise, you'll be forced to discard them. Larger backpacks are also not allowed inside the mausoleum; they must be checked in at the entrance. President Ho's face was serene; remember not to take photos. It was Sunday, but many children were wearing school uniforms and being led by their teachers on the visit—probably part of national education! The queue took about 45 minutes, not particularly interesting, but it showed the locals' respect for this late leader, which had a special meaning.

This is where Ho Chi Minh lived and ruled. An apparently simple and unpretentious man. Large, wide boulevards to stroll along. Get close to the houses and the pond where he fed his fish. He called the fish by standing in a special place by the water and there he clapped his hands and then the fish came to him.

Very busy, long wait to go inside, guide told us it was only open in the morning, but was open in afternoon as well.Interesting but not sure it was worth the wait in the heat, no water bottles allowed. Grounds very nice, but still hard to see due to crowds.
Large dimensions, architectural rigor, military aesthetics, but little interest in all aspects.
While Ho Chi Minh is not actually here the Mausoleum is on a grand scale.  You may even catch the changing of the guard.  Just do a quick walk around.
A large monument, well guarded by the army!

It is really something that one must do when visiting Hanoi, so don't be put off by any reviews and do go. However, it is just such a weird thing, to be walking fairly fast past a waxlike mummy of what was once a real person.Uncle Ho is laying there, just as he would if he was a Madame Tussaud's waxwork, and the crowds are moved along with firm politeness, but is something that needs to be done by the visitor. This is in order to try to get a feel for the awe and respect with which many of the Vietnamese people hold for their former 'Chu Tich' (President). The whole complex is enormous and the gardens and his summer house are also well-worthwhile visiting, something not obvious when first checking in. There's much more to this visit than just the mausoleum.Be prepared to be correctly dressed and to have bags and large cameras, or laptops, removed and retained until you exit, but this is not something I felt you needed to be concerned about.

The mausoleum was closed when we visited. There is strict security on bags and dress code.Best to have your shoulders covered and knees. It is a big place, with beautiful gardens although you are not allowed on the grass and there is a “no go zone” around the building. Spend some time looking at the other structures they are quite interesting.

We arrived with our tour guide with no line at the entrance. You walk through a scanner before entering the premise. The area is huge and an interesting place to visit, walk around the grounds. We were not able to go inside the mausoleum as it is closed on Friday and Monday to visitors.

This is the mausoleum where the body of Ho Chi Minh, the founding father of the nation, is enshrined. The number of visitors was extraordinary; even though we arrived at 8 AM, we had to wait in line for about two hours. We couldn't stop inside the mausoleum, so the actual viewing only took 2-3 minutes. We didn't need to leave our cameras or smartphones, but be aware that all drinks are confiscated at the entrance.

Although standing in the longest queue of my whole life (Saturday 10 AM) it worked out to visit the mausoleum at 11 AM. All bags and cameras will be taken from you and stored to pick up after your visit. Small handbag and camera you can keep. The mausoleum itself takes approx. 1 minutes or less, no pictures and short trousers allowed.The museum afterwards is in my opinion really bad, confusing set-up, bad English description of what has happened, pseudo artistic elements. Compared to the museums in hcmc (very well made) this one is not worth the entrance fee. Only if taking a guide, maybe...beware it closes at 12 PM or similar, so you need to leave early to do both or come back in the afternoon.

The queue was incredibly long, lasting about 30 seconds around the mummified body. Please don't slow down; the guards aren't particularly friendly and will tug at anyone who doesn't keep up! Photography is prohibited throughout the entire tour, starting from the entrance to the complex.

I visited the mausoleum on two occasions. The first time I went I wasn’t able to cross security because I was wearing a vest/string top/tank top (whatever you would like to call it). They said I couldn’t go in unless I was able to cover my shoulders.The second time I went I got through security fine, but the mausoleum itself was closed. I didn’t realise it was only open on specific days - so make sure you check before you go.I was able to walk through the grounds and took a picture outside the mausoleum. I am not sure what I would have been able to see if the mausoleum had been open. Possibly Ho Chi Minh himself (embalmed obviously)? Even if it had been open, I’m sure it would have been busy inside because there were a lot of tourists taking pictures outside.
Given the enormous queues and the ultra-short visit afterwards, we decided to skip this one.

Long waiting times. Tip: go a little before 11 am or very early. The mausoleum is closed in the afternoon. If you do not want to see the inside of the tomb, go in the afternoon when the park is freely accessible.
impressive layout of building and parade square.   dignified and wonderful to see many people going there to honour HCM

They stop selling tickets around 12pm during the week. On the weekend the line extends several city blocks. This is no exaggeration. Also if you go by cab make sure they drop you off at the entrance. Otherwise it'll take you 20ish minutes to walk around the entire complex to get in. This being said we were unable to get inside the mausoleum because of these circumstances.

Waited in line over 2 hrs. to view the body of Ho Chi Minh.   Although he is loved and revered by the VN people, I was not that enamoured.   I probably would have passed on this venue, but did not wish to insult our VN guide.   HCM was a part of history, but it was a long wait for a 2 minute viewing.  I suggested returning later, as the kids were getting agitated, but our guide said it was always this busy!   The grounds are lovely and there are a lot of impressive structures to view.   Saw a group of Chinese children who were anxious to "high five" us and were just delightful !
Its a long process to go through, but ultimately the revered Ho Chi Minh and how they have honoured him is worth seeing.  No photos allowed.
I visited here with my friend and that was worth visiting but nothing interesting as well actually. But worth to see anyway
In my opinion, a landmark in order to do V. It doesn't require a reality to get there, there is a much more beautiful and impressive Buddhist temple behind it.
We went on Monday and found that the gate is from behind and you need to walk for 5 mins. Once we went inside we saw museum was closed and there was a temple to see and take a picture. Nothingmore

We went as part of an organised tour but still had to experience a long queue and a 50 minute wait to get a glimpse of Uncle Ho. The mausoleum is impressive directly opposite the “parliament “. It is fronted by the usual large communist square for grand parades. Worth ticking off your bucket list if you have the interest. Visiting Ho,s house etc is very worthwhile.

Went here on a whim because we had some extra time in the city before our flight and didn't really know what to expect. We got in line and waited about 2 hours to get to the mausoleum with lots of pushing and shoving going on. The actual mausoleum building was cool and the experience inside was interesting but lasted about 30 seconds. It could have been visiting on a Sunday was a busy day but we did get there 10 minutes before it opened at 8 so we were just surprised by how long the whole thing took. It was free though so can't complain too much.

Feel free to line up for hours to see a mummified Ho Chi Minh lying in state. Afterward visit his last home on the same property. The house is what looks like a Mid-Century Modern design. I can see why he preferred this house to the larger official home (not currently open to visitors). Sorry but Trip Advisor appears to be allowing only live shots, so no photos.

We only got to sss the Mausoleum from the outside as the line to visit inside was over 5km long. If you want to see the inside, I suggest arriving well before the gates open to secure a place in the line. You may also need to give a day to this activity based on the length and slow pace the line moved.

Clothing covering the knees and shoulders is required. Luckily, a woman was selling clothes on the street in front. The mausoleum itself closes at 11:00 AM, but it's possible to walk around outside without paying. All electronic devices must be left in the cloakroom before entering the mausoleum.

You shouldn't do it for the mausoleum, but it is still quite an experience to stand in line with hundreds of Vietnamese people. Dressed up with the whole family, they make the outing. Fascinating to see.

This was included as part of our tour, not really something I wanted to do but we had to as it was part of the tour route. Luckily we didn't really have much of a queue, then we shuffled through passing in a gloomily lit room what absolutely looked like a wax model.A bizarre experience!The military control where you can walk in the complex, so pay attention to signage etc, as its very easy to just wander off the designated area and get shouted at!

We wanted to visit this memorial inside, however we could not go inside the building as it was closed for visitors when we went around non time. We were not having prior information that it is open only for an hour or so in the morning, hence went around the place, without seeing the memorial, otherwise outside the building is maintained very well.
Guide books say must see.  I disagree.  Long lines. A lot of official guards.  Then when you enter there is a  casket with a Ho Chi Minh wax replica.

We traveled from Sapa to Hanoi to visit Ho Chi Minh's mausoleum. Unfortunately, we only managed to take photos outside, which was a shame because the area was being prepared for the North Korean and US presidents' summit. We were unable to go inside and could only take pictures from the outside.

It's often very crowded, so arrive early as the site no longer accepts visitors after 11:00 AM. The visit to the mausoleum itself is quite unusual. Soldiers are everywhere, pressing you and keeping a close eye on you (no filming or photography allowed). The rest of the visit, the park and buildings, is more pleasant. Worth doing, but not essential.

One of Hanoi's main landmarks, the interior of this monumental mausoleum cannot be accessed, but nevertheless fairly impressive from the outside. However, I probably would have got more out of this visit if I read about the leader Hi Chi Minh.

The area is free to get in. All packages, purses are scanned and you go through a body scanner. Many guards are around and no high heels or gum chewing is permitted. The mausoleum is very large and there are guards there. It is an impressive site. Many people that were there bowed to the site and were crying. It was touching to see how much Ho Chi Minh was revered.

On the day we wanted to visit the mausoleum, it was closed. We did see the changing of the guard, though. Watch out: don't cross the yellow line, or you'll be whistled back by a stern soldier. I was also not allowed to take a photo while crouching. Why not? No idea. All in all, a nice experience. Incidentally, the area around the Mausoleum was spotless. This cleaning should happen throughout Vietnam.

We went in line to visit it. We noticed that when we looked for the beginning of the line that people didn't move. So when we went in line we were suspicious. We decided to wait 5 minutes and if the line would not move at all, we would leave. So we did leave after 5 or 10 minutes. When we walked around the block, we could see some limo's and police cars. Appearantly there was an official visit going on. Then they make all the ordinary visitors wait without telling them.

If you're going here, be there when they open. The queue we stood in was not fun. The whole visit took three hours, but if you had been there first it would have only taken about 1.5 hours. No cameras or liquids are allowed inside. The camera is deposited and returned after you have gone through the mausoleum. Remember to wear clothes that cover your shoulders and knees, which applies to both sexes.

Perhaps because it was before 8:30 on a weekday, or perhaps because there was a tour group, I couldn't get in, so I walked around the fence. Since it was my second time, I wasn't too concerned about getting in. Also, is photography prohibited? Is it closed on Mondays?

The history surrounding this area is immense. The Mausoleum is closed on a Monday so you can’t actually see the tomb.The area is quieter on a Monday because of this. The massive square and grass areas to the left are over the huge nuclear bomb shelter beneath. The gardens are quite interesting, and the humble house he ( Ho Chi Minh) lived in is inspiring to say the least.
It's a large open space, open to the public as a place of relaxation for the townspeople. There's a single building where people from the past are buried, but otherwise it looks like a big park.
It is said to be the starting point of Vietnam's independence movement.

Important to the nation of Vietnam this is a sombre place where you need to be aware and respectful. There are guards watching to make sure you don't walk in protected areas so look carefully where you walk. When we got there the mausoleum was closed so we could only see the building from the outside. Its an impressive building and very significant to the people of Vietnam as resting place of their leader.

It's one of those things you really need to do...... you queue a long time to see the body.... but good to see the respect Vietnamese have.... but really it's the glimpses of his simple life that are most impactful especially compared to today's useless leaders!!!!

It felt that this was a must do visit but i didn't like officious staff (did not know how to treat binoculars in the end we had to store them separately from cameras so did not have them for the birds in the gardens. The soldiers were always blowing their whistle to keep people in line as we moved in single file to see Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body. Once we had passed through the mausoleum the rest of the complex was more relaxed

This seemed like a must visit locale in Hanoi. The building itself is beautiful as is the landscaping and surroundings which one can wander after visiting the mausoleum. There are guards everywhere asking visitors to remain silent approaching and through the main building. Honestly, there just isn’t much to see. I’m not really sure, though they say it is, that the body is the real deal. The lovely gardens outside also have some buildings Ho Chi Minh lived and worked in. It’s a quick visit in a crowded area. Go early to avoid the crowd.

The Mausoleum of Ho Chi Minh is frequently visited by Vietnamese who hold Uncle Ho in high regard. He ensured the reunification of Vietnam, or at least of the people (!) who went to war and lost thousands of lives during the Vietnam War. It is a stately building adjacent to a large square. Backpacks and bags are checked upon entering the square. The mausoleum is closed on Mondays and Fridays. Access is only permitted in the morning until 11:00 AM. A remarkable building.

I suppose visiting this place is of great historical significance to the Vietnamese. And perhaps in world history. But I don’t find queuing in line and being hurried through the line to be all that exciting. I would have been just as happy to see the outside of the mausoleum and and tour the other parts of the complex.
It is a bit odd to go look at an embalmed body but he was the father of Vietnams revolution and as such is honored and revered.
You stand so far away it becomes like looking at pictures. Spare the long security check and look at pictures everyone else took.

If you are a history buff this is a good place. Personally, I wouldn’t have minded skipping it. But it was a nice add on to the day. The brochure they give you basically summarizes everything to a point you don’t need to see everything and get stuck in the crowds.

My husband thought that the 45 minute wait in line was worth it,I am not convinced.though I enjoyed chatting with the people in the line in very limited language capabilities as many people in the line were Asian. The Mausoleum itself is guarded by soldiers in uniform and when it is finally your turn, you spend 3 minutes touring Ho Chi Minh's open casket where he lies for all to see .His body is preserved with chemicals and of course,he is revered by many. It is said that his body is returned to Russia  every year to be maintained
Of course you should visit this when you are in Vietnam, but it is not a great experience as such.
A visit to "Uncle Ho's" is definitely a must-see, but it does involve some preparation.

The trip to the mausoleum of the father of Vietnam was mixed. We visited the area with a good guide, which raises the review to 3. Uncomfortable with so much military, and you could really feel the communism.

Beautiful grounds, a bit of a walk to find the entrance but was worth a visit. Different experience seeing the armed guards surrounding the embalmed body. Read somewhere that he had wanted to be buried in his hometown but they forgo his wishes and put his body on display which is a bit sad. However the reverence we saw some local people had was amazing. He is still a huge figure to the Vietnamese people. It’s very quick once you get in and it’s only a matter of minutes walking around the case where the body is displayed and out again.

From the moment we entered, it was solemn~~ Shhh!!! Be quiet~~~~ The procedures even included bag checks like at an airport. Do not take the water the guide gave you. It is not allowed through. It was confiscated and destroyed ㅜㅜ My camera was also locked in a locker ㅜㅜ The square was wide and the sun was quite hot, so it was tiring, but seeing him lying in the coffin made my heart ache.
Long lines to enter the mausoleum , no pictures allowed, observe dressing codeThe gardens and residence are nice

This is an okay place to visit with a lot of rules and guards to enforce them. There is no entrance fee to see Ho, but the museum costs 40,000 dong. And yes, it is just a stuffed body lying in a clear refrigerator. Kinda morbid. The grounds are nice and very clean. Bonus: no motorbikes trying to kill you.

Another mausoleum, good for photo-taking if nearby. Not exactly worth making your way here. Unless you are nearby visiting other attractions, like Presidential Palace next to it, or Thanh Long Imperial Citadel about 2 blocks away.
You got the feeling that the socialist party has a lot of influence. It was very tightly organized. It lay there neatly. As if it had only just been put there.
OK place to visit, huge place, lots of guards and rules, took couple of photos and left.Not a must do as far as I'm concerned.

Expected great things from the mausoleum and looking at the scale of the building and the surrounding plazas, one could be forgiven for imagining a much more riveting experience.Having been herded like sheep and literally pulled into two separate lines by the stoney faced uniformed guards, we eventually got to file past the tomb.It was a truly underwhelming experience and I would not waste my time or money to repeat it.

An interesting visit due to the grandeur with which the site and its surroundings are maintained. The visit includes a quick glimpse of the embalmed body of the "leader," who is still largely respected in the country. You need to be very patient with the guards, who are quite thorough when checking backpacks, bags, etc., and once inside, keep quiet and don't take photos if you don't want to get into trouble.
I think it's best to go as early as possible in the morning. If you go a little later, there will likely be a long queue. Since there is a time limit for entry, you should go early.

The mausoleum is extremely popular with tourists and locals wishing to see the body of Ho Chi Minh. Long queues to get onto the site and then a quick walk past the body .Interesting  to see the area around the mausoleum but for us , not the viewing itself ,
Once inside, be aware that there are areas where photography is prohibited.

This place was not what I expected. You wait in long lines and get shoved by the guards if you are not keeping up the pace. Once you enter the building you progress to see a lifelike version of Ho Chi Minh with four guards surrounding the coffin. The grounds are nice around the building.

Strictly guarded by silent stern faced military, slowly file in to view the glass covered body of Ho Chi Minh and slowly file out. No pictures, no talking, no stopping, no hats, no sunglasses etc. A pass of the body only takes about 1 minute, no stopping allowed you have to keep walking so you can't really see very much, with approx 30 minutes slow walk/queuing time. Open Tuesday to Thursday from 7.30am to 10.30am.We walked here from our hotel and we had great difficulty locating the entrance, as not really indicated on a map and was very far from the front entrance to the complex where we thought it would be! So we ended up walking nearly completely around the outskirts of the site, for around 15 minutes, until we were eventually able to access from the other side, somewhere past the pagoda I think it was.We knew that no photos were allowed inside the mausoleum, but as we queued for entrance we were surprised that no photos were allowed of the outside of the mausoleum either. In fact, large cameras are taken off visitors and bagged at a security desk beforehand. After you have been inside photos of the building are allowed however, from the gardens.

This is apparently mandatory. Joining a long line, handing over your camera, no taking photos, no talking inside; all around, you are also pointed out where you are and aren't allowed to stand. Clearly a communist trait. It didn't make much of an impression on us.

You might visit the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleaum on your own, but we used a private guide.  If you are in Hanoi it is part of the sights to visit. But it is everyone's prerogative to make up his/her mind about it. There are many things you have to observe. Security is very strict. Visitors are herded through in files of two. At the entrance you have to pass a metal detector. Don't bring candies or chewing gums. They will be removed. Walk only in line and avoid stepping out of the line. No picture taking is allowed while waiting in line for entering. No hat, no sunglasses, no hands in pockets.The pass of the gasket takes about 2 minutes.After you exit the building you can take pictures.What is impressive is the size, the area and the building itself.

Interesting to see the body of Ho Chi Minh. But it is the most crowded tourist spot I have been in Vietnam. So you should calculate almost 1 hour of waiting time abs then you can see Ho Chi Minh for about 2 minutes. No cameras allowed.

A visit to the mausoleum of ho chi Minh  was part of the day tour we took in Hanoi. It seems all tours start at the same place because it was crowded with tourists when we arrived in the morning. By the time we were leaving after about 2 hours there were very few people left. It is a fairly long walk in a queue round the area before visiting the actual Mausoleum. So those with disabilities and who do not prefer to walk much beware. Once you get in the queue there is no way of giving up the walk. You are not allowed to go back or take a short cut. The body of Ho chi Minh lies embalmed in a dimly lit room. There are soviners shops and cafes in the compound. Not allowed to take photographs.

The Mausoleum of Ho Chi Min is Vietnamese people postmortem monument to the man who gave his entire life to his country and people. Interesting to know that Ho Chi Min - name given by his people - left a note saying that he did not want to be honored with a mausoleum and he wanted his rests become ashes. However, his people built a mausoleum for future generations to honor his memory. The visit consists on a walk in a wide avenue that precedes the monument and from where visitors can admire the gardens that Ho Chi Min used to visit to reflect upon his country.You can get there easily by bus.

I visited this place during a day-long walk around Hanoi. I went in the afternoon, so I couldn't get inside. Later, I looked at a guidebook and learned that you have to go early in the morning, as there are huge lines. I regretted not reading the guidebook beforehand.

We arrived at about 09H40 and joined a long queue, which was very fast moving. Security is very strict and you need to dress and behave appropriately. Cameras are strictly forbidden. You will have to leave it at checked luggage and collect later. All of these procedures are well organised and coordinated. After viewing his body, you can spend more time visiting the grounds of the palace and the places where he worked as well as the one pillar pagoda.That is worth a short visit and you can take as many photos as you wish. The mausoleum closes at 11H30.

One of the most well known structures of Hanoi.     Be careful entry signs are not clear and you can be going around the place figuring how to get in.  Ask the security folks they will direct you instead of following the signs.
Interesting series of Ho Chi Min residencies, but not much more to it. Visit it if you want to cool down after a furious touristic expedition.

I wasnt greatly thrilled. I understand Ho Chi Minh was a great figure, but didnt know that the mausoleum had just his embalmed body. The line was too long and the time inside the cold mausoleum was too short. Also, had they given us some reading material, or set-up a voice over system explaining about Ho Chi Minh's works and his greatness, the tourists will be able to appreciate the visit better. Although I completely respect the reason to maintain silence through the mausoleum.

Of course, we also visited this activity during our 3-day stay in Hanoi. We just walked through the park and stood in front of the entrance to the mausoleum, which was closed. Even if it's nothing, everyone who visits Hanoi should stop here. Overall, Ho Chi Minh didn't want the mausoleum and wanted his ashes to be scattered all over the country.

It's hard for others to understand how revered Ho Chi Minh was and is in Vietnam.  This is an opportunity to get a sense of that.  The landscaping is lovely and worth the visit if you appreciate a beautiful garden.
We were forced to march in two lines. No steps to the right or left. Speaking was prohibited. The experience was below average. Perhaps perhaps a little more insight into the Vietnamese mentality...
The mausoleum was fine to visit. It has a huge place in front of it that some people go run on. Otherwise, the mausoleum is free to go around. Not sure if you can go in though.

We stumbled upon this big space on our walk.  Ypu need to go passed some  security but then your free to walk the long open tarmac.  tale pictures etc. Nothing big there unless there's an event but the scenery is nice. You get a feel for the local admiration for the pres.

Like the Presidential Palace, a visit to the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is part of the package that visitors to Hanoi should include, according to tour operators. The visit is also very quick due to the number of visitors. The queue is extremely long but moves rapidly. Photography is strictly prohibited inside the Mausoleum, and the area is guarded by the military. The museum, located a short walk from the Mausoleum, is interesting and traces Ho Chi Minh's life from his birth to his rise to become the most important figure in Vietnam.

After a very long procession, two at a time, and constant scrutiny from uniformed officers (hands out of pockets, arms at sides, no sunglasses!), we reached the mausoleum. It's a freezing cold, dimly lit room, and Ho Chi Minh's body is guarded by four soldiers. You can only glimpse him as you walk in silence. It was interesting to see the Vietnamese's devotion to their leader; as a Westerner, I sensed a certain sense of oppression. The museum dedicated to Ho Chi Minh is nothing special, and the area around the mausoleum is poorly maintained and a bit scattered. It's an interesting experience, though; I recommend it to anyone with time!
There's nothing to see that can't be seen from afar.

Our visit to the mausoleum did not add anything to our overall trip to Hanoi. I wouldn't advise going out of your way to visit.It's quite difficult to find the entrance. Most of the perimeter is guarded by very stern guards who will point you in a rough direction but shout if you get too close to them, even though the point they're guarding can easily be mistaken for an entrance. Once you pass through a seemingly pointless security point and finally reach the mausoleum, you can't get too close. The structure itself is ok but nothing special.
This tour gave a great close up insight to to the living and working quarters of the famous former president. An important part of Vietnam’s history and well presented.
Long queues to pass briefly at the tomb of HCM; the complex as a whole is more worthwhile than the mausoleum itself.
Suitable for a short walk, high police presence, closed place. Well-maintained.
It seems that the museum displays the achievements of Ho Chi Minh and the history of Vietnam, but to be honest, there was little English signage, so I didn't understand much.

Went this morning,it was New Year’s Day. Everything said to get there by 8. We did and the line was blocks long. The grounds are very beautiful and the architecture is just stunning around the whole area

You will be marched in lines of 2, you will not talk, you will not put hands in pockets, you will not eat or drink, you will remove your hat, you will dress appropriately.If you want to see HCM lying in state, that is what you have to do. You will obey.Apart from all that, a quick in and out, nod to the man and done. Nice gardens although I think I saw a leaf out of place, but didn't want to say anything.

Definitely got to see the well-preserved body of the late HCM.Arrived at about 9am. The line was quite long but does move very quickly. There is a security check and you are not permitted to bring water/drinks inside so bear that in mind.You get moved through in groups and there are staff to keep everyone in line. You get to walk a little loop around the body of HCM so you get to see him for about 10-15 seconds and then you're out and free to wander around.Not exactly something I'd recommend for everyone but it is free so go if it interests you.

I visited on a Monday and was unable to enter. Google said that entry was possible on Mondays, but when I got there, it turned out that entry was not allowed. However, other facilities, excluding the grave, are accessible on Monday mornings.

It’s difficult to miss this whole complex since most journeys pass it day and night (we did). Carefully stage managed and having the air of Red Square in Moscow feels a little alien after having been to other parts of Vietnam but it’s kinda one of those must do’s in Hanoi. The mausoleum where HCM is still kept is open mornings but queues are lengthy (free to enter) - slightly more interesting is the Presidential House and HCM’s working house and lake behind in the sprawling park. All of this gets extremely busy! Don’t step out of line otherwise you will be whistled at by guards.

Being the first embalmed person I visited, I wasnt sure what to expect. The whole thing is in an elaborate military zone with security all around.  We saw several guards marching in the grounds. We needed to scan and deposit our backpacks.  Then we were are to be silent in lines of 2 and walked about 300 mts before we passed in and out of the room with the casket. No stopping time. Total silence.  And army officers keeping a strict eye on everyone. Casket itself is viewed from a 270degree gallery as you move along with about 10-20 feet distance but you can see everything very well. The Google maps location shows the actual mausoleum, not the entrance so we had to walk a fair bit to the entrance. There are a lot of souvenir shops around oon the way out where one can also buy  some coffee or juices. Suggest to combine with a visit to the Citadel and Temple of Literature close by

It was nice to see the place, but it was soooo much people.. we arrived around 9 am.. and there we stood in line for over 1,5 hours.. It was not worth the wait...  So something else instead if the line is bad-

Did not see the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh as we were told that the queues were over an hour. Instead enjoyed the large impressive outside space. You must be dressed respectively ..no shorts or uncovered shoulders permitted.
I visited the Palace, Mausoleum & Museum. It was all good & professional but unfortunately just too much like propaganda for the current regime

I wanted to meet Ho Chi Minh with my baby, but our 8-month-old was denied entry. Since the baby couldn't understand the instructions, I couldn't tell the reason, but it seems babies who can't walk yet aren't allowed. For now, the baby stayed behind with his dad while I went in with just my older child. The line moved faster than expected, and although the moment we met Ho Chi Minh was instantaneous, it felt fascinating and amazing.
For those who still remember this name in the 1960s, this is a must-read. But it's also interesting for understanding the veneration this man enjoys today.

Place where the great ho chi minn is buried , open every day but only in the morning on some days so best to check first . Queues can be up to 4km long so try to pick an early time to visit if possible

Interesting visit and good to see but you can’t actually enter the building. Surrounding area very relaxing. Museum on site shut during our visit. Gift shops and toilets on site. Worth a visit if you have the time.
Open limited hours during the day (only mornings in December). Closed Mondays and Fridays.
A symbolic place not to be missed when visiting Hanoi, a must-see at least once in a lifetime.

The queue was extremely long to see inside the mausoleum so we decided to abandon the idea. The opening hours are limited so even if we had queued there was no guarantee that we would have been admitted.We walked around the outside and it was good and we got some photos of the mausoleum and gardens.

Yes, the queue moves fast, but that's still a pretty long queue and lots of time spent standing if you go mid-morning like I did. Seeing the military processions and the reverence Ho Chi Minh is treated with was interesting though. I only spent a short time here as part of a day tour, so there are parts of the museum I didn't get to see.

I wanted to go inside the cemetery, but I arrived after closing time. Please check the hours carefully before you go. Also, when it's extremely hot, there is no shade or place to rest, so it can be a bit difficult.

The visit was nice. We got to see a bit of the place. But once again, despite the price, there was very little information or explanation... in French and/or English. A real pilgrimage site for Ho Chi Minh fans.
The most impressive thing about this place is how much effort goes into keeping order here. The actual time you get to spend in the room with Ho Chi Minh is very short.
We were only there briefly and looked at everything from the outside. There are nicer things to see in the city, but it was on our itinerary.
Has overtones if Russian or Chinese monuments but interesting none the less, more so to see where hi chi min lived and the gardens around
There was so many Chinese who pushed and pulled so could see very much . Was a bit disappointed . Probably went at the wrong time
It seems you can tour the inside in the morning, but we weren't able to. We only saw the exterior. The plaza in front looks like it's meant for parades. It's very spacious.
It closes at 11:30 AM, and there is only one entrance, so you cannot enter just anywhere; soldiers will stop you. It seems like a good place for a walk.
This "must-see" attraction is a typical product of its system and its time. Honor to those to whom honor is due...

A place with a quick stopover.  Once done we walked over to the HCM museum and to see the One Pillar Pagoda only to be blocked by the security who directed us with hand gesture to go around the block to go thru security check behind the museum which was unexpected so we had to walk a long way unnecessarily.  Signs for directions were not so good making it more difficult.

We didn't go inside the Masoleum, because we hadn't planned ahead before reaching the area and didn't know this was an option. It wasn't obvious from were we walked how you could go inside or what it would cost.We happened to be there at the front of the building as 1pm rolled around, so saw the highly formal changing of the guard, which was an interesting 10 minutes or so.
The queue to enter went through the site and along the street ... didn't fancy waiting for hours so we walked around the site instead, which was still interesting.

The mausoleum is very impressive - a huge edifice dedicated to the leadership of Ho Chi Minh. Very moving experience - try to catch the changing of the guard. The government buildings across the square are equally imposing clearly meant for military type ceremonies in the square. Worth a visit

Watched the flag being taken down. Very impressive to watch the precision by the Vietnamese military taking their flag down in front of the tomb. This is down each day at 9 pm. They raise the flag each day at 6 am. Done rain or shine.

The grounds around the mausoleum are well maintained and it was a great experience to to see his embalmed body, but most of the open space and time between the mausoleum, the stilt house, the official residence is used for lines.
