You line up and walk single file through the mausoleum and it’s the same though the rest of the complex, especially bad if you get stuck behind a large tour groups. Try to come first thing in the morning or later in the afternoon to avoid the groups.
This grand place is along Hung Vuong Street, Ba Dinh district. The building is on Ba Dinh Square where it is Chi final resting place of Ho Chi Minh.There is also of changing of guard daily.

Want to immerse yourself in the true spirit of the Russian/Chinese regime? Come here and you'll find everything you desire! A monumental mausoleum, a huge square, changing of the guards, and guards on every corner...and to think, Ho Chi Minh would have wanted anything but this! It's a must-see to understand the atmosphere.

A strict and sombre visiting experience with a lengthy walk through the complex before you see the remains of Vietnam’s national hero. Fortunately it wasn’t crowded when we visited, (around 9.00) apparently you should avoid it first thing and it closes at 11.00 am

Could not enter the mausoleum when we visited; we could only walk around the exterior and pose for photos with the mausoleum in the background. The area is also comprised of the presidential palace and homes Uncle Ho lived in. We could not go into any of the buildings.

Of course, this is a must-see tourist spot in Hanoi. We arrived quite early and were quickly ushered through security at the entrance. Then we had to leave our backpacks. It was taken from me so fast I didn't even have time to blink. After that, we went through the mausoleum. If you want to take the short route to the exit afterward, you have to pay an entrance fee for the adjacent park. Since my backpack, including money, was unfortunately under guard somewhere, this wasn't an option for us. We had to take a long detour around the outside. So definitely bring money! Opening hours are only from 8:00 to 11:00. And you can only pick up your checked bags from 7:30 to 12:00 and then again from 14:00 to 16:00.
But unfortunately, most of the time, you can't get inside.

Around 8 PM, I saw from the car on the way from the airport to the hotel that locals were lined up in a straight line in front of the building, illuminated by indirect lighting. It seemed that you could only get within a certain distance, and officials in white uniforms (military personnel?) were keeping civilians at bay. Apparently, you can only enter the building in the morning.

A very serious and revered place, not a must see, but it seems to be THE thing to do!There is no entrance fee, we were told by a local that is to ensure there are always people queuing up and making it look good!There is a barrier type walkway you must adhere to - it goes for several hundred meters around the park and the mausoleum building itself before you arrive at the entrance. No photos are allowed when viewing the body. We noticed it was very quiet and respectful while on this walk and in the building, but the noise and babble of many people broke out after exiting the building. We viewed glass fronted rooms of Ho Chi Mins house, looked at the majestic building which was built for him to live in and also the smaller house he preferred.There is a lovely flower market in the street outside

To understand a country's history, I suppose one must delve into the past of its most prestigious current leader. We arrived at the entrance... on a Sunday! A queue almost made us give up, but the guide encouraged us to persevere, so we waited... an hour's wait with all those rules... no phones, no cameras, no crossed arms, no talking in line—in short, an exaggerated strictness that even the locals don't respect. Once inside the mausoleum, however, Uncle Ho's body exudes a natural solemnity, and one understands how this politician managed to inspire a people and defeat the most powerful army in the world (in the 1970s).

Nice place to visit - well laid out wide avenues with the parliament building, ministry of defence and then main attraction - the mausoleum are very good. I believe parade happens here on special occasions like independence day and other special national events day. That must be a grand spectacle!

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is an important and impressive place to visit in Hanoi. The atmosphere is formal and respectful, with guards, queues and a solemn tone. Seeing Ho Chi Minh’s preserved body is unusual and memorable, and the site has …

Interesting area.  Impressive site surrounded by lots of security.  There are a lot of marked off limits areas vigorously enforced by military dressed in all manner of uniforms.  The area is clean and a big tourist attraction.  Picture taking is allowed.  Bathroms are available.
Visited on a Monday morning when it was closed to public so the grounds are relatively peaceful. Can't say how crowded it can get with throngs of tourists.

Explored the area around Ho's tomb---never went in. There's a daily time for viewing what remains of his preserved body which our guide stated there can be long lines for. Plan accordingly. The surrounding area to his tomb is walkable. …
A must-see in Hanoi, but be prepared for strict procedures.  Ho Chi Minh’s Mausoleum is an important national site and definitely worth …

Visited this complex which was an interesting experience. Need to be aware of the dress code (no shorts) and no bottles of water. Many school kids and long queues but moved quickly. Limited viewing time. Can also view the stilt house where Ho Chi Minh lived on and off from 1958 to 1969.
A seriously big mausoleum to the founder of modern Vietnam. Be aware that women MUST wear clothing below the knee to be allowed access. Not so for men....🤔🙄 …
The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is undeniably a significant landmark in Hanoi, offering a glimpse into Vietnam's history and the reverence surrounding its former leader. …
Also the place where you can visit the One Pillar Pagoda nearby.  The entry fee is 45K VND per person. There is a security check at the entrance, …

Really interesting place to visit with many different aspects to the historical site. There is a strict dress code for men and women alike - shoulders and knees must be covered. It's a shame that you can't enter the mausoleum to see Ho Chi Minh like in Russia where you can see Lenin.

Went on Tuesday just before 8am as advised by hotel receptionist, the queuing ling is probably 500-800meters long, full of kids and veterans. We also went to the HochiMinh Museum entrance (instead of Mausoleum), so we were walking anticlock …

It is one of the most visited tourist spots in Hanoi. It is home to Vietnam's first founder shrine, pagoda, leader house, souvenir shops, and museum. Local entry is free but foreigners need to pay. Check the timing for the shrine visit and …
Famous historic places.  No need to buy tickets.

Its a grand parade square to honour Vietnamese greatest Hero and Modern Vietnam founder Mr Ho Chi Minh. Its heavily watched area. Guards will make sure nobody will go close towards the grass or loitering near the main gate. Its a beautiful place for some pictures.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is one of Hanoi’s most important and revered landmarks, where visitors can pay their respects to the embalmed body of Vietnam’s founding leader, Ho Chi Minh. It’s a place of deep historical and cultural …
Place is beautiful and they dont allow e cig and lighter

good event to check-off the list. i wanted to see the body of the comrade. the lines are usually long, but they go fast, thanks to the military guard that give you an experience of communist discipline. advisable to go early; we went around …

Do arrive as early as you can as it gets very crowded with students and locals. You will need to go through screening like in the airport security. You will have to surrender water bottles or any water containers. You will have to surrender …

Guess people come here because it's said to be a must visit place while in Hanoi. Without any guidance or audio guide, I didn't think I could get enough information about the place. Add a visit to the museum if you come here (which unfortunately, not all the information was translated to English).

When you take in the magnitude of what this 1 man means to these people, this is a very special place. Go between 07:00 and 10:30 as otherwise you cannot enter. Free but very strict, organised, no photos, you follow a path, there is no …
Museum if Vietnamese history and their first President Ho Chi Minh. Contains mortal remains of his body but we couldn't see it when we visited earlier this month. …
We were not able to see Ho Chi Minh’s body since it was under maintenance, but the rest of the place is nice to walk around. There was no big wait to enter, probably because of maintenance. …

Didn’t read the reviews before coming here and ended up being denied entry due to my shorts being too short (your knees have to be covered). Long pants available for sale around the corner, but the seller will immediately try to rip you off …
It was closed when we visited as they have maintenance checks inside. It was really hot and humid when we went here. …

Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum is undoubtedly a must-visit destination in Hanoi. Situated in a grand parade square, the mausoleum offers a solemn tribute to the revered leader of Vietnam. One of the highlights of the experience is witnessing the …

we walked down from Tran Quoc Pagoda to visit this area, beautiful day cloudy, very scenic. It was closed due to some high ranking official visit, so saw the presidential palace and other landmarks from outside, took pics and moved on to old quarter. too bad we could not visit inside.
A must see when visiting Ha Noi. The stately mausoleum is in the center of the beautiful, well maintained and landscaped grounds. Please be respectful, quiet and observe the dress code.

This is an important tip. Don't pay entrance fee if your goal is just to take pictures. Just go to the the side part of the place. The view of the Mausoleum if great on that area. Perfecf for picture taking for free.

A place where uncle Ho sleep forever. He was a  great man inside the heart of every Vietnamese. Beware of visiting time, it start from 8am to 11am. Your have to queue as early as possible to enter the mausoleum. We have been waiting almost 2.5hours.
Very much worth going with a guide who can walk you around and explain everything and the history ( one sided as it may be ). …
Don’t bring lighters or water, they will be confiscated and you can’t get it back. …
A big museum dedicated to Ho Chi Minh. Less crowded place.
The Mausoleum is an impressive display with beautifully kept gardens, but the museum has poor opening times and there lacks an overall reading or Audio experience to learn about him. …
Looks very dramatic from the exterior; didn't do the formal tour. Opted for the stilt house instead.
It is worth to visit, you can walk around and it's pretty nice. Bring long pants as necessary to walk inside the area.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum in Hanoi offers a fascinating and respectful glimpse into Vietnam’s history. The site is well-maintained, and the atmosphere is reverent, with visitors paying their respects to the late leader. The historical …

You can see the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh, who died in 1969, and the changing of the guard ceremony is held every hour.

Unable to enter the actual Mausoleum when we visited but still worth it and see the regard that the Vietnamese still hold for Ho. It is straight out of the communist copybook for gargantuan monuments to dead leaders and therefore good exercise to get around if nothing else, but I can't help but think that Ho himself would have been perfectly happy with something a lot more modest.
It was quick to get in, a bit messy once you were inside. Many guards guarding the tomb. Nice to have been here
The founder father of Vietnam, a memorial tomb in memory of Ho Chi Minh, is located in a massive park.Inside, Ho Chi Minh is visited mummified.

There is no doubt that Ho Chi Minh’s Mausoleum is a major national monument in Vietnam. That said, I would like to highlight a few practical points about the procedure and share some helpful warnings.If you are dropped off at the main square in front of the building, be aware that the entrance is actually located at the back. We had to walk all the way around the mausoleum, which took about ten minutes.Security is strict. You are not allowed to bring water inside and will be required to dispose of it at the entrance. Visitors are also expected to dress respectfully, with shoulders and knees covered. Interestingly, some men wearing shorts were permitted to enter, while women with uncovered shoulders were not.Cameras must be placed in provided bags and left at a counter before entering. They are returned after you exit the main chamber, though there may be a short wait.Inside, you are given only a brief moment — around 15 to 20 seconds — to view Ho Chi Minh’s preserved body before being guided onward.Overall, this is an essential visit when in Hanoi, as it represents a central pillar of modern Vietnamese history. However, I found certain aspects of the procedure somewhat lacking in clarity and consistency.

This is a must see whilst you are in the city. Its not just about the Mausoleum but all the other historical sites around it. Be dressed correctly for the mausoleum and prepared to stand in a straight line to show your respect.
Serious, long security lines.  Impressive monument, but one cannot always enter.  Sad that Ho Chi Minh’s wishes to be cremated were ignored.  He didn’t want such an imposing edifice.

In the wake of the visit to the temple of literature we went to the park where is the imposing mausoleum of Ho Chi Minh. The gardens are perfectly maintained by a myriad of women and one can see pretty buildings behind the esplanade facing the mausoleum , If you have some time , the two guards who are stationary under the entrance structure will be replaced , interesting sight to watch .

The entrance with security check and backpack drop-off is located in the SW corner of the widely cordoned off area. Those who step out of the grave incorrectly are allowed to circle the outside of the complex. We were there on Saturday and there were many school classes and other groups of locals on site.After passing the security checkpoint (as at the airport :)) we went to the Mausoleum in a clearly regulated manner. Due to the organizationally short stay in the Mausoleum, the visit was completed after 45 minutes. Admission was free for everyone. Admission to the presidential palace grounds is 40,000 dong for foreigners. We weren't there, though.Overall, an interesting experience.

We managed to get there just before 12 noon. Purelly by fluke, at that time there is a very theatrical changing of the guard. Goose stepping and so on. It was really fascinating. The mausoleum itself was distinctly Soviet in appearance - bland and sombre, but it was all well worth seeing.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum provides a sense of the Vietnamese admiration for this former leader, who is much admired by many Vietnamese.  It also provides an enhanced understanding of the culture and country's history.
Entire property beautiful to see. But the security on the ground is not exactly friendly. Jr bag also goes through the xray again.  Road bandage stuff. Bit of a charade.
The site is impressive and the museum very interesting. The mausoleum was closed, but probably would not have qued anyway. Ho Chi Minhs stilt house worth a visit.

Must see in Hanoi, because Ho Chi Minh has been so important for the origin and independence of Vietnam. Impressive that he's laid out there and looks like it. Beautiful Presential Palace and nice to see where he really lived and worked in the large adjoining garden.

Monument of historical significance especially for the Vietnamese people, I would say not to be missed for a visit to Hanoi. An impressive marble building, it houses the remains of Ho Chi Minh City that can be visited in silence and in continuous procession. You can't take pictures. It is also worth admiring the imposing spaces around the mausoleum

Arrived at around 8am on a Tuesday, very busy. We were ushered into holding rooms which is unsure if that usually happens. After around an hours wait it was explained that there was a diplomat visiting and we couldn’t enter, thankfully that didn’t take too long and we were allowed through. Very happy we waited for the experience. After going inside there is an entry to pay around £1 to walk around the garden and visit his house see artefacts, this was great. Also thoroughly enjoyed the art we bought at the end as shown in photos.

It is not permitted to enter the esplanade with lighters (they must be picked up at the entrance without returning them) and you cannot enter with bare shoulders. No straps or you have to cover them with a scarf.If you forget them at the entrance, there are vendors who sell them.
We couldn't get in because we were in shorts. So let me say for the ladies consider taking a paraao so you can enter this place.

We did a guided tour of the Ho Chi Minh Memorial and really enjoyed it. The guide gave a clear and interesting explanation of Ho Chi Minh’s life, beliefs, and his huge role in Vietnam’s independence. He’s still deeply respected by the people, seen as a symbol of unity and national pride.We also got to see his old stilt house, which was simple but full of meaning—it really reflected his modest lifestyle. The garden around it is beautiful, with a peaceful lake full of fish and shady trees. There were also two of his old Russian cars on display, which was a cool touch of history.Overall, a great learning experience. I recommend to book a guided tour, or rent a “guide headset” to get most out of your visit.There is a security check at the entrance, they mostly look for lighters and stuff like that.We were allowed to bring our own water bottles.Definitely worth visiting if you’re in Hanoi, and if it’s not too hot :-)

The premises can only be entered through a security check. But that was relatively quick. We could only look at the mausoleum from the outside, as it was closed. It is very imposing with guards in front of it and you get an impression of how much Ho Chi Minh is worshipped. Opposite is the presidential palace, both built in a socialist style.

As a tourist, I was very impressed with the solemnity and historical significance of the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. The space was clean, well-organized, and security was strict. However, the waiting time was quite long and the restrictions on bringing many items were a bit inconvenient. Even so, it remains an important and worthwhile destination when visiting Hanoi. I gave it 4 stars for its good organization, but the visitor experience could be improved.

I have visited before and managed to get into the museum but because of the limited opening times it closed on this occasion.I wore a t shirt and shorts and was made to purchase a shawl to cover my shoulders before I was allowed into the open air area in front of the building. This area reminds me of a car park. I could totally understand if I was entering the building but to just walk in the area seemed very strict. Lots of people were turned away as they didn’t have the correct clothes on. So be warned. There was a guy selling them by the entrance.Would I recommend going mmmm yes to tick the box.

The mausoleum is only open til 10 in the morning so get there early as it is a really busy place. Men and women need to be covered to the knee and shoulders covered to and they are strict  on this and that includes access to the house on stilts and the museum as they are all in the same complex there is an admission fee to the house it is only small so will need some money, it is a beautiful complex and the mausoleum is very impressive and over stated and a great tribute to the man that is held in such hi regard by the people of Vietnam

To begin understand the 2,000-year struggle for Vietnam to be independent from invaders and colonial powers, visit the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum.  Get there early but only during the late fall, winter, and early spring when the body laid to rest in a glass coffin is not in Moscow for continued restoration.  The vast and sterile setting, long lines of silent citizens, and eventual passing by his preserved body from head to foot to head and out is somber and respectful.  I have passed the body of Lenin in his Red Square Mausoleum and Mao in his Tian'anmen Square Mausoleum are memorials to be understood in order to understand each of their national histories.

It's a mausoleum with reinforced guard. As with airports, you have to go under security gates. The thing to see is the changing of the guard. For the opening of the mausoleum it is not possible to take pictures and all electronic equipment is confiscated at the entrance. Behind it there is a small pagoda and a small temple. A short walk but especially not in full sun because there is no shade around the mausoleum. Hot stroke assured

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum in Hanoi is the final resting place of an iconic leader and a very important part of Vietnam's history. When I visited, I found it to be a large, solemn space in Ba Dinh Square, and it was clear that respectful attire and silence are crucial here. There might be queues, but it's worth the wait. Inside, the atmosphere is quite dark and quiet, and seeing President Ho Chi Minh's body preserved in a glass case definitely creates a solemn and emotional feeling. The grounds are lovely, with gardens and monuments, and nearby you can find attractions like the Presidential Palace and One Pillar Pagoda. Overall, visiting the mausoleum provides a unique and meaningful experience.

Thousands of people are lining up, but actually it's making reasonable progress. We sat down for 55 minutes, including a visit to the grave. Everything is done with military precision, anyone who steps out of line is immediately reprimanded. Beautiful terrain, interesting information about life and work of Ho Chi Minh.
Unfortunately, it was Monday and we couldn't get inside, but we couldn't see the changing of the guard. There is a bit of distance from the old town, so I took a grab car.
Free to enter (museum is a paid for visit), most of the site seemed to be closed.That said the mausoleum was impressive and the gardens nice.

We did go visit the Ho Chi Minh Memorial Complex to see Uncle Ho's stilt house and how simply he lived.

It was very special to see the body of Ho Chi Minh and I am glad to have gone to the mausoleum however the staff were extremely rude. We were told you walk in two single filed lines and told not to walk too fast and not to step out of line but not really given more direction on where to go and then were yelled at for stepping the wrong way. You walk into the mausoleum and then around the body of Ho Chi Minh. You cannot stop you must keep walking and then back out again and there are guards everywhere. No photos and no talking. It was quite nice to be able to see something like this without people crowding around.You can walk around the grounds and see other information on Ho Chi Minh. I would recommend this but be aware, it is extremely strict and at the security screening many items may be taken off you and they are strict on dress code.
Very easy to get in. Great building and more soldiers present. You really get an insight into how much Vietnamese value the former leader.

Got a taste of being corralled in single file to go to the mausoleum.Don’t step out of line after being screened….😀 or bring any water…Will have to be stored.File passed in two single files and up and out you go.Only open for a small window of opportunity so it will be crowded.Has to be done while in Hanoi.
This is one of those place that if your in the country is worth seeing. Do it on a tour for quicker entry or you may have a 3 to 5 hour wait.No images inside as that is very much taboo.

If the experience didn’t feel like I was in North Korea this would be a 5 star attraction! If you’re in Hanoi this is a must but BE PREPARED! We have been to many religious sites and know about dressing modestly etc, my male partner was caught out for wearing shorts, we went across the road and got a pair of pants for about $5 AUD, no worries. We then got into the line with every other person local and tourists, to be screened by security more than the airports! Don’t bring any bottles of water or any big cameras, they will all be taken, cameras will be stored - to top it all off we get inside after the escapade of security and pants and see other people inside IN SHORTS!! Nevertheless seeing the preserved body was amazing and such a feat, the communist influence throughout was very interesting to see and it was awesome to stand where many great political leaders have stood before! Trzeba zobaczyć .... to moim zdaniem swiatowy ewenement. Nie zachwyca, ale jest w tym miejscu coś niezwyklego. Coś, co świadczy, jak można wyrażać się sen o potędze. Na różne sposoby. Obok jest muzeum. Bilet płatny tylko gotówką 40000 Dengow. Sklepik, w którym można kupić rękodzieło oraz mydło i powidło od sąsiada Wietnamu  też przyjmuje platnosci tylko gotówką. Dla Polaków znalazłam ślad z Polski. Jest na jednym ze zdjęć. Porcelana Wawel zestaw herbaciany dla Ho podatowany przez Polaków.

More than a site of interest, it is a real place of worship where the population goes to give a heartfelt tribute to the remains of Ho Chi Min, enduring long in perfect order. Children are also brought from schools to learn a sense of respect and local worship.In addition to Ho Chi Min's body you can visit his first house (where leaders are now hosted during political events), his second humblest house, built of wood on stilts to "live like the people", the communal kitchens where he ate with his servants. Inside the complex there is a museum open only to students.All immersed in a huge well-kept park, rich in greenery and colorful floral beds, around a lake populated by carp.

It was impressive to see this representation of the resting place of the father of modern Vietnam.  We opted for a walk past the mausoleum as opposed to lining up int the morning for a viewing.  The scope of the monument and its setting, to me, demonstrated the importance of Ho Chi Minh to Vietnam and this helped me gain understanding of the country and its culture.

Here we came,sited in HO CHI MINH quarter,is this huge mausoleum,dedicated to the father of modern VIETNAM,there are lots of gardens and building worthy of a visit,each with is own historical record of past  events,allow 2-3 hours here

Mausoleum of Ho Chi Min - the Vietnamese leader who defeated the US. Venerated and with his body embalsamed for visitation. Beautiful on the outside and beautiful on the inside. Well organized and with controlled entrance. Interesting.

We arrived early to beat the crowds but still waited 30 minutes. The guards were quite strict about dress code and photos. The actual viewing felt rushed - they keep the line moving quickly. Impressive architecture though! Be prepared for security checks and remember to dress respectfully.
If you are visiting Hanoi it’s definitely worth visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, the Presidential Palace as well as the house on stilts where Uncle Ho spent his latter years.

Our morning visit to see inside the Mausoleum and view Ho Chi Minh in state was all done in well under an hour (out before 10am). Despite the long line consisting of locals and visiting local military personnel, it kept moving smoothly, it never felt crowded, and so we moved through quickly. There is a respectful dress code and no photography. Visiting the nearby gardens and Ho Chi Minh Museum add to the experience and knowledge.

Striking building externally and internally. The room with the body is clearly very cold and with low lighting. It's very reminiscent of Lenin's. Before you can get there there is a long walk to do. Luckily we didn't find a big line

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum evoked a truly special and solemn feeling in me. From the moment I entered the mausoleum grounds, the atmosphere of tranquility and reverence enveloped me, making me feel the profound respect everyone had for Uncle Ho. The mausoleum's architecture is solid, simple yet majestic, reflecting the spirit and stature of a great leader.

Interesting way to learn more about Vietnamese history and the story of Ho Chi Minh. We really liked the museum at the mausoleum and would highly recommend getting a free tour guide, they are very knowledgeable and great to learn from.

Tour scheduled by our agency from outside the mausoleum with our Guide. What to do when you are in Hanoi to understand Vietnamese history. Impressive site for its size and architecture. Around there are temples to visit. Attention extremely guarded site, security device at the entrance.
Too bad the mausoleum is closed due to a renovation but the museum was very nice to admire. We also saw the changing of the guard

The Mausoleum grounds is where in September 1945 that Ho Chi Minh read Vietnam’s Declaration of Independence, and it’s here today that the national leader’s body rests within a massive granite mausoleum.  In his speech back in 1954 Ho used quotes out of the preamble of the US constitution to set the pace of getting rid of the French, who had moved back into Vietnam following the Japanese occupation during WW II, and where the country was split in two at the 19 th parallel (communist influence from Russia in the North and wester influence in the South). This area of the Mausoleum is somewhat like what we observed a few years back in Tiananmen Square in Beijing China with Charman Mau’s mausoleum, with a big national flag, and a single military guard under the flag.  Here in Vietnam, there was no solders standing under the national flag, but there were sure a lot of Soldiers at the Mausoleum entrance and posted around his body.We had to check our cameras (as no pictures are allowed inside the mausoleum).  You must remove your hat, and have your knees and shoulders covered, plus no sunglasses (all as a position of respect to the country’s former leader).The line to view the body of Ho Chi Minh, was very / VERY long (well over 1000), but we received a VIP pass and jumped right into the middle of the line.  The Mausoleum is closed on Mondays, and we were originally scheduled to stop here yesterday, but our great guide Henry just swapped the schedule.The setting of the body is very elegant of a well-preserved person.    Originally, Uncle Hoe had requested to be cremated and his ashes scattered over the waters, but following his passing in 1969, the government decided to display his body, as a symbol of the inspiration of a simplistic, modest educated gentleman that had inspired his nation to throw off the yoke of colonialism.The government wanted the body to be preserved like Lenin in Moscow and Mao Zedong in China, and thus approached the Russians for help.  The Chinees did not want the Russians to help as the Chinese and Vietnamese were at odds AGAINST one another, so the body was moved rom cave to cave until the Russians finally preformed the cosmetic preservation.After viewing the guards, flowers, the body of Uncle Ho (no pictures allowed), we continued to the Prudential Palace next door to the Mausoleum; viewing the massive building, and the plush grounds around it.  As part of the grounds, we viewed where Uncle Ho lived on the grounds in a masonry building during the war with a bomb shelter and underground tunnel over to the Citadel where communications to the arms was sent out.  After the war he moved into a stilt house on the grounds until his passing.

It's better to have a guide to understand who Ho Chi Minh was and what he had done to Vietnam and how did he get so respected here rather than the fact he was a communist leader.Ho Chi Minh was humble but seeing the way guards there doing just disturbing me so much.The toilet is disgusting in this important area???

Honestly worth the very long queue. Very respectful shuffle past Uncle Ho's  body was unexpectedly moving, perhaps because he still commands such reverence from the very old to the youngest citizens of this unique country. The mausoleum is now closed until August 12 for his body to be 'maintained'
Very imposing building from the outside. makes beautiful photos. Inside it is a little disappointing. We are guided to where Ho Chi Minh's body is. You can't take photos.

A mastodon-like gray structure is Ho Chi Minh's final resting place. Although he had expressed the wish to be cremated to save costs, the government decided to build this mausoleum for him. His body lies in state there, mumified by Russian specialists. It is flown to Russia twice a year for maintenance.The architects saw a lotus flower in their design. In my opinion, this requires a lot of imagination. We were lucky to experience the changing of the guard. A visit to the refrigerated room was not an option for us.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a large memorial dedicated to the former Vietnamese leader Ho Chi Minh. The monumental building was built in the style of the Lenin Mausoleum in Moscow. There is a large area around the mausoleum that is monitored by the military. You enter the area after strict controls and regulations (such as dress code). When we got there, entry was no longer permitted. Therefore, we looked at this monument outside the area. If you are already in Hanoi, you should visit this mausoleum.

First off, the tomb is within a large complex so there is more to see than just the body of Uncle Ho.  And the surrounding area with the Palace, Gardens, Ho House, Pagoda etc is great for a visit.I titled this visit strange as it is a weird experience for a tourist.  Ho Chi Minh is revered by all of Vietnam so a serious and solemn event to be in this place.  As a Western tourist his reputation is not so golden so he didn't have that aura for me.  I also am not convinced it is his body preserved that you see.  He looks remarkably young considering when he died and even with the body being removed for a few months to Russia for 'maintenance'.  It could actually be a Lenin looky-likey in there...Glad I can say I was there but the security guards are very strict and too much for me.Note that the complex had many gift shops but they basically all just sell Vietnamese flag t-shirts or plates with Ho's face painted on.  Which was a bit of a let down.

The grounds are large and well maintained and entry through security was pretty quick despite the number of people (went around 11am). Because of the size of the grounds, you never feel crowded. Read up  on the history of the mausoleum and Ho Chi Minh beforehand if you aren't going with a tour guide who can give you the info.

The line to see Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum was ridiculously long, and the viewing itself was a quick, underwhelming experience. We felt rushed and couldn't appreciate the historical significance. There are better ways to learn about Vietnam's past.

If you want to visit inside the mausoleum and see Ho Chi Minh, you have to go in the morning. Problem is, that's when thousands and thousands of school children also go. We estimated we'd have to stand in line in the hot sun for about 3 hours to get in. Instead, we returned and visited the external grounds in the afternoon and it was peaceful and lovely. Worth a visit for the architecture, culture, and history.

If in Hanoi this is a place to pay a visit. The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is located in the Ho Chi Minh complex, a luxuriantly large and beautiful esplanade. I was unfortunate to have landed up on a Monday (Mon & Fri closed) but had the opportunity to watch the daily morning parade.

10 April 2023, the mausoleum itself is still closed, so you can’t get to see, nor file past Uncle Ho’s body…. Do you have to queue to get through security, and at 1 pm on a Thursday we wait about 20 minutes so no major drama. You do NOT need to have identification so you don’t need to carry a passport like we did. Importantly, females (women not kids as far as I’m aware) have to have their shoulders covered so at minimum a T-shirt and also the knees covered or you certainly can’t wear tight shorts. If you do, they will not let you in. That said some random dudes offer to sell you scarves to cover your shoulders if you don’t have something appropriate. Whilst you can’t go into the mausoleum itself it is worthwhile having a look at Ho Chi Minh’s Stills house and other buildings on the site, you do not need to prepay you can purchase a ticket after entering security at the internal gate to get into the building area …40,000VND to get in and will take you approximately one hour or so to have a good look around, and you certainly are beautiful place so it is well worth while doing.

This is an experience not to be missed.  It is certainly very interesting but it would be a push to call it excellent.  You can only go so close to the Mausoleum itself.  The scale of the building and the security certainly reflect the esteem in which Ho-Chi-Minh is held by the Vietnamese people.  The mausoleum dominates an absolutely massive square which is devoid of any other structure.  It looks across the square to the National Assembly building as if the great leader is checking that current day leaders are not betraying the revolution.Also on the site and in much more attractive surrounds are the yellow walled official presidential residence (which Ho only used for official business) and two much more humble dwellings which Ho actually lived and worked in.  You can't go in, but you can get a good look in through glass frontages.  A good insight into a leader of the people for the people.

The mausoleum is of great historic and social importance to the people of Vietnam, which in our opinion makes it well worth a visit.   However, it is worth being aware that the lines can be extremely long and it may take up to several hours to get to the inside of the mausoleum. We weren’t aware of this and visited around 8.30 am on a Sunday without having had any breakfast and it was 11 am by the time we finished seeing the mausoleum - we didn’t end up seeing the presidential place, museum, or the stilt house because we were too tired at the end of it/didn’t have the time. The time spent in the mausoleum is about 5 minutes. Our advise is don’t skip your breakfast/cuppa, come prepared to stand in the sun for few hours, and make sure you have sufficient time if you want to see everything in the complex.

The entire complex is escorted with very young soldiers dressed in white uniforms. We visit the place where the embalmed corpse of Ho Chi Min called Tio Ho is. Some Russians embalmed it and they check it from time to time.There is a guard inside the mausoleum for 24 hours, they never leave the body alone.It is located where Ho Chi Min had his modest house on stilts that can be visited on one side.

This was my second visit to Hanoi, however both times I didn't manage to see Mr. Ho Chi Minh's body in his Mausoleum, but was around in front and on other sites around the complex.So basically, you're reading tips from a traveler who didn't make it inside, but for sure will next time :-) HCM Mausoleum is within a huge space with several another sites.When entering the area you pass an airport style security check. After that you can roam freely exploring the area. You need around 2 hours min for that, excluding the Mausoleum, which visit length depends on the queueing line.Besides the Mausoleum within the complex is the Presidential Palace, One Pillar Pagoda, HCM stilt house and a Museum (small entry fees for the latter two).People here review and post pics of all these together, but I'll focus on the Mausoleum, while others you can read in respective reviews.My previous visit in 2017 was during some Vietnam holidays, so the waiting line in front was very long, some 2+ hours as I was told by police, so I gave up.This year, in Jan 2024 I put it high on my to do list saving our last day only for that.Unfortunately, I did not know the Mausoleum is NOT open every day. It's open only 4 days a week and some of these days only 8-11 AM. So, inform yourself beforehand if you want to enter it and see Mr. HCM body.All other attractions on the premises are open every day, so you still have plenty to visit.

The rush of history alone makes the mausoleum and the surrounding area worth a visit.However, you probably don't go to Hanoi just to visit this.There are many other things that draw in a tourist experience that is much more authentic than the norm

Ho Chi Minh’s embalmed body lies in state in this monumental building, set in the middle of a large, manicured square. While the lines to enter are long, the organisation is excellent and they move very quickly, directed by smartly uniformed guards who ensure that visitors behave with dignity (no hands in pockets, sunglasses or talking etc) in the presence of the remains of this important figure in world history. Many Vietnamese were clearly moved in the presence of their country’s revered founder.

There was a huge queue, lots of school children, but it moved quickly. Millions of Vietnamese make the journey to see Ho Chi Minh lying in state, so thought why not. Security is airport style, and there is a strict dress code, don’t wear shorts! If you have a camera, you will be given a canvas bag and told to hand it in before you enter the mausoleum (weird they don’t take your phone off you as they have cameras?) You are guided to the entrance by immaculately dressed guards who will also advise you to take your hat / sunglasses off / hands out your pockets etc. as you enter the only sound is feet on the ground, as you enter the viewing room the carpet sucks up the sound, silence reigns, and there lies the main man. You file past and exit to pick up your camera on the other side of the building. You’re then free to visit the museum (Vietnamese go free, but everyone else has to pay (around £1), lots to see and a couple of gift shops as well. If you’re visiting Hanoi, you should go.

I visited on a tour, so I only got to see the exterior. The guard was changing at that time, so I was able to take photos. Unfortunately, it was cloudy, so I think I could have taken better photos if it had been sunny. If I have the opportunity to visit again, I would definitely like to visit at a time when you can see the inside.
It looks great but the air around it was so polluted from the traffics. The building itself looks stunning and really shows the landmark of Vietnam

A very interesting experience unlike any can have in the uk. Must do it if u have time, we were fortunate and went in January so no crowds. Not sure we believe it is his real body but the respect given to uncle Ho is unbelievable given the time lapse from his death.

Visited here on the morning of Dec-22,2023 & saw the change of guard ceremony too (apparently height requirement for men is 175cm)Also saw his living quarters such as the house on stilts, his rooms like dining, living rooms and the cars he used etc. Nice pond and bridge in the central area.Didn't get to see his preserved actual dead body though.

We visited Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum in the evening of the same day we arrived in Hanoi. It is open from 5.00 pm to 10 pm.  It was the winter in Hanoi I suppose since we felt quite cold. There were thousand of tourists hanging around this place. It is nicely maintained. I think the Vietnamese people give due respect and show their gratitude to the leader who lead them to win the war.

When I visited I only wanted to remain on the outside but when you arrive and go through security screening you are placed in a group and guided through the mausoleum that holds the well preserved body of the great Ho Chi Minh. It was a good experience and I recommend.

This was the first place I visited in Hanoi. It took about 2 hours of walking. You will first visit the main mausoleum, followed by the stilt house, fish pond, one pillar pagoda, and finally Ho Chi Minh Museum.

When you take in the magnitude of what this 1 man means to these people, this is a very special place. Go between 07:00 and 10:30 as otherwise you cannot enter. Free but very strict, organised, no photos, you follow a path, there is no waiting inside but you will see his preserved body, almost glowing. The path takes you out to the stilt house and the pagoda is not far from the exit.

Really a must see, not just for the sight of Ho Chi Minh but the setting with the grand parade ground, the guards, the wreath laying and the general sight of  the variety of visitors. We were lucky as it only took about 10 minutes to get into the Mausoleum.Definitely take the time to go to the parade ground and watch the crowds.
The mausoleum was closed to the public at time of our visit but was still worth a look while in hanoi. Be aware that it’s heavily guarded and they don’t appreciate you walking over the boundary line

We visited here in late afternoon. So we didn’t see the dead body of Ho Chi Minh or natural flag raising at 6AM or flag lowering at 9pm but still we enjoyed the atmosphere. The building of mausoleum of grand, both slogan at left and right deliver strong intentions of the establishment of the country. The square is so vast that we imagine the people who have been enthusiastic for listening the speech of independence. Even you don’t enter the mausoleum, you’re asked to be dressed properly. The modern building at the opposite side of mausoleum is the diet. We wish we could come back to see inside of  mausoleum and see the guards exchange.

This place holds a unique position in the history as well as geography of Vietnam. Built impressively in an expanding area, it is a must visit place while in Hanoi. It is lit up during the night time which adds beauty to the whole complex.

This is where the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh, the leader who dedicated his life to the unification of Vietnam, is located. The part of the mausoleum where Ho Chi Minh's body is kept was closed when I visited; it seems it remains closed for maintenance for several months of the year.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, also known as the Ho Chi Minh Presidential Mausoleum, is located in Ba Dinh Square. The portico, inlaid with rubies, bears the Vietnamese inscription: Chủ Tịch Hồ Chí Minh (President Ho Chi Minh), and was completed in 1975. In his will, Ho Chi Minh stated that he wished to be cremated after his death because "cremation is more hygienic than burial and can save some agricultural land." However, the Viet Cong later violated Ho Chi Minh's wishes by building his mausoleum in the style of Lenin's. Visiting time is limited and reservations are required, so we did not go inside, but the entire mausoleum is magnificent and imposing.
Besides Mao and Lenin, Ho Chi Minh is one of the few historical figures whose life and work you can actually visit.

I only saw the exterior, but I was lucky enough to witness the changing of the guard ceremony. The contrast between the tense atmosphere of the soldiers on duty and the somewhat relaxed look on the faces of the guards leaving was quite heartwarming.

The museum is only open in the morning and we timed our arrival to beat the queue but it was still a very long wait to enter the mausoleum. The building is quite stark and no bags are allowed. We entered in single file and moved past the body of Uncle Ho (as the locals call him). It was in interesting experience and there were many groups of school children in the queue, all keen to practice their English.

The mausoleum houses the body of Ho Chi Minh, who was a national hero of Vietnam.   When we came here, the mausoleum was closed.  The structure was grand and reminded me of the Acropolis in Greece.We took very nice photos in the open square in front of the structure.
After queuing for about thirty minutes, I was finally able to pay my respects to the remains of Vietnam's great leader. It was worth it.

We came as a group of 4 on a Thursday morning arriving about 9.30am, there wasn't a queue outside which was good, we probably queued inside for about 30mins total and most of it was under shade at least.They seem very hit and miss on their must leave bags policy as loads of people were wearing backpacks, but they were strict on not taking pics on way round or inside.It was fairly straight forward going through a small x-ray area and then to the queue.For me seeing HCM was on the little creepy side but at least I can say that I have been.We wandered around the beautiful grounds afterwards and saw the one pillar pagada amongst around 1000 other people - it was certainly a lot busier. I preferred in the past not going to the mausoleum and wandering the grounds on the days that the mausoleum was closed - far more peaceful

This is a well respected place for Vietnamese people and many come to visit mausoleum. We came in the afternoon when crowds disappeared and we could explore everything. The complex contains presidential palace and also few last places where Ho Chi Minh lived last years of his life. He was well respected and it’s amazing to see his truly humbling accommodation

Certainly a symbol for this country, ravaged by years of war, which was able to reunite thanks to its president. From an artistic standpoint, there's little to say. The patriotic value prevails. In any case, it's worth seeing.

As soon as you enter the grounds, you leave the hustle and bustle of hanoi behind,  It is peaceful.  You need to follw the rules and make sure you walk the prescribed route around the Mauslleum.  There are lots of guards and iot is clear that 'Uncle Ho' has a very special place in the heatrs of the Vietnamese people.  if visiting Hanoi, this is amust-do attraction as it is central to the history of modern Vietnam

This is a very strange place, worth visiting once to get an idea of it. Be ready to queue. We had to wait in line for 45 minutes to get in the mausoleum. Check the dress code to visit the place beforehand. Avoid taking a back pack, you will have to leave it at the entrance, and a big camera that you will have to hand over.

Very strict entry rules to go in the Mausoleum. No water allowed and bags are scanned. You need to follow instructions or you will be told. You do see his actual body and no photos. The other parts of the complex are also allowed to be viewed but it shuts at 11.30 am so go early. It’s very pretty. We were too late for the museum
From the outside it's impressive, worth a visit at least once.
The work began on September 2, 1973, and was completed on August 29, 1975.

Visited the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, the final resting place of a revered and hard working leader who liberated and unified Vietnam. We visited on a Saturday and were fortunate that we did not have a long queue. Very orderly and disciplined visit with very strict dress code. The main section where the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh is   guarded. The entry and exit time in the Mausoleum is about 2 minutes as you keep moving. A must visit when you are in Hanoi.

We arrived at 9am on a Saturday to find the queue was over 2km long! This was estimated to take several hours so we chose instead to walk in the grounds, where you can see the one stilt house and the amazing bonsai trees, and view the mausoleum from the outside.

A terrific half day experience. Don’t get there too early as you get caught up with the masses of school kids that visit each morning. I recommend an 11am arrival.Plenty to see and do but your time with Uncle Ho is very short.

bit of a queue to get through the main entrance and then flowed right through to walking through and seeing ho chi minh lying in state.  a bit surreal but worth doing.   then we saw the buildings where he worked, his lovely stilt house on a lake that you can walk through, some old cars, a very cute one story pagoda and a gloriously gold and fruity temple.   dont miss the guide to buddhism on the walls!  all very enjoyable

I saw the mausoleum for the first time in 1993 and each time i visit Hanoi i always take a walk in that area and admire the huge building. You can see the changing of the guards or the flag raising at certain times of the day. In the old days you could cycle to the area but now all the streets are cordoned off and there is a security search and xray machine 150 m before you approach the mausoleum.

The mausoleum was closed. I was fine with that. Interesting displays on the grounds. Nice park like setting. Governor's mansion is nearby. No surprise that the government is attempting to(or has) establish a 'cult of personality' around their leader. Certainly, the reality of the life of HCM, and what is presented here probably do not align. Still worth a visit if you are in Hanoi.
We had a life history lesson when we visited the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum and were amazed at the way Ho Chi Minh's body was so well preserved.

This area has lots to see here. The mausoleum, presidential palace, uncle Ho’s house on stilts, one pillar pagoda, temple of literature and lots of history. Wear good shoes, and bring some water as you’ll be getting your steps in.

This is a must see if you are interested in history and the life of Ho Chi Minh. However you need to keep the following in mind before you visit :1. Check the official website for opening hours as it varies during the year. In October, when we visited the mausoleum was open from 7.30 am to 10.30 am on all days except Fridays and Mondays. 2. Reach early at around 7.30 am to avoid the crowd and the blazing sun. It becomes extremely crowded from 9am onwards and you will have to stand in a long queue, although they move fast.3. Dress modestly and ensure that your arms and legs are covered. Shorts are not allowed and you will have to buy a skirt from the store on the premises/ from hawkers just outside the gate to cover your legs.4. Camera, bags have to be deposited while entering the mausoleum and photography is not allowed inside. 5. Please make sure that after visiting the mausoleum, you visit house no 54 and the stilt house (separate tickets to be purchased which is not included in the mausoleum tickets) before you see the museum. Otherwise you will again have to queue up for a second round of mausoleum visit if you see the museum first and then plan to visit the stilt house. We were not aware of this and we spent 20 mins doing an extra round of the mausoleum before we could go towards the stilt house.

The entrance is at Ngoc Ha street and not via Huong Vuong which is the listed map address. There is security and you are required to check your bags at the counter. Even just a single bottle had to be checked in. Either bring absolutely nothing or be prepared to check in your bag. No photography is allowed within the masoluem but we were lucky we didn’t have to check in our mobile phones. Just don’t take it out where photography is not allowed. There are paid photo taking services after you exit the masoleum where HCM’s body rests. There’s no queue to see HCM as the traffic moves swiftly and you aren’t allowed to stand still for too long. It will take about a minute or two to enter, walk a round past where his body lies, pay your respects and leave. The room is heavily guarded. There are some food, drinks and souvenir shops within the area for you to rest or shop at. Once you enter the area you can also visit other places of interests, some at additional fee (eg HCM museum).Wear long pants and covered shoulders (no sleeveless) if you plan to visit HCM’s body. The other museums are not as strict on dress code.

Got here nice and early as I was expecting a queue. Took me a while to find the entrance as it was right around the back behind the mausoleum. Very few tourists about - it was only 8am so I was able just to walk straight through the line to the front with no waiting. They were doing some sought of quick ceremony when I got up to mausoleum and about 20 military officers walked through ahead of me. That was very interesting to watch them silently salute the body as they stopped in front of it. Only did the mausoleum and one pillar pagoda before heading off elsewhere. As I left there were masses of school students lining up at the entrance waiting to come through so I was glad I was there early.

Abbiamo trascorso otto giorni ad Hanoi (provenienti da Singapore), e il Mausoleo è stata una tappa molto importante dal punto di vista emotivo. Ci ha colpito la maestosità, la rigidità, e l'incredibile dispiegamento di militari che controllavano ogni passo delle migliaia di visitatori, sopratutto locali, che ogni santo giorno portano il loro tributo allo zio Ho. Come loro lo chiamano.Comunque una importante esperienza di vita.Non abbiamo ovviamente foto del Mausoleo perchè era proibito fotografare, ed è giusto rispettare le regole.

Very interesting visit to see the preserved body of the revered leader of Vietnam.Strict control and run by the military. No loitering or talking photos inside the chamber.Cannot take photos inside. But outside of the building & gardens ok

If you want to go inside you will need to come early - check the hours on Google Maps.I looked at the outside only. The building and the grounds outside are quite impressive in scale, all watched over by White-uniformed guards.As you wilk see from all the picture, the Mausoleum is a very stark edifice. The wide open space and straight, clean edged structure contrast quite a lot with the rest of central Hanoi.

Currently, due to COVID-19, you can only view it from a distance. Nevertheless, its grandeur is impressive. At night, you can see the magnificent building illuminated by soft lighting. On weekends, there are many people exercising in the surrounding area.

The registration process at the mausoleum is very quick and efficient. After visiting President Ho Chi Minh's mausoleum, you can spend 1-2 hours exploring the surrounding historical complex. A memorable experience when visiting Hanoi.
It's worth visiting this place to see the monument to Ho Chi Minh; the monument is grand and imposing. It's a shame we couldn't go inside to visit the mausoleum and see Ho Chi Minh's body.

You can only enter the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum in the morning (if you want to see Ho Chi Minh's body). Furthermore, if it's crowded, you might not even be able to get in, so it's best to get in line as early as possible, between 8:00 AM and 9:00 AM.

If you are lucky enough to Visit Hanoi, then you must go and see Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. Our guide Khan took us to see it. It is secure and you will have your bags searched. We were lucky as the changing of the gaurd was taking place as we arrived. In there starch whit uniforms, they looked great. One tip, do not cross the yellow line. It was not very busy because of the covic 19, so it was so good for us to see it all, with only a few other tourists about.
A very interesting place. You can see Ho Chi Minh's house and cars and appreciate his mummified body in perfect condition.

Impressive building and grounds. The actual mausoleum is sombre and respectful as it should be. No photos and some dress rules (sorry cant remember these).No admission charge. Almost no queues as we visited during the COVID 19 outbreak (March 2020).The mausoleum was only open in the morning until 12:00 (I Think)

-Must visit attraction in Hanoi-Free entry-Open for a limited time so arrive early to go through security checks etc-Very strict (Water was thrown away, camera was taken before entering, no sunnies allowed to be worn inside)-Have to queue (low peak we queued for 10 minutes) -One downside was you had to keep walking inside the Mausoleum so you only had a few minutes to view the body (very rushed)-Other attractions in walking distance

If you are in Hanoi, make it a point to visit the mausoleum of Ho Chi Minh. It is important to respect the nation you are visiting. The mausoleum is a modern structure, cubic and gigantic. There is heavy security and long queues for viewing the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh. Maintain decorum and do not do anything which can be construed as disrespectful.The arrangements are quite efficient and the queue moves quickly. Open only between 8 - 11 am, except Monday. Once done, you can walk and explore the entire area. There is much to see - the presidential palace (you can only see from outside), the house where Ho Chi Minh lived and worked and the revered One-Pillar-Pagoda. Soak up some atmosphere in the large square in front of the mausoleum and watch the change if guards.
It was a strange experience walking past the body of Ho Chi Minh, but I am glad we did it. The building itself and the surroundings are also worth seeing.

This is really worth to visit. Very beautiful complex to visit. The guards look very impressive in their uniforms and the other buildings around the Mausoleum is also very impressive. The one pillar Pagoda is also worth the visit. We were there during the Coronavirus outbreak and therefore had the place to ourselves. Really glad we visited this attraction.

Lonely Planet's guide says it is a place of pilgrimage, and it is.  Because I was there during the coronavirus outbreak, the single-file line took only about 30 minutes to get in.  There are lots of immaculately-dressed, unsmiling guards to make sure people are dressed appropriately, wear no hats, and do not take photographs.  "Uncle Ho's" pale body is encased in glass.  The air is kept chilly.  The whole edifice is a monument to communism.  The man himself wanted a simple cremation.  There are other sites in the complex that are worth seeing, like the Stilt House, where Ho Chi Minh lived, and the One Pillar Pagoda.
This place is very impressive place, and I think it must to visit.   Amazing place to go around and see how Ho Chi Minh live there. also the garden and building around.

We did not go in just stopped for a visit. The area is beautiful,well maintained and as you will see very respected. They change the guard every  hour try to catch that. We were there during the virus outbreak so no one there.i am sure during peak season it is packed. No real need to go in,not sure of the cost but we were not interested.

Just do what you are told basically! Very strict. The seeing Ho Chi Minh bit is over very quickly. You can't stop and look for long. No photos of course. Gardens and house etc quite pretty. Need a face mask, and cover up too, out of respect etc. Can't take water/drink in so don't bring, otherwise you have to leave it at a 'luggage drop' place and collect on way out.

We were not going here on our tour. We had just wanted to meet our group at the end but miss communication with our guide, we ended up going through it. Lots of soldiers and security, although 1 did leave his post to help my husband up the steps. I was surprised by the size of the whole place. So much open space but not allowed on the grass!!!! Saw some people on grass and next thing a guard blows a whistle and signs for them to get off. Interesting place though.
Very militaristic with lots of security and soldiers.  Close to the Citadel and parliament. Square is completely open so there is no shade
Worth a visit.   I don't  know if Icould say I enjoyed visiting  Mausoleum. But, it certainly was interesting
This is practically a must-see now, even though there isn't that much to see in the mausoleum. Socialist splendor...
It's an imposing place, with gardens, and you can see the embalmed body of Vietnam's most beloved leader. Security is tight; there are areas where taking photos or speaking is prohibited.
Ho's preserved body (a la Lenin in Moscow) lies inside.  Didn't go in, but the building is imposing and the site worth visiting.

Be prepared for a very long time in a queue. We arrived early in the morning, knowing  there could be a long queue, but were amazed at the sheer size of it - we walked for perhaps a quarter of a mile and didn't reach the end, so we gave up and visited the museum ( in the same park ) , which was very good. Recommended. When we came out, we could see the end of the queue, so decided to join it. We didn't get into the mausoleum for another 35 minutes - not much fun in the rain..... Inside, uncle Ho looked very  serene in his glass case, surrounded by immaculate and totally statue-like guards. Quite impressive.

The mausoleum is a beautiful building, kept respectfully peaceful with young sentries always on guard around the body of Ho Chi Minh. The body itself is extremely well preserved and they apparently take very good care of it. I think that this place would have far greater significance for the Vietnamese people who worship him and what he did for their country. It was a good experience to learn more about this great leader and see his resting place.

Makes you understand how one individual changed the course of history. A reverential place worthy of respect. Museum somewhat confusing and surreal but provides a thorough framework of modern Vietnamese history.
Must do while in Hanoi to pay respects to the culture and history of a great leader. Interesting history, commitment and accomplished life’s work by Ho Chi Minh.

An impressive monument which was slightly spoiled for us due to it raining the whole time we were there. The gardens are exceptionally well maintained and the guards obviously took their duties very seriously.The mausoleum itself is somewhat austere and very soviet.Worth a visit if only to say you have seen it.

A place that most tour companies seem to take you, a bit creepy walking past the corpse. You will need to show respect, cover shoulders and knees , no hats no talking or taking photos. Walk past in single file. Interesting to see where he lived “ no mod cons very basic”. The complex is interesting with some very nice bits . Nice to see and hear the history.
Beautiful grounds surrounding the Mausoleum.  Actual tour went swiftly as we visited Ho Chi Minh’s body lying in state.  Worth a visit for this historical figure.

Although I was unable to visit inside the mausoleum, just to be in the expansive square and seeing the building is impressive. The grounds are extremely well kept and the Congress building across the street equally interesting.  Seeing the honor guard out front, the surrounding area, and the impressive seriousness of it all makes for a good visit and a good photo op   I would like to return and see the inside of the mausoleum.

When you are in Hanoi you have to visit the mausoleum of Ho Chi Minh a very imposant building. It’s on a very large square and in the neighborhood you find diverent  ambassades and the home of the primeminister and the presidential building.

Thousands of people queued but it was so well organised , you kept moving and were in / out within 45 minutes. Very tight security No cameras Videos etc   - VERY STRICT . We went with out guide and glad we went as it was very informative.

It's conveniently located near One Pillar Pagoda and the Ho Chi Minh Museum, making it easy to sightsee efficiently. We happened to arrive just in time for the changing of the guard, and it was wonderful to see the three guards marching towards the mausoleum with their footsteps echoing loudly.
The mausoleum was closed but the building is impressive, the grounds pretty and the Congress building across the street.  Good place for a photo or two.

If interested in the history of Vietnam this is a must. Be careful  about timing as the Mausoleum, palace complex and museum all close in the middle of the day at slightly different times then open  again later in the day. It takes about 40 mins from the gate to file through the Mausoleum. No water allowed in Mausoleum but is allowed in museum.

This majestic structure houses the mummified remains of the nation's leader. Entrance is free, but the lines are long, and you should plan on spending about 60 minutes viewing the attraction. Although you'll only be inside the crypt for about 1 minute.
A very nice visit, it's a shame we didn't get to visit the Mosole itself, but we saw the changing of the guard. We weren't there at the right time.

To understand better Vietnam and its people it really helps to spend a few hours in this widespread mausoleum.  The queues to see the body can be long so our guide got us there quite early in the morning and the rest of the visit went smoothly after that.To learn about the country's great leader and feel his presence around the gardens, the small ponds and his domestic buildings was a great history lesson.You may not agree with his politics but this was such a major character in the nation's history that you understand why his mausoleum is so popular.There's plenty to see and the site is well run, though the entrance security was a bit more stern than necessary. A must-do on any tour of Hanoi.
Very nice place to walk around. There are a lot of local people walking or running. We ere on friday, so mausoleum was closed
There's a museum but within walking distance. It's a must for sight seeing in Hanoi to have a bit of understanding of history.
A visit to the mausoleum of war hero Ho Chi Minh is a must. The Vietnamese are a proud people...

Another must-do when visiting Hanoi.  We went in winter so crowds weren’t a problem.  Just note that the entry is at the back of the complex and if you walk there (like we did), we had to walk nearly another 30min to get to the entrance.The only caution is that it’s free to get in, but once you exit the mausoleum, the only way out that we could find was to pay to go through to the Golden Palace and gardens.  We looked around but didn’t see any other way to exit.

I stopped here during my recent visit in Vietnam. I did not have time to wait in the incredibly long line to enter the building, but I got a feel for importance of Ho Chi Minh to the people of Vietnam. As someone who came of age during the Vietnam era it was very interesting to get a broader perspective on this period in history through my visit and also understand a bit more about Ho Chi Minh. I would recommend going.

The holy of holies for Vietnamese. This is the mausoleum for Uncle Ho and a solemn place. You walk past the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh in full view (or is it really him?). Also in close proximity is the old French designed Presidential Palace & also the simple 2 room house Uncle Ho lived in while he was President. All a must see when in Hanoi.
Quite an experience , to think how long he has been laying in state & you can walk past close to the casket

Weird but totally worth a visit.Well manicured gardens surround it.Well organized queuing at entry.You walk past him cant stop, no hats or as I learnt donot hold yr hands together (I got a slap from a guard for this!)

The historical aspects of seeing Uncle Ho's final resting place is a must-see for its historical and architectural aspects.  However, to see the entombed body, you have to line up and wait in the hot sun, so be sure to have head-wear.  In addition, the entire buidling and its compound is manned by miliatry outfitted guards who are very unfriendly, stern and regimental. Perhaps, they like to put on a show of "strength".  Next door is Ho Chi Minh's house on stilts and lying to one side of the Mausoleum is the One Pillar Pagoda, which was founded by King Ly Thai To in 1049 - an important symbol for the people of Hanoi.
It was quite an interesting visit and worth while seeing though be prepared to stand in line for a while and listen to the guards as they’re very strict.
Very nice and large area. Learned a lot from Vietnam History. If you go in the summer make sure u wear hat, mister... you will be outside and walk in the sun a lot.
This is a must-see place in the capital; the place is heavily imbued with the character

Guess what, I visited on a Monday so was unable to visit the mausoleum itself but was able to spend time wandering around a very attractive site.  The pagoda supported by just one beam is beautiful and I would really recommend the cold fresh coconut juice drinks when you have finished your visit.  The old cars on display are fabulous and it really is a very tranquil place.  Be warned some of the visitors to the site do push you around but try and stand your ground if you can.
A monument to honor the national hero. Everything contributes to the sacredness of the place: military pickets, silence...
Uncle Ho being a very important and inspiring figure, a visit to the place created in his honor is well worth the trip.

We arrived just after 10.00am and it was too late to enter into the mausoleum. The edifice is magnificent and befitting. It was also highly guarded. While this is a revered place, it's majestic presence commands respectable awe.Well worth a visit but check the opening times!

Free to get in to see him. It shuts quite early, about 10 or 11am. As taxi dropped us off at 9am we caught a glimpse of changing of guard. Hoped to see it again but didn't. No kids under 3 allowed in. The guards outside mausoleum very strict about not taking photos of mausoleum (outside). You walk through fairly quickly so don't be put off by queue. Pay VND40k to access his house on stilts and walk round lake. Cheap food available in this area.

Amidst all the chaos and traffic, at least there's no traffic, not even a single motorbike! The mausoleum contains the body of Ho Chi Minh, who wanted a simple burial, but instead is under constant surveillance day and night. Visits to the body are only possible on designated days, while the gardens surrounding the house where the father of the nation lived and worked are open daily. Also worth seeing are the beautiful museum and the one-pillar pagoda, both part of the complex.
I love the passion they have for the father of the nation! A place of respect...nothing more, but that respect is palpable...it's like a must-see pilgrimage site if you're Vietnamese

You can view the mummy of Ho Chi Minh, which is re-embalmed every year. However, the staff provides no information, and lingering in the rooms is discouraged; you are asked to move through quickly. But it's definitely interesting.

We visited the Mausoleum during a recent trip to Hanoi.This is obviously a very important place in Vietnamese history and they very jealously guard respect and proper decorum.The queue took circa 45 minutes and the grounds are lovely.At the Mausoleum you are filtered into two lines to walks past in total silence the preserved body of Ho Chi Minh.As i say in the title “very different”.  Pleased i went - but would not go again.
The building is austere and gray. I didn't visit the body because it's a pilgrimage for the Vietnamese.
It’s a very imposing building, beautifully  maintained and lovely gardens around. You can’t go inside this building.

If approaching from Dien Bien Phu, make a left at Le Hong Phong (embassy row).  After the security check (no water allowed), follow the crowd and direction of uniformed guards.  No charge for mausoleum.  The stilt house, working house and garage need a ticket.(40000 vnd).  The museum is another fee of 40000 vnd.  Follow signs to exit to return to entry gate.

There's a baggage check at the entrance, and drinks are confiscated. Cameras must also be left at the checkpoint, but they're returned on the way out. Photography is prohibited from this point onward, and you walk through the large grounds. You can't stop inside the temple, so even if there are tour groups, you have to move quickly. The size of the temple, and the size of the parliament building across the street, reminded me that this is a socialist country.

Love him or hate him, no one can deny that Uncle Ho has a special and permanent place in the hearts of the Vietnamese population, and this mausoleum is perhaps the ultimate tribute to Vietnam’s iconic leader. His body, which has been embalmed for the past 50 years since his death in 1969, is a must-see for visitors to appreciate this larger-than-life figure. This attraction only opens for a few hours in the morning, so best to be there early when it opens at 7:30 a.m. Entrance is free, but be prepared to endure stringent airport-style security checks and 20 minutes queue, monitored by disciplined white-uniformed guards who disallowed any form of photo-taking. Once near the mausoleum, the line moves quickly as visitors are not allowed to hold the queue when walking past the serene corpse.

The mausoleum is a must-see when in Hanoi. It looks particularly impressive from the outside. Whether Ho Chi Minh is actually buried there, or if it's just a dummy, even the Vietnamese aren't entirely sure.

Aesthetically it's like many of the memorials erected throughout the world to commemorate worthy (or not so worthy) individuals. This commemorates a man without whose passion, devotion, and determination Vietnam would not exist.  For its historical significance this is a place to visit in Vietnam.
There is a baggage check upon entry, and bottled water will be confiscated.

They let us in without any problem in shorts. There were people in line just tying something on top. We arrived at 9:30 on Thursday, and by 10 they were already finished. The line starts at the other side of the square; our hotel taxi stand was set up incorrectly.

We choose not to get up early and queue to see Ho Chi Minh's body. However walking around the outside of the Mausoleum is very interesting.  Guards in white military dress on their whistles when tourists cross the yellow lines. Very strict airport style security to enter the park, you can collect items at the end.
A type of mausoleum reminiscent of Lenin's in Moscow. An important page in Vietnam's history, even if the country is now moving in a different direction, it's worth a visit.
A must-see. Be sure to respect the lines and signs before you are reminded of the rules. You can also see a very beautiful park, as well as the One Pillar Pagoda.

It's practically a must-do excursion if you're visiting Hanoi. You can visit the mausoleum on a short (and very crowded) tour where you can see the mummified remains of President Ho Chi Minh. Cameras and almost all personal belongings are strictly prohibited, and security is extremely tight.

A good place to visit when in Hanoi. But don’t visit on Monday as it remains closed to visitors for inside visit. Also view the adjacent Presidential Palace and the stilted house where HCM lived. Do not miss the Pagoda visitalso.
A part of history and worth seeing. The different areas can be confusing and there is a walk from the front to the actual Mausoleum. The hours are limited so check before you go
The imposing Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum also encompasses the grounds of the former Residence of the Governor-General of Indochina, where the President's former home is located.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is not one of those sites that leaves you breathless, like the view from Brazil’s Christ the Redeemer, or standing in front of India’s Taj Mahal, or beholding Zimbabwe’s Victoria Falls. It’s the historical significance that is worth considering when standing here.Walking through Hanoi’s Ba Đình Square, it is immediately reminiscent of Moscow’s Red Square and Beijing’s Tiananmen Square, those massive public spaces that punctuate major communist cities. It took me back to previous trips in far flung lands.Before you stands a mighty mausoleum containing the body of Ho Chi Minh, the president of North Vietnam at the time they went to war with the United States. This ostentatious burial site is ironic, given that Ho Chi Minh asked to be cremated before dying in 1969. But here he lies, across from the National Assembly in a building for all to see.
