Huge queues but go down very quickly worth a visit just to say you’ve been. Ho Chi Minh is so revered here still and there were many Vietnamese visiting along with holiday makers

If you’re visiting Hanoi you should visit the Ho Chi Mingh complex. There are a lot of pieces to the site. The mausoleum is interesting although not the most interesting part of the visit. You will need to take hats off and prepare to be quiet and respectful during your visit. Also know that there can be quite a line and they will keep you moving - so your visit will be short. No photography is allowed inside the mausoleum and you will have to check bags and big cameras at the desk on the way in.
I was initially surprised by how vast it was; it was so big I couldn't even tell where the entrance was. It's visited not only by tourists, but also by many locals and students.
We only saw the Ho Chi Minh-mausoleum from a distance, but the atmosphere surrounding it was very nice. People were singing, soldiers were training and tourists were walking around.

A very interesting visit for learning about Vietnamese history. The monument is very impressive and well-guarded by handsome guards. The square in front of it is impressive, and it's very busy on weekends. A must-see when you're in Hanoi.

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a sacred place for the Vietnamese people, as it houses the remains of Ho Chi Minh, the founding father of Vietnam. It seems that many Vietnamese people from all over Vietnam, as well as foreigners, visit it. Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is closed on Mondays and Fridays, and on other days, it is only open in the mornings. This time, I only had time on Friday, so I entered Ba Dinh Square in front of the mausoleum and had to give up after only being able to admire the magnificent mausoleum from the outside with the National Assembly building behind me. As is expected when entering the mausoleum, there is also an X-ray security check when entering Ba Dinh Square. I was told by a Vietnamese person that people in sloppy attire will be refused entry, and that photography, eating and drinking, talking, and stopping are prohibited inside the mausoleum. There is no fence between Ba Dinh Square and the mausoleum, only a line drawn, but if you cross this line and get too close to the mausoleum, you will be reprimanded by the guards.

I came to appreciate this tour more over the course of our visit through Viet Nam.  Of course the government reveres the man who started the movement to unite Viet Nam, but as we travelled south, it is became clear to me that whatever issues Viet Nam has, they are happier that at least they are making their own decisions - not the French, not the Chinese, not the Americans.  You begin to appreciate the impact that this man had on the future of the country - and why so many people come to see him.

If you can dodge the crowds here, then this would great experience. We had a personal guide who walked us through the key highlights of the site. Since all tour groups get routed via a one way path, there are some bottlenecks to contend with especially around the private residences as pond. The gardens were also a welcome reprieve from the afternoon sun. Plenty of tacky souvenirs to choose from on the way out.
Its grandeur, guard protocol, and other elements transport you to a spectacle of parades and historical symbolism.

We went at 7am and already there were hundreds in the lines including children on trips. I was quite surprised. Having said that, the lines moved quite quickly. The mausoleum area is well maintained. There are a lot of staff around and all tourists are monitored closely. No photos allowed of the inner area and you have to check in cameras if you bring them with you.

A really great Tribute to the First President of Vietnam who held the country together. Fascinating to see the Masoleum, His house, cars and office . also the simple private quarters attached to a Bunker! Great watwr lake and flowers around it, makes for a fascinating days tour. Must visit when in Hanoi.
Mausoleum with a visit to the embalmed leader, large park and several other sights are must-sees; lots of information about the history of Vietnam.
One of Hanoi's must-see sites. We visited it, and our guide gave us extensive commentary on the building and its surroundings. We also saw the changing of the guard. Absolutely worth doing.

The whole outdoor complex is called the Ho Chi Minh Museum comprising the Mausoleum, Ho's two houses, the One Pillar Pagoda and a large park area with lakes and streams. The Mausoleum is a very large, solid edifice even bigger than Lenin's Tomb and the parade area outside is even bigger than Red Square. If visiting on your own, you may not have been told not to walk inside the yellow lines in front of the Mausoleum. TIP: There is a small changing of the guard ceremony every hour, on the hour.

It feels like a park; it's incredibly spacious. A foreign guide was explaining things enthusiastically, so I tried to listen, but I gave up because it sounded like a foreign language. I think it would be meaningful to visit if you know the history. It also looked like there are places nearby where you can rent Ao Dai.

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is the mausoleum where the body of Ho Chi Minh is laid to rest. Entry is free, but you have to wait in a long line and go through strict security checks before you can finally enter! However, large bags are not allowed. While waiting in line to enter the building after the check, some elderly foreign tourists were talking and were reprimanded by a security soldier. This is a sacred place, so talking is strictly prohibited during your visit! Photography is also prohibited inside! Inside the building, in the middle of a dimly lit, cool room, lay the body of Ho Chi Minh in a glass case. It was a valuable experience.

We went with a tour group today to see Ho Chi Minh’s mausoleum today, and was surprised at the level of security and seriousness around it. The guards in the crypt were in full white dress uniform, which would be uncomfortable at best in the humid conditions, and they physically push you past the crypt, definitely no photos with Uncle Ho here. The level of respect and love they have for HCM is everywhere, but he does look a bit plastic, but he has been dead for 50 years.

Quite curious, in a communist-style square (large and open) and watched at all times by soldiers in white uniforms, you march into the mausoleum, where you can see, without stopping, taking photos, speaking, putting your hands in your pockets, crossing your arms, or covering your head... the embalmed body of the leader.
Long line ups and guards usher you through to the mausoleum. Once through you are able to wander through the gardens and other buildings, Lovely way to spend a morning.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum and Its area is outstanding.  I spent 3 hours going around and admiring everything.  The body of Ho Chi Minh was in Russia which it is sent once a year for preservation uptake.  But enjoyed the stilt House where he lived .....the pond....the main building parliament from outside ( no visitors Inside).  It is a place you should come.

Needless to say, the mausoleum of Ho Chi Minh deserves a visit by all tourists to Hanoi, at least for picture taking. Sadly, during this period, I am fully aware that the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh cannot be viewed as I have ever read somewhere that during the month of Oct/Nov of each year, the body would be sent to Russia for 2-3 months for restoration. Anyway, I will be visit Hanoi again and hence there will be an opportunity to view the embalmed body in the near future.

We had been warned to be here early to avoid queuing but we slept in!! Arrived at 9am and the queue was very long, wrapping around the side and out to the main road. Despite this it moves very quickly because you're not allowed to stop inside. We ended up leaving 40min later which isn't too bad. You have to leave all cameras at a drop off point. Lets just say it was interesting to see although unusual.

A visit to Hanoi wouldn't be complete without seeing Ho Chi Minh's mausoleum. Its understated elegance transforms it into a powerful historical symbol when you understand its significance and delve into the life and story of this remarkable figure. Visit it as part of your cultural experience of the city, as it's easily accessible on foot from other monuments and attractions.

I have to omit that I have never seen the fascination in visiting a mausoleum to see a dead body, be they an important historical figure or not. But the reverence the Vietnamese show to their favourite son is quite moving.The Mausoleum is quite an impressive building in its own right, being modelled on Lenin's mausoleum in Russia, and you can obviously view it from the outside without visiting inside. The organisation for you to visit inside is very well done but does involve queuing and several layers of security removing you of liquids and cameras, but the queue does move quite quickly and it probably took about 30 minutes from the initial security scanners to entering the mausoleum. The dress and behaviour codes to visit are quite strict, even to the level that I had to remove my glasses because, being reactolite, they looked too much like sun glasses to the guards. Fortunately the Mausoleum is dark inside and I could soon put them back on to be able to see Uncle Ho. I have to say his annual trips to Russia for maintenance obviously agree with him and I hope I look half as good when I have been dead for 50 years.I have to say it’s not something I would have chose to do if it hadn’t been a major item on our tour company itinerary, but I am glad I actually experienced it. But for me personally the visit after to the Presidential Palace complex was more interesting.
Much loved by the Vietnamese who affectionately call him 'Uncle Ho'e, to whom they could only dedicate a monument of such grandeur.

It is what it is and it was an experience.. but keep in mind that this might be your only chance in your life to see an embalmed leader's body and likely this will be the last leader in human history to have their body prepared like this. Don't miss out if you have a little interest.The long line you see to get in moves pretty quickly, so don't be scared off. You literally get quickly hurried along. However, go in the morning as early as possible to beat the crowd.Here is a tip:Your taxi driver will likely drop you off at the big intersection next to the Mausoleum. Where you will see the huge field and building. However, You can only enter from the main gate. Your driver will be trying to avoid the traffic jam at the main gate and will screw you and your ignorance over. Set your address correctly and if you can still actually see the Mausoleum when you get out of the car.. then you're in the wrong place and will need to walk about 1km or more to get around the complex to the actual gate.

I have much respect for Ho Chi Minh who had a humble living even as the President of Vietnam. So it was one of the reasons to visit and pay respect to the leader who fought against the world's mightiest country. Ho Chi Minh mausoleum and the surrounding gardens are well-maintained and it takes at least an hour to complete the tour. We had to wait in the queue for about 20 minutes to pass through the entrance. The only thing bothered me heavy military presence as if we were in a garrison.

When I visited, Hanoi was experiencing abnormal temperatures of 42 degrees Celsius. It is a treeless square, so you have to stand in line right in the middle of it. It is a difficult place to visit in the summer because it is very hot and the sun is intense.

Great visit, great history lesson. Didn’t go inside but walked around the outside and saw the changing of the guard.  If you happen to be there at the right time, the changing of the guard is an exercise in precision and well worth watching.

Very worthwhile and interesting how he became a hero of Vietnam. We were there with a guide, which we had already booked through a travel agency, so everything was already arranged. The museum is also interesting.
Better walk in the morning for Ho Chi Minh Complex before getting hot sun and to visit body Ho Chi Minh are early if mistake around 11am
It's a spacious and open area on a vast site. Someone trying to take a picture of a large building was severely reprimanded by a security guard.

I visited Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum, but it was somewhat disappointing that I could not enter due to the embalming process. However, the nearby Ho Chi Minh Birthplace and Presidential Palace were nice because the landscaping was well-maintained.

It looks like a copy of Lenin's Mausoleum, a spectacular avenue, wide as was typical in Soviet cities. You can't go inside, but it's worth visiting even just from the outside; the changing of the guard is quite something if you can see it—everyone in pristine white. Inside the grounds, there are other monuments to visit, such as the one-column pagoda.
Visiting this site you can feel the gratitude that the Vietnamese people have towards President Ho Chi Minh. If you are in the city, this visit is a must, it is worth it.

I went to the Mausoleum in the evening just prior to sunset. I have to say the place looks better after dark when the lights are on. A lot of locals come here for jogging and other exercises, if you can stand breathing in the polluted air in Hanoi, come here for a good jog. To go inside to view Ho Chi Minh's body, you'll have to go early in the morning to line up. Another note is that although short pants are allowed, women need to cover their shoulders to get inside. My gf was wearing a tan-top and was denied entry and she had to buy a scarf nearby to cover her top.

I have always wanted to see the Mausoleum of Uncle Ho and this provided me the perfect opportunity to do so.  I could not help but feel a sense of pride on how his people revere him.  Our guide was quite strict and in no uncertain terms told us not to sneakily take photos inside and even castigated another who was going to!

Uncle Ho is rightly respected by the Vietnamese: he expelled Chinese, Japanese, French, and Americans from his country during his 60 years of leadership. Austere. Honest. Wrong about economics. Closed-minded about politics. Undoubtedly a global figure.
Please note: The mausoleum can only be visited in the morning. Alternatively, Ho Chi Minh's residence can be viewed from the outside for 40,000 dong. It gets very crowded with locals on weekends.

Long queues to get through. Although the queue can keep moving. When you finally see Ho Chi Minh, there is no stopping to pay respects, you keep moving. Then you’re out the door. Interesting place though, looking at his houses and the surrounding gardens.

Awesome building, guarded by goose-stepping guards in immaculate white uniforms. Our guide was full of interesting facts and stories. The gardens are well kept, as is the fleet of cars Ho Chi Minh owned.Interesting view of the history of the Vietnamese, Uncle Ho and where he REALLY worked.

In visiting the Ho Chi Minh mausoleum, tourists and local visitors have to pass through inspection.  There is an x-ray scanner right at the entrance to the complex.  Water bottles are not allowed inside.  At the second gate, visitors are advised to put their cameras inside a red bag since taking of pictures is strictly prohibited inside the mausoleum.   They will be able to get their bags after they exit the mausoleum.  Tourists will then be able to take pictures around the Ho Chi Minh Complex which includes the following:  the Ho Chi Minh Museum, the One Pillar Pagoda, the Residence of the former president which is the House on Stilts and have an amazing view of the lake inside the complex.  It was an amazing trip.

A must-see, requiring no more than 30 minutes of visiting. Admission is free, and from inside the mausoleum you can access the presidential palace for 40,000 euros. Generally, a visit of one hour completes the entire itinerary.

As the mausoleum is only opened for limited time everyday, one can expect the queue to be long, regardless of the time. However, it does move very fast as you can only spend at most 30 seconds in the mausoleum as they do not allow visitors to linger. Remember to dress appropriately and be respectful.

As the title suggests. It's hard to expect anything less, and there certainly won't be any surprises here. Personally, I think it all depends on the tour guide. If they're above average, it'll be fine... It's worse if it's just "another € or $" for them to show you around the mausoleum. Then your tour will undoubtedly turn into an hour-long walk during which you'll see a mummified guest behind glass in an air-conditioned building.

Its a surreal experience viewing Uncle Ho's extremely well preserved body! Honestly felt like he was going to get up any moment and start speaking. The viewing itself is a highly regulation and organised exercise. Everything else is far more relaxed once you exit the mausoleum.The one pillar pagoda is really underwhelming and can be missed. While the mausoleum is free entry, the entrance to the museum is ticketed.
Vietnam's great leader. He led an impressive struggle and is a highly respected figure in his country. As you visit the mausoleum, you notice the respect in people's eyes.

You can get inside the mausoleum and see Ho Chi Min's body - in this sense, very similar to Lenin's mausoleum. You can also visit a former home of the leader and learn a bit of the recent history of the country. The mausoleum is an imponent building reflecting the leader cult.

We had a group visit to the museum, it’s was a good experience seeing the great leader. The residence of the leader were her humble he spent most of time away from the Yellow palace. Great experience.
It is a must visit if you are in Hanoi. It should not be missed. If you can walk, it is walk-able from the old quarters but some walking is required. Less tourist in the early hours.

Good to walk by and take pictures. The place is highly secured and you would be required to go through security prior to entry. If you smoke, leave your lighters and cigarettes at your hotel. Also no chewing gum inside its premises.

I happened to be able to see the flag-lowering ceremony at 9 PM, and the sight of soldiers in their white uniforms marching was quite impressive. There weren't many people, so I was able to see it from very close up. The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum itself was beautifully lit up, and I think it's well worth seeing along with the vast plaza.
Beautiful but not exceptional. They have a lot of respect for Ho Chi Minh, and when you enter the mausoleum, you have to be careful with every step you take.

I had missed this place on my first visit because of the opening times so I made sure I was on time this time around. Getting there was no issue but getting back was a slight issue as in Hanoi, I found that when you to get a Grab back to the hotel  you need to communicate with the  driver by taking a photo of where you are waiting as there is no taxi pick up point. The place is beautifully landscaped with interesting information about Ho Chi Minh.  Make sure you carry an umbrella or rain coat  or hat.  You will need if it rains and protect yourself from the scorching sun on sunny days as the passage in is not well shaded.It was really unfortunate that I did not see any schedule for the changing of the guards and was caught by surprise as I would have love to take photos of the smart military drills. My only unpleasant experience there were a group of lady tourists that were pushing and dashing there way to the toilets really being oblivious of one's presence. Souvenirs are available the exit with may shops offering various goods for sale.There is a charge for entry.An interesting sign board that said -No Chewing Gum, for a moment I had thought that I was in Singapore.

The martial atmosphere accompanies you from the entrance, which is free, right up to the visit. Decent clothing is required, and photography is prohibited inside. It is not so much the model Mausoleum that impresses but the atmosphere, the long queues of visitors. In reality, inside there is the story of a small man who defeated two incredibly strong powers and built a people who made Vietnam great, but now perhaps too much to see.

You need to go early as the number allowed in is limited.  Expect along queue to see ‘The man’ entombed in a glass case but don’t step out of line, take of sunglasses and wear appropriate clothing.  All chatter to stop as you approach the mausoleum.  Get this over with and the lovely grounds are then free to explore.

Visiting the Mausoleum can be disappointing for those who just want to see Ho Chi Minh lying in his glass coffin, nothing much to see and you have to keep moving so there's not time to just stand for a moment to look at the body or reflect. But the surrounding gardens is worth the visit.

Had to walk quite a distance to find the entrance, lots of people in the line but it kept going at walking pace so no real queuing. There is no charge, you can take mobiles and bags but no cameras, they supply you with a bag in which you put the camera and collect it later. Lots of soldiers in uniform line the route and  as you enter you have to be in pairs, no sun glasses or talking! It is well worth seeing and probably took no longer than 15mins. You can then collect your camera and tour the rest of the grounds.
It is necessary to have a tour guide to explain to you the history to understand. I enjoyed it, though we need to queue for 40 to 45mins to just view President Ho Chi Minh's body.

The breath of a people's history. Impressive is an understatement. Interesting, even if the queue is long. The park where the final resting place is located is worth seeing, as is the governor's house.

We are going to the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum first. Due to the closure for 2 months a year, we cannot go inside now. We had a nice tour around the mausoleum and gardens, the outdoor living quarters, the workhouse, etc. of Ho Chi Minh. Beautifully laid out. And what security! You are not even allowed to walk on the sidewalk. You are immediately called back. Even if you want to take a photo of a memorial plaque/small temple, you get reprimanded. But luckily, I had just taken the photo.

I happened to visit during the changing of the guard ceremony. There was a security check of bags under the watchful eyes of the soldiers. When I tried to crouch down to take a picture, I was told to stand up, so it's best to refrain from any clumsy actions. Such a large square probably shows some influence from China or other countries. It's a place worth visiting.

It was incredibly organized and even though the lines are long, they do move consistently well because you can’t do anything but do a walk by. Without a guide to help with the background information, It wouldn’t have been that interesting.

It is a place visited by so many people that long lines form from dawn. It is a place that people of all ages from around the world naturally visit when they come to Hanoi . Although the lines are long, paying respects at the tomb does not take long. It is also a good place to visit for educational purposes.

Definitely worth a visit - like nothing I'd ever done before. Had to queue for about 20 minutes but it was moving at a pace all the time. Guards were very officious but this added to overall experience. Not something you do every day!!

Generally the HCM Mausoleum is amazing and you can see the embalmed body of the great vietnamese leader, Ho Chi Ming in close view. The mausoleum is very informative and documented the life of the great leader and history of the long fought Vietnam War. Plenty of photo collection of HCM and also many interesting art works and cultural display too.

Worth seeing and experiencing something unknown to our Western culture: cult of personality. The Vietnamese take this very seriously when it comes to Ho Chi Minh, he is obviously still very admired, if truly or habitually - who knows. You will be lead in a queue across the huge premises, all the time accompanied by soldiers in white uniforms, until you get to the room where he actually lies. You can't stop, you have to walk on, around the coffin and then you're out again and that's it. Entrance is free. I still recommend to do it when in Hanoi, check out the other sights related to HCM as well.
We learned so much about Ho Chi Minh, how humble he was, & saw how he lived. Inspection before entering but it's ok. Be early. Closed monday to Friday. Saturday only until 10:30 the guide said.

Visiting Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum again in February 2026, I was struck by how powerful the place remains no matter how many times one has seen it before. The architecture is simple yet majestic, built with clean lines and solid stone that express strength, dignity, and permanence. Standing in the vast space of Ba Dinh Square, I felt the same emotion generations of Vietnamese have felt—a quiet pride mixed with gratitude for the history that unfolded here.  The surrounding area forms a harmonious ensemble. On one side rises the modern National Assembly House, confident and forward-looking; on the other are green gardens and wide pathways leading toward the Presidential Palace and One Pillar Pagoda. Together they create a dialogue between past and present, revolution and renewal. The atmosphere is extremely solemn, but never heavy. Guards move with graceful discipline, and visitors instinctively lower their voices, showing natural respect without being reminded.  What makes the experience especially beautiful is the human presence. Groups of students in neat uniforms walked hand in hand, listening attentively to their teachers. Young men and women in colorful áo dài looked bright and gentle against the grey stone background, bringing a touch of youthful energy to this historic space. Their smiles and polite manners reflected the culture of courtesy that Hanoi is famous for.  Inside the Mausoleum, the silence becomes almost sacred. The orderly line, the cool air, and the soft lighting guide every visitor into a moment of reflection. Outside again, the wide square opened like a stage of national memory where important events once took place. Watching the flag flutter in the winter breeze, I felt how architecture here is not only concrete and marble, but also emotion and collective spirit.  The Mausoleum area is more than a monument; it is a living symbol of Vietnam’s journey. Anyone who comes to Hanoi should spend time walking slowly across Ba Dinh Square, breathing the space, and understanding why this place continues to inspire respect, unity, and hope for the future.

I can say this sight was something I will never forget, definitely don't skip this. If you are squeamish I'm sure you could skip paying your respects at his well preserved remains but I was certainly impressed. Beyond the mausoleum the grounds are beautiful and well maintained, and the guards do their job with a sense of importance and dignity which was really cool to witness. Before visiting we wondered why the hours are such but it is so that you will have time to see the place and not be disappointed with running out of time. Something to note, *even men need to wear long pants*,, many men had to buy the street vendors elephant pants to enter which proved pretty amusing.

Great management to get the people to visit inside. It’s quite a long walk from the entrance, but not too long. There was very little to no wait even there were lots of visitors. Inside I got to see a very well preserved body of Ho Chi Minh. It was a very brief visit as all visitors had to keep moving, but overall a great experience. In this complex I also visit other interesting places like the one pillar pagoda, Ho Chi Minh’s house (with a fee), and Ho Chi Minh Museum (with a fee), spending around good 2-3 hours here. Please check the dress code before your visit.

Nice opportunity to experience the rich heritage of the country, and the wars they have fought. No wonder why no superpower could ever actually capture this country. The mausoleum is a holy place for the locals where they have kept the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh. Some points to consider :-  - there is a dress code. Better wear fulls, and strictly avoid shorts - entry to the mausoleum is closed on Fridays - the queue for entry is very long, but moves reasonable fast. Our queue was around 100 meters long, but we reached the entrance within 10-15 mins

A fascinating and respectful place to visit. The complex is well maintained and the atmosphere is calm and dignified. The queue was long but moved quickly, and the guards were very professional. It took around two hours in total, including security checks and walking through the site. It was quite hot and humid, so bring water and dress comfortably. A meaningful and highly recommended experience to learn about an important part of Vietnam’s history.

Never park your motorbike across the street from the section of Le Hong Phong road that faces the Mausoleum. That parking spot has no sign, it's on the sidewalk, and there are two elderly people there. They don't give you a parking ticket, they say it costs 10,000 VND but then charge 30,000 VND. And they only park until 7 PM, so if you don't know and come back late, the traffic police will tow your motorbike away, or the elderly people will leave without watching it.  It's best to park your motorbike in a proper parking lot or a reputable shop for safety and convenience when visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum.

Visitors to Ho Chi Minh City should not miss the opportunity to visit the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, a significant historical site dedicated to President Ho Chi Minh, a highly revered figure in Vietnam. The mausoleum holds great cultural and historical importance, reflecting the deep respect and admiration the Vietnamese people have for their beloved leader. Please be sure to check the opening days and hours in advance and plan your visit accordingly to ensure you arrive on time.

Place where embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh has been kept for visitors to see.  Very peaceful, clean, well decorated with flower beds and gardens this is where visitors show their respect to the great leader. Located in Ba Dinh Square of Hanoi, where independence was declared by Ho Chi Minh in 1945. There are some formalities and restrictions for entering the area which the local guides will clearly spell out before visit.  Must visit.

Good place to visit for the history.  Mausoleum ground was a big place and can take a stroll around the place.  To visit the palace need a ticket.  Like the one pillar pagoda, no fee.  Best to wear attire with sleeves and pants or skirts cover the knee.  Need to check their opening time especially mausoleum have only morning visiting hours which close by 11am.  There is security check but q clear fast.  No water or chewing gums allowed inside.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, dedicated to the great leader of the Vietnamese nation, the guiding light of the working class and proletariat of the world, is considered the heart of Vietnam and is located in the historic Ba Dinh Square. This is a place every Vietnamese person should visit at least once in their lifetime to show their reverence for the Father of the Nation, who sacrificed his entire life for the cause of liberation, independence, and shaping the nation. The flag-raising and flag-lowering ceremonies are performed daily in a solemn atmosphere. The feeling of being immersed in the crowds each morning, listening to the national anthem and watching the national flag being raised and fluttering in the brilliant dawn light, is an unforgettable experience at this historical and sacred site.

A sacred place to visit when in Hanoi is Uncle Ho's mausoleum, the great leader of our country. I am always grateful to him for giving me the comfortable and happy life I have today. At 6:30 AM, I attended the flag-raising ceremony. Although there was a light drizzle, it was still conducted with great solemnity and seriousness. Witnessing and participating in it for the first time was very moving and emotional. Then at 8 AM, I visited Uncle Ho's mausoleum. Even though there were many people waiting, no one complained or pushed; everyone was eager to see Uncle Ho. It was truly wonderful.
President Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum Many artifacts are stored here A visit is recommended Closed on Mondays and Fridays Opening hours are available online No shorts allowed Formal attire required

The Ho Chi Minh Museum offers a powerful insight into how deeply Vietnamese people respect and honor their leaders, particularly President Ho Chi Minh. The museum clearly reflects a strong sense of national pride, identity, and unity, showing how leadership, struggle, and independence are central to Vietnam’s historical narrative.  Through exhibits, photographs, and symbolic displays, visitors can feel how Vietnamese society values its national identity and collective history, as well as the respect shown toward the government and state institutions. The atmosphere is orderly and respectful, reinforcing the importance of discipline and shared values.  Located within the Ba Dinh complex, the museum visit feels more than educational—it is a cultural and civic experience that helps visitors understand the mindset of the Vietnamese people and their relationship with leadership and nationhood.  Overall, the Ho Chi Minh Museum is a meaningful and enlightening place to visit in Hanoi, especially for those who want to understand Vietnam beyond tourism—through its values, pride, and national consciousness.

Should visit when visiting Hanoi to see Uncle Ho's resting place  Small tips: if you want to avoid a long wait, you should come early around 6:30 - 7:00 am because the mausoleum opens from 7:30 am (Tuesday to Sunday, closed Monday and Friday). Dress should be polite, long pants, shirts with sleeves; filming or taking photos inside is not allowed. After visiting the mausoleum, you can visit the Ho Chi Minh Museum or the fish pond, Uncle Ho's stilt house right next door - all are worth seeing

Having studied and heard so many historical and literary works about this great leader, visiting his mausoleum for the first time was incredibly moving. There is a security check upon entry, so please note that you should not bring restricted/prohibited items inside.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum in Hanoi is an absolute must-visit. Upon arriving in Vietnam, we saw how highly Ho Chi Minh is revered, but we didn’t fully understand why. Visiting his mausoleum, the museum inside, and taking a guided tour for just 40,000 VND was incredibly valuable for gaining a deeper understanding of the country, its culture, and its history. I highly recommend visiting if you’re in Hanoi, as it really opens your eyes to the country’s past. The mausoleum entrance itself is free, closes at 11.30a.m., so it’s best to go early

We visited on Saturday and because of school trips - the cue was cray. Waiting time was ~1 hour. But inside so many guards, no pictures allowed inside. And you spend at most 1-2 minutes inside. Guards don’t let anyone stop. The cue is moving all to the same rhythm.  There is actual body of Ho Chi Minh there. As they say  No need to buy tickets.

Beautiful grounds with the palace and mausoleum.  We went right when it opened at 7:30 and there were no crowds. It was easier to appreciate the grandness of it all without tons of your groups.  Much of the grounds are blocked off, but guards were friendly and made it clear where to go.

I'm Anthony, and these are my kneecaps. I was allowed in. My shorts were just touching the tops of my kneecaps. I asked the guard if it was a bit higher, then demonstrated to him, would I be let in? He said no. The security is strict at the metal detector. Ba Dinh Square itself is free to visit. The Ho Chi Minh Museum and the Stilt House area are an additional charge. Viewing the HCM mausoleum from the outside is free. There was a very nice volunteer tour orginazation giving free tours. Definitely recommend reaching out to them.

Visited the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum — a truly respectful and historical site in Hanoi. Entry is *free of charge*, and no tickets are required. The atmosphere is calm and well-organized, with clear security procedures. It’s a must-visit to understand Vietnam’s history and pay respects to their beloved leader. Dress modestly and arrive early as it can get crowded.

I so loved the deep culture of this place and respect the Vietnamese people give to their first president. He is often known as an Uncle too in every Vietnamese family. Good place to visit and see once when at Vietnam
When visiting Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, please dress respectfully and do not bring dangerous or flammable items, etc. When visiting, please remain silent, walk quietly, and minimize noise. 🙏

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a sacred destination that everyone visiting Hanoi should see.  The Mausoleum is open every morning except Mondays and Fridays. If possible, visit the Mausoleum on weekdays and go early; although it will still be quite crowded, it will be less crowded and you will have more time to explore the surrounding area.  There will be a security check before entering the Mausoleum, so do not carry knives or scissors in your bag, and dress appropriately.  After visiting the Mausoleum, you can take photos at Ba Dinh Square. There are photographers available for 50,000 VND per photo, plus 5,000 VND for lamination. Hard copies and digital files are available behind the Mausoleum.

I'm from the South, coming to visit Uncle Ho's Mausoleum... The moment of waiting in the crowd made my heart flutter. It's been 24 years since I've been to Hanoi for the first time, to visit our beloved Uncle Ho. I love Uncle Ho so much. My love and respect for him have made my heart purer. Seeing his countenance once made me realize how beautiful and majestic he was. How much I love Vietnam.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is the most sacred and solemn architectural work in Vietnam, located right in the center of Ba Dinh Square. The mausoleum was built to preserve the body of Uncle Ho, the great leader of the nation. The mausoleum's architecture is solid and solemn, using precious stones and sustainable materials. What is special is that the mausoleum always maintains a solemn atmosphere, attracting millions of domestic and foreign visitors to visit. This is a place to express the gratitude and infinite respect of the Vietnamese people for President Ho Chi Minh.
It was a bit of a shame that we couldn't go inside the mausoleum on the day we visited. A stroll around the mausoleum was also wonderful: the fish pond, the stilt houses, the One-Pillar Pagoda…

A worthy point of interest for anyone visiting Hanoi. Please note that one has to go through security check including baggage x-ray, don't bring anything offensive. The Vietnamese security are not to mess around with.

In line at 8:25 and out by 9:00 AM on a Saturday. Certainly thought it was going to be a lot longer in line. It is a smooth operation. No selfie sticks or large bags - otherwise will need to check-in. No photos. It’s a surreal experience especially as a foreigner.

Its the second time I come here. I suggest coming 30 min before they open. It enables you not to be rushed by the guards and you get to see Ho Chi Minh for over a min. When you come later you might get 20 sec total viewing time. It did take almost an hour of wait time, security checked and backpack storage and walk before we were allowed to enter. I would not miss it if I was in Hanoi. Ohh, and its free. The real entry spot is by Sóng Sánh Café & Roastery. Not where google map or grab stops. You should go there instead. Saves you 15 min walk.

When traveling to Hanoi, you must definitely visit Uncle Ho's Mausoleum, everyone❤️🇻🇳❤️  Thousands of people flock to visit but still maintain order so you don't have to wait too long, you will go through the security area, items except money and phones... all have to be deposited, so you should bring light things. In addition to the mausoleum, in the campus there is also the One Pillar Pagoda, the museum, the Stilt House where Uncle Ho used to live,... there are many places to visit.  Entering the mausoleum, you will feel the cold and the solemn and quiet atmosphere where Uncle Ho slept in the middle of the crowd of people saying goodbye, making you feel very emotional and indescribable.🫶🇻🇳  If you have the opportunity to come to Hanoi, please come visit once, everyone.🤗  P/s: free entrance, everyone 😉

Entrance to the mausoleum is free but mobile devices are not allowed. When entering the mausoleum, you must solemnly take off your hat, sunglasses (sunglasses) and dress politely. Before entering the mausoleum, you will go through a security check and you are not allowed to bring water in, so everyone should take note.  There are 2 different ways to enter and exit the mausoleum, so avoid losing things. A little further out is Uncle Ho's old stilt house, a little further you will see the One Pillar Pagoda  The weather is hot and sunny, so if you go during the sunny season, you should prepare in advance

As a tourist, I found the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum very solemn and moving. The surrounding area is spacious, clean, and beautiful, while inside it is quiet and orderly.  Standing in front of the mausoleum, I clearly felt the sacredness and respect that everyone has for Uncle Ho. This is a place you should visit at least once when you come to Hanoi.
Going on a crowded holiday, lining up in an orderly manner to get in, very proud to visit, no fee, Hanoi tourism this is a place everyone should definitely visit
The place was solemn and clean, but unfortunately I arrived a bit late and missed the visiting hours for Uncle Ho.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is open 5 days a week, on Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Saturday and Sunday mornings, and is closed on Mondays and Fridays for maintenance and upkeep. Opening hours vary by season: from April 1 to October 31 (hot season) from 7:30 am to 10:30 am, and from November 1 to March 31 (cold season) from 8:00 am to 11:00 am.

I asked the guard at the Mausoleum. The Mausoleum is open except on Mondays and Fridays. The remaining days are open, the flag is raised from 6am to 6:30am and lowered at 9pm. Vietnamese people do not need to buy tickets to visit Uncle Ho's stilt house. This time, I came at the wrong time so I did not have time to enter the Mausoleum.

The Mausoleum viewing entry is free, and you must go through security check point to enter this area. During my visit I did see two people turned away due to dress code so prepare to visit properly dressed.  Beyond the Mausoleum you can enter the palace grounds for 40,000VND to see the palace, pond, one pillar pagoda.

Awesome place to learn and appreciate the impact and contribution of Ho Chi Minh in Vietnam. It is a large complex so be prepared to walk.  Note that security is super strict - dress modestly, keep phones in your bags during security check, follow the line, no taking of pictures inside the mausoleum.
The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is very beautiful and majestic. There is a museum and the One Pillar Pagoda nearby. It's best to go early for a visit, and Vietnamese citizens don't need to buy tickets.
I arrived late in the afternoon, so I only managed to stop by for a short while before leaving, but I was filled with many emotions.

Important to know: free visiting hours end at 1 PM and shorts are not allowed. The mausoleum is an impressive monument with a solemn atmosphere, well-kept gardens, and strong historical significance. Worth a quick stop while exploring Hanoi.

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum – A place where the nation’s gratitude settles Located solemnly in Ba Dinh Square, Hanoi, Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a special historical and cultural structure, where the body of Uncle Ho – the great leader of the Vietnamese people is preserved. This is not only a famous tourist attraction but also a place for every citizen and international visitor to express their love and gratitude to Uncle Ho.  The mausoleum began construction in 1973 and was inaugurated in 1975, with a solid and majestic architecture, using many precious materials from all over the country. Entering the mausoleum, the solemn and quiet atmosphere helps everyone to calm down and remember Uncle Ho’s great contributions to the independence and freedom of the nation.  In addition to the mausoleum, the relic site also has the Ho Chi Minh Museum, the Presidential Palace, Uncle Ho’s stilt house, and the fish pond – where Uncle Ho used to live and work. Each work, each relic tells a simple yet great story about his life.  Coming to Uncle Ho's Mausoleum, visitors not only learn about history, but also deeply feel the patriotism, humanity and simple lifestyle that Uncle Ho left for future generations.
Nice place, clean and well maintained The only problem is the long wait to get through security. By the way, there is no need for security control…

Easily 30,000 people.  My advise: arrive before it opens, wear appropriate clothes (full length pants and shirt. Women full length dress and cover your shoulders), and enjoy the scenery while you walk the lines to get in. Great experience
Absolutely beautiful! 🇻🇳 Uncle Ho, you are the unwavering belief in victory for the Vietnamese nation 🇻🇳

President Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum opens every Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Saturday and Sunday morning. However, the Mausoleum will be closed on Mondays and Fridays. A sacred place that every Southerner wants to visit once, it is an honor and happiness to be able to go on the first day after a month of temporary closure for maintenance. Unforgettable memories of the days of August 2025 preparing for the historic September 2nd ceremony

Without being overly concerned, coming from the South to visit the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum made me very proud and I felt like I had dreamed of setting foot here. The air was very fresh and pleasant, the green trees and plants looked very cool and peaceful. Only after coming here did I realize how truly tranquil the place was.

Visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a truly meaningful and memorable experience for anyone traveling to Hanoi. Located in Ba Dinh Square, this place is not only a famous tourist attraction but also a sacred historical site where people come to pay respect to President Ho Chi Minh – the beloved leader of the Vietnamese people.  The architecture of the mausoleum is solemn and impressive, built with grey stone and inspired by traditional Vietnamese and Soviet styles. The atmosphere around the square is calm and respectful. When entering the mausoleum, everything is kept quiet and orderly. Security is tight but polite, and visitors are expected to dress modestly and behave respectfully.  Inside, visitors have the opportunity to see President Ho Chi Minh’s embalmed body preserved in a glass case. It’s a powerful moment – not just for Vietnamese, but for foreigners too – to witness the lasting legacy of a man who played such a vital role in Vietnam’s history.  One thing to note is that the mausoleum is only open in the mornings and closed on Mondays and Fridays, so planning ahead is necessary. There may be a queue, especially during holidays, but the line moves quite efficiently.

According to the announcement from the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum Command, after the annual periodic renovation, from August 2, the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum Command continues to organize the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum Visitation Ceremony according to the regular plan, welcoming people and international visitors to visit Ho Chi Minh.   The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum Visitation Ceremony is held in the mornings: Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Saturday, Sunday. No visitation ceremony is held on Monday and Friday.   Hot season (from April 1 to October 31): Time from 7:30 to 10:30. Saturday, Sunday and holidays from 7:30 to 11:00.   Cold season (from November 1 to March 31 of the following year): Time from 8:00 to 11:00. Saturday, Sunday and holidays from 8:00 to 11:30.   May 19, September 2 and the first day of Lunar New Year, if they fall on Monday or Friday, will still hold a ceremony to pay respects to President Ho Chi Minh.

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum – A Sacred and Solemn Space Located in Ba Dinh Square, the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is the resting place of President Ho Chi Minh – the great leader of the Vietnamese nation. The area surrounding the mausoleum is spacious and airy, surrounded by lush greenery and meticulously maintained gardens, creating a peaceful and solemn atmosphere.  Visitors come here not only to pay respects at the mausoleum but also to explore Ba Dinh Square, the Presidential Palace complex, and the Ho Chi Minh Museum. In the early morning, the solemn flag-raising ceremony attracts a large number of locals and tourists. This is one of the destinations with special spiritual and historical value, leaving many unforgettable emotions in the hearts of everyone.  The mausoleum is closed for maintenance on Mondays and Fridays, so visitors cannot visit the mausoleum. Please note the visiting hours.

October 14, 2023 Uncle Ho's Mausoleum is increasingly beautiful and associated with memories, memories, stories and experiences that can never be forgotten by a soul like me. Free for Vietnamese citizens, foreign visitors will pay the entrance fee.

The embalmed body of the great Ho Chi Minh is preserved and displayed here, and visiting the mausoleum is truly a moving experience. It reminded me of my visit to Lenin’s Mausoleum in Moscow years ago — the setup and atmosphere are quite similar, filled with respect and solemnity. As it was a weekend, the place was very crowded, and the heat made the wait a bit challenging. Still, it was worth every moment to witness such an important piece of Vietnamese history. Entry is free, but there are strict rules in place to maintain the sanctity of the site. Photography and videography are strictly prohibited inside the mausoleum. Visitors must dress modestly — clothing that reveals shoulders or knees is not allowed. Silence and respectful behavior are expected throughout the visit. This is not just a tourist spot but a place of deep national pride for Vietnam. If you’re in Hanoi, don’t miss the opportunity to pay your respects to the man who played a pivotal role in the country’s independence.
National Day celebration, September 2nd, 2025. Preliminary and General Rehearsal ceremonies, August 27-31. Independence Day commemoration. A80. …
Visiting the capital without paying respects to Uncle Ho is a great loss for any Vietnamese person.

The project is a place to preserve Uncle Ho's remains according to the wishes and sentiments of the Party Central Committee and the people. The construction of the mausoleum began on September 2, 1973 and was inaugurated on August 29, 1975. The mausoleum was built in 3 layers, 21.6 meters high and 41.2 meters wide. Below is a three-step staircase leading up to the central structure with the remains room and corridors and stairs. The top part is the mausoleum roof designed in a three-step shape.

Located inside Ba Dinh Square, this is the Ho Chi Minh Memorial Tomb, set within a large, spacious complex. You can walk around and explore the surrounding parks, which are peaceful and well-maintained. It’s also common to see locals running and exercising in the area, adding to the lively atmosphere. A great spot to reflect and enjoy the surroundings!

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, the president and prime minister of Vietnam is a place that you absolutely must visit when in Hanoi. Remember the regulations and how to dress. I recommend going in the early morning hours, because a few hours later there will be very long lines. Kindergartens, schools, students, military, civilians and tourists. The most crowded location. Please remember not to use your phone and take pictures in certain areas because they can take them away. In addition to the Mausoleum, there is the presidential palace, the building and the garden where the leader rested. There is also a museum, overall it is a really large area to visit.

I took my friend from the South to visit the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. We left at 9 am, so there weren't many people and we didn't have to wait. When we left, it was very crowded (around 9:30 am). The officers in white uniforms were very handsome, tall, and serious, hehe. Everyone should remember to dress respectfully and not bring prohibited items.

The place looks simple from the outside, but once you enter to see the great Leader Ho Chi Minh resting, the feeling goes to the next level. I literally felt chills seeing how lively the preserved body Ho Chi Minh looked.

I, a child from the South, came to visit President Ho Chi Minh's mausoleum. I saw in the mist the vast rows of bamboo. Oh, the green bamboo of Vietnam! Standing tall and straight through storms and rain.

Review of the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum (Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum) 🇻🇳 The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, located in the historic Ba Dinh Square, is a sacred architectural work and a noble symbol of the gratitude and respect of the Vietnamese people for their Father of the Nation. Inaugurated in 1975, it houses the remains of President Ho Chi Minh.  Historical and Architectural Significance The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is not only a place to pay respects to President Ho Chi Minh but also part of Ba Dinh Square, where he read the Declaration of Independence in 1945. The mausoleum's architecture harmoniously combines modern style with traditional national features. The mausoleum has a sturdy, square shape, clad in gray granite. The roof is in a three-tiered design, creating a sense of solemnity.  Above the mausoleum, the words "PRESIDENT HO CHI MINH" are prominently carved in red precious stone. In front of the Mausoleum are large stone columns and two rows of lush green bamboo, symbolizing the vitality and longevity of the Vietnamese nation.  Visiting the Mausoleum Visiting the Mausoleum is a solemn and moving experience. Visitors must adhere to strict dress code and order regulations to show absolute respect. The moment of stepping into the tranquil space and seeing President Ho Chi Minh resting in peace creates a sacred feeling, helping every Vietnamese person to further deepen their gratitude for his contributions.  Besides the Mausoleum, visitors can explore other important sites within the complex:  * Presidential Palace: A building with ancient French architecture.  * Ho Chi Minh's Stilt House: Where President Ho Chi Minh lived and worked simply.  * Ho Chi Minh's Fish Pond: Closely associated with his daily life.  * Ho Chi Minh Museum: Where artifacts about the life and career of President Ho Chi Minh are preserved.  Overall Review The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is an indispensable cultural, historical, and spiritual destination when visiting Hanoi. It is not only a place to commemorate a great man but also a place to educate generations about traditions, patriotism, and revolutionary ideals. The solemnity, dignity, and serene atmosphere of the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum leave a deep impression on all visitors, both domestic and international. Proud to be a child of Vietnam, with red blood and yellow skin ❤️🇻🇳

The feeling is very sacred and the feeling is very solemn when I set foot here. The grounds of Uncle Ho's Mausoleum are very spacious, with many trees, so it is still cool even in the summer. The square is a bit hot, suitable for going early in the morning or late in the afternoon. The guards are solemn. If everyone has the opportunity, they should visit Uncle Ho once when coming to Hanoi. President Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum, also affectionately known as Uncle Ho's Mausoleum, is one of the most sacred and solemn structures in Vietnam. Located in Ba Dinh Square. Inside the mausoleum is the place to keep President Ho Chi Minh's body, which is carefully preserved and allows people to visit during certain hours. The atmosphere here is always quiet, respectful and full of emotions. Surrounding Uncle Ho's Mausoleum is a complex of relics: Stilt houses, fish ponds, Presidential Palace, Ho Chi Minh Museum... all creating a valuable cultural - historical space for people and tourists to learn, remember and be grateful to the great leader of the Vietnamese people. This is an indispensable destination when you set foot in the capital Hanoi.
It's so majestic and beautiful. Every Vietnamese person born in this S-shaped country should visit at least five times in their lifetime.

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, Uncle Ho's Mausoleum, is where the body of President Ho Chi Minh is kept. The mausoleum was officially started on September 2, 1973, at the old stage in the middle of Ba Dinh Square, where President Ho Chi Minh used to preside over important meetings. The mausoleum was inaugurated on August 29, 1975, consisting of 3 layers with a height of 21.6 m, a width of 41.2 m; the lower layer is shaped like a three-step staircase, the middle layer is the central structure of the mausoleum including the body room and corridors, stairs. The outside of the mausoleum is covered with gray granite, the inside is made of polished gray and red stone. Around the 4 sides are rows of square marble columns, the top layer is the mausoleum roof in a three-step shape. On the main facade is the inscription: "CHU TICH HO-CHI-MINH" in ripe plum-colored pink stone. Surrounding the mausoleum are gardens where more than 250 species of plants are grown from all over Vietnam.

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is one of the most important and sacred historical sites in Vietnam. This is not only the resting place of President Ho Chi Minh but also a symbol of the love and respect that the Vietnamese people have for him.   Evaluation of Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum  1. Architecture  The mausoleum was built in a solid, solemn architectural style, imbued with Vietnamese culture combined with the style of the former Soviet Union.  Construction materials were taken from many regions across the country, demonstrating the solidarity and gratitude of the Vietnamese people.  2. Surrounding space  Ba Dinh Square is large and airy, where many important events take place.  The trees and flower gardens around the mausoleum are carefully tended, creating a solemn space but still close to nature.  3. Historical significance  This is where the body of President Ho Chi Minh is kept, showing respect and remembrance of his contributions to the country.  This is a place that attracts millions of visitors every year, both domestic and foreign.  4. Experience when visiting  Visitors must strictly follow the regulations when entering the mausoleum, including polite attire, maintaining order, and not taking photos inside.  The mausoleum visit ceremony is solemnly organized, creating a feeling of emotion and respect.  In short, the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is not only a special architectural work but also a symbol of respect and national pride. This is a destination not to be missed when traveling to Hanoi.
The dream of visiting Uncle Ho's mausoleum has now come true.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, also affectionately known as the Mausoleum of President Ho Chi Minh, is located at 2 Hung Vuong Street, Dien Bien Ward, Ba Dinh District, Hanoi. The mausoleum houses the remains of President Ho Chi Minh, fulfilling the wishes and sentiments of the Central Committee of the Communist Party and the people. Construction began on September 2, 1973, and the mausoleum was inaugurated on August 29, 1975.  The mausoleum consists of three layers, standing 21.6 meters high and 41.2 meters wide. Below are three steps leading up to the central structure, which includes the reliquary chamber and surrounding corridors and staircases. The top section is a three-tiered roof.  The exterior is clad in gray granite, surrounded by rows of square granite columns, and prominently displayed in the center is the inscription "PRESIDENT HO CHI MINH" in plum-colored pink stone. The interior is made of polished gray and red stone. The grounds surrounding the mausoleum are planted with many species of trees and flowers characteristic of various regions across the country. This is a famous tourist attraction in the capital, a place that any Vietnamese person would want to visit at least once.

Everyone should park their cars on Dien Bien Phu Street before entering; it's the cheapest at 10,000 VND (according to the gatekeeper nearby). When I went, it was close to April 30th/May 1st, so there were many people taking pictures wearing beautiful ao dai (traditional Vietnamese dress). The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum holds immense significance for the Vietnamese people, both historically, politically, and spiritually. Specifically: - A symbol of gratitude and respect: The mausoleum expresses the deep gratitude of the Vietnamese people for the immense contributions of President Ho Chi Minh – the revolutionary leader who achieved national independence.  - A symbol of national unity: The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a spiritual focal point, where people from all over the country (and international friends) come to remember and rekindle their patriotism and will to build the nation.  - A place for revolutionary education: Visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum helps the younger generation better understand the history of the national struggle and the moral values ​​such as diligence, frugality, integrity, and righteousness that President Ho Chi Minh exemplified.  - A sacred and revered space: The Mausoleum is considered a "sacred place" of Vietnamese patriotism, a place where spiritual strength, ideals, and faith in the future converge.  - Affirming the historical stature of President Ho Chi Minh: Preserving his remains and building the Mausoleum demonstrates the honoring of President Ho Chi Minh as an outstanding leader with a great influence not only domestically but also on the national liberation movement worldwide.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is one of the most sacred and solemn historical sites in Hanoi. The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, also known as the Mausoleum of President Ho Chi Minh, is one of the iconic landmarks of Hanoi and a must-see destination when visiting Vietnam. Here are some detailed reviews:  1. Location and Space The mausoleum is located in Ba Dinh Square, a place associated with many important historical events of the country. The surrounding space is spacious and solemn, surrounded by lush greenery, creating a peaceful and dignified atmosphere.  2. Architecture The mausoleum is built in a unique architectural style, embodying solemnity and simplicity, reflecting the thoughts and lifestyle of President Ho Chi Minh. Visiting Experience Entrance Procedure: Visitors must adhere to strict regulations, from attire to behavior, to show respect for President Ho Chi Minh. Forever grateful to the great President Ho Chi Minh In his will, President Ho Chi Minh wanted to be cremated and have his ashes placed in three regions of the country to save money. But with the affection and respect for him, I would like to go against his wishes for once - to preserve President Ho Chi Minh's body for a long time so that later people across the country, especially people in the South, and international tourists can visit. Therefore, the Mausoleum was built and inaugurated on August 29, 1975, consisting of three layers with a height of 21.6 m, a width of 41.2 m; the lower layer is shaped like a three-step staircase, the middle layer is the central structure of the mausoleum including the body room and corridors, stairs. If you have the opportunity to go to Hanoi, please take some time to visit Uncle Ho. The ceremony to pay respect to President Ho Chi Minh is held on weekday mornings except Monday and Friday. Time from 8am-11am. No entrance fee. After lining up to visit, you will continue your journey to learn about Uncle Ho's former living places, such as the Stilt House, Fish Pond, Presidential Palace, etc. You should rent a self-guided tour guide to get detailed explanations of each location, the fee is 30,000 VND. At the end, you can continue to visit the One Pillar Pagoda and the Ho Chi Minh Museum, which are located on the same campus. Note that when you visit, you should dress politely, line up and not take photos or videos while visiting the mausoleum. Taking photos of the outside areas such as the Square and the Stilt House is also very beautiful, so remember to follow the rules so you don't get reminded.

President Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, also known as Uncle Ho's Mausoleum, is located at 2 Hung Vuong, Dien Bien Ward, Ba Dinh District, Hanoi City. The project is a place to preserve Uncle Ho's remains according to the wishes and sentiments of the Party Central Committee and the people. The mausoleum was started on September 2, 1973 and inaugurated on August 29, 1975. The mausoleum was built with 3 layers, 21.6 meters high and 41.2 meters wide. Below is a three-step staircase leading up to the central structure with the remains room and corridors and stairs. The top part is the mausoleum roof designed in a three-step shape. The outside of the mausoleum is covered with gray granite, surrounded by four rows of square granite columns and in the middle stands out the words "CHU TICH HO-CHI-MINH" in pink stone the color of ripe plum. The inside area is made of polished gray and red stone. The grounds around the mausoleum are planted with many species of trees and flowers typical of regions across the country.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum in Hanoi, Vietnam, is one of the most significant historical and cultural landmarks in the country. It houses the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh, the revolutionary leader and former president who played a key role in Vietnam's fight for independence. The mausoleum is located in Ba Dinh Square, where Ho Chi Minh declared Vietnam's independence in 1945.  Architecturally, the mausoleum is an imposing granite structure, influenced by Lenin’s Mausoleum in Moscow, yet it incorporates distinct Vietnamese touches. The solemn atmosphere reflects the reverence the Vietnamese people hold for their national hero.  Visitors, including foreigners, can enter the mausoleum and pay their respects. The experience is orderly and respectful, with a strict dress code and rules prohibiting photography inside. The line to enter is usually long, but it moves efficiently thanks to the disciplined guards.  If you get the chance to visit, entering the mausoleum is a profound experience, offering insight into Vietnam’s deep respect for Ho Chi Minh and its history. Keep in mind that the mausoleum is typically closed for maintenance for a few months each year (usually from September to November), so it's good to check ahead of time if you plan to go.

Admission is free, but visitors must adhere to the strict policies in place, including proper attire and respectful behavior.

Visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum was a profound and respectful experience. The site is very well maintained, with a solemn atmosphere that reflects the importance of Ho Chi Minh in Vietnamese history. The guards are strict but polite, and the line moves efficiently despite the large crowds. It's worth noting that photography is not allowed inside, and there's a dress code, so plan accordingly. The surrounding Ba Dinh Square and nearby attractions like the Presidential Palace and Ho Chi Minh’s stilt house add to the historical value of the visit. A must-see for anyone interested in Vietnam's past.

At the heart of the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is the marble stone chamber where President Ho Chi Minh's body is enshrined. Very nice place. We took pictures here. I had the opportunity to see the parliament of Vietnam. Nice place to take photographs & very nice view during evening.

Upon arriving at the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum – the heart of Hanoi – I felt deeply moved and awestruck. A long line of people silently queued, each carrying within them a profound reverence for the beloved leader of the nation. As I slowly walked inside, I was struck by the image of Uncle Ho resting peacefully in eternal sleep. The soft light and solemn atmosphere made me feel a sense of closeness and simplicity in him – just like the life he lived and dedicated to the nation. Although I had read much about Uncle Ho, seeing him in person truly made me deeply understand what patriotism, self-sacrifice, and the greatness of a man who dedicated his entire life to the nation meant. Leaving the Mausoleum, I still felt a profound mix of emotion and pride. Outside, the vast Ba Dinh Square and the lush green bamboo groves further enhanced the majestic and serene atmosphere of his resting place. Visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum was not only a memorable experience, but also a profound lesson about the principle of "drinking water, remembering the source" and gratitude towards those who sacrificed for the country.

A must-see historical landmark when visiting Hanoi. The Travel Station is about 10 minutes away – we usually suggest guests wake up early, walk to the Mausoleum, and then have breakfast at the Station. Arriving right on time for the flag-raising ceremony is even more impressive!

Uncle Ho's Mausoleum is a solemn place. Please pay attention to simple and discreet clothing. Avoid bringing too many unnecessary belongings. Absolutely silent and solemn during the visit here. There are guards to guide you in detail.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, located in the heart of Hanoi, is one of Vietnam's most important historical sites. Inaugurated in 1975, it is the final resting place of President Ho Chi Minh, the founder of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam.  The mausoleum's architecture is heavily influenced by a blend of modern and traditional styles. Constructed of granite and rectangular in shape, it exudes solemnity and dignity. Inside, visitors can admire the preserved remains of President Ho Chi Minh.  The surrounding grounds are beautifully designed with greenery, flowers, and supporting facilities such as the Ho Chi Minh Museum. It serves not only as a memorial but also as a historical education destination, helping people and tourists better understand the life and career of President Ho Chi Minh.  The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum attracts millions of visitors every year, serving as a symbol of patriotism and national pride, and expressing respect for the great leader of the Vietnamese nation.

In my opinion, the President's mausoleum is very beautiful and majestic. Many people visit every day, especially on weekends. So, if you have time to go during the week, you'll avoid the long queues, but if you're in a hurry, the surrounding area is still beautiful 😊 …

Me and Em had the honor to visit Uncle Ho's Mausoleum at 2 Hung Vuong Street, Dien Bien Ward, Ba Dinh District, Hanoi City. The mausoleum is located in a complex including Ba Dinh Square, the Presidential Palace, Uncle Ho's stilt house... This is where the body of President Ho Chi Minh, the outstanding leader of the nation, a world cultural celebrity, is kept. The mausoleum officially started construction on September 2, 1973 and was completed on August 19, 1975.  The itinerary to visit Uncle Ho's mausoleum is as follows: Uncle Ho's Mausoleum -> stilt house -> fish pond -> museum -> One Pillar Pagoda.  From the entrance, follow the instructions at 19 Ngoc Ha or you will see a large crowd of people lining up to visit. Once inside the mausoleum, follow the signs. Note that when you enter, you should keep order to visit and are not allowed to record or take photos. In addition, when visiting Uncle Ho's mausoleum, you cannot miss the solemn and sacred flag raising and lowering ceremony with the image of the red flag with a yellow star Only collects tickets for foreign visitors 25,000 VND/entrance and 25,000 VND to visit the stilt house. Uncle Ho's mausoleum is open every day of the week except Monday and Friday, if it coincides with a holiday, it is still open as usual. In addition, the opening hours of Uncle Ho's mausoleum also change depending on the season, weekdays, holidays and weekends.

Forever grateful to the great President Ho Chi Minh  I come from the South to visit Uncle Ho's Mausoleum, from a young age until now I have only seen Hanoi through books, photos, and the internet, so I really like it and always want to have the opportunity to come here and finally I have gone 4 times in 4 consecutive years and every time, the first place when I set foot in Hanoi is Uncle Ho's Mausoleum, solemn, heroic, and great are what I see when I am here.  Opposite Uncle Ho's Mausoleum is the National Assembly, the Presidential Palace, the Stilt House, and the Ho Chi Minh Museum, so the area is very large, I always come here in the afternoon so I have never visited all the places at the same time, I hope to have the opportunity to return to Hanoi soon to attend the Flag Raising Ceremony in the morning, as well as see the beauty of Uncle Ho's Mausoleum when it is lit up in the evening.

It is a very peaceful and beautiful place, be aware, there's no shade if you are going in the middle of the day, it will get very hot, also, be aware that the Mausoleum as well the museum has visiting hours (to go inside) for example Mondays are closed. You can go to the one pillar pagoda while you're here, also make sure you have a T-shirt, or long sleeve attire...

After entering you follow the path to the museum, foreigners pay 40k vnd per person, Vietnamese people enter without being charged which is a good thing 😄😄 u will see many belongings of HO CHI MINH his clothes, office, tools and books he used when he was alive, very interesting way of life, he loved children and his family, ha had a great way of life following great principles. He was a man of power and gained people’s hearts and respect. The museum closes at 16.30 😄

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, located in Ba Dinh Square, Hanoi, is one of Vietnam's most important historical landmarks, housing the remains of President Ho Chi Minh – the great leader of the nation. Construction began in 1973 and was completed in 1975, exuding solemnity and profound respect for Uncle Ho.  The mausoleum boasts a simple yet dignified architecture, with a large facade and stone steles commemorating historical events. The surrounding grounds are lush green gardens, creating a peaceful and tranquil atmosphere. Visitors not only admire the unique architecture but also have the opportunity to participate in solemn ceremonies, such as the morning flag-raising ceremony.  Inside the mausoleum, President Ho Chi Minh's remains are preserved in a glass coffin, always under strict security. To enter, visitors must adhere to dress code and conduct regulations to maintain the solemnity of the site. Besides the Mausoleum, the surrounding area also includes the Stilt House and Ho Chi Minh's Fish Pond, important relics associated with the life of President Ho Chi Minh.  The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is not only a tourist attraction but also a symbol of patriotism, national pride, and respect for the man who dedicated his entire life to the independence and freedom of the Vietnamese nation.
The most important place in Hanoi Vietnam. Been here at Weekends, packed with people (local or foreigner). Queue for around 2 hours to get inside mausoleum. Worth to visit anyways.

The President Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum  Besides the historical and cultural relics dating back thousands of generations, or the museums dating back hundreds of years, surely everyone wants to visit the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum once, a special and meaningful project for each Vietnamese people.  The mausoleum was officially started on September 2, 1973, at the location of the old platform in the middle of Ba Dinh Square, where Ho Chi Minh used to chair important meetings. After 2 years of construction with the consensus and consensus of the whole nation, Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum was inaugurated. Welcoming President Ho Chi Minh (Uncle Ho) to the Mausoleum, the task of long-term preservation and absolute safety of President Ho body moved to a new stage. Uncle Ho's body is protected and preserved in a large area, every day thousands of people come to visit Uncle.  Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a cultural work with profound political significance for the revolutionary cause of the Party, State and people, and has practical value for moral education, lifestyle, personality for each Vietnamese citizen. ---------- Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum  Besides the historical and cultural relics that have existed for thousands of years, or the museums that have existed for hundreds of years, surely everyone wants to visit Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum at least once, a special and meaningful project for every Vietnamese person.  The mausoleum was officially started on September 2, 1973, at the location of the old stage in the middle of Ba Dinh Square, where Ho Chi Minh used to preside over important meetings. After 2 years of construction with the consensus of the entire nation, Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum was inaugurated. Welcoming President Ho Chi Minh to the Mausoleum, the task of preserving and absolutely protecting Uncle Ho's body entered a new phase.
