I live in the South, and have only seen Uncle Ho's mausoleum through books, newspapers, TV, etc. The first time I came here, I felt excited and proud. I went on the weekend so it was very crowded, but the queue was quick and everyone was very orderly and serious. Opposite you can see the Vietnamese National Assembly building.

I heard that the flags would be hoisted around 6 am, so I woke up early at 5:30, brushed my teeth and washed my face to see it before returning to Saigon, and it was definitely worth seeing! 🌸🌸👍👍 In the morning, you'll see elderly people getting up early to exercise, and the weather is cool and pleasant! When entering the Mausoleum, you'll be asked to go through a security check, but don't worry if you're not carrying anything inappropriate! The Mausoleum is quite cold, and the viewing time is quite short because people aren't allowed to stand still and have to keep moving. 😭 Overall, this is an experience you should try at least once if you go to Hanoi. 🫶🫶

I was on a business trip to Hanoi and took advantage of the morning to jog here. Because my jogging pants were a bit short, I couldn't get close to the mausoleum. The flag was raised at 6:00 am, in the winter it was 6:30 am. The atmosphere was very solemn and proud. In the morning, many people came here to exercise.

As a citizen of the Capital, since my elementary school years, my school has organized for me to visit Uncle Ho's Mausoleum every year. Now that I'm an adult, every time I pass by Uncle Ho's Mausoleum, I still feel moved and proud. Although I've seen it many times, Uncle Ho's Mausoleum always has a beauty that is nowhere else to be found.
I was out of sync because I went during the renovation. Walking around the mausoleum was also very beautiful and meaningful. I didn't get to meet Uncle Ho, so I'll see you another time.

Wow, this is really a "must visit" if visiting Hanoi. Free entry, you pass through security and then follow the one way route upto and through the Mausoleum. Make sure you dress appropriately or you won't be allowed in; no shorts and wear long sleeves. This building is austere and imposing, it commands your respect and reverence. Entering the chamber you must continue moving around the three sides on silence. His body is amazingly preserved and clearly viewed through the glass sided coffin. Four guards stand motionless in their pristine white uniforms at each corner of the sunken pit where the coffin resides, and another five guards attend the walkway and ensure you keep moving. No headwear allowed in the chamber and obviously no photos/video.

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum Opening Hours:  Hot season (April – October): 7:30 – 10:30 AM  Cold season (November – March): 8:00 – 11:00 AM  The Mausoleum is usually closed on Mondays and Fridays, and open on Tuesdays, Wednesdays, Thursdays, Saturdays, and Sundays.  Solemn & Meaningful: The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum evokes a very solemn, peaceful, and respectful atmosphere. Visitors will feel the reverence before an important historical figure.

When visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, everyone must strictly follow the regulations: Have a serious attitude, dress neatly, cleanly, and tidily; Must check in luggage and go through the security check gate; Allow to bring a handbag containing money, precious metals, phones, small cameras with the power off, but do not bring specialized cameras and cameras.  On the way to visit, you must follow the instructions of the Organizing Committee, do not jostle or push. When arriving at the entrance of the Mausoleum, everyone should hold their hat, hold the hat in their right hand, with the inside of the hat facing outwards.  When entering the Mausoleum, do not make noise, do not point, touch the wall, do not put your hands in your pockets, do not smoke. It is forbidden to film, take photos, draw the room where President Ho Chi Minh's body is placed in the Mausoleum and post these images on the mass media.

Even though I went there when I was very young, I still can't forget that feeling. The only difference is that I got to visit Uncle Ho's mausoleum in the morning. This was the first time in my life that I experienced the flag lowering ceremony, a night in Hanoi that was neither too cold nor too hot. In the solemn atmosphere from the people to the guards in the mausoleum, I felt an indescribable sense of peace.
This is the resting place of President Ho Chi Minh. It is located on the historic Ba Dinh Square. Everyone should come early at 5am for the morning flag raising and salute ceremony.

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum opens in the morning on Tuesdays, Wednesdays, Thursdays, Saturdays, and Sundays every week. No visiting ceremonies are held in the afternoon. No visiting ceremonies are held on Mondays and Fridays. On May 19, September 2, and the first day of the Lunar New Year, if they fall on Mondays or Fridays, a visit to President Ho Chi Minh will still be held.  Note the regulations when visiting Uncle Ho's Mausoleum. Visitors must strictly follow the rules to ensure the solemnity of the relic site:  - Do not take pictures and keep your phone on silent mode.  - Dress neatly, cleanly, and tidily.  - Security check: Check your luggage and only bring a handbag, phone, and small camera that has been turned off. Specialized cameras and cameras are not allowed in.  - Follow the instructions of the organizing committee, avoid jostling and pushing.  - Respect when coming to the Mausoleum: Hold your hat or cap in your right hand, with the cap facing outwards. - Maintain order and silence: Do not make noise, do not point, do not touch the wall, do not put your hands in your pockets. - Smoking is prohibited. - Filming, photography or drawing in the body room of President Ho Chi Minh is prohibited.

We entered the mausoleum following a young father with his little son. We felt a deep respect for Ho Chi Minh and the people who go there today, who  are  proud of their past!  Being there on Tet was great!

It was a joy to visit President Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum around his birthday, May 19th. The day I went, there were many children – both foreigners and Vietnamese – paying their respects. I was very happy and moved by the sight of so many young children, holding hands and clinging to each other's clothes, respectfully visiting President Ho Chi Minh. In fact, many schools organized visits that day. I'm very happy that the schools facilitated this for the children. These future generations will grow up to be talented and patriotic, with love for President Ho Chi Minh, the Party, and the people. Then Vietnam will develop further, standing shoulder to shoulder with the world's leading nations, as President Ho Chi Minh hoped.
Flag salute ceremony: (April 1st - Oct 31st: summer time, start at 6:00 AM) (Nov 1st - March 31s: winter time, start at 6:30 AM)

The first time I came to Hanoi, it was also the first time I visited Uncle Ho's Mausoleum. It was so beautiful and proud. Around Uncle Ho's Mausoleum, there are many buildings to visit such as the building to welcome heads of state, Uncle Ho's office, Ho Chi Minh museum, National Assembly building...

Nice way to start your morning in Hanoi. Come here early as it closes sharp @ 11:30 am. Have to leave your baggage at the entrance., it’s safe and free. They will give you a token. Long pants and decent attire a must. Whole place has a very solemn vibe and rightly so, please respect that by keeping your voice low and phone on silent. Entry is free and it’s a nice walk around a vast complex

The Ho Chi Minh Museum is a meaningful and proud place, especially for Vietnamese people like me. Coming here, I feel the respect for President Ho Chi Minh, who devoted his whole life to fighting for national independence and freedom. The artifacts and exhibitions in the museum clearly show his great contributions and the difficult years that our nation has gone through.   However, despite its many historical values, the museum needs some improvements. Some exhibition areas are old and somewhat outdated, not lively enough to attract visitors, especially the younger generation. In addition, the space of some galleries is quite narrow and lacks light.   However, I still think this is an indispensable destination for anyone who wants to learn about national history and precious cultural values. Ho Chi Minh Museum is a place that makes me feel proud of what the nation has gone through and the sacrifices that previous generations have made for the country. If the museum is upgraded and renewed, I believe that this will be a place that attracts many domestic and foreign tourists.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, also known as the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum or Uncle Ho Mausoleum, is the resting place of President Ho Chi Minh's remains. Construction officially began on September 2, 1973, on the site of the former ceremonial platform in Ba Dinh Square, where Ho Chi Minh presided over important meetings.  More than 15,000 people visit the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum each week. Many individuals and organizations also visit on Vietnamese holidays and important anniversaries.  The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a monument of immense significance, reflecting the deep affection of the Vietnamese people for their leader. Since its inauguration in 1975, generations of Vietnamese people, individuals and organizations, along with millions of foreign visitors, have visited the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. With its clean, beautiful, and majestic surroundings, the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum and the entire Ba Dinh Square area are worthy of being the cultural center of Hanoi.  The mausoleum does not charge an entrance fee, and visitors are required to adhere to requirements such as dressing respectfully, not bringing cameras or mobile phones with video recording or photography functions, turning off their phones, not bringing food or drinks, and maintaining order inside the mausoleum.  As of 2012, nearly 50 million people had visited the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, including over 8 million international visitors. For visitors from afar, accommodation is thoughtfully and attentively arranged. Meals, refreshments, protection from the cold, and services for severely wounded soldiers and the elderly are all carefully planned and organized. The reception process has been improved, such as renovating the waiting areas into two spacious sections, allowing visitors to rest, admire the flowers and plants, and watch documentary films before entering. For groups of amputated soldiers, honor guards always have special wheelchairs ready to assist them. The viewing room is divided into two rows; the inner row, closer to where the remains are placed, is reserved for children.  The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is open five days a week, on Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Saturday, and Sunday mornings. During the hot season (from April 1st to October 31st): from 7:30 AM to 10:30 AM; during the cold season (from November 1st to March 31st of the following year): from 8:00 AM to 11:00 AM; on holidays, Saturdays, and Sundays, it is open for an additional 30 minutes. The Mausoleum is closed annually for two months: October and November for scheduled maintenance. If May 19th, September 2nd, and the first day of the Lunar New Year fall on a Monday or Friday, the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is still open for visits.  The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is currently located at the site of the former ceremonial platform in the middle of Ba Dinh Square, where President Ho Chi Minh once presided over rallies. To reach the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, you can travel by private vehicle or public transport, both are very convenient.  If traveling by private vehicle, please note these two parking spots: Ong Ich Khiem Street, opposite the Mausoleum Protection Command Ngoc Ha Street, at the entrance to the Ho Chi Minh Museum If choosing to take the bus, you can refer to some routes that pass by Ba Dinh Square as follows: Bus No. 09: Hoan Kiem Lake – Hoan Kiem Lake Bus No. 33: Yen Nghia Bus Station – Xuan Dinh Bus No. 22: Gia Lam Bus Station – Kim Ma Bus No. 45: Times City – Nam Thang Long Bus Station Bus No. 50: Long Bien – National Stadium

Great piece of history and well maintained. Entry is cheap, and while security here is high, they will guide you if you walk in the wrong spot and can also be friendly and say hello. Beautiful location worth the cheap entry

This is the resting place of beloved Uncle Ho - the father of the nation. The atmosphere here is solemn and respectful. Every day, many Vietnamese people, foreign tourists and domestic and foreign delegations visit the mausoleum to pay their respects to Uncle Ho. In the mausoleum grounds, you can also visit Uncle Ho's residence and workplace, his artifacts, visit the One Pillar Pagoda, and visit the museum. The mausoleum is only open on Tuesdays, Wednesdays, Thursdays, and Saturday mornings (from 7:30 to 11:00). Mondays and Fridays are closed for maintenance.

This was my first time in Hanoi, but I had always wanted to visit Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. It was a great honor and a sacred occasion. The mausoleum is very majestic, solidly and solemnly built. The area where Uncle Ho lived and worked is so simple and rustic. The scenery is wonderful; the garden has many green trees and ancient trees, and there's a fish pond where Uncle Ho kept fish. We will forever remember and be grateful to him, the great father of the Vietnamese nation...
A very meaningful place to visit and tell history to children. Children love it. The air here is always cool and airy.

A must do experience but very crowded on a Saturday morning. Hats, sunglasses, cameras, and cell phones may not be worn or used inside.  Luckily, our tour guide had some connections to get us in the VIP line which only took 30 minutes, rather than 90 minutes.

The entrance for individual tourists is near the intersection of Le Hong Phong Street and Ngoc Ha Street (group tours are at a different intersection), and there is a bus stop nearby. Security checks and lockers are available at the entrance. No ticket is required, and visitors can simultaneously tour the mausoleum, former residence, and square. Electronic devices surrendered at the entrance can be retrieved at the exit. Although there are no places to sit and rest throughout the tour, the large number of elderly people and children in the queues demonstrates the people's reverence for Ho Chi Minh.

I happened to visit during the holiday and saw so many children taking the tour bus, which reminded me of my elementary school field trips to the Taoyuan Daxi Mausoleum. If you have time, experiencing this place would be quite nice.

The body of Ho Chi Minh, the founding father of Vietnam, is laid to rest here and can be viewed in its preserved state. Security is very strict, traffic flow is strictly controlled from the entrance to the exit, and you are not allowed to stop, so the viewing time is short. Plastic bottles must be left at the entrance.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a must-see when visiting Hanoi. And what's more, admission is free. To be honest, that alone was a surprise.  I recommend visiting as early as possible. Standing in line in the clear morning air, surrounded by a solemn atmosphere, felt more like an experience than a tourist spot. Security was tight, and private conversations were strictly prohibited. The sense of tension quietly conveyed just how special this place is for Vietnam.  The body of President Ho Chi Minh, seen inside, was more lifelike than I'd imagined; it took my breath away. It resembled a wax figure, yet somehow different. It had such presence that it felt as if he might open his eyes and rise at any moment, and it made me feel like my spine was standing tall. Even though it was only a short time, it was a deeply memorable experience.  While this may be a place that divides opinion, visiting here is significant in understanding the country of Vietnam. It goes beyond being a tourist destination and offers a place to quietly engage with history and philosophy. This is a spot I highly recommend visiting early in the morning, with plenty of time to spare.
I could only see it from afar across the square, but I could feel the atmosphere. I tried to enter from the north side of the square, but I couldn't. I still don't know where to enter!

We hadn't planned on going, but since we passed by, we went in and thought it was well worth a visit. The mausoleum is very cool, very well organized, and impressive how they preserved Ho Chi Minh's body! The surrounding garden with its beautiful benzathine trees is also lovely.
Those wearing white are unmarried men inside the park. Those in green are maintaining security on the outer perimeter. Those in khaki are traffic police.

Standing in the square in front of the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, we listened as our guide, Xiao Meng, recounted Vietnam's history and the life story of Ho Chi Minh. He led Vietnam in its struggle for national independence and reunification, becoming the Father of the Nation and a highly revered leader. ​He dedicated his entire life to the country, remaining unmarried and committing himself to national liberation. He was the principal founder of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam and held positions such as Chairman of the Workers' Party of Vietnam and first President of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam. He was also one of the founders of the Communist Party of Vietnam, developing it into the core force leading the Vietnamese Revolution. Furthermore, he led the Vietnamese people through the First Indochina War (against the French) and the Second Indochina War (the War against the Americans to save the nation), laying the groundwork for the ultimate realization of national reunification. ​Even though many years have passed since his death, he remains the spiritual leader of the Vietnamese people, and his thoughts and sayings profoundly influence contemporary Vietnam. After his Passing by, his mausoleum was erected in North Vietnam, the Ho Chi Minh Museum was built in Central Vietnam, and the former South Vietnamese city of Saigon was renamed Ho Chi Minh City, symbolizing the unity of North, Central, and South Vietnam.  As Xiao Meng spoke, he shed tears. Listening to the history of countless invasions and the tearful saga of division that the Vietnamese nation endured, I also understood why photos and portraits of Ho Chi Minh are so often seen in Hanoi and how deeply the Vietnamese people revered him.  Arriving at the square in front of the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, listening to our guide Xiao Meng recount the history of Vietnam and the deeds of Mr. Ho Chi Minh, who led Vietnam in its struggle for national independence and reunification, becoming the Father of the Nation and a highly respected leader. He dedicated his life to the nation, never marrying and working tirelessly for national liberation. He was a principal founder of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam, serving as Chairman of the Workers' Party of Vietnam and the first President of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam. He was also one of the founders of the Communist Party of Vietnam, developing it into the core force leading the Vietnamese revolution. He led the Vietnamese people in the wars against French and American aggression, laying the foundation for the eventual reunification of the country.  Although he has been dead for many years, he remains a spiritual leader of the Vietnamese people. His thoughts and words profoundly influence contemporary Vietnam. Even after his death, his mausoleum was erected in North Vietnam, the Ho Chi Minh Museum was built in Central Vietnam, and Saigon, formerly part of South Vietnam, was renamed Ho Chi Minh City, symbolizing the unification of North, Central, and South Vietnam.  As Xiao Meng spoke, tears welled up in her eyes. Listening to Vietnam's history of countless invasions and divisions, I understood why photos and portraits of Ho Chi Minh are frequently seen in Hanoi, and how much the Vietnamese people admire him.

A place worth visiting. This is where Ho Chi Minh rests. There's a security check at the entrance, and military personnel are guarding the upper floors of the mausoleum. President Ho's face remains serene, offering the public a moment of veneration. A place worth visiting.

The Mausoleum houses the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh; an impressive experience, like touching history firsthand. No admission fee is required, but you must enter before 11:00 a.m. Security is quite strict, with metal detectors and backpacks not allowed inside. The complex also houses other interesting features, such as the house where "Uncle Ho" lived.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum in Ba Dinh Square, Hanoi, is the tomb of Vietnamese leader Ho Chi Minh. Built of gray granite, it was inspired by Lenin's Mausoleum in Moscow and incorporates Vietnamese style.
It was a place that seemed majestic, spread out over a wide plain, and the soldiers(?) who were constantly guarding it made it clear that it was an important location.
You can see Ho Chi Minh's body up close. It's a moving experience to be able to see such an important figure in history.

I visited the coffin on the Saturday a week before National Day. It was crowded with visitors, and locals dressed in traditional folk clothing, creating a wonderful atmosphere.  Google data indicates that it's open Tuesday through Thursday and Saturday and Sunday from 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM, with entry closed after 10:15 AM. I arrived at 9:30 AM and finished by 10:45 AM.  There was a security checkpoint. Wearing tank tops that expose the arms, shorts above the knee, large backpacks, water bottles, and food required storage. It's advisable to avoid these items (although there are vendors selling long pants at the entrance). Storage is time-consuming, and you need to return to the original location after the visit; there's no flexible departure option.  President Hu's face was kind and serene; it's worth seeing.

My water bottle was flagged during baggage inspection. When it was returned later, the baggage claim number was written on it (lol). It washed off with detergent when I got home.  The inside is spacious, but it gets stuffy in hot weather.

An iconic and solemn place for the Vietnamese people, the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum (Lăng Chủ tịch Hồ Chí Minh) is a site of enormous historical and symbolic importance for Vietnam, as it is where the communist revolution began. The solemn architecture, including well-kept parks, a pagoda, and government buildings, including the modern parliament, is located here. The annual parade on September 2nd, the day of independence from France, is held here.

The building itself isn't very large, but its interior is very impressive, especially when you see Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body. They don't allow photos of the front, much less of the interior, so I've only added one. Admission is by group. It's located in a huge complex where the Vietnamese leader used to live, now a museum complex.

Here, we don't laugh at the memory of Uncle Ho. Search, line up, formal attire required. We enter the mausoleum and are not allowed to stop, nor to photograph or film the embalmed remains of the father of the nation, which are in a glass cage guarded by guards. It's an interesting sight for a foreigner.
After exploring the main square, there's another area you can enter with a ticket; I recommend it.

Open to the public in the morning, I recommend arriving before 7:30. You'll pass through security. Avoid water bottles; selfie sticks will be confiscated if you have one, and you'll get them back upon exit. Proper attire is required: long pants and a t-shirt. Getting inside is fairly quick. Here is a recommendation tour plan for 8 hours when you visit Ha Noi:13:00-13:30: Arriving to President Ho Chi Minh's mausoleum13:30-14:30: Visiting Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, Museum, and One Pillar Pagoda (note to dress politely and properly)14:30-15:30: Visiting the Temple of Literature15:30-17:00: Visiting Hoa Lo Prison17:00-18:00: Choosing to enjoy dinner with the dishes suggested by the tour18:00 - 19:15: Moving to watch the  Quintessence of Tonkin show19:15- 19:30: Buying tickets and check-in 19:30-20:45: Enjoying the Quintessence of Tonkin show20:45: End of schedule.I/ A few descriptions of the above locations:1. Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum, located in Ba Dinh Square, Hanoi, is one of Vietnam's most important historical landmarks. It serves as the resting place of President Ho Chi Minh, the beloved leader who led the country to independence and unification. Constructed from 1973 to 1975, the mausoleum's architecture reflects the simplicity and solemnity of his spirit. Inside, President Ho Chi Minh's body is preserved in a special glass chamber, attracting millions of visitors annually. This site is not only a symbol of historical significance but also an educational space for future generations.2.The Ho Chi Minh Museum, located in Hanoi, is a grand tribute to the life and legacy of President Ho Chi Minh. Opened in 1990, the museum is designed to resemble a lotus, symbolizing purity and the revered leader's modesty. It houses a vast collection of artifacts, photographs, and documents that chronicle Ho Chi Minh's journey from his early life, revolutionary activities, to his leadership in Vietnam's struggle for independence. Through its exhibits, the museum offers visitors profound insights into the history of Vietnam and the enduring influence of Uncle Ho's ideals and vision for the nation.3. One Pillar Pagoda, located in Hanoi, is one of Vietnam's most iconic Buddhist temples. Built in 1049 during the Ly Dynasty, it stands on a single stone pillar, resembling a lotus blossom rising from a pond. The unique architecture symbolizes purity, akin to a lotus blooming in muddy water. Originally constructed to honor the bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, the pagoda has become a symbol of Vietnam's rich cultural heritage. Despite being destroyed and rebuilt over the centuries, it remains a beloved landmark and a testament to the country's enduring spiritual traditions.4.The Temple of Literature - Quoc Tu Giam, in central Hanoi, is one of Vietnam's most significant historical and cultural sites. Built in 1070 during the Ly dynasty to honor Confucius, it became Vietnam's first university in 1076, training many talented individuals. This site symbolizes Vietnam's dedication to education and meritocracy. Its five distinct areas include Van Mieu Mon, Dai Trung Mon, Khue Van Cac, Bai Duong, and Hau Palace. A highlight is the 82 stone steles of doctors from the 15th to 18th centuries, recognized by UNESCO. Today, it's a venue for cultural and educational events and a place where students pray for exam success, showcasing Vietnam's long-standing respect for education.5.Hoa Lo Prison, centrally located in Hanoi, stands as a poignant symbol of Vietnam's history. Built by the French in 1896, this "Maison Centrale" initially held revolutionaries opposing colonial rule. Known as the "Hanoi Hilton" during the Vietnam War, it detained captured American pilots, including Senator John McCain. Despite its grim past, today it houses a historical museum displaying documents and artifacts from different eras. Attracting numerous visitors, the site highlights Vietnam's struggle and resilience, educating future generations about patriotism and the indomitable spirit of its people.6."The Quintessence of Tonkin" is a unique art program that authentically recreates the culture, history, and life of the Northern Delta region of Vietnam. Performed on an outdoor stage near Thay Pagoda, it attracts a wide audience. The program features a large water stage blending with nature, enhanced by modern lighting, creating a vibrant artistic space. It comprises six chapters depicting various aspects of Northern culture and history through dance, folk music, and modern theater techniques. Combining tradition and modernity, it utilizes lighting and projection technology alongside traditional elements like ao dai costumes and ethnic musical instruments. This program not only honors traditional values but also serves as a profound cultural lesson, promoting Vietnamese heritage and pride.II/ Transporting Recommendation:Travel by private rental car or book a taxi on the Xanh SM app (when visitors experience the quality of Xanh SM services, they will also learn more about innovation in using public transportation in Vietnam when traveling with a car company manufactured by Vietnamese people, it also helps save travel costs)III/ Recommended Cuisine:1. Cha Ca Thang Long Restaurant (21 Duong Thanh, Hoan Kiem, Hanoi) is renowned for its unique grilled fish, a symbol of Hanoi's culinary culture. Fresh fish, usually lentil or fruit fish, is marinated with turmeric and traditional spices, then grilled and stir-fried with dill and spring onion. Served with vermicelli, grilled rice paper, roasted peanuts, herbs, and shrimp paste with lemon and chili, this dish attracts both local and international diners eager to experience Vietnamese cuisine.2. Bun Dau Hang Khay (31 Hang Khay, Hoan Kiem, Hanoi) is a traditional dish and a must-visit for vermicelli with tofu and shrimp paste lovers. Located near Hoan Kiem Lake, it attracts locals and tourists with its fresh ingredients: crispy fried tofu, soft vermicelli, fragrant boiled meat, and signature spring rolls. The shrimp paste dipping sauce, seasoned with lemon, chili, and hot fat, creates an unforgettable flavor. Served on bamboo trays, the small, bustling eateries offer an authentic Hanoi atmosphere. For a true taste of old town cuisine, Hang Khay vermicelli and tofu is a top choice.3. Bat Dan beef pho (49 Bat Dan, Hoan Kiem district, Hanoi) is a traditional Vietnamese dish beloved worldwide. Originating from Hanoi, it features soft rice noodles, thinly sliced beef (often rare or thin), and a clear, sweet broth simmered from beef bones with spices like anise, cinnamon, herbs, ginger, and onions. This dish symbolizes the sophistication of Vietnamese cuisine, blending ingredients meticulously. Starting your morning with a hot bowl of beef pho promises a delightful experience.4. Co Vietnamese Restaurant & Vegan (21 Hang Can, Hoan Kiem, Hanoi) specializes in serving international guests with fresh, typical Vietnamese dishes. The menu features traditional flavors, including both classic dishes and unique vegetarian options. Whether you're exploring Hanoi's bustling streets or seeking a cozy dining spot, Co Vietnamese Restaurant offers a warm, inviting atmosphere for a memorable meal.IV/ Favorable weather and travel time:- Time: Because Hanoi's weather changes with the seasons, the best time to visit is in the fall and spring (from September to November and March to May).- The weather is comfortable, moderately sunny, not too hot or cold, and suitable for outdoor activities.
