If you love history, I strongly recommend a guided tour here. The whole complex is filled with architectural gems, incredible stories and beautiful gardens. Lying in state for over 50 years I feel that his spirit remains strong. It is obvious why Uncle Ho is still revered today and that successive government remain open armed to the rest of the world. In the same way that dispersed Vietnamese people across the world continue to charm us all, made us curious enough to find out more. I learnt more on this day, then from any book, film or documentary. The highlight was to see quite a few war veterans in full uniform with their comrades still paying homage.

Standing before the grand structure in Hanoi, we felt a profound connection to Vietnam's history. The solemn atmosphere inside compelled us to reflect on the nation's journey. It's a humbling experience we'd recommend to anyone seeking cultural insight.

A landmark and historic visit to the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. The atmosphere is full of respect and reflection. We were impressed by the fervour of the Vietnamese coming to mourn the remains of their national hero. An experience to live to understand the importance of "Uncle Ho" in Vietnamese culture.

We arrived early to witness the changing of the guards - what an impressive ceremony! The atmosphere is incredibly reverent, and although the visit was brief, it gave us profound insights into Vietnam's deep respect for their revolutionary leader.

Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a place that every Vietnamese should visit at least once in their life. The solemnity and respect from the time of lining up until entering the mausoleum made me feel grateful and respectful towards Uncle Ho. The surrounding scenery with the large Ba Dinh Square and historical buildings made for a meaningful trip

The solemn atmosphere and the respect of thousands of people visiting Uncle Ho's Mausoleum made me feel proud and moved. The line of people was long but moved very orderly. This is a destination that every Vietnamese person should visit at least once in their life.
As an American, I am always looking for new experiences and challenges. I love to be active, explore, and enjoy exciting activities, from sports to adventure travel.

Username: Hoi An Free Walking tour & Cycling tour
Comment: Such a Good place to learn about the life of President Ho Chi Minh and Vietnamese history. You should avoid to visit this place at the weekend  because a lot Vietnamese  people come here to show their respect and love to President Ho Chi Minh. HCM mausoleum is the place where VIP of the other countries must visit when they have meetings in HaNoi.  Next to the mausoleum,  you can buy a ticket to visit the place where President Ho Chi Minh use to live and work.
It is a sacred place for the Vietnamese people and world tourists in general. The surrounding landscape is solemn but also poetic and full of love

This symbol of Vietnamese independence really touched us. The solemn atmosphere and respect of the local visitors were palpable. We were fascinated by the imposing architecture and the guard of honor. Don’t miss to understand the soul of modern Vietnam.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a historical landmark and important symbol of Hanoi, the final resting place of President Ho Chi Minh. The space is solemn, the architectural design impressive and inspiring. Visitors queue in an orderly manner, receiving attentive guidance, ensuring respect. Visiting the Mausoleum is an opportunity to remember and honor the great leader of the Vietnamese nation.

We were struck by the solemn atmosphere and the reverence shown by locals and tourists alike. The imposing structure houses the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh, Vietnam's revolutionary leader. We learned about his significant role in the country's history and independence. The mausoleum's strict rules and meticulous maintenance reflect its status as a national treasure. It's an educational experience that offers insight into Vietnam's cultural and political landscape.

This impressive monument, dedicated to Uncle Ho, offers a peaceful atmosphere despite its historical importance. The imposing architecture and the guard of honour ceremony create an atmosphere of profound respect. We enjoyed the order and tranquility that prevails in the complex. The surrounding garden adds a touch of serenity to the whole. For couples interested in Vietnamese history, this site is unmissable.
This mausoleum reminds us of personal sacrifice..goal and human struggle for independence. Open square for parade helps to do reflective thinking. A must visit
This was really cool.  Stand in line for (if you are lucky) about 30 min and go in and see Ho Chi Minh in his final resting place.  He looks great for being dead for 70 years!

Might be classed as a little morbid, but the Vietnamese have so much respect for him and so he could be with his people and they can still see him, his body is preserved in a glass case and once a year the people embark on paying their respect to him. We had to queue but the guards have it all under control and everyone is more than happy to wait their turn. We queued for about 45 mins in total.

We went very early as it was extremely hot . Did have to queue for a long time . However the line kept moving . As we entered the resting place of Ho Chi Minh it was darker and cooler . I was told off for talking loud to my wife by a guard . However upon reflection realised it was in fact disrespectful . As we walked (shuffled) past his body both of us found it very intense and moving . Would definitely recommend this to anyone as a ""do not miss""
The mausoleum is currently closed, but the whole area is impressively beautiful and revealing . It provides a deep insight into the history of this proud people and their Uncle Ho.
Must visit site! It is in Old Quarter and walking distance from any hotels you stay in this area. When we were there in early May 2024, the entrance was free. Usually, entrance fee is very minimal.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is an amazing place. We went there early morning, as it is meant to be open only for a few hours early in the day. The day of our visit was a special one as it fell on a public holiday and thousands of visitors had already lined up by the time we arrived (around 9.00am)! The whole operation was conducted with military precision! Visitors had to line up, single file, and be exactly behind one another! Officials with loudspeakers were in charge. No bags or water could be taken and had to be left in storage for free and collected at exiting. Water fountains could be seen at various spots throughout and cups were provided, however we did not take up this opportunity! It was a very long walk around the whole area before we eventually reached the room with Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body, which was placed in what seemed to be a horse carriage, with his upper body slightly elevated so he could be seen easily by visitors. No photos were allowed to be taken inside. It was only a few seconds of viewing as we had to keep walking. It was very special and a moving moment. The Mausoleum is a beautiful building and photos of the building can be taken from a distance outside. The place is steeped in history and we could sense locals had a deep regard for "Uncle Ho" as they call him.

Wildly busy at weekends and closed Monday and Friday so we went for the opening time of 0730 on Tuesday morning (it’s only open 0730-1100 in summer). The queue was about 200 yards long. Predictably, everything ran like clockwork and we were moved forward to the door just before 0730. We were divided into two adjacent lines (tip: left-hand line is closer to the body). Total silence, guards everywhere, in you walk, keep moving, no photos and out by 0738. Amazing. And it’s free.Then into the stilt house (40000VND) and out by 0800. We didn’t bother with the HCM museum.Shock! When we left there was no queue at all so we could have had another 30 minutes in bed.Make sure you are dressed appropriately; no uncovered shoulders and no knees showing.Another freebie that we enjoyed was the lowering of the flag at 2100. Get there at 2045. You can also see the raising of the flag at 0600.
A piece of history! Went on a sunday when nearly all Vietnamese coming to see the founder of their current nation. Very interesting.

I would just say that this is well worth a visit for a sense of history and the legend that is interwoven in the culture of this country. Plus, being communist, its free....If the queue looks ENORMOUS when you get there, take heart! It is so well managed by a plethora of police and military, that it takes so much less time than you may initially think. It took us 45 minutes, from halfway down the road outside of the start of the queue to being in the mausoleum itself. And when you emerge, staff are on hand to offer free water and snacks! Who would have thought?

Had always wanted to visit Ho Chi Minhs Mausoleum. First time we tried it was a Sunday, don't try on a Sunday totally packed ques massive. Finally got there on a Tuseday as they close on Monday's. Super sureal. Its free to enter and the ques though longish move well. Once you are in the Mausoleum its a 30 second walk past to view Ho Chi Minh, but surprisingly satisfying, in a strange way.  Be respectful in what you wear, plenty of info on do's and don'ts.

I visited this destination at night and i was a great place to refresh some cool breeze, i do love the massive and green square grass. Next time I will visit it on daylight to have a chance to come in to the mausolem....
Visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum was a somber but powerful experience. It offered a chance to learn about Vietnam's history and pay respect to a revered leader.
We paid our respects at the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. The somber atmosphere reminded us of Vietnam's historic struggles. A place to learn and appreciate the sacrifices made for Vietnamese independence.

Through Booking.com , I was able to book a walking tour with a  Vietnamese college student. Cost for this service was only for $5.00. Entrance to the Complex is quite restrict. We had to leave our backpack, handbags and personal stuffs at the Entrance Lockers. We were ushered in walkways that have roofs before entering the Mausoleum. Security Guards kept reminding us NOT to  take photos and keep moving. The body is an enclosed glass compartment,  with 4 guards standing around it. There are more security guards along the way. In the wide, open space infront of the mausoleum were several groups of school children who also visited the Mausoeleum. Other attractions in the Complex include the HCM Museum, Pagoda Shrine, Gardens,  Palace (closed during my visit). Recommended to visit them as well.Note: Visiting days and hours to the Mausoleum are limited. Entrance is FREE, consult with the locals regarding their opening and closing hours.Picture- taking at the other attractions in the Complex is allowed, NOT just inside the Mausoleum!

One of the tourist attraction in Hanoi. The place is magnificent. I did not visit inside because it was already noon (closed by 11 am). The next morning we saw the lines of people wanting to get there, my tour guide said it takes 2 hours in line to get inside. We took nice pictures just outside

Visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum in Hanoi is a memorable and poignant experience. The mausoleum, which is an iconic monument, has deep spiritual significance for the Vietnamese people. Inside, a solemn atmosphere and an aura of respect surround the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh.A strict dress code must be followed and respect for the location must be maintained. Be prepared to queue as the mausoleum is a popular attraction. A visit to the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is not just a tourist activity. This is an opportunity to bear witness to a historical figure and pay tribute to a leader who left an indelible mark on Vietnam's history.
A respectful memorial to the leader who unified the nation. Amazing legacy: overcoming foreign control. Highly revered now and an important place for understanding today’s Viet Nam.

Visited in March. The weather was perfect. Was warm but not hot.  Lot's of people and school children but the lines go fast so dont let that intimate you. The lines stop at 11AM as it closes by 11:30AM. Closed Monday and Friday.Amazing how well preserved this guy is.  Is it plastination? Is it a wax figure? Who knows but word is he is taken to Russia for touch ups every year.  Has Russia found the fountain of youth for the dead?

The detailed sculptures were an inspiring sight, each one meticulously maintained. The mausoleum stands as a testament to the resilience and spirit of the Vietnamese people. A visit to this iconic landmark is a must for any solo traveler seeking to delve deeper into the rich tapestry of Vietnam’s past.

It was a fun day, the atmosphere was beautiful, and there was a very wonderful organization on the website to visit this remarkable person and the great leader who led the country to freedom. He deserves all the appreciation, reverence and respect.

Be prepared for long lines. They do move pretty quickly. The mausoleum is based on one in Russia. Basically you go into a cooled building and file past Ho Chi Minh’s body. The interesting part is seeing how much he is loved and revered by his people. When you come out you can see the Presidential Palace he refused to live in. Then go to house no 54 where he did live  for 4 years, which is basically the servants quarters for the presidential palace. Then you can go through the stilt house that he lived in for the remainder of his life.
this was special to visit... HCM is preserved with so much dignity and reverence. the line is long but it moves very fast.

Incredible experience. Strangely moving.Our tour of the mausoleum was made all the better thanks to Huyen out tour guide. Her knowledge of Uncle Ho and his life was second to none. Thank you Huyen for spending the time with us and educating us. Every days a school day!!!

We visited on a Saturday and managed to see the body of Ho Chi Minh which is preserved here. Very strange experience but worth it. Get there early to avoid queues as it’s only open certain hours and these are limited.

When in Hanoi Vietnam, never fail to pay homage to the Uncle of the Country by visiting Ho Chi Mihn's Moseleum. The truest tribute to such a distinguished individual and continuing inspiration to his people

Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum, located in the center of Hanoi, is an extremely impressive and sacred historical site. With unique architecture, the mausoleum provides a solemn and respectful space, expressing the Vietnamese people's deep respect for President Ho Chi Minh.Visiting the mausoleum is a solennel experience, from moving in the long walkway lined with lush green trees, to entering the mausoleum space with ceremony and gratitude. The President Ho Chi Minh Museum next to the mausoleum helps visitors gain a deeper understanding of the President's life and career.In addition to its historical aspect, the mausoleum is also a place of cultural exchange, attracting international and foreign tourists to visit. Here, people often feel the solemnity and gratitude of an entire nation.Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum is not only a destination for Vietnamese people but also a symbol with global significance, expressing respect and gratitude to an outstanding leader.

The atmosphere is very solemn and ancient. All tourists seriously follow the regulations of this sacred place.I was able to participate and witness two wonderful moments: Flag raising and Flag loweringI feel very happy and sacred.
Amazing place, not to be missed.  Luckily not too busy when we went but guide said normally long queues.

I didn’t get into the mausoleum, unfortunately ;( you need to find out the schedule. You are allowed into the territory in clothes with long sleeves, or take a scarf to cover your shoulders and arms, short shorts are also not allowed. I liked the museum, the entrance costs 40,000. There is also small temple. Lots of souvenir shops, cafes.
A must see while you are in Hanoi. We did not go inside, just stopped by the see the outside. A very impressive mausoleum.
Worth it. Be aware it says it closes at 11.30am but that really means 10.50am. And it's closed two days each week. Go early.
The Vietnam government did this as if they never did anything wrong during the war. How ever there is a lot of interesting pictures , information and Ho  Ho Minh’s body laying in state
This is a magnificent monument to the old leader of the country, everything is immaculate and a vast open area with fine gardens, well worth a visit.

Early in the morning at 5 am it was illuminated with red light. In the evening on the first day I arrived and there was a pathway that people liked to walk around on. There is a yellow painted line that visitors are not allowed to cross. Avoid using your selfie stick here outside the masoleum. Although there is no sign indicating this, but the guards will tell you no and indicate you are not allowed to use one. Of course inside you're not allowed to take pictures. You'll walk through the masoleum and be able to view Ho Chi Minh. Leave your hands to your sides. Don't wear glasses or sunglasses on top of your head.

Very impressive, to enter I had a lot of difficulty because I was wearing Bermuda shorts and it was not allowed, I bought a skirt from a street vendor and entered not the Mausoleum, but outside the Mausoleum, where Ho Chi Minh was kept.

Visiting the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum in Hanoi was an educational and spiritual experience. The reverential aura and respectful atmosphere created by the place was palpable. Despite the solemnity of the occasion, I couldn't help but be amused by the fact that I was standing in the presence of the embalmed body of a former leader! It was an eye-opening experience that gave me insight into the spiritual significance of the mausoleum and the reverence it holds in Vietnamese culture.

The president Ho Chi Minh was burred in this place called Mausoleum, this is main attraction of Hanoi in our tour, there many other important official building nearby the Mausoleum, the beautiful building is surrounded by parade march pass ground.
We arrived around 9:00 AM and joined the queue. It moved relatively quickly, so we were finished about 40 minutes later. We hadn't expected that at all.

We visited as part of our motorbike tour , dropped off and walked through . Huge monumental business , awe inspiring and to be respected .Great gardens behind to have a cool drink with well kept gardens .

The line is long, but it moves fast. Very, very solemn and reverent scene inside the mausoleum itself. For the Vietnamese, it's more than about Communism. Ho Chi Minh was their leader in liberation and independence, and he is widely revered. There were people literally crying when we went by. Obviously no photos allowed inside. Outside, however it's entirely different. Hordes of schoolchildren and ordinary folks taking pictures. The kids are eager to meet foreigners, take selfies and practice their English. It's a really festive scene. The best way to do this is to go very early. Both Tripadvisor and Google are wrong about opening times. When I was there it was open 3 days a week, 7:30-10:30 AM only. And it's best to use the museum entrance on the West side and leave your bags there. If you got through the South entrance, they can hold your bags, but you have to rush back before they close that checkpoint at 11AM to get them. That is extremely far out of the way, because generally there is a whole circuit that begins at the museum entrance, goes to the mausoleum, then through the old presidential palace, Ho Chi Minh's stilt house, one pillar pagoda, and back to the museum. The museum is open till 12 and then closes for lunch. You generally can't go backwards on the circuit.
You feel awe when you see their leader, but many hours of waiting in line!! However, I don't regret it because we go once!!!

HCM Mausoleum (Open 730AM-1030AM) Rode GRAB Motorbike @ 730AM. 20KVND/85 cents charged to my CC. There were 2 different places to leave possessions. One was for cameras (not phone). They gave me a plastic tag. And said the camera will delivered to a pickup point after viewing HCM.At a 2nd place I dropped off my backpack with phone inside. And they gave me another numbered plastic tag. After the viewing; the camera was awaiting me at a nearby pickup point. But, I had to go back to the original dropoff point to retrieve my backpack. Then, went to the HCM Museum.(Open 8AM-Noon).While inline waiting to enter Mausoleum; watched soldiers perform changing of the guard and laying of wreaths, which was interesting.

one should  not miss visiting this mausoleum while in hanoi. Went there at 9:00AM and there was not much waiting time to enter the mausoleum.  One is not allowed to carry camera and bags into the mausoleum and need to deposit our belongings in the security office before entering the mausoleum.The entrance is free and the mausoleum is open in the morning and after a break, it is also open in the afternoon, the surrounding gardens are beautiful  and  well  maintained
A place that is absolutely worth seeing if you stop in Hanoi, rich in history and majesty, it dominates next to some pitchforks and what was once Ho Chi Minh's residence.

The whole grounds were beautiful. We couldn't go inside the Mausoleum, but we did see a changing of the guard. We took pictures with a group of very friendly and elegant Vietnamese ladies dressed in traditional clothing.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a monument of immense significance, reflecting the deep love and affection of the Vietnamese people for their revered leader. Inaugurated in 1975, the Mausoleum has been visited by countless generations, organizations, individuals, and millions of international visitors with admiration. With its clean, beautiful, and solemn setting, the Ba Dinh Square area in general, and the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum in particular, rightfully deserves to be considered the cultural center of Hanoi.
It is really big to explore . Beautiful place with the rich history. I spent a lot time walking around this place. You will have a nice tour
It was beautiful. We only understood more after reading about the history. I recommend doing some research before you go.

The mausoleum itself is an impressive structure made of grey granite, with a large central hall that houses the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh. Visitors can pay their respects to the leader by walking past the glass sarcophagus containing his body.Visitors are required to dress modestly and respectfully, and photography is not allowed inside the mausoleum. The mausoleum is usually open to the public on weekday mornings, but it may be closed on certain days or during official events.Overall, the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is an important historical and cultural site in Vietnam, and a visit can provide insight into the country's revolutionary past and the legacy of its revered leader.

We got up early to beat the queues which was a really good idea as they were already there in large numbers at 7am. We were the only westerners there as the Vietnamese queued in large family groups to pay respects to the country's former leader and revolutionary. It was fascinating just to see that on such a scale. We paid our £2 and shuffled along in a queue and soon enough filed past the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh, lying in a glass case, clearly revered by the visitors. It's all over very quickly but compared to the chaos of the city in general, this was well organised, clean, respectful and interesting. The soldiers on duty are immaculate and there's some marching and laying of wreaths. As one of just 5 remaining communist countries in the world it was fascinating to see the real respect the people showed.

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is a must-visit destination for anyone interested in Vietnamese history and culture. Located in Hanoi, it is the final resting place of Ho Chi Minh, the revolutionary leader who helped to liberate Vietnam from French colonial rule. The mausoleum is open to the public for a limited time each day, and visitors are required to follow strict dress codes and conduct themselves with respect and decorum. It is recommended to check the opening hours and guidelines before visiting the mausoleum. You need to check the schedule before visiting this place as it might be closed,

My wife and myself are currently visiting Hanoi (staying at the excellent Lasiesta Hotel - Review to follow) and earlier today we visited Ho Chi Minh’s Mausoleum.To my mind, this is a must do but needs a little planning as not only is the Mausalium closed on Mondays & Fridays but only open in the mornings on the other days. It is also very popular and attracts large crowds. Decorum (closely marshalled by the Presidential Guards) and a dress code (no shorts or certainly none below the knee) also applies.My advice go later rather than easier when the queue can be very long but the choice is very much down to the individual but - above all - go as this is a must do!!
The burial place of Ho Chi Minh and where he frequently gave speeches. No pictures/dress code required.

We had read online that Ho Chi Minh suggested he wanted to be cremated and his ashes scattered in different places. Clearly it’s a bit controversial, but nevertheless we thought the experience was very interesting. You only get around 20 seconds or so in the room with the embalmed body, and the guards are extremely strict on clothing and the way you act when you are there. I suggest long trousers and something to cover your sleeves (men included) as I was told to put my jumper on. Also cover your ankles, feet and shoulders. Entry is free and queues can be long, and be wary of opening times as my understanding is you can only see him in the morning.
Part of Vietnam's history, you absolutely have to go, check the schedules and days carefully, check where you can take photos because that's where the soldiers are, bring water, it's very hot

This was my first time visiting President Ho Chi Minh's mausoleum, and I was truly impressed. I never imagined such a magnificent structure could exist. It's been so many years since we've been to Hanoi. Thank you so much.
The place is really cool! Besides being wonderful and very pleasant, I'd like to warn you: NEVER TAKE A TAXI IN FRONT OF THERE. I WAS SCAMMED BECAUSE OF A TAMPERED TAXIMETER!! BE CAREFUL!!

This encompassing commemoration is a reminder of Vietnam's communist identity. The mausoleum is a Lenin-sque monument to the founding father of red Vietnam. Patriotic Vietnamese goes inside to see the remains.

We went here on a Saturday (morning - NB: it closes at 11am) and followed the long queues of school children. Despite it taking around an hour and a half to enter, it was worth the wait…and the kids were really sweet. Something you will probably never experience anywhere else in the world.
A bit crowded but interesting to take a visit to experience a glimpse of history. The area is nice with many places to visit nearby.

This will probably be a stop on most city tours, and rightfully so. It gives you deeper insight into the history of the country as well as the respect the Vietnamese people have for their country. As such, there are a lot of basic rules for respect, such as no shorts, dress modestly, and don't take photos in certain areas. For some reason, people did not understand this and showed up in beachwear. For bizarre and inexplicable reasons, their toddlers had bare midriffs (but were denied access). And people of course kept taking photo, photo, photo. Interesting glimpse into Vietnamese history. Sad and depressing glimpse into human behavior.
The mausoleum can only be visited on certain days of the week, when it is strictly guarded by gendarmes and you must walk in single file, without being allowed to take photos.

Everyone visits this site. Check opening times because they vary by day/week/month/holidays. Do not visit on holidays because the lines are huge. Go early as well. A lot of rules to follow - dress code, hats, how to walk (!), no talking (inside). After seeing HCM himself you can then walk around the grounds and visit his house etc. Easy to spend a long time here.

We went on a weekend morning around 10am. There a bag drop at the entrance which is convenient. We were fortunate as there was only a very short wait to visit, about 10 minutes. Similar to visit Chairman Mao, we queued and made our way to see the well preserved body. We then made our way to his old house and in total spent a good 1 hour visiting. Entry was free. Most were dressed in smart casual which is the appropriate wear as a courtesy and respect to the host.

Questa è un'esperienza che consiglio a tutti i visitatori del Vietnam. Non tanto per il mausoleo dove è conservata la salma, bensì per lo stie di vita che aveva da presidente. viveva in una piccola casa con una camera da letto ed uno studio. non aveva cucina e neppure bagno. Lui usava quelli usati dal personale che erano a circa 150 mt dal suo ufficio. Il tutto in un complesso enorme dove ha anche sede il governo attuae. tutto molto ben tenuto, prati, fiori ed alberi da frutto

Well maintained and huge campus. At least you need  minimum of 2 hours to walk around the full museum and the surroundings.Lot of information about Ho Chi Minh the great man of Vietnam. The presidential palace, his house, cars all are in display. Entrance ticket is 40,000 VND per person.To know the Vietnamese history and to walk around and see the palace, mango path, buddha tree root pathways are worth walking around. Luckily rain god stopped and we could enjoy the walk.

La precisione è quello che colpisce entrando qui.Precisione nella manutenzione del luogo,nelle file ben ordinate,  anche dei  bambini.nei giardini..ogni cosa è impeccabile.Quando entri al cospetto della salma mummificata di Ho  Chi Minh..ti pervade un senso di ...rispetto..Conservata perfettamente sembra che dorma.Per i vietnamiti è  stato un personaggio di grane importanza storica e culturale, e qui respiri questo sensi di devozione più che mai!

We walked up from the Old Quarter which only took about 30 minutes. You drop off your bag and then we did not queue for long at all before we were in the mausoleum. Uncle was resting in a glass case. We loved the experience and it is free. Note you must wear long trousers and shirt with sleeves (short sleeves ok). A must see!

I got a motorbike taxi from old town for 22,000 VND. Arrived at 8am on a Saturday morning. Queued at the main entrance & quite quickly got through. Took around 30mins waiting in total before I got to enter the main Mausoleum building. Water and food you have in your bag is held in a secure free of charge area at the reception which you walk back to on the way out & collect. Was able to take backpack with phone inside, strictly not phones or photos allowed once you reach the main mausoleum compound. Make sure you wear long trousers and T-shirt. Well worth doing, amazing to see the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh at rest. Lovely walk back through the gardens afterwards. You can take photos of the mausoleum building once you are outside. Picked up a motorbike taxi back to old town from the main entrance area.
Really enjoyed our visit here. The outside of the mausoleum is impressive and the gardens and lake are lovely. Lots of interesting history to learn about..

A very interesting and unmissable place to visit. It's important to wear clothing that falls below the knee and has sleeves. The visit to see Ho Chi Minh's mausoleum is in the morning and closes at 11:00 AM. After that, you can visit the center of the mausoleum (which is equally impressive) and Ho Chi Minh's workshop.

It is an impressive monument and everyone who visit Hanoi should see it. Many people recommend to go early at 7.30 am, but I can tell you from my experience that I enter the gate at 8.50 am and at 9.10 I've already seen the Mausoleum.It is a check at entrance and then a queue, but It is all very organized and you walk very quickly from the gate  to  the Mausoleum.
It's definitely worth a visit because it's very beautiful and elegant. Especially, you can learn a lot about the history of your country.
A beautiful sunrise, great weather too. Dress respectively and don't bring any bags as these will not be allowed in. On days when visitation is allowed.
If you're visiting Hanoi, you can't miss the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum. It's a place that evokes feelings of heroism and the majesty of Vietnam. Thank you. Please
The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is one of the most sacred places in the capital. I was fortunate enough to attend a flag-raising ceremony there, which was very moving.
Very interesting experience. Good value for money. Wear long pants and tops with covered shoulders. Come at 0700hrs to secure a spot in the mausoleum viewing queue.

Visiting the home of the Father of the Nation on a trip with the 5th grade students from Chu Van An High School on Cat Ba Island, the feeling of queuing in long lines to visit him was an unforgettable and moving experience. I will return to remember his face even more. I love you, Father.
I did not not only go to the mausoleum, but the grounds gave it a 5 due to historical value. There is a lot of beauty in the grounds.

Amazing place to understand more about the History of Vietnam and what the people have gone through! Hien from Hanoi Free City Tour Guides was super amazing and took us through the museum with a lot of patience and details

Our family with young children visited the Ho Chin Minh museum on 19 June, Monday. We took up the free tour guide service provided by the museum and was assigned to Kelsey. She spoke fluent english and explained each of the exhibits clear enough for the children to understand. Kelsey was really passionate when bringing us around the museum, and was very warm and friendly towards us tourists. Our visit to the museum was very educational and informative because of this tour!

There were atleast a thousand people in queue when we reached and it took around 30-40 minutes of dragging to reach to chamber where the body of their great leader is preserved. The time and the pain was worth at the end and I will recommended visitors to definitely include this place in their itinerary.One should start early when the mausoleum opens to get quicker access. Also visitors with sleeveless tops or short pants/skirts are not allowed entry. Men with knee length shorts are Ok. Will recommend sober full clothing to avoid disappointment.
Had a blast while visiting here!i like the freindlines of the people here,and the architecture here.Would visit again!

I went here before the pandemic - Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum is the resting place of President Ho Chi Minh. You can enter the mausoleum to view his embalmed body on a glass guarded with soldiers. You must be quite early to get in the queue. I didnt go inside and just took a photo outside

We visited Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum on March 10th, a public holiday, so there was a large influx of visitors. It was also the beginning of summer, so the weather was quite humid. Especially during this period, with COVID-19 still prevalent, the organization, traffic management, coordination, and supervision were very strict. Many people from the South came to visit Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum on that occasion; their faces showed excitement, joy, and a mix of nostalgia and emotion. Some were seeing Ho Chi Minh for the first time, while others had visited before but longed for a return visit.
Peaceful and lovely place to visit, which locates right in the center of Hanoi capital. Our walk there was so interesting, with a connection visit to Westlake and Tran Quoc pagoda

While the building itself is significant, its purpose and contents are undoubtedly even more so. Inside lie the remains of Ho Chi Minh, the most important leader and figure in Vietnamese history. He was, and continues to be, a central figure for all Vietnamese people. The solemnity of visiting the mausoleum to pay respects and pay homage is truly awe-inspiring. You must maintain proper decorum and decorum. The people of Vietnam hold him in high esteem and will not tolerate even the slightest disrespect. Photography is prohibited inside, as is speaking during the tour. Naturally, your attire should also be appropriate. The security surrounding the mausoleum is impressive, with armed military personnel stationed throughout. I personally deeply appreciate that when someone gives their life for a cause, for a country, and does so admirably, and is considered by the entire population to have done so, they are given the respect and recognition they deserve.

This is where the Great Leader, Ho Chi Minh is resting. You can see the body of Ho Chi Minh, resting in the glass case. Its a nice place to go and pay respects. Its very strict there, no loitering around, you can't take pics inside the mausoleum and talk too loudly. All this shows how much of love and respect is given for Ho Chi Minh. If you are there, please do show some respect for the Great Leader. There are soldiers in white uniforms on guard duty and marching around the Mausoleum. I did try to ask permission to take a photo with one of the soldier stationed there. He politely told us that he is on duty and he cant take pictures. We respected that and we moved on.

I wouldn't say "Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum" is a tourist attraction, but it is certainly one of the most revered monuments by all Vietnamese. At Hanoi's "Ba Dinh Square",  President Ho Chi Minh's (*May 9, 1890 +September 2, 1969) embalmed body lies inside a glass case with dim lights. Outside the impressive marble building, inspired in "Lenin's Mausoleum" in Moscow (but with Vietnamese architectural elements), huge queues and honor guard. Before going, check its visiting hours, as it changes according to seasons. Dress properly, join the queue, keep the pace, be silent and respectful. No pictures, no stops and no entry fees. Enjoy!

It was a sacred feeling standing before the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum, watching the large red flag with a yellow star fluttering against the starry sky. This was my first time here, and the cool November weather in Hanoi was lovely. The strict guarding by the soldiers made it very safe.
A must-see, a place of honor for the unique Ho Chi Minh, is a must-see when visiting Vietnam.

We had such a lesson of Vietnam's history on our visit to the Mausoleum. The campus looks so nice, peaceful and solemn. We had great time to get inside the Mausoleum, visit the stilt house and one pillar pagoda. Memorable!
The mausoleum resembles Lenin's in Moscow. Everything is very dignified. You can tell how important this place is to the Vietnamese.

I last came to this place a long time ago, and I had just visited it again, to see Ho Chi Minh and feel the cold atmosphere, a strange feeling of gratitude. It's horrifyingly quiet, you can hear yourself thinking. The guards are like silhouettes. The visit was lovely and I cant wait to find an opportunity to go again.

Very interesting place to visit. Ensure you check that it is open the day you go as we went on a Friday and the you could only go in the grounds so we went back the next day. You need to dress respectful and behave respectfully. We had to wear a mask the day we went and not sure if this is standard practice but go prepared.

What an amazing place. The atmosphere is sombre but you can sense the respect and love that the locals hold for this great man. Obviously you need to be dressed in a suitable manner, covering shoulders etc. Take your cameras with you cos although you cant use them in the first part of the tour, you can in the grounds and his house etc, which you get to see after passing through the mausoleum. We were lucky enough to have a very informative guide who gave us an insight into this mans life.

A visit to Ho Chi Minh's Mausoleum in Hanoi would make you aware of the respect Vietnamese people attach to this towering personality. From September for a couple of months every year, it gets closed as Ho Chi Minh's body is taken to Russia for getting embalmed again. Photographs are allowed but it'd be great if you pay a visit to the Mausoleum first. Entry here is free for all.

President Ho Chi Minh - Vietnamese national liberation hero and great cultural figure. After his death on September 2, 1969, in accordance with the aspirations of the Vietnamese people, the Communist Party decided to build the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum and the Ho Chi Minh Museum. After 20 years of preparation and construction, the Ho Chi Minh Museum was opened on September 2, 1990, on the occasion of the president's 100th birthday.

Located inside the Ba Dinh Square. Make sure u enter from the right entrance. We went to the wrong entry point, so walked around 15 minutes to reach the right one. The Mausoleum is open to visit only for certain hours during the day. Make sure to be informed of the time beforehand. U have to behave in an orderly manner,walking in a straight line to enter the Mausoleum. Once ur in the whole atmosphere inside gives u goosebumps. Soldiers standing in attention around the remains of Ho Chi Minh without moving a single muscle. In my opinion its a must visit place. No food allowed inside, u can leave it at the counter outside
The highlight was seeing Ho Chi Minh. Memorable. Also enjoyed visiting the Museum. Learnt alot about the ideas of Ho Chi Minh. We went on a Sunday morning. Little queue.

Temporarily due to Corona concerns there is a policy visitors must wear a mask. Two drug stores sell them across the street. About half a block away from the entrance next to a hardware store is a little shop with a better cheaper variety.

We went to the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum to go to the museum and of course to see Ho Chi Minh lying in state.   He is in air tight glass case and it is a very somber and quiet moment as you walk around the glass case viewing his body.   Now some people would think this is kind of weird but I saw Lenin in Moscow and it is still a part of history.   They are open only in the mornings and woman must be dressed in a sedate and respectful manner.  No shorts, no sleeveless blouses and or flip flops.  Men..no dago tees, flip flops or running shorts.   There are honor guards all along the walk ways and going up the stairs and into the mausoleum.  No cameras are allowed so don't plan to bring one in.   The entire viewing will take about 30 seconds because they will move you along but it is still a chance to see Ho Chi Minh lying in state.   The rest of the grounds, the stilt house, the museum, and the parade grounds is worth seeing.  Allow at least 2 to 3 hours.   Have a good tour.

An impressive palatial building and square which feels at the heart of Hanoi.The final resting place for Ho Chi Minh and the parliamentary buildings around. Then into the grounds where the traditional french design led building followed by the more simple but equally beautiful former house of Ho Chi Minh.There is a lot of history information here and plenty to take in. We walked down a little further and also saw I’m the one pillar pagoda which is also an amazing feet of design. You are surrounded by history and it brings all the elements of Vietnam together.
A very interesting place! So much to learn! A lovely place to explore, stroll around, and learn about its history.

Found this experience slightly strange but impressive with guards standing like statues in respect.  Was told to take my hands out of pockets which I fully understood.  The country obviously have heard just one side of the story of his "reign".

A striking large Mausoleum built in a Russian Style.Sitting on the edge of the large parade Square. Very Interesting regardless of your political views. Just as a sight to see from outside it is good. You must pass through a security check point to get into the parade Square.Only open in the mornings (except Monday & Friday) for 2-3 hours from 0730.
The guide who took us was very knowledgable and this definitely made the visit worthwhile. Having the explanations and understanding the history was essential to get full benefit from the visit

Our guide brought us past the Presidential palace to the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum.After passing through security to a large square, we walked up to this huge and stoic monument only to find it closed.However, not feeling a great need to see a tomb, walking past it was good enough.Hanoi, for all its claims to be a communist city was more like a democratic place, or at least the people have a bent for democracy.  I don’t think it will be too long before the people throw out the communist party and it’s elite in favor of power to the people.

For me, the most impressive sight in Hanoi. Despite the long queue at the entrance, everything went quite quickly, as no one is allowed to stand still inside the mausoleum and photography is prohibited. In my opinion, the palace on the same grounds isn't worth visiting.
The permanent guard at the mausoleum is surprising, and seeing Ho Chi Minh's body is moving; a powerful and intense experience.

It is a very stirring building from the outside, reminding me of the Lincoln Memorial in Washington DC. We were there on a Monday and it was closed. Every 2 hours they do have the changing of the guard which we saw, complete with goose-stepping marching. Directly across the square from the Mausoleum is the beautiful Vietnam parliament building.

A truly unique experience at Uncle Ho's mausoleum, with all the guards and soldiers. A bit spooky, given the constant 16 degrees Celsius, and considering he's been lying there for about 50 years, he doesn't look his best. Otherwise, very stately, a true national hero.
Our sincere thanks to AUTENTIK VIETNAM and Mr. Victor. The organization was perfect; you respected all our wishes.
Impressive building and site that lets you feel some of the history of the country. Very clean tidy and well worth visiting.
The mausoleum was definitely eye opening it Is honestly amazing to pay respects to such a great man and to see him laying there left me speechlessHighly recommended viewing and paying respects
What a tribute this is to Ho Chi Minh. The grounds and building are very well-groomed. An excellent piece of history to witness.  Very interesting to watch the guards walk the grounds.  Beautiful.

This was my first mausoleum visit and at the same time that it is very strange also it is very interesting. It is a place of highly respect so no hats, no hands in pockets, conservative clothes too. The visit is very short but after the mausoleum you have the opportunity to see some of other buildings of importance. It was very unclear how to get to the entrance, so I had to do extra walk to find it. Getting there: the entrance is located on the corner of Le Hong Phong and Ngoc Ha. You will go thru security and if you have electronic such as cameras you will have to check then in before proceeding to the visit. You can take cellphones with you but they have to be putted away. I also heard that liquids were not allowed so I left water, hand sanitizer at the hotel to prevent any delays. I arrived around 7:40am and the line was with about 35 people, highly recommend early arrival. My hotel told me that the mausoleum opened at 8 but I was glad I followed google and went with 7:30.
Has to be visited if you visit Hanoi. The most important leader in Vietnam for a century is honored in a magnificent memorial. Appropriate dress and manners required

It was spooky to witness Vietnamese coming to pay their respects to the man who gained independence in 1953.It it many ways was their thank you for freedom.I had seen Lenin in 1983 and it was just as eerie.The grounds and historic displays were fascinating.

This really is a must, what a fascinating insight into the life of Ho Chi Minh. No photos are allowed when in the mausoleum and Ho Chi Minh really does look like he is sleeping. While there you can also see the stilted house he lived in, which is a must see too.
I had booked with Hanoi Transfer service and on one day city tour, we had visited to Ho Chi Minh mausoleum and its was worth place to visit and knowing about Lated President Ho Chi Minh history

You have to visit the mausoleum - our guide used as a base for the recent history of Vietnam.Yes it is a dead body but it's your way of showing respect to a country that is so welcoming and respectful to you

This is an interesting place to visit. A stroll past the mausoleum (heavily guarded) and lots of instructions where to walk and where not to! Lovely gardens and ancient pagodola behind. Worth visiting if you are in Hanoi

I don’t know whether we inadvertently latched on to someone else’s tour but we had read that a) we would have to pay for entry and b) wouldn’t be allowed anywhere near the mausoleum itself. Imagine then our surprise, when we found ourselves filing past the body of old HCM himself. Quite the experience and not even much of a line to wait in.

If you come to Hanoi it seems a fitting part of Vietnam history to visit the Mausoleum. Only there is no time to ponder over Ho Chi Minh and history as you just have to keep walking past and I guess this is because of volume of visitors.  The Mausoleum was free entry but the surrounding grounds were a small fee for entry.  We enjoyed our visit, no photographs permitted and was all over quite quickly.

Not visited, seen at night! A long line is drawn across the path, excluding those who cross. Guards are stationed at the entrance. The nighttime lighting is beautiful, making the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum very impressive; the grounds are vast!

We went there yesterday and It is a lengthy excursion. Be prepared to walk around and no shorts or straps for clothes. The admissions place closes at 10:30 and it is a lengthy event. Watch the taxi drivers to and from and get a firm price for where you are going and have the exact amount in hand beforehand. They are pros at getting to you and you are an amateur.
Our guide toured us through the Mausoleum and was very knowledgable. The guide gave us a very good narrative of the historical importance
Quite impressive... easy to reach... it was a holiday in Hanoi, so I couldn't go in, but as I said, it was still a good idea to stop by.
I thought the whole place was properly and respectfully arranged. An important place to visit if you’re visiting Hanoi.
Felt great seeing his body and feel his soul.Great man! The greatest! I recommend visit it for sure!

This was an experience that very few people can say they have done. As it can only be done in very few countries. When in Vietnam be sure to wake up early and take part in the visiting of Ho Chi Minh in his embalmed state. Watch the changing of the guards and enjoy the beauty of the area around the mausoleum. Be sure if you want to go into the mausoleum to go to the entrance near the ho chi Minh museum about an our before it opens, depending on the time of the year it's either 7:30 am or 8:00 am. Entrance is free.
Enough has already been said and written about the history, but if you are in Hanoi, this visit is certainly worthwhile.

This place is well worth a visit. Very clean and respectful. You absolutely must see the interior and the embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh. The gardens of the presidential residence are also worth seeing. The mausoleum is a must-see when visiting Hanoi.

For anyone who comes to visit Hanoi, the place is impressive in its beauty, very large and worth a visit. There is a place inside the complex called the Lonely Pillar Pagoda . As its name suggests, he built a small pagoda on a pillar and around the pillar there is water with fish.
The body of Ho Chi Minh is preserved so well. Definitely a must visit. Also do dress conservatively to visit this place
Clearly great pride is had! The gardens are stunning, the environment is well controlled by staff, and the hall is serene. Don't forget to check out the buddhist temole too. Worth the visit!

It was pretty amazing seeing Ho Chi Minh and his resting place. Don't you dare try to take a photo. Nice how it was free also. At the end we opted not to see the presidential palace, as you can't go in it, it's just a money grab in my opinion. Enjoy.

We went here to pay or respects to Ho Chi Minh and it was well worth the trip! We went to the Temple of Literature first as we were too late to go inside the Mausoleum but the structure was still exciting to see from the outside. We saw the changing of the guards and then went to the museum which was also great! Although we both knew who Ho Chi Minh was we had a much better understanding of his importance to Vietnam and the person he was after the visit.

We went with a friend who was in a wheelchair due to an injury. The courtesy shown was amazing. We were expedited through the lines with the staff pushing the wheel chair. Although the lines seemed to be moving pretty fast even without the helpful assistance. When we neared the mausoleum building a collection of white-uniformed guards appeared and carried the wheelchair with friend in it up the numerous series of steps in and out of the mausoleum. Incredibly thoughtful and kind.

Ho Chi Minh is revered by the Vietnamese and was humbling to see his body. Extremely well organised and efficient queuing. This site is well worth a visit whilst in Hanoi. Also, worth checking out Ho's house on stilts which is nearby. Very large complex and not far from Old Quarter.

The mausoleum of statesman Ho Chi Minh is definitely worth a visit. It's best to go early in the morning, when the lines aren't quite so long. Just a heads-up: shoulders and knees must be covered, and cameras must be handed over; photography is strictly prohibited.

Amongst other places, my family and I visited was the Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum where the preserved body of Vietnam’s first President Ho Chi Minh is on display. He is highly revered - and I mean extremely high. Queues of mainly locals & school kids blended with tourists like us attended this location. I must admit, it is a very solemn moment when you walk around his body which is well policed by the local military (all immaculately dressed) to ensure everyone’s behaviour is in check. President Ho Chi Minh died in 1969 and I respectfully say that he looks very well cared for. Photography in the building is a huge NO.
it was nice, very organized. he was loved and well respected by the proud people of Vietnam, this place is a must visit.
The entire area surrounding the mausoleum is a bit creepy, with this avenue monitored by a guard every 10 meters. The structure is quite simple but majestic.

Located adjacent to Ba Dinh Square, you can view Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body firsthand upon entering. Security checks are conducted upon entry, and bags and personal belongings must be left at the cloakroom; photography is prohibited inside. Additionally, please refrain from speaking loudly or engaging in disrespectful behavior.

We arrived too late to enter into the mausoleum, but boy were we treated with the lit up structure and changing of the guards was an added bonus!  While this is a solemn place, it's majestic presence commands respectable awe.
Ho Chi Minh is a humble but extraordinary, hugely popular and most respected leader of the nation. This feels exactly like a pilgrimage. A must visit place.
It is very impressive to see the remarkably well-preserved mummy of Ho Chi Minh. The museum is also worth seeing. Definitely recommended.

As a child with a passion for US history and with one of my biggest passions the cold war I came to Vietnam to learn more about learn more about the history of the country and to see important historical sites.  This was a fascinating chance to see the last resting place of perhaps the most important person in Vietnamese history.  Was truly amazing to be able to see Ho Chi Minh and this Mausoleum and is a day I won't forget.
This is a must do for anyone who visits Hanoi. It is a very solemn experience to see Ho Chi Minh lining in state 50 years after his passing.

Very strict upon entry. No bare shoulders and no short skirts. Take a jacket to cover up. Any liquid bottles you take in are confiscated, opened or not.You have to check in your cameras and they are returned to you after you exit the mausoleum.Definitely a must do if your around the area. The mausoleum part is free but the other section you have to pay for.
The remains of Ho Chi Minh, the founding father of Vietnam, are enshrined here after being embalmed.

It's been over 50 years since the death of Ho Chi Minh and the end of the Vietnam War only a few years later, so to visit this mausoleum and see Uncle Ho lying in state was.....incredible!  Regardless of the politics and the arguments about the war, Ho Chi Minh was a mighty leader for what he believed in, and again, whatever one may think about it all, the reunification of Vietnam was something to which he  devoted his life.
A place steeped in history, where we entered the mausoleum very early in the morning to avoid the crowds.
Free activity to do everyday 5am and 9pm where you see the troops raise and pull down the Viet Flag with marching parade.
You all enter in a line and move block by block escorted by a guard who takes you from point A to point B, then another guard arrives who takes you to point C, all strictly in line.

Beautifully build in front of big green lawn. This site is one of most important site to preserve Vietnamese history! The site itself is now becoming the instagramable site in Hanoi. Come and have a visit!
The mausoleum is well worth a visit. The plaza where it stands is pedestrianized and makes for a lovely walk.
The most important thing, It is a historic piece that keeps the spirit who guides and projects the actual Vietnam. When you are there the emotions will come and showing you the Great's Ho Chi Minh.
A place to pay respect to their local great politician. Quite crowded with locals and tourist. It is a solemn and respectful environment so please dress appropriately and abide to the rules.

Great place to visit but watch for the closed days which are Monday and I think Fridays. Free to walk around but not sure if cost involved to enter the Mausoleum. Watching the guards changing is with waiting around for (they are regular).
Formal atmosphere, strict security. Inside quiet, fresh air. Some sections have soft, soothing music. In the mausoleum slightly cold.
Great site to visit Ho Chi Minh Buriel site well maintained and great sight seeing venue in a great location.
A guide should definitely be used during the visit to fully understand all the connections. Interesting, but also plenty to ask questions about.

After checking your bags, you can enter the large mausoleum complex as part of a guided tour. However, visits are only possible in the morning. There are guards everywhere to ensure you stay on the paths. I was lucky and managed to join the last tour. Photography and stopping are not allowed inside the mausoleum. You walk slowly past Ho Chi Minh, constantly watched by security personnel. Outside the mausoleum, you walk through a park to Ho Chi Minh's original residence. You can also see his cars and the simple house he had built. This is where he lived and worked most of the time. It's very interesting to see how this man and politician lived.

We decided to visit the mausoleum after seeing the imposing but beautiful building from the road. Having visited though, I thought it was an amazing cultural experience for the entire family and would recommend a visit for any travellers in Hanoi. It’s an important part of the Vietnamese national identity and we felt very humbled by the experience. Save yourself some time and read the dress code online (don’t wear shorts or short skirts, and cover your shoulders) and don’t bring any baggage (you will have to check in anything bigger than a small ladies bag).There was already a long queue when we arrived (fairly early in the morning) however it moved surprisingly quickly as everyone was politely but sternly shepherded by the guards. There was a palpable sense of solemnity as we approached the mausoleum itself. The grounds and surrounding gardens are meticulously (or perhaps militantly) kept, it was beautiful though it felt disconcertingly too square and too organised. The building itself is an amazing piece of architect, and you can take pictures outside but no photography was permitted inside. The guards kept a very brisk pace so viewing time was very short but more than enough to catch sight of the man himself.

Really interesting.  We visited on a weekday morning arriving around 8:30am.  The mausoleum visit took about 30 mins from security screening to exit.  Very efficient.  The line moves quickly and the soldiers ensure no-one steps out of line - a few people were told off for their lack of line discipline - so pay attention or that will be you.  The actual viewing of the body is brief as you walk around it.  Then before you know it you are walking down the stairs and out. I really recommend it as an unusual and interesting place to visit. We also went to the museum on the same site (5 minutes walk from the mausoleum). That was excellent too.  Possibly we got lucky as when we asked about audio guides they were all out.  No problem there was a volunteer guide available - Lyn (Lin?) a law student with a very keen interest in Ho Chi Minh who volunteers when she has time.  Wow what she didn’t know about the man and the museum exhibits would not be worth knowing.  Absolutely fascinating and we learnt so much.  Highly recommend the museum and if there is a volunteer guide available even better.

The place was strictly run by the military but you can understand why when they are guarding the preserved body of President Ho Chi Minh. I did not expect to see him so the feeling was momentous indeed. Then a tour to the Presidential grounds to how he had lived. A truly remarkable yet humble man whatever your political slant
If time allowing, do visit this site. Open skies and lovely plants. Well preserved architecture and interesting history.
Ho Chin Minh masouleum is a must visit with stately body preserved of uncle Ho along with well maintained gardens

The Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum boasts understated yet imposing architecture and a prime location. The grounds are vast, and the guards are ever-present; please do not cross the line. The changing of the guard is carried out calmly and with dignity. Tourists move about with the utmost respect for this figure who left his mark on Vietnam for so many years. (On December 10, 2019, during our visit, it was impossible to visit the interior.) The gardens are beautiful, and the grounds are very well maintained.

Seeing "Uncle Ho" was a very unique experience, and a highlight of my tour to Vietnam.  Do yourself a favour. Research the man before you arrive at the mausoleum, so that you understand his journey and how he united Vietnam. It is a powerful story, and he is a very interesting man. Yes, the queues are crazy, but as you get 30 seconds to see him, everything runs very smoothly. Just follow the rules! Seeing the place where Ho Chi Minh lived was also very interesting.

So we read that “uncle Ho” goes to Russia to be restored from Oct to Dec - luckily we went today and he’s back. The most humbling and amazing experience to see him you are not allowed to loiter or take photos and you must be dressed respectfully and walk in two lines around him- 3 minutes us all it took tops but would highly recommend you to do this - just not in Oct to mid Dec!

My second time back to visit, this time with our adult children. Though the lines are Long, they move at a steady pace and the wait does not feel long. The mausoleum and embalmed body of Ho Chi Minh succeed in providing respect and conveying a sense of gravitas for a man who embodied the struggle of a people’s will and desire to be free from colonialism and foreign subjugation. Be sure not to miss the nearby museum, where Ho Chi Minh lived and worked in a one-bedroom house.

It's always moving to stand before the burial place of a human being who shaped a period of history still so close to us. Beloved, almost idolized by a people who endured the tragedy of the civil war. In that mausoleum lies the country's recent history. A respectful bow and the visit can continue.
Military efficiency at it's best despite the long queues we went through very quickly. Definitely worth a visit.

Respect us the word that comes to the mind when one visit this place. No food water big cameras allowed. One is not allowed to speak its complete silence. That’s how they pay homage to the leader when u enter it feels that The great leader is just lying down and would be with us any moment. No hats or sunglasses on....... all I can say is hat off

I had the experience when I visited the place. There were thousands of students, the order was exemplary. When you arrive at the place, you're sure it will take hours to get through and see Ho Chi Minh's embalmed body, but everything flows.

Interesting experience as you make the Disneyland-like lines (which move quite fast) towards the mauseoleum.  Do follow the security guard's instructions / hand gestures.  Inside, the experience is surreal as you witness the body of Ho Chi Minh dimly lit in a unique glass and wood-carved display case.  You solemnly parade in and out in about 20 seconds.  No photos or video allowed.  This experience is free and definitely worth it.

Actually, this was interesting. First, I was a bit worried because of the long line, but it moves really quickly, so it took line 30 minutes only to get to the mausoleum.You can't take your camera, but you can have your phone. Taking photos isnstrictly prohibited inside.You can't wear any sunglases (even in the line outside the soilders told to get them off), no sleevless shirts or shorts or short skirts.The mausoleum is free of charge (you pay only the entrance to the komplex after the mausoleum.
Despite arriving near 11am closing time, we managed to enter the mausoleum and say Ho Chi Minh's body which was very amazingly embalmed. There wasn't any queue on Sat so I was pleasantly surprised.
A pilgrimage for every Vietnamese person. Respect is required to view the embalmed body. It's impressive to see all these people.

The Vietnamese have built a mausoleum complex befitting Ho Chi Minh's struggle. It's very impressive. This kind of tribute to a great struggle is a beautiful example of respect. The museum, his house, his final resting place, and the gardens are magnificent. I recommend you see and visit it.

Visiting this mausoleum is a very moving experience. You join a long queue of local visitors and tourists. Nevertheless, it moves quickly until you are allowed to file past the sarcophagus of the deceased Ho Chi Minh.
Thank you Chuong of Seven Tours for touring us and explaining the history of this place. I rate your service 5 star.

We visited on a Sunday. Long queues that moved quickly, and to my delight were authoritarianly marshalled by the local police. We (the queue) were told to move more quickly, to get in single file, and get closer to the kerb. The Mausoleum was also great, and there is clearly deep and genuine affection for their saviour and father of a nation.

It's better to be there around 8 am, maybe even earlier!!!! People come from everywhere by the hundreds!!!! You'll have to walk quite a bit, making a full circle until you reach the entrance of the museum, since it's forbidden to "cut the road". Police everywhere! Guards everywhere! The measures are strict, you're not allowed to talk loudly, or make noise in general. Ladies would do well to wear long shirts, since the dress code is strict, and gentlemen should wear long pants.
Very well organised & am impress with the help given by the seeing I'm struggling  with my walking stick.
